# Melissa copy manuscript

<!-- This normalized file is generated from the raw DOCX for review/search. Do not treat Markdown heading levels as editorial decisions. -->

<!-- paragraph:1 para_id:00000001 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 37

<!-- paragraph:2 para_id:00000002 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 37

<!-- paragraph:4 para_id:00000004 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5 para_id:00000005 style:Normal -->
Over the next few days of practice, Maya had to give Davian credit: If she told him a drill would help, he did it; if she told him to keep going, he kept going; and if she told him to stop, he stopped without argument. It would have been admirable if it didn’t come paired with a strange, selective stubbornness that surfaced whenever Harvick gave the same instructions.

<!-- paragraph:6 para_id:00000006 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s concealment of his Ink Pact still bothered her—the choice to keep it hidden even after everything that had happened. He must not see her as a full magus. Not even after the Presentation, not after the responsibilities she had already taken on.

<!-- paragraph:7 para_id:00000007 style:Normal -->
Aldira was still working through their network, trying to disentangle the layered lattices of deception Drakhal had draped over their movements and whatever concealed relationship they were maintaining with Archeon beneath it. It had been a hexday since the loss of the Heart, and Davian grew more restless with each passing day, circling back again and again to the same idea: go to Archeon, find his parents, end the uncertainty by force. Maya had explained—more than once—that they didn’t even know if his parents were there, that acting on incomplete information would only compound their disadvantage.

<!-- paragraph:8 para_id:00000008 style:Normal -->
They needed something actionable. A point of leverage. A way to strike before the hammer fell instead of waiting to be crushed beneath it. Until then, preparation was the only option.

<!-- paragraph:9 para_id:00000009 style:Normal -->
Maya spent her days in the courtyard, splitting her attention between Davian and Selwin. Teaching them at the same time forced her to articulate things she had never needed to explain before, and the act of explaining sharpened her own understanding in ways she had not anticipated. At night, the work changed but did not ease. She iterated on her own schema, testing and discarding variations designed to survive Archeon's close-range power. Their strength wasn’t just overwhelming; it was immediate, compressing decision and consequence into the same instant. Any response she developed had to match that speed or exceed it, which meant simplifying where she could and accepting risk where she couldn’t.

<!-- paragraph:10 para_id:0000000A style:Normal -->
Parallel to that, she worked on the Registry’s zan lattices, attempting to rewrite systems that had been designed in another age. She cursed herself for struggling with the ancient architecture, cursed the architects themselves for relying on near-infinite power input rather than building an efficient system. At a minimum, she felt like she should be able to bring Registry identification online at the gateways, but even those local areas required far more power than traditional zanwork sinks could supply.

<!-- paragraph:11 para_id:0000000B style:Normal -->
Erynn hovered at the edges with a stubborn practicality Maya did not have time for. “You are not the Creator,” she had said more than once, arms folded and expression immovable. “You are a teenager, and you need to sleep.” Maya had not found the argument persuasive. There was too much to do, and not enough time left to do it.

<!-- paragraph:12 para_id:0000000C style:Normal -->
So today a somewhat sleep-deprived Maya stood at the edge of the courtyard, watching Harvick and Davian circle while Selwin sat cross-legged a short distance away, eyes closed, hands resting loosely on his knees. Keeping them in the same space was partly convenience and partly necessity; she could monitor both at once, and she had not found anything more effective for Selwin than simply having him sit and try to find the reflex on his own. That, in itself, bothered her. When she had been younger than Selwin, Niall had not given her options—only pressure. Hours staring at a wall, the same warning repeated over and over: bring up your Shroud, or you will die. She remembered lying awake at night, staring at the ceiling, crying until she was too exhausted to move, the fear sitting heavy in her chest until one night her tears froze against her skin and she slept, deeply and without interruption, for the first time in what felt like weeks. The next day, the reflex had been there. She was not willing to put Selwin through that. But as she watched him sit there, still failing to find it, she couldn’t ignore the possibility that the pressure had been what was necessary—and that by avoiding it, she was simply delaying the inevitable.

<!-- paragraph:13 para_id:0000000D style:Normal -->
Motion snapped her attention back to the present as Harvick and Davian cut across her line of sight again. Harvick’s glowing blade hammered against Davian’s Shroud in a tight, relentless rhythm, each strike sending a faint ripple across the barely-visible barrier. Davian deflected one with his forearm, the surface of the Shroud flaring there as if the impact had been localized, then lunged forward, trying to close the distance and get his arms around Harvick. Harvick blurred backward just out of reach. Harvick had been dismayed when Maya had banned Davian from using a practice sword - or tools of any kind. But Maya had seen immediately that the tools were a distraction for Davian, and so he’d quickly learned that the only way to avoid taking a hit was constant focus on his Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:14 para_id:0000000E style:Normal -->
Even so, he was still doing it wrong. Maya watched as he shifted again, raising his arm to catch another blow, acting like his forearm was a shield instead of seeing his Shroud as one piece that protected his whole body.

<!-- paragraph:15 para_id:0000000F style:Normal -->
Her gaze lingered a moment longer than it should have, tracking the movement of his shoulders, the tension in his frame, the way the Shroud flickered across his body.

<!-- paragraph:16 para_id:00000010 style:Normal -->
On impulse, Maya stepped forward. “Good work, Harvick,” she said, before she had quite decided what she meant to do. “Give us a moment.”

<!-- paragraph:17 para_id:00000011 style:Normal -->
Harvick glanced between her and Davian, then lifted both hands and backed away with a look that suggested he had several thoughts and was wisely choosing to express none of them. Maya took his place across from Davian in the center of the courtyard, letting her gaze run over him with more clinical intent than she felt. Davian shifted under the scrutiny, suddenly uncomfortable in a way that made her realize she had been silent too long.

<!-- paragraph:18 para_id:00000012 style:Normal -->
Right. She needed to say something.

<!-- paragraph:19 para_id:00000013 style:Normal -->
“You’re thinking about too many things at once,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:20 para_id:00000014 style:Normal -->
Davian gave her a look. “You made an entire list of things for me to think about earlier.”

<!-- paragraph:21 para_id:00000015 style:Normal -->
“That isn’t what I mean.” Maya frowned, searching for a way to explain something that had always felt obvious in her own body. “It’s all there—the Ink, the Shroud, your magic, your body—but you’re treating them like separate problems. They need to be together. Made of many parts, yes, but still one thing.”

<!-- paragraph:22 para_id:00000016 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at her with an intensity that made the air between them feel oddly narrow. “One thing.”

<!-- paragraph:23 para_id:00000017 style:Normal -->
Frustration prickled under Maya’s skin. It felt so intuitive to her and so distant from him, as if he kept trying to assemble a person from scattered pieces instead of being one. “Just come at me.”

<!-- paragraph:24 para_id:00000018 style:Normal -->
Time to test the new anti-Archeon schema.

<!-- paragraph:25 para_id:00000019 style:Normal -->
She had forgotten how obedient Davian had become once he accepted an instruction. He lunged immediately, crossing the distance with startling speed, but this was her ground. The schema rose in her mind before he reached her, redirection surfaces carving into place with practiced clarity. By the time his strike drove toward her, her Shroud had anchored deep into the cool stone beneath her feet, the containment pattern waiting below like an inverted buried sphere.

<!-- paragraph:26 para_id:0000001A style:Normal -->
His arm stopped cold at the shimmering edge of her Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:27 para_id:0000001B style:Normal -->
Davian pulled up in stunned shock, but Maya could feel his Shroud pressing against hers where the fields overlapped, not breaking, not yielding cleanly, but vying for the same space with a stubborn, uneven force. She amplified the containment schema, carving more pathways to drag heat down into the inverted surface beneath them. Her body remained neutral, almost still, while his strike hung suspended inches from her. She could feel everything at once: the pressure of his Shroud against hers, the schema held bright in her mind, the carved pathways straining as they forced heat to move against the laws of reality.

<!-- paragraph:28 para_id:0000001C style:Normal -->
For one bright instant, the pattern felt right. Clean in a way Maya had been chasing since the Codex—since that strange reflected moment of purpose she had felt from her mother, sharp and luminous enough to make everything else seem briefly aligned. Davian leaned closer, straining against the border of her Shroud as heat continued to build in the ground beneath her. Maya smiled without meaning to. Through the effort locked across his face, Davian gave her a puzzled smile back.

<!-- paragraph:29 para_id:0000001D style:Normal -->
She lifted her hand to his chest.

<!-- paragraph:30 para_id:0000001E style:Normal -->
The carvings snapped into place. The containment sphere discharged all at once, heat surging up through the pathways she had forced open and converting into a shaped cone of flame.

<!-- paragraph:31 para_id:0000001F style:Normal -->
Davian flew backward across the courtyard, hit the stone inside his Shroud, bounced once, and somehow rolled back to his feet. The whole exchange had lasted only a few moments.

<!-- paragraph:32 para_id:00000020 style:Normal -->
“How can you do that so fast?” he said, wide eyed but somehow excited.

<!-- paragraph:33 para_id:00000021 style:Normal -->
Maya was still smiling. “For that last part, the hand movement isn’t strictly necessary. But linking a schema to a movement is one way to learn it more quickly, even if it makes the technique less flexible.”

<!-- paragraph:34 para_id:00000022 style:Normal -->
From the edge of the courtyard, Harvick called, “I heard ‘more quickly,’ and I agree with whatever it is you’re saying.”

<!-- paragraph:35 para_id:00000023 style:Normal -->
Davian rubbed at his chest, still watching her with that too-intent focus. “That seems really useful. You could have told me before.”

<!-- paragraph:36 para_id:00000024 style:Normal -->
“It isn’t really the way High Clades are supposed to do things,” Maya said. “It’s considered a trick. Something a lesser magus would rely on in the absence of true strength.” She hesitated, then looked away from him. “I came across it in my research when I was trying to understand Archeon's abilities.”

<!-- paragraph:37 para_id:00000025 style:Normal -->
Not when she had been looking for a way to help him.

<!-- paragraph:38 para_id:00000026 style:Normal -->
Davian either didn’t notice the pause or chose not to press it. “Okay. Let’s try that. Let’s keep going.”

<!-- paragraph:39 para_id:00000027 style:Normal -->
They practiced as the sun crossed the sky. Maya tried to explain the theory as best she could, having just learned it herself. Davian threw himself at every drill she gave him with a level of commitment that would have been absurd if it hadn’t been so effective. He failed, adjusted, failed again, and kept coming back without needing encouragement. It reminded Maya of their earlier training, before the theft, before the betrayal, and for a moment she regretted the distance now held between them by Shrouds and careful language.

<!-- paragraph:40 para_id:00000028 style:Normal -->
By the time the shadows began to lengthen, Davian was breathing hard and still trying to square his stance for another attempt.

<!-- paragraph:41 para_id:00000029 style:Normal -->
“That’s enough,” Maya said.

<!-- paragraph:42 para_id:0000002A style:Normal -->
He looked up immediately. “Let me try a few more times. I think I’m getting somewhere.”

<!-- paragraph:43 para_id:0000002B style:Normal -->
“You have to train tomorrow too.”

<!-- paragraph:44 para_id:0000002C style:Normal -->
“Yeah, but I just want more time with you—” He stopped so abruptly it was almost painful. “I mean. You know. Because you’re good at training.”

<!-- paragraph:45 para_id:0000002D style:Normal -->
It might have been a trick of the reddening sunlight, but his face seemed flushed.

<!-- paragraph:46 para_id:0000002E style:Normal -->
Maya held his gaze for a moment longer than she should have. “I will be here tomorrow too.”

<!-- paragraph:47 para_id:0000002F style:Normal -->
Some of the tension went out of him. “Okay. Yeah. Then, um...” He glanced away, then back. “Should we eat? Together. Like last time.”

<!-- paragraph:48 para_id:00000030 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer immediately.

<!-- paragraph:49 para_id:00000031 style:Normal -->
“I mean,” Davian added quickly, “you probably remember what we did—what we ate, I mean—so it’s fine, but...”

<!-- paragraph:50 para_id:00000032 style:Normal -->
Maya became intensely aware of Harvick and Erynn staring at the back of her head.

<!-- paragraph:51 para_id:00000033 style:Normal -->
“Okay,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:52 para_id:00000034 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:53 para_id:00000035 style:Normal -->
Most of the zanwrights were on reduced schedules while Maya redrafted the ward plans. That meant more of them were in the hall at once, clustered at the long tables with bowls, cups, half-finished diagrams, and the low restless chatter of people who had too much time and too little information.

<!-- paragraph:54 para_id:00000036 style:Normal -->
It was inconvenient. It was also unavoidable. Maya had been forced to confine the workers to the citadel to help control what news spread about the treasury and the Registry. More practically, they were some of the few people in the city whose auras had already been vetted. She tried not to think about how Davian had tricked the Registry with an Ink Pact. She had interrogated him about it enough to be reasonably certain it was not a simple thing to replicate, not the kind of weakness that meant the citadel might be packed wall to wall with hidden enemies.

<!-- paragraph:55 para_id:00000037 style:Normal -->
Reasonably certain was not the same as certain. Her pulse picked up. The thought tried to branch, one possibility catching another, each one pulling her toward the spiraling path that ended in the Skein. Too many catastrophes waited there. Too many futures where the next gap in her planning became the one that killed someone she should have protected.

<!-- paragraph:56 para_id:00000038 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Davian said. “Are you okay?”

<!-- paragraph:57 para_id:00000039 style:Normal -->
His voice cut through the spiral. Maya blinked and found herself standing just inside the dining hall with Davian beside her, Harvick and Erynn a few steps behind, and far too many people looking in their direction with varying degrees of success at pretending they were not.

<!-- paragraph:58 para_id:0000003A style:Normal -->
How long had she been standing there? Creator, I hope I wasn’t muttering to myself.

<!-- paragraph:59 para_id:0000003B style:Normal -->
Now was not the time for that. Right. She needed to answer.

<!-- paragraph:60 para_id:0000003C style:Normal -->
“I’m fine, of course,” she said, then immediately realized that sounded too abrupt to explain whatever he had noticed. “Just a little tired. From training.”

<!-- paragraph:61 para_id:0000003D style:Normal -->
Davian did not look convinced, but he gave her a small smile anyway. “I don’t know how you do it. All that training, all the other things you’re working on, and then eating and sleeping on top of it?”

<!-- paragraph:62 para_id:0000003E style:Normal -->
Maya grimaced despite herself. “Yes,” she said. “I have been doing all of those things. Especially the last two.” She could almost hear Erynn’s eyes rolling behind her.

<!-- paragraph:81 para_id:00000051 style:Title -->
# 👫 Prologue

<!-- paragraph:84 para_id:00000054 style:Heading2 -->
## Prologue

<!-- paragraph:86 para_id:00000056 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:89 para_id:00000059 style:Normal -->
Maya stood before the ornate mirror in her chambers, the <!-- comment-start:0 -->faint glow of the enchanted sconces cast a warm light over her figure<!-- comment-end:0 --><!-- comment-ref:0 -->, illuminating the cascade of her midnight-dark hair, pulled up in graceful simplicity. One large curl, like a gleaming obsidian ringlet, tumbled free, spilling over her shoulder to catch the light with its soft, lustrous sheen. Her golden eyes seemed to shimmer with quiet curiosity as she gazed upon her reflection. The dress she wore was a masterpiece of craftsmanship: midnight blue silk embroidered with threads of silver that shimmered like starlight. Beneath its beauty, it concealed protective enchantments woven into the fabric, offering defense without sacrificing elegance. The design allowed for freedom of movement—a necessity, given Maya’s magical abilities and the responsibilities of her station.

<!-- paragraph:91 para_id:0000005B style:Normal -->
Erynn moved deftly behind her, adjusting the intricate folds of her gown. “Stand straight, my lady,” Erynn said gently as she tugged at the laces of the gown. “The council will see every detail.”

<!-- paragraph:93 para_id:0000005D style:Normal -->
Maya’s lips quirked into a faint smile. “Is that your way of saying I should stop slouching?”

<!-- paragraph:95 para_id:0000005F style:Normal -->
“I would never presume,” Erynn replied with a mock-seriousness that drew a quiet laugh from Maya.

<!-- paragraph:97 para_id:00000061 style:Normal -->
Behind them, the heavy oak door creaked open, and Niall entered. His presence filled the room—not through intimidation but through the sheer weight of his authority. He carried himself with the quiet confidence of a man who had weathered countless storms. His robes, embroidered with lightning motifs, were a stark contrast to the subdued refinement of Maya’s attire.

<!-- paragraph:99 para_id:00000063 style:Normal -->
“You look radiant,” he said, his voice warm and low. “I am so proud of how much you've grown up these past few years.”

<!-- paragraph:101 para_id:00000065 style:Normal -->
Maya’s chest tightened, a mixture of gratitude and longing. “Thank you,” she replied softly, meeting his gaze in the mirror. Erynn stepped back, giving Niall space to approach.

<!-- paragraph:103 para_id:00000067 style:Normal -->
He placed a hand on Maya’s shoulder, his touch grounding. “The High Clade Council is not easily impressed, but you have everything you need to stand tall before the<!-- comment-start:1 --><!-- comment-start:2 -->m. <!-- comment-end:1 --><!-- comment-ref:1 --><!-- comment-end:2 --><!-- comment-ref:2 -->Remember that.”

<!-- paragraph:105 para_id:00000069 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, steadying herself with a breath. “I won’t let you down.”

<!-- paragraph:107 para_id:0000006B style:Normal -->
Before Niall could respond, the door creaked again. A young runner entered, breathless and pale, his cloak dusted with ash. He approached Niall, leaning in to whisper hurried words.

<!-- paragraph:109 para_id:0000006D style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression darkened instantly, the warmth in his eyes replaced by sharp focus. He straightened and turned to Maya, his hand dropping from her shoulder. “I have to go,” he said briskly. “Stay here, Maya. Do not leave this room.”

<!-- paragraph:111 para_id:0000006F style:Normal -->
Without another word, Niall strode from the room, the runner close on his heels.

<!-- paragraph:114 para_id:00000072 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:116 para_id:00000074 style:Normal -->
Davian crouched low in the shadowed alley, the cold bite of the stone wall pressing against his back. Around him, the other members of the Twilight Concord waited in perfect silence, cloaked figures nestled into the dark like fragments of night itself. The only movement came from the flickering lanterns carried by soldiers in unmarked armor, marching briskly down the street.

<!-- paragraph:117 para_id:00000075 style:Normal -->
Jalen, his father, knelt beside him. Eyes sharp, posture coiled, one hand resting lightly on the hilt of his dagger. His fingers tapped rhythmically against the leather grip—not out of nerves, but something older. Habit. Thought. Calculation.

<!-- paragraph:118 para_id:00000076 style:Normal -->
Davian muttered, too low for the soldiers to hear, “So they’re really doing it. An attack on the Rezins… can’t believe the High Clades are this stupid.”

<!-- paragraph:119 para_id:00000077 style:Normal -->
Jalen tapped his arm sharply. Then, with clipped precision, signed: Quiet. Too many ears. Mask on. Use your hands.

<!-- paragraph:120 para_id:00000078 style:Normal -->
Davian grimaced but pulled his mask from his belt. It was matte black, like all Concord gear—but across the front, etched into the surface, were five long, bright scratches. Deliberate. Shallow, but impossible to miss. One for each magus he’d killed.

<!-- paragraph:121 para_id:00000079 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s eyes narrowed behind his own mask.

<!-- paragraph:122 para_id:0000007A style:Normal -->
I told you not to mark it, he signed, tight and fast. You want them singling you out?

<!-- paragraph:123 para_id:0000007B style:Normal -->
Davian’s fingers answered without hesitation. If they’re looking at me, they’re not looking at anyone else.

<!-- paragraph:124 para_id:0000007C style:Normal -->
No, Jalen signed. You want to be seen. That’s different.

<!-- paragraph:125 para_id:0000007D style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t respond. The mask settled into place. Mouth closed. Voice gone.

<!-- paragraph:126 para_id:0000007E style:Normal -->
Jalen watched him a beat longer, then turned back toward the street. The soldiers passed, boots pounding like war drums down the alley’s edge.

<!-- paragraph:127 para_id:0000007F style:Normal -->
Still feels reckless, Davian signed. High Clades turning on each other? Madness.

<!-- paragraph:128 para_id:00000080 style:Normal -->
It’s tradition, Jalen replied, his hands steady. Every generation, the same rot. They’re too busy bleeding each other dry to see what’s coming.

<!-- paragraph:129 para_id:00000081 style:Normal -->
Davian tilted his head toward the soft glow rising beyond the ridge. The sword at his hip pulsed faintly, its zän reacting to the magic beneath his skin. This plan better be airtight.

<!-- paragraph:130 para_id:00000082 style:Normal -->
It is. Jalen’s response was immediate. And the timing’s perfect.

<!-- paragraph:131 para_id:00000083 style:Normal -->
A low boom echoed in the distance, followed by the sharp, unmistakable clash of steel. Jalen straightened slightly. That’s our cue. Stay close.

<!-- paragraph:132 para_id:00000084 style:Normal -->
Davian rose in one smooth motion. The rest of the Concord moved with him—silent shadows peeling from the alley walls, converging on the low perimeter of the manor. Not a word spoken.

<!-- paragraph:133 para_id:00000085 style:Normal -->
He climbed the wall first, fingers locking into the grooves like they were carved for his hands. The stone was slick with cold, but his footing never faltered. One by one, the others followed, a blur of motion and discipline.

<!-- paragraph:134 para_id:00000086 style:Normal -->
On the far side, the manor loomed like a mausoleum—dark windows staring outward, still and watchful. Davian glanced back at Jalen.

<!-- paragraph:135 para_id:00000087 style:Normal -->
The nod was small. Final.

<!-- paragraph:136 para_id:00000088 style:Normal -->
There was no turning back.

<!-- paragraph:137 para_id:00000089 style:Normal -->
The air whispered past Davian’s skin as he turned forward, breath steady, heart sharp, and led them into the dark.

<!-- paragraph:139 para_id:0000008B style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:141 para_id:0000008D style:Normal -->
Maya paced the length of her chamber, her skirts swishing softly with each step. Her gaze flicked toward the door yet again, her father’s abrupt departure gnawing at the edges of her focus. *Clade business, probably. Nothing I need to concern myself with,* she thought, though the pit in her stomach didn’t agree.

<!-- paragraph:143 para_id:0000008F style:Normal -->
“Do you think something’s gone wrong?” Erynn asked, leaning casually against the edge of a vanity.

<!-- paragraph:145 para_id:00000091 style:Normal -->
Maya gave her a sharp look. “You’re supposed to be calming me, not stressing me out.”

<!-- paragraph:147 para_id:00000093 style:Normal -->
Erynn smirked, brushing a strand of light hair out of her eyes. “Fine. Then let’s focus on what’s important—your very impressive dress. If anyone at the Presentation can tear their eyes away from you long enough to notice your magic, it’ll be a miracle.”

<!-- paragraph:149 para_id:00000095 style:Normal -->
“You’re insufferable,” Maya muttered, but she felt a small smile tug at the corner of her lips.

<!-- paragraph:151 para_id:00000097 style:Normal -->
Erynn grinned unabashedly and flopped onto the nearby settee, smoothing her own skirts in mock elegance. “Seriously though, Maya, you’re going to be fine. You’ve been working toward this for years. You’ve memorized every speech, mastered every ward. You could recite the lineage of every High Clade in your sleep. You’re ready.”

<!-- paragraph:153 para_id:00000099 style:Normal -->
“I don’t feel ready,” Maya admitted, sinking into a chair. She toyed with her dress, the protective enchantments woven into the fabric humming faintly against her skin.

<!-- paragraph:155 para_id:0000009B style:Normal -->
Erynn leaned forward, resting her chin in her hands. “Nervous about the High Clade Council?”

<!-- paragraph:157 para_id:0000009D style:Normal -->
“Wouldn’t you be?” Maya asked, looking at her reflection in the mirror. “This is the first time I’ve been allowed outside the citadel. My identity, my abilities—everything’s been hidden, protected. And now, suddenly, I’m on display for every High Clade in the city.”

<!-- paragraph:159 para_id:0000009F style:Normal -->
Erynn tilted her head thoughtfully. “I’d be nervous. But I’d also be excited. You’re finally getting to show the world what you’re made of. They’ve been keeping you under wraps because you’re valuable, Maya. This is your chance to prove it.”

<!-- paragraph:161 para_id:000000A1 style:Normal -->
“It’s not that simple,” Maya said softly. “The Presentation isn’t just for me. It’s for the family. It’s about showing the Council that the Rezins are strong without revealing so much that we give away tactical advantages. If I slip up... if I make the wrong impression...” She trailed off, the weight of her thoughts pressing heavily on her.

<!-- paragraph:163 para_id:000000A3 style:Normal -->
Erynn stood and crossed the room, sitting beside her. “You’re not going to slip up,” she said firmly. “You’re a Rezin. You’ve spent your whole life preparing for this. And I know how much it matters, to you and to your father. But you’re ready, Maya. Even if you don’t feel it, you are.”

<!-- paragraph:165 para_id:000000A5 style:Normal -->
Maya sighed, but Erynn’s confidence was infectious. She glanced at her friend, who was watching her with an unshakable grin. “You’re annoying, you know that?”

<!-- paragraph:167 para_id:000000A7 style:Normal -->
“And yet, you keep me around,” Erynn teased, nudging her with her elbow.

<!-- paragraph:169 para_id:000000A9 style:Normal -->
Maya laughed softly, and for the first time that morning, the knot in her stomach eased. She straightened, “You’re right. I can do this.”

<!-- paragraph:171 para_id:000000AB style:Normal -->
Erynn grinned. “Things would be a lot easier if you just remembered I'm always right.”

<!-- paragraph:174 para_id:000000AE style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:177 para_id:000000B1 style:Normal -->
The manor loomed ahead, its cold walls bathed in the eerie glow of flickering wards, casting restless shadows that danced across the cobblestone courtyard. Faint outlines of guards shifted in and out of view, their positions betrayed by the occasional glint of steel and the murmur of hushed voices. Davian crouched low alongside his father and the rest of the group, his pulse steady but his focus razor-sharp. Now, Jalen signed.

<!-- paragraph:179 para_id:000000B3 style:Normal -->
The first guard never saw the bolt coming. Davian pulled the trigger of his crossbow with precision, the weapon spitting silent death into the man’s throat. The others were just as efficient, felling sentries with an ease born of practice.

<!-- paragraph:180 para_id:000000B4 style:Normal -->
As Davian reloaded, a soft thud marked another guard's body crumpling to the ground nearby. One of the crew glanced over and signed, sharp and contemptuous: Anyone who works for the Low Clades deserves this. Trash.

<!-- paragraph:181 para_id:000000B5 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t look up. His fingers moved in reply, crisp and cold. Trash is easier to clean up.”

<!-- paragraph:182 para_id:000000B6 style:Normal -->
They reached the edge of the garden, the manor house looming ahead, its windows glinting faintly in the moonlight. The air carried an unnatural tension, humming with latent energy. As Jalen gestured toward the main door, Tarek stepped forward, and Davian moved close behind, the others fanning out silently into the shadows.

<!-- paragraph:184 para_id:000000B8 style:Normal -->
Tarek reached for the door handle, but Davian felt it—a subtle, unnatural warping in the air, as if the world itself had drawn a sharp, shallow breath. The sensation was hard to place, but Davian knew it well, a warning only those with hereditary magic could sense.

<!-- paragraph:186 para_id:000000BA style:Normal -->
“Get back!” he barked, lunging forward.

<!-- paragraph:188 para_id:000000BC style:Normal -->
The door erupted outward with a deafening crack, and a brilliant, deadly spell shot out, slicing through the air toward Tarek with terrifying speed. Davian shoved him aside, his hand already closing around the hilt of his sword. The blade hissed as it left its sheath, the zän etched into its surface flaring to life in a burst of eerie light.

<!-- paragraph:190 para_id:000000BE style:Normal -->
Without hesitation, Davian arced the sword through the air in a single, precise slash, meeting the energy head-on. The spell shattered—violently. The backlash hit like a concussive wave, sharp with static and heat.

<!-- paragraph:191 para_id:000000BF style:Normal -->
Davian’s mask split apart instantly, shattering down the center with a metallic crack. Fragments scattered across the ground as one jagged piece sliced his cheek, drawing a thin line of blood that trailed down toward his jaw.

<!-- paragraph:192 para_id:000000C0 style:Normal -->
He staggered but didn’t fall. His breath was ragged, eyes locked forward.

<!-- paragraph:193 para_id:000000C1 style:Normal -->
The remnants of the mask hung for a moment from the strap at his temple—then fell, useless, to the ground. The sword lowered slightly in his hand.  The glow of its zän faded, leaving the name they spelled—Antithesis—briefly visible before disappearing entirely.

<!-- paragraph:194 para_id:000000C2 style:Normal -->
The air crackled with residual magic, and a figure emerged into the doorway, his silhouette stark against the faint, glowing light of his facial sigil. The mark, etched in radiant lines on his right cheek and curling subtly around his eye, pulsed with an otherworldly rhythm, a silent testament to the strain of hereditary magic he wielded. His robes - and bare arms - marked him as a magus, and his paradoxical presence struck Davian immediately—calm yet brimming with deadly confidence. The man’s glowing sigil intensified as his gaze locked onto Davian, and a faint smile curled his lips.

<!-- paragraph:196 para_id:000000C4 style:Normal -->
“So it’s really you,” the man said, his tone almost conversational. “The Shade.”

<!-- paragraph:198 para_id:000000C6 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:200 para_id:000000C8 style:Normal -->
The sound of raised voices pulled Maya’s attention from her pacing. She turned toward the door, her brow furrowing as it opened abruptly to reveal a commotion outside. A Rezin soldier, his face streaked with grime and desperation, was struggling against the firm grip of her personal guards.

<!-- paragraph:202 para_id:000000CA style:Normal -->
"Let me through!" the soldier insisted, his voice cracking under the strain. “I need to speak with Lady Maya!”

<!-- paragraph:204 para_id:000000CC style:Normal -->
Erynn gasped softly, rising from her seat, but Maya stepped forward, her tone calm and commanding. “Let him go.”

<!-- paragraph:206 para_id:000000CE style:Normal -->
The guards exchanged uncertain glances but obeyed, releasing the soldier, who staggered forward. He dropped to one knee out of respect, though his gaze darted up nervously, as if he were unsure if he should even be speaking to her.

<!-- paragraph:208 para_id:000000D0 style:Normal -->
“What’s happened?” Maya asked, her voice steady but tinged with curiosity. “Why would you take such a risk to come here?”

<!-- paragraph:210 para_id:000000D2 style:Normal -->
The soldier swallowed hard, his hands clenching at his sides. “Lady Maya… we’re taking heavy casualties. They—they attacked on both sides of the citadel at once. Lord Niall was drawn away to defend the eastern wall, but the western defenses—” He stopped, his voice faltering. “We’re… we’re losing ground.”

<!-- paragraph:212 para_id:000000D4 style:Normal -->
Maya could see the tension in his face, the way he avoided meeting her eyes too long. He knew he wasn’t supposed to be here. But his desperation was written in every line of his posture, and it pierced through her usual calm.

<!-- paragraph:214 para_id:000000D6 style:Normal -->
Erynn stepped beside her, whispering urgently. “You’re supposed to stay here. Your Presentation is only hours away—”

<!-- paragraph:216 para_id:000000D8 style:Normal -->
Maya cut her off with a sharp glance. “What’s the point of a Presentation if I can’t protect the people my family is responsible for?” She turned back to the soldier, her voice firm. “Take me there.”

<!-- paragraph:218 para_id:000000DA style:Normal -->
Her personal guards stiffened but said nothing. Maya understood their hesitation; as of today, she was becoming an official, public member of the Rezin family. They didn’t know the extent of her authority yet, nor were they willing to challenge it outright. She carried herself as though the matter were already settled, and with her head held high, she followed the soldier out of the room.

<!-- paragraph:220 para_id:000000DC style:Normal -->
As they made their way through the winding corridors, Maya felt a chill crawl up her spine. The muted roar of battle grew louder as they neared the citadel walls. When they finally emerged into the open air, what she saw took her breath away.

<!-- paragraph:222 para_id:000000DE style:Normal -->
The western defenses were barely holding. Soldiers in Rezin armor fought desperately, locked in combat with an unrelenting tide of attackers. The fighting extended all the way to the base of the wall.

<!-- paragraph:224 para_id:000000E0 style:Normal -->
She forced herself to focus, summoning the training she had honed for years. Her hand rose instinctively, and a shimmering Shroud flickered to life around her—a protective barrier of refracted energy. She felt its familiar weight settle over her like a second skin, a tangible reminder of her control.

<!-- paragraph:226 para_id:000000E2 style:Normal -->
From her vantage point on the high terrace overlooking the ramparts, Maya could see the chaotic ebb and flow of the battle. She descended the stone steps with measured purpose, each step cooling the air around her. Archers stationed along the wall shivered, their breath fogging in the sudden chill as she passed. Ice crept faintly along the edges of the arrow slits, unnoticed in the chaos of battle.

<!-- paragraph:228 para_id:000000E4 style:Normal -->
The first arrow struck her Shroud, freezing instantly and shattering into glittering fragments. Maya stepped forward, her breath puffing in white clouds as the temperature plummeted around her. The ground beneath her feet cracked sharply, ice spreading in jagged veins through the earth, while the air itself seemed to hiss and grow heavier with each step.

<!-- paragraph:230 para_id:000000E6 style:Normal -->
Her magic reached out, weaving through the unseen fabric of reality. Heat drained from the space around her, the energy coalescing in her grasp like the drawn string of a bow. Frost rimed her hair and lashes.

<!-- paragraph:232 para_id:000000E8 style:Normal -->
Arrows came faster now, striking her Shroud one after another, each freezing mid-flight before shattering harmlessly. A soldier nearby gasped, his armor beginning to frost over, and stumbled backward. Maya didn’t spare him a glance, her focus locked on the chaos ahead. The frozen ground hissed, faint tendrils of steam rising as her magic gathered force, heat and cold balanced on the edge of something cataclysmic.

<!-- paragraph:234 para_id:000000EA style:Normal -->
"You should probably stand back," Maya said, her voice steady despite the storm she carried. Her eyes never left the battle ahead as the soldier obeyed, retreating with wide-eyed awe.

<!-- paragraph:235 para_id:000000EB style:Normal -->
Maya stepped forward, her gaze fixed on the chaos below. Enemy soldiers swarmed at the base of the wall, their <!-- comment-start:3 -->torches and armor gleaming like fireflies in the night.<!-- comment-end:3 --><!-- comment-ref:3 --> She exhaled slowly, opening her mind to the threads of reality. They spread out before her in a dizzying array, like an infinite weave of potential pathways. Her focus sharpened as she began to trace patterns in the threads, carving deliberate channels for the energy to flow. It was a delicate balance, like drawing rivulets in the sand for water to follow—a single misstep could send it all collapsing.

<!-- paragraph:236 para_id:000000EC style:Normal -->
The first pathway came easily: she directed the cold outward, pulling heat from the stones beneath her feet and the air around her. Frost spiraled outward in jagged veins, and an icy shield began to form, glinting faintly in the torchlight. Her facial sigil flared to life across her right cheek, its intricate geometry casting sharp, pale light. The design was as unique as her bloodline, its complex, interlocking lines the unmistakable sign of a true magus. She expanded the shield steadily, directing the cold to encase the soldiers nearby.

<!-- paragraph:237 para_id:000000ED style:Normal -->
A gasp of pain snapped her focus, and she turned to see a soldier collapse, an arrow protruding from his chest. Her breath caught, but only for a moment. She forced herself to push the grief away. She had to protect the others. Gritting her teeth, she expanded her focus, carving broader pathways for the cold to flow. The icy barrier stretched further, covering more of the ramparts and the soldiers huddled behind her protection.

<!-- paragraph:238 para_id:000000EE style:Normal -->
But the more she pulled, the more the displaced heat built within her. It seared through her like a coiled snake, writhing and hissing with nowhere to go. Her body trembled under the strain, her mind straining to maintain control of the delicate balance. Every scrap of heat she drew away had to go somewhere, and the channels she’d carved were beginning to fray under the pressure. Her breathing quickened, shallow and uneven, her heart pounding as though it might burst. She clenched her teeth, struggling to contain the chaotic storm within her, but the heat refused to yield. It coiled tighter and tighter, a pressure she could not release, as anger and desperation surged like a tide. She would not let her home be conquered. Maya staggered, her knees nearly buckling. She couldn’t hold it all. The threads of reality quivered under her touch, threatening to unravel. Then she made her choice.

<!-- paragraph:239 para_id:000000EF style:Normal -->
She carved one last pathway in her mind, her focus narrowing to the mass of enemy soldiers swarming at the base of the wall. Every ounce of heat she had displaced surged toward them, funneled through the thread with devastating precision. The energy snapped free of her control, leaving her gasping as the storm she had held finally escaped.

<!-- paragraph:240 para_id:000000F0 style:Normal -->
The world didn’t wait for her to recover. The searing heat lanced downward, and for the briefest moment, Maya caught the first glimmers of chaos in the ranks of the enemy below. Then everything went white.

<!-- paragraph:242 para_id:000000F2 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:245 para_id:000000F5 style:Normal -->
The magus stepped out onto the grounds with deliberate ease, his confidence radiating like the shimmer of his Shroud, which cloaked him in a palpable barrier of power. Davian’s grip on the hilt of Antithesis tightened as he measured the man, noting the careful precision in his movements and the way he carried himself with the arrogance of someone who thought he’d already won.

<!-- paragraph:247 para_id:000000F7 style:Normal -->
“How predictable,” the magus said, his voice dripping with disdain as his gaze swept over Davian with a sneer. “The infamous Shade is at it again, but I’m not like the other mages you assassinated in the cover of darkness. I knew you were coming. ”

<!-- paragraph:249 para_id:000000F9 style:Normal -->
The words were aimed to unnerve, but Davian didn’t flinch. His crew began to fan out, spreading into the shadows around the magus, their movements careful and deliberate. The magus noticed but didn’t react, clearly confident in his ability to handle them all.

<!-- paragraph:251 para_id:000000FB style:Normal -->
Jalen, standing just behind Davian, raised his arm slightly, drawing his attention. The tattoos etched into his forearm writhed subtly, moving like shadows alive beneath his skin. Take it, he signed to Davian.

<!-- paragraph:253 para_id:000000FD style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head, signing, You should know better. I don’t need the help.

<!-- paragraph:255 para_id:000000FF style:Normal -->
Jalen’s arm dropped, his expression unreadable, but Davian could feel the tension in the air between them. Then, without warning, Davian moved. In a blur of motion, he drew a throwing knife from his belt and sent it spinning toward the magus. The blade struck the Shroud and ricocheted harmlessly to the ground, the magus’s shimmering barrier absorbing the impact with a faint ripple.

<!-- paragraph:257 para_id:00000101 style:Normal -->
The magus laughed, his confidence unshaken, and lifted a hand in a mocking beckon. “Come, then. Show me why they whisper your name in fear.”

<!-- paragraph:259 para_id:00000103 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t respond. Instead, he sprinted forward, his boots crunching on the gravel-covered ground.

<!-- paragraph:261 para_id:00000105 style:Normal -->
Before he could reach the magus, a thunderous explosion erupted in the distance, sending tremors through the earth. The ground shook violently beneath them, forcing both Davian and the magus to stagger and regain their footing. Dust fell in soft clouds from the manor’s eaves, and the faint echo of collapsing stone rolled through the air.

<!-- paragraph:263 para_id:00000107 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s voice cut sharply through the momentary pause. “Whatever that was, it means our time’s short. Finish this.”

<!-- paragraph:265 para_id:00000109 style:Normal -->
Davian steadied himself, then surged forward once more, the shock of the explosion already forgotten as his focus honed in on the magus.

<!-- paragraph:268 para_id:0000010C style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:271 para_id:0000010F style:Normal -->
The bodies of enemy soldiers pinwheeled through the air, hurled<!-- comment-start:4 --> like ragdolls <!-- comment-end:4 --><!-- comment-ref:4 -->by the explosion of heat Maya had unleashed within the massed troops at the base of the wall. The force of it sent plumes of dust and shattered debris spiraling into the air, and for a moment, all sound seemed to vanish in the aftermath of the detonation. Maya froze, her breath catching as she took in the horrifying scene—the twisted forms, the charred remains.

<!-- paragraph:273 para_id:00000111 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:5 -->Her stomach churned. She had done this.<!-- comment-end:5 --><!-- comment-ref:5 --> The same power she’d spent years learning to control had become an uncontrollable wave of destruction. <!-- comment-start:6 -->For a moment, her grip on the threads of reality faltered, h<!-- comment-end:6 --><!-- comment-ref:6 -->er focus slipping as guilt and revulsion flooded in.

<!-- paragraph:275 para_id:00000113 style:Normal -->
Then her eyes darted to the <!-- comment-start:7 -->Rezin <!-- comment-end:7 --><!-- comment-ref:7 -->soldiers scattered along the battlements, their expressions a mixture of awe and fear. They were too close. <!-- comment-start:8 -->Another uncontrolled surge could annihilate the very people she was trying to protect. Maya clenched her fists, the threads of reality trembling under her grip, and forced<!-- comment-end:8 --><!-- comment-ref:8 --> herself to push the emotion down. There was no room for <!-- comment-start:9 -->hesitation<!-- comment-end:9 --><!-- comment-ref:9 -->—not here, not now.

<!-- paragraph:277 para_id:00000115 style:Normal -->
Drawing the heat in again, she felt the air around her grow colder still. Ice crept further along the ground, jagged crystals spreading like veins of frost under her boots. Her breath escaped in white clouds, the chill sharp in her lungs. As energy surged within her, <!-- comment-start:10 -->she felt her facial sigil <!-- comment-end:10 --><!-- comment-ref:10 -->glowing ever brighter, casting a faint light that shimmered against the frosted ground. Her focus narrowed as she channeled the energy into a precise conduit. A single ray of fire burst from her outstretched hand, a brilliant, condensed line of flame that roared with intensity.

<!-- paragraph:279 para_id:00000117 style:Normal -->
She swept the beam through the enemy ranks, cutting throug<!-- comment-start:11 -->h s<!-- comment-end:11 --><!-- comment-ref:11 -->oldiers like a scythe through wheat. The fire consumed them in an instant, leaving nothing but ash drifting in the air. Maya’s heart twisted at the sight, her stomach turning as she saw faces caught in their last moments of terror before vanishing into oblivion.

<!-- paragraph:281 para_id:00000119 style:Normal -->
Emotions surged within her—grief, anger, doubt—all churning together like a storm. The beam in her hand wavered, flaring brighter as her focus falter<!-- comment-start:12 -->ed. <!-- comment-end:12 --><!-- comment-ref:12 -->It swept upward, out of control, and slashed through the top turret of a nearby manor. Stone crumbled as the turret sheared cleanly from the structure, the fiery cut leaving molten edges. For a moment, the massive piece of stone hung suspended in the air, before gravity claimed it, sending it plummeting toward the ground below.

<!-- paragraph:284 para_id:0000011C style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:287 para_id:0000011F style:Normal -->
Davian lunged forward, Antithesis arcing toward the magus with a precision born of countless hours of training. The blade struck the Shroud, skimming off its surface with a burst of sparks that shimmered in the dark air. The magus barely moved, a self-satisfied smirk curling his lips as he extended his hand, unleashing lines of force that tore through the earth like invisible blades. Trees splintered and crashed to the ground as the deadly magic carved furrows through the dirt.

<!-- paragraph:289 para_id:00000121 style:Normal -->
“You can’t even scratch me,” the magus sneered, his voice dripping with contempt as his facial sigil gleamed ominously—a testament to his well-bred magical lineage. “Is this the best your mongrel line can manage?”

<!-- paragraph:291 para_id:00000123 style:Normal -->
Davian ignored the taunt, his focus unyielding as he pressed the attack. His sword hammered against the Shroud in relentless arcs, each strike lighting up the barrier with flashes of sparks. Yet, no matter how hard or fast he moved, the magus remained untouched, his laughter echoing through the chaos as he wantonly fired destructive blasts, each strike devastating the manor grounds further.

<!-- paragraph:293 para_id:00000125 style:Normal -->
“You’re just proof of why the Clades must rule,” the magus went on, voice rising with each spell. “Power is inherited. Discipline is bred. You think strength can be stolen or mimicked, but we refine it—generation after generation. That’s why we rule. That’s why you’ll die here.”

<!-- paragraph:295 para_id:00000127 style:Normal -->
“You fight like a dog chasing shadows,” he added, conjuring another line of force that narrowly missed Davian, slicing a nearby tree cleanly in half. The air reeked of scorched wood and soot, the battlefield a warzone of ruin and ash.

<!-- paragraph:297 para_id:00000129 style:Normal -->
Before Davian could close the gap again, a deafening whoosh of flame roared through the air, followed by the gut-wrenching sound of stone rending apart. Both men instinctively leapt back as a fiery beam sliced through the turret of the manor house. The molten edges of the stone structure glowed like embers as it crumbled, and then, with a thunderous crash, the turret slammed into the ground between them. The impact sent shockwaves through the earth, scattering debris and forcing Davian to steady himself.

<!-- paragraph:299 para_id:0000012B style:Normal -->
“That’s your refinement?” Davian snapped, gesturing to the smoking remains of the turret. “Slaughter cloaked in bloodlines and etiquette. Doesn’t matter if it’s fire or force—everything you touch ends up ash.”

<!-- paragraph:301 para_id:0000012D style:Normal -->
The magus’s sneer faltered, but only for a moment. Davian surged forward, sprinting through the haze of dust and heat, closing the distance between them. The magus extended both hands, conjuring a powerful line of force and firing it directly at Davian. The attack carved the air with deadly precision, but Davian was faster. He brought his sword up, gripping it with both hands as he swung it down in a decisive arc. The glowing zän on the blade flared as it struck the line of force, slicing through it with a burst of crackling energy. The magic dissipated in a flash, and Davian’s momentum carried the sword straight into the magus’s Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:303 para_id:0000012F style:Normal -->
The impact sent a ripple through the shimmering barrier, the blade grinding against it as sparks erupted in violent bursts. The magus’s composure wavered for the first time, his confidence dimming as Davian bore down, his face twisted with rage.

<!-- paragraph:305 para_id:00000131 style:Normal -->
“You don’t care who burns,” Davian spat, his voice shaking with fury as he forced the blade deeper into the Shroud. “As long as it’s not one of you!”

<!-- paragraph:307 para_id:00000133 style:Normal -->
The magus’s sigil flared brighter, a desperate attempt to reinforce his Shroud. “This—this is a futile effort,” he stammered, but his words were drowned out as a sigil blazed to life on Davian’s own face, burning with an intensity that cast harsh shadows across his features. The magus’s bravado gave way to fear, his eyes wide as he began to stammer. “Wait—”

<!-- paragraph:309 para_id:00000135 style:Normal -->
Davian roared, his voice a guttural sound of unrestrained fury as the sigil on his face pulsed in unison with the glowing zän on Antithesis. The blade, now burning like molten steel, surged forward. The Shroud shuddered, flickered—and then shattered with a resounding crack. The magus’s words turned into a gurgled gasp as Antithesis cleaved through his neck, the glowing blade cutting cleanly through flesh and bone.

<!-- paragraph:311 para_id:00000137 style:Normal -->
The magus crumpled to the ground, his lifeless body collapsing in a heap as the light of his sigil dimmed to nothing. Davian stood over him, chest heaving, the glow of his own sigil fading as the adrenaline ebbed. For a moment, the battlefield was silent, save for the faint crackling of lingering flames and the soft thud of falling debris.

<!-- paragraph:313 para_id:00000139 style:Normal -->
Suddenly, another beam of fire arced through the walls of the manor house, slicing cleanly through stone and wood. The ground trembled as debris rained down, forcing Davian, Jalen, and the rest of their crew to duck for cover. The beam’s heat lingered in the air, the acrid smell of scorched stone filling their noses.

<!-- paragraph:315 para_id:0000013B style:Normal -->
Jalen signed, Documents, now! Then leave, and sprinted toward the open door of the manor,

<!-- paragraph:317 para_id:0000013D style:Normal -->
Davian moved on instinct, following the others into the manor’s shattered entryway. The interior was in chaos, debris scattered from the earlier explosion, but their target was clear—a sturdy, rune-marked case lying amidst the wreckage. One of the crew snatched it up, and they turned to leave, their movements quick and efficient.

<!-- paragraph:319 para_id:0000013F style:Normal -->
They dashed back into the street, the sounds of distant battle and chaos filling the air. As the crew darted toward the alley, Davian slowed, something drawing his attention. He glanced up at the towering walls of the Rezin citadel, his eyes catching on a figure standing atop the ramparts.

<!-- paragraph:321 para_id:00000141 style:Normal -->
A woman.

<!-- paragraph:323 para_id:00000143 style:Normal -->
She stood amidst the chaos, beams of fire streaming from her outstretched hands, each ray searing through the enemy ranks below. The flames lit her silhouette in an almost otherworldly glow, her facial sigil burning brightly against the dark sky.

<!-- paragraph:325 para_id:00000145 style:Normal -->
For a moment, Davian simply stared. Then, Jalen hand gripped his shoulder, snapping him out of his trance. Move! Jalen signed.

<!-- paragraph:327 para_id:00000147 style:Normal -->
Shaking his head, Davian tore his gaze away and sprinted into the shadows.

<!-- paragraph:329 para_id:00000149 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:331 para_id:0000014B style:Normal -->
The fire leapt from Maya’s hands in wild, erratic bursts, each searing beam tearing through the enemy lines. Soldiers screamed and scattered, formation breaking as bodies dropped and armor blackened. Her Shroud rippled around her, gleaming with raw force—an untouchable shield.

<!-- paragraph:332 para_id:0000014C style:Normal -->
But as the soldiers faltered, her attacks began to slow. The chaos before her was too much—bodies crumpled in flame, the scent of burning flesh curling into her lungs. Her breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:333 para_id:0000014D style:Normal -->
How many? Her hands trembled. How many lives just—gone?

<!-- paragraph:334 para_id:0000014E style:Normal -->
She lowered her arms as the enemy began to retreat, boots thudding against packed earth as they withdrew. They’d broken. She let them go.

<!-- paragraph:335 para_id:0000014F style:Normal -->
Then, in an instant, the battlefield lit up with a hundred threads of light. Streaks of lightning, impossibly thin and devastatingly fast, tore through t<!-- comment-start:13 -->he ene<!-- comment-end:13 --><!-- comment-ref:13 -->my ranks, slicing through armor and flesh alike. Soldiers fell in droves, their bodies crumpling where they stood. Maya staggered back and turned, her breath catching at the sight.

<!-- paragraph:337 para_id:00000151 style:Normal -->
Niall Rezin strode forward, his hands raised high, directing the storm with precision. The sigil on his face glowed brightly as cascades of lightning descended from the heavens, each bolt splitting into finer threads that mowed down the soldiers with merciless efficiency. The enemy forces broke, the last scraps of order dissolving into terror. Soldiers threw down their weapons and fled, but the lightning pursued them, cutting them down as they ran.

<!-- paragraph:339 para_id:00000153 style:Normal -->
"Stop!" Maya screamed, her voice breaking through the din. "They're retreating!" But Niall didn’t stop. His pace was unbroken, his expression unreadable as he continued to wield his magic, striking down every fleeing figure in his path.

<!-- paragraph:341 para_id:00000155 style:Normal -->
Maya’s heart pounded as she sprinted after him, grabbing his arm with both hands. "Father, stop!" she yelled, pulling him to a halt.

<!-- paragraph:343 para_id:00000157 style:Normal -->
Niall turned to her, but the face she saw wasn’t her father’s. It was a mask of emptiness, his eyes cold and distant, as if he didn’t even recognize her. He stared at her for a long, chilling moment before his lips parted. “Alara?” he murmured, his voice barely audible.

<!-- paragraph:345 para_id:00000159 style:Normal -->
The name hung in the air like a ghost, and for a moment Maya felt like she wasn’t even there. Then, as if waking from a dream, Niall blinked, his features softening. Recognition returned to his eyes, and his hand lowered, cutting off the storm mid-strike.

<!-- paragraph:346 para_id:0000015A style:Normal -->
The battlefield was silent now, save for the distant cries of the wounded and dying. Maya stared at her father, her chest tight with unspoken words.

<!-- paragraph:348 para_id:0000015C style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:14 --><!-- comment-start:15 --><!-- comment-start:16 -->Niall straightened, his expression shifting to something more composed. Soldiers on the walls were watching, their eyes filled with awe and apprehension. “You did well defending the wall,” he said, his voice calm but with a sharp undertone. “Now return to your chambers and prepare for your Presentation.”<!-- comment-end:14 --><!-- comment-ref:14 --><!-- comment-end:15 --><!-- comment-ref:15 --><!-- comment-end:16 --><!-- comment-ref:16 -->

<!-- paragraph:350 para_id:0000015E style:Normal -->
It was clear from his expression that the matter wasn’t over. The subtle tightening of his jaw and the flicker in his eyes promised a reckoning later. But Maya nodded, stepping back reluctantly as Niall turned away, the weight of what had just transpired settling heavily in her chest.

<!-- paragraph:354 para_id:00000162 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 1

<!-- paragraph:355 para_id:00000163 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 1

<!-- paragraph:357 para_id:00000165 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:360 para_id:00000168 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:17 -->The air was still thick with the acrid scent of charred wood and flesh. It clung to Maya’s senses, a phantom of the battle that felt more like a fever dream the further she walked from the wall. The sounds of the fight still echoed faintly in her ears—clashing steel, screams, and the roar of magic. She moved as if through water, the weight of it all pressing down on her. Each step felt distant, disconnected, as though someone else were guiding her legs while her mind remained suspended in the chaos she had left behind.

<!-- paragraph:362 para_id:0000016A style:Normal -->
The path back to her chambers was eerily quiet. Soldiers nodded respectfully as she passed, their gazes lingering on her, though their expressions were unreadable. A few murmured, “Thank you, Lady Maya,” but the words barely registered. Their voices seemed to come from far away, muffled and indistinct, like whispers through a thick fog. Maya inclined her head slightly in acknowledgment, her movements automatic, her thoughts caught in the loop of what she had done.

<!-- paragraph:364 para_id:0000016C style:Normal -->
By the time she reached the door to her chambers, the haze had begun to lift, replaced by a gnawing ache in her chest. She pressed her hand to the door and hesitated, closing her eyes for a moment before pushing it open.

<!-- paragraph:366 para_id:0000016E style:Normal -->
“Maya?” Erynn’s voice broke through the quiet as the door clicked shut behind her. The maid stood in the middle of the room, her hands clasped tightly in front of her, her brow furrowed with concern. “What happened? I heard the alarms, and then—” She stopped, her gaze scanning Maya’s face. “You look… Are you hurt?”

<!-- paragraph:368 para_id:00000170 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, the sound of her name pulling her back fully to the present. She looked at Erynn, her mind slowly catching up. “I…” Her voice faltered, and she shook her head. “No. I’m fine. Just… tired.”

<!-- paragraph:370 para_id:00000172 style:Normal -->
Erynn didn’t look convinced, but she stepped closer, her voice soft. “You don’t seem fine. Maya, please—what happened?”

<!-- paragraph:372 para_id:00000174 style:Normal -->
Maya sank into the nearest chair, the weight in her chest growing heavier. She stared at her hands, the faint tingling of residual magic still dancing across her fingertips. “The citadel was attacked,” she said finally, her voice flat. “They broke through to the walls. My father was drawn away… There was no one else to hold the line.”

<!-- paragraph:374 para_id:00000176 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s eyes widened, and she knelt beside Maya, her hands resting lightly on the arm of the chair. “You held the line? By yourself?”

<!-- paragraph:376 para_id:00000178 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, the memory rushing back in vivid flashes—the fire, the screams, the bodies. “I… I used my magic. More than I ever have. There were so many of them, Erynn. I didn’t think. I just… acted.”

<!-- paragraph:378 para_id:0000017A style:Normal -->
Erynn was quiet for a moment, her expression unreadable. Then, her hand moved to rest gently over Maya’s. “You saved them. That’s what matters. They’re alive because of you.”

<!-- paragraph:380 para_id:0000017C style:Normal -->
Maya’s throat tightened, and she looked away, unable to meet Erynn’s eyes. “But at what cost? I killed so many…”

<!-- paragraph:382 para_id:0000017E style:Normal -->
Erynn hesitated, then gave Maya’s hand a squeeze. “You did what you had to. No one else could have done it. And those soldiers—our soldiers—they’ll remember that.”

<!-- paragraph:384 para_id:00000180 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded faintly, though her thoughts remained clouded. She glanced toward the desk, the neatly stacked intelligence reports offering a welcome distraction. “I need to prepare for the council meeting,” she said, pulling her hand free and standing abruptly. “We still don’t know who sent those soldiers. We have to figure it out before tonight.”

<!-- paragraph:386 para_id:00000182 style:Normal -->
Erynn rose slowly, her worry clear but unspoken. “Of course,” she said quietly, moving to fetch the reports. “But don’t forget—you’re more than what happened out there. You’re still you, Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:388 para_id:00000184 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond. She sat at the desk, pulling the reports closer, and forced her focus to shift to the cold logic of alliances and betrayals. Yet, even as she scanned the pages, her mind wandered, the faces of the soldiers she had burned etched into her memory.

<!-- paragraph:390 para_id:00000186 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:392 para_id:00000188 style:Normal -->
The chamber door swung open with a deliberate creak, and Niall entered, his expression carved from stone. Maya’s breath caught at the sight of him; he wasn’t yelling, wasn’t raging, but the cold fury in his eyes was far worse. He closed the door with measured precision and gestured for a nearby runner to approach.

<!-- paragraph:394 para_id:0000018A style:Normal -->
“Bring me the wall commanders. Now,” he said, his voice low and calm, yet carrying the weight of an order that brooked no hesitation.

<!-- paragraph:396 para_id:0000018C style:Normal -->
The runner darted out of the room, and Niall lowered himself into a chair with an air of grim purpose. He didn’t look at Maya or Erynn, his attention fixed on the middle distance, his jaw tight. The silence that stretched was suffocating, the weight of it pressing down on the room.

<!-- paragraph:398 para_id:0000018E style:Normal -->
Minutes later, the runner returned, followed by two men in battered armor. Maya recognized them instantly—Commander Harlun, a seasoned soldier whose face bore the lines of countless campaigns, and Commander Enver, younger but with a reputation for meticulous precision. Both looked wary as they stepped inside, their gazes flickering to Niall before settling uncomfortably on the floor.

<!-- paragraph:400 para_id:00000190 style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t waste time. “Explain to me how this could happen,” he said, his voice as sharp as a blade.<!-- comment-end:17 --><!-- comment-ref:17 -->

<!-- paragraph:402 para_id:00000192 style:Normal -->
Harlun shifted, his weathered face taut with unease. “My lord, the scale of the attack—this is the largest force we’ve seen in over a decade. The coordination, the resources… No one could have anticipated a probe of this magnitude.”

<!-- paragraph:404 para_id:00000194 style:Normal -->
“No one?” Niall repeated, leaning forward slightly. The quiet intensity of his tone made both men flinch. “You are commanders. It is your purpose to anticipate. Did you think your responsibility ended at counting heads and standing on a wall?”

<!-- paragraph:406 para_id:00000196 style:Normal -->
Enver spoke up, his voice trembling slightly. “The cost to whoever financed this, my lord—it must have been massive. Unprecedented. We had no way of knowing they’d commit so much to a single assault.”

<!-- paragraph:408 para_id:00000198 style:Normal -->
Niall’s gaze bored into them, cold and unyielding. “And yet, they did. While you sat comfortably in your assumptions, dozens of our soldiers bled and died to hold the line. My daughter—” He stopped himself, the only flicker of emotion breaking through his otherwise measured words. He straightened, letting the silence speak louder than any accusation.

<!-- paragraph:410 para_id:0000019A style:Normal -->
Harlun opened his mouth to respond, but Niall raised a hand, cutting him off. “I don’t want excuses. I want answers. You will not set foot in this room again until you find evidence—concrete, actionable evidence—of who financed and coordinated this attack. Is that understood?”

<!-- paragraph:412 para_id:0000019C style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:18 -->“Yes, my lord,” Harlun and Enver said in unison, bowing deeply before retreating from the room.

<!-- paragraph:414 para_id:0000019E style:Normal -->
The door shut behind them, and the room fell silent once more. Niall exhaled slowly, his posture still rigid but his fury momentarily tempered. For the first time since entering, he turned to Maya. His expression softened, if only slightly, but the strain behind his eyes was unmistakable.<!-- comment-end:18 --><!-- comment-ref:18 -->

<!-- paragraph:416 para_id:000001A0 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:19 -->Niall turned to Erynn, his gaze firm. “Leave us,” he said simply.

<!-- paragraph:418 para_id:000001A2 style:Normal -->
Erynn hesitated, glancing at Maya as though silently asking for permission to stay. Maya gave her a faint nod, and Erynn bowed her head, retreating reluctantly. The door closed softly behind her, leaving the room heavy with silence.<!-- comment-end:19 --><!-- comment-ref:19 -->

<!-- paragraph:420 para_id:000001A4 style:Normal -->
Niall crossed to the window, his hands clasped tightly behind his back as he looked out over the citadel grounds. His posture was tense, his shoulders stiff. When he finally spoke, his voice was measured, but each word was sharp and deliberate.

<!-- paragraph:422 para_id:000001A6 style:Normal -->
“You made a mistake,” he began, the weight of his words settling over the room like a storm cloud. He didn’t turn to look at her. “Two mistakes, to be precise.”

<!-- paragraph:424 para_id:000001A8 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth to protest, but Niall raised a hand, silencing her before she could utter a word.

<!-- paragraph:426 para_id:000001AA style:Normal -->
“First,” he continued, “you have never been in a battle before. What you did out there was reckless. It is pure luck—luck—that you were able to hold your Shroud while fighting. One misstep, one moment of hesitation, and you could have been killed.”

<!-- paragraph:428 para_id:000001AC style:Normal -->
Maya frowned, her lips pressing into a tight line. “I—”

<!-- paragraph:430 para_id:000001AE style:Normal -->
“Do not,” Niall interrupted, his tone brooking no argument. “Do not think for a moment that what you did was safe. Much more experienced mages than you have crumbled under the mental demands of combat. You have power, Maya, but you lack control. The battlefield is not a place for overconfidence.”

<!-- paragraph:432 para_id:000001B0 style:Normal -->
Maya bit her tongue, her frustration simmering beneath the surface. She hated being scolded like a child, but she also couldn’t deny the truth in his words.

<!-- paragraph:434 para_id:000001B2 style:Normal -->
“Second,” Niall said, his voice hardening, “this attack was precisely aligned with the day of your Presentation. That is not a coincidence. Whoever financed this assault was willing to spend an exorbitant amount of resources to force our hand. They wanted you on that wall. They wanted to either kill you or gauge the extent of your power. And now, they have seen too much.”

<!-- paragraph:436 para_id:000001B4 style:Normal -->
The words stung, but before she could muster a response, Niall exhaled slowly, his tone shifting. “That is why I killed as many of them as I could. The ones who hired those soldiers will have relied heavily on mercenaries. Mercenaries who will think twice before accepting their coin again, knowing the risks.”

<!-- paragraph:438 para_id:000001B6 style:Normal -->
He fell silent for a moment, his gaze distant as though reliving the chaos of the battle. Maya’s chest tightened as she watched him, unsure of what to say.

<!-- paragraph:440 para_id:000001B8 style:Normal -->
Finally, Niall spoke again, his voice softer, almost reluctant. “Despite all of this, you should know… I am proud of you.” He paused, his hands clenching behind his back. “You did what your mother would have done.”

<!-- paragraph:442 para_id:000001BA style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath caught in her throat. Niall rarely spoke of her mother, and when he did, it was always with a guarded tone, as though the memory pained him too deeply to dwell on.

<!-- paragraph:444 para_id:000001BC style:Normal -->
Still, he didn’t turn to face her. The lines of his back were taut, his voice strained with something unspoken. Maya wanted to reach out, to bridge the distance between them, but she hesitated, unsure if he would welcome the gesture.

<!-- paragraph:446 para_id:000001BE style:Normal -->
“Thank you, Father,” she said quietly, her voice steady despite the lump forming in her throat.

<!-- paragraph:448 para_id:000001C0 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:20 --><!-- comment-start:21 --><!-- comment-start:22 -->After Niall’s words, Maya focused intently on the tasks ahead. Erynn returned and the preparation for Maya's Presentation resumed. They worked to review the ceremonial components while Niall sifted through reports and correspondence. It was methodical, almost calming, though the specter of the earlier battle lingered, shadowing their movements.

<!-- paragraph:450 para_id:000001C2 style:Normal -->
Hours passed before the wall commanders returned. Maya looked up from her notes as the door opened, immediately sensing the unease in their demeanor. Their armor was still scuffed from the fight, and the way they shifted under Niall’s gaze made her stomach tighten.<!-- comment-end:20 --><!-- comment-ref:20 --><!-- comment-end:21 --><!-- comment-ref:21 --><!-- comment-end:22 --><!-- comment-ref:22 -->

<!-- paragraph:452 para_id:000001C4 style:Normal -->
“We found something,” one of the commanders said. His voice was low, almost reluctant.

<!-- paragraph:454 para_id:000001C6 style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes narrowed, and he rose from his chair. “Show me.” He glanced at Maya. “You made yourself a part of this. Come. See it through.”

<!-- paragraph:456 para_id:000001C8 style:Normal -->
Maya followed her father and the commanders down a series of halls leading to the edge of the citadel. The air grew colder as they approached one of the wall’s ancillary chambers, a space used for triage during battles. Inside, the smell of charred flesh and blood was overwhelming.

<!-- paragraph:458 para_id:000001CA style:Normal -->
Propped against the wall was a man—if he could still be called that. His body was <!-- comment-start:23 -->a<!-- comment-end:23 --><!-- comment-ref:23 --> ruin. The right side of his chest and arm had been seared away, leaving blackened, cracked skin. His breathing came in wet, labored gasps. Maya’s heart clenched as she realized that this must have been her doing, the result of one of her fire spells during the battle.

<!-- paragraph:460 para_id:000001CC style:Normal -->
The wall commander gestured at the man. “We couldn’t move him any farther safely. He’s dying.”

<!-- paragraph:462 para_id:000001CE style:Normal -->
The man’s remaining eye flicked open, dull with pain but sharp enough to land on Niall. “You think… you’ll get anything out of me?” His voice was a strained rasp, each word dragged from his scorched throat. “You’re a fool.”

<!-- paragraph:464 para_id:000001D0 style:Normal -->
Niall crouched beside him, his hands resting on his knees. “What’s your name?”

<!-- paragraph:466 para_id:000001D2 style:Normal -->
The man’s lips twitched in a weak smile. “Why does it matter?”

<!-- paragraph:468 para_id:000001D4 style:Normal -->
“It matters because I don’t negotiate with nameless men,” Niall said, his voice calm.

<!-- paragraph:470 para_id:000001D6 style:Normal -->
The soldier hesitated before rasping, “Tarran.”

<!-- paragraph:472 para_id:000001D8 style:Normal -->
“Tarran,” Niall repeated, inclining his head slightly. “You should know this—High Clade politics are ruthless. I wouldn’t have taken any action simply because of an attack on my citadel.” His tone sharpened, and he leaned in slightly. “But you and your people attacked the wall where my daughter stood. You put her in danger. And that made it personal.”

<!-- paragraph:474 para_id:000001DA style:Normal -->
Tarran let out a wet, gurgling laugh, shaking his head slightly. “Danger?” he choked, his voice bitter. “The danger wasn’t to her. High Clades… all the same. All of you.”

<!-- paragraph:476 para_id:000001DC style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t flinch, his face impassive. Tarran coughed and turned his head slightly, as if even meeting Niall’s gaze was too much effort. “I won’t tell you… anything,” he said, his voice fading into a whisper. “I’m dying.”

<!-- paragraph:478 para_id:000001DE style:Normal -->
Maya stepped forward before Niall could respond, her voice calm but resolute. “You don’t have to die,” she said, her words cutting through the heavy silence. “We have magical healers. They can save you.”

<!-- paragraph:480 para_id:000001E0 style:Normal -->
Tarran’s eye narrowed, a faint flicker of suspicion flashing across his face. “Heal me… first,” he whispered, each word labored.

<!-- paragraph:482 para_id:000001E2 style:Normal -->
Niall’s jaw tightened, and his gaze flicked briefly to Maya. She met his look but didn’t falter. “Even if he wanted to tell us something, I can barely understand him like this,” she said evenly, her tone measured. “If he’s dead, we gain nothing.”

<!-- paragraph:484 para_id:000001E4 style:Normal -->
The room was heavy with unspoken tension as Niall nodded sharply. “Send for a healer,” he instructed one of the commanders. “Tell them to bring their equipment here immediately. The soldier stays where he is.”

<!-- paragraph:486 para_id:000001E6 style:Normal -->
The commander saluted, his expression conflicted, and left at a brisk pace. Tarran’s breathing grew more ragged, and the faint shuffle of boots in the hall was the only sound.

<!-- paragraph:488 para_id:000001E8 style:Normal -->
Niall turned slightly, lowering his voice as he addressed Maya. <!-- comment-start:24 --><!-- comment-start:25 -->“That was well done,” he said, his tone carrying an air of reluctant approval. “You didn’t show leniency to an enemy in front of our soldiers. Some of them lost friends defending these walls. Seeing you give him a chance, but not too freely, matters.”<!-- comment-end:24 --><!-- comment-ref:24 --><!-- comment-end:25 --><!-- comment-ref:25 -->

<!-- paragraph:490 para_id:000001EA style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, though her chest tightened. She hadn’t thought of any of that—she’d only wanted to save the man’s life and maybe glean some information from him. But now, as her father’s words sank in, the lessons she’d been taught about political bearing came rushing back. She had always practiced them in abstract, rehearsing poised responses to hypothetical situations. This moment, raw and real, felt entirely different.

<!-- paragraph:492 para_id:000001EC style:Normal -->
“Remember,” Niall continued, his voice steady and deliberate, “every resource has a cost. Using one of our healers on him could mean delaying treatment for one of our soldiers. Someone could <!-- comment-start:26 -->be maimed—or worse—<!-- comment-end:26 --><!-- comment-ref:26 -->because of this choice. That’s why you can’t afford to waste <!-- comment-start:27 -->it. When we speak to him after he’s healed, tread carefully. Healing<!-- comment-end:27 --><!-- comment-ref:27 --> him first gives us less leverage than we might have had otherwise.”

<!-- paragraph:494 para_id:000001EE style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach churned as Niall turned back to Tarran, already focusing on the next move.

<!-- paragraph:496 para_id:000001F0 style:Normal -->
The healer arrived quickly, their entrance heralded by the soft padding of their bonded animal—a sleek, almost ethereal creature with shimmering fur and luminous eyes. The animal, rare and impossibly expensive, padded silently to the wounded man and sat beside him, its gaze fixed. <!-- comment-start:28 -->Maya recognized it from lessons on magical healing; such animals required a diet of rare imported foods, and their upkeep was exorbitant.
