Jalen hesitated, his jaw tightening slightly. “It… modified Antithesis,” he said carefully. “It’s a workman’s knife right now, but the Boon will let him return it to its former size.”

<!-- paragraph:820 para_id:00000334 style:Normal -->
Kelyra rolled her eyes, leaning back in her chair. “Creator save us from boys and their swords,” she muttered. “What’s the point of that? It sounds like more trouble than it’s worth.”

<!-- paragraph:821 para_id:00000335 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t respond at first. His jaw clenched, and his hands curled slowly into fists. “I might’ve… agreed to kill Niall Rezin,” he said at last, voice low and flat.

<!-- paragraph:822 para_id:00000336 style:Normal -->
The room froze.

<!-- paragraph:823 para_id:00000337 style:Normal -->
Kelyra sat upright. “You what?”

<!-- paragraph:824 para_id:00000338 style:Normal -->
“I thought—” Davian hesitated, then pushed through. “I thought if I offered, the Syndicate would accept me instead of Jalen. That it would buy us some time. That they’d—”

<!-- paragraph:825 para_id:00000339 style:Normal -->
Jalen slammed his hand on the table. “You thought they’d what? Be moved by your sacrifice? They don’t care who volunteers, Davian. They care who gets results.”

<!-- paragraph:826 para_id:0000033A style:Normal -->
Davian turned on him. “You were going to die. I wasn’t going to just stand there while they chose you.”

<!-- paragraph:827 para_id:0000033B style:Normal -->
“And you think martyring yourself is any better?” Kelyra’s voice was sharp now, incredulous. “You didn’t even ask us. You just threw yourself into it, like it was some heroic gesture. Like you were proving a point.”

<!-- paragraph:828 para_id:0000033C style:Normal -->
Davian’s voice cracked. “That’s not what I was doing!”

<!-- paragraph:829 para_id:0000033D style:Normal -->
“No?” Jalen leaned forward, eyes dark. “Because it looked an awful lot like the time you went to take down those bounty hunters alone in the tunnels. Or when you broadcast our safehouse location just to draw out a kill-team. Every time, it’s the same—you deciding what’s best, and the rest of us left cleaning up the blood.”

<!-- paragraph:830 para_id:0000033E style:Normal -->
Davian looked from one to the other. “I was trying to protect you. Every time.”

<!-- paragraph:831 para_id:0000033F style:Normal -->
Kelyra snorted. “And every time, it ended the same way. You nearly died.”

<!-- paragraph:832 para_id:00000340 style:Normal -->
“I thought…” Davian’s voice dropped. “I thought if I made myself useful, they’d leave you alone.”

<!-- paragraph:833 para_id:00000341 style:Normal -->
Jalen stared at him, but his expression didn’t soften. “You think this is about usefulness? You think this is about you? This isn’t a story where you win the crowd with some bold gesture. This is a war, Davian. A real one. And your name is already inked into the wrong pages.”

<!-- paragraph:834 para_id:00000342 style:Normal -->
Davian’s throat worked. “It was the only way they were going to let me switch places.”

<!-- paragraph:835 para_id:00000343 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s expression was unreadable. “Or maybe it was just another excuse to chase glory. To kill a High Clade and feel righteous doing it.”

<!-- paragraph:836 para_id:00000344 style:Normal -->
He couldn’t even answer that. Not properly. Not with the look they were giving him.

<!-- paragraph:837 para_id:00000345 style:Normal -->
Because if even they thought he was just chasing power—what did that mean?

<!-- paragraph:838 para_id:00000346 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stared at him for a long moment, then sighed deeply, rubbing her temples as if warding off a headache. As she lowered her hands, her gaze flicked briefly toward the corner of the room, where a small shrine stood. Fresh flowers rested there, their vibrant petals a stark contrast to the worn stone around them. “Davian,” she said, her tone clipped, “you know just as well as I do what the High Clades deserve. But agreeing to kill Niall—without even being asked to—is possibly the stupidest part of this entire arrangement.”

<!-- paragraph:839 para_id:00000347 style:Normal -->
Davian opened his mouth to retort, but Kelyra cut him off with a sharp look.

<!-- paragraph:840 para_id:00000348 style:Normal -->
“You’d better hope the Syndicate will be happy with just the Heart,” she added, her voice biting. Then, almost to herself, she muttered, “As if ‘just the Heart’ isn’t already the most ridiculous thing I’ve ever said.”

<!-- paragraph:841 para_id:00000349 style:Normal -->
Jalen pulled out a bundle of documents, their edges worn and creased from handling, and spread them across the table. “These are the files we procured,” he said, gesturing to the neat rows of text and diagrams. “They include the names and descriptions of the arcane craftsmen hired for the ward overhaul on the Rezin fortress.”

<!-- paragraph:842 para_id:0000034A style:Normal -->
Kelyra scanned the papers quickly, her finger tapping on the sample contract attached to the top. “The contractors are added to the Rezin’s Registry and expected to live in the citadel during the project,” she murmured. Her sharp gaze flicked up to Jalen. “That’s interesting. It’s been longer than it should have since their wards were updated. I wonder if this has anything to do with the new heir.”

<!-- paragraph:843 para_id:0000034B style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “New heir?”

<!-- paragraph:844 para_id:0000034C style:Normal -->
Kelyra gave him a pointed look. “One of our informants saw a young woman today, riding alongside Niall Rezin with her Shroud up. They were heading to the Central Spire. And if they’re showing her off, it means she’s reached the point where they consider her magic strong enough to be a threat.”

<!-- paragraph:845 para_id:0000034D style:Normal -->
Davian snorted. “<!-- comment-start:52 -->I might have gotten an early taste of her magic<!-- comment-end:52 --><!-- comment-ref:52 -->.”

<!-- paragraph:846 para_id:0000034E style:Normal -->
Kelyra raised an eyebrow, and Jalen’s gaze sharpened. “Explain,” Kelyra said flatly.

<!-- paragraph:847 para_id:0000034F style:Normal -->
Davian leaned back in his chair, his expression both frustrated and begrudgingly impressed. “During the attack on the Low Clade manor, a beam of fire sliced straight through one of the turrets. It almost fell on me. We were right by the Rezin citadel, and everyone knows Niall uses lightning - so <!-- comment-start:53 -->who else could it be<!-- comment-end:53 --><!-- comment-ref:53 -->?”

<!-- paragraph:848 para_id:00000350 style:Normal -->
Kelyra let out a low breath, her expression darkening. “The magic of the High Clades isn’t even on the same playing field as the Low Clades’. What you saw was honed by generations of training and bloodlines bred for dominance.”

<!-- paragraph:849 para_id:00000351 style:Normal -->
“Then we plan accordingly,” Jalen said grimly, pointing at the documents. “The craftsmen are our way in. We start by identifying who we can replace—and how to deal with the Registry.”

<!-- paragraph:850 para_id:00000352 style:Normal -->
They pored over the documents, the three of them leaning over the table as Jalen shuffled through the pages. Each file was meticulously detailed, with names, skills, and contract terms for the arcane craftsmen slated to work on the Rezin fortress wards.

<!-- paragraph:851 para_id:00000353 style:Normal -->
“This one,” Jalen said, tapping on a name halfway down the page. “Arrek Hadrin. Glyphwright. He’s scheduled to arrive in three days.”

<!-- paragraph:852 para_id:00000354 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “And the plan is what, exactly? We find him, take him out, and I replace him?”

<!-- paragraph:853 para_id:00000355 style:Normal -->
“Essentially,” Kelyra said, her tone sharp. “You’ll copy his aura using the Boon and get yourself added to the Registry under his name. Then, while the Boon is still active, you’ll go back to the Registry and add your true name and aura to it before it wears off.”

<!-- paragraph:854 para_id:00000356 style:Normal -->
Davian grimaced. “That sounds overly complicated. Why can’t I just say I’m this guy and add my aura to the Registry the normal way?”

<!-- paragraph:855 para_id:00000357 style:Normal -->
Kelyra rolled her eyes, her tone brimming with exasperation. “Do you have any idea what kind of magic the Registry is? This isn’t some scribbled list in a ledger, Davian. It’s ancient, High Clade-level magic. It operates on immutable truths—<!-- comment-start:54 -->your aura must match your true name, or it simply won’t work.<!-- comment-end:54 --><!-- comment-ref:54 --> Any attempt to force it would be futile.”

<!-- paragraph:856 para_id:00000358 style:Normal -->
“And,” Jalen added, his expression grim, “if you try, it might just kill you instead.”

<!-- paragraph:857 para_id:00000359 style:Normal -->
Davian crossed his arms, scowling. “Fine, but then my true name will be in the Registry. You do realize how much of a liability that is, right?”

<!-- paragraph:858 para_id:0000035A style:Normal -->
“You’ll just have to deal with it,” Kelyra snapped. “And erase it when you’re leaving. But listen carefully—this is important. The Registry has to be in passive mode when you do any of this. Removing your true name will trip the alarms. And if it’s in active mode, it won’t just alert the Rezins. The magic will burn you out of existence.”

<!-- paragraph:859 para_id:0000035B style:Normal -->
Davian leaned back in his chair, exhaustion pulling at his body but his mind still racing. “How long do we have to plan this?”

<!-- paragraph:860 para_id:0000035C style:Normal -->
Jalen glanced at the documents spread across the table, his expression grim. “Not long enough. The contractors are supposed to present themselves tomorrow morning. We need to take Arrek out tonight.”

<!-- paragraph:861 para_id:0000035D style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “Tonight? Come on.”

<!-- paragraph:862 para_id:0000035E style:Normal -->
Kelyra shot him a sharp look. “Did you think the Syndicate was going to give us a leisurely timeline? If this is going to work, we can’t waste a second.”

<!-- paragraph:863 para_id:0000035F style:Normal -->
Jalen nodded. “We’ll identify where Arrek is staying, get you in, and handle the rest. You’ll need to mimic him flawlessly by morning.”

<!-- paragraph:864 para_id:00000360 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled, running a hand through his hair. “Fine. Just don’t expect me to be thrilled about it.”

<!-- paragraph:865 para_id:00000361 style:Normal -->
“Thrilled or not, you’ll need to be ready,” Kelyra said curtly. “Go get some rest while we finalize the details.”

<!-- paragraph:866 para_id:00000362 style:Normal -->
Davian rose stiffly from his chair, his bruised forearms protesting the movement. He stepped into the hallway, rubbing his temples as the door creaked shut behind him. He blinked in mild surprise when he saw Tarek standing a few steps away with a startled expression.

<!-- paragraph:867 para_id:00000363 style:Normal -->
“Tarek?” Davian said, his tone curious.

<!-- paragraph:868 para_id:00000364 style:Normal -->
Tarek recovered quickly, offering a tentative smile. “Davian. I, uh… I just wanted to say thanks. For pulling me out,” he said, his voice level. “You didn’t have to.”

<!-- paragraph:869 para_id:00000365 style:Normal -->
Davian shrugged, brushing off the lingering embarrassment in Tarek’s tone. “Don’t worry about it. I’m sure you’d have done the same for me.”

<!-- paragraph:870 para_id:00000366 style:Normal -->
Tarek hesitated for just a second before nodding. “Yeah. Of course.”

<!-- paragraph:871 para_id:00000367 style:Normal -->
Davian gave him a firm clap on the shoulder, the gesture meant to ease any lingering tension. “Get some rest, Tarek. We’ve got a big day ahead tomorrow.”

<!-- paragraph:872 para_id:00000368 style:Normal -->
As Davian walked away, he found himself glancing back briefly, catching Tarek still standing in the same spot, his shoulders slightly hunched. He shook his head, dismissing the sight. No one likes needing to be saved, Davian thought. He’ll get over it.

<!-- paragraph:873 para_id:00000369 style:Normal -->
—

<!-- paragraph:874 para_id:0000036A style:Normal -->
Davian’s dreams fractured into wakefulness as a rough hand shook his shoulder. He blinked blearily, disoriented by the faint glow of torchlight filtering through the cracks in the shutters.

<!-- paragraph:875 para_id:0000036B style:Normal -->
“Up, Davian,” came the voice of Calder, one of the Concord’s more seasoned members. His wiry frame cast a sharp silhouette against the dim light, and his face was marked with a scar running from his cheek to his jawline, a relic of some long-forgotten skirmish. “It’s time.”

<!-- paragraph:876 para_id:0000036C style:Normal -->
Davian groaned, rubbing his face as he sat up. His body felt surprisingly whole, the pain from the Ink Boon a distant memory. His forearms, however, still bore deep, angry bruises. He glanced at his diminutive workman’s knife and sighed ruefully, tucking it into his belt.

<!-- paragraph:877 para_id:0000036D style:Normal -->
As Davian stepped into the hallway, the air still thick with night silence, he heard a sudden clatter behind him.

<!-- paragraph:878 para_id:0000036E style:Normal -->
He spun, instinctively reaching for his knife—then froze.

<!-- paragraph:879 para_id:0000036F style:Normal -->
A small wooden sword lay on the floorboards, just outside a half-open doorway. A moment later, a tiny hand darted out and yanked it back into the shadows.

<!-- paragraph:880 para_id:00000370 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled and pinched the bridge of his nose. “Kael,” he said tiredly, “come out.”

<!-- paragraph:881 para_id:00000371 style:Normal -->
There was a pause, then Kael emerged sheepishly, clutching the wooden sword tight to his chest like a real blade. His curls were wild with sleep, but his eyes burned with determination.

<!-- paragraph:882 para_id:00000372 style:Normal -->
“I’m going too,” Kael announced.

<!-- paragraph:883 para_id:00000373 style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head, already feeling the weight behind his brother’s words. “No, you’re not.”

<!-- paragraph:884 para_id:00000374 style:Normal -->
Kael’s grip tightened. “I am. I know it’s something important. And if you’re doing it, then… it must be the right thing.”

<!-- paragraph:885 para_id:00000375 style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. Of all the things he’d expected, that wasn’t one of them. After the day he’d had—accusations, lectures, doubts—those simple words hit harder than he liked. He crouched to Kael’s level, meeting his gaze.

<!-- paragraph:886 para_id:00000376 style:Normal -->
“It’s hard to tell what the right thing is,” Davian said quietly. “Harder than I ever thought it would be.”

<!-- paragraph:887 para_id:00000377 style:Normal -->
Kael frowned but said nothing. Davian reached out and ruffled his hair.

<!-- paragraph:888 para_id:00000378 style:Normal -->
“You need a few more years of sword drills and vegetable-eating before you’re ready to come with me. Deal?”

<!-- paragraph:889 para_id:00000379 style:Normal -->
Kael wrinkled his nose. “I hate vegetables.”

<!-- paragraph:890 para_id:0000037A style:Normal -->
“Yeah, well.” Davian smiled faintly. “Sacrifices are part of being a warrior.”

<!-- paragraph:891 para_id:0000037B style:Normal -->
Kael nodded solemnly and turned back toward his room. Davian waited, watching his little frame retreat down the hall. Just before he reached the door, Kael looked like he might turn around—but then he slipped back inside and shut it softly behind him.

<!-- paragraph:892 para_id:0000037C style:Normal -->
Davian let out a slow breath, then headed for the courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:893 para_id:0000037D style:Normal -->
By the time he stepped outside, the cool night air cleared the remnants of sleep from his mind. The courtyard was lit by flickering torches, their flames casting long shadows on the cracked stone walls. Jalen stood with Tarek and Calder, along with a few other Concord members, their postures tense but resolute.

<!-- paragraph:894 para_id:0000037E style:Normal -->
“We let you sleep as long as we could,” Jalen said, his tone clipped. “The plans are finalized, and we need to move.”

<!-- paragraph:895 para_id:0000037F style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, falling into step with the group as they slipped out through the gate and into the shadowed streets of the city. The sounds of the manor faded behind them, replaced by the distant hum of urban life: the clatter of distant carts, muffled voices, and the occasional bark of a stray dog.

<!-- paragraph:896 para_id:00000380 style:Normal -->
As they wound through the maze of backstreets, Jalen dropped into step beside Davian. He signed without looking over: We have a lead. Arrek drinks near Redbridge. Our agent confirmed—he’s there tonight.

<!-- paragraph:897 para_id:00000381 style:Normal -->
Davian signed back: And after?

<!-- paragraph:898 para_id:00000382 style:Normal -->
We follow, Jalen replied. He takes the alleys home. Same route every time. That’s where we do it. Clean. Quiet.

<!-- paragraph:899 para_id:00000383 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed the unease rising in his chest. It wasn’t the first time the Concord had done something like this, but it felt different knowing he’d be stepping into Arrek’s place, assuming his identity to infiltrate one of the most dangerous fortresses in the city.

<!-- paragraph:900 para_id:00000384 style:Normal -->
The group reached the tavern, its warm glow spilling onto the cobbled street. The faint hum of drunken laughter and the occasional clink of mugs filtered through the cracked windows. Calder led the way, his steps careful as he scanned their surroundings.

<!-- paragraph:901 para_id:00000385 style:Normal -->
Near the side of the building, one of the Concord’s agents—Garren, thin and sharp-featured—stepped out of the shadows. He signed: He’s inside. A nod toward a smudged window. Far corner. Third tankard.

<!-- paragraph:902 para_id:00000386 style:Normal -->
Davian crept closer, peering through the dirty glass. The lighting was dim, but the figure was clear: hunched, wiry, poorly groomed. Pockmarked face, crooked nose—Arrek looked like someone who lost most fights but never stopped starting them.

<!-- paragraph:903 para_id:00000387 style:Normal -->
Davian wrinkled his nose, turned to Jalen, and signed: Seriously? That’s me?

<!-- paragraph:904 para_id:00000388 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s reply was steady: Best we’ve got. Independent. No close contacts.

<!-- paragraph:905 para_id:00000389 style:Normal -->
Davian jabbed a thumb toward the window. He looks like he lost a duel with a brick wall.

<!-- paragraph:906 para_id:0000038A style:Normal -->
Then say you spent your bonus on magic healing, Jalen signed flatly.

<!-- paragraph:907 para_id:0000038B style:Normal -->
Calder, lurking a bit too close, leaned over for a glance, then signed with a crooked grin: Twins. Uncanny.

<!-- paragraph:908 para_id:0000038C style:Normal -->
Davian shot him a glare, but Calder just smirked wider.

<!-- paragraph:909 para_id:0000038D style:Normal -->
This isn’t funny, Davian signed.

<!-- paragraph:910 para_id:0000038E style:Normal -->
No, Jalen replied, expression hardening. It isn’t. But it’s what we have. Make it work.

<!-- paragraph:911 para_id:0000038F style:Normal -->
Inside, Arrek drained his tankard and waved off the offer of another, his ruddy face flushed from drink. With a groggy determination, he rose from his seat, muttering something unintelligible to the barkeep as he stumbled toward the door.

<!-- paragraph:912 para_id:00000390 style:Normal -->
The cool night air greeted him as he stepped outside, the flickering lamplight casting uneven shadows on the cobblestones. Arrek weaved unsteadily, heading toward the alleys with the sluggish, aimless gait of someone too drunk to care where they were going.

<!-- paragraph:913 para_id:00000391 style:Normal -->
From the shadows, Davian, Jalen, and the others trailed him, their movements precise and soundless, each step a practiced art of pursuit. The din of the tavern faded behind them, replaced by the soft rustle of cloaks and the faint scuff of Arrek’s boots echoing in the narrowing streets.

<!-- paragraph:914 para_id:00000392 style:Normal -->
Jalen raised a hand, halting the group at a bend in the corridor. Up ahead, Arrek stumbled into view under the weak swing of a lantern, muttering to himself as he checked a worn clipboard. He looked harmless. Ordinary.

<!-- paragraph:915 para_id:00000393 style:Normal -->
Jalen caught Davian’s eye and signed quickly: Now. Quiet. Fast. Blade high. No mistakes.

<!-- paragraph:916 para_id:00000394 style:Normal -->
Davian froze. His heart was pounding too loudly. The sword felt distant in his hand, like it belonged to someone else.

<!-- paragraph:917 para_id:00000395 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s eyes narrowed. His hands dropped. “Do it,” he whispered aloud. “He chose to work for them. For the High Clades. That makes him no better than the ones who killed your sister.”

<!-- paragraph:918 para_id:00000396 style:Normal -->
Davian flinched. “He’s just a contractor. He didn’t—”

<!-- paragraph:919 para_id:00000397 style:Normal -->
“He knew,” Jalen hissed. “They all know. They look away, take the money, and build palaces over our bones. He’s not innocent. None of them are.”

<!-- paragraph:920 para_id:00000398 style:Normal -->
The darkness pressed in at the edges of Davian’s vision. Arrek blurred. His own limbs felt disconnected, like a puppet being nudged forward by strings he couldn’t see. A hollow numbness settled into his chest, deeper than rage, deeper than fear. Like falling.

<!-- paragraph:921 para_id:00000399 style:Normal -->
A flash: Selina, laughing, tugging at his sleeve. Then: Selina, broken—her body twisted on the stones where the High Clade magus left her. Because she got in the way. Because she didn’t matter.

<!-- paragraph:922 para_id:0000039A style:Normal -->
He stepped forward, the words slipping from his mouth like a curse he’d memorized in another life. “For Selina.”

<!-- paragraph:923 para_id:0000039B style:Normal -->
Arrek paused, his body stiffening at the sound. He turned, squinting into the dark. “Hello? Who’s—”

<!-- paragraph:924 para_id:0000039C style:Normal -->
Davian lunged. He didn’t feel his feet leave the ground—just the impact as he slammed Arrek against the wall, blade punching into the man’s chest. The gasp that left Arrek’s mouth was cut short by Davian’s hand clamping over it, desperate and shaking. The two of them were locked together, bodies trembling, Arrek’s blood hot against his skin.

<!-- paragraph:925 para_id:0000039D style:Normal -->
He couldn’t think. Couldn’t breathe. His vision tunneled until there was nothing but Arrek’s wide, panicked eyes and the sound of his own heartbeat, too fast, too loud.

<!-- paragraph:926 para_id:0000039E style:Normal -->
Antithesis thrummed in his grip.

<!-- paragraph:927 para_id:0000039F style:Normal -->
Then the Boon activated.

<!-- paragraph:928 para_id:000003A0 style:Normal -->
Ink surged down Davian’s arm like it had come alive, tendrils of liquid shadow threading into Arrek’s body. The air thickened, pressure building around them as Arrek convulsed. A faint, translucent glow—his aura—was pulled free, spiraling toward Davian’s arm and vanishing into the shifting patterns.

<!-- paragraph:929 para_id:000003A1 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t let go, not even when Arrek went limp. He held him there, back against the wall, hand still covering his mouth, as if afraid that letting go would mean falling apart. Finally, the ink stilled, the new pattern settling into place—sharper, more complex. Etched in silence. Etched in death.

<!-- paragraph:930 para_id:000003A2 style:Normal -->
The alley grew quiet again, save for the faint rustle of Davian lowering the body to the ground. His breath came in shallow gasps, his heart pounding in his ears.

<!-- paragraph:931 para_id:000003A3 style:Normal -->
Jalen and the others moved instantly, their efficiency honed by years of experience. Calder produced a heavy canvas wrap, and the group swiftly bundled Arrek’s body, working in silent coordination.

<!-- paragraph:932 para_id:000003A4 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped back, his breath uneven as he watched the others lift the wrapped corpse. The weight of what he’d just done pressed down on him like a tangible force, but there was no time to dwell on it.

<!-- paragraph:933 para_id:000003A5 style:Normal -->
Jalen bent down, rifled through Arrek’s belongings, and tossed his coin purse to Davian with a sharp flick of the wrist. He signed: Congratulations. You're Arrek now.

<!-- paragraph:934 para_id:000003A6 style:Normal -->
Davian caught it reflexively, the weight thudding into his palm like a judgment. He stood there, frozen, until Garren stepped beside him and signed: We’re going to Arrek’s house. Follow.

<!-- paragraph:935 para_id:000003A7 style:Normal -->
Jalen gave a curt nod, then added: Anything useful—we need it. Sell the role. Move.

<!-- paragraph:936 para_id:000003A8 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed hard, his hand tightening around the purse as he followed Garren and Jalen out of the alley. The rest of the group melted into the shadows, carrying their grim burden back to the manor, where the fires would erase the last traces of Arrek.

<!-- paragraph:940 para_id:000003AC style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 5

<!-- paragraph:941 para_id:000003AD style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 5

<!-- paragraph:943 para_id:000003AF style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:945 para_id:000003B1 style:Normal -->
The ride back to the Rezin citadel blurred past in tense silence. Elarion’s streets felt dim and judgmental, shadows pressing in as if listening. Niall said nothing, but his urgency bled into everything—his posture, his pace, even the Ironstriders beneath them, hooves striking faster than usual.

<!-- paragraph:946 para_id:000003B2 style:Normal -->
They dismounted without a word. Niall strode ahead, his focus razor-sharp, and Maya fell in behind him without thinking. She didn’t know what to say. Her words at the Presentation echoed louder with each step.

<!-- paragraph:947 para_id:000003B3 style:Normal -->
He led her straight to the training courtyard—familiar, scorched, lined with arcane tools she knew by heart. No attendants. No guards inside. Just a door shutting behind them with a heavy finality that made her stomach twist.

<!-- paragraph:948 para_id:000003B4 style:Normal -->
Sunlight slanted through the high walls, catching etched zän and making the air shimmer faintly. Niall turned, eyes sharp enough to cut through whatever composure she had left.

<!-- paragraph:949 para_id:000003B5 style:Normal -->
“What you did today,” Niall said quietly, “was not what we agreed on.”

<!-- paragraph:950 para_id:000003B6 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms, her heartbeat still faintly thrumming with the aftershocks of flame. “No. It wasn’t.”

<!-- paragraph:951 para_id:000003B7 style:Normal -->
He turned then, gaze fixed on hers. “You chose spectacle. A column of fire tearing open the sky. That’s not just power, Maya. That’s provocation.”

<!-- paragraph:952 para_id:000003B8 style:Normal -->
She held his gaze. “You told me to show restraint. But restraint is what they expect. It’s what they dismiss. I saw their faces - they were barely watching me until I burned a hole through the clouds.”

<!-- paragraph:953 para_id:000003B9 style:Normal -->
His jaw tightened. “And what did you see in their eyes afterward?”

<!-- paragraph:954 para_id:000003BA style:Normal -->
“I don’t know.” Her voice dropped. “But it wasn’t dismissal.”

<!-- paragraph:955 para_id:000003BB style:Normal -->
Niall’s silence pressed down like a weight. When he finally spoke, his voice was low and cold. “You’ve made yourself a target. They’ll see it as a challenge. Not just to them. To everything.”

<!-- paragraph:956 para_id:000003BC style:Normal -->
“I don’t regret it,” she said, too quickly. “I needed them to see what I could do. That they can’t intimidate us.”

<!-- paragraph:957 para_id:000003BD style:Normal -->
“And now they’ll test you. That’s what happens when you declare strength in this world.” His tone sharpened. “I told you: keep your head high, but don’t make yourself a threat.”

<!-- paragraph:958 para_id:000003BE style:Normal -->
“You told me to make them respect me without giving them a reason to fear me. That’s impossible.”

<!-- paragraph:959 para_id:000003BF style:Normal -->
“No, it’s survival,” he snapped. He took a deep breath. “While you’re within the citadel, the Registry will protect you. But once you step beyond our walls, your Shroud stays active. At all times. No exceptions.”

<!-- paragraph:960 para_id:000003C0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression hardened. “I’m not going to live in fear.”

<!-- paragraph:961 para_id:000003C1 style:Normal -->
“You’re not scared?” His voice turned bitter. “Then you don’t understand the game you’ve stepped into. And if you keep playing like this, you’ll start to understand it the moment someone tries to kill you.”

<!-- paragraph:962 para_id:000003C2 style:Normal -->
She hesitated. “Do you really think it was the wrong choice?”

<!-- paragraph:963 para_id:000003C3 style:Normal -->
His silence stretched too long.

<!-- paragraph:964 para_id:000003C4 style:Normal -->
“I think it was a winning choice,” he said at last. “But the cost will be paid in blood.”

<!-- paragraph:965 para_id:000003C5 style:Normal -->
He turned back to her, face unreadable. “It worked. They saw you. They’ll never unsee you. But that power you showed? You don’t get to set it down now. You’ve claimed a seat among monsters, Maya. You’ll have to fight to keep it.”

<!-- paragraph:966 para_id:000003C6 style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach twisted at his words, but she pressed on. “I didn’t do this to challenge them. I did it to save lives, to protect our family and soldiers.”

<!-- paragraph:967 para_id:000003C7 style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression darkened, his jaw tightening. “And perhaps you will save lives—if the other Clades test you and don’t find you wanting. Then, they’ll know that attacking the Rezins is a waste of resources.”

<!-- paragraph:968 para_id:000003C8 style:Normal -->
Her brow furrowed, and she took a step closer. “How can you call lives ‘resources’ so callously?”

<!-- paragraph:969 para_id:000003C9 style:Normal -->
For a moment, Niall’s icy composure cracked, revealing a flicker of something darker. His eyes hardened, that same dead, icy look she had seen on the battlefield when he was cutting down fleeing soldiers without hesitation. “The other Clades will exploit anything—any hesitation, any bond—to get what they want. In this world, empathy doesn’t protect you. It marks you. Make you vulnerable. I’ve seen it too many times.”

<!-- paragraph:970 para_id:000003CA style:Normal -->
Maya stared at him, searching his face for any sign of warmth or humanity, but all she saw was the hollow mask of a man who had hardened himself beyond recognition. The deadness in his eyes chilled her to the bone.

<!-- paragraph:971 para_id:000003CB style:Normal -->
Before she could gather her thoughts to argue, the sound of youthful footsteps echoed down the stone corridor leading to the courtyard. Both of them turned toward the noise.

<!-- paragraph:972 para_id:000003CC style:Normal -->
“Selwin,” Niall muttered, his tone softening just a fraction. It could only be him, her younger brother - he was the only one the guards would have allowed to pass.

<!-- paragraph:973 para_id:000003CD style:Normal -->
Maya looked toward the source of the sound just in time to see Selwin burst through the archway, his face alight with excitement. His sudden arrival was a jarring contrast to the tension that hung between her and Niall, a tension she could almost see lingering in the frosted air between them.

<!-- paragraph:974 para_id:000003CE style:Normal -->
“Maya!” Selwin’s voice cut through her thoughts as he came to a stop before her, his grin wide and unrestrained. “How did your Presentation go? I bet it was amazing! I bet everyone was in awe!”

<!-- paragraph:975 para_id:000003CF style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, his words catching her off guard. She felt warmth rising in her chest, softening the edges of her lingering frustration. She glanced at Niall, whose gaze shifted between them. His expression softened, the rigid set of his jaw easing for the first time since they’d begun their discussion.

<!-- paragraph:976 para_id:000003D0 style:Normal -->
“It went fine,” Maya said, her tone gentler than it had been moments before. “You shouldn’t put so much faith in me, though.”

<!-- paragraph:977 para_id:000003D1 style:Normal -->
“Why not?” Selwin asked, his head tilting with genuine curiosity. “You’re the best.”

<!-- paragraph:978 para_id:000003D2 style:Normal -->
Niall let out a low chuckle, almost inaudible, and shook his head. “Maya, you might as well work with him on basic magical principles today,” he said, his voice carrying a faint note of resignation. “It will do you both some good.”

<!-- paragraph:979 para_id:000003D3 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth to respond but stopped herself. Instead, she looked at Selwin, whose eyes lit up with excitement, and gave him a small nod. “All right,” she said. “Let’s see what we can do.”

<!-- paragraph:980 para_id:000003D4 style:Normal -->
As Selwin started rattling off questions about what they’d practice, Maya caught the faintest flicker of something in Niall’s expression—something almost like relief. For now, the tension had dissipated, leaving room for something lighter. But Maya knew the weight of their earlier conversation wasn’t gone, only set aside. For the moment, though, she let it be and focused on the simple joy of teaching her younger brother.

<!-- paragraph:981 para_id:000003D5 style:Normal -->
"Close your eyes, Selwin," she said, her tone patient but firm. "And focus. Magic begins in the mind, not the body. If your mind isn’t clear, you can’t bend the threads of reality."

<!-- paragraph:982 para_id:000003D6 style:Normal -->
Selwin sighed but obeyed, shutting his eyes with exaggerated reluctance. Maya inhaled deeply, her voice calm and steady as she continued. "Think of the movement of energy as water running across sand. If your thoughts are chaotic, your mind will run wild, splashing the water - getting nothing done. Buf if your mind is focused, you can create channels in the sand - order - and the energy will follow."

<!-- paragraph:983 para_id:000003D7 style:Normal -->
She gave him a moment, watching as his brow furrowed slightly. He was trying, though she could tell the effort wasn’t wholly sincere. Still, it was a start.

<!-- paragraph:984 para_id:000003D8 style:Normal -->
"Now," she said, "continue the breathing pattern I showed you. In through your nose, hold, and out through your mouth. Steady and controlled."

<!-- paragraph:985 para_id:000003D9 style:Normal -->
Satisfied for the moment, Maya rose and moved a few paces away, turning her focus to her own exercises. She summoned her Shroud with practiced ease, the refracted energy shimmering around her like a protective second skin. With a flick of her wrist, the Shroud shifted, jagged edges forming and dissolving as she manipulated its shape and density. She moved through the motions fluidly, her thoughts sinking into the familiar rhythm.

<!-- paragraph:986 para_id:000003DA style:Normal -->
It wasn’t long before she caught a faint movement out of the corner of her eye. Glancing sideways, she saw Selwin’s eyes open just a sliver, watching her with open curiosity.

<!-- paragraph:987 para_id:000003DB style:Normal -->
"Selwin," she said, her tone laced with gentle reproach. "What are you supposed to be doing right now?"

<!-- paragraph:988 para_id:000003DC style:Normal -->
He snapped his eyes shut, a sheepish grin spreading across his face. "Practicing centering," he admitted, though his voice lacked remorse. "But it’s boring. I want to make a Shroud like yours."

<!-- paragraph:989 para_id:000003DD style:Normal -->
Maya sighed, crossing her arms as she walked back to him. "You can’t create a Shroud yet," she said, crouching down to meet his gaze. "A Shroud isn’t just a barrier. It’s unique to the person who wields it, shaped by the type of magic they command. Without knowing what your magic is, it’s impossible to create one."

<!-- paragraph:990 para_id:000003DE style:Normal -->
Maya knelt beside Selwin, her voice patient but firm as she explained. "I made my Shroud myself, you know. It wasn’t easy—it took weeks of practice and figuring things out. See, heat is really just tiny particles moving around like they’re in a chaotic dance. My magic works by pulling that heat away, and when I do it right, I can stop that motion completely. That’s how my Shroud works—it’s like an invisible field around me that stops fast-moving things, like arrows or blades."

<!-- paragraph:991 para_id:000003DF style:Normal -->
Selwin frowned, his fingers twitching on his knees. "But what if my magic’s not strong enough? What if I can’t make a good Shroud?"

<!-- paragraph:992 para_id:000003E0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression softened. "Your magic will come when it’s ready," she said. "And when it does, we’ll figure it out together. But right now, you need to focus on the basics. The clearer your mind, the better you’ll be able to control your magic when it manifests. That’s what matters right now."

<!-- paragraph:993 para_id:000003E1 style:Normal -->
He nodded reluctantly, shutting his eyes again with a little more determination this time. Maya watched him for a moment, her mind drifting.

<!-- paragraph:994 para_id:000003E2 style:Normal -->
Selwin was at the most dangerous age for a High Clade child. No one knew what kind of magic he had—or how it would manifest. For some, it came with a slow, steady awakening, a manageable process. But for others, magic erupted violently, like a dam breaking, leaving chaos and death in its wake. Too much power drawn too quickly could destroy the caster. Backlash could cripple them. Even collateral damage from errant spells could kill a child too young to understand their limits.

<!-- paragraph:995 para_id:000003E3 style:Normal -->
This was the unspoken truth of the Presentation. Until a child could fight on the front lines and form a strong Shroud, they were too dangerous—or too embarrassing—<!-- comment-start:55 -->to risk exposing to the outside world. The<!-- comment-end:55 --><!-- comment-ref:55 --> Presentation wasn’t just a rite of passage; it was survival, both for the child and for the Clade.

<!-- paragraph:996 para_id:000003E4 style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled quietly, glancing at Selwin again. He sat straighter now, his expression more focused, though his lips were pressed into a thin line of concentration. "Good," she murmured, returning to her own exercises. "Keep at it, Selwin. You’ll get there."

<!-- paragraph:997 para_id:000003E5 style:Normal -->
Maya worked with Selwin until the sky outside had deepened into an inky blue, the stars faint pinpricks of light in the distance. The exercises grounded her, each movement and moment of focus easing the turmoil that had churned within her throughout the day. Selwin’s occasional grumbles about the monotony of his drills brought a faint smile to her lips, and for a time, she forgot about the weight of what lay ahead.

<!-- paragraph:998 para_id:000003E6 style:Normal -->
When she finally sent him to his quarters and retired to her own, the silence of the citadel wrapped around her like a heavy cloak. She lay down, her body exhausted but her mind still whirring. The thought of tomorrow lingered in the back of her mind—a shift in her role, her duties, her place within the Rezin legacy. Yet, for now, the rhythm of the day’s practice stayed with her, soothing her enough to drift into a fitful sleep.

<!-- paragraph:999 para_id:000003E7 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:56 -->The dream began as a sensation rather than an image. Maya floated, untethered, in a space that twisted and shifted like a living tapestry. The world around her folded in ways that defied understanding, patterns of light and shadow curving into shapes she couldn’t fully grasp. She moved—or perhaps was carried—through this strange realm, her body weightless yet her senses sharp.

<!-- paragraph:1000 para_id:000003E8 style:Normal -->
And then she felt it.

<!-- paragraph:1001 para_id:000003E9 style:Normal -->
A presence loomed behind her, steady and cold, its weight pressing against her awareness. She tried to turn, but her body refused to obey. Her breath quickened, the unshakable sensation of being watched setting her nerves on edge. Still, she pressed forward, her path leading her toward something she couldn’t name.<!-- comment-end:56 --><!-- comment-ref:56 -->

<!-- paragraph:1002 para_id:000003EA style:Normal -->
Ahead, a woman emerged, walking through the corridors of the citadel. At first, it was familiar. Maya recognized the gleaming stone walls and the intricate zän etched into their surfaces. She had walked this path before, though the way ahead felt impossibly long, stretching further than it should. The woman ahead moved with purpose, her steps silent but deliberate, and Maya’s heart tightened in recognition.

<!-- paragraph:1003 para_id:000003EB style:Normal -->
Her mother. It had to be.

<!-- paragraph:1004 para_id:000003EC style:Normal -->
Maya followed, her steps quickening as they descended deeper into the citadel. The corridors grew darker, the zän fading into shadow. The walls narrowed, the air thickening with a weight she couldn’t explain. Maya had never been this far below the citadel before, and the unease gnawed at her as the figure ahead slowed, finally pausing before a small alcove carved into the stone. A faint light glowed from within, casting sharp, flickering shadows across the corridor.

<!-- paragraph:1005 para_id:000003ED style:Normal -->
Maya’s chest ached. “Mother?” she whispered, her voice trembling and faint.

<!-- paragraph:1006 para_id:000003EE style:Normal -->
The figure didn’t respond. Maya reached out, her hand shaking as she touched the woman’s shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:1007 para_id:000003EF style:Normal -->
“Mother?”

<!-- paragraph:1008 para_id:000003F0 style:Normal -->
The figure turned.

<!-- paragraph:1009 para_id:000003F1 style:Normal -->
The face was a void, an endless inky blackness that writhed and swirled with unnatural energy. There were no eyes, no mouth—just an empty abyss where a face should have been. The void seemed to stretch toward her, swallowing the dim light around them.

<!-- paragraph:1010 para_id:000003F2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath hitched, her heart pounding as she stumbled back, the sheer wrongness of the figure overwhelming her senses. Her vision blurred, and the weight of the void pressed into her mind.

<!-- paragraph:1011 para_id:000003F3 style:Normal -->
She woke with a start.

<!-- paragraph:1012 para_id:000003F4 style:Normal -->
Her breath came in ragged gasps, her heart slamming against her ribs. The familiar dim glow of her chambers grounded her, though the suffocating unease from the dream clung to her like a second skin. It wasn’t just the fear. This dream had been different. The way the world had shifted around her, the pull of that strange presence—it reminded her of the threads she touched when weaving magic.

<!-- paragraph:1013 para_id:000003F5 style:Normal -->
She sat up, running a hand through her hair, her thoughts racing. Could she retrace the route she had followed in the dream? It was absurd, yet the memory of the path was vivid in her mind, as though etched into her very being.

<!-- paragraph:1014 para_id:000003F6 style:Normal -->
And the presence—that inescapable presence—lingered just on the edge of her awareness.

<!-- paragraph:1015 para_id:000003F7 style:Normal -->
Maya lay in bed, staring up at the dark ceiling of her chamber. The dream still clung to her like a suffocating veil, its images vivid and unsettling. She couldn’t shake the feeling of that faceless figure, the whisper of the void, the oppressive weight of the depths. Her mind circled around the thought of her mother—was it really her? Or was it just her subconscious dredging up buried memories? The more she tried to rationalize it, the less certain she felt.

<!-- paragraph:1016 para_id:000003F8 style:Normal -->
Her chest tightened with frustration, and after what felt like hours of tossing and turning, she admitted defeat. Sleep wasn’t coming. Her thoughts drifted to the path she had followed in the dream, the strange sense of familiarity it carried. Swinging her legs off the bed, she rose and dressed quickly.

<!-- paragraph:1017 para_id:000003F9 style:Normal -->
When she opened the door, the two guards stationed outside snapped to attention, their expressions sharp with concern.

<!-- paragraph:1018 para_id:000003FA style:Normal -->
“Lady Maya,” one of them said, his voice steady but questioning. “Is something wrong? Do you need assistance?”

<!-- paragraph:1019 para_id:000003FB style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated for the briefest moment, then her mind formed an excuse. “I just remembered,” she said evenly, “I need to check the anchor point for one of the wards before tomorrow. It won’t take long.”

<!-- paragraph:1020 para_id:000003FC style:Normal -->
The guards exchanged a glance, their hesitation clear. “We should accompany you,” the other guard said. “Just in case.”

<!-- paragraph:1021 para_id:000003FD style:Normal -->
She resisted the urge to sigh. “The Registry has been reactivated,” she reminded them. <!-- comment-start:57 -->“No assassin could possibly be inside the citadel.”<!-- comment-end:57 --><!-- comment-ref:57 -->

<!-- paragraph:1022 para_id:000003FE style:Normal -->
They still looked uneasy, their duty conflicting with her reasoning. Maya straightened her posture, recalling the way Niall issued orders with unyielding authority. Her voice took on the same sharp edge. “Stay here. That’s an order. I will return shortly.”

<!-- paragraph:1023 para_id:000003FF style:Normal -->
The guards stiffened, and though they didn’t look happy about it, they nodded reluctantly. “Yes, Lady Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:1024 para_id:00000400 style:Normal -->
Maya strode down the corridor, her Shroud flaring faintly to life as unease pricked at her. The passages grew darker as she descended, the familiar glow of the citadel’s magical lights fading. She conjured a small flame to light her way, its flickering glow casting eerie shadows on the ancient stone walls. These tunnels were unlike the well-trod paths of the citadel above—they felt older, forgotten, as though they belonged to a different era entirely.

<!-- paragraph:1025 para_id:00000401 style:Normal -->
The air grew heavier, the silence pressing in on her ears as she followed the route from her dream. The passage darkened with every step, the faint glow of her flame casting erratic shadows on the ancient, weathered walls. Finally, she reached the alcove. It was unmistakable—its shape and position exactly as she had seen it.

<!-- paragraph:1026 para_id:00000402 style:Normal -->
She stepped closer, her breathing shallow, and her Shroud flared brighter as unease prickled at the edges of her mind. Just as her eyes fixed on the alcove, a faint sensation brushed against her consciousness. It wasn’t a sound, but a presence, soft yet undeniable, forming words she didn’t hear so much as feel: I knew you would come.

<!-- paragraph:1027 para_id:00000403 style:Normal -->
Maya froze, her flame trembling in her palm as her eyes darted around the darkened corridor. “Who’s there?” she called out, her voice sharp and demanding, though her pulse raced beneath the surface.

<!-- paragraph:1028 para_id:00000404 style:Normal -->
The whisper didn’t respond, and the silence deepened, almost suffocating in its intensity. Then, a grating noise broke through, sudden and jarring. Stone against stone.

<!-- paragraph:1029 para_id:00000405 style:Normal -->
She whipped around, her flame flaring with the motion, and saw it: a small, square block of stone sliding forward from the alcove wall. Its edges were worn smooth, as though touched by countless hands over the years. The sound ceased as the block settled into place, slightly ajar, revealing a narrow hollow behind it.

<!-- paragraph:1030 para_id:00000406 style:Normal -->
Her heart thundered in her chest as she hesitated, the stillness pressing against her like a weight. Then, with deliberate care, she reached out. The stone moved easily under her fingers, sliding free with an almost unnatural smoothness.

<!-- paragraph:1031 para_id:00000407 style:Normal -->
Inside, nestled in the hollow, was a small leather-bound book. The cover was aged, its corners frayed, but the texture of the leather was surprisingly supple. Her breath hitched as she pulled it free, her fingers trembling slightly as she turned it over in her hands.

<!-- paragraph:1032 para_id:00000408 style:Normal -->
A single inscription adorned the front cover in looping, elegant script: Alara Rezin.

<!-- paragraph:1033 para_id:00000409 style:Normal -->
Her mother.

<!-- paragraph:1034 para_id:0000040A style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the journal, her mind racing. How could this be here? How could the dream have led her to this? She flipped through the pages, but the text within was written in a cipher she didn’t recognize. The symbols and patterns danced across the pages, frustratingly illegible.

<!-- paragraph:1035 para_id:0000040B style:Normal -->
As she tucked the journal securely into her robe, something caught her eye. Imprints in the dust. They were faint but unmistakable—footsteps. And they weren’t hers. Maya froze<!-- comment-start:58 -->, her Shroud flaring instinctively as her eyes darted around the alcove. The steps ended here, at the alcove, and her disturbed path on the way in meant she couldn’t trace where they’d come from.<!-- comment-end:58 --><!-- comment-ref:58 -->

<!-- paragraph:1036 para_id:0000040C style:Normal -->
Her heart pounded as she turned back toward the way she’d come, her senses on high alert. The unease from earlier deepened, her flame casting long, uncertain shadows as she retraced her steps. She kept her Shroud up the entire way back to her room, her tension easing only slightly when she saw the guards still stationed outside her door.

<!-- paragraph:1037 para_id:0000040D style:Normal -->
“Lady Maya?” one of them asked, his eyes flicking to the faint shimmer of her Shroud. “Is everything all right?”

<!-- paragraph:1038 para_id:0000040E style:Normal -->
“Just practicing,” she said quickly, forcing her voice to remain steady. “Good night.” Without waiting for a response, she stepped inside and shut the door firmly behind her.

<!-- paragraph:1039 para_id:0000040F style:Normal -->
The journal felt impossibly heavy in her hands as she sat at her desk, the flickering light of her flame casting uneven shadows across its worn leather cover. She turned it over, tracing the embossed title with her fingers.

<!-- paragraph:1040 para_id:00000410 style:Normal -->
Her mind swirled with unanswered questions. The dream, the whisper, the glowing runes, the stone—it all defied explanation. Had it been her mother’s voice? Or something else entirely? And those footprints…

<!-- paragraph:1041 para_id:00000411 style:Normal -->
She shook her head, trying to focus, and flipped the journal open again. The ciphered text stared back at her, a labyrinth of unfamiliar symbols and patterns that refused to yield their meaning. Maybe Alden…

<!-- paragraph:1042 para_id:00000412 style:Normal -->
Maya closed the journal with a decisive snap and exhaled slowly, willing herself to let the tension ease. Tomorrow would be a long day, and whatever mysteries the j<!-- comment-start:59 -->ournal held could wait a few hours more. She stood, extinguishing the flame with a flick of her wrist, and slid the journal carefully into her chest before turning toward her bed.<!-- comment-end:59 --><!-- comment-ref:59 -->

<!-- paragraph:1043 para_id:00000413 style:Normal -->
As she lay down, her mind wouldn’t quite settle, the strange events of the night flickering at the edges of her thoughts. Still, the faint trace of a plan brought a small measure of comfort. Whatever came next, she would face it.

<!-- paragraph:1044 para_id:00000414 style:Normal -->
Sleep claimed her slowly, the journal’s secrets waiting for the dawn.

<!-- paragraph:1048 para_id:00000418 style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 6

<!-- paragraph:1049 para_id:00000419 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 6

<!-- paragraph:1051 para_id:0000041B style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1053 para_id:0000041D style:Normal -->
The house was cramped and smelled faintly of oil and damp wood, its walls leaning inward as though exhausted by years of neglect. Davian’s eyes adjusted quickly to the gloom as he stepped over the threshold, following Jalen into the narrow main room. The furniture was sparse and mismatched—a low, wobbly table, a battered chair, and a sagging cabinet shoved into the corner. Dust clung to every surface, betraying the fact that Arrek Hadrin had likely lived alone for a long time.

<!-- paragraph:1054 para_id:0000041E style:Normal -->
Garren closed the door softly behind them, positioning himself just outside to keep watch. His sharp, pale eyes flicked back and forth as he scanned the quiet street before vanishing from view.

<!-- paragraph:1055 para_id:0000041F style:Normal -->
Jalen motioned to Davian. “Look sharp. We don’t have much time, and we need to make sure there’s nothing here that could trip you up.”

<!-- paragraph:1056 para_id:00000420 style:Normal -->
The two began searching the room in silence, their footsteps muffled by the threadbare rug covering the uneven floorboards. Davian pulled open a small drawer near the cot, rifling through its contents. A scattering of coins, a whetstone, and a tarnished amulet that might have once been valuable.

<!-- paragraph:1057 para_id:00000421 style:Normal -->
“This guy lived like a rat,” Davian muttered, brushing the dust from his hands. He stepped toward the doorway leading to the next room. “Doesn’t look like there’s much here.”

<!-- paragraph:1058 para_id:00000422 style:Normal -->
Jalen glanced over his shoulder as he poked through a cluttered corner of the main room. “There doesn’t have to be much. All it takes is one detail we miss—one scrap of paper, one name you can’t explain.”

<!-- paragraph:1059 para_id:00000423 style:Normal -->
The next room was little more than a cramped workspace, cluttered with tools and half-finished zän etched into sheets of metal. The air carried a faint metallic tang. Davian frowned at the scattered remnants of Arrek’s trade. Glyphwrighting was meticulous work—an art that required precision, focus, and an intimate understanding of magical theory. He picked up a chisel, its edge dulled from overuse, and turned it over in his hand.

<!-- paragraph:1060 para_id:00000424 style:Normal -->
Jalen entered behind him, his gaze sweeping the room. “You’ll need to learn enough about this to fool the others,” he said, nodding toward the workbench. “It’s not possible to turn you into an expert, but if you can’t even talk about zän without stumbling, someone will notice.”

<!-- paragraph:1061 para_id:00000425 style:Normal -->
Davian grimaced, setting the chisel back down. “I’ll figure it out. I can use my magic if I have to. Subversion should cover for my lack of skill.”

<!-- paragraph:1062 para_id:00000426 style:Normal -->
Jalen rounded on him, his expression sharp. “No. Absolutely not. If anyone sees your sigil when you channel, this whole mission is over.”

<!-- paragraph:1063 para_id:00000427 style:Normal -->
Davian clenched his fists, frustration bubbling to the surface. “I’m so tired of this. Of hiding my magic, of being forced to pretend it’s something shameful. I didn’t choose this, Jalen.”

<!-- paragraph:1064 para_id:00000428 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s face softened, but only slightly. “None of us did. But this is the world we live in. Your magic is a threat to the High Clades because it upends everything they value—raw power, destruction, domination. If they know what you are, they’ll kill you, no questions asked.”

<!-- paragraph:1065 para_id:00000429 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled, tension knotting his shoulders. His father wasn’t wrong. The High Clades had spent centuries twisting magic into a tool for war and control, and the Virellis family’s secret inheritance was a direct rebuke of that ideology. Subversion didn’t create—it dismantled. It wasn’t designed to conquer—it was designed to destroy power itself. And for that, they were hunted.

<!-- paragraph:1066 para_id:0000042A style:Normal -->
Jalen broke the heavy silence. “Search his room. See if there’s anything useful—books, notes, anything that can teach you just enough zän to get by. I’ll handle the rest.”

<!-- paragraph:1067 para_id:0000042B style:Normal -->
Davian nodded mutely and moved toward the door at the back of the house. Arrek’s personal quarters were as shabby as the rest of the home. A narrow bed took up most of the space, flanked by a small nightstand stacked with empty mugs. A single shelf on the wall held a scattering of books, their spines cracked and faded.

<!-- paragraph:1068 para_id:0000042C style:Normal -->
One book, resting haphazardly atop the cluttered shelf, caught Davian’s eye. Its cover was battered and unremarkable, but something about it made him pause. The dust around it was disturbed, marking it as something that was used more frequently. Pulling it down, he flipped it open to find pages filled with cramped, uneven handwriting interspersed with poorly organized columns of numbers.

<!-- paragraph:1069 para_id:0000042D style:Normal -->
A ledger. And a journal, all rolled into one.

<!-- paragraph:1070 para_id:0000042E style:Normal -->
Davian frowned as he scanned the entries. The early pages were mundane: rent payments, expenses for tools, basic food supplies. The handwriting was rough, almost frantic, as if every note had been made in haste. But one column caught his attention. A regular portion of Arrek’s income—substantial sums, considering what zänwrights generally earned—was being sent to someone named Jorra Halvek.

<!-- paragraph:1071 para_id:0000042F style:Normal -->
Davian’s brow furrowed. Jorra wasn’t a name he recognized, and as he pieced together the scattered notes in the margins, a picture began to form. Arrek wasn’t living in this squalor because he couldn’t afford better. He was sending nearly all of his money away.

<!-- paragraph:1072 para_id:00000430 style:Normal -->
Turning the page, Davian came across an entry that stopped him cold: “If I can keep this contract long enough, I’ll have enough to buy out their debt entirely. Maybe then we can all finally breathe. Just a little more time. This is our chance.”

<!-- paragraph:1073 para_id:00000431 style:Normal -->
Davian’s chest tightened as the meaning sank in. Arrek had been trying to free someone—no, two people. Cousins, if the scribbled notes were correct. Debt contracts.

<!-- paragraph:1074 para_id:00000432 style:Normal -->
The words churned in Davian’s mind. Slavery was technically outlawed, but debt contracts might as well have been the same thing. A person’s debts didn’t just vanish when they died; they passed to their next of kin. Compound interest ensured that those caught in the system were almost guaranteed to remain there. For generations.

<!-- paragraph:1075 para_id:00000433 style:Normal -->
The High Clades didn’t care. They were too focused on competition with each other to bother with enforcing laws meant to ensure the common good. Arrek had probably taken the job because the promise of High Clade pay was the only way out for his family.

<!-- paragraph:1076 para_id:00000434 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:60 -->Davian shut the book slowly, his jaw tight. He could almost picture it—Arrek scraping by, the house falling apart around him, while every coin went toward a promise he might never be able to keep. The kind of hope that could crush you, even as you clung to it.

<!-- paragraph:1077 para_id:00000435 style:Normal -->
Davian <!-- comment-end:60 --><!-- comment-ref:60 -->slammed the journal shut, his hands trembling. The image of Selina rose unbidden in his mind—her laughter, her fierce determination, and the hollow ache she left behind. She had believed in the same kind of promise. She’d died for it.

<!-- paragraph:1078 para_id:00000436 style:Normal -->
“Davian,” Jalen’s voice cut through his thoughts, sharp and urgent. He stood in the doorway, his eyes sweeping the room. “What did you find?”

<!-- paragraph:1079 para_id:00000437 style:Normal -->
“Nothing,” Davian muttered, his voice tight.

<!-- paragraph:1080 para_id:00000438 style:Normal -->
Jalen frowned, stepping into the room. “We don’t have time for this. Focus. Anything here could help you pass as him.”

<!-- paragraph:1081 para_id:00000439 style:Normal -->
Davian surged to his feet, the journal dropping to the floor. “You made me kill him!” His voice cracked as he slammed a fist into the wall, the plaster splintering beneath his knuckles. Dust rained down as he turned on his father, his breath coming in ragged gasps. “Do you even care?”

<!-- paragraph:1082 para_id:0000043A style:Normal -->
Jalen’s face hardened, but his eyes flicked briefly to the journal lying open on the floor. A shadow crossed his features, and for a moment, the cold determination wavered.

<!-- paragraph:1083 para_id:0000043B style:Normal -->
“Give me your arm,” Jalen said suddenly, his voice quiet but firm.

<!-- paragraph:1084 para_id:0000043C style:Normal -->
“What?” Davian blinked, caught off guard.

<!-- paragraph:1085 para_id:0000043D style:Normal -->
“Your arm,” Jalen repeated, stepping closer. “I’m giving you the Ink Boon.”

<!-- paragraph:1086 para_id:0000043E style:Normal -->
“No.” Davian shook his head, pulling back instinctively. “You need it. Without it, you can’t protect yourself from a Trial of Ascension.”

<!-- paragraph:1087 para_id:0000043F style:Normal -->
Jalen’s jaw tightened. “And without it, you’ll die the second you face someone like Niall Rezin.”

<!-- paragraph:1088 para_id:00000440 style:Normal -->
Davian flinched at the name, but his father pressed on. “Do you think I wanted any of this? Do you think I wanted to go to the Syndicate?” Jalen’s voice cracked, raw with emotion. “I went to them the day after Selina died because I didn’t have the strength to protect her. Because I couldn’t stop them.” His hands curled into fists, and his gaze bored into Davian. “This might be the only chance we ever get,” Jalen said, his voice gaining steel. “To kill a High Clade magus. To make them finally feel what they’ve made the rest of us live with every day.”

<!-- paragraph:1089 para_id:00000441 style:Normal -->
The room fell silent, the weight of his father’s words pressing down like a storm cloud. For the first time, Davian saw the festering hate etched into Jalen’s face—years of pain and guilt turned into cold fury. And in that moment, he realized he felt it too.

<!-- paragraph:1090 para_id:00000442 style:Normal -->
“I can’t do this alone,” Jalen said, his voice dropping to a near whisper. “If you want to do something for Selina, you kill Niall Rezin. You make the High Clades finally know fear.”

<!-- paragraph:1091 para_id:00000443 style:Normal -->
Davian’s throat tightened. His fingers flexed, the aura etched into his arm a burning reminder of the task ahead. Slowly, he extended his arm.

<!-- paragraph:1092 para_id:00000444 style:Normal -->
Jalen grasped it firmly, his grip unyielding. The Ink Boon on his forearm began to stir, the intricate patterns writhing like living tendrils. Inch by inch, the ink began to flow across his skin, bridging the gap onto Davian’s forearm. The sensation was searing, a mix of heat and cold that made Davian grit his teeth. The patterns settled into place, a stark contrast against his bruised skin.

<!-- paragraph:1093 para_id:00000445 style:Normal -->
Jalen released his grip, his arm now marked only with the faintest remnants of the Boon. “There were four uses left,” he said, his voice steady but hollow. “You’re getting three. That leaves me one—enough for anyone foolish enough to challenge me.”

<!-- paragraph:1094 para_id:00000446 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at the Boon, its intricate patterns now etched into his skin, pulsing faintly with latent power. The weight of it—of everything—pressed down on him like an iron shroud.

<!-- paragraph:1095 para_id:00000447 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s voice cut through the silence, low and sharp. “Make them feel fear.”

<!-- paragraph:1096 para_id:00000448 style:Normal -->
Davian’s fingers curled into a fist, his jaw tightening. The hate burned, cold and hot all at once. “They’ll feel it,” he said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:1097 para_id:00000449 style:Normal -->
From there, the night blurred into a haze of zänwright texts and hurried explanations. Arrek’s cramped living space, dimly lit by flickering lamps, became their makeshift classroom. Davian’s head pounded as he scanned page after page of diagrams, notations, and dense descriptions of magical constructs.

<!-- paragraph:1098 para_id:0000044A style:Normal -->
Jalen’s voice carried through the room, brisk and unrelenting. “Focus on the basic principles. Kelyra and I drilled magical theory into you for a reason. It’s not the education the High or Low Clades would’ve given you, but it’s enough to keep you afloat here.”

<!-- paragraph:1099 para_id:0000044B style:Normal -->
Davian gritted his teeth, tracing the unfamiliar zän with his finger. “You mean it’s barely enough to keep me from failing spectacularly.”

<!-- paragraph:1100 para_id:0000044C style:Normal -->
“Exactly.” Jalen leaned over, tapping a diagram. “That’s why you need to stay under the radar. Don’t volunteer for anything supervised. Keep studying while you’re inside the citadel. Stick to the timeline. It’s all about getting in, getting the Heart, and getting out.”

<!-- paragraph:1101 para_id:0000044D style:Normal -->
“And if I can’t keep up?” Davian muttered, his eyes burning from hours of strain.

<!-- paragraph:1102 para_id:0000044E style:Normal -->
“Then don’t look amazing at your job. Just don’t look so incompetent that they revoke your contract.” Jalen’s tone was sharp but laced with an edge of reassurance. “It’s fine to be invisible. The less attention you draw, the better.”

<!-- paragraph:1103 para_id:0000044F style:Normal -->
By the time the first light of dawn bled through the cracks in the shutters, Davian’s mind buzzed with fragmented knowledge. He felt far from prepared, but Jalen seemed satisfied enough as they gathered what they needed and left the house.

<!-- paragraph:1104 para_id:00000450 style:Normal -->
The cool morning air bit at Davian’s skin as they stepped into the street. Jalen kept speaking, his words sharp and purposeful as he gave final instructions. “Don’t hesitate. Every day you’re in there, they’ll be watching. Use your magic sparingly—if at all. You can’t risk them seeing your sigil.”

<!-- paragraph:1105 para_id:00000451 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, his gaze distant as they walked. He was barely listening now, the weight of what was ahead pressing harder with every step.

<!-- paragraph:1106 para_id:00000452 style:Normal -->
“Keep your focus, Davian,” Jalen said firmly, catching his shoulder. “The moment you falter, this mission’s over. Stay on track.”

<!-- paragraph:1107 para_id:00000453 style:Normal -->
“I know,” Davian muttered, his voice tight.

<!-- paragraph:1108 para_id:00000454 style:Normal -->
They turned a corner, and the shift in their surroundings hit him immediately. The streets here were cleaner, wider, and quieter than those they had passed through earlier. His footsteps slowed as his eyes lifted, taking in the towering spire of the Rezin citadel ahead. The open gates loomed down the road, flanked by guards in pristine armor.

<!-- paragraph:1109 para_id:00000455 style:Normal -->
Garren stepped forward from the shadows, his expression tense. “This is where we part ways,” he said curtly. “We’re too close now. Jalen and I will draw attention if we linger.”

<!-- paragraph:1110 para_id:00000456 style:Normal -->
Jalen nodded, turning to Davian. “This is it. Stay sharp.”

<!-- paragraph:1111 para_id:00000457 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled slowly, his gaze fixed on the citadel gates as his heart thudded in his chest. “I’ll see you on the other side,” he said, his voice steadier than he felt.

<!-- paragraph:1112 para_id:00000458 style:Normal -->
Jalen clapped his shoulder, his grip firm. “Good luck.”

<!-- paragraph:1113 para_id:00000459 style:Normal -->
As the two melted back into the shadows, Davian squared his shoulders, forcing his steps forward. The spire loomed larger with every step, the weight of its presence pressing down on him. He could feel the eyes of the guards at the gates, their expressions unreadable as he approached.

<!-- paragraph:1114 para_id:0000045A style:Normal -->
He gripped the folded zänwright contract taken from Arrek’s house, the edges slightly crumpled. Forcing his expression into what he hoped resembled confidence, he stepped up to the guards stationed by the gates.

<!-- paragraph:1115 para_id:0000045B style:Normal -->
“I’m expected,” he said, his voice steady but not overly assertive. He cleared his throat, adding, “Arrek Hadrin.”

<!-- paragraph:1116 para_id:0000045C style:Normal -->
The guards exchanged a glance. Their polished armor caught the early morning light, a sharp contrast to the worn edges of Davian’s borrowed clothes. One of them nodded and gestured to a smaller gatehouse nearby.

<!-- paragraph:1117 para_id:0000045D style:Normal -->
“Wait here,” the guard said, his tone professional but not unfriendly.

<!-- paragraph:1118 para_id:0000045E style:Normal -->
Moments later, a scribe emerged from the gatehouse, a thin, meticulous figure with ink-stained hands and a faintly exasperated expression. He held a ledger bound in supple leather, the faint glow of a protective zä on its cover catching Davian’s eye.

<!-- paragraph:1119 para_id:0000045F style:Normal -->
“Name?” the scribe asked without looking up, flipping through the pages.

<!-- paragraph:1120 para_id:00000460 style:Normal -->
“Arrek Hadrin,” Davian repeated. He held out the contract, trying to appear nonchalant.

<!-- paragraph:1121 para_id:00000461 style:Normal -->
The scribe’s eyes flicked over the contract briefly before comparing it to the ledger. “Hadrin... yes, here you are. New contractor for the ward overhaul.” He nodded and made a notation in the book, then handed the contract back with an air of dismissal. “Escort him to the main hall.”

<!-- paragraph:1122 para_id:00000462 style:Normal -->
Another guard stepped forward, motioning for Davian to follow. As they walked, the weight of the citadel seemed to press down on him. The polished stone walls, etched with glowing zän, radiated a sense of overwhelming authority.

<!-- paragraph:1123 para_id:00000463 style:Normal -->
“Security’s tight today,” the guard said conversationally, his tone still maintaining a professional edge. “Registry’s off because of you contractors. Don’t wander off. Stay in the main hall until you’ve been added. They’ll process you before the Registry comes back online.”

<!-- paragraph:1124 para_id:00000464 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, keeping his expression neutral. “What happens if someone’s not registered when it’s back on?” he asked, keeping his tone light.

<!-- paragraph:1125 para_id:00000465 style:Normal -->
The guard glanced at him briefly. “Let’s just say you don’t want to be here when it happens. It’s not pleasant.”

<!-- paragraph:1126 para_id:00000466 style:Normal -->
Davian forced a small laugh, as if humoring the warning, while his mind raced. He knew exactly how unpleasant it would be. If his aura wasn’t in the Registry, its active magic would treat him as an intruder—and intruders didn’t leave the citadel alive.

<!-- paragraph:1127 para_id:00000467 style:Normal -->
As they neared the main hall, he tried to take in every detail he could. The citadel was more than just a fortress—it was a living embodiment of the High Clades’ power. Every carved glyph, every flicker of latent energy in the walls, screamed authority and control.

<!-- paragraph:1128 para_id:00000468 style:Normal -->
The guard stopped at a heavy set of doors and pushed one open, motioning for Davian to enter. “Wait here. Someone will process you soon.”

<!-- paragraph:1129 para_id:00000469 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped inside, the air in the hall cooler and tinged with the faint metallic scent of active magic. As the door shut behind him, he exhaled slowly, scanning the room.

<!-- paragraph:1130 para_id:0000046A style:Normal -->
Almost immediately, a young woman approached him, her steps brisk and purposeful. She was about Davian’s age, with close-cropped hair and a sharp, watchful gaze that belied her otherwise easy smile.

<!-- paragraph:1131 para_id:0000046B style:Normal -->
“Arrek Hadrin, right?” the young woman asked, extending a hand. “I’m Alra. I handle processing and adding contractors to the Registry. Glad you made it.”

<!-- paragraph:1132 para_id:0000046C style:Normal -->
Davian shook his hand, nodding. “Nice to meet you.”

<!-- paragraph:1133 para_id:0000046D style:Normal -->
Alra tilted his head slightly, her eyes briefly flicking to the visible bruises on Davian’s forearms and the faint edges of his tattoos. Her smile turned curious. “You’re here as a zänwright? You look more like someone who’d be out fighting than etching.”

<!-- paragraph:1134 para_id:0000046E style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse quickened, but he kept his expression neutral. Thinking quickly, he shrugged. “My family lives in a rough part of the city. Sometimes... fights are inevitable.”

<!-- paragraph:1135 para_id:0000046F style:Normal -->
Alra’s expression shifted, a flicker of disapproval crossing her face. “Well, you’d better make sure none of that follows you into the citadel. We don’t tolerate that kind of trouble here.”

<!-- paragraph:1136 para_id:00000470 style:Normal -->
“Understood,” Davian said, his voice calm but inside cursing himself for having already drawn unwanted attention.

<!-- paragraph:1137 para_id:00000471 style:Normal -->
Alra nodded, then gestured to the small bag Davian carried. “Let’s take a look at what you’ve brought. Standard procedure.”

<!-- paragraph:1138 para_id:00000472 style:Normal -->
Davian placed the bag on a nearby table and opened it, revealing the books on zänwright practice, a zä etching chisel, some spare clothing, and Antithesis, still in the form of a workman’s knife. The blade’s faintly gleaming surface caught Alrik’s attention, as did the small zä etched into it.

<!-- paragraph:1139 para_id:00000473 style:Normal -->
Alra picked up the knife, turning it over in her hands. “What’s this for? Doesn’t look like something a zänwright usually carries.”

<!-- paragraph:1140 para_id:00000474 style:Normal -->
Davian forced a small, easy smile. “It’s for fine modifications in zä work. Sometimes you need something smaller than a chisel for precision adjustments.” He pointed to the zä on the blade. <!-- comment-start:61 -->“That’s a hardening charm, keeps the edge from dulling or chipping.”

<!-- paragraph:1141 para_id:00000475 style:Normal -->
Alra studied the knife for a moment longer, then gave a faint shrug. “Fair enough. I’ve seen stranger tools in this line of work.” <!-- comment-end:61 --><!-- comment-ref:61 -->She placed the knife back in the bag and closed it, then stepped back. “Come on,” she said, her tone brisk. “I’ll take you to the Registry.”

<!-- paragraph:1142 para_id:00000476 style:Normal -->
Davian fell into step behind him, his pulse steady but his thoughts churning. As they wound through the citadel’s hallways, Alra spoke with the casual cadence of someone well-versed in her responsibilities.

<!-- paragraph:1143 para_id:00000477 style:Normal -->
“The Registry is the backbone of security here,” Alra explained. “Your access can be revoked at any time, so it’s important to stay aware of your standing with House Rezin. Entering or exiting the citadel without clearance isn’t just forbidden—it’s dangerous.”

<!-- paragraph:1144 para_id:00000478 style:Normal -->
Davian made a noncommittal sound, his eyes scanning the polished stone walls and the subtle etchings of protective zän that pulsed faintly with residual magic.

<!-- paragraph:1145 para_id:00000479 style:Normal -->
“You’ll need to provide your true name and aura,” Alra continued, glancing back at him. “It’s the only way to ensure that everyone within the citadel is accounted for. It’s precise, foolproof. The High Clades wouldn’t have it any other way.”

<!-- paragraph:1146 para_id:0000047A style:Normal -->
Davian tensed slightly at the mention of his true name, but he kept his expression neutral. “Feels a bit invasive,” he said, letting just enough unease creep into his tone to seem authentic.

<!-- paragraph:1147 para_id:0000047B style:Normal -->
Alra smirked faintly, shaking her head. “You knew what you were signing up for when you took a contract with the High Clades. Comfort, wealth, and security all have their costs. If you can’t handle that, you shouldn’t be here.”

<!-- paragraph:1148 para_id:0000047C style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed back a sharp retort, forcing a thin smile. “Fair point.”

<!-- paragraph:1149 para_id:0000047D style:Normal -->
But inwardly, anger churned in his chest. Comfort, wealth, security—the promises dangled before every contractor, like bait on a hook. He knew better than most how hollow those promises were. The High Clades didn’t offer better lives; they offered control, binding workers to them with the illusion of opportunity. The Registry wasn’t just a safeguard; it was a leash, and Davian had no doubt it would be used to choke him if he so much as stepped out of line.

<!-- paragraph:1150 para_id:0000047E style:Normal -->
As they turned another corner, the towering double doors of the Registry came into view, flanked by two guards whose expressions were as impassive as the carved stone sentinels adorning the entrance. The air seemed heavier here, laden with the weight of countless names and auras bound to this citadel.

<!-- paragraph:1151 para_id:0000047F style:Normal -->
Alra stopped just short of the doors, her hand resting lightly on the latch. She glanced back at Davian. “This is it. Just follow the instructions, and we’ll get you set up.”

<!-- paragraph:1152 para_id:00000480 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:62 -->Davian stepped into the Registry, his gaze sweeping over the room as he mentally noted every detail. The chamber was circular, the stone walls smooth and lined with faintly glowing zän that seemed to pulse in time with his heartbeat. The heavy double doors through which he had just entered marked the only exit. It’ll be tough to get back in here when I need to erase my aura.<!-- comment-end:62 --><!-- comment-ref:62 -->

<!-- paragraph:1153 para_id:00000481 style:Normal -->
At the center of the room stood a sharp-featured older man, flanked by two armored guards. The man’s presence was commanding, his hawkish gaze scanning Davian with an intensity that set his nerves on edge. Davian’s mother, Kelyra, had shown him sketches of the Rezin leadership, and he recognized this man immediately. The spymaster. I’ll have to watch myself around him.

<!-- paragraph:1154 para_id:00000482 style:Normal -->
Before the man, an intricate arcane circle shimmered faintly, its runes weaving a pattern of ethereal light.
