Within it floated a translucent projection of the entire citadel, rendered with breathtaking precision. Every detail, from the soaring spires to the shadowy depths, glowed faintly, the projection shifting subtly as though alive.

<!-- paragraph:1155 para_id:00000483 style:Normal -->
Alden turned toward Davian, his piercing eyes narrowing slightly. “Name?” he asked, his tone clipped.

<!-- paragraph:1156 para_id:00000484 style:Normal -->
“Arrek Hadrin,” Davian replied, keeping his voice steady. Alden consulted a list, then inscribed the name onto a small slip of paper with an elegant flourish. Handing it to Davian, he gestured to the pedestal at the center of the circle.

<!-- paragraph:1157 para_id:00000485 style:Normal -->
“Step forward,” Alden instructed. “Place your hand on the paper and speak your true name.”

<!-- paragraph:1158 para_id:00000486 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped forward, his pulse quickening as he approached the pedestal. His hand settled on the cool surface of the paper, and he forced himself to say the name again, clear and confident. As the words left his lips, the circle around him flared to life.

<!-- paragraph:1159 para_id:00000487 style:Normal -->
The moment the words left his lips, he felt his hand almost fuse to the pedestal. zän flared to life around the room, their light intensifying in a cascade of colors. A faint hum filled the air, growing into a resonant vibration that seemed to seep into his bones. Davian’s arm burned with the heat of the Ink Boon, and he resisted the urge to glance down, knowing any sign of discomfort could draw suspicion.

<!-- paragraph:1160 para_id:00000488 style:Normal -->
The vibration in the room grew sharper, almost grating, before it abruptly ceased. The zän dimmed, and the paper flew from the pedestal in a blur, affixing itself to the wall. It ignited in a flash, leaving the <!-- comment-start:63 --><!-- comment-start:64 -->name Arrek Hadrin etched into the stone, glowing faintly.

<!-- paragraph:1161 para_id:00000489 style:Normal -->
In the projection of the citadel, the image shifted, zooming in with unsettling precision. The room they stood in appeared, and within it, a small mote of light labeled Arrek Hadrin flickered into existence. <!-- comment-end:63 --><!-- comment-ref:63 --><!-- comment-end:64 --><!-- comment-ref:64 -->Davian’s stomach churned as he took in the scene. The level of control and surveillance was suffocating, an embodiment of everything he hated about the High Clades.

<!-- paragraph:1162 para_id:0000048A style:Normal -->
“That took a little longer than usual,” Alden said, his voice tinged with curiosity as he gave Davian a searching look. For a moment, Davian’s breath caught, the intensity of Alden’s gaze unsettling.

<!-- paragraph:1163 para_id:0000048B style:Normal -->
Before Alden could press further, the door opened, and the next contractor stepped in. Alra appeared at Davian’s side, motioning for him to leave. “You’re done,” she said briskly. “This way.”

<!-- paragraph:1164 para_id:0000048C style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, forcing himself to walk at a measured pace. Every instinct told him to get out of the room as quickly as possible, but he kept his steps steady, his expression neutral. As he exited the Registry, the faint hum of the zän still echoed in his ears.

<!-- paragraph:1165 para_id:0000048D style:Normal -->
Alra led Davian through another set of winding corridors until they emerged into a large meeting hall. The space was buzzing with activity—contractors, some dressed in plain work clothes and others in more formal attire, milled about in clusters. The faint murmur of conversation filled the air, punctuated by occasional laughter or the clink of tools. Guards stood stationed at every entrance, their sharp eyes scanning the room.

<!-- paragraph:1166 para_id:0000048E style:Normal -->
“You’ll need to wait here until the rest of the contractors have been Registered,” Alra said briskly. “Once that’s done, you’ll receive your instructions.” Without another word, she turned and strode away, leaving Davian to fend for himself.

<!-- paragraph:1167 para_id:0000048F style:Normal -->
He leaned against a wall, keeping to the edges of the room, and crossed his arms in what he hoped looked like casual disinterest. He had no intention of engaging with anyone. His mind churned with thoughts of the Registry, its invasive power still fresh in his memory. It was clear the arcane mechanisms behind it were incredibly advanced, far beyond anything his family—or even Low Clade magi—could manage. And yet, somehow, the Syndicate had magic capable of fooling it, at least temporarily.

<!-- paragraph:1168 para_id:00000490 style:Normal -->
Davian’s brow furrowed. Jalen had said they had nothing to worry about, but Davian wasn’t so sure. He hated when things didn’t add up. How could the Syndicate have access to magic powerful enough to deceive the Registry? And why did they even want the Heart?

<!-- paragraph:1169 para_id:00000491 style:Normal -->
He was drawn out of his musings by a voice. “Hi! I don’t think we’ve met yet.”

<!-- paragraph:1170 para_id:00000492 style:Normal -->
Davian looked up to see a young woman standing in front of him. She was perhaps a little younger than him, with bright eyes and a wide, eager smile that contrasted sharply with the grim determination most of the other contractors seemed to carry.

<!-- paragraph:1171 para_id:00000493 style:Normal -->
She extended a hand. “I’m Tanelle. And you are?”

<!-- paragraph:1172 para_id:00000494 style:Normal -->
“Arrek,” Davian said after a brief pause, shaking her hand and forcing a polite smile.

<!-- paragraph:1173 para_id:00000495 style:Normal -->
“What do you do?” she asked, her tone brimming with enthusiasm.

<!-- paragraph:1174 para_id:00000496 style:Normal -->
“I’m a zänwright,” he said simply, his voice as neutral as he could make it.

<!-- paragraph:1175 para_id:00000497 style:Normal -->
Her face lit up. “Really? Same here! Isn’t this amazing? I can’t believe I landed a contract with the Rezins. I’ve heard the zänwrights are going to get to work for the new Rezin heir. Not directly, of course, but even so—we might actually get to see a High Clade magus! Can you imagine?”

<!-- paragraph:1176 para_id:00000498 style:Normal -->
Davian forced himself not to grimace. Her excitement grated on him, her admiration for the High Clades so casual, so unthinking. He couldn’t keep himself from asking, “Why? They’re human, just like the rest of us.”

<!-- paragraph:1177 para_id:00000499 style:Normal -->
She laughed, the sound light and airy, and completely sincere. “Good one,” she said, clearly believing he was joking.

<!-- paragraph:1178 para_id:0000049A style:Normal -->
Davian clenched his jaw, biting back a retort. He could barely keep himself from rolling his eyes. You’re betraying everyone else by not just working for the High Clades, but worshipping them, he thought bitterly. He was about to excuse himself when a sharp voice cut through the murmur of the crowd.

<!-- paragraph:1179 para_id:0000049B style:Normal -->
“Attention!” one of the guards called out, his tone commanding. The noise in the room fell silent almost immediately. “You are about to be honored by an audience with Lady Maya, the Rezin heir.”

<!-- paragraph:1180 para_id:0000049C style:Normal -->
A ripple of excitement swept through the crowd. Tanelle gasped audibly, her hands flying to her mouth. She turned to Davian, her eyes wide. “I didn’t think we’d get to see her so soon,” she whispered, almost reverent.

<!-- paragraph:1181 para_id:0000049D style:Normal -->
Davian’s gaze lifted as movement at the edge of the room caught his attention. A woman ascended the dais with measured grace, her presence alone commanding silence among the crowd. She was striking—long, dark hair braided neatly over one shoulder, her piercing eyes sweeping over the gathered contractors with a sharpness that seemed to pierce through them all. Her arms were bare, revealing the faint shimmer of a Shroud that flickered faintly, as if she needed to remind anyone of the power that surrounded her like a second skin.

<!-- paragraph:1182 para_id:0000049E style:Normal -->
Her attire struck a balance between elegance and readiness for battle. She wore an ornate tabard, the intricate embroidery gleaming faintly with glyphic etchings that whispered of arcane strength. Below it, tailored leggings afforded her unrestrained movement, the kind that spoke of preparation for conflict—though her stance made it clear she wouldn’t need to lift a hand in a fight. Her magic, overwhelming and undeniable, would do the work for her. She didn’t have to say it; her very presence declared it.

<!-- paragraph:1183 para_id:0000049F style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:65 -->Davian swallowed hard, his chest tightening despite himself. She was beautiful, but it wasn’t a comforting beauty. It was sharp and dangerous, like the jeweled rock snakes in the deserts outside Elarion, their glittering scales a lure before they struck. Everything about her—her posture, her gaze, the faint tilt of her chin—radiated control. She stood like a predator among prey, her gaze scanning the room as if she owned every soul in it.

<!-- paragraph:1184 para_id:000004A0 style:Normal -->
Davian’s fists clenched at his sides, his nails digging into his palms. She did think she owned them, didn’t she? The look in her eyes, the faint tilt of her chin—it all screamed of dominance, of command. His jaw tightened as he resisted the urge to look away, forcing himself to meet her gaze when it flicked briefly over him. He couldn’t afford to falter, even as the weight of her presence pressed against his resolve like an unseen hand.<!-- comment-end:65 --><!-- comment-ref:65 -->

<!-- paragraph:1185 para_id:000004A1 style:Normal -->
Maya’s voice filled the hall, clear and commanding, each word resonating with practiced precision. “House Rezin thanks you all for your service,” she began. “You are here to undertake a complete overhaul of the citadel’s inner wards, which are reaching the end of their cycle. Routine maintenance can no longer keep them at full strength.”

<!-- paragraph:1186 para_id:000004A2 style:Normal -->
Davian’s mind raced at the implications of her words. Decades old, he thought. For the High Clades to let anything reach that point meant they must have been preoccupied with something else - likely cultivating Maya’s power in secret until she could reach her Presentation.

<!-- paragraph:1187 para_id:000004A3 style:Normal -->
“This is an enormous project,” Maya continued, her piercing gaze sweeping across the room. “To ensure its success, you will be divided into groups, each reporting to a designated supervisor. Those supervisors, in turn, will report directly to me.”

<!-- paragraph:1188 para_id:000004A4 style:Normal -->
Her tone allowed no room for questions or dissent. As she began announcing the names of the supervisors, one by one they approached the dais, bowing briefly before receiving their assignments. The process was methodical, almost clinical, her efficiency keeping the room in silent awe.

<!-- paragraph:1189 para_id:000004A5 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:66 -->The fourth supervisor stepped forward, a man in plain but well-kept attire. His demeanor seemed calm, unremarkable—until a blade gleamed suddenly in his hand.<!-- comment-end:66 --><!-- comment-ref:66 -->

<!-- paragraph:1190 para_id:000004A6 style:Normal -->
Davian barely registered the movement before the man lunged, his strike aimed directly at Maya’s heart.

<!-- paragraph:1191 para_id:000004A7 style:Normal -->
The air seemed to hold its breath as Maya, her expression unchanging, shifted one step back. Her Shroud erupted to life in an instant, its icy brilliance swallowing the attacker whole. Frost crackled across his body, encasing him entirely. The room fell into a stunned silence as the man’s frozen form toppled forward, shattering into countless crystalline fragments against the stone floor.

<!-- paragraph:1192 para_id:000004A8 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t flinch. Her voice was as steady as it had been moments before. “Seal the room,” she commanded the guards, her tone cutting through the shock like a blade. “Summon Alden.”

<!-- paragraph:1193 para_id:000004A9 style:Normal -->
The guards snapped into action, their movements precise and hurried, but Maya’s calm was unshaken. Her gaze swept over the gathered contractors, her expression unreadable. “This project is temporarily on hold,” she announced, her voice devoid of any hint of emotion. Without another word, she turned and exited the hall, leaving behind only the sound of the guards locking the doors.

<!-- paragraph:1194 para_id:000004AA style:Normal -->
Davian’s stomach churned as he stared at the icy shards scattered across the ground. She had killed the man as if it were nothing—without a flicker of hesitation or remorse. This is what the High Clades think of us. To them, we’re nothing more than cattle. Disposable. He thought of Selina, of how she had been used up and discarded like so many others. And for what? The false promise of a better life under their rule.

<!-- paragraph:1195 para_id:000004AB style:Normal -->
One day, they’ll pay, he vowed. Every single one of them.

<!-- paragraph:1199 para_id:000004AF style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 7

<!-- paragraph:1200 para_id:000004B0 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 7

<!-- paragraph:1202 para_id:000004B2 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1205 para_id:000004B5 style:Normal -->
Maya’s steps echoed sharply in the corridor as she left the meeting room, each measured and precise. The calm radiating from her was deliberate, calculated—a shield against the chaos she had just left behind. Her face remained serene, her posture unshaken, but the tension in her body was a taut thread ready to snap. The guards flanking her doorway stiffened to attention as she approached, but she waved them off with a flick of her fingers.

<!-- paragraph:1206 para_id:000004B6 style:Normal -->
“Wait here,” she instructed, her tone leaving no room for argument. “No one comes in unless it’s <!-- comment-start:67 -->Alden.”<!-- comment-end:67 --><!-- comment-ref:67 -->

<!-- paragraph:1207 para_id:000004B7 style:Normal -->
“Yes, Lady Maya,” one guard said, though his voice carried a hint of hesitation. She didn’t wait to see if they would question her further, stepping into the small, private study and shutting the door firmly behind her.

<!-- paragraph:1208 para_id:000004B8 style:Normal -->
The moment the latch clicked into place, the composure shattered. Maya staggered to the nearest chair, collapsing into it as though her strength had drained away in an instant. Her chest heaved with uneven breaths, her heart hammering against her ribs. She pressed a hand to her forehead, her fingers trembling faintly.

<!-- paragraph:1209 para_id:000004B9 style:Normal -->
A man had just tried to kill her.

<!-- paragraph:1210 para_id:000004BA style:Normal -->
The memory replayed unbidden in her mind: the sudden glint of the blade, the lunge, the flare of her Shroud responding instinctively. And then—ice. The man frozen mid-strike, his expression etched in shock and rage as he toppled forward, shattering into nothing. The sound of his body breaking against the stone floor rang in her ears, sharper than any weapon.

<!-- paragraph:1211 para_id:000004BB style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:68 --><!-- comment-start:69 --><!-- comment-start:70 -->On the battlefield, it had been different. The chaos of war dulled the edges, wrapped her actions in a veil of necessity. She hadn’t looked into the eyes of every soldier she’d struck down; she’d been too focused on protecting her side. But this...

<!-- paragraph:1212 para_id:000004BC style:Normal -->
This had been personal. She had been standing just feet away, close enough to see the light leave his eyes.<!-- comment-end:68 --><!-- comment-ref:68 --><!-- comment-end:69 --><!-- comment-ref:69 --><!-- comment-end:70 --><!-- comment-ref:70 -->

<!-- paragraph:1213 para_id:000004BD style:Normal -->
Her breathing quickened, her vision narrowing as the walls of the room seemed to press in on her. She didn’t even realize her nails were digging into the arms of the chair until the sharp bite of pain jolted her back to herself. How long had she been sitting there, caught in that memory?

<!-- paragraph:1214 para_id:000004BE style:Normal -->
A faint noise from outside—the soft tread of boots on stone—pulled her sharply back to the present. Maya straightened in the chair, her back snapping into its usual elegant posture. Her Shroud flickered faintly to life, a reflexive armor against the vulnerability she had just displayed. The icy mask of composure slid back into place, cold and perfect.

<!-- paragraph:1215 para_id:000004BF style:Normal -->
The door opened, and Alden stepped in, his sharp, hawkish features as unreadable as ever. He closed the door behind him with deliberate care, his movements measured. His piercing gaze swept over her, taking in every detail with unnerving precision. For a moment, the silence hung heavy between them, and Maya met his scrutiny with a steady gaze of her own.

<!-- paragraph:1216 para_id:000004C0 style:Normal -->
Maya broke the silence first, her voice sharp and demanding, though a faint tremor threatened to creep into her tone. “How could this happen? All of the contractors were vetted by the Sorevins. They’re Low Clade; their reputation isn’t in question.”

<!-- paragraph:1217 para_id:000004C1 style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression was unreadable, but his voice carried a note of quiet steel. “The Sorevins did their part. The man who attacked you wasn’t their mistake. He wasn’t Registered yet. That means someone working directly for House Rezin—someone within this citadel—was bribed to let him bypass the initial screening.”

<!-- paragraph:1218 para_id:000004C2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s lips thinned, her chest tightening. “Bribed?” she said sharply. “How is that possible? The guards, the staff—everyone knows what’s at stake.”

<!-- paragraph:1219 para_id:000004C3 style:Normal -->
“Yet it happened,” Alden replied, his tone as steady as his gaze. “And not just anyone could have done this. The assassin entered under a false name, taking the identity of an existing contractor—one approved by the Sorevins. That man was likely killed before reaching the citadel.”

<!-- paragraph:1220 para_id:000004C4 style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach churned, the weight of the situation pressing harder. “How did someone even get close enough to take his place? How could they enter the citadel under our watch?”

<!-- paragraph:1221 para_id:000004C5 style:Normal -->
“The name he used wasn’t just plucked from thin air,” Alden explained, his eyes narrowing slightly. “I’ve already begun piecing it together. This morning I was notified that the Sorevin manor was attacked two nights ago - the same night that we were attacked. Whoever orchestrated this used the chaos to seize their records—lists of approved contractors. With that information, they could target individuals, replace them, and plant an assassin right here under our noses.”

<!-- paragraph:1222 para_id:000004C6 style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath caught. “What happened to the Sorevins?” she asked, her voice hushed but trembling with disbelief.

<!-- paragraph:1223 para_id:000004C7 style:Normal -->
Alden gave a grim nod. “Wiped out. They were targeted for this, I have no doubt. The timing and the precision make it clear. But the records they stole were only useful for a short window of time. Anyone who’s been added to the Registry has had their aura verified. The magic doesn’t lie. Whoever was responsible for this attack has no more opportunities to infiltrate.”

<!-- paragraph:1224 para_id:000004C8 style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled slowly, the logic clicking into place but offering little comfort. “You’re sure of that?” she asked, her voice quieter now.

<!-- paragraph:1225 para_id:000004C9 style:Normal -->
Alden inclined his head. “Absolutely. Anyone who’s already Registered is who they claim to be. They’ve been fully vetted. This man was the last chance for whoever sent him.”

<!-- paragraph:1226 para_id:000004CA style:Normal -->
Maya leaned forward, her fingers tightening on the armrests. “There’s just one piece missing. How could they have killed the Sorevin head magus? He was known to be deadly—at least for a Low Clade.”

<!-- paragraph:1227 para_id:000004CB style:Normal -->
Alden’s mouth pressed into a thin line, his eyes darkening. “That’s the question, isn’t it? This isn’t the first Low Clade assassination of its kind. There’s a pattern here. Head magi from several Low Clades have been targeted over the past year. Always quick, clean, and without leaving much behind. No one knows who’s behind it, but...” He paused, his tone dropping. “If I had to guess, I’d say it’s the work of one of the High Clades. Someone is subverting the Low Clades for their own political gain.”

<!-- paragraph:1228 para_id:000004CC style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach twisted. The idea of such calculated violence wasn’t surprising, but the scale of it was unnerving. “Do you know who? Or why?”

<!-- paragraph:1229 para_id:000004CD style:Normal -->
Alden shook his head, his hawkish gaze sharpening. “Not yet. But I’m working on it. Whoever it is, they’re careful. And they’ve left just enough plausible deniability to keep suspicion vague.”

<!-- paragraph:1230 para_id:000004CE style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled slowly, the pieces of the puzzle swirling in her mind. She needed more answers.

<!-- paragraph:1231 para_id:000004CF style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression shifted, and his tone turned sardonic. “Of course, the public has their own theories. They’ve taken to calling this assassin ‘the Shade.’”

<!-- paragraph:1232 para_id:000004D0 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “The Shade?”

<!-- paragraph:1233 para_id:000004D1 style:Normal -->
Alden rolled his eyes. “The bogeyman, essentially. A specter that creeps in the dark, picking off magi one by one. The stories are growing more elaborate by the day. Some say this Shade is a ghost; others, that they’re wielding forbidden magics that no one’s seen in generations. It’s laughable.” He waved a dismissive hand. “The common folk will believe anything that paints the Clades in a bad light.”

<!-- paragraph:1234 para_id:000004D2 style:Normal -->
Maya straightened in her chair, her tone sharpening. “Whether or not the Shade is real, the assassin today certainly was. And the list of people who could have been bribed to let him skip Registration must be short.”

<!-- paragraph:1235 para_id:000004D3 style:Normal -->
Alden nodded, his hawkish eyes narrowing. “It is. I already have my men working on it. The exits from the citadel are sealed, and every remaining contractor is being verified against the Registry. No one leaves until we’re certain.”

<!-- paragraph:1236 para_id:000004D4 style:Normal -->
Before Maya could respond, a sharp knock sounded at the door. Alden moved swiftly to answer it, opening the door just wide enough to speak to whoever stood outside. A guard leaned in, murmuring a quick report. Alden gave a curt nod and closed the door, turning back to Maya.

<!-- paragraph:1237 para_id:000004D5 style:Normal -->
“The verification is complete,” he said, his voice steady. “Every contractor now in the citadel has had their aura verified against the Registry. There are no further anomalies.”

<!-- paragraph:1238 para_id:000004D6 style:Normal -->
Maya rose smoothly, adjusting her tabard as she regained the regal composure expected of her. “Good. Then I’m going back. Whoever planned this can’t be allowed to think they’ve scared me.”

<!-- paragraph:1239 para_id:000004D7 style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression flickered, a hint of approval crossing his otherwise inscrutable face. “Wise,” he said, his voice carrying a note of genuine respect.

<!-- paragraph:1240 para_id:000004D8 style:Normal -->
Without waiting for his guidance, Maya stepped toward the door, her posture rigid with determination. Alden fell into step behind her as they left the study, his presence a silent reassurance of her safety. The walk back to the meeting room was brisk, guards saluting as they passed, their movements sharper than usual in the aftermath of the attack.

<!-- paragraph:1241 para_id:000004D9 style:Normal -->
Maya pushed open the doors to the meeting hall herself, stepping inside with an unshaken air of command. The contractors, still gathered and murmuring anxiously, turned their attention to her at once. The room stilled, the weight of her presence falling over them like a drawn bowstring.

<!-- paragraph:1242 para_id:000004DA style:Normal -->
Maya’s gaze swept the dais as she reentered the meeting hall. The frozen remains of the would-be assassin had already been cleared away, leaving the polished stone floor spotless once more. She felt certain Alden had ordered the fragments taken somewhere for further analysis; he would leave no detail unchecked.

<!-- paragraph:1243 para_id:000004DB style:Normal -->
Facing the contractors, she let her voice cut cleanly through the heavy silence. “For the time being, any members of the group that no longer has a supervisor will report to the supervisor of Group One.” She turned her sharp gaze to the man leading that group, her tone unwavering. “Congratulations. You’ve been promoted. I expect plans for an adjusted work schedule and assignments delivered to my office by the end of the day.”

<!-- paragraph:1244 para_id:000004DC style:Normal -->
The man straightened, his face briefly flickering with both pride and trepidation. “Yes, Lady Maya,” he said, bowing his head.

<!-- paragraph:1245 para_id:000004DD style:Normal -->
Maya nodded once, then turned and strode from the room, her steps measured and deliberate. The faint hum of conversation resumed behind her as the tension began to ease, but she didn’t look back. Alden followed a step behind, his presence silent yet ever-watchful.

<!-- paragraph:1246 para_id:000004DE style:Normal -->
The walk to her office was longer than the journey to the small study she had used earlier. As Maya approached, the subtle shift in the atmosphere became apparent, a reflection of the grandeur that surrounded her. Her office was located in the heart of the Rezin citadel, a space designed as much for appearances as for functionality.

<!-- paragraph:1247 para_id:000004DF style:Normal -->
The doors to her office opened smoothly, and Maya stepped inside, momentarily pausing to take in the opulence of the room. The high, arched ceiling bore intricate carvings that told the storied history of the Rezin lineage, their triumphs and dominion etched in stone. Heavy velvet drapes framed tall windows that let in slanted rays of golden light, casting warm patterns across the polished black marble floor. A large desk of dark mahogany sat in the center, its surface immaculate save for a single sheaf of papers arranged neatly atop it.

<!-- paragraph:1248 para_id:000004E0 style:Normal -->
Behind the desk stood a chair that was more akin to a throne, its high back adorned with gilded edges and soft, deep-blue upholstery that matched the Rezin house colors. The walls were lined with shelves holding tomes bound in fine leather, glowing softly with protective zän. Ornate sconces, shaped like twisting vines of iron and silver, cast an ambient glow that seemed almost alive.

<!-- paragraph:1249 para_id:000004E1 style:Normal -->
Maya walked to the desk, her hand brushing over the cool surface as she rounded it. The room was a statement—a reminder of the power she represented. She lowered herself into the throne-like chair, her back straight and her expression composed. But as the door clicked shut behind her, sealing her in solitude, the faintest trace of a sigh escaped her lips. She was just beginning to collect her thoughts when the door burst open with a rush of motion.

<!-- paragraph:1250 para_id:000004E2 style:Normal -->
“Maya!” Erynn’s voice was breathless, high with worry as she hurried into the room. Her wide, anxious eyes scanned Maya from head to toe. “I heard what happened! Are you okay? Are you hurt?”

<!-- paragraph:1251 para_id:000004E3 style:Normal -->
Before Maya could respond, Erynn crossed the room in quick strides and threw her arms around her. The sudden embrace caught Maya off guard, and for a moment, she stiffened, her instincts screaming at her to maintain composure. But then, Erynn’s warmth and genuine concern broke through the cracks of her guarded exterior.

<!-- paragraph:1252 para_id:000004E4 style:Normal -->
Maya let herself collapse into the hug, just for a moment. Her head rested lightly against Erynn’s shoulder, the weight of the day momentarily lifting as she drew strength from the simple comfort of her friend’s presence. “I’m okay,” she murmured, her voice quieter than she intended. “I’m fine.”

<!-- paragraph:1253 para_id:000004E5 style:Normal -->
Erynn pulled back just enough to look at her, her hands still resting on Maya’s shoulders. “You don’t look fine,” she said, her brow furrowing. “I tried to come see you earlier, but the guards wouldn’t let me through. They said you’d only allowed Alden. I was so worried.”

<!-- paragraph:1254 para_id:000004E6 style:Normal -->
Maya managed a faint smile, though her heart ached with gratitude. “It’s all right. Alden and I had to deal with... logistics. But I’m glad you’re here now.”

<!-- paragraph:1255 para_id:000004E7 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s expression softened, her grip on Maya’s shoulders tightening briefly before she wrapped her arms around her again. Maya allowed herself one last moment of vulnerability, letting the warmth of her friend’s presence anchor her.

<!-- paragraph:1256 para_id:000004E8 style:Normal -->
Then, the sound of footsteps echoed faintly outside the door.

<!-- paragraph:1257 para_id:000004E9 style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath hitched, the noise dragging her back to reality. She straightened in her chair, her expression shifting instantly back to its familiar, icy calm. Erynn seemed to sense the change and let go, stepping back to stand at Maya’s shoulder, her face composed but her concern still evident.

<!-- paragraph:1258 para_id:000004EA style:Normal -->
The door swung open, and Niall entered with his usual commanding presence, his gaze immediately finding Maya. His expression was hard, his jaw set tightly, and his eyes carried that same dead, unfeeling look she had seen before. But his words, when they came, were calm and measured.

<!-- paragraph:1259 para_id:000004EB style:Normal -->
“I heard you did well,” he said, his voice low but steady. “I’m proud of your ability to defend yourself and remain composed under pressure.”

<!-- paragraph:1260 para_id:000004EC style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth to respond, but his gaze sharpened, and he continued, his tone taking on a hard edge. “Whoever planned this attempt gravely underestimated the Rezins. That was their first mistake, and it will be their last. Now, tell me everything that happened, in detail. We need to know if there’s any information to be gained.”

<!-- paragraph:1261 para_id:000004ED style:Normal -->
The bluntness of his demand hit her like a blow, but Maya swallowed the lump in her throat and nodded. She began recounting the events, forcing herself to keep her voice steady. “He came forward when his name was called. He didn’t look any different from the others… but then, he drew a blade.”

<!-- paragraph:1262 para_id:000004EE style:Normal -->
Her breathing quickened as the memory unfurled, sharp and vivid. The flash of the weapon, the split-second decision to flare her Shroud, the man’s frozen body shattering before her. Her chest felt tight, her vision narrowing. She had been calm in the moment, but now, under Niall’s scrutiny, the weight of it threatened to crush her.

<!-- paragraph:1263 para_id:000004EF style:Normal -->
She faltered, her voice catching. Her breaths became shallow, and she stared at the floor, trying to collect herself, but the panic was rising too quickly.

<!-- paragraph:1264 para_id:000004F0 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s voice cut through like a whip. “Can’t you see she needs time to process what just happened?” she snapped, stepping forward with her hands clenched into fists. “You’re pushing her too hard.”

<!-- paragraph:1265 para_id:000004F1 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head, her voice faint. “No, it’s okay. I can do it.”

<!-- paragraph:1266 para_id:000004F2 style:Normal -->
Niall turned to Erynn, his expression briefly flickering with irritation, but then he turned back to Maya. For a moment, the hard lines of his face softened. He stepped forward and knelt beside her, his hand resting lightly on the arm of her chair.

<!-- paragraph:1267 para_id:000004F3 style:Normal -->
“I’m sorry,” he said quietly, his voice barely above a whisper. “I put these burdens on you because I believe you are capable of bearing them. Perhaps more than anyone.”

<!-- paragraph:1268 para_id:000004F4 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at him, her throat tightening with anger and something else—something raw and unguarded. “You always treat me like a problem to be solved,” she said, her voice trembling with emotion. “But I’m your daughter.”

<!-- paragraph:1269 para_id:000004F5 style:Normal -->
Niall’s mouth opened, but no words came. His gaze wavered, the icy mask cracking for the briefest moment. He drew back slightly, as if unsure how to respond.

<!-- paragraph:1270 para_id:000004F6 style:Normal -->
A knock at the door broke the heavy silence.

<!-- paragraph:1271 para_id:000004F7 style:Normal -->
“Come in,” Niall said, his voice regaining its sharp edge.

<!-- paragraph:1272 para_id:000004F8 style:Normal -->
The door opened, and Alden stepped in, his expression as composed as ever. His sharp eyes flicked between Niall, Maya, and Erynn, clearly taking in the charged atmosphere.

<!-- paragraph:1273 para_id:000004F9 style:Normal -->
Niall straightened, his focus shifting entirely. “What have you found?” he asked, his voice clipped.

<!-- paragraph:1274 para_id:000004FA style:Normal -->
Alden inclined his head slightly. “The guards caught Alra, the scribe, attempting to leave the citadel with a satchel of possessions. She’s been detained.”

<!-- paragraph:1275 para_id:000004FB style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes narrowed, the dead look settling over them once more. “Bring her in,” he commanded, his tone as cold and unyielding as steel.

<!-- paragraph:1276 para_id:000004FC style:Normal -->
The heavy doors swung open, and Alra was dragged in, her hands bound behind her back. Her once-confident demeanor had crumbled, her face pale and drawn, but defiance still burned in her eyes. The guards forced her to her knees in the center of the room, their weapons ready, standing as silent sentinels behind her.

<!-- paragraph:1277 para_id:000004FD style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:71 -->Niall stood towering over her, his expression as cold and unyielding as the stone walls of the citadel. The faint hum of crackling energy radiated from his clenched fists, tiny arcs of lightning snapping through the air. He stared down at her, his voice like a blade as he spoke. “Why would you betray us, Alra? You were well-paid, you had a position of safety and privilege. And this is how you repay the trust House Rezin gave you?”<!-- comment-end:71 --><!-- comment-ref:71 -->

<!-- paragraph:1278 para_id:000004FE style:Normal -->
Alra raised her head, her lip curling with disdain. “Safety?” she spat, the word heavy with venom. “My brother died defending your wall, fighting your war, when you weren’t even there. If Maya had come out sooner instead of cowering in her chambers, he’d still be alive. But no, you wanted to keep her hidden, safe, precious—safe until her big Presentation.” Her voice cracked, raw with grief and fury. “And for that, my brother had to die. So go ahead, do your worst. I’ll never talk.”

<!-- paragraph:1279 para_id:000004FF style:Normal -->
The crackling around Niall’s fists intensified, and Maya saw his eyes go cold, the same dead look she had come to recognize. “I believe you,” he said quietly, his voice chilling. His hands rose, electricity sparking dangerously.

<!-- paragraph:1280 para_id:00000500 style:Normal -->
“Stop!” Maya’s voice rang out, clear and sharp, cutting through the tension like a blade. She stepped forward, her Shroud flickering faintly to life as she approached. The guards stiffened, their hands tightening on their weapons, their eyes locked on Alra’s every movement. Maya walked around her, crouching so that she was at eye level with the kneeling woman.

<!-- paragraph:1281 para_id:00000501 style:Normal -->
“I’m sorry,” Maya said, her voice soft but steady. “I wish I could have saved your brother.”

<!-- paragraph:1282 para_id:00000502 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:72 --><!-- comment-start:73 -->Alra’s head snapped up, and she spat on the ground between them. “I don’t want your sympathy,” she hissed. “Don’t pretend you care.”<!-- comment-end:72 --><!-- comment-ref:72 --><!-- comment-end:73 --><!-- comment-ref:73 -->

<!-- paragraph:1283 para_id:00000503 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at her for a moment, then lifted her gaze to Niall. He was watching Alra as though she were already dead, his hands still brimming with destructive power.

<!-- paragraph:1284 para_id:00000504 style:Normal -->
Maya stood, squaring her shoulders as she addressed him. “Let her live,” she said firmly, her voice leaving no room for argument. “Remove her name from the Registry and cast her out of the citadel.”

<!-- paragraph:1285 para_id:00000505 style:Normal -->
Niall’s jaw tightened, his cold eyes narrowing as he took a step toward her. “Maya—” he began, his tone heavy with protest.

<!-- paragraph:1286 para_id:00000506 style:Normal -->
She cut him off, her voice sharp. “Letting her live sends a stronger message than killing her. It shows that she’s nothing. No threat at all.” Her words hung in the air, the finality in her tone daring him to challenge her judgment.

<!-- paragraph:1287 para_id:00000507 style:Normal -->
Niall’s fists slowly unclenched, the crackling energy dissipating as his expression darkened. He gave a curt nod to the guards. “Remove her,” he ordered.

<!-- paragraph:1288 para_id:00000508 style:Normal -->
The guards seized Alra, dragging her toward the doors. She struggled, her voice rising in a furious snarl. “Bitch!” she screamed at Maya. “You’re too much of a coward to kill me yourself!”

<!-- paragraph:1289 para_id:00000509 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t flinch, standing tall as Alra’s cries faded into the distance. She turned to Niall, their eyes meeting. His expression was inscrutable, but for a moment, Maya thought she saw the faintest flicker of uncertainty behind his cold gaze.

<!-- paragraph:1290 para_id:0000050A style:Normal -->
Niall held her gaze for a beat longer, then turned on his heel, his cloak billowing slightly as he strode toward the door. “You’ve made your choice,” he said, his voice clipped. “Let’s hope it doesn’t come back to haunt you.”

<!-- paragraph:1291 para_id:0000050B style:Normal -->
As the guards led Alra away and Erynn followed reluctantly at Maya’s insistence, the room fell silent. Only Alden remained, standing near the door with a curious expression. His sharp eyes flicked to Maya, reading her as he always seemed to.

<!-- paragraph:1292 para_id:0000050C style:Normal -->
“Something else, Lady Maya?” he asked, his tone neutral but faintly edged with intrigue.

<!-- paragraph:1293 para_id:0000050D style:Normal -->
“If someone theoretically came across an encrypted book,” she said carefully, “how would one go about decrypting it?”

<!-- paragraph:1294 para_id:0000050E style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression didn’t change, though his gaze sharpened subtly. “What sort of theoretical book are we talking about?”

<!-- paragraph:1295 para_id:0000050F style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated briefly, then replied, “A journal.”

<!-- paragraph:1296 para_id:00000510 style:Normal -->
The faintest smirk touched Alden’s lips, as if she’d confirmed something he’d suspected. “Ah. In that case, what you’re describing is likely an auratic codex. Such books aren’t encrypted in a conventional sense. Instead, they’re encoded with a specific incantation to access the memories stored within.”

<!-- paragraph:1297 para_id:00000511 style:Normal -->
Maya tilted her head, feigning curiosity. “And how does one decipher an ‘auratic codex’?”

<!-- paragraph:1298 para_id:00000512 style:Normal -->
“It depends on how it was crafted,” Alden said thoughtfully, folding his hands behind his back. “A well-made journal will have a structured key—an incantation unique to its creator, usually tied to their aura or intent. Poorly made ones, however, can be a frustrating process of trial and error. With the right reference materials, however, it’s relatively straightforward.”

<!-- paragraph:1299 para_id:00000513 style:Normal -->
He paused, then added, “If you’re interested in this subject, I can have the necessary materials delivered to your quarters.”

<!-- paragraph:1300 para_id:00000514 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, her expression calm. “That would be helpful. Thank you, Alden.”

<!-- paragraph:1301 para_id:00000515 style:Normal -->
Alden inclined his head slightly and turned to leave but paused just as he reached the door. Glancing back, his hawkish gaze held a faint but unmistakable warning. “One word of advice, Lady Maya. Be cautious. The memories in such journals can be… vivid. Experiencing them often feels like living them in the moment. Intensity like that can be overwhelming, even dangerous.”

<!-- paragraph:1302 para_id:00000516 style:Normal -->
Maya met his eyes, keeping her composure. “I’ll keep that in mind.”

<!-- paragraph:1303 para_id:00000517 style:Normal -->
Alden studied her for another long moment before nodding and stepping out. The door clicked shut behind him, leaving Maya alone in the opulent room. She exhaled quietly, her thoughts already drifting toward the journal hidden in her chambers.

<!-- paragraph:1307 para_id:0000051B style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 8

<!-- paragraph:1308 para_id:0000051C style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 8

<!-- paragraph:1310 para_id:0000051E style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1311 para_id:0000051F style:Normal -->
Davian had barely heard Maya’s words when she returned to the meeting hall, her voice carrying over the anxious crowd. His focus had been on projecting calm, blending into the mass of contractors as best he could. But the dismissal that followed had brought an unexpected surge of relief. He’d been penned in that room like cattle, forced to wait under the eyes of guards. They’d come around, checking each person there with some sort of magical device to verify their aura was contained within the Registry. Finally released, he’d wasted no time heading to the barracks, eager to claim his assigned room and stash his belongings.

<!-- paragraph:1312 para_id:00000520 style:Normal -->
Now, sitting in the mess hall, his meal picked at but largely untouched, Davian kept his head down and his demeanor sharp. Antithesis was tucked securely under his belt, the faint zä on the blade a comfort against the unease coiled in his stomach. He’d chosen a corner seat, deliberately isolating himself from the buzzing clusters of contractors filling the hall. His goal was clear: avoid drawing attention.

<!-- paragraph:1313 para_id:00000521 style:Normal -->
And then Tanelle plopped down across from him, shattering his fragile reprieve.

<!-- paragraph:1314 para_id:00000522 style:Normal -->
“Can you believe it?” she said, her voice practically humming with excitement. “Someone actually tried to assassinate Lady Maya today! I mean, what were they even thinking? High Clades are way too powerful for that kind of thing to work. They must have been insane!”

<!-- paragraph:1315 para_id:00000523 style:Normal -->
Davian grimaced, his eyes narrowing as he glanced at her. He said nothing, his jaw tightening as he hoped she’d take the hint and leave. But Tanelle either didn’t notice or chose to ignore his obvious displeasure, barreling forward with her stream of chatter.

<!-- paragraph:1316 para_id:00000524 style:Normal -->
“And wait until I tell my family about this!” she continued, her tone as bright as if they were discussing the weather. “They’re going to think it’s the most incredible thing ever. I mean, imagine being here, in the Rezin citadel, when all this is happening. It’s practically history in the making.”

<!-- paragraph:1317 para_id:00000525 style:Normal -->
Davian cleared his throat, leaning slightly forward to excuse himself. “I should—”

<!-- paragraph:1318 para_id:00000526 style:Normal -->
“Oh! Hold that thought!” Tanelle interrupted, her grin widening as she waved over a couple of other contractors. “Hey, over here! Join us! This is Arrek—he’s new like us. Isn’t it exciting that we’re all working together?”

<!-- paragraph:1319 para_id:00000527 style:Normal -->
Davian froze, his escape plan vanishing as two others—a wiry man with pale hair and a stocky woman with a sharp gaze—took seats at the table. Tanelle wasted no time introducing them. “This is Kolvar and Jessa. They’re zänwrights too. We’re all in the same group!”

<!-- paragraph:1320 para_id:00000528 style:Normal -->
Wonderful. He forced a tight smile, his mind racing. Leaving now would look suspicious, and the last thing he needed was unwanted attention from anyone. Reluctantly, he stayed seated, lowering his gaze to his barely touched food in hopes of minimizing his presence.

<!-- paragraph:1321 para_id:00000529 style:Normal -->
Tanelle, however, was a force of nature. She leaned forward, her enthusiasm practically vibrating off her. “So, isn’t this amazing? Working in the Rezin citadel, fixing the wards for the High Clades? And we might even get to see Lady Maya again! I mean, not that I’m expecting it, but still—so cool.”

<!-- paragraph:1322 para_id:0000052A style:Normal -->
Kolvar gave a noncommittal grunt, while Jessa nodded curtly. Davian kept his expression neutral, shoving a small piece of bread into his mouth as an excuse to stay silent. He hoped the conversation would run out of steam, but Tanelle wasn’t finished.

<!-- paragraph:1323 para_id:0000052B style:Normal -->
“You know what? Let’s do introductions!” she declared, clapping her hands together. “We’re going to be working together, so we might as well get to know each other. Where did everyone work before this? Who did you train under?”

<!-- paragraph:1324 para_id:0000052C style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse quickened. He felt the eyes of the group turning toward him as Tanelle’s grin remained fixed on her face, expectant and eager. He suppressed a grimace, mentally sifting through the answers Jalen had drilled into him the night before.

<!-- paragraph:1325 para_id:0000052D style:Normal -->
Kolvar spoke first, his tone flat but professional. “I worked with Serin Mahr on the outer wards of the Tyvara Gate. Mostly maintenance contracts.”

<!-- paragraph:1326 para_id:0000052E style:Normal -->
Jessa went next, her voice brisk. “Two years under Lyn Corvalis. City wards, eastern district.”

<!-- paragraph:1327 para_id:0000052F style:Normal -->
Davian realized it was his turn. He swallowed, carefully repeating the answers he’d memorized. “I trained with Elar Danor. Mostly private work—small contracts for businesses in Westgrove.”

<!-- paragraph:1328 para_id:00000530 style:Normal -->
Tanelle’s eyes lit up. “Oh, I’ve heard of Danor! Wasn’t he the one who—”

<!-- paragraph:1329 para_id:00000531 style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” Davian cut in quickly, eager to divert the conversation. “He’s well-known for his focus on precision zänwork.”

<!-- paragraph:1330 para_id:00000532 style:Normal -->
Tanelle nodded enthusiastically, but before she could ask more questions, Jessa interjected, her sharp eyes narrowing slightly. “Westgrove, huh? Not much call for zänwrights in a place like that.”

<!-- paragraph:1331 para_id:00000533 style:Normal -->
Davian forced an easy shrug, retroactively thankful for the endless repetition of minor facts that Jalen had forced him through last night. “Plenty of small businesses need maintenance. And the pay’s decent enough.”

<!-- paragraph:1332 para_id:00000534 style:Normal -->
Jessa didn’t look entirely convinced, but Tanelle’s excitement drowned out any further questioning. “Well, I think it’s amazing that we’re all here! We’re part of something historic, you know? And we’ll be working together to make it happen. Isn’t that incredible?”

<!-- paragraph:1333 para_id:00000535 style:Normal -->
Davian barely held back a sigh. The group Tanelle was so thrilled about wasn’t just any group—it was the one that had just lost its supervisor in a dramatic assassination attempt. That meant extra scrutiny, extra attention from both Maya and Alden. It was exactly the kind of exposure he’d wanted to avoid.

<!-- paragraph:1334 para_id:00000536 style:Normal -->
He cursed his bad luck silently, letting Tanelle’s words wash over him as he sat back in his chair. For now, all he could do was keep his answers vague, avoid standing out, and hope that Tanelle’s relentless enthusiasm didn’t make his life more difficult than it already was.

<!-- paragraph:1335 para_id:00000537 style:Normal -->
Davian shoveled the last of his food into his mouth with more haste than grace, nodding along absently as Tanelle’s endless questions flitted around him like gnats. “I’ll catch you later,” he mumbled through a mouthful of bread, rising quickly before she could press him further. “Need to exercise.”

<!-- paragraph:1336 para_id:00000538 style:Normal -->
Tanelle’s face lit up. “Oh! What kind of exercise? Running? I bet you’d be good at—”

<!-- paragraph:1337 para_id:00000539 style:Normal -->
“Just... general fitness,” he cut in quickly, trying not to sound too terse. He grabbed his satchel, muttered a vague farewell, and slipped away before she could pin him down with more questions.

<!-- paragraph:1338 para_id:0000053A style:Normal -->
The cool air of the courtyard was a relief, the open sky above painted in soft twilight hues. Davian exhaled slowly, tension easing as the chatter of the mess hall faded into memory. He glanced around the empty space, taking in the practice dummies and wooden weapons lined neatly along the walls. It wasn’t much, but it would do.

<!-- paragraph:1339 para_id:0000053B style:Normal -->
He approached the center of the courtyard, turning the events of the day over in his mind. The Registry, Maya’s icy authority, the assassination attempt—everything was a tangled knot in his thoughts, and he needed something to cut through it. Sword practice would have helped, but it would be too noticeable.

<!-- paragraph:1340 para_id:0000053C style:Normal -->
Instead, he settled on something simpler. A hand-to-hand kata his mother had taught him when he was young. It focused on balance and flexibility, more meditation than combat, and it was exactly what he needed to steady his thoughts.

<!-- paragraph:1341 para_id:0000053D style:Normal -->
He began moving through the motions, his limbs flowing in practiced arcs, his breathing steady. The exercise was grounding, a reminder of Kelyra’s lessons. If your body is under control, so is your mind. The mantra echoed faintly in his thoughts as his muscles remembered the movements instinctively.

<!-- paragraph:1342 para_id:0000053E style:Normal -->
The tension in his chest eased, his mind clearing with each deliberate step and strike. He could almost hear his mother’s voice correcting his stance, her calm presence a sharp contrast to the storm of the day.

<!-- paragraph:1343 para_id:0000053F style:Normal -->
“Now this,” came a gruff voice behind him, “must be some kind of new dance—one you do without a partner.”

<!-- paragraph:1344 para_id:00000540 style:Normal -->
Davian froze mid-motion, turning sharply to find a middle-aged man leaning casually against the wall. His clothing marked him as a Rezin guard, though his breastplate and armor were half-unbuckled, leaving only the leathers beneath. The man’s rugged face was split by an amused grin, his thick arms crossed as he raised a brow at Davian’s defensive stance.

<!-- paragraph:1345 para_id:00000541 style:Normal -->
The guard gave a good-natured wink. “Relax, lad. Just having some fun. But if you really want to learn how to fight, a sword’s better than dancing.”

<!-- paragraph:1346 para_id:00000542 style:Normal -->
Davian straightened, forcing his expression into something polite but distant. “I was just... practicing balance. I’ll get out of your way.”

<!-- paragraph:1347 para_id:00000543 style:Normal -->
The guard chuckled, shaking his head. “Out of my way? You think I’ll let a strapping young lad like you wander off without giving you some proper education?” Before Davian could protest, the man stepped forward and tossed him a wooden sword.

<!-- paragraph:1348 para_id:00000544 style:Normal -->
Davian caught it reflexively, his fingers closing around the worn hilt. His pulse quickened, though he kept his face neutral. “I appreciate the offer, but I—”

<!-- paragraph:1349 para_id:00000545 style:Normal -->
“Don’t worry, I won’t rough you up too bad.” The guard’s grin widened as he finished pulling off his armor and  picked up another practice sword, spinning  it easily in one hand. “Come on, humor an old man. Just a few swings. It’s good exercise, eh?”

<!-- paragraph:1350 para_id:00000546 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated, his mind racing. The guard’s friendly demeanor made it harder to refuse, and leaving abruptly might raise more questions than staying. Reluctantly, he shifted his stance, holding the wooden blade in what he hoped looked like a novice grip.

<!-- paragraph:1351 para_id:00000547 style:Normal -->
The guard’s grin softened into something almost approving. “That’s the spirit. Let’s see what you’ve got.” He raised his own sword, stepping lightly into position. “Don’t worry, lad—I’ll take it easy on you.”

<!-- paragraph:1352 para_id:00000548 style:Normal -->
Davian tightened his grip, steeling himself. Just get through this, he thought grimly.

<!-- paragraph:1353 para_id:00000549 style:Normal -->
The guard moved with a fluidity that immediately set him apart from the Low Clade fighters Davian had encountered before. His steps were light yet grounded, his swings precise, as though every motion was calculated to test Davian’s limits.

<!-- paragraph:1354 para_id:0000054A style:Normal -->
Davian played the novice, keeping his movements awkward and uncoordinated. He allowed the guard’s strikes to land—sharp smacks on his arm and shoulder. His grip on the wooden sword was intentionally loose, his stances just off balance enough to sell the illusion.

<!-- paragraph:1355 para_id:0000054B style:Normal -->
“Come on, lad,” the guard said with a chuckle, circling him. “You can’t fool me. You’re holding back.”

<!-- paragraph:1356 para_id:0000054C style:Normal -->
Davian grunted noncommittally, feigning a clumsy step forward. The guard capitalized on the mistake, sweeping his leg under Davian’s and sending him sprawling onto the hard-packed dirt. The impact rattled through him, and before he could recover, the guard’s wooden sword hovered inches from his chest.

<!-- paragraph:1357 para_id:0000054D style:Normal -->
“Obvious you’re not trying,” the guard said, his tone half amused and half reproachful. “No one moves like that without knowing a thing or two. So, what is it? You worried about embarrassing me?” He stepped back, lowering his sword but keeping his gaze sharp.

<!-- paragraph:1358 para_id:0000054E style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse pounded in his ears, anger simmering just beneath the surface. He thought of the inked patterns on his arm, the Boon that burned against his skin, a reminder of what he was here to do. I came here to kill Niall Rezin, he thought bitterly. And I can’t even beat one of his guards?

<!-- paragraph:1359 para_id:0000054F style:Normal -->
The heat of shame and frustration twisted in his chest. He shoved himself up, gripping the wooden sword with renewed determination. His stance shifted—not quite the polished form his mother had taught him, but closer. His gaze met the guard’s, and something in it must have changed, because the man’s grin returned, wider this time.

<!-- paragraph:1360 para_id:00000550 style:Normal -->
“That’s the spirit,” the guard said, raising his sword again. “Let’s see what you’ve really got.”

<!-- paragraph:1361 para_id:00000551 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t hesitate. He stepped forward with a quick swing aimed at the guard’s midsection. The man parried easily, the wooden blades cracking against each other, but there was a flicker of surprise in his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:1362 para_id:00000552 style:Normal -->
The sparring shifted then, the playful energy replaced by something sharper. Davian swung again and again, his movements precise but not overly polished—enough to seem like natural talent honed by necessity rather than formal training. The guard’s strikes came faster, heavier, testing Davian’s reflexes and forcing him to adjust his footing constantly.

<!-- paragraph:1363 para_id:00000553 style:Normal -->
“You’ve got fire in you,” the guard said, blocking a strike aimed at his shoulder. His tone was approving, almost jovial. “Good. But fire alone won’t win you a fight.”

<!-- paragraph:1364 para_id:00000554 style:Normal -->
Davian said nothing, his jaw tight as he pressed forward. Each clash of wood against wood reverberated through his arms, grounding him in the moment. The frustration that had gnawed at him began to melt away, replaced by a singular focus: stay standing, stay sharp.

<!-- paragraph:1365 para_id:00000555 style:Normal -->
The guard danced back, nodding slightly. “Better,” he said, his voice laced with a hint of respect. “Much better. But you’ve still got a long way to go, lad.”

<!-- paragraph:1366 para_id:00000556 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t respond, focusing instead on the rhythm of the fight. This wasn’t about winning; it was about proving to himself that he could stand his ground. And for now, that was enough.

<!-- paragraph:1367 para_id:00000557 style:Normal -->
Suddenly, the guard’s blade swept up with a sharp twist, catching Davian’s wooden sword and sending it clattering to the ground. Before Davian could react, the guard stepped in and tapped his chest lightly with the flat of his weapon.

<!-- paragraph:1368 para_id:00000558 style:Normal -->
“Yield,” the man said, a grin tugging at the corner of his mouth.

<!-- paragraph:1369 para_id:00000559 style:Normal -->
Davian let out a breath and raised his hands in mock surrender. “Fine. I yield.”

<!-- paragraph:1370 para_id:0000055A style:Normal -->
The guard lowered his sword, then held out a calloused hand. Davian hesitated for a fraction of a second before clasping it, allowing himself to be pulled to his feet.

<!-- paragraph:1371 para_id:0000055B style:Normal -->
“Not bad,” the man said, his grip firm. “Not great, but not bad.”

<!-- paragraph:1372 para_id:0000055C style:Normal -->
Davian brushed the dirt from his clothes, glancing at the discarded sword. It wasn’t much of a stretch to let him win, he admitted silently. The man was good—very good.

<!-- paragraph:1373 para_id:0000055D style:Normal -->
“I’m Harvick,” the guard said, extending his hand again. “And you are?”

<!-- paragraph:1374 para_id:0000055E style:Normal -->
Davian’s mind raced, but he managed to keep his face neutral. “Arrek,” he replied, gripping Harvick’s hand once more.

<!-- paragraph:1375 para_id:0000055F style:Normal -->
“Arrek,” Harvick repeated, rolling the name over his tongue as if testing its weight. “Well, Arrek, if you ever need to exercise again, I’d be happy to teach you a thing or two.”

<!-- paragraph:1376 para_id:00000560 style:Normal -->
Davian forced a small smile, hoping it came across as self-deprecating rather than strained. “Doesn’t seem like I have much of a choice.”

<!-- paragraph:1377 para_id:00000561 style:Normal -->
Harvick let out a booming laugh, clapping Davian on the shoulder. “You don’t! I’d be a fool to let a diamond in the rough like you slip away. What guard regiment are you assigned to?”

<!-- paragraph:1378 para_id:00000562 style:Normal -->
Davian fought the urge to tense under the guard’s hand. Instead, he offered a faint shrug. “I’m not assigned to any regiment. I’m just a zänwright trying to get some exercise.”

<!-- paragraph:1379 para_id:00000563 style:Normal -->
Harvick raised an eyebrow, his gaze sweeping over Davian’s broad shoulders and the faint bruises visible on his forearms. “A zänwright, huh?” He crossed his arms, his expression skeptical but still friendly. “That’s a funny kind of story for someone who moves like you do.”

<!-- paragraph:1380 para_id:00000564 style:Normal -->
Davian straightened his posture, keeping his tone even. “I’m from a rough part of the city. Always had to know how to defend myself.”

<!-- paragraph:1381 para_id:00000565 style:Normal -->
Harvick studied him for a moment longer, then chuckled. “Fair enough. I come from a place like that myself. And don’t worry—I won’t ask too many questions. Everyone’s got their reasons for the kind of life they’ve lived.”

<!-- paragraph:1382 para_id:00000566 style:Normal -->
Davian offered a nod, grateful for the lack of prying. “Thanks.”

<!-- paragraph:1383 para_id:00000567 style:Normal -->
Harvick waved him off with a grin. “Go on, then. You’ve earned your break. But don’t think this lets you off the hook. Next time, I expect you to actually try.”

<!-- paragraph:1384 para_id:00000568 style:Normal -->
“I’ll keep that in mind,” Davian said dryly, picking up the wooden sword and placing it back where he’d found it.

<!-- paragraph:1385 para_id:00000569 style:Normal -->
The walk leading back to Davian’s room seemed quieter than it had been earlier, the faint echoes of his footsteps the only sound. As he walked, his thoughts churned, piecing together the events of the day. For all the grandeur and intimidation of the High Clade citadel, the people inside it seemed strangely… normal. Contractors bustling about their work, Tanelle’s incessant chatter, even Harvick’s good-natured ribbing—it didn’t match the image he’d built of a cold, untouchable fortress.

<!-- paragraph:1386 para_id:0000056A style:Normal -->
But then, his mind snapped back to Maya, standing on the dais, her expression unreadable as she froze the assassin in place. That impassive face, that precise step back as her Shroud flared to life—it wasn’t normal. He’d seen people kill before, but there was something different about the way she did it. It wasn’t ruthless; it was detached, like she hadn’t even seen the man as a person.

<!-- paragraph:1387 para_id:0000056B style:Normal -->
His thoughts shifted uneasily to Harvick. He wasn’t just good; he was far better than any Low Clade guard Davian had ever encountered. The memory of the man’s laughter grated against his nerves. What if Harvick wasn’t just a guard? The thought flared in his mind, sharp and intrusive. What if the High Clades already know who I am? What if they sent Harvick to test me?

<!-- paragraph:1388 para_id:0000056C style:Normal -->
The paranoia clamped down like a vice. He replayed every moment of the sparring session, dissecting every mistake, every hesitation. Showing any skill with a sword had been a mistake. What if that was enough to betray me?

<!-- paragraph:1389 para_id:0000056D style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head sharply, trying to dispel the thought. It’s just nerves, he told himself. The past few days had been a relentless blur of stealing, fighting, and killing. He hadn’t had a full night’s rest in days.

<!-- paragraph:1390 para_id:0000056E style:Normal -->
Arrek’s face flickered in his mind like a flash of lightning, stark and unavoidable. The weight of the knife in his hand, the sharp gasp as it went in—it hit him all over again. His chest tightened, the memory growing unbearable. He forced the thought down, shoving it into the dark corner of his mind where it belonged. He couldn’t afford to think about it. Not here. Not now.

<!-- paragraph:1391 para_id:0000056F style:Normal -->
The walls seemed to close in as he turned the final corner to his room. His pulse pounded in his ears, and his breathing quickened. By the time he reached his door, he was gripping the handle so tightly his knuckles went white. He wrenched it open, stepping inside and slamming it shut behind him. Leaning back against the door, he pressed his palms flat against the cool wood, trying to steady his breathing.

<!-- paragraph:1392 para_id:00000570 style:Normal -->
The quiet was deafening, the emptiness of the room pressing down on him. His chest heaved as he fought to pull himself back from the edge, closing his eyes and trying to focus on the solid feel of the door against his back.

<!-- paragraph:1393 para_id:00000571 style:Normal -->
A voice outside made him jolt. “Arrek? Are you okay?”

<!-- paragraph:1394 para_id:00000572 style:Normal -->
His heart leapt into his throat. It was Tanelle.

<!-- paragraph:1395 para_id:00000573 style:Normal -->
She’s in on it. The thought hit him like a blow, the paranoia surging back full force. The Rezins have her watching me. They’ve been watching my every move, waiting for me to slip up.

<!-- paragraph:1396 para_id:00000574 style:Normal -->
He forced himself to respond, his voice strained but steady enough. “I’m fine. I just—” His throat tightened, and he swallowed hard. “I just need to sleep.”

<!-- paragraph:1397 para_id:00000575 style:Normal -->
There was a pause, and for a horrible moment, he thought she wouldn’t leave. Then, mercifully, her footsteps faded away.

<!-- paragraph:1398 para_id:00000576 style:Normal -->
Davian sagged against the door, his body trembling. Get it together. He clenched his fists, breathing deeply to try to calm the storm inside him. He just needed sleep. That was all. If he could sleep, everything would be fine.

<!-- paragraph:1399 para_id:00000577 style:Normal -->
He crossed the room and threw himself onto the cot, not bothering to undress. He lay there, staring at the ceiling as his thoughts whirled. Slowly, exhaustion dragged him under.

<!-- paragraph:1400 para_id:00000578 style:Normal -->
He was standing in Arrek’s home, the knife in his hand. He drove it forward, over and over, the blood splattering against his arms and face. But when he looked down, the lifeless eyes staring back at him weren’t Arrek’s. They were Selina’s.

<!-- paragraph:1401 para_id:00000579 style:Normal -->
Davian bolted upright, his chest heaving, the sheets tangled around him like a snare. His skin was slick with sweat, his heart hammering so hard it felt like it might burst. He pressed his hands to his face, trying to block out the image, but it lingered, burned into his mind.

<!-- paragraph:1402 para_id:0000057A style:Normal -->
It’s guilt, he told himself. That’s all this is. The guilt of not being able to save Selina. Of being too weak. He turned onto his side, his fists clenched tightly as he glared at the shadows pooling in the corners of the room. This is why I’m here, he thought fiercely. To make it right. To make them pay.

<!-- paragraph:1403 para_id:0000057B style:Normal -->
But no matter how tightly he clung to the thought, the ache in his chest didn’t ease. The darkness pressed in closer, and he lay there for hours, staring blankly at the ceiling, the weight of memory anchoring him to the bed.

<!-- paragraph:1406 para_id:0000057E style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 9

<!-- paragraph:1407 para_id:0000057F style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 9

<!-- paragraph:1409 para_id:00000581 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1411 para_id:00000583 style:Normal -->
Maya walked through the <!-- comment-start:74 -->quiet halls of the citadel, her measured steps echoing<!-- comment-end:74 --><!-- comment-ref:74 --> faintly against the polished stone. Her mind was still occupied with the proposal the newly-appointed supervisor of the combined contractor group had delivered to her office earlier that evening. Though the man’s promotion had been abrupt, he’d demonstrated a level of diligence that impressed her—if only slightly. The proposed changes to the work assignments had been thorough, though not flawless. She’d spent the last hour meticulously reviewing each adjustment, ensuring the tasks were perfectly aligned with her priorities.

<!-- paragraph:1412 para_id:00000584 style:Normal -->
As she reached her quarters, Maya allowed herself a brief exhale of relief. The guards stationed at her door straightened at her approach, but she barely acknowledged them. She gestured for them to remain stationed outside, knowing they would be relieved around midnight. They nodded, and she stepped inside, closing the door behind her with a quiet finality.

<!-- paragraph:1413 para_id:00000585 style:Normal -->
Her gaze immediately fell on the desk at the center of the room, where a small stack of books awaited her. Alden had been as good as his word—these were the decryption materials she had asked about. She approached the desk, brushing her fingers over the leather bindings, her thoughts straying to the codex hidden within her quarters.

<!-- paragraph:1414 para_id:00000586 style:Normal -->
She flipped through a few of the books, skimming the initial chapters. The process of decoding an auratic codex was intricate, relying on the journal’s unique key incantation. Without it, the task could stretch into days or weeks, yielding little progress.

<!-- paragraph:1415 para_id:00000587 style:Normal -->
Her exhaustion gnawed at her—sleep beckoned—but she couldn’t resist. Crossing to the ornate chest at the foot of her bed, she lifted the lid and retrieved the journal she had hidden there. It felt heavier in her hands than she remembered, as though the weight of its secrets had grown in her absence.

<!-- paragraph:1416 para_id:00000588 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:75 --><!-- comment-start:76 -->Sitting at her desk, Maya opened the journal. Her eyes immediately caught on an inscription inside the front cover. It shimmered faintly under the soft light of the sconces, the characters forming a short phrase written in a precise, unfamiliar script. She leaned closer, her brow furrowing. It was an incantation—she was certain of it. The key, if her understanding of the codex was correct.

<!-- paragraph:1417 para_id:00000589 style:Normal -->
But a flicker of doubt wormed its way into her thoughts. Had this been here before? She didn’t think so. Yet she had been exhausted the night she found it, her senses dulled by fear and fatigue. Perhaps she had missed it; she certainly hadn’t known then to look for a key incantation. <!-- comment-end:75 --><!-- comment-ref:75 --><!-- comment-end:76 --><!-- comment-ref:76 -->

<!-- paragraph:1418 para_id:0000058A style:Normal -->
She traced the shimmering characters with her fingertip, her curiosity flaring anew. This was the doorway to whatever secrets the journal held. She considered what she had just read in Alden’s books. She had read that accessing the memories required focus on a specific page while reciting the incantation. The process seemed straightforward, but the warnings Alden had mentioned lingered in her mind. Even so, the temptation to try was irresistible.

<!-- paragraph:1419 para_id:0000058B style:Normal -->
She flipped to the first page of the codex, her gaze tracing the faint zän etched into the parchment. The text shimmered faintly, almost alive under the soft glow of the lamps in her quarters. Drawing a deep breath, she began to recite the incantation.

<!-- paragraph:1420 para_id:0000058C style:Normal -->
The room around her vanished.

<!-- paragraph:1421 para_id:0000058D style:Normal -->
Maya was no longer seated at her desk. Instead, she found herself standing amidst chaos. Screams and the clash of weapons filled the air. The acrid scent of smoke and blood burned her nostrils. Before her, ranks of soldiers surged, their expressions grim as they charged. Her hands were alight, flames licking at her fingers, and with a flick of her wrists, beams of fire seared through the advancing lines. The heat was intense, the destruction immediate.

<!-- paragraph:1422 para_id:0000058E style:Normal -->
The soldiers faltered, retreating as the ground around them blackened. An opening cleared, and from the thinning ranks emerged a man. He wore an elegant outfit—finely tailored, its silken fabric more fitting for a ballroom than a battlefield. His wide-brimmed hat cast a shadow over his face, but his bare arms were unmistakable. A Low Clade magus.

<!-- paragraph:1423 para_id:0000058F style:Normal -->
He moved with deliberate purpose, his steps unhurried as his gaze locked onto hers. Without warning, he slammed his palm into the ground. The earth rippled violently, a tidal wave of stone and debris rushing toward her. Instinctively, Maya extended her hand, channeling her magic. Heat drained from the air and the earth alike, freezing the wave mid-surge. Shards of frost glittered in the air, catching the dim light of the battlefield.

<!-- paragraph:1424 para_id:00000590 style:Normal -->
The magus hesitated, his brow furrowing in frustration. Maya raised her right hand, her magic surging to its peak. A single, precise beam of flame erupted from her fingers, aimed directly at him. His Shroud flared to life, a shimmering shield of energy that wrapped around him like armor. Yet as she advanced, her attack unrelenting, his strength faltered. His knees buckled, his expression twisting in pain as her fire pressed closer.

<!-- paragraph:1425 para_id:00000591 style:Normal -->
A surge of foreign joy coursed through her—exhilaration, pride, victory. She heard herself speak, her voice cutting through the chaos like a blade. “Are all of Elarion’s children this weak? You will never take our home while I still live.”

<!-- paragraph:1426 para_id:00000592 style:Normal -->
With a final burst of power, the beam intensified, breaking through his Shroud. The magus’s scream was short-lived, silenced as flames consumed him. His body crumpled to ash, scattering in the wind.

<!-- paragraph:1427 para_id:00000593 style:Normal -->
The battlefield disappeared in an instant.

<!-- paragraph:1428 para_id:00000594 style:Normal -->
Maya was back in her quarters, the silence of the room stark and jarring. She staggered, her breath hitching as she looked down at her right hand. Smoke rose faintly from her fingertips, the heat lingering unnaturally on her skin. Her pulse thundered in her ears, and her heart raced as if she had just left the battlefield.

<!-- paragraph:1429 para_id:00000595 style:Normal -->
Shaking, she placed the codex back on the desk. Her mind churned, the vividness of the memory etched into her senses. It wasn’t just something she had seen. She had lived it—felt the power, the rage, and the foreign emotions that still lingered uneasily in her chest.

<!-- paragraph:1430 para_id:00000596 style:Normal -->
The memory—or whatever it was—replayed in her mind with startling clarity. The heat of battle, the sharp focus of her magic, the surge of triumph as she bested the Elarion magus. But what unsettled her most wasn’t the violence—it was the joy. She had felt a fierce satisfaction as she fought, a deep, undeniable sense of purpose. It felt so vivid, so real—more real than the endless petty squabbles of the High Clades that defined her days.

<!-- paragraph:1431 para_id:00000597 style:Normal -->
Her gaze drifted to the codex on her desk. For a moment, she considered testing another page, wondering what else it might reveal. But fatigue bore her down - today had been a long day, and the journal would still be here tomorrow. Rising slowly, she moved toward her bed, the exhaustion of the day settling into her bones.

<!-- paragraph:1432 para_id:00000598 style:Normal -->
Pulling back the covers, she sank onto the mattress, the cool fabric brushing against her skin. She lay still, staring at the ceiling as her thoughts churned. Is this who my mother was? She knew so little about her. Niall almost never spoke of her mother, as if the mere mention of her name reopened a wound too deep to heal. She had known her mother was from another city, but these memories showed someone who had fought against Elarion. What drove her into that war? Maya’s thoughts returned to the burning, fierce sense of joy she had felt in the memory. Was this what she had been missing? A purpose that meant more than survival or ambition? Her eyes drifted closed as exhaustion finally pulled her under, her thoughts swirling with questions she couldn’t yet answer.

<!-- paragraph:1433 para_id:00000599 style:Normal -->
Too soon she felt a gentle shake of her shoulder pulling her from sleep. "You asked to be woken by dawn," Erynn said, her tone cheerful but firm. Light streamed through the tall windows, bathing the room in golden hues. Maya blinked against the brightness, a strange heaviness lingering in her limbs, and stretched, wincing slightly at the ache in her muscles.

<!-- paragraph:1434 para_id:0000059A style:Normal -->
"That doesn’t sound like something I’d have asked for," Maya muttered, her voice groggy as she sat up.

<!-- paragraph:1435 para_id:0000059B style:Normal -->
Erynn grinned, hands on her hips. "Well, I had to wake up even earlier to be ready to wake you, so I’m pretty sure you did. Don’t be grumpy."

<!-- paragraph:1436 para_id:0000059C style:Normal -->
Maya huffed a soft laugh despite herself and swung her legs over the side of the bed. Erynn was already bustling around, laying out another elaborate sleeveless tabard and matching leggings. The fabric gleamed faintly in the morning light, the intricate embroidery glinting with the faint sheen of inlaid silver.

<!-- paragraph:1437 para_id:0000059D style:Normal -->
As Maya washed her face and began to shake off the remnants of sleep, Erynn returned with a comb in hand. "Come, let me do your hair," she said, motioning for Maya to sit. Maya obliged, feeling the familiar rhythm of Erynn’s deft fingers weaving through her dark strands, twisting them into an intricate braid.

<!-- paragraph:1438 para_id:0000059E style:Normal -->
Once Erynn finished, she stepped back, clearly pleased with her work. Then, her eyes lit up as she reached for a small box on a nearby table. "Oh, I almost forgot! This came late yesterday. Niall had it made for you, but apparently, the workmen were a day behind schedule."

<!-- paragraph:1439 para_id:0000059F style:Normal -->
Erynn opened the box with a flourish, revealing a tiara of gleaming silver set with diamonds. The delicate band shimmered with faint magical zän, casting a soft, ethereal light.

<!-- paragraph:1440 para_id:000005A0 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the piece, her expression unreadable. "A day late," she repeated quietly, running her fingers over the intricate design. It was beautiful, undeniably so, but she couldn’t help but wonder if the delay was real—or if this was Niall’s way of apologizing for their argument the day before.

<!-- paragraph:1441 para_id:000005A1 style:Normal -->
Erynn carefully set the tiara on Maya’s head, stepping back to admire the effect. "You look radiant," she said with a bright smile, her pride evident.

<!-- paragraph:1442 para_id:000005A2 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, distracted. "Thank you, Erynn." She straightened, her composure settling into place. "Send a runner to let the zänwright supervisors know I’ll meet with them after breakfast to finalize work assignments."

<!-- paragraph:1443 para_id:000005A3 style:Normal -->
