Erynn nodded, already moving to carry out the request. Maya stood, the weight of the tiara and the morning’s responsibilities settling on her shoulders. As she made her way toward the dining hall, she couldn’t shake the lingering soreness in her body—or the ghost of the battlefield etched into her memory.

<!-- paragraph:1444 para_id:000005A4 style:Normal -->
The dining hall was bathed in soft morning light filtering through tall, arched windows. Maya entered quietly, her footsteps muted against the polished stone floor. Niall was already seated at the long table, a plate of food untouched before him. His gaze was fixed on a document in his hands, but he looked up as she approached, his expression carefully neutral.

<!-- paragraph:1445 para_id:000005A5 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded in greeting and took a seat across from him. Erynn stood just behind her, her hands clasped neatly in front of her as she waited attentively

<!-- paragraph:1446 para_id:000005A6 style:Normal -->
The silence stretched between them, heavy and awkward. Niall folded the document and set it aside, focusing on his plate as if it contained the answers to every unresolved question between them.

<!-- paragraph:1447 para_id:000005A7 style:Normal -->
The silence stretched, brittle and awkward. Finally, Maya broke it. "Why did my mother come to Elarion?"

<!-- paragraph:1448 para_id:000005A8 style:Normal -->
Niall froze, his fork hovering above his plate. He lowered it carefully and met her gaze, his expression unreadable. "Why are you asking me that now?"

<!-- paragraph:1449 para_id:000005A9 style:Normal -->
"Is it so odd that I want to know about her?" Maya’s tone was calm, but a hint of frustration bled through.

<!-- paragraph:1450 para_id:000005AA style:Normal -->
For a moment, it seemed like he might answer. Instead, his face twisted with something she couldn’t quite name—pain, regret, maybe both. "Please… don’t," he said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:1451 para_id:000005AB style:Normal -->
The words stung more than she expected. She set her fork down with deliberate care, holding his gaze even as the silence between them grew heavier.

<!-- paragraph:1452 para_id:000005AC style:Normal -->
When it became clear he wouldn’t say more, Maya rose, smoothing the fabric of her tabard. "You can’t avoid talking about this forever," she said, her voice low but firm.

<!-- paragraph:1453 para_id:000005AD style:Normal -->
She turned to leave, Erynn already stepping to follow, but Niall’s voice stopped her at the door. "You remind me of her. Very much."

<!-- paragraph:1454 para_id:000005AE style:Normal -->
Maya paused, her back still to him. The words hung in the air, charged and heavy, but he didn’t elaborate.

<!-- paragraph:1455 para_id:000005AF style:Normal -->
Maya froze, her breath catching. Slowly, she turned back to him. He sat rigidly, his gaze fixed on the table, but she could see the effort those words had cost him etched into his face.

<!-- paragraph:1456 para_id:000005B0 style:Normal -->
"Thank you," she said softly.

<!-- paragraph:1457 para_id:000005B1 style:Normal -->
For a moment, they sat in silence, the weight of his admission hanging between them. Maya glanced at Erynn, who shifted slightly but remained silent.

<!-- paragraph:1458 para_id:000005B2 style:Normal -->
"I have a meeting," Maya said at last, breaking the stillness. She inclined her head to Niall, then walked toward the door, her steps measured and deliberate.

<!-- paragraph:1459 para_id:000005B3 style:Normal -->
As they walked through the quiet halls, the faint sound of their footsteps echoing against the stone, Erynn glanced at Maya. Her brows were knit in mild confusion. "What was that all about?"

<!-- paragraph:1460 para_id:000005B4 style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated, her gaze fixed forward. Erynn rarely pried, but her tone was light, almost teasing—an invitation rather than a demand.

<!-- paragraph:1461 para_id:000005B5 style:Normal -->
Weighing the question, Maya decided to speak. "I found her journal," she said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:1462 para_id:000005B6 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s step faltered. "Your mother’s journal?"

<!-- paragraph:1463 para_id:000005B7 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded. "It’s not a normal journal, though. It’s some kind of… repository. For memories."

<!-- paragraph:1464 para_id:000005B8 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s eyes widened, her curiosity immediately piqued. "Memories? What kind of memories?"

<!-- paragraph:1465 para_id:000005B9 style:Normal -->
Maya took a slow breath. "I saw her fighting. Against Elarion." Her voice dropped to a near whisper. "And it felt… right. Like it meant something. Like she had a purpose that wasn’t just political gain or posturing."

<!-- paragraph:1466 para_id:000005BA style:Normal -->
Erynn stared at her for a moment before asking, half-joking but with a sharp edge, "So what? Are you going rogue now? Going to start attacking the other High Clades?"

<!-- paragraph:1467 para_id:000005BB style:Normal -->
Maya gave a short, humorless laugh. "I’m not sure," she admitted. "But I’m tired of the games they play. All the scheming, the petty power grabs… none of it matters. Not really."

<!-- paragraph:1468 para_id:000005BC style:Normal -->
Erynn glanced at her, a mixture of concern and bemusement crossing her face. "You’ve been an official Rezin heir for two days, Maya," she said dryly.

<!-- paragraph:1469 para_id:000005BD style:Normal -->
"Two days too long," Maya muttered, though the words lacked real heat.

<!-- paragraph:1470 para_id:000005BE style:Normal -->
They reached the tall doors of the meeting hall, the ornate carvings on the wood catching the morning light. Maya straightened, pulling her composure back into place.

<!-- paragraph:1471 para_id:000005BF style:Normal -->
Inside, the five zänwright supervisors were already waiting, seated around a large table piled with documents and zä schematics. Their conversation ceased as Maya entered, each of them rising to their feet in deference.

<!-- paragraph:1472 para_id:000005C0 style:Normal -->
Erynn lingered just behind her, casting one last curious glance at Maya before stepping to the side to observe.

<!-- paragraph:1473 para_id:000005C1 style:Normal -->
Maya took her seat at the head of the table, her posture straight and composed as she scanned the array of documents before her. The supervisors followed her lead, taking their seats in silence.

<!-- paragraph:1474 para_id:000005C2 style:Normal -->
"Let’s get started," Maya began, her voice calm but firm. "The project is a complete overhaul of the inner wards protecting the citadel’s treasury, vault, and internal fortifications. You’ve all reviewed the plans. The wards are designed to work in tandem with the Registry to identify auras."

<!-- paragraph:1475 para_id:000005C3 style:Normal -->
She looked around the table, ensuring their attention. "When the Registry is in passive mode, the wards will alert us to unauthorized access. When in active mode, however, the wards are capable of directing lethal defensive magic to the precise location of any breach."

<!-- paragraph:1476 para_id:000005C4 style:Normal -->
A ripple of murmurs spread through the group. One supervisor, a wiry man with salt-and-pepper hair, cleared his throat. "So, the wards will… discharge energy directly through the barriers?"

<!-- paragraph:1477 para_id:000005C5 style:Normal -->
"Correct," Maya replied. "And because of that, they are capable of channeling incredibly dangerous forces. This is why the utmost caution is required during installation and calibration. Any mistakes could result in… unintended casualties."

<!-- paragraph:1478 para_id:000005C6 style:Normal -->
The murmurs grew louder, unease clear in the supervisors’ expressions. Maya waited a beat before continuing.

<!-- paragraph:1479 para_id:000005C7 style:Normal -->
"You are all receiving hazard pay for a reason," she reminded them, her tone sharper now. "The risks are known, and you were informed of them before agreeing to this assignment. If anyone feels uncomfortable with the work, you are free to annul your contract and leave now. No repercussions."

<!-- paragraph:1480 para_id:000005C8 style:Normal -->
The room fell silent. The supervisors exchanged glances, but no one spoke up.

<!-- paragraph:1481 para_id:000005C9 style:Normal -->
"Good," Maya said, her voice softer but no less firm. "Now, let’s review the timeline and team assignments. I want to ensure every step of this process is clear."

<!-- paragraph:1482 para_id:000005CA style:Normal -->
Maya spread the schematics across the table, the faint glow of zä diagrams illuminating the faces of the supervisors. "Group One," she began, gesturing to the combined team noted in the documents, "will be assigned to the treasury wards. Your first task is to dismantle and reconfigure the existing protections in alignment with the new system. The treasury’s wards are older but similar in design to the ones on the vault."

<!-- paragraph:1483 para_id:000005CB style:Normal -->
One of the supervisors, a stocky woman with a sharp gaze, raised her hand. "Forgive me, my lady, but why isn’t anyone assigned to the vault yet? Wouldn’t it be more efficient to split the teams and work on both simultaneously?"

<!-- paragraph:1484 para_id:000005CC style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. "The vault wards are exceptionally dangerous. They’re designed to channel a higher concentration of defensive magic and have a broader range of lethal capabilities. They’re far less forgiving of errors. Additionally, the treasury wards operate in a manner that envelops the vault wards, allowing us to work from the outside in" Her eyes swept across the table, her tone steady but grave. "This sequence will allow your team to become familiar with the system’s nuances before tackling the more dangerous tasks. I won’t risk anyone’s safety by rushing this process."

<!-- paragraph:1485 para_id:000005CD style:Normal -->
A murmur of acknowledgment rippled through the group. The stocky supervisor leaned back, nodding thoughtfully.

<!-- paragraph:1486 para_id:000005CE style:Normal -->
"Any further questions?" Maya asked, letting her gaze linger on each supervisor in turn.

<!-- paragraph:1487 para_id:000005CF style:Normal -->
When none came, she continued<!-- comment-start:77 -->. "Once group one completes the tre<!-- comment-end:77 --><!-- comment-ref:77 -->asury overhaul, we’ll evaluate the results and address any issues before proceeding to the vault. I expect regular updates on your progress and any unforeseen complications."

<!-- paragraph:1488 para_id:000005D0 style:Normal -->
The supervisors nodded in unison, their earlier unease replaced with a sense of purpose. Maya leaned back slightly, satisfied that her point had been made.

<!-- paragraph:1489 para_id:000005D1 style:Normal -->
"Let’s move on," she said, gesturing to the next set of documents.

<!-- paragraph:1490 para_id:000005D2 style:Normal -->
Maya continued through the remaining protocols with precision. The supervisors followed along in silence, jotting notes and nodding occasionally.

<!-- paragraph:1491 para_id:000005D3 style:Normal -->
As she reached the end, Maya set down the final document and folded her hands on the table. Her gaze swept over the group, sharp and calculating.

<!-- paragraph:1492 para_id:000005D4 style:Normal -->
"You’ve all been thoroughly vetted and Registered for this project," she said, her tone calm but unyielding. "You are also being paid generously for your work. That said, I don’t need to remind you of the value of what is stored in the treasury and the vault. These objects are not just rare—they are irreplaceable and, in some cases, dangerous."

<!-- paragraph:1493 para_id:000005D5 style:Normal -->
The room remained silent, the weight of her words settling over the supervisors like a heavy cloak.

<!-- paragraph:1494 para_id:000005D6 style:Normal -->
"Anyone working in these areas will be searched upon exiting, and access will be strictly limited to designated work hours," Maya continued. "This is not a reflection on your integrity but a necessary precaution for the safety of the citadel and its holdings."

<!-- paragraph:1495 para_id:000005D7 style:Normal -->
There were no murmurs this time, only solemn nods of acknowledgment.

<!-- paragraph:1496 para_id:000005D8 style:Normal -->
"That’s all for now," Maya said, standing and signaling the end of the meeting. "You are dismissed. Begin preparations immediately."

<!-- paragraph:1497 para_id:000005D9 style:Normal -->
The supervisors rose, offering respectful bows before gathering their documents and filing out of the room.

<!-- paragraph:1498 para_id:000005DA style:Normal -->
As the doors closed behind them, Maya let out a soft breath. She turned to Erynn, who had remained silently observing throughout the meeting.

<!-- paragraph:1499 para_id:000005DB style:Normal -->
"I’m planning on doing the key work on the vault wards myself," Maya said quietly, her voice lowering so only Erynn could hear.

<!-- paragraph:1500 para_id:000005DC style:Normal -->
Erynn raised an eyebrow. "I assume you’re not planning to tell them that."

<!-- paragraph:1501 para_id:000005DD style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. "No. I want to see who among them seems competent enough to assist me when the time comes. But they don’t need to know I’m watching for that. If they think I’m testing them, it might influence their work."

<!-- paragraph:1502 para_id:000005DE style:Normal -->
Erynn smiled faintly. "Subtle, as always."

<!-- paragraph:1503 para_id:000005DF style:Normal -->
Maya gave a small laugh. "This isn’t about subtlety. It’s about making sure the job gets done right. And keeping everyone alive while it happens. Now let’s get to work."

<!-- paragraph:1507 para_id:000005E3 style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 10

<!-- paragraph:1508 para_id:000005E4 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 10

<!-- paragraph:1510 para_id:000005E6 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1512 para_id:000005E8 style:Normal -->
Davian woke slowly, his body stiff from a restless night. He had spent most of it staring at the ceiling, his thoughts looping endlessly, the weight of his mission pressing heavily on his chest. When morning came, he felt as though he’d barely slept at all.

<!-- paragraph:1513 para_id:000005E9 style:Normal -->
He considered heading to the dining hall but dismissed the thought almost immediately. The last thing he wanted was to make small talk with the others. Instead, he opted to skip breakfast, staying in his bunk until the barracks began to clear.

<!-- paragraph:1514 para_id:000005EA style:Normal -->
As the room quieted, he overheard a few of the others discussing the day’s schedule. The supervisors were meeting with Lady Maya after breakfast for assignments, but the rest of them had time off until work began later in the day.

<!-- paragraph:1515 para_id:000005EB style:Normal -->
Perfect.

<!-- paragraph:1516 para_id:000005EC style:Normal -->
Davian rose, dressed quickly, and stepped outside into the crisp morning air. The citadel grounds were bathed in golden sunlight, the spire’s shadow stretching long across the courtyard. He decided to use the free time to get a better sense of the citadel’s layout - their intelligence on this had been surprisingly sparse.

<!-- paragraph:1517 para_id:000005ED style:Normal -->
He started with a circuit of the grounds, keeping his pace slow and his eyes sharp. The citadel was essentially a city unto itself. High outer walls loomed overhead, connected to the central spire by raised stone walkways. The spire itself was built into the outer wall, rising to dizzying heights overlooking the city. The spire seemed to have originally been part of the city; the walls and outer sections of the citadel radiated outward and away from the spire, enclosing what had become a self-contained fortress—and, over time, almost a small city of its own.

<!-- paragraph:1518 para_id:000005EE style:Normal -->
Davian passed a number of outbuildings scattered across the grounds: the dining hall, several barracks—including his own—a smithy, storage facilities, practice yards, and the quartermaster’s post. The sheer scale of the citadel was staggering.

<!-- paragraph:1519 para_id:000005EF style:Normal -->
He paused at the base of the spire, his gaze traveling upward. The towering structure loomed over the rest of the citadel, its upper floors vanishing into the morning haze. The spire was an architectural marvel—and a fortress in its own right. Davian couldn’t help but appreciate its strategic design: no entrances on the side facing the city, no windows except for narrow arrow slits until hundreds of feet in the air. If he needed to leave in a hurry, that design would complicate things. Yesterday, he had memorized the route to the Registry, located slightly below ground level within the spire. The memory of that descent was clear, etched into his mind as part of his meticulous preparation.

<!-- paragraph:1520 para_id:000005F0 style:Normal -->
The vault, however, was another question. If he had to guess, it was likely higher up. Elevating it to the upper floors would make sense—easier to restrict access, easier to defend.

<!-- paragraph:1521 para_id:000005F1 style:Normal -->
For a moment, he considered trying to slip inside the spire, to scout its layout further. The thought was tempting, but he pushed it aside almost as quickly as it came. He would learn everything he needed to know soon enough. His work on the wards would inevitably bring him to the vault and its defenses. There was no need to risk drawing attention by being caught somewhere he wasn’t authorized to be yet.

<!-- paragraph:1522 para_id:000005F2 style:Normal -->
Davian returned to his room, closing the door behind him with a quiet sigh. Sitting on the edge of his cot, he pulled out a small manual on zänwright work. He skimmed its pages quickly, cramming in as much as he could. The basics came back easily enough—his parents had ensured he had a broad education, including foundational knowledge of zänwork—but the details still made him uneasy.

<!-- paragraph:1523 para_id:000005F3 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t the work itself that bothered him; it was the prospect of doing it in front of others. No matter how much he reviewed, there would be gaps in his knowledge, little tells that could raise suspicion if anyone looked too closely. What he needed, he thought, was to be stationed next to someone who wouldn’t notice—or care—if he leaned on them for cues.

<!-- paragraph:1524 para_id:000005F4 style:Normal -->
Someone talkative, distracted, maybe even a little self-absorbed.

<!-- paragraph:1525 para_id:000005F5 style:Normal -->
A groan escaped him as the realization hit. That someone would almost certainly be Tanelle.

<!-- paragraph:1526 para_id:000005F6 style:Normal -->
She fit the description perfectly—cheerful, airy, and endlessly excitable. She was the kind of person who talked enough to fill any silence and would miss things others wouldn’t.

<!-- paragraph:1527 para_id:000005F7 style:Normal -->
Shaking his head, Davian put the manual away and stood. He couldn’t afford to dwell on it. He’d manage, somehow.

<!-- paragraph:1528 para_id:000005F8 style:Normal -->
Heading back out, he made his way to the meeting outbuilding where the contractors had been told to wait. Inside, the air buzzed with quiet conversation, the other zänwrights gathered in loose clusters.

<!-- paragraph:1529 para_id:000005F9 style:Normal -->
Tanelle was already there, along with Kolvar and Jessa, chatting near the back of the room. Davian steeled himself, pushing aside the reluctance gnawing at him, and approached them.

<!-- paragraph:1530 para_id:000005FA style:Normal -->
“Hi, Tanelle,” he said, keeping his tone neutral.

<!-- paragraph:1531 para_id:000005FB style:Normal -->
Her eyes lit up as she turned to him. “Davian!” she exclaimed, practically bouncing on her heels. “Can you believe this place? It’s like something out of a storybook! The spire, the walkways, the wards—I mean, I can’t even imagine what they’re working on in there! It’s like everything was planned down to the tiniest detail! And the work we’re going to do—I mean, dangerous, yes, but thrilling, right?”

<!-- paragraph:1532 para_id:000005FC style:Normal -->
The words spilled out of her in a flood, and Davian found himself nodding mechanically, letting her chatter wash over him. At least she was consistent, he thought wryly.

<!-- paragraph:1533 para_id:000005FD style:Normal -->
Kolvar and Jessa exchanged amused glances, but neither of them joined in, leaving Davian to weather the full brunt of Tanelle’s enthusiasm alone.

<!-- paragraph:1534 para_id:000005FE style:Normal -->
He allowed himself a small, resigned sigh. This was going to be a long day.

<!-- paragraph:1535 para_id:000005FF style:Normal -->
The hum of conversation in the outbuilding quieted as the five supervisors filed in. Each carried a stack of documents, their faces a mix of focus and mild tension. The supervisor for group one—a lean man with an angular face and a clipped, efficient manner—stopped in front of a large table and motioned for the members of his combined group to gather around.

<!-- paragraph:1536 para_id:00000600 style:Normal -->
“I’m Marek,” he began briskly, spreading a set of schematics across the table. “I’m sure you know what my role is here. Let’s get to work.”

<!-- paragraph:1537 para_id:00000601 style:Normal -->
Davian joined the circle, his gaze dropping to the intricate diagrams as Marek began explaining the project. The schematics detailed the wards surrounding the treasury, their overlapping layers of zän arranged with maddening precision. Davian frowned slightly. The designs offered little insight into the spire’s overall layout.

<!-- paragraph:1538 para_id:00000602 style:Normal -->
Annoying, he thought, but not unexpected. These diagrams weren’t meant to be maps—they were technical tools for zänwrights. He set the frustration aside and focused on understanding the schematics, knowing that the layout of the spire itself would become clearer with time.

<!-- paragraph:1539 para_id:00000603 style:Normal -->
As Marek continued, Davian considered asking about the vault, weighing the risk of showing too much interest. Before he could decide, Tanelle, bless her boundless curiosity, spoke up.

<!-- paragraph:1540 para_id:00000604 style:Normal -->
"What about the vault?" she asked, her voice bright and unbothered. "Who’s going to be working on that?"

<!-- paragraph:1541 para_id:00000605 style:Normal -->
Marek glanced at her, his expression softening slightly. "The vault wards are both deeper and more dangerous than the treasury wards. We will start with the treasury, familiarize everyone with the system, and then move inward to the vault wards when everyone is comfortable enough to not make a lethal mistake.”

<!-- paragraph:1542 para_id:00000606 style:Normal -->
Tanelle nodded enthusiastically, clearly satisfied with the explanation.

<!-- paragraph:1543 para_id:00000607 style:Normal -->
"Now," Marek continued, his tone brisk again, "I’ll be dividing you into smaller teams to handle specific sections of the treasury wards. Pay close attention to your assignments and follow your team leads—they’ll be responsible for ensuring every step is done to standard."

<!-- paragraph:1544 para_id:00000608 style:Normal -->
As Marek began handing out work assignments, Davian made sure to maneuver himself next to Tanelle. She immediately lit up, her face breaking into a delighted grin.

<!-- paragraph:1545 para_id:00000609 style:Normal -->
“Oh, Davian! Looks like we’re working together! Isn’t that great?” she said, her voice full of enthusiasm.

<!-- paragraph:1546 para_id:0000060A style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, forcing a smile that he hoped didn’t look too much like a grimace. “Yeah. It’ll be great.”

<!-- paragraph:1547 para_id:0000060B style:Normal -->
The response seemed to invigorate Tanelle even further as they followed Marek and the others into the spire. She chattered about how exciting the project was, how incredible the citadel seemed, and how lucky they were to be chosen for such important work.

<!-- paragraph:1548 para_id:0000060C style:Normal -->
They reached a pair of imposing double doors flanked by guards. Marek gestured to the guards, who stepped forward and pushed the doors open with a low, resonant groan. Beyond the threshold was a spiral staircase winding upward, its stone steps polished smooth by centuries of use.

<!-- paragraph:1549 para_id:0000060D style:Normal -->
Davian’s gaze lingered on the staircase. The treasury—and likely the vaults—were positioned high within the spire, just as he had guessed.

<!-- paragraph:1550 para_id:0000060E style:Normal -->
Marek led the group forward, and they began their ascent. The air grew cooler as they climbed, the walls lined with narrow arrow slits that allowed faint streams of light to filter in. Davian caught glimpses of the ground growing farther and farther away through the slits, the courtyard below shrinking with every turn of the spiral.

<!-- paragraph:1551 para_id:0000060F style:Normal -->
Tanelle, undeterred by the climb, kept talking. “I mean, have you seen the way the spire is built? It’s like something out of a storybook, don’t you think? And the wards—oh, the wards! They’re incredible. I’ve been trying to imagine how they must function with the Registry, but I can’t wait to see them up close. And—oh—”

<!-- paragraph:1552 para_id:00000610 style:Normal -->
She paused, taking a breath before continuing in shorter bursts. “And you—don’t you think—this is all—just—fascinating?”

<!-- paragraph:1553 para_id:00000611 style:Normal -->
Davian glanced at her out of the corner of his eye. Despite the strain in her voice, she pressed on with unrelenting enthusiasm, her words tumbling out between breaths.

<!-- paragraph:1554 para_id:00000612 style:Normal -->
He couldn’t help but marvel at her conversational stamina. The staircase seemed endless, the climb steep enough to leave most people silent with exertion. Yet Tanelle managed to fill the air with a steady stream of commentary, as though she were fueled by sheer excitement alone.

<!-- paragraph:1555 para_id:00000613 style:Normal -->
Davian gave a noncommittal hum, hoping it would suffice to keep her happy. At least her chatter distracted from the effort of the climb.

<!-- paragraph:1556 para_id:00000614 style:Normal -->
Ahead, Marek’s steady pace never wavered. The group ascended in silence save for the faint shuffle of boots on stone and Tanelle’s persistent, breathless monologue.

<!-- paragraph:1557 para_id:00000615 style:Normal -->
The group finally reached the top of the staircase, and Davian found himself face-to-face with yet another set of heavy double doors, flanked by another pair of guards. The zänwrights paused, catching their breath after the grueling climb.

<!-- paragraph:1558 para_id:00000616 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Marek said dryly, glancing back at the group, “I didn’t realize zänwright work would require this much stamina. Consider it a bonus fitness regimen.”

<!-- paragraph:1559 para_id:00000617 style:Normal -->
There were a few tired chuckles, but the group remained mostly silent as they gathered themselves and entered through the doors. Davian leaned lightly against the wall, his gaze taking in the space around them.

<!-- paragraph:1560 para_id:00000618 style:Normal -->
The area was brightly lit, the ceiling lined with glowing zän that bathed the room in an even, warm light. It was an open chamber, the high, domed ceiling soaring far above them. The sheer scale of the space was staggering. Davian couldn’t help but marvel at the architecture—such an immense structure seemed impossible to support without magic.

<!-- paragraph:1561 para_id:00000619 style:Normal -->
But it wasn’t the room itself that captured his attention.

<!-- paragraph:1562 para_id:0000061A style:Normal -->
Floating in the center of the chamber, suspended by nothing, was an enormous stone sphere. Its surface shimmered faintly, as though alive with a subtle, internal light. The sphere radiated a quiet power, an almost palpable presence that seemed to hum in the air around it.

<!-- paragraph:1563 para_id:0000061B style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes remained fixed on the sphere, its sheer size and strange presence dominating the room. As he looked closer, he noticed something unexpected: a doorway, a dark opening cut into the stone surface. There was no visible way to reach it—no stairs, no walkways, no bridges. The sphere seemed to float completely untethered.

<!-- paragraph:1564 para_id:0000061C style:Normal -->
“This,” Marek said, gesturing toward the massive stone sphere, “is the treasury itself. Everything stored within is secured inside that sphere.”

<!-- paragraph:1565 para_id:0000061D style:Normal -->
A ripple of murmurs passed through the group as they stared at the monolithic structure, their awe mingling with confusion.

<!-- paragraph:1566 para_id:0000061E style:Normal -->
“How do we get to it?” Tanelle asked, her voice tinged with curiosity.

<!-- paragraph:1567 para_id:0000061F style:Normal -->
Without answering, Marek stepped further into the room. He bent down and grasped a rope secured by an iron ring to the floor, then paused and crouched slightly before springing upward. To the group’s astonishment, instead of landing back on the ground, Marek floated effortlessly into the air.

<!-- paragraph:1568 para_id:00000620 style:Normal -->
As he drifted higher, he pulled on the rope and spun to face them. “This chamber,” he explained, his voice calm and steady, “has the ability to render anything within it weightless. It’s one of the many security measures built into the spire. The Registry can enable or disable this effect at will, even targeting specific individuals or objects.”

<!-- paragraph:1569 para_id:00000621 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:78 --><!-- comment-start:79 --><!-- comment-start:80 -->He gestured to the doorway in the sphere. “When enabled, the lack of gravity makes accessing the treasury possible—but only for those with permission. If you’re not on the Registry’s approved list, well…” He trailed off, letting the implication hang in the air.<!-- comment-end:78 --><!-- comment-ref:78 --><!-- comment-end:79 --><!-- comment-ref:79 --><!-- comment-end:80 --><!-- comment-ref:80 -->

<!-- paragraph:1570 para_id:00000622 style:Normal -->
Tanelle’s wide eyes darted between Marek and the sphere. “That’s… incredible,” she said, her voice filled with wonder.

<!-- paragraph:1571 para_id:00000623 style:Normal -->
Davian’s mind raced as he absorbed the information. The weightlessness was not just a convenience—it was a highly effective defense. Without access to the Registry, attempting to reach the sphere would be nearly impossible. The room could become a deadly trap as easily as it could be an access point.

<!-- paragraph:1572 para_id:00000624 style:Normal -->
Marek pulled himself back down with unpracticed movements. Eventually, his boots landed heavily on the floor.

<!-- paragraph:1573 para_id:00000625 style:Normal -->
“I think we’ll all need to spend some time today getting used to the chamber,” he said, addressing the group. “The weightlessness only takes effect once you stop touching the floor. Start slow—don’t jump too hard, and never jump without a rope or an anchor point. The last thing we need is someone floating out of reach.”

<!-- paragraph:1574 para_id:00000626 style:Normal -->
Davian’s gaze shifted to Tanelle. She was already bouncing slightly on the balls of her feet, her wide eyes filled with excitement as she glanced between the floor and the sphere. He suppressed a groan, hoping her eagerness wouldn’t translate into recklessness.

<!-- paragraph:1575 para_id:00000627 style:Normal -->
“All right,” Marek said, clapping his hands once. “Take the next few minutes to get a feel for the room. Move carefully, test your footing, and learn how to control your movements. We’ll begin ward work once everyone is comfortable.”

<!-- paragraph:1576 para_id:00000628 style:Normal -->
Davian adapted to the weightlessness faster than he expected. The sensation was strange at first—his body unsure of how to respond without the usual pull of gravity—but he quickly found a rhythm. Small, controlled movements worked best, and with a bit of trial and error, he managed to navigate the chamber with relative ease.

<!-- paragraph:1577 para_id:00000629 style:Normal -->
Tanelle, unsurprisingly, took a different approach. Her initial excitement translated into overzealous jumps and flailing attempts to stabilize herself. Twice, Davian had to intercept her mid-air, gently redirecting her trajectory before she could crash into one of the walls or another zänwright.

<!-- paragraph:1578 para_id:0000062A style:Normal -->
“Thanks, Davian!” she chirped breathlessly after the second save, her cheeks flushed with exertion.

<!-- paragraph:1579 para_id:0000062B style:Normal -->
He gave her a tight-lipped nod, watching as she drifted off again. Her enthusiasm was exhausting, but at least it gave him an opportunity.

<!-- paragraph:1580 para_id:0000062C style:Normal -->
Using one of her aimless drifts as cover, Davian floated past the massive stone sphere. Up close, the treasury was even more imposing than it had appeared from the floor. The surface of the stone was etched with intricate carvings, zän that shimmered faintly in the light of the chamber.

<!-- paragraph:1581 para_id:0000062D style:Normal -->
His gaze lingered on the dark doorway cut into its side. Beyond that threshold lay the heart of the treasury—and likely, at its very center, the vault. He imagined layer upon layer of overlapping wards, each one more intricate and deadly than the last, shielding the citadel’s most valuable secrets.

<!-- paragraph:1582 para_id:0000062E style:Normal -->
As he floated past, a strange sensation prickled at the edge of his awareness. It was as though he could feel the Heart within, pulsing faintly with power, like a heartbeat calling to him through the stone. Without thinking, he found himself speaking.

<!-- paragraph:1583 para_id:0000062F style:Normal -->
“What do you think is in there?” he asked absently, glancing toward Tanelle as she floated nearby.

<!-- paragraph:1584 para_id:00000630 style:Normal -->
Tanelle’s eyes widened, her lips already parting to answer. But before she could speak, Marek’s voice echoed through the chamber, sharp and commanding.

<!-- paragraph:1585 para_id:00000631 style:Normal -->
“All right, everyone, back down to the ground!” he called. “Time to hand out assignments.”

<!-- paragraph:1586 para_id:00000632 style:Normal -->
Davian gave himself a small push toward the floor, using the weightlessness to glide downward in controlled arcs. Tanelle followed, chattering excitedly about how fun the chamber was and how she couldn’t wait to see what they’d be working on.

<!-- paragraph:1587 para_id:00000633 style:Normal -->
When his boots touched the floor, Davian glanced back at the sphere one last time. The feeling was gone, leaving him uncertain whether it had been real at all.

<!-- paragraph:1588 para_id:00000634 style:Normal -->
Marek waited for the group to gather, his expression stern but composed. “Now that you’ve had a chance to familiarize yourselves with the chamber,” he began, “we’re going to start the real work.”

<!-- paragraph:1589 para_id:00000635 style:Normal -->
Marek handed out the assignments with his usual clipped efficiency, though the tasks themselves quickly proved to be as tedious. Each zänwright was responsible for meticulously inspecting every zä etched onto the outer walls of the chamber and the exterior surface of the treasury sphere. The work was precise, redundant, and utterly unforgiving—no zä could be overlooked.

<!-- paragraph:1590 para_id:00000636 style:Normal -->
Davian positioned himself so that he and Tanelle were assigned to the sphere. As they floated into position, he offered to start by assisting her.

<!-- paragraph:1591 para_id:00000637 style:Normal -->
“It’s a new environment, can’t be too careful,” he added, keeping his tone casual.

<!-- paragraph:1592 para_id:00000638 style:Normal -->
Tanelle, as usual, was delighted. “Oh, absolutely! I love when we can work together on something like this. You’re going to love it—it’s so satisfying when the zän start to shine again, don’t you think?”

<!-- paragraph:1593 para_id:00000639 style:Normal -->
Davian merely nodded, grateful she didn’t question his motives.

<!-- paragraph:1594 para_id:0000063A style:Normal -->
As they floated alongside the massive stone surface, Davian quickly realized just how intricate the task was. Any zä that had lost its magical potency had to be carefully re-etched, and certain sequences needed to be replaced in a specific order to ensure the system’s functionality wasn’t disrupted during the restoration. It was painstaking work that demanded focus and precision.

<!-- paragraph:1595 para_id:0000063B style:Normal -->
Tanelle, to his surprise, was remarkably skilled at it. She worked with a speed and confidence that belied her bubbly personality, her hands moving deftly as she re-etched worn zän and traced new sequences into place. She never seemed to need to check the diagrams, somehow having already memorized the work order.

<!-- paragraph:1596 para_id:0000063C style:Normal -->
What was even more remarkable was that she never stopped talking.

<!-- paragraph:1597 para_id:0000063D style:Normal -->
“And this one here,” she said, pointing to a faintly glowing glyph, “is part of a stabilizing sequence, see? It keeps the wards from overloading when the Registry adjusts the flow of magic. You’ve got to make sure it’s aligned just right—oh, and these auxiliary zän here? They act like a magical buffer, so if anything goes wrong…”

<!-- paragraph:1598 para_id:0000063E style:Normal -->
Her voice trailed off into another enthusiastic explanation, but Davian wasn’t annoyed. For once, her chatter served a purpose. Every detail she shared gave him a clearer picture of the work, turning what could have been an overwhelming task into something he could manage.

<!-- paragraph:1599 para_id:0000063F style:Normal -->
He nodded along, occasionally murmuring an acknowledgment or asking a question when something wasn’t clear. For all her incessant talking, Tanelle had a knack for teaching without realizing it.

<!-- paragraph:1600 para_id:00000640 style:Normal -->
As Davian methodically traced the zän, his focus was broken by movement below. His gaze shifted, and his stomach tightened as he saw Lady Maya enter the room.

<!-- paragraph:1601 para_id:00000641 style:Normal -->
Her presence immediately commanded attention, though she seemed oblivious to the workers pausing mid-task to glance her way. She lifted her hands, and small jets of flame flared from her palms, propelling her gracefully into the air. With effortless precision, she floated upward, her trajectory controlled with subtle bursts of fire.

<!-- paragraph:1602 para_id:00000642 style:Normal -->
Davian’s jaw tightened. Of course she’d find a way to flaunt her power.

<!-- paragraph:1603 para_id:00000643 style:Normal -->
She wove her way around the chamber, her sharp eyes scanning everything—the zän, the workers, the sphere itself. To Davian, it felt like she was inspecting their work, and the thought rankled. Did she really think so little of them that she couldn’t trust them to do their jobs correctly?

<!-- paragraph:1604 para_id:00000644 style:Normal -->
As she ascended, her path curved toward him and Tanelle. Davian’s annoyance flared hotter. Tanelle, on the other hand, was utterly starstruck. The moment Maya floated close, Tanelle gave an awkward bow in the weightless air, nearly spinning herself sideways in her eagerness.

<!-- paragraph:1605 para_id:00000645 style:Normal -->
“It’s such an honor to meet you, Lady Maya!” Tanelle gushed, her voice filled with awe. “I can’t believe I get to work on something so important under your leadership! This is just—this is incredible!”

<!-- paragraph:1606 para_id:00000646 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:81 -->Davian, meanwhile, found himself staring at her. His eyes traced the sharp line of her dark hair, the way it framed her face, and her piercing dark eyes framed on one side by the soft glow of her sigil. He remembered her standing on the wall, facial sigil ablaze, beams of fire streaming from her outstretched hands while flames lit her silhouette against the dark sky. <!-- comment-end:81 --><!-- comment-ref:81 -->

<!-- paragraph:1607 para_id:00000647 style:Normal -->
His pulse quickened, a heat rising to his face. He hated her family—hated everything they represented.

<!-- paragraph:1608 para_id:00000648 style:Normal -->
She turned her gaze to him then, her dark eyes locking onto his. Her expression shifted slightly, her brow furrowing in faint confusion.

<!-- paragraph:1609 para_id:00000649 style:Normal -->
Davian realized, with a jolt, that he had been glaring at her.

<!-- paragraph:1610 para_id:0000064A style:Normal -->
Quickly, he dropped his gaze, masking the tension in his features. He forced himself to bow slightly, the motion stiff and awkward. “My lady,” he muttered, his voice low and tight.

<!-- paragraph:1611 para_id:0000064B style:Normal -->
When he glanced up again, Maya was already moving away, her trajectory carrying her farther into the chamber.

<!-- paragraph:1612 para_id:0000064C style:Normal -->
As Davian continued working, his focus was momentarily broken by movement below. He glanced down and spotted Niall at the base of the chamber, standing near Marek and another supervisor. The sight of him sent a wave of cold anger through Davian, his mind immediately turning to the mission that loomed over everything he did here.

<!-- paragraph:1613 para_id:0000064D style:Normal -->
A thought began to take shape, sparked by the massive sphere floating above the room. Slowly, he turned to Tanelle, who was still chattering away about the nuances of zä alignment.

<!-- paragraph:1614 para_id:0000064E style:Normal -->
“Tanelle,” he interrupted, keeping his tone light, “how many of the zän would have to go inactive before the sphere itself… fell?”

<!-- paragraph:1615 para_id:0000064F style:Normal -->
Tanelle blinked at him, startled by the question, then let out a soft laugh. “Oh, that’s funny! I mean, I’ve never thought about it, but probably more than half? There’s a lot of redundancy in the system. The zän are designed to support one another, so losing a few here and there wouldn’t do much. Why do you ask?”

<!-- paragraph:1616 para_id:00000650 style:Normal -->
Davian forced a shrug, pretending to focus on a nearby glyph. “Just curious about how it all works.”

<!-- paragraph:1617 para_id:00000651 style:Normal -->
She smiled, clearly unbothered. “Well, it would take something massive to disrupt it that badly. Like, coordinated sabotage or something—though I’m sure that’s not happening.” She gave a bright, airy laugh and floated a little farther along the sphere.

<!-- paragraph:1618 para_id:00000652 style:Normal -->
Davian watched her go, his mind turning over the possibilities. It would be difficult - but not impossible - if he stretched his magic to its limits. If he could disable enough of the zän, the sphere would lose its magical support. Gravity would take care of the rest.

<!-- paragraph:1619 para_id:00000653 style:Normal -->
His gaze drifted downward, back to where Niall stood below, his figure small but unmistakable against the polished floor. A dark thought crept into Davian’s mind, unbidden yet irresistibly satisfying. Poetic justice, he thought. Niall crushed under the weight of his own wealth.

<!-- paragraph:1620 para_id:00000654 style:Normal -->
The idea sharpened as he considered the details. If the treasury sphere fell, it might shatter on impact, breaking open the layers of wards protecting the Heart. In the ensuing chaos, he could seize it and escape, using the confusion as cover.

<!-- paragraph:1621 para_id:00000655 style:Normal -->
He took a deep breath, forcing his rising tension back under control. One step at a time, he reminded himself. For now, he needed to stay unnoticed, learn everything he could, and wait.

<!-- paragraph:1622 para_id:00000656 style:Normal -->
And when the time came, he would be ready.

<!-- paragraph:1627 para_id:0000065B style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 11

<!-- paragraph:1628 para_id:0000065C style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 11

<!-- paragraph:1630 para_id:0000065E style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1632 para_id:00000660 style:Normal -->
The spiral staircase seemed to stretch endlessly before Maya, the stone steps worn smooth by centuries of use. Her hand trailed lightly along the cool wall as she descended, the rhythmic sound of her boots echoing softly in the confined space. The air here was cooler, carrying the faint scent of stone and magic, and it usually helped her focus.

<!-- paragraph:1633 para_id:00000661 style:Normal -->
Today, though, her thoughts refused to settle.

<!-- paragraph:1634 para_id:00000662 style:Normal -->
The wards project was starting off well enough, a relief after the initial chaos following her assassination attempt. The contractors seemed competent enough, and the supervisors had maintained order without issue. Yet, her mind lingered on an unsettling interaction from moments before.

<!-- paragraph:1635 para_id:00000663 style:Normal -->
Most people - other than her father, Alden, and Erynn - struggled to meet her gaze. The contractors were either too intimidated or too deferential, practically bowing themselves into knots when she entered a room. She was used to that. Expected it, even.

<!-- paragraph:1636 para_id:00000664 style:Normal -->
But this one had been different.

<!-- paragraph:1637 para_id:00000665 style:Normal -->
His dark eyes had locked onto hers, sharp and unyielding, and for a fleeting moment, she’d felt pinned beneath that gaze. There hadn’t been the usual fear or awe, nor even the neutral professionalism she might have expected. No, his look had carried something else—something raw, smoldering just beneath the surface.

<!-- paragraph:1638 para_id:00000666 style:Normal -->
Anger? she thought, frowning slightly as she descended another step. That didn’t make sense. Why would he be angry? She should dismiss the idea—it was ridiculous. Maybe he just didn’t know how to respond.

<!-- paragraph:1639 para_id:00000667 style:Normal -->
Yes, that was more likely. Perhaps he was overwhelmed or uncertain, unsure how to carry himself in her presence. That would explain the tension in his posture, the intensity in his gaze. And yet, the thought didn’t quite sit right. There had been something deliberate about the way he’d looked at her, something that felt anything but unsure.

<!-- paragraph:1640 para_id:00000668 style:Normal -->
Her thoughts strayed further, unbidden, to his face. His features were strong, angular, with a jaw set tight enough to suggest he was holding something back. His brown hair was a shade too dark to be called chestnut, yet it gleamed faintly in the zä light, tousled just enough to seem effortless. And those <!-- comment-start:82 -->arms<!-- comment-end:82 --><!-- comment-ref:82 -->—

<!-- paragraph:1641 para_id:00000669 style:Normal -->
Maya caught herself mid-thought, heat rising unbidden to her cheeks. What am I doing?

<!-- paragraph:1642 para_id:0000066A style:Normal -->
But it was hard to push the image aside. His muscles had been taut, his arms flexing subtly as though preparing for action, tension radiating from him even in the weightless air. It had been disconcerting—and undeniably magnetic.

<!-- paragraph:1643 para_id:0000066B style:Normal -->
She shook her head, pressing her lips into a thin line. No one had ever looked at her like that before—not with something so charged, so unrefined. It wasn’t the polished charm of a suitor or the false reverence of a subordinate. It was something darker, more primal.

<!-- paragraph:1644 para_id:0000066C style:Normal -->
And it had unnerved her.

<!-- paragraph:1645 para_id:0000066D style:Normal -->
As she rounded another turn of the staircase, Maya forced herself to refocus. The wards needed her attention, and she had no time to waste on foolish distractions. He was just another contractor, she reminded herself, a name she didn’t even know. Whatever that moment had been, it was irrelevant.

<!-- paragraph:1646 para_id:0000066E style:Normal -->
She quickened her pace, hoping the descent would help clear her head.

<!-- paragraph:1647 para_id:0000066F style:Normal -->
As Maya reached the bottom of the staircase, the air grew warmer, carrying the faint hum of activity from the nearby corridors. Sitting on a bench near the base was Erynn, her posture relaxed but her sharp eyes immediately turning to Maya as she approached.

<!-- paragraph:1648 para_id:00000670 style:Normal -->
“You should really come up the stairs with me sometime,” Maya said, a hint of teasing in her tone as she stopped beside her. “It would do you some good.”

<!-- paragraph:1649 para_id:00000671 style:Normal -->
Erynn gave a soft laugh, waving a hand dismissively. “Oh, no, thank you. By the time I got up there, I’d be so sweaty and out of breath that I’d be no use to you. Besides,” she added with a grin, “it’s not like you need me up there in the treasury room. You’ve got it all under control, as always.”

<!-- paragraph:1650 para_id:00000672 style:Normal -->
Maya raised an eyebrow, crossing her arms. “That’s hardly the point.”

<!-- paragraph:1651 para_id:00000673 style:Normal -->
Erynn leaned back slightly, looking unabashed. “I’m just being practical. I stay down here so I can be ready and fresh when you come back. Someone has to make sure you don’t immediately march off to something else without stopping to think.”

<!-- paragraph:1652 para_id:00000674 style:Normal -->
Maya rolled her eyes as she started walking toward the corridor leading to her private chambers. “Convenient excuse. Anyway, I’m going back to my quarters.”

<!-- paragraph:1653 para_id:00000675 style:Normal -->
Erynn trailed after her, a curious glint in her eye. “Does this have to do with...” She leaned closer and dropped her voice to a whisper, “...the journal thing?”

<!-- paragraph:1654 para_id:00000676 style:Normal -->
Maya stiffened slightly but shook her head. “I just feel like taking some afternoon rest,” she replied evenly, her tone deliberately casual.

<!-- paragraph:1655 para_id:00000677 style:Normal -->
Erynn raised an eyebrow.

<!-- paragraph:1656 para_id:00000678 style:Normal -->
When they reached Maya’s quarters, the familiar sight of two guards stationed at the door greeted them. One of the guards, a tall, dark-haired man with sharp features, glanced their way briefly before resuming his stoic watch.

<!-- paragraph:1657 para_id:00000679 style:Normal -->
Erynn slowed her pace, and Maya didn’t miss the way her friend’s posture shifted, her steps turning just a touch lighter, her gaze lingering on the taller guard for a beat too long.

<!-- paragraph:1658 para_id:0000067A style:Normal -->
“I don’t think you’ll need me, then,” Erynn said, her tone far too nonchalant as she gestured toward the guards. “Maybe I’ll just stay out here for a bit.”

<!-- paragraph:1659 para_id:0000067B style:Normal -->
Maya followed Erynn’s gaze, noting the faint smile curling at the edges of her lips. She sighed, her own lips quirking in a faint smirk. “Fine. As long as you stay right outside.”

<!-- paragraph:1660 para_id:0000067C style:Normal -->
Erynn grinned, her eyes lighting up. “Of course. You know me—always responsible.”

<!-- paragraph:1661 para_id:0000067D style:Normal -->
Maya snorted softly and turned to enter her quarters.

<!-- paragraph:1662 para_id:0000067E style:Normal -->
The faint murmur of Erynn’s voice drifted through the closed door as Maya settled into the room. “So,” Erynn was saying to the guard, her tone effortlessly casual, “standing here all day must be fascinating.”

<!-- paragraph:1663 para_id:0000067F style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. Whatever Erynn was up to, at least she wouldn’t be wandering far.

<!-- paragraph:1664 para_id:00000680 style:Normal -->
She moved to the chest at the foot of her bed, her fingers brushing over the ornate carvings before lifting the lid. Inside, nestled beneath a neatly folded shawl, was the journal.

<!-- paragraph:1665 para_id:00000681 style:Normal -->
She sat on the edge of her bed, turning the journal over in her hands. The last time she’d opened it, the memory had been overwhelming—vivid and visceral, the power almost too much to contain. For a moment, a flicker of doubt crossed her mind. Could this be dangerous? Could she be exposing herself to something she wasn’t ready to face?

<!-- paragraph:1666 para_id:00000682 style:Normal -->
She shook her head, banishing the thought. No. If there’s danger, I’ll face it. I can’t rely on Niall to tell me anything about her. This is all I have.

<!-- paragraph:1667 para_id:00000683 style:Normal -->
With a steady breath, <!-- comment-start:83 --><!-- comment-start:84 -->she flipped to the next page, the shimmering zän seeming to shift and ripple under her gaze. She spoke the key incantation, the unfamiliar words rolling off her tongue.<!-- comment-end:83 --><!-- comment-ref:83 --><!-- comment-end:84 --><!-- comment-ref:84 -->

<!-- paragraph:1668 para_id:00000684 style:Normal -->
The room vanished.

<!-- paragraph:1669 para_id:00000685 style:Normal -->
She - Alara - stood beneath the shade of a pavilion, her arms crossed as she observed the heated argument between an elderly man—his weathered face set in a stern scowl—and the High Clade magus standing across from him. The open field stretched endlessly around them, the harsh sun above muted only by the canopy’s fabric, but neither man seemed to notice the heat.

<!-- paragraph:1670 para_id:00000686 style:Normal -->
The Elarion magus was striking in his appearance, with dark brown hair cropped close to his head and blunt, soldierly features that gave him an air of unyielding discipline. His arms were bare, faint traces of lightning scars etched into the skin of his arms like trophies. He was younger than Maya had expected, but the way he carried himself—with calm authority and a confidence that bordered on arrogance—left no doubt as to who he was. Niall Rezin. The most powerful of the High Clade magi.

<!-- paragraph:1671 para_id:00000687 style:Normal -->
The voice of the man next to her - her father - rose, cutting through the tense quiet of the field. “You speak of peace and unity, yet you march your armies across our lands and demand submission! Do not insult us with empty platitudes.”

<!-- paragraph:1672 para_id:00000688 style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t flinch. His expression remained calm, his tone measured. “This conflict can end,” he said, his gaze steady. “Your people have much to gain by allying with Elarion. Prosperity, protection, order—this war doesn’t have to go on.”

<!-- paragraph:1673 para_id:00000689 style:Normal -->
She barely heard the response. Her focus had narrowed on the man before her—the one who had come here as a representative of everything she hated about Elarion. He wasn’t just a soldier. He was the symbol of the system she despised, the one that had sent wave after wave of Low Clade magi to their deaths in this pointless war. And now, they had sent him.

<!-- paragraph:1674 para_id:0000068A style:Normal -->
Her lips pressed into a thin line. Before either man could speak again, she stepped forward, interrupting the exchange.

<!-- paragraph:1675 para_id:0000068B style:Normal -->
Her dark eyes locked onto Niall, their intensity unwavering. “The only thing we would gain by allying with Elarion,” she said, her voice cold and sharp as glass, “is a caste system obsessed with magical killing potential.”

<!-- paragraph:1676 para_id:0000068C style:Normal -->
For the first time, the magus faltered. His composure slipped just enough for her to notice the faint flush rising to his face. His mouth opened slightly as if to respond, but no words came immediately.

<!-- paragraph:1677 para_id:0000068D style:Normal -->
As he stood there, taken aback, Alara’s gaze lingered on him a moment longer than she intended. His blunt, rugged features were undeniably handsome in a way she didn’t want to admit, and the faint lightning scars that adorned his bare arms gave him a raw, almost unpolished appeal.

<!-- paragraph:1678 para_id:0000068E style:Normal -->
“I wish,” he said finally, his voice quieter now, “that we were not on opposite sides of this conflict.”

<!-- paragraph:1679 para_id:0000068F style:Normal -->
The sincerity in his words startled her, though she quickly masked the reaction. She felt a faint, unwelcome heat rise to her cheeks. Her jaw tightened. Whatever this man felt, whatever regrets he might harbor, they didn’t matter.

<!-- paragraph:1680 para_id:00000690 style:Normal -->
Alara <!-- comment-start:85 --><!-- comment-start:86 -->took another step forward, her voice dropping into a low, cutting tone. “I have burned every other magus from Elarion out of the fabric of reality,” she said, her words slicing through the air like a blade. “If you try to threaten my people, I will burn you out, too.”<!-- comment-end:85 --><!-- comment-ref:85 --><!-- comment-end:86 --><!-- comment-ref:86 -->

<!-- paragraph:1681 para_id:00000691 style:Normal -->
The flush on his face deepened, but he held her gaze. She turned sharply, her cloak sweeping behind her as she left the pavilion without another word.

<!-- paragraph:1682 para_id:00000692 style:Normal -->
Maya gasped, her hand flying to her chest as her room came into focus around her. The warm light of the enchanted sconces bathed the familiar stone walls, grounding her in the present. She was back—back in her quarters, the journal still open in her trembling hands.

<!-- paragraph:1683 para_id:00000693 style:Normal -->
Her heart pounded as the memory replayed in her mind, vivid and unrelenting. Her mother, fierce and unyielding beneath the pavilion. The Elarion magus, striking and confident, standing across from her. The charged air between them, the veiled tension in every word exchanged.

<!-- paragraph:1684 para_id:00000694 style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath caught in her throat. The man her mother had been threatening—challenging, staring down with all the force of her will—had been her father. She hadn’t recognized him at first, younger and unbowed by the weight of the years she knew so well. Her parents had met during the war to unite the outlying cities under Elarion’s rule. The war her mother had fought so fiercely against.

<!-- paragraph:1685 para_id:00000695 style:Normal -->
She set the journal down carefully on the desk, her thoughts spinning. What had happened between them? How had that initial confrontation—so charged, so full of animosity—led to the life they’d built together?

<!-- paragraph:1686 para_id:00000696 style:Normal -->
But as the questions swirled, one thought rose above the rest, sending a chill down her spine.

<!-- paragraph:1687 para_id:00000697 style:Normal -->
The look her father had given her mother at the end of the memory. The way his gaze lingered on her, despite the tension of the moment. It had been startlingly similar to the look that he had given her—the nameless zänwright.

<!-- paragraph:1688 para_id:00000698 style:Normal -->
Maya swallowed hard, her pulse quickening at the thought. It wasn’t the same, she told herself. It couldn’t be. But the connection unsettled her, the memory of his piercing gaze refusing to leave her mind.

<!-- paragraph:1689 para_id:00000699 style:Normal -->
Maya slid the journal back into the chest at the foot of her bed, her hands steady despite the lingering unease in her chest. She closed the lid quietly and stood, smoothing the fabric of her tunic as she turned toward the door.

<!-- paragraph:1690 para_id:0000069A style:Normal -->
When she stepped out, Erynn glanced up from where she was still seated on the bench, her expression shifting from curiosity to mild disappointment.

<!-- paragraph:1691 para_id:0000069B style:Normal -->
“I thought you needed rest?” Erynn asked, standing reluctantly.

<!-- paragraph:1692 para_id:0000069C style:Normal -->
Maya gave her a faint smile. “I decided I don’t need as much as I thought.”

<!-- paragraph:1693 para_id:0000069D style:Normal -->
Erynn cast a wistful look at the tall guard she had been chatting with, her shoulders drooping slightly as she followed Maya down the corridor. “You’re a hard woman to keep up with, you know that?”

<!-- paragraph:1694 para_id:0000069E style:Normal -->
As they walked, the faint echo of their footsteps filled the empty hall. Erynn finally broke the silence. “Where are we going, anyway?”

<!-- paragraph:1695 para_id:0000069F style:Normal -->
Instead of answering, Maya glanced at her sideways, her brow furrowing slightly. “What does it mean when a man stares at you?”

<!-- paragraph:1696 para_id:000006A0 style:Normal -->
Erynn stopped in her tracks, her eyes lighting up with a mix of intrigue and mischief. “Oh, this is interesting. What kind of stare are we talking about?”

<!-- paragraph:1697 para_id:000006A1 style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated, her lips pressing together as she considered her response. “I’m… not sure. Maybe more like a glare?”

<!-- paragraph:1698 para_id:000006A2 style:Normal -->
Erynn raised an eyebrow, the intrigue on her face giving way to confusion. “A glare? That’s not what I expected. Are we talking angry, or like… intense and brooding? Because those are two very different things.”

<!-- paragraph:1699 para_id:000006A3 style:Normal -->
“I don’t know,” Maya admitted, her tone slightly sharper than she intended. “It just felt… focused. Controlled. Like he was trying to figure something out.”

<!-- paragraph:1700 para_id:000006A4 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s head tilted as she studied Maya, a small smile tugging at her lips. “You know, this is a really weird question.”

<!-- paragraph:1701 para_id:000006A5 style:Normal -->
“Forget it,” Maya muttered, quickening her pace down the corridor.

<!-- paragraph:1702 para_id:000006A6 style:Normal -->
“Oh no, you can’t bring something like this up and expect me to just drop it,” Erynn said, jogging a few steps to catch up. “You’re asking about a man glaring at you? This doesn’t exactly scream romance.”

<!-- paragraph:1703 para_id:000006A7 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s grin grew wider, and she leaned in conspiratorially as they walked. “So, who is it?”

<!-- paragraph:1704 para_id:000006A8 style:Normal -->
Maya kept her expression carefully neutral, her gaze fixed straight ahead. “It’s theoretical,” she said flatly.

<!-- paragraph:1705 para_id:000006A9 style:Normal -->
“Theoretical?” Erynn repeated, her voice laced with disbelief. “You’re asking what it means when a man stares at you—glares at you, actually—and you’re telling me it’s just a theory?”

<!-- paragraph:1706 para_id:000006AA style:Normal -->
“It is,” Maya interrupted firmly - she certainly wasn’t going to tell Erynn she was thinking about a random contractor whose name she didn’t even know.

<!-- paragraph:1707 para_id:000006AB style:Normal -->
As they turned down another corridor, Erynn glanced at Maya, her brows raised in curiosity. “Are you going to tell me where we’re heading now?”

<!-- paragraph:1708 para_id:000006AC style:Normal -->
“Alden’s office,” Maya said curtly, her tone leaving little room for further questions.

<!-- paragraph:1709 para_id:000006AD style:Normal -->
Erynn let out a small sigh. “Of course. Can’t let him go a single day without a check-in, can you?”

<!-- paragraph:1710 para_id:000006AE style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond as she kept walking. The attack on their citadel still hung over her like a storm cloud, its implications gnawing at her. If they could pin it on Drakhal, they could act decisively, rally their allies, and cut through the growing uncertainty.

<!-- paragraph:1711 para_id:000006AF style:Normal -->
She arrived at the door to Alden’s office and pushed it open, the faint smell of parchment and ink greeting her. Alden, seated behind his cluttered desk, looked up as she entered. His dark eyes met hers, and though his expression was neutral, there was an unmistakable weariness in his posture.

<!-- paragraph:1712 para_id:000006B0 style:Normal -->
“Lady Maya,” he said, setting down the document he had been reviewing. “What brings you here?”

<!-- paragraph:1713 para_id:000006B1 style:Normal -->
“Any progress?” she asked without preamble, crossing the room to stand in front of his desk. “On identifying the attackers? Can we pin this on Drakhal?”

<!-- paragraph:1714 para_id:000006B2 style:Normal -->
Alden leaned back in his chair, his fingers steepling thoughtfully. “At this point, it won’t matter.”

<!-- paragraph:1715 para_id:000006B3 style:Normal -->
Maya’s brow furrowed. “What do you mean, it won’t matter? It’s only been two days.”

<!-- paragraph:1716 para_id:000006B4 style:Normal -->
“That’s an eternity in Elarion,” Alden replied, his voice calm but edged with grim certainty. “The High Clades have already moved on. Once the news about the attack on Sorevin got out, it spread through the city like wildfire. The High Clades are watching each other closely now, wondering which one of them is responsible for thinning out the Low Clades.”

<!-- paragraph:1717 para_id:000006B5 style:Normal -->
Maya’s jaw tightened, her fingers curling into fists at her sides. “Drakhal attacked us. If we can prove it, we can hold them accountable.”

<!-- paragraph:1718 para_id:000006B6 style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression softened, but his tone remained firm. “Maya, even if we could present irrefutable proof tomorrow, it wouldn’t undo what’s already in motion. Everyone is scrambling to secure their position. We don’t even know for sure that it was Drakhal.”

<!-- paragraph:1719 para_id:000006B7 style:Normal -->
Maya’s eyes narrowed. “Then what was the point of questioning Tarran?”

<!-- paragraph:1720 para_id:000006B8 style:Normal -->
Alden leaned back in his chair, crossing his arms. “It wasn’t my decision to heal him. If it were up to me, I’d have let the man die.”

<!-- paragraph:1721 para_id:000006B9 style:Normal -->
Maya’s lips pressed into a thin line, but before she could respond, Alden straightened, pulling a small, elegant envelope from the stack of papers on his desk. “Speaking of Drakhal, this came for you.”

<!-- paragraph:1722 para_id:000006BA style:Normal -->
He handed her the envelope, the Drakhal crest embossed on its wax seal. Maya’s name was written in bold, flowing script across the front.

<!-- paragraph:1723 para_id:000006BB style:Normal -->
Her breath caught for a moment as she took it. It was the first time she had ever received a letter addressed specifically to her.

<!-- paragraph:1724 para_id:000006BC style:Normal -->
“What is this?” she asked, breaking the seal carefully.

<!-- paragraph:1725 para_id:000006BD style:Normal -->
“Why don’t you read it and find out?” Alden replied, his tone dry.

<!-- paragraph:1726 para_id:000006BE style:Normal -->
Maya unfolded the letter, her eyes scanning the page. The words were written in the same elegant hand, each stroke deliberate and precise:

<!-- paragraph:1727 para_id:000006BF style:Normal -->
Lady Maya Rezin,
It would be my honor to visit your citadel at your earliest convenience. Please let me know a time that would be amenable for our meeting.
—Caius Drakhal

<!-- paragraph:1728 para_id:000006C0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s brow furrowed as she read. Beside her, Erynn craned her neck ever so slightly, clearly trying not to look like she was reading over Maya’s shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:1729 para_id:000006C1 style:Normal -->
“Now can I ask ‘what is this?’” Maya said, holding the letter out for Alden to see.

<!-- paragraph:1730 para_id:000006C2 style:Normal -->
He glanced at it, his lips curving into a wry smile. “It looks like a request for a meeting,” he said, his tone pointedly neutral.

<!-- paragraph:1731 para_id:000006C3 style:Normal -->
“For what purpose?” Maya pressed.

<!-- paragraph:1732 para_id:000006C4 style:Normal -->
Alden leaned back, his gaze flicking between the letter and Maya. “If I had to guess? A courtship.”

<!-- paragraph:1733 para_id:000006C5 style:Normal -->
Erynn gasped audibly, her eyes widening as she looked between Maya and the letter.

<!-- paragraph:1734 para_id:000006C6 style:Normal -->
Maya stiffened, her mind reeling. “A what?”

<!-- paragraph:1735 para_id:000006C7 style:Normal -->
“Courtship,” Alden repeated, his tone completely matter-of-fact. “It’s not unusual for a High Clade heir to make such overtures, particularly to someone of your standing.”

<!-- paragraph:1736 para_id:000006C8 style:Normal -->
Erynn’s hand flew to her mouth, though it barely muffled her delighted squeal. “A courtship request? From Caius Drakhal? Maya, this is huge!”

<!-- paragraph:1737 para_id:000006C9 style:Normal -->
Maya shot her a glare. “This is ridiculous,” she muttered, folding the letter sharply and slipping it into her pocket.

<!-- paragraph:1738 para_id:000006CA style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression was unreadable, though there was a faint edge of amusement in his voice as he said, “Ridiculous or not, you’ll need to decide how to respond.”

<!-- paragraph:1739 para_id:000006CB style:Normal -->
Maya turned on her heel, her mind racing as she strode out of the room. “Come on, Erynn,” she snapped, her tone brooking no argument.

<!-- paragraph:1740 para_id:000006CC style:Normal -->
Erynn hurried to follow, but not before giving Alden a knowing grin.

<!-- paragraph:1741 para_id:000006CD style:Normal -->
As they made their way down the corridor, Erynn walked briskly to keep up with Maya’s pace, her voice bubbling with excitement.

<!-- paragraph:1742 para_id:000006CE style:Normal -->
“Caius Drakhal, Maya! He’s so handsome—I mean, have you seen him? Green eyes, mahogany hair, those sharp cheekbones... Honestly, he’s what half the women around here dream of.” She clasped her hands dramatically. “And the Drakhals are very well-situated among the High Clades. If anyone’s a catch, it’s him.”

<!-- paragraph:1743 para_id:000006CF style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond, her face set in a tight mask of annoyance.

<!-- paragraph:1744 para_id:000006D0 style:Normal -->
“And let’s not forget the political advantages,” Erynn continued, unfazed.<!-- comment-start:87 --><!-- comment-start:88 --> “If the Drakhals are interested in an alliance with the Rezins,<!-- comment-end:87 --><!-- comment-ref:87 --><!-- comment-end:88 --><!-- comment-ref:88 --> that’s a huge deal. It would solidify your position. Oh, and did I mention how handsome he is?”

<!-- paragraph:1745 para_id:000006D1 style:Normal -->
Maya’s steps quickened, her boots striking the stone floor a little harder than necessary. “Erynn,” she said, her tone sharp enough to make her companion hesitate.

<!-- paragraph:1746 para_id:000006D2 style:Normal -->
“Yes?”

<!-- paragraph:1747 para_id:000006D3 style:Normal -->
“Stop talking.”

<!-- paragraph:1748 para_id:000006D4 style:Normal -->
Erynn blinked, then let out a soft laugh. “All right, fine. But you can’t tell me you’re not at least a little flattered.”

<!-- paragraph:1749 para_id:000006D5 style:Normal -->
Maya shot her a glare, though her cheeks betrayed her with the faintest hint of color.

<!-- paragraph:1750 para_id:000006D6 style:Normal -->
Erynn grinned, falling into step beside her. “This is going to be so interesting,” she murmured, half to herself.

<!-- paragraph:1754 para_id:000006DA style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 12

<!-- paragraph:1755 para_id:000006DB style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 12

<!-- paragraph:1756 para_id:000006DC style:Heading2 -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1757 para_id:000006DD style:Normal -->
Davian worked in silence, the low hum of zän filling the treasury chamber as he carefully etched corrections into the intricate network of magical sequences. Tanelle had been an endless fountain of chatter over the past few days, but today, thankfully, she had been assigned elsewhere. He’d managed to learn enough from her—both from her constant explanations and from cramming zänwright manuals at night—to work competently without showing just how much he’d been faking it.

<!-- paragraph:1758 para_id:000006DE style:Normal -->
The more he studied, the more he grasped the enormous complexity of the sequences that supported the treasury sphere. The sheer amount of redundancy built into the design made it painfully clear how difficult it would be to sabotage. Every stabilization zä had three others reinforcing it. The weightless suspension of the massive vault sphere wasn’t controlled by one sigil or even a dozen, but by a sprawling, interwoven lattice of layered inscriptions. It was a masterwork of magical engineering.

<!-- paragraph:1759 para_id:000006DF style:Normal -->
And he had to break it.

<!-- paragraph:1760 para_id:000006E0 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled slowly and glanced around, checking for any watching eyes. The other workers were absorbed in their own tasks, their focus on the minor zä repairs assigned to each of them.

<!-- paragraph:1761 para_id:000006E1 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:89 -->He reached inward, calling on Subversion, the familiar cold rush of magic curling through his veins. His sigil began to burn under his skin, and he started to force energy through his fingertips as he pressed them against one of the smaller supporting zän. He turned his head slightly, angling his face away from anyone who might glimpse the telltale glow. The goal was simple—drain the magic just enough to deactivate it without setting off any alarms.<!-- comment-end:89 --><!-- comment-ref:89 -->

<!-- paragraph:1762 para_id:000006E2 style:Normal -->
The zä resisted.

<!-- paragraph:1763 para_id:000006E3 style:Normal -->
Davian grit his teeth, sweat beading at his temple as he pushed harder. Damn. He had assumed it would be simple, that a single zä would flicker out under his power like a candle being snuffed. Instead, it fought back, the layers of redundancy bleeding energy into the weakened node before it could fully fade. He had to dig deeper, channeling more magic than he wanted to, forcing his Subversion through the latticework of the treasury’s defenses.

<!-- paragraph:1764 para_id:000006E4 style:Normal -->
At last, the zä died.

<!-- paragraph:1765 para_id:000006E5 style:Normal -->
A tiny victory, but the effort left him cold. He took a step back, rolling his shoulders as he resisted the urge to curse aloud. That was hard. Too hard. And that was only one glyph. He had planned to disable dozens, maybe hundreds, to weaken the entire structure enough that it would collapse under its own weight. But if each one fought back like this, if he had to force his magic through layer after layer of reinforcement, there was no way he could do it fast enough—not without someone noticing.

<!-- paragraph:1766 para_id:000006E6 style:Normal -->
His mind spun. This is impossible.

<!-- paragraph:1767 para_id:000006E7 style:Normal -->
The vault sphere was too well-guarded, its magic too deeply rooted. His original plan—to sever enough of the sequences to make the whole thing come crashing down—felt naive now, a fantasy of clean execution that ignored the sheer scale of what he was trying to do. Even if he worked at this day after day, he’d never get through enough before someone caught on.

<!-- paragraph:1768 para_id:000006E8 style:Normal -->
He clenched his fists, forcing himself to breathe. He couldn’t afford to spiral. He had to do this. He had already come too far. He had spent too much time letting the High Clades decide who lived and died. He wasn’t going to walk away because it was hard.

<!-- paragraph:1769 para_id:000006E9 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed down the creeping doubt, steadying himself with sheer force of will. He would figure it out.

<!-- paragraph:1770 para_id:000006EA style:Normal -->
For now, he would keep experimenting. He would push carefully, one step at a time. And he would find a way to bring the vault down—even if it killed him.

<!-- paragraph:1771 para_id:000006EB style:Normal -->
His reverie was broken by Marek’s voice ringing through the chamber. “Break for lunch,” the supervisor called.

<!-- paragraph:1772 para_id:000006EC style:Normal -->
A few people groaned as they pushed off surfaces and floated back toward the ground. The ropes secured to their belts let them descend smoothly, keeping them from drifting aimlessly. Others, more experienced with the weightless environment, simply adjusted their movement midair and launched themselves toward the stone floor with practiced control.

<!-- paragraph:1773 para_id:000006ED style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled slowly, forcing his tension down along with his rising panic. He gave one last look at the darkened zä he’d drained before letting go of his focus, slipping his expression into something neutral as he reached for his own rope. He tugged gently and let the tension in the line guide him downward, landing with ease. His boots hit the stone floor, and he straightened, rolling his shoulders to dispel the tightness creeping into them.

<!-- paragraph:1774 para_id:000006EE style:Normal -->
Around him, the workers began filing toward the stairs, heading just far enough down the spiraling steps to be outside the weightless chamber. No one wanted to walk the entire way down to the lower levels just to eat—every step taken down meant an exhausting climb back up. Tanelle was already chatting with a few others, moving toward the archway with her usual unrelenting energy.

<!-- paragraph:1775 para_id:000006EF style:Normal -->
He had no interest in joining them.

<!-- paragraph:1776 para_id:000006F0 style:Normal -->
His mind was still caught on the task before him. He needed space to think, and he certainly wasn’t going to get it sitting on a step while Tanelle and the others filled the air with conversation.

<!-- paragraph:1777 para_id:000006F1 style:Normal -->
Making a quick decision, he turned toward Marek, who was still overseeing the others as they drifted toward the stairway.

<!-- paragraph:1778 para_id:000006F2 style:Normal -->
“Mind if I head downstairs?” Davian asked, keeping his tone light. “I need some fresh air.”

<!-- paragraph:1779 para_id:000006F3 style:Normal -->
Marek barely looked up from the notes he was marking. “Do what you want, as long as you’re back in time.”

<!-- paragraph:1780 para_id:000006F4 style:Normal -->
Davian gave a short nod, then slipped past the others before anyone could stop him. He stepped through the archway and onto the narrow spiral staircase that led down the spire, adjusting his footing as the shift back to normal gravity settled into his body. The air here was warm and slightly stifling, carrying the scent of stone and the faint traces of old magic that permeated the fortress.

<!-- paragraph:1781 para_id:000006F5 style:Normal -->
Each step downward felt like a countdown to something he couldn’t name.

<!-- paragraph:1782 para_id:000006F6 style:Normal -->
Davian moved quickly, keeping his shoulders relaxed, his head down, his hands loose at his sides—just another worker on an errand, someone who had no reason to draw attention. But his mind churned, too restless to be soothed by the repetitive rhythm of his descent.

<!-- paragraph:1783 para_id:000006F7 style:Normal -->
He hated being here.

<!-- paragraph:1784 para_id:000006F8 style:Normal -->
Not just in the citadel—though that was bad enough—but being stuck inside it, too deep to leave frequently without suspicion, too embedded in the work to escape when he wanted.
