Jalen had promised he’d be fine, that nothing would happen while Davian was away, but Jalen was a known quantity in the Concord. A target. Someone ambitious might see an opportunity. Someone eager might challenge him to the Trial of Dominion before Davian could be there to ensure he survived it.

<!-- paragraph:1785 para_id:000006F9 style:Normal -->
The thought made his fingers twitch.

<!-- paragraph:1786 para_id:000006FA style:Normal -->
He could almost hear Jalen now, telling him to get his head out of the damn gutter. To focus. Worrying wouldn’t help. But the more time he spent in this place, the more he felt trapped—adrift in a structure built to keep people like him exactly where the High Clades wanted them.

<!-- paragraph:1787 para_id:000006FB style:Normal -->
By the time he realized how far he’d gone, his boots hit the last step with a dull thud, and something small but insistent jabbed into his shin.

<!-- paragraph:1788 para_id:000006FC style:Normal -->
Davian jolted, his immediate instinct to back away, his mind already forming a defensive maneuver—

<!-- paragraph:1789 para_id:000006FD style:Normal -->
Then he looked down.

<!-- paragraph:1790 para_id:000006FE style:Normal -->
A child stood before him, tousled brown hair a mess, his wide green eyes filled with curiosity. His small hand, still half-raised from poking him, twitched at his side. But it wasn’t the child that made Davian tense.

<!-- paragraph:1791 para_id:000006FF style:Normal -->
It was the two guards flanking him.

<!-- paragraph:1792 para_id:00000700 style:Normal -->
They were massive. Armored, poised, weapons hanging at their sides but not out of reach. They weren’t just standard citadel security. Their silent, unyielding presence screamed High Clade elite.

<!-- paragraph:1793 para_id:00000701 style:Normal -->
The guards were staring at him, suspicion clear in their sharp gazes.

<!-- paragraph:1794 para_id:00000702 style:Normal -->
Before he could decide whether to speak or just turn and run, the child beamed up at him. “Hi! Who are you? My name is Selwin. Are you one of Father’s friends?”

<!-- paragraph:1795 para_id:00000703 style:Normal -->
Davian barely had time to process the question before one of the guards spoke, voice firm. “No one is supposed to be here right now.”

<!-- paragraph:1796 para_id:00000704 style:Normal -->
Shit.

<!-- paragraph:1797 para_id:00000705 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed down his first instinct, which was to bolt. Instead, he forced himself to straighten just slightly, keeping his stance relaxed. “I—uh—sorry. I’m a zänwright. Working on the treasury.” He gestured vaguely upward. “Took a break, went for a walk. Didn’t realize how far down I’d gone.”

<!-- paragraph:1798 para_id:00000706 style:Normal -->
The guards didn’t look convinced.

<!-- paragraph:1799 para_id:00000707 style:Normal -->
“You need to leave,” the first one said, stepping forward slightly, his hand resting lightly on the hilt of his weapon.

<!-- paragraph:1800 para_id:00000708 style:Normal -->
But before Davian could nod and obey, Selwin turned toward them with an easy, almost careless certainty. “I want to talk to him instead.”

<!-- paragraph:1801 para_id:00000709 style:Normal -->
The guards hesitated. Davian caught the flicker of uncertainty in their expressions, the way their rigid posture softened just slightly. And that was when it clicked.

<!-- paragraph:1802 para_id:0000070A style:Normal -->
The child’s offhand question. The way the guards—these warriors—had deferred to him. Selwin was a High Clade scion. Davian kept his expression blank, but his gut twisted. Even a child could command him here.

<!-- paragraph:1803 para_id:0000070B style:Normal -->
Not just him—the guards, the workers, anyone who wasn’t born into power. And they obeyed without hesitation, because this was how the world worked. Because this child was High Clade. It didn’t matter that he was small, untested, still years away from his Presentation. His blood alone meant that one day, people like Davian would kneel before him or be crushed under his boot.

<!-- paragraph:1804 para_id:0000070C style:Normal -->
Selwin didn’t seem to realize it, though. He was still looking up at Davian with bright, curious eyes, his expression open and eager. He didn’t have the cold, calculating stare of someone raised to wield power like a blade.

<!-- paragraph:1805 para_id:0000070D style:Normal -->
Not yet.

<!-- paragraph:1806 para_id:0000070E style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled slowly through his nose. He shouldn’t care. He shouldn’t feel anything but disgust for this child, a machine in the making, one more weapon forged to keep the rest of the world in chains. But it was hard to look into that small, beaming face and see a monster.

<!-- paragraph:1807 para_id:0000070F style:Normal -->
“I’m Arrek,” he said instead, the lie settling in his mouth like ash. “And I don’t know your father.”

<!-- paragraph:1808 para_id:00000710 style:Normal -->
Selwin tilted his head, his brow furrowing slightly. “Everyone knows my father.” He said it with the absolute certainty of a child who had never once questioned his world. Then he straightened proudly. “My father is Niall Rezin. He’s the strongest and smartest man in the world.”

<!-- paragraph:1809 para_id:00000711 style:Normal -->
Davian’s breath hitched. But what caught Davian off guard wasn’t the words—it was the way Selwin said them. Without arrogance, without expectation. Just simple, steadfast belief.

<!-- paragraph:1810 para_id:00000712 style:Normal -->
And for one terrible moment, Davian remembered Kael. His little brother, grinning up at him before he’d left home, asking to come with him, sure he could conquer anything to keep them safe. The memory burned in his throat.

<!-- paragraph:1811 para_id:00000713 style:Normal -->
He crouched down, keeping his movements slow, measured. The guards tensed immediately, their hands twitching toward their weapons, but he ignored them.

<!-- paragraph:1812 para_id:00000714 style:Normal -->
He forced himself to smile just slightly. “I didn’t know Niall was your father,” he said, voice light, careful. “That must mean you’re pretty important.”

<!-- paragraph:1813 para_id:00000715 style:Normal -->
Selwin blinked at him, then frowned slightly, like he was thinking very hard about something.

<!-- paragraph:1814 para_id:00000716 style:Normal -->
Then he shook his head.

<!-- paragraph:1815 para_id:00000717 style:Normal -->
“Father used to say that it’s not who you are that makes you important,” Selwin said, his voice suddenly quiet, solemn in a way that didn’t belong in a child’s mouth. “It’s whether you can protect the people you love.”

<!-- paragraph:1816 para_id:00000718 style:Normal -->
Davian stilled. For a moment, he forgot himself, forgot where he was. The words lodged deep, sinking into the raw places he didn’t let himself touch. Can you protect them?

<!-- paragraph:1817 para_id:00000719 style:Normal -->
His fingers curled slightly. “Does he still say that?”

<!-- paragraph:1818 para_id:0000071A style:Normal -->
Selwin hesitated. Then he looked down, his small hands twisting together. “No,” he admitted. “Not since he got sad.”

<!-- paragraph:1819 para_id:0000071B style:Normal -->
Selwin glanced up at him again, as if gauging his reaction, then quickly added, “But it’s okay. Adults get sad sometimes. And then they don’t want to play games with you anymore.” He said it with the matter-of-fact certainty of a child who had decided that was just the way things were.

<!-- paragraph:1820 para_id:0000071C style:Normal -->
Then he brightened, as if shaking the thought away. “I can tell you’re sad too,” he said, staring up at Davian with his deep brown eyes. “I hope you feel better.”

<!-- paragraph:1821 para_id:0000071D style:Normal -->
Before Davian could respond—before he could figure out what the hell to say—Selwin turned and walked away, the guards falling into step behind him without a word.

<!-- paragraph:1822 para_id:0000071E style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t move.

<!-- paragraph:1823 para_id:0000071F style:Normal -->
His hands curled into fists, then slowly relaxed.

<!-- paragraph:1824 para_id:00000720 style:Normal -->
Then, finally, he exhaled, stood, and started back up the stairs.

<!-- paragraph:1825 para_id:00000721 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1826 para_id:00000722 style:Normal -->
By the time evening settled over the citadel, Davian felt like his entire day had been wasted. He had worked for hours—meticulously etching zän, studying the sequences, trying to unravel the labyrinthine redundancies in the treasury sphere’s wards—and he had almost nothing to show for it. His attempts to use Subversion had been slow, painstaking, and barely effective.

<!-- paragraph:1827 para_id:00000723 style:Normal -->
When the workday finally ended, he had no appetite to eat with the others. He didn’t want to listen to Tanelle’s incessant talking, didn’t want to sit among the contractors who had started treating him like one of them.

<!-- paragraph:1828 para_id:00000724 style:Normal -->
So instead, he found himself here—alone in one of the citadel’s training courtyards, methodically going through movements, trying to work the tension out of his muscles. His body fell into the familiar rhythm of combat exercises, but his mind remained distant, turning over the same dark thoughts.

<!-- paragraph:1829 para_id:00000725 style:Normal -->
He had spent too much time in the citadel already. The days were passing faster than he wanted, and every moment he lingered, something could go wrong. What if someone challenged Jalen to the Trial of Dominion? What if the Concord faltered without him?

<!-- paragraph:1830 para_id:00000726 style:Normal -->
He had spent years convincing himself that he wasn’t shackled to anything, that he could move freely, go where he wanted. But now, standing here, suffocated by the walls of the Rezin stronghold, he had never felt more trapped.

<!-- paragraph:1831 para_id:00000727 style:Normal -->
He was so caught up in his own thoughts that he barely noticed the approaching footsteps. But he did hear the voice.

<!-- paragraph:1832 para_id:00000728 style:Normal -->
“You done dancing yet, or are you actually going to do some real work this time?”

<!-- paragraph:1833 para_id:00000729 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled sharply and turned. Harvick stood a few paces away, arms crossed, an easy grin playing at the corners of his mouth.

<!-- paragraph:1834 para_id:0000072A style:Normal -->
Somehow, Davian wasn’t surprised.

<!-- paragraph:1835 para_id:0000072B style:Normal -->
He felt the old paranoia prickle at the edges of his mind—was Harvick testing him? Watching him? Looking for a weakness? But he pushed the thought away. It didn’t make sense. If the High Clades were truly suspicious of him, they wouldn’t need to play games. They had all the power in the world. If they wanted him gone, they could lock him up, drag him into a cell, rip his secrets out however they pleased.

<!-- paragraph:1836 para_id:0000072C style:Normal -->
He shoved that thought away, too.

<!-- paragraph:1837 para_id:0000072D style:Normal -->
Harvick tossed a wooden practice sword toward him. Davian caught it automatically, feeling the rough grain against his palm. “Don’t tell me you forgot already,” Harvick said. “You promised you’d actually try this time.”

<!-- paragraph:1838 para_id:0000072E style:Normal -->
“I don’t recall agreeing to—”

<!-- paragraph:1839 para_id:0000072F style:Normal -->
Harvick’s sword whipped toward his head without warning, the heavy wood whistling through the air. Davian reacted on pure reflex, snapping his blade up in a desperate parry. The impact jarred his arm, vibrating painfully up to his shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:1840 para_id:00000730 style:Normal -->
Harvick grinned broadly, eyes alight. “Ah, there we go. Looks like you’re starting to remember after all.”

<!-- paragraph:1841 para_id:00000731 style:Normal -->
The wooden blades clashed, sending a sharp vibration up Davian’s arms. He barely kept his footing as Harvick pressed forward, his swings forceful but controlled, testing.

<!-- paragraph:1842 para_id:00000732 style:Normal -->
Davian wasn’t at his best. He knew it. His body was moving, but his mind was stuck elsewhere—on the treasury sphere, on Jalen, on the sheer impossibility of what he needed to do. He had spent the entire day fighting against those zän, trying to sap the magic from them without making it obvious, and he had nothing to show for it. Every second he spent in this citadel was another second that something could go wrong outside.

<!-- paragraph:1843 para_id:00000733 style:Normal -->
Harvick noticed.

<!-- paragraph:1844 para_id:00000734 style:Normal -->
“You’re stiff,” he said, striking toward Davian’s ribs. Davian caught the blow, but only just. “Overthinking. Hesitating.”

<!-- paragraph:1845 para_id:00000735 style:Normal -->
Davian scowled and adjusted his grip. He tried to focus, but his mind kept getting in the way, spiraling through everything that could go wrong.

<!-- paragraph:1846 para_id:00000736 style:Normal -->
Another strike. Another. Harvick was barely moving his feet, letting his arms do the work, but he was still controlling the pace of the fight.

<!-- paragraph:1847 para_id:00000737 style:Normal -->
“You ever let go of that weight you’re carrying?” Harvick asked.

<!-- paragraph:1848 para_id:00000738 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “What?”

<!-- paragraph:1849 para_id:00000739 style:Normal -->
“All that thinking,” Harvick said. “You’re hauling it around like a sack of bricks. Worrying, planning, guessing what I’m about to do—none of it’s helping you.” He moved in and smacked Davian’s shoulder with the flat of his practice sword. “You’re not even here. No wonder you’re slow.”

<!-- paragraph:1850 para_id:0000073A style:Normal -->
Davian tightened his jaw. He wasn’t slow. He was just tired. And distracted. And—

<!-- paragraph:1851 para_id:0000073B style:Normal -->
Harvick read his thoughts on his face. “Try something for me,” he said. “Just once. Instead of thinking ahead, feel where you are. Right now.” He tapped his fingers against his own arm. “Your grip. Your stance. Where your balance is. Don’t fight your thoughts—just let them pass.”

<!-- paragraph:1852 para_id:0000073C style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled sharply. “That sounds stupid.”

<!-- paragraph:1853 para_id:0000073D style:Normal -->
“Then do it badly,” Harvick said, grinning. “I don’t care. Just try.”

<!-- paragraph:1854 para_id:0000073E style:Normal -->
Davian braced himself, rolling his shoulders, and made himself take in his stance. His weight was just slightly too far forward. He adjusted it. He felt the rough wood of the sword hilt against his palms. The cool air against the back of his neck. Harvick’s breathing, even and steady, across from him.

<!-- paragraph:1855 para_id:0000073F style:Normal -->
Harvick attacked. Davian reacted—but he was still a second too slow. The practice sword caught him in the ribs. He grunted, staggering back.

<!-- paragraph:1856 para_id:00000740 style:Normal -->
Harvick shook his head. “You’re still waiting to think about what to do next. Stop that.”

<!-- paragraph:1857 para_id:00000741 style:Normal -->
Davian glared at him, but Harvick just spread his hands. “Again.”

<!-- paragraph:1858 para_id:00000742 style:Normal -->
They reset. Harvick feinted, then came in low. Davian tried to let go of everything but what was in front of him, but his thoughts still dragged at him. He parried late, his block weak.

<!-- paragraph:1859 para_id:00000743 style:Normal -->
Another hit.

<!-- paragraph:1860 para_id:00000744 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled through his nose, frustration curling through his chest.

<!-- paragraph:1861 para_id:00000745 style:Normal -->
“You’re still trying to fix things before they happen,” Harvick said. “You can’t steer a ship by grabbing at the water. Stop thinking about where the fight is going and just be in it.”

<!-- paragraph:1862 para_id:00000746 style:Normal -->
Davian shook out his shoulders and let himself focus—really focus. Not by force, not by trying to direct his attention, but by letting go of the things that weren’t here. He felt the weight of his sword. The tension in his legs. The shift in Harvick’s stance.

<!-- paragraph:1863 para_id:00000747 style:Normal -->
This time, when Harvick moved, Davian didn’t think.

<!-- paragraph:1864 para_id:00000748 style:Normal -->
He acted.

<!-- paragraph:1865 para_id:00000749 style:Normal -->
Their blades met, but this time, Davian felt the rhythm of the fight instead of chasing it. His body moved before his mind had time to second-guess. The fight sharpened, his strikes faster, his blocks clean. His heartbeat synced with the pace of the fight, and he wasn’t worried about what was next—he was just there, in the moment.

<!-- paragraph:1866 para_id:0000074A style:Normal -->
The noise in his head dulled. The weight pressing down on him lightened.

<!-- paragraph:1867 para_id:0000074B style:Normal -->
Harvick’s strikes came harder, faster, but now Davian was keeping up—no, more than keeping up. His movements were sharper, more fluid. The tension melted from his limbs. He wasn’t fighting for survival, wasn’t overthinking each motion. He was just moving, just acting, just existing.

<!-- paragraph:1868 para_id:0000074C style:Normal -->
And it felt good.

<!-- paragraph:1869 para_id:0000074D style:Normal -->
His speed picked up, almost without him realizing. He pressed forward, his strikes growing faster and faster, his footwork more aggressive. For the first time in a long time, he felt free—unburdened, unshackled, weightless.

<!-- paragraph:1870 para_id:0000074E style:Normal -->
Then, in one clean motion, he knocked Harvick’s practice sword from his hand.

<!-- paragraph:1871 para_id:0000074F style:Normal -->
The sound of the wood clattering against the courtyard stones snapped Davian back to reality.

<!-- paragraph:1872 para_id:00000750 style:Normal -->
Davian froze, his chest heaving. He had gotten carried away. Again. He scrambled for an excuse, anything to explain why a supposed zänwright had just disarmed a seasoned fighter. “I—I got lucky,” he stammered.

<!-- paragraph:1873 para_id:00000751 style:Normal -->
Harvick bent down, retrieved his sword, and looked at him with something that wasn’t suspicion. Something closer to knowing. “It’s okay,” he said, his voice level. “You don’t have to hide.”

<!-- paragraph:1874 para_id:00000752 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed. “I don’t know what you mean.”

<!-- paragraph:1875 para_id:00000753 style:Normal -->
Harvick exhaled slowly, rolling his shoulders. “I can see it. You had a rough life. So did I.” He paused, weighing his words. “And you’re hiding something. Maybe you’re running from someone.”

<!-- paragraph:1876 para_id:00000754 style:Normal -->
Davian’s grip tightened on the practice sword.

<!-- paragraph:1877 para_id:00000755 style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t press. “But listen,” he continued, his tone steady. “You’ve got talent. And I hate to see it go to waste. Glyphwright work is impressive, but it’ll always be contract work for the High Clades. You do it, they use you, then they send you on your way.”

<!-- paragraph:1878 para_id:00000756 style:Normal -->
He met Davian’s gaze. “If you stay in the guards, though? That’s permanent. Whoever you’re running from—whoever you’re protecting someone from—they won’t be able to touch you if you stay here.” He let the words settle, then added, “Think about that.”

<!-- paragraph:1879 para_id:00000757 style:Normal -->
Harvick clapped him on the shoulder and walked off, leaving <!-- comment-start:90 -->Davian standing alone in the empt<!-- comment-end:90 --><!-- comment-ref:90 -->y courtyard, the weight of those words pressing down on him.

<!-- paragraph:1880 para_id:00000758 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:91 --><!-- comment-start:92 -->

<!-- paragraph:1881 para_id:00000759 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-end:91 --><!-- comment-ref:91 --><!-- comment-end:92 --><!-- comment-ref:92 -->

<!-- paragraph:1882 para_id:0000075A style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 13

<!-- paragraph:1883 para_id:0000075B style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 13

<!-- paragraph:1884 para_id:0000075C style:Heading2 -->
---

<!-- paragraph:1886 para_id:0000075E style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:93 -->The wind tugged at her cloak, warm and dry, carrying the scent of dust and sweat and wild grasses trampled under iron boots. Alara stood in the center of the line, just ahead of the shallow ridge where her archers waited, spread wide and low like teeth in the earth.<!-- comment-end:93 --><!-- comment-ref:93 -->

<!-- paragraph:1887 para_id:0000075F style:Normal -->
Her heartbeat was steady, her hands loose at her sides. She was always like this before battle—calm, alert, humming with magic just beneath the skin. The sun hung low and hot above the treeline, casting the distant canopy in molten orange.

<!-- paragraph:1888 para_id:00000760 style:Normal -->
They were coming.

<!-- paragraph:1889 para_id:00000761 style:Normal -->
Scouts had seen them at dawn: the Elarion army marching south, too quickly, too confidently. No flanking units. No illusion of strategy. She’d known what that meant the moment the messenger spoke the name.

<!-- paragraph:1890 para_id:00000762 style:Normal -->
Niall Rezin.

<!-- paragraph:1891 para_id:00000763 style:Normal -->
They’d put him in charge. Young still, unproven, but not untested. She’d heard of his work on the battlefield—elegant, brutal, economical. But more than that, she remembered the man himself. Blunt, rugged features. Quiet voice. Eyes that looked at you like they saw something. Like regret was a luxury he still believed in.

<!-- paragraph:1892 para_id:00000764 style:Normal -->
Alara exhaled and pushed the thought away. She had no patience for foolishness today.

<!-- paragraph:1893 para_id:00000765 style:Normal -->
Her plan was simple. She would stand here, visible, a threat in the open—her presence a challenge he couldn’t ignore. He would come for her. And the archers, hidden behind the ridge and spaced wide across the field, would rain arrows on him the moment he committed.

<!-- paragraph:1894 para_id:00000766 style:Normal -->
Arrows wouldn’t pierce a High Clade shroud. Not at first. But they didn’t have to. Not every arrow needed to land. They only needed to distract. To force attention. To drag his awareness in too many directions. If the shroud flickered, even for a second—

<!-- paragraph:1895 para_id:00000767 style:Normal -->
That would be enough.

<!-- paragraph:1896 para_id:00000768 style:Normal -->
It had worked before. It would work again.

<!-- paragraph:1897 para_id:00000769 style:Normal -->
She let her hand drift to the banded bracers on her forearms, the runes etched into them faintly glowing. Her magic stirred, ready. The grass whispered around her boots.

<!-- paragraph:1898 para_id:0000076A style:Normal -->
Then, the forest moved.

<!-- paragraph:1899 para_id:0000076B style:Normal -->
Not a whole army. Not the thunder of a hundred boots or the shimmer of warded steel.

<!-- paragraph:1900 para_id:0000076C style:Normal -->
Just one man.

<!-- paragraph:1901 para_id:0000076D style:Normal -->
He walked out of the treeline alone, arms bare, no helmet, no visible armor. The sunlight caught on the dark red sash wrapped around his waist, the gleam of twin rings at his belt.

<!-- paragraph:1902 para_id:0000076E style:Normal -->
And she knew.

<!-- paragraph:1903 para_id:0000076F style:Normal -->
That was him.

<!-- paragraph:1904 para_id:00000770 style:Normal -->
He kept walking—slow, measured, confident. The way you might cross a field to speak to a neighbor. No hesitation. No guards at his flanks. No soldiers cresting the rise behind him. Just the solitary crunch of his boots on brittle grass.

<!-- paragraph:1905 para_id:00000771 style:Normal -->
Alara narrowed her eyes, waiting.

<!-- paragraph:1906 para_id:00000772 style:Normal -->
Any second now. Reinforcements should follow. A distraction, maybe. Illusion magic. Some kind of trick.

<!-- paragraph:1907 para_id:00000773 style:Normal -->
She flicked two fingers at the scout crouched behind her.

<!-- paragraph:1908 para_id:00000774 style:Normal -->
The boy sprinted over, breathing fast, his cheeks flushed.

<!-- paragraph:1909 para_id:00000775 style:Normal -->
“Well?” she asked, keeping her voice low.

<!-- paragraph:1910 para_id:00000776 style:Normal -->
He shook his head. “Nothing, Commander. No movement on the flanks. No hidden signals from the outriders.”

<!-- paragraph:1911 para_id:00000777 style:Normal -->
Alara turned her gaze back to the figure crossing the field.

<!-- paragraph:1912 para_id:00000778 style:Normal -->
So he really meant to fight alone.

<!-- paragraph:1913 para_id:00000779 style:Normal -->
Typical High Clade arrogance. She shouldn’t be surprised. They thought themselves gods. Why bring an army when your blood made you a weapon?

<!-- paragraph:1914 para_id:0000077A style:Normal -->
Fine. If he wanted to die alone, she would oblige him.

<!-- paragraph:1915 para_id:0000077B style:Normal -->
And yet, some quiet part of her—not the soldier, not the commander—felt a flicker of regret.

<!-- paragraph:1916 para_id:0000077C style:Normal -->
She signaled with her hand—flat and sharp, straight through the air.

<!-- paragraph:1917 para_id:0000077D style:Normal -->
The archers responded instantly. Dozens of them drew their bows in practiced unison, their bodies tense and still as the strings stretched taut.

<!-- paragraph:1918 para_id:0000077E style:Normal -->
Alara closed her eyes briefly and reached downward. The warmth of the soil rushed up into her skin, coursing through her veins. Frost bloomed outward from her boots, crawling across the grass like delicate fingers. Her Shroud solidified around her, invisible but real, a silent curtain of killing force ready to snap into being.

<!-- paragraph:1919 para_id:0000077F style:Normal -->
Niall raised his arms.

<!-- paragraph:1920 para_id:00000780 style:Normal -->
Even from this distance, she saw it—faint but unmistakable. The crisscrossing network of scars, running like tangled zän along his forearms. Intertwined. Ritualistic.

<!-- paragraph:1921 para_id:00000781 style:Normal -->
He stood still. Waiting.

<!-- paragraph:1922 para_id:00000782 style:Normal -->
She brought her hand down.

<!-- paragraph:1923 para_id:00000783 style:Normal -->
The archers released.

<!-- paragraph:1924 para_id:00000784 style:Normal -->
A volley of black-feathered arrows streaked into the air, arcing like a swarm of starlings toward him.

<!-- paragraph:1925 para_id:00000785 style:Normal -->
And then—

<!-- paragraph:1926 para_id:00000786 style:Normal -->
The world became lightning.

<!-- paragraph:1927 para_id:00000787 style:Normal -->
No flash—detonation.

<!-- paragraph:1928 para_id:00000788 style:Normal -->
No storm—obliteration.

<!-- paragraph:1929 para_id:00000789 style:Normal -->
The sky cracked wide and roared down, blinding and white and infinite. Not a bolt. Not a strike. A cascade, pouring from the heavens like judgment.

<!-- paragraph:1930 para_id:0000078A style:Normal -->
The air turned to glass. The ground buckled. Alara’s ears rang with the shriek of a hundred arcs collapsing at once. Her Shroud faltered under the weight of it, and the heat in her limbs scattered like a child’s toy thrown to the dirt.

<!-- paragraph:1931 para_id:0000078B style:Normal -->
She didn’t know when she fell to her knees. Didn’t know when her fingers stopped channeling. Just that the light had become everything, and then—

<!-- paragraph:1932 para_id:0000078C style:Normal -->
Silence.

<!-- paragraph:1933 para_id:0000078D style:Normal -->
Her eyes blinked against the afterimage, vision swimming. Her mouth was dry. Her thoughts were static.

<!-- paragraph:1934 para_id:0000078E style:Normal -->
And she was still alive.

<!-- paragraph:1935 para_id:0000078F style:Normal -->
She swallowed, coughed once, and forced herself upright.

<!-- paragraph:1936 para_id:00000790 style:Normal -->
Bodies lay sprawled across the field. Not smoking, not mangled—just… down. Motionless. Dozens of them.

<!-- paragraph:1937 para_id:00000791 style:Normal -->
Her archers.

<!-- paragraph:1938 para_id:00000792 style:Normal -->
Her soldiers.

<!-- paragraph:1939 para_id:00000793 style:Normal -->
She blinked hard again, head spinning.

<!-- paragraph:1940 para_id:00000794 style:Normal -->
She saw him again. Much closer now, arms lowered, walking at that same calm pace. No gloating. No threat in his posture. Like he’d just gone for a walk.

<!-- paragraph:1941 para_id:00000795 style:Normal -->
Why am I still alive?

<!-- paragraph:1942 para_id:00000796 style:Normal -->
She stared at him. Tried to gather her magic again, but the currents inside her had gone sluggish, like a river choked with mud.

<!-- paragraph:1943 para_id:00000797 style:Normal -->
If this was the power the High Clades held, then her people had never stood a chance.

<!-- paragraph:1944 para_id:00000798 style:Normal -->
Not against this.

<!-- paragraph:1945 para_id:00000799 style:Normal -->
Not against him.

<!-- paragraph:1946 para_id:0000079A style:Normal -->
When he reached her, Niall extended a hand.

<!-- paragraph:1947 para_id:0000079B style:Normal -->
Alara didn’t take it.

<!-- paragraph:1948 para_id:0000079C style:Normal -->
Up close, his features were rugged, but arrestingly handsome—more so now than she remembered. The sigil on his face glowed like a brand of purpose, bright and unmarred, while the scars that spiraled up and down both bare arms looked almost ceremonial in the morning light.

<!-- paragraph:1949 para_id:0000079D style:Normal -->
And he was smiling.

<!-- paragraph:1950 para_id:0000079E style:Normal -->
Not the cold, arrogant smile she expected from a High Clade victor. It was radiant, startling in its sincerity—a smile like new spring after a brutal winter. Warm. Unreasonably warm.

<!-- paragraph:1951 para_id:0000079F style:Normal -->
She swallowed. “Why did you spare me?”

<!-- paragraph:1952 para_id:000007A0 style:Normal -->
He blinked, tilting his head like the question confused him. “Spare you?” he echoed. “I didn’t hurt anyone. Or, well—” he glanced around at the groaning forms of her soldiers, “—I tried not to. They should be all right. Mostly just stunned. It’s hard to thread lightning that carefully, but I did my best.”

<!-- paragraph:1953 para_id:000007A1 style:Normal -->
He rubbed the back of his neck with one scarred hand. “They might ache for a few days. But they did shoot quite a lot of arrows at me.”

<!-- paragraph:1954 para_id:000007A2 style:Normal -->
That ridiculous smile was still on his face.

<!-- paragraph:1955 para_id:000007A3 style:Normal -->
His hand was still outstretched.

<!-- paragraph:1956 para_id:000007A4 style:Normal -->
She stared at it for a long moment… then reached.

<!-- paragraph:1957 para_id:000007A5 style:Normal -->
His grip closed firmly around her forearm—strong, steady, grounding—and with one smooth pull, she was on her feet.

<!-- paragraph:1958 para_id:000007A6 style:Normal -->
Her heart hammered for reasons that had nothing to do with the battle.

<!-- paragraph:1959 para_id:000007A7 style:Normal -->
“Why?” she asked again, her voice quieter now. “Why do this?”

<!-- paragraph:1960 para_id:000007A8 style:Normal -->
He tilted his head again, as if the answer should have been obvious. “I’ve been trying to get a meeting with you for weeks. Sent messengers. Thought maybe if we just talked—” He paused, then gave her a rueful look. “To be fair, a couple of them came back with arrows in them. But I tried not to take that personally.”

<!-- paragraph:1961 para_id:000007A9 style:Normal -->
Alara folded her arms. “Further negotiation is pointless. We both know what this is. If we don’t fight, Elarion takes control.”

<!-- paragraph:1962 para_id:000007AA style:Normal -->
He nodded slowly. “Actually, I just wanted to tell you…” He reached into a pouch and pulled out a sealed letter, handing it to her. “I’ve ordered the Elarion army to pull out. Completely.”

<!-- paragraph:1963 para_id:000007AB style:Normal -->
She stared at him.

<!-- paragraph:1964 para_id:000007AC style:Normal -->
He gave a short nod, turned on his heel, and began to walk away.

<!-- paragraph:1965 para_id:000007AD style:Normal -->
“That doesn’t answer the question,” she called after him. “Why?”

<!-- paragraph:1966 para_id:000007AE style:Normal -->
He paused mid-step and looked back over his shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:1967 para_id:000007AF style:Normal -->
“I guess I found something more important than expanding our territory,” he said simply.

<!-- paragraph:1968 para_id:000007B0 style:Normal -->
Alara stood in stunned silence, the sealed letter heavy in her hand. Her soldiers were stirring, groaning, blinking against the fading dazzle of residual magic. One of them sat up, rubbing his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:1969 para_id:000007B1 style:Normal -->
“What… what did he want?” the soldier asked groggily.

<!-- paragraph:1970 para_id:000007B2 style:Normal -->
She opened the letter.

<!-- paragraph:1971 para_id:000007B3 style:Normal -->
The first section was formal: a declaration that Elarion troops were withdrawing from the region. Permanently. No retaliation, no follow-up, no strings attached. Her response to the next part, it said, would not alter that decision.

<!-- paragraph:1972 para_id:000007B4 style:Normal -->
Then her eyes fell to the second half.

<!-- paragraph:1973 para_id:000007B5 style:Normal -->
It was written in the same hand, but the tone had shifted—light, casual, like a conversation between acquaintances.

<!-- paragraph:1974 para_id:000007B6 style:Normal -->
It was an invitation.

<!-- paragraph:1975 para_id:000007B7 style:Normal -->
To a gala.

<!-- paragraph:1976 para_id:000007B8 style:Normal -->
In Elarion.

<!-- paragraph:1977 para_id:000007B9 style:Normal -->
He was asking her on a date.

<!-- paragraph:1978 para_id:000007BA style:Normal -->
Maya came out of the vision with a shuddering breath, nearly gasping as the memory dissolved.

<!-- paragraph:1979 para_id:000007BB style:Normal -->
The cold air of her chambers grounded her, but it felt wrong—smaller, duller, less than what she had just experienced. That clarity, that sense of purpose—it had filled every part of her mother. Alara had stood on that field certain of what needed to be done. Certain of who she was.

<!-- paragraph:1980 para_id:000007BC style:Normal -->
The loss of it left Maya hollow.

<!-- paragraph:1981 para_id:000007BD style:Normal -->
She blinked, sat for a moment longer, then stood abruptly. She needed to move. Do something. She grabbed her outer cloak and stepped into the hall. Her bodyguards straightened immediately. Erynn, who’d been loitering nearby, fell into step beside her.

<!-- paragraph:1982 para_id:000007BE style:Normal -->
“Training courtyard,” Maya said shortly.

<!-- paragraph:1983 para_id:000007BF style:Normal -->
She didn't explain further, and no one asked.

<!-- paragraph:1984 para_id:000007C0 style:Normal -->
As they walked, Maya’s mind turned over the vision, playing it back in pieces. That had been her father—young, radiant, almost unreal. She never saw him smile like that any more.

<!-- paragraph:1985 para_id:000007C1 style:Normal -->
She had memories, faint and distant, of that same smile. Smiles meant for Alara. Never for the world.

<!-- paragraph:1986 para_id:000007C2 style:Normal -->
And the magic—gods, the power of it. She had always known Niall was strong, but that display had been... staggering. Effortless, overwhelming, precise. Beautiful. Was that youth? Or had he simply never shown her what he was capable of?

<!-- paragraph:1987 para_id:000007C3 style:Normal -->
The thought left her uneasy.

<!-- paragraph:1988 para_id:000007C4 style:Normal -->
They descended into the courtyard. Maya was still lost in her thoughts when she noticed someone already there.

<!-- paragraph:1989 para_id:000007C5 style:Normal -->
Selwin.

<!-- paragraph:1990 para_id:000007C6 style:Normal -->
He stood with his little fists clenched at his sides, bodyguards watching from a polite distance. A single pebble lay on the stone in front of him, and he was staring at it with intense focus.

<!-- paragraph:1991 para_id:000007C7 style:Normal -->
Maya approached, her steps quiet. “Selwin? What are you doing?”

<!-- paragraph:1992 para_id:000007C8 style:Normal -->
He jumped slightly, then looked over, face set in something almost like grim determination. “Training.”

<!-- paragraph:1993 para_id:000007C9 style:Normal -->
“Training for what?” she asked, folding her arms.

<!-- paragraph:1994 para_id:000007CA style:Normal -->
“I figured…” Selwin glanced down. “Maybe the reason Father doesn’t want to play anymore is because he thinks I should focus on getting stronger. So I’m practicing. So I can protect people too.”

<!-- paragraph:1995 para_id:000007CB style:Normal -->
Maya’s chest tightened.

<!-- paragraph:1996 para_id:000007CC style:Normal -->
She started to tell him he didn’t need to do that, not yet, not like this. But the look on his face stopped her—the frustrated determination, the way his small hands curled at his sides like he was bracing himself against a world too large. She saw too much of herself in that posture. Too much of someone else, too.

<!-- paragraph:1997 para_id:000007CD style:Normal -->
“Focusing is hard,” she said instead, crouching beside him. “Everyone always says it’s the first step. That doesn’t mean it’s easy.”

<!-- paragraph:1998 para_id:000007CE style:Normal -->
Selwin frowned. “But if I can’t even do that—”

<!-- paragraph:1999 para_id:000007CF style:Normal -->
“You think I don’t struggle with it?” she asked, voice soft. “Half the time when I try to center myself, it feels like the entire world is clawing at my attention. Responsibilities. Expectations. People. My own thoughts.” She glanced sideways at him. “The trick isn’t pushing them all away. It’s letting them be there, but choosing what you pay attention to.”

<!-- paragraph:2000 para_id:000007D0 style:Normal -->
He didn’t answer right away. Just looked back at the pebble, brow furrowed.

<!-- paragraph:2001 para_id:000007D1 style:Normal -->
“You’re not doing this wrong,” Maya said. “This is what it looks like. The trying. The failing. The trying again.”

<!-- paragraph:2002 para_id:000007D2 style:Normal -->
He nodded slowly, the gesture small but real.

<!-- paragraph:2003 para_id:000007D3 style:Normal -->
She turned to go, but not all the way. She lingered long enough to see him close his eyes—not scrunched tight in frustration this time, but with deliberate calm. His chest rose and fell. And when he opened his eyes again, they settled back on the pebble with renewed focus.

<!-- paragraph:2004 para_id:000007D4 style:Normal -->
She let him be.

<!-- paragraph:2005 para_id:000007D5 style:Normal -->
Erynn gave her a sidelong glance as they crossed to the far end of the courtyard. “You okay?”

<!-- paragraph:2006 para_id:000007D6 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded slowly. “I was just… feeling something. A kind of clarity. Purpose.” She hesitated. “My mother—she always seemed to know what she was doing. What her actions meant. I think I felt that for a moment.”

<!-- paragraph:2007 para_id:000007D7 style:Normal -->
“Must be nice,” Erynn said lightly.

<!-- paragraph:2008 para_id:000007D8 style:Normal -->
Maya gave a humorless laugh. “It was. But now I’m back to managing five layers of political posturing, arguments over minor vault protocols, and deciding whether to let Caius come visit me because he’s suddenly decided courtship sounds fun.”

<!-- paragraph:2009 para_id:000007D9 style:Normal -->
Erynn smirked. “Sounds so hard.”

<!-- paragraph:2010 para_id:000007DA style:Normal -->
“That’s not it,” Maya said, sighing. “I just… I need something. A reason. Something that reminds me why all this matters. Why I matter.”

<!-- paragraph:2011 para_id:000007DB style:Normal -->
Erynn was quiet for a beat, then nudged her arm gently. “Then find it. Even if it’s just a pebble.”

<!-- paragraph:2012 para_id:000007DC style:Normal -->
Maya glanced back at Selwin.

<!-- paragraph:2013 para_id:000007DD style:Normal -->
He hadn’t moved. Still staring. Still hoping.

<!-- paragraph:2014 para_id:000007DE style:Normal -->
Maya’s eyes drifted around the training courtyard, her thoughts still caught somewhere between the past and the present.

<!-- paragraph:2015 para_id:000007DF style:Normal -->
In the far corner, a cluster of Rezin elite guards were sparring, the rhythmic thunk of practice swords and the low grunt of exertion rising above the background murmur. She had opened this space intentionally—for herself, yes, but also for them. The battlefield could never be recreated in the safety of a courtyard, but it was something. A place where she could try to hone her focus amid distractions. And a place where they could grow used to standing in the orbit of High Clade magic without flinching.

<!-- paragraph:2016 para_id:000007E0 style:Normal -->
It was one of the few things she felt certain about lately.

<!-- paragraph:2017 para_id:000007E1 style:Normal -->
She glanced toward Erynn, who was watching Selwin tilt his head at the pebble again, brow furrowed with almost comical concentration.

<!-- paragraph:2018 para_id:000007E2 style:Normal -->
“If Niall has a weakness,” Maya said suddenly, “what do you think it is?”

<!-- paragraph:2019 para_id:000007E3 style:Normal -->
Erynn blinked. “You mean besides being cranky and deeply allergic to sleep?”

<!-- paragraph:2020 para_id:000007E4 style:Normal -->
Maya cracked a smile, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “I mean in battle. Strategically.”

<!-- paragraph:2021 para_id:000007E5 style:Normal -->
Erynn looked at her for a moment, the joke falling away. “I… don’t know. He’s your father. You tell me.”

<!-- paragraph:2022 para_id:000007E6 style:Normal -->
Maya’s gaze returned to the sparring soldiers. “He can’t be everywhere. That’s what let the attackers get close to the wall. What let me into the fight.” Her voice was quiet now, but resolute. “They attacked in too many places at once. And he couldn’t be at all of them.”

<!-- paragraph:2023 para_id:000007E7 style:Normal -->
Erynn made a thoughtful noise. “Omnipresence is a difficult trait to acquire.”

<!-- paragraph:2024 para_id:000007E8 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped back, eyes scanning the courtyard, her voice low. “I think there’s a way to move faster. Not just faster than a soldier, or a messenger... faster than anyone.” She glanced at Erynn. “If I can learn to be where I’m needed—everywhere they strike—maybe next time no one breaks through.”

<!-- paragraph:2025 para_id:000007E9 style:Normal -->
Erynn raised an eyebrow. “You know that’s not actually possible, right?”

<!-- paragraph:2026 para_id:000007EA style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer right away. Her eyes drifted toward the stone beneath her feet. “Heat. Pressure. Momentum. It’s just physics.”

<!-- paragraph:2027 para_id:000007EB style:Normal -->
Erynn folded her arms. “So what—you’re going to be at every wall, stop every enemy, save every soldier yourself?”

<!-- paragraph:2028 para_id:000007EC style:Normal -->
“It should be possible,” Maya said. “That’s what I was born for. Or what I should be. Perfect. Otherwise what was the point of the Presentation? Of all this?”

“I get wanting to be the one who fixes it all,” Erynn went on. “But that’s not control. That’s fear in a nice dress.”

<!-- paragraph:2029 para_id:000007ED style:Normal -->
Maya’s fingers tightened at her sides. “You didn’t see it,” she said, barely above a whisper. “He stopped the whole war by himself. One man. A storm wrapped in skin.” Her voice caught. “And I’m his daughter.”

<!-- paragraph:2030 para_id:000007EE style:Normal -->
Erynn was quiet for a long beat. “That doesn’t mean you have to become him.”

<!-- paragraph:2031 para_id:000007EF style:Normal -->
“It means I should be able to,” Maya snapped, turning toward her. “It’s in my blood. My birthright. You don’t understand.”

<!-- paragraph:2032 para_id:000007F0 style:Normal -->
“Maybe I don’t,” Erynn said. “But everyone has limits.”

<!-- paragraph:2033 para_id:000007F1 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t reply right away. She turned on her heel and started walking toward the far side of the courtyard where the wards flickered faintly under the torchlight.

<!-- paragraph:2034 para_id:000007F2 style:Normal -->
“Not me,” she muttered. “I can’t.”

<!-- paragraph:2037 para_id:000007F5 style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 14

<!-- paragraph:2038 para_id:000007F6 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 14

<!-- paragraph:2039 para_id:000007F7 style:Heading2 -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2040 para_id:000007F8 style:Normal -->
The streets narrowed around him like a funnel, shadows drawn tight across uneven cobblestones. Davian kept his hood low and his gaze lower, boots scraping quietly as he weaved through the thinning crowd. The market noise faded behind him, replaced by the rustling hush of forgotten alleys and the wind’s low moan between rooftops.

<!-- paragraph:2041 para_id:000007F9 style:Normal -->
This had become his life. Routine wrapped in stone and silence. Days spent working zän deep in the citadel, chipping away at the vault’s defenses one redundant thread at a time. Nights in the training courtyards, the clash of wooden swords with Harvick’s barked commentary echoing off the walls. He moved, he fought, he planned. Always moving. Always acting.

<!-- paragraph:2042 para_id:000007FA style:Normal -->
But never feeling it again.

<!-- paragraph:2043 para_id:000007FB style:Normal -->
Not like before—not like that night in the courtyard, when something inside him had lifted, just for a moment. When the weight had fallen away and his body had moved like it belonged to him again. He kept chasing that moment in every swing, every breath, every shift of stance.

<!-- paragraph:2044 para_id:000007FC style:Normal -->
He never found it.

<!-- paragraph:2045 para_id:000007FD style:Normal -->
He cut through a narrow passage between two collapsed buildings, checking behind him with practiced ease. No one. Just the wind.

<!-- paragraph:2046 para_id:000007FE style:Normal -->
Still, the sense of being watched clawed at the edge of his awareness. He turned down another alley, then another, his path snaking deeper into the neglected veins of the lower city. Slum streets, unmarked, unloved. The buildings leaned like drunks whispering to one another. Doors sagged on broken hinges. Magic didn’t come here.

<!-- paragraph:2047 para_id:000007FF style:Normal -->
He wished he could strip his mind bare. Scrub it clean of nerves, guilt, doubt. Of thoughts. If he could just not feel anything—if he could just be a blade, sharp and hollow—he could do what needed to be done.

<!-- paragraph:2048 para_id:00000800 style:Normal -->
But the feelings clung to him like wet ash. Sticky. Heavy. Burning.

<!-- paragraph:2049 para_id:00000801 style:Normal -->
Eventually, he stopped.

<!-- paragraph:2050 para_id:00000802 style:Normal -->
An abandoned building loomed ahead, its stone façade cracked and weathered, windows like hollowed-out eyes. He ducked through the doorway and slipped inside, pausing just past the threshold. The front hall stretched in crumbling silence, lit dimly from the hole where the upper floors had collapsed. He stayed far enough from the door that only someone looking directly across from another window would see him.

<!-- paragraph:2051 para_id:00000803 style:Normal -->
Which was the point.

<!-- paragraph:2052 para_id:00000804 style:Normal -->
His gaze lifted, seeking movement in the building opposite.

<!-- paragraph:2053 para_id:00000805 style:Normal -->
There.

<!-- paragraph:2054 para_id:00000806 style:Normal -->
Second floor, narrow window, broken shutters. A shadow shifted—then steadied. One hand rose in greeting.

<!-- paragraph:2055 para_id:00000807 style:Normal -->
Davian raised his own, then signed: You alone?

<!-- paragraph:2056 para_id:00000808 style:Normal -->
Always alone, came the reply from across the street, Jalen’s fingers moving in slow, deliberate arcs. Someone follow you?

<!-- paragraph:2057 para_id:00000809 style:Normal -->
No one I saw. Davian hesitated. But maybe. Getting harder to know.

<!-- paragraph:2058 para_id:0000080A style:Normal -->
Jalen’s hands moved again, faster this time. They suspect?

<!-- paragraph:2059 para_id:0000080B style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head once. Then signed: Not yet. I’m careful.

<!-- paragraph:2060 para_id:0000080C style:Normal -->
How close?

<!-- paragraph:2061 para_id:0000080D style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled, jaw tight. Not close enough.

<!-- paragraph:2062 para_id:0000080E style:Normal -->
Jalen paused. His next signs were slower. What’s wrong?

<!-- paragraph:2063 para_id:0000080F style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. Then: Too many zän. Too much magic. Every time I drain one, others wake up. I don’t know if I can do it in time.

<!-- paragraph:2064 para_id:00000810 style:Normal -->
He looked away, toward the jagged sliver of sky above.

<!-- paragraph:2065 para_id:00000811 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s fingers moved again, slow and sharp in the shadows.

<!-- paragraph:2066 para_id:00000812 style:Normal -->
Any sign from the Registry? Something that says time’s running out?

<!-- paragraph:2067 para_id:00000813 style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head, grimacing. No. Nothing like that. From what I’ve heard… when the Ink Boon fails, I’ll just die. No warning. Just gone.

<!-- paragraph:2068 para_id:00000814 style:Normal -->
He watched Jalen shift slightly in the window—frustration, maybe. Or concern. Hard to read from this distance.

<!-- paragraph:2069 para_id:00000815 style:Normal -->
Then be careful, Jalen signed, each motion clipped. But fast.

<!-- paragraph:2070 para_id:00000816 style:Normal -->
Davian’s fingers stilled. He let out a short breath, not quite a laugh. I know. I know that.

<!-- paragraph:2071 para_id:00000817 style:Normal -->
He looked down at his hands. The joints ached from too many hours channeling Subversion into wards that refused to die.

<!-- paragraph:2072 para_id:00000818 style:Normal -->
He looked back up. Anyone challenged you yet? For Ascension?

<!-- paragraph:2073 para_id:00000819 style:Normal -->
There was a long pause.

<!-- paragraph:2074 para_id:0000081A style:Normal -->
Jalen signed, slower now. No. But… Kelyra’s heard the grumbling.

<!-- paragraph:2075 para_id:0000081B style:Normal -->
Grumbling?

<!-- paragraph:2076 para_id:0000081C style:Normal -->
Concord’s restless. They say I’m too focused on magi. Too distracted. That I care more about killing High and Low Clade than running the Concord.

<!-- paragraph:2077 para_id:0000081D style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. Do they remember why you’re here?

<!-- paragraph:2078 para_id:0000081E style:Normal -->
Some do, Jalen signed. Some pretend to. Doesn’t matter. I took control after the last leader faltered. Someone will try to do the same to me, eventually. I knew that going in.

<!-- paragraph:2079 para_id:0000081F style:Normal -->
And if they do?

<!-- paragraph:2080 para_id:00000820 style:Normal -->
Jalen didn’t hesitate. Then I kill them.

<!-- paragraph:2081 para_id:00000821 style:Normal -->
Davian’s throat tightened, but he nodded once. That was the truth of it. That was always the truth.

<!-- paragraph:2082 para_id:00000822 style:Normal -->
The job comes first, Jalen added, gaze narrowing. We finish this. We deal with the Syndicate. They put me in power. We owe them. We can’t leave them with a knife pointed a<!-- comment-start:94 --><!-- comment-start:95 -->t our backs.<!-- comment-end:94 --><!-- comment-ref:94 --><!-- comment-end:95 --><!-- comment-ref:95 -->

<!-- paragraph:2083 para_id:00000823 style:Normal -->
Davian signed nothing for a long moment. Then:

<!-- paragraph:2084 para_id:00000824 style:Normal -->
Yeah. I know.

<!-- paragraph:2085 para_id:00000825 style:Normal -->
Davian laid out everything he knew—the structure of the zä lattice, the pace of his progress, the frustrating resilience of the treasury wards. He kept it brief but precise, hands moving efficiently through the silent code they both knew. Jalen nodded at each detail, his face tight with focus. When they were done, they agreed on a fallback meeting point—another abandoned structure on the opposite edge of the warded district—just in case. Same signal. A letter, plain and folded, addressed to Arrek from a friend.

<!-- paragraph:2086 para_id:00000826 style:Normal -->
Then Davian slipped back into the alleys, shadows stretching long across the broken stones. The slums pressed in around him: leaning tenements, windows covered in cracked cloth, walls sagging like tired bones. He passed a family huddled around a pot that steamed faintly with what might have been water and roots. No one looked up.

<!-- paragraph:2087 para_id:00000827 style:Normal -->
This is their fault, he thought. The High Clades, with their spires and laws and smiles. Their shining towers built from everything they took. The lives they ground down to make their magic cleaner, easier, more precise. They didn’t fix anything. They just made sure it was someone else’s problem.

<!-- paragraph:2088 para_id:00000828 style:Normal -->
The city shifted as he moved—brick giving way to marble, stink fading under the scent of lavender oil and enchanted wardstones. When he reached the gate to the Rezin citadel, the guards on duty tensed only slightly before one of them squinted, then grinned.

<!-- paragraph:2089 para_id:00000829 style:Normal -->
“Arrek, right?” the guard said. “Didn’t expect you back so soon.”

<!-- paragraph:2090 para_id:0000082A style:Normal -->
Davian gave a lopsided smile. “Yeah, well. I was visiting your sister. She gets bored when you’re stuck on gate duty.”

<!-- paragraph:2091 para_id:0000082B style:Normal -->
The guard let out a bark of laughter. “Then I better see you at the practice yard soon. Gotta defend her honor.”

<!-- paragraph:2092 para_id:0000082C style:Normal -->
Davian forced himself to chuckle, stepping through as the warded gate buzzed faintly. Once he was out of sight of the gate, his jaw tightened.

<!-- paragraph:2093 para_id:0000082D style:Normal -->
That man. Smiling. Joking. Laughing. Helping keep the world exactly as it was. Holding the line so that no one could climb any higher. Just another cog in the machine, grinning while it crushed the rest of them.

<!-- paragraph:2094 para_id:0000082E style:Normal -->
Davian’s stomach churned. He shouldn’t have made that joke. Shouldn’t have let them get comfortable with him.

<!-- paragraph:2095 para_id:0000082F style:Normal -->
And yet, the thought of skipping the next practice with Harvick made his hands shake.

<!-- paragraph:2096 para_id:00000830 style:Normal -->
It was the only time the noise in his head quieted down. The only time he didn’t feel like he was drowning in it.

<!-- paragraph:2097 para_id:00000831 style:Normal -->
Davian forced himself to the mess hall, the heavy weight of his thoughts pressed deep into his spine. He knew he couldn’t skip every meal—suspicion thrived in patterns too cleanly broken. Tanelle’s voice reached him before he even sat down, her endless stream of stories already weaving their usual buffer around the table.

<!-- paragraph:2098 para_id:00000832 style:Normal -->
He let her carry the conversation, nodding when appropriate, offering the occasional grunt. Kolvar made a few dry comments, Jessa asked him a question he barely heard, and the food tasted like it always did—bland and warm and hard to swallow. He excused himself the moment it was polite to do so and didn’t wait to hear if anyone called after him.

<!-- paragraph:2099 para_id:00000833 style:Normal -->
The courtyard was dim with twilight, lanterns casting slow flickers against the stone. Harvick stood leaning against a pillar, arms crossed, the posture casual but coiled.

<!-- paragraph:2100 para_id:00000834 style:Normal -->
“Got a special treat for you tonight,” Harvick said, pushing off the wall. “Come on.”

<!-- paragraph:2101 para_id:00000835 style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse ticked up a notch. Something in the tone—too easy, too deliberate. He felt for the hilt of his knife under his coat and kept his posture relaxed.

<!-- paragraph:2102 para_id:00000836 style:Normal -->
“Where are we going?”

<!-- paragraph:2103 para_id:00000837 style:Normal -->
Harvick just smirked and motioned for him to follow.

<!-- paragraph:2104 para_id:00000838 style:Normal -->
The path wound through the deeper interior of the citadel, and Davian’s discomfort only grew as they passed two separate checkpoints. The elite guards at each let them through without hesitation, offering quick salutes to Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:2105 para_id:00000839 style:Normal -->
“He’s with me,” Harvick said, and that was enough.

<!-- paragraph:2106 para_id:0000083A style:Normal -->
Davian tried not to show how tightly he was watching every angle.

<!-- paragraph:2107 para_id:0000083B style:Normal -->
Then came the noise—dull thuds, far off and uneven. He paused, barely perceptible, and Harvick glanced back.

<!-- paragraph:2108 para_id:0000083C style:Normal -->
“Relax,” Harvick said. “That’s the point.”

<!-- paragraph:2109 para_id:0000083D style:Normal -->
The sounds got louder as they continued. Not regular enough to be practice drills. Not sharp enough to be alarms. Just… force. Compressed and distant.

<!-- paragraph:2110 para_id:0000083E style:Normal -->
“I know you’ve been pissed off,” Harvick said. “Can’t focus. Can’t get your head right.”

<!-- paragraph:2111 para_id:0000083F style:Normal -->
Davian’s voice was dry. “So you figured the answer was taking me somewhere really loud?”

<!-- paragraph:2112 para_id:00000840 style:Normal -->
Harvick barked a laugh. “What gets you stronger—lifting something light, or lifting something heavy?”

<!-- paragraph:2113 para_id:00000841 style:Normal -->
Davian muttered, “Well, if it’s too heavy to lift at all, then I guess it just crushes you.”

<!-- paragraph:2114 para_id:00000842 style:Normal -->
Harvick grinned like that was exactly the answer he’d hoped for. “Yeah. So you’d better not let it.”

<!-- paragraph:2115 para_id:00000843 style:Normal -->
They stepped through the archway into the training courtyard just as a blast of fire exploded against a dummy at the far end, sending splinters and smoke spiraling into the air. Frost cracked outward from her boots in delicate tendrils, white against the dark stone.

<!-- paragraph:2116 para_id:00000844 style:Normal -->
Davian froze.

<!-- paragraph:2117 para_id:00000845 style:Normal -->
She stood in the center of the chaos—completely still. Dark hair framed her face in stark contrast to her pale skin, the strands lifting slightly in the heat that still shimmered around her. Her expression was focused, unflinching, like she hadn’t even noticed the violence she’d just unleashed. She was poised in a way that didn’t feel taught, just… natural.

<!-- paragraph:2118 para_id:00000846 style:Normal -->
The sigil on her face burned—clean lines and perfect symmetry, glowing like molten silver. Not just bright, but radiant, sharp, purposeful. Like a second sun had opened behind her eyes.

<!-- paragraph:2119 para_id:00000847 style:Normal -->
And for a moment, everything else faded. The sound, the motion, even the tight coil of anxiety that had been pulling at his chest all day. All of it dulled to nothing.

<!-- paragraph:2120 para_id:00000848 style:Normal -->
He didn’t need to hear her name to know who she was..

<!-- paragraph:2121 para_id:00000849 style:Normal -->
He remembered her on the wall. The night sky behind her, beams of fire lancing down into the enemy below. Unflinching. Unstoppable.

<!-- paragraph:2122 para_id:0000084A style:Normal -->
A small shape crouched nearby pulled him back. Selwin was seated cross-legged, staring at a rock with furrowed concentration like it might whisper its secrets if he looked hard enough. The contrast was almost funny. Almost.

<!-- paragraph:2123 para_id:0000084B style:Normal -->
Maya turned toward them, and her eyes locked on Harvick first, then slid to Davian. Her brow arched.

<!-- paragraph:2124 para_id:0000084C style:Normal -->
“Why are you bringing zänwrights down here?” she asked, cool and direct. “This space is for elite guard training. With my permission.”

<!-- paragraph:2125 para_id:0000084D style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t miss a beat. “I don’t know, I think he’s pretty elite. He did beat me once.”

<!-- paragraph:2126 para_id:0000084E style:Normal -->
Maya’s gaze returned to Davian, unreadable.

<!-- paragraph:2127 para_id:0000084F style:Normal -->
“I suppose I can make an exception.”

<!-- paragraph:2128 para_id:00000850 style:Normal -->
Davian felt her eyes like a brand—sharp, invasive, impossible to ignore. She looked at him like she saw everything. Like she was peeling him open and weighing what she found. The flush hit him before he could stop it, heat rising up his neck.

<!-- paragraph:2129 para_id:00000851 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:96 -->He told himself it was rage. It had to be. <!-- comment-end:96 --><!-- comment-ref:96 -->The arrogance in her tone, the way she spoke like she owned everything around her—including him.

<!-- paragraph:2130 para_id:00000852 style:Normal -->
Harvick cleared his throat.

<!-- paragraph:2131 para_id:00000853 style:Normal -->
Davian blinked and forced out a stiff, “Thank you.”

<!-- paragraph:2132 para_id:00000854 style:Normal -->
He turned quickly, following Harvick deeper into the courtyard, but he could still feel her watching him. Like a tether between his shoulder blades. Like heat against his skin.

<!-- paragraph:2133 para_id:00000855 style:Normal -->
Harvick led Davian over to the sparring area, kicking aside a stray training baton as they walked. When he turned back around, his eyes twinkled with amusement.

<!-- paragraph:2134 para_id:00000856 style:Normal -->
“A little warm in here for you?” he asked, casually wiping at his brow. “You’re looking a bit flushed.” Then, with an exaggerated wink, he added, “Want me to ask her to cool it down a bit?”

<!-- paragraph:2135 para_id:00000857 style:Normal -->
Davian scowled, but before he could respond, Harvick tossed him a wooden sword.

<!-- paragraph:2136 para_id:00000858 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:97 -->A sudden boom cracked through the courtyard—another explosion of fire against the training dummies—and Davian flinched. The sword clattered to the ground beside him.<!-- comment-end:97 --><!-- comment-ref:97 -->

<!-- paragraph:2137 para_id:00000859 style:Normal -->
He turned instinctively. She hadn’t moved much, her gaze still locked on the dummies, posture rigid and precise. But... he thought he saw it. Just the barest curve at the corner of her lips. A flicker of amusement.

<!-- paragraph:2138 para_id:0000085A style:Normal -->
Or maybe he was imagining things.

<!-- paragraph:2139 para_id:0000085B style:Normal -->
His cheeks burned again, this time with embarrassment. He forced himself to turn back to Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:2140 para_id:0000085C style:Normal -->
“What’s the difference between a good swordsman and a bad one?” Harvick asked, adjusting his stance and twirling his own practice blade.

<!-- paragraph:2141 para_id:0000085D style:Normal -->
Davian rubbed at his jaw. “Strength, probably.”

<!-- paragraph:2142 para_id:0000085E style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted. “A relatively weak expert can take down a strong amateur without breaking a sweat.”

<!-- paragraph:2143 para_id:0000085F style:Normal -->
Before Davian could respond, another crack shook the ground, a shockwave of heat brushing the side of his face. His focus wavered—and Harvick lunged.

<!-- paragraph:2144 para_id:00000860 style:Normal -->
Davian scrambled to block, catching the blow just in time. The impact jarred his arms.

<!-- paragraph:2145 para_id:00000861 style:Normal -->
“The real difference,” Harvick said, circling, “is in here.” He tapped his temple with two fingers.

<!-- paragraph:2146 para_id:00000862 style:Normal -->
Davian tried to push everything else away—the heat, the sound, the flickering awareness of her presence across the courtyard. He let his grip shift into something more fluid. Tried to feel where his balance was. To let the rhythm carry him.

<!-- paragraph:2147 para_id:00000863 style:Normal -->
Then—another explosion, closer this time. His concentration shattered.

<!-- paragraph:2148 para_id:00000864 style:Normal -->
And Harvick swept his legs out from under him.

<!-- paragraph:2149 para_id:00000865 style:Normal -->
Davian hit the stone hard, the impact rattling his spine. He let out a breath between clenched teeth.

<!-- paragraph:2150 para_id:00000866 style:Normal -->
“That’s not fair,” he muttered. “You’ve fought like this before.”

<!-- paragraph:2151 para_id:00000867 style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged, offering no sympathy. “Maybe. But if this was a real fight, you’d be dead. Stop making excuses.”

<!-- paragraph:2152 para_id:00000868 style:Normal -->
Davian pushed himself off the ground, dusting grit from his palms. “It’s not an excuse,” he said, glaring at Harvick. “This—”

<!-- paragraph:2153 para_id:00000869 style:Normal -->
BOOM. Another explosion rocked the air behind him, punctuating the sentence with a wave of pressure and heat. Davian scowled. “—is really unreasonable. And unpleasant.”

<!-- paragraph:2154 para_id:0000086A style:Normal -->
Harvick gave an insufferable grin. “You ever do strength training and think, ‘wow, this is nice and relaxing’?”

<!-- paragraph:2155 para_id:0000086B style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t answer.

<!-- paragraph:2156 para_id:0000086C style:Normal -->
“Exactly,” Harvick went on. “It hurts. It’s uncomfortable. That’s how your body grows. Why would your mind be any different?” He gave Davian a pointed look, gesturing vaguely at his arms and shoulders. “And you’ve clearly put in the work there. You didn’t get that build by doing things that felt good.”

<!-- paragraph:2157 para_id:0000086D style:Normal -->
Davian shifted uneasily. The memory of long, grueling months of pain and soreness surfaced—every ache, every failure before progress started to show. And now he was supposed to do it again, all of it, but in his head?

<!-- paragraph:2158 para_id:0000086E style:Normal -->
His stomach knotted. But he nodded slowly, jaw set. “Fine,” he muttered, stepping back into stance. “Let’s get on with it.”

<!-- paragraph:2159 para_id:0000086F style:Normal -->
He could handle unpleasant. He just had to keep going.

<!-- paragraph:2161 para_id:00000871 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 15

<!-- paragraph:2162 para_id:00000872 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 15

<!-- paragraph:2163 para_id:00000873 style:Heading2 -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2164 para_id:00000874 style:Normal -->
He’d stared at her like he meant to set her on fire. Or, like she was fire and being near her burned him.

<!-- paragraph:2165 para_id:00000875 style:Normal -->
Certainly not with pure admiration. Something sharper. More intense. It wasn’t fear. Or awe. She knew those looks. She saw them every day, even from the elite guards. Everyone, except maybe Harvick, still hesitated around her. Still flinched at the weight of her name, her power.

<!-- paragraph:2166 para_id:00000876 style:Normal -->
But him? He’d looked at her like she was just a person. Like he didn’t care that her veins lit with Convergence or that her sigil marked her as something rare and dangerous. Like he was trying to figure her out and resented himself for even wanting to. It had left her strangely off-balance.

<!-- paragraph:2167 para_id:00000877 style:Normal -->
Now, sweat clung to his bare shoulders as he and Harvick circled each other across the courtyard, practice blades raised. He’d taken his shirt off a few minutes into the fight, probably trying to cool down, though it hadn’t helped. Not with the heat rolling off her magic in waves, the stone still shimmering faintly from her last spell.

<!-- paragraph:2168 para_id:00000878 style:Normal -->
She told herself she was watching his technique.

<!-- paragraph:2169 para_id:00000879 style:Normal -->
But there was something else, too. The way his muscles moved. The sheen of sweat across ink-dark tattoos that curved along his arms and shoulders like half-formed zän. She found herself staring, her mind briefly catching on the details—on the strange, almost arcane patterning of it.

<!-- paragraph:2170 para_id:0000087A style:Normal -->
She blinked and looked away quickly.

<!-- paragraph:2171 para_id:0000087B style:Normal -->
Right. Focus.

<!-- paragraph:2172 para_id:0000087C style:Normal -->
Another explosion tore through the far end of the courtyard, more forceful than necessary. She didn’t even watch it hit—just listened for the impact behind her, eyes on the stone.

<!-- paragraph:2173 para_id:0000087D style:Normal -->
A second later, she heard Harvick’s laugh, and then the heavy thud of a hit connecting.

<!-- paragraph:2174 para_id:0000087E style:Normal -->
She allowed herself a small, private smile.

<!-- paragraph:2175 para_id:0000087F style:Normal -->
After a while, footsteps scuffed softly across the stone behind her.

<!-- paragraph:2176 para_id:00000880 style:Normal -->
“You look a little distracted,” Erynn murmured.

<!-- paragraph:2177 para_id:00000881 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t glance over. “That’s the point.”

<!-- paragraph:2178 para_id:00000882 style:Normal -->
Erynn made a thoughtful noise. “Didn’t realize you had to train to avoid such… specific distractions.”

<!-- paragraph:2179 para_id:00000883 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “What?”

<!-- paragraph:2180 para_id:00000884 style:Normal -->
“Mmhm.” Erynn tilted her head toward the sparring ring, where Davian had just taken a solid hit to the ribs and staggered back, teeth gritted.

<!-- paragraph:2181 para_id:00000885 style:Normal -->
But Erynn’s expression shifted a moment later. “Wait—something’s going on with Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:2182 para_id:00000886 style:Normal -->
Maya’s attention snapped back instantly. She turned toward the far end of the courtyard, where Selwin still sat cross-legged on the stone, seemingly locked in the same position as before. At first glance, nothing had changed. But—

<!-- paragraph:2183 para_id:00000887 style:Normal -->
Erynn pointed. “The pebble.”

<!-- paragraph:2184 para_id:00000888 style:Normal -->
Maya squinted. The stone in front of Selwin… looked smaller. Not cracked. Not scorched. Just—less.

<!-- paragraph:2185 para_id:00000889 style:Normal -->
A chill crept down her spine.

<!-- paragraph:2186 para_id:0000088A style:Normal -->
She moved fast, crossing the courtyard in long, purposeful strides. The heat from her magic hadn’t even faded from the stone.

<!-- paragraph:2187 para_id:0000088B style:Normal -->
“Selwin,” she called as she approached. “What are you doing?”

<!-- paragraph:2188 para_id:0000088C style:Normal -->
He didn’t answer at first. Just reached down and picked up the pebble—what remained of it—and turned to face her.

<!-- paragraph:2189 para_id:0000088D style:Normal -->
It was dissolving.

<!-- paragraph:2190 para_id:0000088E style:Normal -->
Not crumbling, not breaking. Disintegrating, almost imperceptibly, in his palm.

<!-- paragraph:2191 para_id:0000088F style:Normal -->
But that wasn’t what stopped her heart.

<!-- paragraph:2192 para_id:00000890 style:Normal -->
A symbol glowed faintly on Selwin’s face, just above the cheekbone—eerie and unfamiliar. A sigil. Active.

<!-- paragraph:2193 para_id:00000891 style:Normal -->
And not one she recognized.

<!-- paragraph:2194 para_id:00000892 style:Normal -->
Her voice dropped low. Urgent. “Selwin. You were supposed to be working on focus. Not channeling. You need to stop. Right now. This is dangerous.”

<!-- paragraph:2195 para_id:00000893 style:Normal -->
He looked up at her with wide, earnest eyes, his small fingers curling around the melting stone like it was the last thing holding him together.

<!-- paragraph:2196 para_id:00000894 style:Normal -->
“I need to do this,” he said. “To keep you safe. To keep all of you safe.”

<!-- paragraph:2197 para_id:00000895 style:Normal -->
And then his sigil flared—white-hot and blinding—flooding the courtyard in raw, uncontrolled light.

<!-- paragraph:2198 para_id:00000896 style:Normal -->
Maya flinched at the brilliance and instinctively threw an arm across her face. “Back!” she shouted, turning on her heel and shoving Erynn behind her with enough force to stagger her. “Get Father—now!”

<!-- paragraph:2199 para_id:00000897 style:Normal -->
She pivoted back toward Selwin, squinting against the glare. “Selwin, listen to me! You need to calm your magic. Pull it back in—focus!”

<!-- paragraph:2200 para_id:00000898 style:Normal -->
But Selwin’s voice cracked across the courtyard, sharp with frustration. “Everyone always says that! Focus, calm down—like it’s easy!”

<!-- paragraph:2201 para_id:00000899 style:Normal -->
The stone beneath him shuddered.

<!-- paragraph:2202 para_id:0000089A style:Normal -->
Then it began to decay.

<!-- paragraph:2203 para_id:0000089B style:Normal -->
The pattern spread outward in jagged rings, the ground pitting and wearing away as if time itself had clenched its jaws around it. Years, centuries of erosion crushed into seconds.

<!-- paragraph:2204 para_id:0000089C style:Normal -->
Maya took a step back, her pulse hammering.

<!-- paragraph:2205 para_id:0000089D style:Normal -->
“You’re not ready for this,” she said, trying to keep her voice steady. “You have to stop—”

<!-- paragraph:2206 para_id:0000089E style:Normal -->
“You only care about magic too!” Selwin shouted, face twisted in something between anguish and rage. “You and Father both!”

<!-- paragraph:2207 para_id:0000089F style:Normal -->
The circle widened.

<!-- paragraph:2208 para_id:000008A0 style:Normal -->
The decay reached the base of the courtyard wall—and Maya’s breath caught. The stone groaned, hairline fractures racing through the foundation. It was about to collapse inward.

<!-- paragraph:2209 para_id:000008A1 style:Normal -->
She threw her arms up.

<!-- paragraph:2210 para_id:000008A2 style:Normal -->
Cold surged from her palms, air snapping into frost around the fractured wall. She poured everything into it—freezing, slowing, binding the crumbling structure in a shell of brittle ice.

<!-- paragraph:2211 para_id:000008A3 style:Normal -->
But the weight was immense.

<!-- paragraph:2212 para_id:000008A4 style:Normal -->
A thunderclap of backlash erupted behind her as the displaced heat roared into the air. It hit her like a furnace, her back arching from the impact, skin seared by the pressure. Flame burst from the ground in a shockwave, ripping heat from her bloodstream. Her ears rang. Her legs buckled. She clenched her jaw and held.

<!-- paragraph:2213 para_id:000008A5 style:Normal -->
Her breath came in ragged pulls.

<!-- paragraph:2214 para_id:000008A6 style:Normal -->
Sweat slicked her spine. Her fingers trembled from the strain of channeling too much, too fast. Her lips tasted like blood—bitten without realizing.

<!-- paragraph:2215 para_id:000008A7 style:Normal -->
“Selwin!” she screamed. “Stop! You’ll kill yourself!”

<!-- paragraph:2216 para_id:000008A8 style:Normal -->
But he didn’t respond.

<!-- paragraph:2217 para_id:000008A9 style:Normal -->
He was on his knees now, arms limp at his sides, his eyes unfocused and glassy as his head lolled back. His sigil flared one last time—so bright it turned his whole face to white.

<!-- paragraph:2218 para_id:000008AA style:Normal -->
And then he collapsed.

<!-- paragraph:2219 para_id:000008AB style:Normal -->
The last wave of magic rolled out in a ripple—and the wall groaned again, louder this time, stone shearing against stone. Maya barely registered the flames tearing out behind her, heat flaring against her arms and neck. Her boots skidded on the slicked courtyard as she held the freeze steady, channeling through the pain, through the noise, through the panic.

<!-- paragraph:2220 para_id:000008AC style:Normal -->
Please, she thought. Please let the guards be smart enough to get clear.

<!-- paragraph:2221 para_id:000008AD style:Normal -->
The wall shuddered against her magic, the strain like trying to hold back an avalanche with her bare hands.

<!-- paragraph:2222 para_id:000008AE style:Normal -->
Suddenly—Arrek was there.

<!-- paragraph:2223 para_id:000008AF style:Normal -->
Maya blinked sweat from her eyes, the world reeling from exertion and heat—and saw him crouched beside Selwin, arms already around the boy’s limp frame.

<!-- paragraph:2224 para_id:000008B0 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:98 -->She didn’t have time to think about how he’d gotten there.

<!-- paragraph:2225 para_id:000008B1 style:Normal -->
“Ice!” he shouted. “Now!”

<!-- paragraph:2226 para_id:000008B2 style:Normal -->
Then he was moving, hauling Selwin into his arms and sprinting toward her, boots slamming against the frost-slick stone.

<!-- paragraph:2227 para_id:000008B3 style:Normal -->
She dropped the freeze on the wall. No time.

<!-- paragraph:2228 para_id:000008B4 style:Normal -->
Arrek leapt past her.<!-- comment-end:98 --><!-- comment-ref:98 -->

<!-- paragraph:2229 para_id:000008B5 style:Normal -->
Her hands snapped forward, and a jagged wall of ice erupted in front of her, thick and slanted just enough to catch the collapse—but she couldn’t vent the heat behind her toward Davian and Selwin. No way. Not without burning them both.

<!-- paragraph:2230 para_id:000008B6 style:Normal -->
So she threw her arms up overhead and screamed through clenched teeth, channeling the surge upward. A beam of pure flame lanced into the sky, searing white, vaporizing shards of falling stone and carving a hole in the clouds. The air sucked back hard with a thunderous crack, heat whipping her hair across her face.

<!-- paragraph:2231 para_id:000008B7 style:Normal -->
She staggered, chest heaving, body nearly buckling as the last of the fire tore through her.

<!-- paragraph:2232 para_id:000008B8 style:Normal -->
The courtyard wall slammed down against the ice shield. Cracks split across it like spiderwebs, a deafening groan filling the courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:2233 para_id:000008B9 style:Normal -->
But it held. Barely.

<!-- paragraph:2234 para_id:000008BA style:Normal -->
Maya turned, still gasping—and saw him.

<!-- paragraph:2235 para_id:000008BB style:Normal -->
Arrek stood hunched, panting hard, sweat streaking down his bare chest, arms tight around Selwin’s limp form. His face was pale, jaw clenched, muscles taut as he shielded the boy with his entire body.

<!-- paragraph:2236 para_id:000008BC style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:99 -->She took a step forward.

<!-- paragraph:2237 para_id:000008BD style:Normal -->
They were close. Closer than she realized. His breath was ragged. So was hers. Their eyes locked.

<!-- paragraph:2238 para_id:000008BE style:Normal -->
He was looking at her again—that same look from earlier. Like she was a question he didn’t want to ask. Like he was furious to be standing there at all.<!-- comment-end:99 --><!-- comment-ref:99 -->

<!-- paragraph:2239 para_id:000008BF style:Normal -->
But there was something else beneath it. A flicker of unguarded heat. Hunger. Shock. Something wild and unspoken.

<!-- paragraph:2240 para_id:000008C0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s heart thudded once, too hard.

<!-- paragraph:2241 para_id:000008C1 style:Normal -->
She reached out, almost without thinking, hand brushing Selwin’s arm—but catching his, too. Skin to skin.

<!-- paragraph:2242 para_id:000008C2 style:Normal -->
“Thank you,” she said, voice low.
