<!-- paragraph:2243 para_id:000008C3 style:Normal -->
And just like that, the moment snapped.

<!-- paragraph:2244 para_id:000008C4 style:Normal -->
His expression contorted—pain, shame, and anger flickering all at once. He recoiled from her touch like it stung, like her gratitude had sunk deep into something he didn’t want anyone to see.

<!-- paragraph:2245 para_id:000008C5 style:Normal -->
Disgust shadowed his face—<!-- comment-start:100 -->not at her, but at himself<!-- comment-end:100 --><!-- comment-ref:100 -->.

<!-- paragraph:2246 para_id:000008C6 style:Normal -->
Without a word, he transferred Selwin to her arms. Not roughly—but not gently, either. Deliberate. Final.

<!-- paragraph:2247 para_id:000008C7 style:Normal -->
“Here,” he muttered.

<!-- paragraph:2248 para_id:000008C8 style:Normal -->
She looked up, but he was already turning. Already pulling on his shirt.

<!-- paragraph:2249 para_id:000008C9 style:Normal -->
“How—” she started, breath still ragged. “How did you get there in time?”

<!-- paragraph:2250 para_id:000008CA style:Normal -->
Arrek paused mid-step, looking up like he was surprised to be where he was. For a split second, something unguarded crossed his face—then vanished, replaced by that same piercing intensity from before.

<!-- paragraph:2251 para_id:000008CB style:Normal -->
“If you run a lot,” he said flatly, “eventually you get fast.”

<!-- paragraph:2252 para_id:000008CC style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth to ask more—but she stopped. Selwin was limp in his arms, his face pale and slack.

<!-- paragraph:2253 para_id:000008CD style:Normal -->
“Healer!” she shouted, forcing strength back into her voice. “Now!”

<!-- paragraph:2254 para_id:000008CE style:Normal -->
Footsteps pounded behind her. A woman in crimson robes rushed forward, her bonded familiar already glowing at her heels.

<!-- paragraph:2255 para_id:000008CF style:Normal -->
Maya crouched quickly, cradling Selwin as gently as she could while the healer knelt across from her. The familiar let out a quiet, keening sound and pressed its head against Selwin’s chest, the glow between them flickering into sync. Maya tried to stay focused—answering the healer’s questions, watching Selwin’s breathing, noting the faint shimmer of his still-active sigil.

<!-- paragraph:2256 para_id:000008D0 style:Normal -->
Maya caught a movement out of the corner of her eye.

<!-- paragraph:2257 para_id:000008D1 style:Normal -->
Arrek was almost out of the courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:2258 para_id:000008D2 style:Normal -->
She blinked, stunned for a moment that he hadn’t said anything else. Just—gone. No look back. No acknowledgment.

<!-- paragraph:2259 para_id:000008D3 style:Normal -->
“Wait,” she called, her voice catching slightly.

<!-- paragraph:2260 para_id:000008D4 style:Normal -->
He slowed.

<!-- paragraph:2261 para_id:000008D5 style:Normal -->
“My father will want to thank you,” she said, pushing herself to her feet, legs trembling from magic and adrenaline. “You saved Selwin’s life.”

<!-- paragraph:2262 para_id:000008D6 style:Normal -->
Arrek paused mid-step. His shoulders rose and fell once, sharply. Then he half-turned, just enough for her to see the side of his face—tense, unreadable.

<!-- paragraph:2263 para_id:000008D7 style:Normal -->
“No need,” he said without turning. His voice was unreadable. “I didn’t do it for him.”

<!-- paragraph:2264 para_id:000008D8 style:Normal -->
He started walking again, grabbing his shirt from the scorched and frozen ground without breaking stride, boots crunching through the frost-slick courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:2265 para_id:000008D9 style:Normal -->
She watched him go, her breath still uneven. Her eyes flicked to his back.

<!-- paragraph:2266 para_id:000008DA style:Normal -->
Just for a second—barely a heartbeat—the tattoos across his arms shimmered. Not just with sweat or movement. Something deeper. Like they weren’t ink at all, but threads submerged just below the surface. Moving. Watching.

<!-- paragraph:2267 para_id:000008DB style:Normal -->
She swallowed. Her hand twitched at her side.

<!-- paragraph:2268 para_id:000008DC style:Normal -->
“But that’s not possible,” she murmured.

<!-- paragraph:2269 para_id:000008DD style:Normal -->
A trick of the light, she told herself.

<!-- paragraph:2270 para_id:000008DE style:Normal -->
And yet… she kept staring after him, long after he’d vanished from view.

<!-- paragraph:2271 para_id:000008DF style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2273 para_id:000008E1 style:Normal -->
The halls to the infirmary echoed with quiet footsteps and anxious breath. Selwin<!-- comment-start:101 --> lay pale and<!-- comment-end:101 --><!-- comment-ref:101 --> still on the stretcher, his small frame looking impossibly fragile against the reinforced oak and leather. The healer walked briskly beside him, her bonded familiar lying next to Selwin, resting its head against his chest, pulse-syncing in gentle rhythm.

<!-- paragraph:2274 para_id:000008E2 style:Normal -->
Maya stayed close, her boots scuffing lightly on the stone. The acrid scent of scorched stone and ozone still clung to her clothes. She didn’t speak. Didn’t know what she’d say if she tried.

<!-- paragraph:2275 para_id:000008E3 style:Normal -->
Erynn trailed behind, eyes fixed on Selwin. Maya glanced back once, and Erynn just shook her head faintly—what just happened?

<!-- paragraph:2276 para_id:000008E4 style:Normal -->
The door to the infirmary swung open as they approached, and inside, the space bloomed with cool light. Wards on the walls shimmered with layered healing zän. Selwin was transferred smoothly to a padded cot, the healer murmuring something to her familiar that Maya didn’t catch.

<!-- paragraph:2277 para_id:000008E5 style:Normal -->
She took a step forward—then stopped. Her legs trembled with residual effort, and something deep in her back ached with every breath. She didn’t realize she was still holding the raw edge of her own magic until the zän on the wall flared in response.

<!-- paragraph:2278 para_id:000008E6 style:Normal -->
“Easy,” the healer said gently, without turning. “You’ve spent too much.”

<!-- paragraph:2279 para_id:000008E7 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond. Her eyes were on Selwin.

<!-- paragraph:2280 para_id:000008E8 style:Normal -->
Then the room shifted. She didn’t hear him come in, but she felt it. That subtle drawing inward, like the walls leaning to listen.

<!-- paragraph:2281 para_id:000008E9 style:Normal -->
Niall stepped through the side doorway, eyes scanning the room in a single sweep before settling on his daughter.

<!-- paragraph:2282 para_id:000008EA style:Normal -->
“What happened?” His voice was quiet, but it cut through the space like steel.

<!-- paragraph:2283 para_id:000008EB style:Normal -->
Maya turned, squaring her shoulders. “He channeled… something. I’ve never seen the sigil he manifested.”

<!-- paragraph:2284 para_id:000008EC style:Normal -->
Niall moved to Selwin’s bedside without a word, crouching beside the cot. He reached out slowly, his hand hovering just above Selwin’s arm. His face remained expressionless, but Maya saw it—the slight tension in his jaw, the way his gaze lingered too long on the still-glowing lines across Selwin’s cheek.

<!-- paragraph:2285 para_id:000008ED style:Normal -->
“It’s not mine,” he murmured. “Or hers.”

<!-- paragraph:2286 para_id:000008EE style:Normal -->
Maya stepped closer. “He wasn’t ready. I told him to stop. He wouldn’t. He said… he said you only pay attention to magic and war.”

<!-- paragraph:2287 para_id:000008EF style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t move.

<!-- paragraph:2288 para_id:000008F0 style:Normal -->
“I pushed you too hard after Alara died,” he said finally. “Tried not to make the same mistake with him.”

<!-- paragraph:2289 para_id:000008F1 style:Normal -->
Maya’s voice was sharper than she meant it to be. “You didn’t push him. He did this because he wanted you to see him. To be with him.”

<!-- paragraph:2290 para_id:000008F2 style:Normal -->
Niall was silent for a moment, watching the rise and fall of Selwin’s chest.

<!-- paragraph:2291 para_id:000008F3 style:Normal -->
“Then I’ve already failed him.”

<!-- paragraph:2292 para_id:000008F4 style:Normal -->
The words landed heavier than she expected. He didn’t say them with drama or grief—just certainty. Like something he’d known for a long time and finally allowed to form.

<!-- paragraph:2293 para_id:000008F5 style:Normal -->
The healer’s familiar shifted slightly, and Maya stepped aside to let her check the sigil’s residual glow. Niall stood slowly, his gaze drifting toward Maya.

<!-- paragraph:2294 para_id:000008F6 style:Normal -->
Niall glanced toward the doorway, voice low. “One of the guards said someone pulled Selwin out. Which of them was it? He should be rewarded.”

<!-- paragraph:2295 para_id:000008F7 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “It wasn’t a guard.”

<!-- paragraph:2296 para_id:000008F8 style:Normal -->
He turned to her, brow creasing faintly. “Then who?”

<!-- paragraph:2297 para_id:000008F9 style:Normal -->
“A zänwright,” she said. “From the treasury team. Arrek.”

<!-- paragraph:2298 para_id:000008FA style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes narrowed slightly. “Arrek? What was he doing in the courtyard?”

<!-- paragraph:2299 para_id:000008FB style:Normal -->
“He’s been sparring with Harvick,” Maya replied. “Apparently he’s decent enough that Harvick brought him down for more advanced training.”

<!-- paragraph:2300 para_id:000008FC style:Normal -->
That made him pause.

<!-- paragraph:2301 para_id:000008FD style:Normal -->
“He didn’t wait around?” Niall asked, gaze sharpening.

<!-- paragraph:2302 para_id:000008FE style:Normal -->
“No.” Maya crossed her arms. “He handed Selwin off to the healer and left. He said he didn’t need to be thanked..”

<!-- paragraph:2303 para_id:000008FF style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression darkened, just slightly. “Then what does he want?” He looked away. “Everyone wants something.”

<!-- paragraph:2304 para_id:00000900 style:Normal -->
He let the thought hang in the air a moment, then nodded to one of the guards. “Bring Harvick. I want to know who we’re dealing with.”

<!-- paragraph:2305 para_id:00000901 style:Normal -->
While they waited, Niall stood by Selwin’s bedside, watching the shallow rise and fall of his chest.

<!-- paragraph:2306 para_id:00000902 style:Normal -->
“I should’ve seen it coming,” he said quietly. “I thought… with you stepping up, with everything on your shoulders, it meant Selwin wouldn’t need as much. That I could…” He shook his head, jaw tightening. “But that’s not how it works, is it.”

<!-- paragraph:2307 para_id:00000903 style:Normal -->
Maya was silent for a moment. Then, softly: “He thought you didn’t want to be with him anymore. Like he did something wrong.”

<!-- paragraph:2308 para_id:00000904 style:Normal -->
Niall closed his eyes briefly. “No. No, that’s not it. But I see how he’d think that.”

<!-- paragraph:2309 para_id:00000905 style:Normal -->
A knock came, and the door opened. Harvick stepped in, his uniform sweat-damp and smudged with courtyard grit. He gave a nod to Maya, then a sharper one to Niall.

<!-- paragraph:2310 para_id:00000906 style:Normal -->
“Wanted to see me?”

<!-- paragraph:2311 para_id:00000907 style:Normal -->
“We did.” Niall stepped forward. “You were with Arrek when this all happened?”

<!-- paragraph:2312 para_id:00000908 style:Normal -->
Harvick nodded. “Yeah. He was sparring with me.”

<!-- paragraph:2313 para_id:00000909 style:Normal -->
“You trust him?”

<!-- paragraph:2314 para_id:0000090A style:Normal -->
Harvick hesitated. “He’s hiding something.”

<!-- paragraph:2315 para_id:0000090B style:Normal -->
Niall’s brows lifted. “Go on.”

<!-- paragraph:2316 para_id:0000090C style:Normal -->
“I looked into him after Alden flagged him—seemed underqualified for the Sorevin nomination. Turns out he’s got family—cousins, technically—tied up in some bad debt contracts. My guess? He called in every favor he had to get this job.”

<!-- paragraph:2317 para_id:0000090D style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “So why not take advantage? There are things he could’ve stolen. Sensitive areas he could’ve breached.”

<!-- paragraph:2318 para_id:0000090E style:Normal -->
“Exactly,” Harvick said. “But he hasn’t. He shows up. Works hard. Doesn’t act like he’s trying to make a quick buck.”

<!-- paragraph:2319 para_id:0000090F style:Normal -->
“Then what is he doing?” Niall asked.

<!-- paragraph:2320 para_id:00000910 style:Normal -->
Harvick met Niall’s gaze, voice firm. “Once you fight someone, you get a sense of them. You asked me if I trust him. Well, that’s a hard question to answer. But I know that he’s determined and hardworking, and he ran in there when I was holding everyone else back.”

<!-- paragraph:2321 para_id:00000911 style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes narrowed. “Tell me more about that decision of yours.”

<!-- paragraph:2322 para_id:00000912 style:Normal -->
“It was the right call,” Harvick said. “No Shroud? They would’ve been ripped apart. I won’t apologize for that.”

<!-- paragraph:2323 para_id:00000913 style:Normal -->
Niall glanced at Maya. “How did he get to Selwin then? You were right there. You didn’t see him?”

<!-- paragraph:2324 para_id:00000914 style:Normal -->
Maya wiped a hand down her face, remembering the sting of sweat in her eyes, the heat flaring through her limbs. “I was… occupied. Holding the wall. Arrek must have run to Selwin the second that his sigil deactivated. I didn’t even see him until he was there.”

<!-- paragraph:2325 para_id:00000915 style:Normal -->
Niall turned to Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:2326 para_id:00000916 style:Normal -->
“I wasn’t looking either,” Harvick admitted. “He had to be fast. Real fast. That, or reckless as hell. I guess he could also just be stupid. But if that’s the case, I’ll take more stupid like him.”

<!-- paragraph:2327 para_id:00000917 style:Normal -->
Niall exhaled slowly, still watching the door. “Then why did he leave?”

<!-- paragraph:2328 para_id:00000918 style:Normal -->
Harvick blinked. “Sir?”

<!-- paragraph:2329 para_id:00000919 style:Normal -->
“He saved Selwin. He needs money. He must’ve known there’d be a reward. But he handed the boy over and walked away.” Niall’s voice was even, but sharp at the edges. “Why?”

<!-- paragraph:2330 para_id:0000091A style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t answer at first. His eyes flicked to Selwin, then back to Niall.

<!-- paragraph:2331 para_id:0000091B style:Normal -->
Harvick crossed his arms. “I’ve seen that look before. Someone who’s been ground down, told over and over who they are and what they’ll never be. Then they get a chance. Not to get even—but to start over.”

<!-- paragraph:2332 para_id:0000091C style:Normal -->
His voice dropped a notch. “He reminds me of who I was. Before you gave me a way to be something different.”

<!-- paragraph:2333 para_id:0000091D style:Normal -->
Niall studied him for a long beat. “So you admit this is a blind spot for you.”

<!-- paragraph:2334 para_id:0000091E style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged. “Maybe. But maybe he’s not here to steal anything. Maybe he’s not here for power, or status, or even recognition. Maybe he just wants to matter. To be the kind of man who runs toward the fire, even if no one sees him do it.”

<!-- paragraph:2335 para_id:0000091F style:Normal -->
He paused.

<!-- paragraph:2336 para_id:00000920 style:Normal -->
“And if that’s the case… you can trust him. Because that kind of person doesn’t stop when things get hard. They stop when it stops meaning something.”

<!-- paragraph:2337 para_id:00000921 style:Normal -->
Niall was quiet for a moment. Then he looked back up, exhaustion lining his features, “Fine. I’ll trust you this time.”

<!-- paragraph:2338 para_id:00000922 style:Normal -->
He looked to Maya.

<!-- paragraph:2339 para_id:00000923 style:Normal -->
“I hear you’re in need of a new supervisor.”

<!-- paragraph:2340 para_id:00000924 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth, then hesitated. Her thoughts snagged—on the memory of heat, of motion, of a lean form sprinting toward danger without hesitation. On the way Arrek had looked at her—with intensity that almost set her on fire. Not as something to be put on a pedestal and admired, not something to be feared and kept at arms’ length. She could still see the zän etched across his back, strange and sharp and half-lit in the flames. The muscles beneath it, taut with motion.

<!-- paragraph:2341 para_id:00000925 style:Normal -->
She cleared her throat. “I suppose… I am.”

<!-- paragraph:2342 para_id:00000926 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave her a curious look but said nothing as they stepped out together, the heavy doors clicking shut behind them. Maya glanced back one last time.

<!-- paragraph:2343 para_id:00000927 style:Normal -->
Niall hadn’t moved from Selwin’s bedside. He was hunched forward, elbows on his knees, face buried in his hands.

<!-- paragraph:2344 para_id:00000928 style:Normal -->
Harvick paused just outside the heavy doors, the polished steel still vibrating faintly from where they’d shut behind them. “I’ll go tell your new supervisor the good news,” he said, a faint grin tugging at the edge of his mouth. “Try not to scare him off too fast.”

<!-- paragraph:2345 para_id:00000929 style:Normal -->
Maya gave a dry snort. “He’s survived sparring with you. I think he’ll manage.”

<!-- paragraph:2346 para_id:0000092A style:Normal -->
Harvick winked and turned down the corridor, footsteps fading with easy confidence.

<!-- paragraph:2347 para_id:0000092B style:Normal -->
Maya scanned the hall. A flash of auburn caught her attention—Erynn, standing near one of the courtyard doors, shoulder tilted subtly toward one of Maya’s guards. He said something, and she laughed too brightly, tucking a loose strand of hair behind her ear with theatrical nonchalance.

<!-- paragraph:2348 para_id:0000092C style:Normal -->
Maya approached quietly. “You’re not being nearly as subtle as you think.”

<!-- paragraph:2349 para_id:0000092D style:Normal -->
Erynn jumped, then recovered with an exaggerated sigh. “I was just—”

<!-- paragraph:2350 para_id:0000092E style:Normal -->
Maya leaned in slightly. “You’ll have plenty of time to keep flirting in a second.”

<!-- paragraph:2351 para_id:0000092F style:Normal -->
Erynn flushed. She glanced back at the guard as they walked, trying not to seem obvious about it—and failing. “You think he noticed?”

<!-- paragraph:2352 para_id:00000930 style:Normal -->
“I think everyone noticed,” Maya said dryly.

<!-- paragraph:2353 para_id:00000931 style:Normal -->
They walked in silence for a moment, their boots echoing on the tile. Then Erynn glanced sideways. “So… back to the codex?”

<!-- paragraph:2354 para_id:00000932 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, slower this time. “There’s something I need to find.”

<!-- paragraph:2355 para_id:00000933 style:Normal -->
Erynn tilted her head, curious.

<!-- paragraph:2356 para_id:00000934 style:Normal -->
Maya’s voice dropped to something quieter. “When I looked last time… I saw a piece of him. My father. Before he was like this. He laughed. He joked. He looked at my mother like she was the only thing in the world that mattered.”

<!-- paragraph:2357 para_id:00000935 style:Normal -->
She paused, exhaling. “He wasn’t always this—distant. Hard. Like he’s holding something broken together with clenched teeth. I need to understand what happened. If I can figure that out… maybe I can help him put some of it down.”

<!-- paragraph:2358 para_id:00000936 style:Normal -->
When she reached her room, Maya murmured instructions to her guards that she should not be disturbed. Erynn raised an eyebrow, but said nothing—just gave a nod and leaned against the wall, arms loosely crossed. Maya shut the door behind her with a soft click and turned the lock, then turned and crossed to the chest at the foot of her bed.

<!-- paragraph:2359 para_id:00000937 style:Normal -->
The codex sat nestled beneath a layer of folded tunics, wrapped in soft cloth like something too fragile for light. She pulled it free and sat on the edge of the mattress, opening the cover and reciting the key incantation.

<!-- paragraph:2360 para_id:00000938 style:Normal -->
The codex pulled her under like water—cool, heavy, and absolute.

<!-- paragraph:2361 para_id:00000939 style:Normal -->
And then she was Alara.

<!-- paragraph:2362 para_id:0000093A style:Normal -->
There was wind—real wind, not conjured, unbounded by spell or shroud. It carried the smells of stone and sweat, of citrus trees in bloom, of city air thick with dust and breath and too many hearts beating close together.

<!-- paragraph:2363 para_id:0000093B style:Normal -->
Alara stood in a high-carved chariot, its rails etched with the symbol of her House. Guards in blue and bronze surrounded her, their eyes scanning every rooftop, every alley as the wheels creaked over the uneven road.

<!-- paragraph:2364 para_id:0000093C style:Normal -->
Elarion rose before them, impossibly tall. A city of contradictions—polished towers gleaming like truth-stones above slums so tight the alleys ran with filth. The Rezin Citadel loomed ahead, not just massive but intentional, its spires rising like a challenge against the sky.

<!-- paragraph:2365 para_id:0000093D style:Normal -->
Alara’s breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:2366 para_id:0000093E style:Normal -->
She’d heard stories, of course. But she hadn’t expected beauty so tangled with cruelty. Opulence grown like mold across the surface of suffering.

<!-- paragraph:2367 para_id:0000093F style:Normal -->
“Why must it be,” she whispered to herself, “that the good is so timid, and the evil so bold?”

<!-- paragraph:2368 para_id:00000940 style:Normal -->
One of the guards glanced at her but didn’t speak.

<!-- paragraph:2369 para_id:00000941 style:Normal -->
She dismounted at the outer steps, lifting her skirt just slightly to keep it from dragging. And there—waiting, just beyond the archway—stood Niall Rezin.

<!-- paragraph:2370 para_id:00000942 style:Normal -->
He looked younger than she remembered. Not in years, but in something behind his expression. No armor. No bristling rank or grandeur. Just a well-cut tunic, sleeves absent, his arms bared and marked with sigils that pulsed faintly beneath his skin.

<!-- paragraph:2371 para_id:00000943 style:Normal -->
He flushed when he saw her.

<!-- paragraph:2372 para_id:00000944 style:Normal -->
He’s blushing, she thought, startled. Gods, he’s really blushing.

<!-- paragraph:2373 para_id:00000945 style:Normal -->
“You came,” he said. Then laughed softly. “I thought maybe you’d take the peace and stay home.”

<!-- paragraph:2374 para_id:00000946 style:Normal -->
“You look like you’ve never invited anyone to a ball before.”

<!-- paragraph:2375 para_id:00000947 style:Normal -->
“I haven’t.” His smile deepened, just slightly. “But I wanted you to see this.” He offered her his arm and drew her inside.

<!-- paragraph:2376 para_id:00000948 style:Normal -->
They stood just within the threshold of a vast ballroom, Alara’s posture iron-straight beneath ceremonial robes that felt suddenly too plain.

<!-- paragraph:2377 para_id:00000949 style:Normal -->
The scent hit her: perfumed air tinged with incense and the faintest trace of wine. Light spilled through crystalline domes above, scattering fractal shadows over silken drapes and gold-threaded banners. Laughter drifted across the marble floors like mist, gilded and false.

<!-- paragraph:2378 para_id:0000094A style:Normal -->
Alara had seen opulence before—but never like this. Never so sharpened, so deliberate. It glittered. It threatened. It drowned.

<!-- paragraph:2379 para_id:0000094B style:Normal -->
The crowd parted before Niall like reeds before wind. People turned. Heads dipped. Whispers spun between fluted glasses and jeweled hands. His presence was not requested—it was accepted. Expected. His power needed no introduction.

<!-- paragraph:2380 para_id:0000094C style:Normal -->
They passed a man of middle age with skin like polished obsidian, clad in crimson and black, sleeves also conspicuously absent. He raised two fingers in a sharp gesture of respect.

<!-- paragraph:2381 para_id:0000094D style:Normal -->
“Cryndor,” Niall murmured in acknowledgment.

<!-- paragraph:2382 para_id:0000094E style:Normal -->
Alara’s eyes flicked to his bare arms again.

<!-- paragraph:2383 para_id:0000094F style:Normal -->
“Do you all just hate sleeves?”

<!-- paragraph:2384 para_id:00000950 style:Normal -->
Niall looked over at her, mildly sheepish. “It’s a statement. Power as identity. A show of confidence. No armor, just magic.”

<!-- paragraph:2385 para_id:00000951 style:Normal -->
“That’s not what makes a man strong,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:2386 para_id:00000952 style:Normal -->
He raised an eyebrow.

<!-- paragraph:2387 para_id:00000953 style:Normal -->
Alara drew back and glanced at the jagged scars winding along his forearms. “The Clades call what they do magic, but I see magic in every man’s heart beating. In the light behind his eyes, the fact that he knows. That he wants. Just because we’ve gotten used to those things doesn’t make them less miraculous.”

<!-- paragraph:2388 para_id:00000954 style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression faltered—not with offense, but with something quieter. Something like awe.

<!-- paragraph:2389 para_id:00000955 style:Normal -->
“I think that’s why I wanted you here,” he said softly.

<!-- paragraph:2390 para_id:00000956 style:Normal -->
She didn’t answer.

<!-- paragraph:2391 para_id:00000957 style:Normal -->
Eventually, he offered her his arm again, and she took it, allowing him to lead her out of the ballroom through a hidden side passage that opened into a private garden—elevated above the noise, where the stars burned unmarred by glass.

<!-- paragraph:2392 para_id:00000958 style:Normal -->
They sat in the quiet for a while, watching the dusk settle.

<!-- paragraph:2393 para_id:00000959 style:Normal -->
“I don’t usually enjoy parties,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:2394 para_id:0000095A style:Normal -->
“I suppose I should have guessed that.”

<!-- paragraph:2395 para_id:0000095B style:Normal -->
“But I wanted to see if the man I met on the battlefield was real. The one who smiled instead of sneering. Who stepped forward, but not to kill. Who risked himself before ordering others to.”

<!-- paragraph:2396 para_id:0000095C style:Normal -->
Niall looked away, his voice quiet. “I don’t know if he’s real either. But I want him to be.”

<!-- paragraph:2397 para_id:0000095D style:Normal -->
She studied him for a long moment.

<!-- paragraph:2398 para_id:0000095E style:Normal -->
And then he spoke again, voice tight with emotion.

<!-- paragraph:2399 para_id:0000095F style:Normal -->
“How do we know what’s right?”

<!-- paragraph:2400 para_id:00000960 style:Normal -->
She blinked.

<!-- paragraph:2401 para_id:00000961 style:Normal -->
“I mean… how do we know?” he asked, as if the question had been haunting him for years. “What is it in us that recoils from cruelty, even when no one tells us it’s wrong? Why does injustice taste bitter in our mouths?”

<!-- paragraph:2402 para_id:00000962 style:Normal -->
“I don’t know,” Alara said slowly. “But where I come from, we would’ve called that Created. Axiomatic. A thing that does not require an explanation.”

<!-- paragraph:2403 para_id:00000963 style:Normal -->
He turned to her, eyes catching starlight—and held out his hand.

<!-- paragraph:2404 para_id:00000964 style:Normal -->
“Then maybe you can help me find it. Maybe you already know.”

<!-- paragraph:2405 para_id:00000965 style:Normal -->
Her breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:2406 para_id:00000966 style:Normal -->
“You are the most beautiful, fierce woman I’ve ever met. I want to make a union with you. Not just marriage. Union. I want us to work together. Change this place.”

<!-- paragraph:2407 para_id:00000967 style:Normal -->
Alara’s heart thudded once, heavy in her chest.

<!-- paragraph:2408 para_id:00000968 style:Normal -->
“This is… fast,” she said. “I don’t even know what to say.”

<!-- paragraph:2409 para_id:00000969 style:Normal -->
“I don’t either,” Niall admitted, a little helpless. “Within the High Clades, this would normally be arranged, not requested. I thought I’d have to fight to bring my father around, but he said he had no concerns. That he supports this.”

<!-- paragraph:2410 para_id:0000096A style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:102 -->Alara didn’t answer. Her thoughts spun like leaves in a storm wind.

<!-- paragraph:2411 para_id:0000096B style:Normal -->
But his passion—his sincerity—held her fast. That quiet awkwardness. That deep, aching want to be more than what the world had made him.

<!-- paragraph:2412 para_id:0000096C style:Normal -->
Before she realized it, the words slipped from her mouth.

<!-- paragraph:2413 para_id:0000096D style:Normal -->
“Yes,” she said. “We’ll work together. We’ll make this world better.”

<!-- paragraph:2414 para_id:0000096E style:Normal -->
Niall closed his eyes, and when he opened them, he smiled.<!-- comment-end:102 --><!-- comment-ref:102 -->

<!-- paragraph:2415 para_id:0000096F style:Normal -->
A new presence approached—soft steps, deliberate and feather-light, but somehow impossible to ignore. Maya—Alara—turned.

<!-- paragraph:2416 para_id:00000970 style:Normal -->
The man was ancient. That much was clear at a glance. His skin bore the translucent sheen of glass-thin parchment stretched over sinew, and yet there was no tremor in his gait. His spine straight, his eyes sharp, his smile fixed in an expression just shy of manic delight.

<!-- paragraph:2417 para_id:00000971 style:Normal -->
Niall straightened, stepping slightly in front of Alara.

<!-- paragraph:2418 para_id:00000972 style:Normal -->
“Father.”

<!-- paragraph:2419 para_id:00000973 style:Normal -->
So this was him. Elric Rezin.

<!-- paragraph:2420 para_id:00000974 style:Normal -->
The old man’s smile widened, lines folding in patterns too precise to be accidental. “So it’s true, then?” he asked, his voice full of brittle joy. “The union has been approved?”

<!-- paragraph:2421 para_id:00000975 style:Normal -->
He looked between the two of them, eyes glittering like blackened quartz. Niall turned slightly toward Alara—hesitating for only a moment—then gave a single nod.

<!-- paragraph:2422 para_id:00000976 style:Normal -->
“Yes.”

<!-- paragraph:2423 para_id:00000977 style:Normal -->
Elric’s hands clasped in front of him. “Marvelous. Marvelous. I must confess, I was… concerned. When my son unilaterally pulled our forces back from the southern front, I feared he'd been ensnared by some... moral virus. But now...” He leaned slightly closer to Alara. “Now I see. He was wise.”

<!-- paragraph:2424 para_id:00000978 style:Normal -->
Alara felt her spine stiffen.

<!-- paragraph:2425 para_id:00000979 style:Normal -->
Elric cocked his head, birdlike. “Have you ever felt it, girl? The forbidden song of the strands of fate? That hidden thrum beneath the world’s crust? The shiver of possibility aching to be heard?”

<!-- paragraph:2426 para_id:0000097A style:Normal -->
His voice had taken on a strange quality—still soft, but almost resonant. Too measured. Too practiced.

<!-- paragraph:2427 para_id:0000097B style:Normal -->
Alara answered cautiously. “This thing is not spoken of among my people.”

<!-- paragraph:2428 para_id:0000097C style:Normal -->
And then everything stilled.

<!-- paragraph:2429 para_id:0000097D style:Normal -->
Niall froze mid-breath, hand still half-raised in some gesture. The garden torches ceased their flicker. The wind halted. Even the stars above seemed to hold.

<!-- paragraph:2430 para_id:0000097E style:Normal -->
Only Elric moved.

<!-- paragraph:2431 para_id:0000097F style:Normal -->
He stepped closer, but his gaze was no longer on Alara.

<!-- paragraph:2432 para_id:00000980 style:Normal -->
It passed through her.

<!-- paragraph:2433 para_id:00000981 style:Normal -->
“And how about you,” he murmured, “my granddaughter?”

<!-- paragraph:2434 para_id:00000982 style:Normal -->
Maya felt it like ice plunging into her veins—paralyzing, electric. She couldn’t move. Couldn’t speak. The memory should have ended, but it hadn’t. The codex held her fast.

<!-- paragraph:2435 para_id:00000983 style:Normal -->
Elric’s expression softened—but not in kindness. There was something reverent there. Something gleeful and terrible.

<!-- paragraph:2436 para_id:00000984 style:Normal -->
“You think you know magic,” he said, voice lilting like a litany. “You’ve been told it is blood and birthright, zä and sigil, discipline and will.”

<!-- paragraph:2437 para_id:00000985 style:Normal -->
He took another step forward. The air around him shimmered—not with heat, but something else. Something off.

<!-- paragraph:2438 para_id:00000986 style:Normal -->
“But there are currents beneath the lattice. Old and rootless. They do not speak—they sway. They do not burn—they bend.”

<!-- paragraph:2439 para_id:00000987 style:Normal -->
His breath brushed her cheek, and she couldn’t even shudder.

<!-- paragraph:2440 para_id:00000988 style:Normal -->
“There are truths older than the first zä,” he whispered. “And they are not truths you seek. They are truths that seek you.”

<!-- paragraph:2441 para_id:00000989 style:Normal -->
Elric raised a hand—not in threat, but in invitation.

<!-- paragraph:2442 para_id:0000098A style:Normal -->
“Come. Let me show you. Let me drape your mind in the shape of the unknowable. Let me pour into you the taste of the thing that dreams through us all.”

<!-- paragraph:2443 para_id:0000098B style:Normal -->
His voice was soft. Almost kind.

<!-- paragraph:2444 para_id:0000098C style:Normal -->
“You are not ready. But you are necessary.”

<!-- paragraph:2445 para_id:0000098D style:Normal -->
Elric placed his hand gently on her brow.

<!-- paragraph:2446 para_id:0000098E style:Normal -->
And the world inverted.

<!-- paragraph:2447 para_id:0000098F style:Normal -->
The garden shattered into glass slivers of meaning. Time bloomed sideways. Distance curled back into itself. Shapes wore other shapes as skins. Thought slurred into color.

<!-- paragraph:2448 para_id:00000990 style:Normal -->
She saw the garden—no, the memory—no, the shape of the moment—folded through itself like a ribbon in boiling water. Niall's expression: both hopeful and sorrowful and never-having-been-born. Elric: a spiral collapsing inward. The torchlight: teeth.

<!-- paragraph:2449 para_id:00000991 style:Normal -->
Maya floated in a chamber with no walls, suspended not in space, but possibility. Everything flickered with layered transparency—as if seen from the wrong angle through the wrong kind of eye.

<!-- paragraph:2450 para_id:00000992 style:Normal -->
And then—

<!-- paragraph:2451 para_id:00000993 style:Normal -->
She saw.

<!-- paragraph:2452 para_id:00000994 style:Normal -->
Threads. A thousand thousand filaments, no thicker than a whisper, no brighter than a breath. Each vibrated in place, hanging in the void like veins of consequence. Some glowed with frost, some with fire. But there—there was one in front of her.

<!-- paragraph:2453 para_id:00000995 style:Normal -->
Not distant. Not near. Just there.

<!-- paragraph:2454 para_id:00000996 style:Normal -->
It pulsed with frenetic life—wound tight like a violin string mid-scream. Around it: nothing. A cold that wasn’t cold, a dark that didn’t lack light. A silence that watched.

<!-- paragraph:2455 para_id:00000997 style:Normal -->
And the thread called to her.

<!-- paragraph:2456 para_id:00000998 style:Normal -->
It was like the ones she moved when she wove heat or bent cold with Convergence. But it was deeper. Older. This one belonged to something. Meant something.

<!-- paragraph:2457 para_id:00000999 style:Normal -->
She knew, with clarity that turned her bones to salt, that she could reach out and touch it.

<!-- paragraph:2458 para_id:0000099A style:Normal -->
Just a brush. Just a whisper of will.

<!-- paragraph:2459 para_id:0000099B style:Normal -->
And the world would change.

<!-- paragraph:2460 para_id:0000099C style:Normal -->
Then Elric’s voice echoed through the void—not from behind, not from within. From above the structure of knowing.

<!-- paragraph:2461 para_id:0000099D style:Normal -->
Some threads burn when pulled. Some unravel what should never have been stitched. But the ones worth weaving? They scream like children in the dark.

<!-- paragraph:2462 para_id:0000099E style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth—

<!-- paragraph:2463 para_id:0000099F style:Normal -->
And screamed.

<!-- paragraph:2464 para_id:000009A0 style:Normal -->
Reality surged back like a breaking wave.

<!-- paragraph:2465 para_id:000009A1 style:Normal -->
Her body convulsed. The codex flew from her hands, striking the floor with a sound like a shattering clock. The memory ruptured.

<!-- paragraph:2466 para_id:000009A2 style:Normal -->
The room spun. Her vision doubled, then tripled, then twisted into a single axis of now.

<!-- paragraph:2467 para_id:000009A3 style:Normal -->
The door smashed inward.

<!-- paragraph:2468 para_id:000009A4 style:Normal -->
Her guards burst through in a blur of steel and panic, Erynn behind them, eyes wide with terror.

<!-- paragraph:2469 para_id:000009A5 style:Normal -->
“Maya—!”

<!-- paragraph:2470 para_id:000009A6 style:Normal -->
But all Maya could say—her voice hoarse, barely human—was: “He saw me—he saw me through the wrong side of the mirror—”

<!-- paragraph:2471 para_id:000009A7 style:Normal -->
Then she fell into black.

<!-- paragraph:2474 para_id:000009AA style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 16

<!-- paragraph:2475 para_id:000009AB style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 16

<!-- paragraph:2476 para_id:000009AC style:Heading2 -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2477 para_id:000009AD style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:103 -->I am such an idiot.

<!-- paragraph:2478 para_id:000009AE style:Normal -->
Davian paced the narrow length of his quarters, fists clenched, boots thudding against stone. His pulse hadn’t fully slowed, and his chest still ached from Harvick’s last blow. Or maybe from the aftermath—of what he’d done.

<!-- paragraph:2479 para_id:000009AF style:Normal -->
Selwin. Idiot boy. Idiot him.<!-- comment-end:103 --><!-- comment-ref:103 -->

<!-- paragraph:2480 para_id:000009B0 style:Normal -->
He stopped in front of the far wall. Looked at it like it might offer an answer.

<!-- paragraph:2481 para_id:000009B1 style:Normal -->
Then he drew Antithesis and slammed it into the stone.

<!-- paragraph:2482 para_id:000009B2 style:Normal -->
The blade sank deep with a crack, zän flickering faintly along the hilt. The impact echoed through the small chamber. Still not loud enough to drown out the noise in his head.

<!-- paragraph:2483 para_id:000009B3 style:Normal -->
Why had he done it? Why had he run?

<!-- paragraph:2484 para_id:000009B4 style:Normal -->
He could have let the wall fall. Let the magic burn itself out. Maybe Maya would’ve been crushed. Maybe Selwin would’ve died.

<!-- paragraph:2485 para_id:000009B5 style:Normal -->
Two High Clades, gone. What a gift to the world that would’ve been.

<!-- paragraph:2486 para_id:000009B6 style:Normal -->
But he hadn’t.

<!-- paragraph:2487 para_id:000009B7 style:Normal -->
He’d sprinted. Reacted. Moved before he even thought. Like some damn hero out of the stories the Veiled Clades whispered to their children when the hunger got too loud.

<!-- paragraph:2488 para_id:000009B8 style:Normal -->
Davian braced his hands against the wall beside the blade, forehead dropping to the cool stone.

<!-- paragraph:2489 para_id:000009B9 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t just the action. It was the feeling—that flicker in his chest when he saw Maya struggling, the wall about to collapse. That need to protect. To matter. He hated it.

<!-- paragraph:2490 para_id:000009BA style:Normal -->
He’d seen that desperation in Selwin’s eyes—the desire to be noticed, to be enough. And he’d seen himself. A younger version. Reaching. Bleeding. Dying to be worthy of a father who only valued results. Who only praised kill counts and clean executions.

<!-- paragraph:2491 para_id:000009BB style:Normal -->
He had bragged to the Syndicate - that he was capable of anything. Of killing anyone. But what kind of assassin runs toward a collapsing wall?

<!-- paragraph:2492 para_id:000009BC style:Normal -->
What kind of monster hesitates because a woman with fire in her hands and ice at her feet brushes his skin and says thank you?

<!-- paragraph:2493 para_id:000009BD style:Normal -->
His skin still burned where she’d touched him. Like her voice had wormed into the raw space where guilt and need had settled.

<!-- paragraph:2494 para_id:000009BE style:Normal -->
The Ink Boon. Activated again. And for what?

<!-- paragraph:2495 para_id:000009BF style:Normal -->
He rubbed his forearms, teeth clenched. It wasn’t supposed to happen like that. It was supposed to be controlled. Strategic. Useful.

<!-- paragraph:2496 para_id:000009C0 style:Normal -->
He could feel the aftershocks still—like something vital had been drained. That power wasn’t his. It was Jalen’s, too. Given to him with trust. With purpose. And he’d spent it on instinct. On emotion.

<!-- paragraph:2497 para_id:000009C1 style:Normal -->
He let out a ragged breath and turned, leaning against the wall, spine pressing into the stone.

<!-- paragraph:2498 para_id:000009C2 style:Normal -->
There had been a moment—back in the courtyard—training with Harvick. Where everything had almost made sense. When the sword moved the way he wanted it to. When his mind quieted for just long enough that he could hear something else underneath.

<!-- paragraph:2499 para_id:000009C3 style:Normal -->
Something like meaning.

<!-- paragraph:2500 para_id:000009C4 style:Normal -->
It had felt good. And that made it worse.

<!-- paragraph:2501 para_id:000009C5 style:Normal -->
He slid down the wall, arms draped over his knees. Pain flared in his ribs. Harvick had gotten a clean hit earlier. It grounded him. Gave the guilt something to attach to.

<!-- paragraph:2502 para_id:000009C6 style:Normal -->
He reached out and tugged Antithesis free from the wall. The blade slid out with a low, metallic whisper, the runes along its length pulsing faintly in the dim light.

<!-- paragraph:2503 para_id:000009C7 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at it for a long moment.

<!-- paragraph:2504 para_id:000009C8 style:Normal -->
Then, softly—almost like he was afraid someone might hear—he said, “I don’t know if I’m strong enough for this, Selina.”

<!-- paragraph:2505 para_id:000009C9 style:Normal -->
The name hit the air like a confession.

<!-- paragraph:2506 para_id:000009CA style:Normal -->
“I thought I’d come here and see monsters. Cold eyes. Empty hearts. Something I could cut down and walk away from.” His fingers tightened on the hilt. “Instead I saw… me. I saw a boy just trying to be enough. A girl trying to hold the world together.”

<!-- paragraph:2507 para_id:000009CB style:Normal -->
He let the blade rest across his knees. The runes flared once, then dimmed.

<!-- paragraph:2508 para_id:000009CC style:Normal -->
“Would you be ashamed of me right now?” he asked. “For hesitating? For helping?”

<!-- paragraph:2509 para_id:000009CD style:Normal -->
He looked down at the knife—sleek, dark, unyielding. “You gave me this because you said carrying it made you think too much about killing. That you’d give up your violence and look for a world that didn’t need it. But it still found you. Closing your eyes didn’t make it go away.”

<!-- paragraph:2510 para_id:000009CE style:Normal -->
A long pause.

<!-- paragraph:2511 para_id:000009CF style:Normal -->
“If you’d kept it… would you still be alive?”

<!-- paragraph:2512 para_id:000009D0 style:Normal -->
The silence that followed was vast and aching.

<!-- paragraph:2513 para_id:000009D1 style:Normal -->
And then—knock knock.

<!-- paragraph:2514 para_id:000009D2 style:Normal -->
Davian flinched, turning sharply toward the door, the blade clutched tighter in his hands.

<!-- paragraph:2515 para_id:000009D3 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” came Harvick’s voice, muffled but unmistakable. “You in there, Arrek? Got some news you’re gonna want to hear.”

<!-- paragraph:2516 para_id:000009D4 style:Normal -->
Davian tucked Antithesis back into its wrap and shoved it under the mattress in one quick motion. Then he splashed a little water on his face and scrubbed at his hair, making it look just rumpled enough. By the time he opened the door, his best impression of someone half-asleep was already in place.

<!-- paragraph:2517 para_id:000009D5 style:Normal -->
Harvick took one look at him and raised an eyebrow. “Sleeping? Really? After all that?”

<!-- paragraph:2518 para_id:000009D6 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned against the frame. “You missed it—an entire citadel almost fell on me. I figured I earned a nap.”

<!-- paragraph:2519 para_id:000009D7 style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted and pushed past him, stepping into the room without waiting for permission. He shut the door behind him with a firm click.

<!-- paragraph:2520 para_id:000009D8 style:Normal -->
Davian tensed. “What do you want?”

<!-- paragraph:2521 para_id:000009D9 style:Normal -->
Harvick turned around slowly, expression unreadable for once. “I know your secret.”

<!-- paragraph:2522 para_id:000009DA style:Normal -->
Every part of Davian went still. His hand inched toward the bed without looking. Toward the knife.

<!-- paragraph:2523 para_id:000009DB style:Normal -->
Harvick raised both hands, palms out. “Whoa there, son.”

<!-- paragraph:2524 para_id:000009DC style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t move.

<!-- paragraph:2525 para_id:000009DD style:Normal -->
“I did a little digging,” Harvick said, lowering his hands. “Found out about your cousins. Debt contracts wrapped up tighter than a war mage’s grip.”

<!-- paragraph:2526 para_id:000009DE style:Normal -->
It took a second for the words to register. When they did, the relief hit so fast it left him lightheaded. He staggered slightly, and Harvick reached out without hesitation to steady him.

<!-- paragraph:2527 para_id:000009DF style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Harvick said, voice suddenly gentler. “You all right?”

<!-- paragraph:2528 para_id:000009E0 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, barely. His legs felt shaky, like the panic had nowhere left to go.

<!-- paragraph:2529 para_id:000009E1 style:Normal -->
Harvick grinned. “Guess you overexerted yourself, being such a big damn hero and all.”

<!-- paragraph:2530 para_id:000009E2 style:Normal -->
Davian shot him a look, but didn’t pull away.

<!-- paragraph:2531 para_id:000009E3 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Harvick said, stepping back, “I figured you’d want to hear it from me. Niall and Maya know the truth now. And they’re promoting you to supervisor.”

<!-- paragraph:2532 para_id:000009E4 style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. “What?”

<!-- paragraph:2533 para_id:000009E5 style:Normal -->
“You’re getting a squad. A title. Real responsibility.” Harvick smiled, but it was tinged with something harder to place. “Congrats, kid.”

<!-- paragraph:2534 para_id:000009E6 style:Normal -->
“I don’t want it.”

<!-- paragraph:2535 para_id:000009E7 style:Normal -->
“I knew you’d try to turn this down,” Harvick said, already turning to lean against the wall. “That’s why I came to tell you myself. Because tough shit. Life is just a long chain of doing things you don’t want to do until you either get used to them or die.”

<!-- paragraph:2536 para_id:000009E8 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at him.

<!-- paragraph:2537 para_id:000009E9 style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged. “You saved that kid. Maybe not for the right reasons. Maybe you’re angry about it. But you did it. And we both know you need the money, so I’m not going to let you sabotage this for yourself.”

<!-- paragraph:2538 para_id:000009EA style:Normal -->
He let the silence stretch, then added more quietly, “It’s awkward for both of us, all right? I’m not a recruiter. But I’ve been where you are. And I’m telling you—you don’t have to like the world to fight for a better piece of it.”

<!-- paragraph:2539 para_id:000009EB style:Normal -->
Davian wanted to say no.

<!-- paragraph:2540 para_id:000009EC style:Normal -->
The words were there—sharp, familiar. A reflex. He didn’t want to be trusted. He didn’t want to be seen. He didn’t want to be known.

<!-- paragraph:2541 para_id:000009ED style:Normal -->
But then Harvick just stood there, arms crossed, like the world was heavy and he was tired of carrying it alone—but willing to try again, if Davian would help.

<!-- paragraph:2542 para_id:000009EE style:Normal -->
Not judging. Not prodding. Just… there.

<!-- paragraph:2543 para_id:000009EF style:Normal -->
And for some reason, that made it harder.

<!-- paragraph:2544 para_id:000009F0 style:Normal -->
Like maybe it wasn’t a trap. Maybe it was awkward—for both of them—but that was fine. That was life. And Harvick, somehow, made the weight feel a little more manageable. Like they could shoulder it together.

<!-- paragraph:2545 para_id:000009F1 style:Normal -->
So instead of saying no, Davian exhaled, slow and resigned.

<!-- paragraph:2546 para_id:000009F2 style:Normal -->
“Fine,” he muttered. “But I’m not wearing the stupid armband.”

<!-- paragraph:2547 para_id:000009F3 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s grin returned, quick and wolfish. “That’s the spirit. You will though.”

<!-- paragraph:2548 para_id:000009F4 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2549 para_id:000009F5 style:Normal -->
When Davian stepped into the dining hall, the shift was immediate. Conversations faltered. Heads turned. Utensils froze midair.

<!-- paragraph:2550 para_id:000009F6 style:Normal -->
He paused just past the threshold, stomach sinking.

<!-- paragraph:2551 para_id:000009F7 style:Normal -->
Oh no.

<!-- paragraph:2552 para_id:000009F8 style:Normal -->
He scanned the room—no visible injuries, no collapsed ceiling, no dead bodies. Just everyone staring at him.

<!-- paragraph:2553 para_id:000009F9 style:Normal -->
How does word travel this fast?

<!-- paragraph:2554 para_id:000009FA style:Normal -->
Before he could retreat, Tanelle was already barreling toward him with the velocity of an arrow in flight. Her hair bounced with each step, her grin wide enough to qualify as weaponized.

<!-- paragraph:2555 para_id:000009FB style:Normal -->
“Congratulations!” she said, seizing his arm with both hands.

<!-- paragraph:2556 para_id:000009FC style:Normal -->
He groaned. “Congratulations for what?”

<!-- paragraph:2557 para_id:000009FD style:Normal -->
“I saw that guard guy you’re always sparring with? He was by the west corridor bragging about you. Said you got promoted! Told me to spread the news, so I did.”

<!-- paragraph:2558 para_id:000009FE style:Normal -->
Davian winced. “That’s true, I guess. They must’ve finally noticed my immense talents.”

<!-- paragraph:2559 para_id:000009FF style:Normal -->
He tried to walk past her, but she matched pace without missing a beat.

<!-- paragraph:2560 para_id:00000A00 style:Normal -->
“So, obviously it’s because of what happened with Selwin. The guards say you ran through fire. And you’ve been practicing with Harvick down in the elite training grounds - how did you get down there anyway - and then there was some kind of magic thing and an explosion and Lady Maya was there and she made a big wall of ice.”

<!-- paragraph:2561 para_id:00000A01 style:Normal -->
Davian picked up a tray and started assembling the most nondescript plate of food imaginable.

<!-- paragraph:2562 para_id:00000A02 style:Normal -->
Tanelle kept going, her voice an unrelenting hum of enthusiasm. “You know, I’ve always said you seemed like supervisor material. They never listen to me, but clearly I was right. It’s so exciting! You’re going to be working with us but like in charge now and it’ll be so much better to not only have one supervisor for both groups - not that I don’t like Marek but sometimes he’s just not very fun and…” Tanelle had to stop briefly - very briefly - to take a breath.

<!-- paragraph:2563 para_id:00000A03 style:Normal -->
Davian slid into the seat across from Kolvar and Jessa with the weary grace of a man trying to disappear by sheer force of posture.

<!-- paragraph:2564 para_id:00000A04 style:Normal -->
Tanelle sat with him.

<!-- paragraph:2565 para_id:00000A05 style:Normal -->
Kolvar raised an eyebrow over the rim of his mug. Jessa gave him a sympathetic smile. Davian shot them a helpless look, somewhere between please rescue me and just kill me now.

<!-- paragraph:2566 para_id:00000A06 style:Normal -->
Tanelle didn’t notice. “—and I told him, I said, if you think that guy’s just some lackey, then you clearly haven’t seen how he swings a sword. Like, actually swings it, not just, you know, in theory. Obviously he’s a fast learner too. And fast at running! Through fire and ice - I know that Lady Maya can do both at once - or like, she has to? I heard—”

<!-- paragraph:2567 para_id:00000A07 style:Normal -->
Despite the barrage of words, it was astonishing how little it required of him. Tanelle filled every silence with unrelenting enthusiasm, her questions entirely rhetorical, her conclusions self-sustaining. He nodded once, maybe twice, and otherwise focused on pretending to chew.

<!-- paragraph:2568 para_id:00000A08 style:Normal -->
When she finally took another breath—then a bite of bread—he seized the opportunity.

<!-- paragraph:2569 para_id:00000A09 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned forward, still chewing, and asked—loudly, too loudly—“So, Kolvar. I hear you’re an expert on… ward destabilization analysis? Is that exciting?” He hoped that didn’t sound too made-up.

<!-- paragraph:2570 para_id:00000A0A style:Normal -->
Kolvar stared at him for a beat, the corner of his mouth twitching like he knew exactly what this was. “It’s riveting,” he said at last. “The best day of my life, I spent six hours watching volatile zä residue slowly destabilize in a sealed ward chamber. It was like falling in love, but with more screaming.”

<!-- paragraph:2571 para_id:00000A0B style:Normal -->
Jessa snorted into her tea.

<!-- paragraph:2572 para_id:00000A0C style:Normal -->
Davian nodded solemnly. “Sounds intense.”

<!-- paragraph:2573 para_id:00000A0D style:Normal -->
Kolvar lifted his mug. “Every day’s a gift.” Then, with a small glance at Tanelle—who was just inhaling to start another round—he turned back to Davian. “But I did see something strange yesterday. Want to hear about it?”

<!-- paragraph:2574 para_id:00000A0E style:Normal -->
Davian almost sagged in relief. “Desperately.”

<!-- paragraph:2575 para_id:00000A0F style:Normal -->
Kolvar leaned back slightly, voice low. “Met a guard yesterday who said he was part of the force that attacked the Rezins a few days ago. Claimed he got torched—bad. Skin peeling, lungs gone. Said he was done for.”

<!-- paragraph:2576 para_id:00000A10 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “And yet here he is?”

<!-- paragraph:2577 para_id:00000A11 style:Normal -->
Kolvar nodded. “Said it wasn’t Niall who saved him. It was Lady Maya. Apparently she stepped in personally, demanded he be stabilized. Got him patched up. All she wanted was information.”

<!-- paragraph:2578 para_id:00000A12 style:Normal -->
Jessa tilted her head. “And then what? Let him go?”

<!-- paragraph:2579 para_id:00000A13 style:Normal -->
Kolvar shook his head. “He knew he was marked after that. If he left, his old squad would kill him. So he stayed. Claimed Niall gave him a job.”

<!-- paragraph:2580 para_id:00000A14 style:Normal -->
Davian raised an eyebrow. “That doesn’t sound plausible. Hiring a soldier who just tried to kill your people?”

<!-- paragraph:2581 para_id:00000A15 style:Normal -->
“It does sound like the sort of story you’d plant,” Jessa said, swirling her tea. “Mercy, redemption—make your enemies think surrendering might save their lives. Loosen their tongues.”

<!-- paragraph:2582 para_id:00000A16 style:Normal -->
Kolvar shrugged. “Maybe. I’m just telling you what he said.”

<!-- paragraph:2583 para_id:00000A17 style:Normal -->
Jessa leaned forward. “Was this the same friend you made playing dice in the guard barracks?”

<!-- paragraph:2584 para_id:00000A18 style:Normal -->
“Even so,” Kolvar replied with mock gravity, “gamblers are famously devoted to truth above all else.”

<!-- paragraph:2585 para_id:00000A19 style:Normal -->
She rolled her eyes and went back to her drink.

<!-- paragraph:2586 para_id:00000A1A style:Normal -->
Davian glanced between them. “But—did Maya really save him?”

<!-- paragraph:2587 para_id:00000A1B style:Normal -->
Kolvar nodded again, slower this time. “That part I believe. Some of the older guards say Niall used to do that too. Intervene. Said he’d tell them they weren’t expendable.”

<!-- paragraph:2588 para_id:00000A1C style:Normal -->
He lifted his mug and took a sip.

<!-- paragraph:2589 para_id:00000A1D style:Normal -->
“Not for a while, though.”

<!-- paragraph:2590 para_id:00000A1E style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head. “I don’t know if I buy it. Niall Rezin’s not exactly known for… mercy. He’s a magus famous for wiping out entire armies, not sparing foot soldiers.”

<!-- paragraph:2591 para_id:00000A1F style:Normal -->
Kolvar smirked. “Well, now that you’re a fancy supervisor, maybe you can ask him yourself.”

<!-- paragraph:2592 para_id:00000A20 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:104 --><!-- comment-start:105 -->Tanelle gasped. “Wait, really? Is that something you could actually do?”

<!-- paragraph:2593 para_id:00000A21 style:Normal -->
Davian tried to keep his expression neutral. “I doubt it.”

<!-- paragraph:2594 para_id:00000A22 style:Normal -->
Tanelle poked him in the ribs with an excited grin. “But you’ll be reporting directly to Lady Maya, right? That’s almost the same thing!”<!-- comment-end:104 --><!-- comment-ref:104 --><!-- comment-end:105 --><!-- comment-ref:105 -->

<!-- paragraph:2595 para_id:00000A23 style:Normal -->
He attempted to smile. It looked more like a wince.

<!-- paragraph:2596 para_id:00000A24 style:Normal -->
Jessa raised her brows. “Gods, don’t look so thrilled. You’ll give the poor girl a complex.”

<!-- paragraph:2597 para_id:00000A25 style:Normal -->
Davian groaned. “Turns out, dragging crazy High Clade children out from under collapsing citadels is kind of exhausting.”

<!-- paragraph:2598 para_id:00000A26 style:Normal -->
He pushed back from the table and stood.

<!-- paragraph:2599 para_id:00000A27 style:Normal -->
“I’m going to turn in before someone congratulates me again.”

<!-- paragraph:2600 para_id:00000A28 style:Normal -->
Kolvar raised his mug. “Don’t trip on the weight of your new responsibilities.”

<!-- paragraph:2601 para_id:00000A29 style:Normal -->
“Or your ego,” Jessa added.

<!-- paragraph:2602 para_id:00000A2A style:Normal -->
“Or both,” Tanelle said brightly.

<!-- paragraph:2603 para_id:00000A2B style:Normal -->
Davian gave them a tired mock salute and turned toward the dorms. But as he walked away, something about their teasing stuck with him—warmth curling at the edges of his exhaustion.

<!-- paragraph:2604 para_id:00000A2C style:Normal -->
It was good-natured. Kind. Like he belonged.

<!-- paragraph:2605 para_id:00000A2D style:Normal -->
And for a brief, fragile second, he didn’t hate the way that felt.

<!-- paragraph:2606 para_id:00000A2E style:Normal -->
As Davian stepped into the quiet hallway, the echo of laughter behind him fading, the contrast struck him hard. This warmth, this casual teasing over shared food, was a world away from the slums of Elarion—where every word was measured, every smile a threat with teeth behind it. Where he worked under his father’s cold shadow, using their power in the shadows out of fear that light would expose them to more danger. Where even having the Concord’s leadership only painted a target between your shoulders.

<!-- paragraph:2607 para_id:00000A2F style:Normal -->
Here, in the heart of the enemy, people let their guards down.

<!-- paragraph:2608 para_id:00000A30 style:Normal -->
They ate. Laughed. Mocked each other for fun, not dominance.

<!-- paragraph:2609 para_id:00000A31 style:Normal -->
It burned, that difference. Not just with envy—but with anger. Because this wasn’t some miracle of merit. This was what people got when they were born beneath a High Clade’s banner. Safety. Community. Protection that reached out like a net and actually caught you.

<!-- paragraph:2610 para_id:00000A32 style:Normal -->
But only if you were one of theirs.

<!-- paragraph:2611 para_id:00000A33 style:Normal -->
Even Harvick’s offer—promotion, training, a place in the guard—was just that. His safety. Not his siblings’, not Jalen’s. Not the twisted, half-burned tenements of the west edge where the light barely penetrated past the encircling spires.

<!-- paragraph:2612 para_id:00000A34 style:Normal -->
That protection was never meant to reach the rest of the city.

<!-- paragraph:2613 para_id:00000A35 style:Normal -->
And he wasn’t here to change that.He was here to steal.

<!-- paragraph:2614 para_id:00000A36 style:Normal -->
To kill.

<!-- paragraph:2615 para_id:00000A37 style:Normal -->
To do the job he’d been sent for. And the fact that—for one awful moment—he forgot that?

<!-- paragraph:2616 para_id:00000A38 style:Normal -->
That was the worst part.

<!-- paragraph:2617 para_id:00000A39 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned against the cool stone wall, still stewing in his own thoughts. If he couldn’t lift his people out of the gutter, maybe he could bring the High Clades down into it—make them taste what it was like to drown in the dirt, to scramble for scraps while the gods looked away. Some grim, gnawing part of him craved it.

<!-- paragraph:2618 para_id:00000A3A style:Normal -->
But even as the thought sharpened inside him, another part of him recoiled. There was something wrong with that. Something hollow.

<!-- paragraph:2619 para_id:00000A3B style:Normal -->
Movement stirred at the edges of his awareness. He blinked and realized the dining hall was emptying, the tide of bodies flowing around him while he stood motionless, a rock in a river.

<!-- paragraph:2620 para_id:00000A3C style:Normal -->
Tanelle’s bright voice snapped him back to himself.
 “Hey! What are you doing just lurking out here?” she asked, half laughing.

<!-- paragraph:2621 para_id:00000A3D style:Normal -->
Davian looked for the first excuse he could find. His eyes landed on Marek—a thick-shouldered, hawk-faced man stalking toward the far door.
 “I need to talk to Marek,” he said hastily, already turning away.

<!-- paragraph:2622 para_id:00000A3E style:Normal -->
Tanelle gave a curious look but bounced off toward the departing crowd. Davian jogged a few steps to catch up with Marek, falling into step beside him. For a moment, neither spoke.

<!-- paragraph:2623 para_id:00000A3F style:Normal -->
Finally, Davian cleared his throat. “I need help. With being a supervisor.”

<!-- paragraph:2624 para_id:00000A40 style:Normal -->
Marek gave him a slow, sidelong glance, his expression caught somewhere between irritation and dry amusement.

<!-- paragraph:2625 para_id:00000A41 style:Normal -->
“If you didn’t know how to do the job,” Marek said, “you shouldn’t have manipulated your way <!-- comment-start:106 -->into it.”

<!-- paragraph:2626 para_id:00000A42 style:Normal -->
Davian grit his teeth, but kept his voice level. “I didn’t ask for this position. Pretty hard to say no when a Rezin tells you to do something.”

<!-- paragraph:2627 para_id:00000A43 style:Normal -->
He hesitated, then added, “And I need the money. That’s why I took the job in the first place. Not because I was trying to climb over anyone.”

<!-- paragraph:2628 para_id:00000A44 style:Normal -->
Marek’s steps slowed a fraction. Davian pressed on, feeling the words stick in his throat.
 “Look, I know you’re doing two supervisors’ work right now. You’re stuck with me whether you like it or not. So we might as well make the best of it.”<!-- comment-end:106 --><!-- comment-ref:106 -->

<!-- paragraph:2629 para_id:00000A45 style:Normal -->
Marek let out a long, grating sigh.
 “Fine,” he said at last, like a man agreeing to sit in a muddy ditch during a rainstorm. “But neither of us is going to enjoy this.”

<!-- paragraph:2630 para_id:00000A46 style:Normal -->
Davian managed an exhausted grin. “Wasn’t really expecting to.”

<!-- paragraph:2631 para_id:00000A47 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2632 para_id:00000A48 style:Normal -->
Hours later, Davian stumbled through the halls with diagrams and logistics swirling in his skull, his body half on autopilot. The torchlights blurred past him. His mind was numb from the torrent of information Marek had directed into it.

<!-- paragraph:2633 para_id:00000A49 style:Normal -->
When he shoved the door to his room open, he stopped cold.

<!-- paragraph:2634 para_id:00000A4A style:Normal -->
Someone was sitting inside.

<!-- paragraph:2635 para_id:00000A4B style:Normal -->
Davian’s hand went instinctively to Antithesis—but he froze when the figure lifted his head.

<!-- paragraph:2636 para_id:00000A4C style:Normal -->
A young man, surprisingly young, no older than Davian, lounged in the chair like he owned the place. Familiar hazel eyes gleamed with something like dark amusement—or maybe hunger. He smiled, wide and sharp.

<!-- paragraph:2637 para_id:00000A4D style:Normal -->
It was the man from the Syndicate.

<!-- paragraph:2638 para_id:00000A4E style:Normal -->
Davian had never seen him outside of their arranged meetings. He wasn’t supposed to. He wasn't supposed to exist at all, once the deals were made.

<!-- paragraph:2639 para_id:00000A4F style:Normal -->
Davian’s mouth was dry. “How did you get past the Registry?”

<!-- paragraph:2640 para_id:00000A50 style:Normal -->
The man laughed—a bright, awful sound that bounced off the stone walls.

<!-- paragraph:2641 para_id:00000A51 style:Normal -->
“That has never been a concern for me,” he said, voice lilting oddly, too many syllables drawn out like he was savoring them. He didn’t introduce himself. He didn’t need to. His presence filled the room like a poison slowly sinking into the air.

<!-- paragraph:2642 para_id:00000A52 style:Normal -->
"Relax," the man said. "Loosen your joints, little Shade. Sit. Sit."
 Something oily in his voice compelled Davian forward. Against every instinct he had, he sat stiffly on the edge of the bed.

<!-- paragraph:2643 para_id:00000A53 style:Normal -->
The man was already moving, seizing Davian’s arm with a startling speed. His touch was cold and heavy, like being shackled by water.

<!-- paragraph:2644 para_id:00000A54 style:Normal -->
Davian tensed, but didn’t pull away as the man ran his fingers over the black ink twining down his forearm—where Arrek’s absorbed aura had warped the Ink Boon into something new, something strange.

<!-- paragraph:2645 para_id:00000A55 style:Normal -->
“Tsk,” the man said. “Slow. So slow. You’ll be burned out, burned out of the threads like cheap cloth… this fading, ill-gotten, stolen skin of a dead man. But what is dead, or alive, or stolen, or free? Names change, dust blows.” His smile widened. “Makes no difference at the end of the dance.”

<!-- paragraph:2646 para_id:00000A56 style:Normal -->
Davian said nothing. His heart thudded thickly against his ribs. He couldn’t even think of an answer.

<!-- paragraph:2647 para_id:00000A57 style:Normal -->
The man’s sigil flashed—an ugly, alien brand across his face—and pain ignited in Davian’s arm.

<!-- paragraph:2648 para_id:00000A58 style:Normal -->
He bit the inside of his cheek hard enough to taste blood to keep from making a sound.

<!-- paragraph:2649 para_id:00000A59 style:Normal -->
The ink on his arm writhed, darkening, thickening. He could feel it burrowing deeper into him, feel something taking hold.

<!-- paragraph:2650 para_id:00000A5A style:Normal -->
The man straightened, releasing him like discarding a broken toy.

<!-- paragraph:2651 para_id:00000A5B style:Normal -->
“No second chances,” he said, brushing his hands together lightly. “But you—” he chuckled, almost fondly—"you have given me something very interesting. Something unexpected. And I do pay for results, Shade.”

<!-- paragraph:2652 para_id:00000A5C style:Normal -->
Davian fought to steady his breathing. His fingers curled tightly into the bedclothes.

<!-- paragraph:2653 para_id:00000A5D style:Normal -->
“What do you need me for?” he muttered. “If you can do all this yourself?”

<!-- paragraph:2654 para_id:00000A5E style:Normal -->
The man’s head tilted at an unnatural angle. His eyes gleamed too brightly.

<!-- paragraph:2655 para_id:00000A5F style:Normal -->
“He knows not that I come,” he said, voice slipping into a strange cadence, as if quoting something from long ago. “But they know of me. They weave walls against storms and blades. But against a shadow, the blood in the water that begins the hunt? They weave nothing.”

<!-- paragraph:2656 para_id:00000A60 style:Normal -->
He stepped closer, lowering his voice to a murmur thick with glee. “Niall is too clever to be broken by the lash, too strong to be drowned by the tide. Better a rat in the walls. Better the Shade, forgotten and unseen. Better... you.”

<!-- paragraph:2657 para_id:00000A61 style:Normal -->
Davian said nothing. He couldn't.

<!-- paragraph:2658 para_id:00000A62 style:Normal -->
The man’s youthful grin sharpened one last time, full of terrible promise—then, he opened the door and was gone.

<!-- paragraph:2659 para_id:00000A63 style:Normal -->
For a long moment, Davian didn’t move.

<!-- paragraph:2660 para_id:00000A64 style:Normal -->
The torchlight flickered against the stone walls, casting long, distorted shadows. His arm throbbed in time with his heartbeat.

<!-- paragraph:2661 para_id:00000A65 style:Normal -->
Slowly, he forced himself to stand.

<!-- paragraph:2662 para_id:00000A66 style:Normal -->
He crossed the room in three unsteady steps and shoved open the narrow window. Cold night air slapped him across the face, sharp and real, grounding.

<!-- paragraph:2663 para_id:00000A67 style:Normal -->
Davian pressed his forehead to the cool stone.

<!-- paragraph:2664 para_id:00000A68 style:Normal -->
You're not here to save anyone, he reminded himself.

<!-- paragraph:2665 para_id:00000A69 style:Normal -->
The words tasted bitter.

<!-- paragraph:2666 para_id:00000A6A style:Normal -->
He closed his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:2667 para_id:00000A6B style:Normal -->
Tomorrow he would put on the mask again. The Shade. The rat. The weapon.

<!-- paragraph:2668 para_id:00000A6C style:Normal -->
But for tonight, he stayed there, breathing in the cold, blood still trickling down his arm where the ink had carved itself deeper.

<!-- paragraph:2671 para_id:00000A6F style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 17

<!-- paragraph:2672 para_id:00000A70 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 17

<!-- paragraph:2673 para_id:00000A71 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2674 para_id:00000A72 style:Normal -->
Maya heard someone calling her name.

<!-- paragraph:2675 para_id:00000A73 style:Normal -->
It sounded distant, muffled—like she was deep underwater and the sound barely reached her. She fought her way toward it, limbs heavy, the world around her an endless ocean of inky blackness.

<!-- paragraph:2676 para_id:00000A74 style:Normal -->
Then—like breaking the surface—her eyes snapped open.

<!-- paragraph:2677 para_id:00000A75 style:Normal -->
Niall was crouched over her, hands gripping her shoulders, his face pale and drawn with terror. He shook her again, sharply this time.

<!-- paragraph:2678 para_id:00000A76 style:Normal -->
"Maya! Maya!"

<!-- paragraph:2679 para_id:00000A77 style:Normal -->
She blinked up at him, disoriented. Her body felt heavy, uncooperative, like she’d been drained dry.

<!-- paragraph:2680 para_id:00000A78 style:Normal -->
"I..." Her voice rasped, thin and hoarse. "I'm okay. Just... tired."

<!-- paragraph:2681 para_id:00000A79 style:Normal -->
Niall exhaled a shuddering breath, clutching her for one second longer before pulling back. His hands were trembling.

<!-- paragraph:2682 para_id:00000A7A style:Normal -->
He held up the codex in front of her—its surface faintly flickering with zän—and she caught her own name, scrawled across the cover in curling script. No—Alara Rezin.

<!-- paragraph:2683 para_id:00000A7B style:Normal -->
Erynn edged forward hesitantly, wringing her hands. "I'm sorry," she whispered. "I— I didn’t know it would—"

<!-- paragraph:2684 para_id:00000A7C style:Normal -->
"Get out," Niall said, his voice a low snarl.

<!-- paragraph:2685 para_id:00000A7D style:Normal -->
The air crackled.

<!-- paragraph:2686 para_id:00000A7E style:Normal -->
Small arcs of lightning danced at the corners of his eyes, flickering and snapping in the tense, charged silence. The other guards, the healer—everyone edged instinctively backward from the raw pulse of his wrath.

<!-- paragraph:2687 para_id:00000A7F style:Normal -->
Erynn stumbled over herself in retreat, vanishing through the door without another word.

<!-- paragraph:2688 para_id:00000A80 style:Normal -->
For a moment, Niall stood frozen, rigid—then something in him broke.

<!-- paragraph:2689 para_id:00000A81 style:Normal -->
The storm drained away from his expression, leaving behind something hollow and exhausted. His eyes dimmed into a flat, cold darkness as he backed away and collapsed into a chair like a puppet whose strings had been cut.

<!-- paragraph:2690 para_id:00000A82 style:Normal -->
"Check her," he said hoarsely to the healer, barely moving.

<!-- paragraph:2691 para_id:00000A83 style:Normal -->
The healer rushed to Maya’s side, but Maya’s gaze stayed fixed on Niall.

<!-- paragraph:2692 para_id:00000A84 style:Normal -->
He looked… shrunk. Like something vast and vital inside him had cracked open, and all the fire and fury had bled out, leaving nothing but a brittle shell.

<!-- paragraph:2693 para_id:00000A85 style:Normal -->
Despite everything—the rage, the terror, the sudden collapse—he was still holding the codex. His fingers cradled it almost reverently, like it was something sacred. One of the metal corners was dented now from where she had dropped it. But he didn’t seem to notice.

<!-- paragraph:2694 para_id:00000A86 style:Normal -->
Maya swallowed, her throat thick. She thought about Selwin—his bright, desperate magic flaring to life—and about what it must feel like for Niall. Watching both of his children slip beyond his grasp in the span of a few hours.

<!-- paragraph:2695 para_id:00000A87 style:Normal -->
"I’m sorry," she whispered.

<!-- paragraph:2696 para_id:00000A88 style:Normal -->
Niall opened his eyes slowly, the exhaustion in them almost painful to look at. He shook his head, a sharp, weary motion.

<!-- paragraph:2697 para_id:00000A89 style:Normal -->
"Don't," he said quietly. "Not right now."

<!-- paragraph:2698 para_id:00000A8A style:Normal -->
Then he turned his head slightly and barked toward the doorway without getting up. "Runner. Get Alden."

<!-- paragraph:2699 para_id:00000A8B style:Normal -->
A guard snapped to attention and bolted from the room.

<!-- paragraph:2700 para_id:00000A8C style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t even watch him go. He just slumped back further into the chair, his arms loosely wrapped around the battered codex, eyes closed, as if the weight of it—and everything inside it—was the only thing tethering him upright.

<!-- paragraph:2701 para_id:00000A8D style:Normal -->
The healer finished her scan and gave Maya a terse nod, stepping away.

<!-- paragraph:2702 para_id:00000A8E style:Normal -->
Maya stayed where she was, staring at her father across the dim room.

<!-- paragraph:2703 para_id:00000A8F style:Normal -->
And for the first time in a long time, she wasn’t sure if the distance between them could ever be crossed.

<!-- paragraph:2704 para_id:00000A90 style:Normal -->
When the healer finished, Niall waited only a moment before straightening to his full height. The cold had returned to his face—a brittle, dangerous calm. He turned toward the guards, voice low but sharp as a blade.

<!-- paragraph:2705 para_id:00000A91 style:Normal -->
"Out."

<!-- paragraph:2706 para_id:00000A92 style:Normal -->
No one hesitated.

<!-- paragraph:2707 para_id:00000A93 style:Normal -->
Niall remained standing over Maya, his shadow cutting across the bed.

<!-- paragraph:2708 para_id:00000A94 style:Normal -->
"Tell me," he said. "How you found this. And what you saw."

<!-- paragraph:2709 para_id:00000A95 style:Normal -->
Maya swallowed against the dryness in her throat. She sat up a little straighter, the aches and fatigue of channeling pushed to the side.

<!-- paragraph:2710 para_id:00000A96 style:Normal -->
"I found it in the tunnels under the citadel," she said carefully, omitting the dream that had drawn her there. "Hidden in a hollow in the stone."

<!-- paragraph:2711 para_id:00000A97 style:Normal -->
She hesitated, glancing toward the battered book cradled still in Niall's arm.

<!-- paragraph:2712 para_id:00000A98 style:Normal -->
"I stepped into a memory," she said. "It showed me… you. You proposed to her, in the gardens. But at the end—"

<!-- paragraph:2713 para_id:00000A99 style:Normal -->
She trailed off, feeling a chill gather around them.

<!-- paragraph:2714 para_id:00000A9A style:Normal -->
"At the end," she forced herself to say, "your father appeared. He spoke to her—or at least I thought he was speaking to her. But then... he spoke to me. He saw me. Through her."

<!-- paragraph:2715 para_id:00000A9B style:Normal -->
Niall’s entire body went still. Like a hunting cat poised at the moment before the kill.

<!-- paragraph:2716 para_id:00000A9C style:Normal -->
His voice, when it came, was flat and lethal.

<!-- paragraph:2717 para_id:00000A9D style:Normal -->
"That memory," he said, "is false."

<!-- paragraph:2718 para_id:00000A9E style:Normal -->
Maya blinked.

<!-- paragraph:2719 para_id:00000A9F style:Normal -->
"My father was not there that night."

<!-- paragraph:2720 para_id:00000AA0 style:Normal -->
Before she could say anything else, there was a knock at the door. It opened without waiting for permission—only one man would be that bold—and Alden stepped inside, sharp eyes immediately taking in the tension that coiled like a storm between Niall and Maya.

<!-- paragraph:2721 para_id:00000AA1 style:Normal -->
He inclined his head slightly. "You summoned me, sir?"

<!-- paragraph:2722 para_id:00000AA2 style:Normal -->
Niall didn't move. He just held up the codex.

<!-- paragraph:2723 para_id:00000AA3 style:Normal -->
Alden looked at it, then at Maya, then back at Niall. His posture eased slightly, but there was wariness in his gaze.

<!-- paragraph:2724 para_id:00000AA4 style:Normal -->
"How can I assist?"

<!-- paragraph:2725 para_id:00000AA5 style:Normal -->
Maya found her voice. "If a memory was altered... inserted into a codex somehow... is that even possible?"

<!-- paragraph:2726 para_id:00000AA6 style:Normal -->
Alden’s brows drew together. "In theory? No. Codices are anchored through the aura sequence of the original writer. Memories embedded like that are immutable unless the anchoring magic is corrupted." He stepped closer, palms up. "May I?"

<!-- paragraph:2727 para_id:00000AA7 style:Normal -->
Niall held the codex a moment longer, as if weighing some internal decision. Then, slowly, he handed it over.

<!-- paragraph:2728 para_id:00000AA8 style:Normal -->
Alden turned it carefully in his hands, flipping to the first page—and immediately frowned.

<!-- paragraph:2729 para_id:00000AA9 style:Normal -->
