"This is strange," he said. He ran a gloved fingertip lightly along the inside cover. "The key incantation... it's far longer than necessary. And this—" he tapped the edge of the scrawled script, "—is in a different hand than the notes inside."

<!-- paragraph:2730 para_id:00000AAA style:Normal -->
Maya leaned forward, her heart hammering faster.

<!-- paragraph:2731 para_id:00000AAB style:Normal -->
"What does that mean?" she asked.

<!-- paragraph:2732 para_id:00000AAC style:Normal -->
Alden turned another page, frowning deeper. His hand hovered, hesitant, over a faint shimmer in the ink—barely visible, unless you knew how to look for it.

<!-- paragraph:2733 para_id:00000AAD style:Normal -->
"This key incantation..." he murmured. "It's not just long. It's wrong."

<!-- paragraph:2734 para_id:00000AAE style:Normal -->
Maya leaned closer. "Wrong how?"

<!-- paragraph:2735 para_id:00000AAF style:Normal -->
Alden traced a zä with his fingertip, careful not to touch it directly. "The structure’s off. It's layered with something else—an overlay that doesn’t belong. Almost like it’s been corrupted, stitched together with threads that shouldn't coexist."

<!-- paragraph:2736 para_id:00000AB0 style:Normal -->
He looked up at Niall, grim. "Sir, this isn’t just an anchor for memories. Whoever wrote this—they embedded a bridge."

<!-- paragraph:2737 para_id:00000AB1 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. "A bridge?"

<!-- paragraph:2738 para_id:00000AB2 style:Normal -->
"A way to speak through the codex," Alden said, voice tight. "In any memory where they’re represented. If you accessed the memory, and they were present—or maybe the memory was in a location that they knew very well—they could hijack the sequence and interact with you."

<!-- paragraph:2739 para_id:00000AB3 style:Normal -->
He shook his head. "It shouldn’t be possible. The laws that bind memory-magic are supposed to prevent this."

<!-- paragraph:2740 para_id:00000AB4 style:Normal -->
Niall’s face was white with fury.

<!-- paragraph:2741 para_id:00000AB5 style:Normal -->
"Impossible," he said. His voice was low, shaking with contained violence. "He’s dead."

<!-- paragraph:2742 para_id:00000AB6 style:Normal -->
Maya flinched at the rawness in it. She found her voice, quieter. "How do you know?"

<!-- paragraph:2743 para_id:00000AB7 style:Normal -->
Niall's gaze snapped to her, blazing.

<!-- paragraph:2744 para_id:00000AB8 style:Normal -->
"Because I killed him."

<!-- paragraph:2745 para_id:00000AB9 style:Normal -->
The words cracked across the room like a whip.

<!-- paragraph:2746 para_id:00000ABA style:Normal -->
"I burned his rotten body to ash," Niall said. His hands were fists at his sides, trembling. "Down to the core. I watched it happen."

<!-- paragraph:2747 para_id:00000ABB style:Normal -->
Maya’s mouth was dry. "Then how—"

<!-- paragraph:2748 para_id:00000ABC style:Normal -->
Alden spoke before she could finish. His voice was careful, deliberate, like trying to calm a cornered animal.

<!-- paragraph:2749 para_id:00000ABD style:Normal -->
"There might be another explanation," he said. "Codices don’t just store memories. They anchor auras. Like the Registry.. It’s possible..." He hesitated. "Possible that this bridge spell didn’t summon Elric’s consciousness. It only granted the appearance of sentience to the imprint he left behind."

<!-- paragraph:2750 para_id:00000ABE style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t look convinced. He stared at the codex like it was something vile.

<!-- paragraph:2751 para_id:00000ABF style:Normal -->
Maya, swallowing, told him the rest. She described the memory—the gardens, the ball, the flicker of nervous excitement in his younger self—and then, carefully, she described the moment when everything had gone wrong. When Elric had turned, and she had seen the threads—vibrant and wrong, pulsing in the frozen void.

<!-- paragraph:2752 para_id:00000AC0 style:Normal -->
Niall’s face closed down immediately, every muscle locking.

<!-- paragraph:2753 para_id:00000AC1 style:Normal -->
"You saw the strands?" he asked. His voice was a dead thing.

<!-- paragraph:2754 para_id:00000AC2 style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated. Then nodded.

<!-- paragraph:2755 para_id:00000AC3 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat, Niall said nothing. Then he stepped back, running a hand over his face like he needed to wipe the moment away.

<!-- paragraph:2756 para_id:00000AC4 style:Normal -->
"You are never to look there again," he said. His voice was flat, chilling. "Do you understand me?"

<!-- paragraph:2757 para_id:00000AC5 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth—but there was something so fierce, so brittle in his expression that she found herself just nodding, small and silent.

<!-- paragraph:2758 para_id:00000AC6 style:Normal -->
Niall turned away sharply, pacing once, his hands flexing at his sides.

<!-- paragraph:2759 para_id:00000AC7 style:Normal -->
A heavy silence hung in the room after Niall turned away. Maya could still feel the aftershock of his command thrumming through the space, like the tail end of an earthquake. Her pulse hadn’t slowed. Her limbs still trembled, not with fear—but with something close.

<!-- paragraph:2760 para_id:00000AC8 style:Normal -->
She breathed slowly through her nose, trying to stay grounded. Calm. Useful.

<!-- paragraph:2761 para_id:00000AC9 style:Normal -->
Alden was the one to break the quiet, stepping forward and gently placing the codex on the table with a reverence that mirrored Niall’s earlier grip.<!-- comment-start:107 -->

<!-- paragraph:2762 para_id:00000ACA style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-end:107 --><!-- comment-ref:107 -->“We should consider how this was done,” he said carefully, not quite meeting Niall’s gaze. “Whoever wrote that overlay into the key incantation didn’t do it recently. The ink was stabilized through a very old method. Ritual-layered, likely performed during the initial anchoring. Which would mean—”

<!-- paragraph:2763 para_id:00000ACB style:Normal -->
“Elric,” Niall said flatly.

<!-- paragraph:2764 para_id:00000ACC style:Normal -->
Alden inclined his head once. “Almost certainly.”

<!-- paragraph:2765 para_id:00000ACD style:Normal -->
Maya glanced between them. “But how? The book was buried. Hidden. If he planted it there himself—”

<!-- paragraph:2766 para_id:00000ACE style:Normal -->
“He didn’t need to,” Niall snapped. “He could’ve given it to one of his acolytes. One of the scholars he kept around who thought he was some visionary. He had a network, Maya. Dozens of people who hung on his every word.”

<!-- paragraph:2767 para_id:00000ACF style:Normal -->
“Still,” Alden said, “it’s not just a question of how he wrote it. It’s why. A bridge like that… it wasn’t meant to store a fragment of himself. It was meant to reach someone. To find someone.”

<!-- paragraph:2768 para_id:00000AD0 style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression darkened further. “It found my daughter.”

<!-- paragraph:2769 para_id:00000AD1 style:Normal -->
Maya straightened at that. “Because I activated it.”

<!-- paragraph:2770 para_id:00000AD2 style:Normal -->
“Exactly,” he said, eyes cutting to hers. “You should never have gone into those tunnels. Never picked up something you didn’t understand.”

<!-- paragraph:2771 para_id:00000AD3 style:Normal -->
“I was trying to understand,” Maya said sharply, sitting up straighter. “I wanted to understand you. What you lost. What you loved.”

<!-- paragraph:2772 para_id:00000AD4 style:Normal -->
His face twisted, something breaking beneath the surface. “And instead you invited a ghost into your mind.”

<!-- paragraph:2773 para_id:00000AD5 style:Normal -->
Alden stepped in before Maya could fire back. “It may not be that literal, sir. A ghost would imply identity and volition. This might’ve been more like... a contingency. A trap left long ago, waiting for a specific kind of resonance.”

<!-- paragraph:2774 para_id:00000AD6 style:Normal -->
Maya looked to him, grateful for the moment of rationality. “But it worked. It spoke to me. It knew me.”

<!-- paragraph:2775 para_id:00000AD7 style:Normal -->
Niall exhaled sharply, pacing. His hand scrubbed through his hair, leaving it wild and disordered. “It’s all falling apart,” he muttered. “The threads, the seals, the protections—none of it is holding anymore.”

<!-- paragraph:2776 para_id:00000AD8 style:Normal -->
Alden folded his arms. “You’re assuming too much from too little.”

<!-- paragraph:2777 para_id:00000AD9 style:Normal -->
Niall turned on him, eyes flashing. “Don’t expect me to give a charitable interpretation to the intelligence of your comments just because they’re vague.”

<!-- paragraph:2778 para_id:00000ADA style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:2779 para_id:00000ADB style:Normal -->
Alden just blinked slowly. “You’re lashing out, sir.”

<!-- paragraph:2780 para_id:00000ADC style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t respond. He looked suddenly old—more than tired, more than strained. A man standing at the edge of a cliff and realizing the ground beneath him was already cracked.

<!-- paragraph:2781 para_id:00000ADD style:Normal -->
Then, without another word, he crossed the room, seized the codex from the table, and left.

<!-- paragraph:2782 para_id:00000ADE style:Normal -->
The door clicked shut behind him.

<!-- paragraph:2783 para_id:00000ADF style:Normal -->
Maya let the silence settle again. The weight of everything—the codex, the corrupted memory, the vision of Elric—pressed down like stone on her chest.

<!-- paragraph:2784 para_id:00000AE0 style:Normal -->
“I didn’t mean for that to happen,” she said softly.

<!-- paragraph:2785 para_id:00000AE1 style:Normal -->
“I know,” Alden replied.

<!-- paragraph:2786 para_id:00000AE2 style:Normal -->
She rubbed her face. “And now Selwin’s manifested, and nearly died. And Niall is...” She trailed off, unable to even define the shape of his unraveling. “We can’t keep pretending everything’s fine.”

<!-- paragraph:2787 para_id:00000AE3 style:Normal -->
“No,” Alden said quietly. “We can’t.”

<!-- paragraph:2788 para_id:00000AE4 style:Normal -->
He didn’t say anything else at first. Just stood with his arms loosely folded, watching the closed door Niall had disappeared through.

<!-- paragraph:2789 para_id:00000AE5 style:Normal -->
Then he turned to her. “We need to talk about Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:2790 para_id:00000AE6 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, caught off guard. “What about him?”

<!-- paragraph:2791 para_id:00000AE7 style:Normal -->
“He can’t be locked away,” Alden said. “He’s too powerful. Too aware. It would backfire. And your father—he’s... compromised. At best.”

<!-- paragraph:2792 para_id:00000AE8 style:Normal -->
“I can take him,” Maya said immediately.

<!-- paragraph:2793 para_id:00000AE9 style:Normal -->
But Alden was already shaking his head. “And put the two most volatile magical forces in the fortress together? With this—” he gestured vaguely toward the codex, “—barely behind you?”

<!-- paragraph:2794 para_id:00000AEA style:Normal -->
Maya bristled. “I can handle it.”

<!-- paragraph:2795 para_id:00000AEB style:Normal -->
“I know,” he said. “But you shouldn’t have to.”

<!-- paragraph:2796 para_id:00000AEC style:Normal -->
She opened her mouth. Then closed it again.

<!-- paragraph:2797 para_id:00000AED style:Normal -->
Because that was the truth, wasn’t it? She shouldn’t have to. But she did. Because no one else would.

<!-- paragraph:2798 para_id:00000AEE style:Normal -->
“Where does that leave us?” she asked.

<!-- paragraph:2799 para_id:00000AEF style:Normal -->
Alden paced a slow circle, brow furrowed in thought. “We need somewhere structured. Supervised. Somewhere Selwin can experiment, but with containment parameters. He needs to feel useful, not dangerous. Protected, but not smothered.”

<!-- paragraph:2800 para_id:00000AF0 style:Normal -->
She looked at him. “You have something in mind.”

<!-- paragraph:2801 para_id:00000AF1 style:Normal -->
He nodded. “The old vault training chamber beneath the reserve wing. It was decommissioned when the outer defenses went up—has shielding, containment seals. I can repurpose it into something that feels less like exile and more like… responsibility.”

<!-- paragraph:2802 para_id:00000AF2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression darkened. “We’re not locking a child underground.”

<!-- paragraph:2803 para_id:00000AF3 style:Normal -->
Alden lifted a brow. “We’d be giving him space. Structure. Safety.”

<!-- paragraph:2804 para_id:00000AF4 style:Normal -->
“He’s not a threat,” she said sharply. “He’s a scared kid who lost control once.”

<!-- paragraph:2805 para_id:00000AF5 style:Normal -->
“That one time nearly killed him, and you, and destroyed an entire courtyard,” Alden said. “We still don’t understand what his magic does.”

<!-- paragraph:2806 para_id:00000AF6 style:Normal -->
“Which is exactly why he shouldn’t be alone.” She exhaled, steadying herself. “Here’s what we’re going to do: he trains down there during the day. Structured, supervised. Then he comes up in the evenings—he stays with me and my father. He sleeps in his own bed. No cells. No exile. We keep him close.”

<!-- paragraph:2807 para_id:00000AF7 style:Normal -->
Alden didn’t respond at first. Then, slowly, “That’s risky. We don’t know what could set him off again.”

<!-- paragraph:2808 para_id:00000AF8 style:Normal -->
“I’m not going to punish him for being scared,” she said, more quietly now. “I always hoped my strength would mean Selwin wouldn’t have to go through what I did. I won’t let his one mistake mean he ends up with something worse.”

<!-- paragraph:2809 para_id:00000AF9 style:Normal -->
He looked at her, long and level. “I don’t agree. But it’s your decision. And if it goes wrong, you’ll have to live with that.”

<!-- paragraph:2810 para_id:00000AFA style:Normal -->
She nodded once. “I’d have to live with the other choice too.”

<!-- paragraph:2811 para_id:00000AFB style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled and rubbed her temple. “I’ll have to bring my father in on this.”

<!-- paragraph:2812 para_id:00000AFC style:Normal -->
Alden’s expression didn’t change. “I don’t think now is the best time.”

<!-- paragraph:2813 para_id:00000AFD style:Normal -->
“It never is,” she snapped, more bitterly than she meant to. “It’s never the right time for him to really see what’s happening.”

<!-- paragraph:2814 para_id:00000AFE style:Normal -->
He didn’t argue. He didn’t have to.

<!-- paragraph:2815 para_id:00000AFF style:Normal -->
Maya turned, gathering herself. “I’ll take Selwin’s guards and Erynn. If you can begin preparations on the chamber…”

<!-- paragraph:2816 para_id:00000B00 style:Normal -->
He inclined his head. “Of course.”

<!-- paragraph:2817 para_id:00000B01 style:Normal -->
Outside, her guards were waiting. Erynn hovered at the edge of the hallway, wringing her hands. “Maya, I—about earlier, I didn’t know—”

<!-- paragraph:2818 para_id:00000B02 style:Normal -->
Maya held up a hand. “I know. But we can talk about that later.”

<!-- paragraph:2819 para_id:00000B03 style:Normal -->
Erynn flinched slightly, but nodded.

<!-- paragraph:2820 para_id:00000B04 style:Normal -->
Maya’s voice lowered, more to herself than to anyone else. “I’ve been trying to be calm. Reasonable. Careful. But he never listens when I’m any of those things.”

<!-- paragraph:2821 para_id:00000B05 style:Normal -->
She turned on her heel and strode down the hall.

<!-- paragraph:2822 para_id:00000B06 style:Normal -->
Toward the one place she knew he would be.

<!-- paragraph:2823 para_id:00000B07 style:Normal -->
The corridor leading to the meditation chamber was silent. There were no guards posted—there never were. This was Niall’s sanctum, carved high into the Rezin spire itself. At the chamber’s apex, a circular iris in the stone roof stood open, revealing a patch of dusky twilight sky, just starting to shift from cobalt into deep night.

<!-- paragraph:2824 para_id:00000B08 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped through the threshold and shut the door behind her.

<!-- paragraph:2825 para_id:00000B09 style:Normal -->
She had come braced for a fight. She had imagined fury, denial, cold deflection. She had rehearsed the words she would throw back when he tried to silence her.

<!-- paragraph:2826 para_id:00000B0A style:Normal -->
But Niall wasn’t standing.

<!-- paragraph:2827 para_id:00000B0B style:Normal -->
He was seated in the center of the chamber, legs folded beneath him, posture straight but unmoving. The open sky lit him in a soft halo—faint silver on his shoulders, fading to shadow.

<!-- paragraph:2828 para_id:00000B0C style:Normal -->
As she entered, he opened his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:2829 para_id:00000B0D style:Normal -->
No fury. No ice. Just exhaustion. Not the sharp-edged kind she was used to, but something weightier. Like all the scaffolding of his strength had quietly collapsed and he hadn’t even bothered to get out from under it.

<!-- paragraph:2830 para_id:00000B0E style:Normal -->
Maya froze in the doorway, her anger half-choked by the quiet.

<!-- paragraph:2831 para_id:00000B0F style:Normal -->
Maya walked forward slowly, the hush of her footsteps swallowed by the vastness of the chamber. She lowered herself to the floor across from him, legs folding beneath her, the stone cool through the fabric of her clothes. The open iris above cast pale light on both of them now—twilight spilling soft and blue across Niall’s shoulders, glinting faintly on the sigil at Maya’s temple.

<!-- paragraph:2832 para_id:00000B10 style:Normal -->
They sat in silence.

<!-- paragraph:2833 para_id:00000B11 style:Normal -->
The wind murmured faintly above them.

<!-- paragraph:2834 para_id:00000B12 style:Normal -->
Finally, Niall’s gaze rose toward the sliver of sky, his voice low and strange.

<!-- paragraph:2835 para_id:00000B13 style:Normal -->
“My mind can’t see the paths anymore, Maya. My thoughts used to soar to the heights—beyond the clouds, to the latticework where the sky breathes. Now they’re locked below. Everything is murk and darkness and pressure.”

<!-- paragraph:2836 para_id:00000B14 style:Normal -->
Maya studied him, uncertain what she was hearing. His voice sounded thin—less like Niall and more like a ghost that remembered him.

<!-- paragraph:2837 para_id:00000B15 style:Normal -->
“You don’t have to see the whole path,” she said softly. “You just have to walk it. Selwin and I—we still need you to be the man I know you can be.”

<!-- paragraph:2838 para_id:00000B16 style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t respond at first. When he did, his eyes didn’t leave the sky.

<!-- paragraph:2839 para_id:00000B17 style:Normal -->
“When you were born,” he said quietly, “I held you in my arms and felt something come alive in me. Something wild. I remember thinking—I would burn the world to make a place for you.”

<!-- paragraph:2840 para_id:00000B18 style:Normal -->
Maya flinched. Anger rose in her throat before she could stop it.

<!-- paragraph:2841 para_id:00000B19 style:Normal -->
“I don’t want you to burn it,” she said, sharper than she intended. “I want you to make it better. That’s what you used to want, too. Isn’t it?”

<!-- paragraph:2842 para_id:00000B1A style:Normal -->
Niall finally looked at her. The grief in his eyes didn’t soften; if anything, it settled deeper. But there was a flicker of something else now, too—something almost like shame.

<!-- paragraph:2843 para_id:00000B1B style:Normal -->
Maya stood up slowly, brushing her hands against her knees.

<!-- paragraph:2844 para_id:00000B1C style:Normal -->
“I wish I didn’t have to get angry for you to see me,” she said, voice trembling with the effort to keep it steady. “I wish I didn’t have to push or snap just to be heard. I don’t want to be the problem. I want to be part of the solution.”

<!-- paragraph:2845 para_id:00000B1D style:Normal -->
She turned toward the door, then paused.

<!-- paragraph:2846 para_id:00000B1E style:Normal -->
“We’re going to keep Selwin in the old training vault,” she said without turning around. “During the day. But in the evenings—he’s with us. You and me. You’ll need to be present.”

<!-- paragraph:2847 para_id:00000B1F style:Normal -->
There was a long silence behind her.

<!-- paragraph:2848 para_id:00000B20 style:Normal -->
When she finally glanced back, Niall was standing, his expression calm, professional—mask firmly in place.

<!-- paragraph:2849 para_id:00000B21 style:Normal -->
“Of course,” he said. “Thank you for preparing this plan.”

<!-- paragraph:2850 para_id:00000B22 style:Normal -->
Maya returned to her chambers in silence. Erynn opened her mouth to speak, but Maya just shook her head. “Not tonight,” she said, quietly, and Erynn stepped back with a nod.

<!-- paragraph:2851 para_id:00000B23 style:Normal -->
Alone, she stripped off her boots and cloak and collapsed into bed, the blankets heavy and cold. Her body ached with the weight of the day. But when she closed her eyes, there was no peace—only the shimmer of threads behind her eyelids, vast and pulsing. Past and future tugged at her mind like a tide she couldn’t resist, unraveling sleep before it could take hold.

<!-- paragraph:2852 para_id:00000B24 style:Normal -->
Maya wasn’t sure if she’d slept at all. Her eyes burned, and her body ached with a restless fatigue that didn’t feel like tiredness so much as unraveling. Moments flickered behind her eyelids whenever she blinked—echoes of yesterday, of flame, of cold, of her father's gaze. When she focused too long, her thoughts slipped sideways, replaying old scenes or projecting catastrophic futures she didn’t remember imagining.

<!-- paragraph:2853 para_id:00000B25 style:Normal -->
She shook her head, trying to scatter the thoughts like dust.

<!-- paragraph:2854 para_id:00000B26 style:Normal -->
“Maya,” Erynn’s voice cut through the haze, gentle but brisk. “It’s morning.”

<!-- paragraph:2855 para_id:00000B27 style:Normal -->
Was it?

<!-- paragraph:2856 para_id:00000B28 style:Normal -->
The curtains had been drawn back, letting in the muted blue-gray light of early dawn. Erynn was already bustling about, helping her into her tabard and leggings. As the fabric settled on her shoulders, Maya realized her limbs moved before her mind caught up.

<!-- paragraph:2857 para_id:00000B29 style:Normal -->
“You’ve got an early one today,” Erynn said, fastening the clasps with practiced efficiency. “Supervisors’ daily update—construction, provisioning, transit logistics. Exciting things.”

<!-- paragraph:2858 para_id:00000B2A style:Normal -->
Maya managed a weak noise of agreement. Erynn kept talking—light things, comments about mismatched tiles in the west hallway or how one of the younger guards kept losing their belt. It helped. A little.

<!-- paragraph:2859 para_id:00000B2B style:Normal -->
She kept her gaze ahead as they walked through the corridor, the echo of their footsteps strangely grounding. The present. Stay in the present.

<!-- paragraph:2860 para_id:00000B2C style:Normal -->
As they neared the chamber for the meeting, a voice reached her ears—wry, a little too loud, unmistakably Arrek’s.

<!-- paragraph:2861 para_id:00000B2D style:Normal -->
“I didn’t realize becoming a supervisor meant waking up an hour earlier every day. Is that in the fine print?”

<!-- paragraph:2862 para_id:00000B2E style:Normal -->
Her stomach tightened. She had almost forgotten. Almost.

<!-- paragraph:2863 para_id:00000B2F style:Normal -->
She stepped through the doorway.

<!-- paragraph:2864 para_id:00000B30 style:Normal -->
Arrek broke off mid-sentence, turning from Marek to face her. His posture straightened slightly, as if caught off guard—but it wasn’t his stance that struck her. It was the way he looked at her. Direct. Intense. Like nothing else in the room mattered.

<!-- paragraph:2865 para_id:00000B31 style:Normal -->
Her gaze flicked over him automatically. His sleeves were rolled just enough to show the ink curling along his forearms, the designs somehow unfamiliar—had they always looked like that? There was bruising around his elbows and across his forearms, and it wasn’t hard to imagine why. Sparring. Saving Selwin.

<!-- paragraph:2866 para_id:00000B32 style:Normal -->
The sharp lines of tension across his frame, the darkness of his eyes, the sheer gravity he seemed to carry—

<!-- paragraph:2867 para_id:00000B33 style:Normal -->
He looked like the version of him she couldn’t stop remembering. The one in the courtyard, bare-chested, breathing hard, bloodied and alive.

<!-- paragraph:2868 para_id:00000B34 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:108 -->Erynn coughed.<!-- comment-end:108 --><!-- comment-ref:108 -->

<!-- paragraph:2869 para_id:00000B35 style:Normal -->
Maya startled and realized with cold horror that they had been staring at each other. Not just a glance—staring.

<!-- paragraph:2870 para_id:00000B36 style:Normal -->
Every supervisor in the room was looking at her.

<!-- paragraph:2871 para_id:00000B37 style:Normal -->
Recover. Now.

<!-- paragraph:2872 para_id:00000B38 style:Normal -->
“I… had forgotten a new supervisor would be joining us today,” she said quickly, sweeping her gaze over the others. “I trust you’ve all had a chance to introduce yourselves properly?”

<!-- paragraph:2873 para_id:00000B39 style:Normal -->
There were a few murmurs of assent. Arrek didn’t say anything. He didn’t look away.

<!-- paragraph:2874 para_id:00000B3A style:Normal -->
She took her seat. Her heart had no interest in calming.

<!-- paragraph:2875 para_id:00000B3B style:Normal -->
Maya sat straighter than she needed to, arms folded as Marek outlined the division of labor between the two newly formed teams. Her attention, however, kept straying.

<!-- paragraph:2876 para_id:00000B3C style:Normal -->
Arrek was staring at her.

<!-- paragraph:2877 para_id:00000B3D style:Normal -->
Not constantly—but frequently enough that it became impossible to ignore. Every time she looked away and glanced back, his gaze was on her again. It wasn’t neutral, either. It felt heated. Frustrated. Like he was trying to sear a message into her skull without saying a word.

<!-- paragraph:2878 para_id:00000B3E style:Normal -->
He wasn't disruptive. In fact, his section of the presentation—shared with Marek—was completely adequate. Thorough, even. He’d clearly prepared. While Marek’s team would cover the zä lattice on the inner chamber walls, Arrek’s team would reinforce the enchantments along the vault sphere’s outer shell. His notes were organized, his tone even. But still—he was brimming with intensity. She could feel it pressing into her skin like a second atmosphere.

<!-- paragraph:2879 para_id:00000B3F style:Normal -->
The meeting wrapped up with the usual slow shuffle of bodies rising, chairs scraping, murmurs rising like mist. Maya had stood and was beginning to gather her things when she caught a movement from the side of her vision—Marek, elbowing Arrek sharply in the ribs.

<!-- paragraph:2880 para_id:00000B40 style:Normal -->
A beat later, Arrek strode over.

<!-- paragraph:2881 para_id:00000B41 style:Normal -->
“Thank you,” he said, his voice flat as slate. “For the promotion.”

<!-- paragraph:2882 para_id:00000B42 style:Normal -->
Maya arched an eyebrow, adjusting a folded parchment in her hands. “You don’t sound particularly thankful.”

<!-- paragraph:2883 para_id:00000B43 style:Normal -->
His eyes burned into hers, low and unwavering. “I told you I didn’t want a reward.”

<!-- paragraph:2884 para_id:00000B44 style:Normal -->
“Then why are you even here?” she shot back. “If you don’t care about any of this, why take the job at all?”

<!-- paragraph:2885 para_id:00000B45 style:Normal -->
His jaw tensed. “I regret it.”

<!-- paragraph:2886 para_id:00000B46 style:Normal -->
That landed harder than she expected. For a moment, her breath caught. “Then leave. No one’s stopping you.”

<!-- paragraph:2887 para_id:00000B47 style:Normal -->
He didn’t move. “I know.”

<!-- paragraph:2888 para_id:00000B48 style:Normal -->
They stared at each other—something sharp and unspoken coiling between them, too tangled to name. She could feel it in the way his eyes held hers, like he was trying to say something without actually giving her the words.

<!-- paragraph:2889 para_id:00000B49 style:Normal -->
Erynn, sensing the heat rising, stepped in. “Maya, maybe you should get some rest. After everything that happened.”

<!-- paragraph:2890 para_id:00000B4A style:Normal -->
Arrek’s head snapped toward he<!-- comment-start:109 --><!-- comment-start:110 -->r. “What happened?”<!-- comment-end:109 --><!-- comment-ref:109 --><!-- comment-end:110 --><!-- comment-ref:110 -->

<!-- paragraph:2891 para_id:00000B4B style:Normal -->
“It’s nothing,” Maya said, voice clipped. “Why would you care?”

<!-- paragraph:2892 para_id:00000B4C style:Normal -->
He gave a short, humorless shrug. “You’re my employer. It’s in my best interest to keep you alive long enough to pay me.”

<!-- paragraph:2893 para_id:00000B4D style:Normal -->
Maya’s eyes narrowed. “Right. Of course. Everything’s a joke to you.”

<!-- paragraph:2894 para_id:00000B4E style:Normal -->
His face shifted—just slightly. The mask cracked.

<!-- paragraph:2895 para_id:00000B4F style:Normal -->
“No,” he said. “This isn’t a joke to me.”

<!-- paragraph:2896 para_id:00000B50 style:Normal -->
She took a step forward, frustration flaring. “You did the same thing when you saved Selwin. You brushed it off like it meant nothing. Just a clever line and a quick exit. Why even bother if you didn’t care?”

<!-- paragraph:2897 para_id:00000B51 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s expression tightened. “Because he’s a child. He’s too young to be implicated in the corruption of your Clades.”

<!-- paragraph:2898 para_id:00000B52 style:Normal -->
“Oh, is that what this is?” she said, voice sharpening. “We’re all just so rotten and vile to you? Is that the issue? The horrible, evil High Clades, oppressing the helpless masses—and yet here you are, taking our jobs when the coin’s good enough.”

<!-- paragraph:2899 para_id:00000B53 style:Normal -->
That hit. His jaw clenched, and for a moment he looked—struck. Not angry. Not smug. Just quietly, bitterly hurt.

<!-- paragraph:2900 para_id:00000B54 style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” he said after a beat. “Something like that.”

<!-- paragraph:2901 para_id:00000B55 style:Normal -->
The air between them crackled. Neither of them spoke.

<!-- paragraph:2902 para_id:00000B56 style:Normal -->
Then he turned and walked away.

<!-- paragraph:2903 para_id:00000B57 style:Normal -->
Maya stared after him, her fists clenched at her sides. “He is so—frustrating. And rude. And just… the absolute worst.”

<!-- paragraph:2904 para_id:00000B58 style:Normal -->
Erynn gave her a serene look, “Don’t worry. I’m sure he hates you exactly as much as you hate him.”

<!-- paragraph:2908 para_id:00000B5C style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 18

<!-- paragraph:2909 para_id:00000B5D style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 18

<!-- paragraph:2910 para_id:00000B5E style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2911 para_id:00000B5F style:Normal -->
The door to the meeting room clicked shut behind him, but the heat clung to Davian’s skin. He stalked down the corridor, trying to outpace the burning in his chest, the flush on his face, the infuriating afterimage of Maya Rezin’s eyes—sharp as glass, too clever by half.

<!-- paragraph:2912 para_id:00000B60 style:Normal -->
Self-righteous, imperious, insufferable.

<!-- paragraph:2913 para_id:00000B61 style:Normal -->
And too beautiful by design.

<!-- paragraph:2914 para_id:00000B62 style:Normal -->
He clenched his fists. Of course she looked like that—graceful, poised, with that voice like a blade wrapped in silk. The High Clades bred their children like prized falcons, trained them to dazzle and dominate. Her face was designed for symmetry, her figure sculpted by tutors and dieticians to better snare some other gifted tyrant and bind him in golden chains.

<!-- paragraph:2915 para_id:00000B63 style:Normal -->
She wasn’t supposed to make his heart race. And yet—damn her—she did.

<!-- paragraph:2916 para_id:00000B64 style:Normal -->
He forced himself to stop thinking about her mouth.

<!-- paragraph:2917 para_id:00000B65 style:Normal -->
This was how they did it. This was how the Clades conquered—by being untouchable, untouchably lovely, and convincing the rest of the world to thank them for it.

<!-- paragraph:2918 para_id:00000B66 style:Normal -->
He was halfway through muttering a curse under his breath when he spotted Marek, leaning casually against a stone column like he hadn’t just been eavesdropping.

<!-- paragraph:2919 para_id:00000B67 style:Normal -->
“So it’s true, then,” Marek said, cocking an eyebrow. “You really didn’t ask for the promotion.”

<!-- paragraph:2920 para_id:00000B68 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled hard. “I thought I made that clear.”

<!-- paragraph:2921 para_id:00000B69 style:Normal -->
“You made it a little too clear,” Marek replied, chuckling. “Hope you won’t make me take over both crews again when you get fired for insulting our patron.”

<!-- paragraph:2922 para_id:00000B6A style:Normal -->
“She doesn’t get to talk down to us like that.”

<!-- paragraph:2923 para_id:00000B6B style:Normal -->
“She does, actually,” Marek said, straightening with a dry laugh. “She’s a Rezin. She could vaporize half the city and still have the law on her side - since her family is one of the ones who gets to write it. Not to mention her father could probably destroy the other half without breaking a sweat. And need I remind you that they literally need a giant floating sphere to hold part of their wealth?”

<!-- paragraph:2924 para_id:00000B6C style:Normal -->
Davian scowled, jaw tight. He couldn’t argue. Not really.

<!-- paragraph:2925 para_id:00000B6D style:Normal -->
But still.

<!-- paragraph:2926 para_id:00000B6E style:Normal -->
Every word she’d thrown at him burned—not just for their sharpness, but because for one unbearable moment, she’d looked at him like she expected something good. Like she thought there might be something decent in him. And he’d had to destroy that, twist her view before it could settle into something he couldn't live up to. Now she’d think of him the way they all did—dangerous, selfish, malicious. Just like his parents.

<!-- paragraph:2927 para_id:00000B6F style:Normal -->
Davian rubbed at the back of his neck, jaw clenched. “I need to go spar with Harvick,” he muttered.

<!-- paragraph:2928 para_id:00000B70 style:Normal -->
Marek arched a brow. “You haven’t been fired yet. You know you have to actually do some work to get paid.”

<!-- paragraph:2929 para_id:00000B71 style:Normal -->
Davian snorted but said nothing. They split off, and he moved toward the workers' wing, pulling the assignment list Marek had helped him draft.

<!-- paragraph:2930 para_id:00000B72 style:Normal -->
His newly-assigned crew was waiting—Tanelle, Kolvar, and Jessa—each looking varying degrees of skeptical and curious.

<!-- paragraph:2931 para_id:00000B73 style:Normal -->
Kolvar was the first to speak, tipping his head. “So what are we doing today, boss?”

<!-- paragraph:2932 para_id:00000B74 style:Normal -->
Davian ran through the plans, outlining how they’d coordinate access to the outer wall segments of the vault sphere. He tried not to sound like he was reciting Marek’s instructions verbatim. He’d stayed up late combing through the schematics, quietly grateful Marek had indulged his ignorance—for a price. Davian had promised him a cut of the supervisor's bonus in exchange for tutoring.

<!-- paragraph:2933 para_id:00000B75 style:Normal -->
He prided himself on being a quick study, and after a shaky start, the rest came easier. They rejoined Marek’s team and climbed the long, spiraling staircase to the treasury.

<!-- paragraph:2934 para_id:00000B76 style:Normal -->
Inside the vault chamber, golden light caught the arc of the enormous containment sphere, suspended in magical stasis. Davian spent most of the morning coordinating labor, making sure people weren't getting in each other's way, but his mind wandered.

<!-- paragraph:2935 para_id:00000B77 style:Normal -->
Once the others were focused on their tasks, he drifted to a quieter section of the sphere.

<!-- paragraph:2936 para_id:00000B78 style:Normal -->
“You good?” Jessa asked, passing by with a coil of conduit wire.

<!-- paragraph:2937 para_id:00000B79 style:Normal -->
“Bored,” he replied flatly. “Figured I’d do some real work.”

<!-- paragraph:2938 para_id:00000B7A style:Normal -->
She shrugged and kept going. That was all the cover he needed.

<!-- paragraph:2939 para_id:00000B7B style:Normal -->
He crouched near one of the less-used anchor zän, tracing its etched edges with a gloved finger. Now that he’d seen Marek’s plans, it was easier to understand how the zän nested—how some served as stabilizers and others as limiters for magical flow.

<!-- paragraph:2940 para_id:00000B7C style:Normal -->
If he could isolate the lynchpin zän—the ones Marek had flagged as foundational to the containment weave—then just a small extra nudge might be enough. A weakened lattice, one hard jolt, and the entire sphere would drop. If the containment failed mid-fall, the structure would shatter on impact. Hopefully. That should be enough to breach the inner vault and get to the Heart.

<!-- paragraph:2941 para_id:00000B7D style:Normal -->
He’d have to time it perfectly. Wait until the crews had cleared out. Maybe adjust assignments, stagger shift changes.

<!-- paragraph:2942 para_id:00000B7E style:Normal -->
The thought of coordinating around their schedules—Tanelle’s wry grin, Kolvar’s sarcastic questions, even Jessa’s sharp eye—brought the familiar sting of his father’s voice: Don’t get attached to people who live on scraps from tyrants.

<!-- paragraph:2943 para_id:00000B7F style:Normal -->
Davian shook it off.

<!-- paragraph:2944 para_id:00000B80 style:Normal -->
And Maya—no. She definitely couldn’t be there. She was too powerful - dangerous. It was tactical, smart to ensure she was nowhere nearby.

<!-- paragraph:2945 para_id:00000B81 style:Normal -->
He glanced around. No one was watching. The workers were busy at their stations, distracted by the noise of the tools and the meticulous rhythm of restoration. Slowly, he drew Antithesis from its sheath—its dark blade dull in the low light, but humming faintly with potential.

<!-- paragraph:2946 para_id:00000B82 style:Normal -->
He knew the theory. Most hereditary magics channeled primal forces through the mind’s intent. Subversion wasn’t different in principle. It just worked sideways. Not generation of force, but the repurposing of existing energies. A redirection. A theft.

<!-- paragraph:2947 para_id:00000B83 style:Normal -->
He let his breath slow, recalling Harvick’s instructions from the courtyard. Breathe in through the nose. Feel the weight of the body. Anchor. Eyes closed. Let the world fall quiet.

<!-- paragraph:2948 para_id:00000B84 style:Normal -->
For a moment, it did.

<!-- paragraph:2949 para_id:00000B85 style:Normal -->
With the exhale, his eyes snapped open. He brought Antithesis down onto one of the zä nodes etched into the base of the sphere.

<!-- paragraph:2950 para_id:00000B86 style:Normal -->
His focus tightened. Everything narrowed. The humming lattice flared into his senses—lines of intention and stored force humming just beneath the surface of the stone. His awareness reached in and twisted, not forcefully, but with deliberate intent. A turn of the flow. A change in angle. The magic bent, unwillingly at first, then with a rush, redirected into the blade.

<!-- paragraph:2951 para_id:00000B87 style:Normal -->
Antithesis drank it in.

<!-- paragraph:2952 para_id:00000B88 style:Normal -->
And for that one moment—clarity. The shadows in the corners of his vision withdrew. The pressure behind his eyes eased. Everything felt... aligned.

<!-- paragraph:2953 para_id:00000B89 style:Normal -->
Then someone called out across the chamber. A name, a question. The spell broke.

<!-- paragraph:2954 para_id:00000B8A style:Normal -->
Davian floated back, blinking. The moment was gone—but it had been real.

<!-- paragraph:2955 para_id:00000B8B style:Normal -->
He sheathed the blade slowly, mind racing. If he could clear his mind like that again—channel his attention with that same intensity—he could wield Subversion with far more control. With far less pain.

<!-- paragraph:2956 para_id:00000B8C style:Normal -->
That was the heart of it: Subversion wasn’t about force. It was about redirection. Transformation. The more efficient the redirection, the less backlash. Less wasted force. More effect on magical energies.

<!-- paragraph:2957 para_id:00000B8D style:Normal -->
He flexed his fingers once, then rested his hand on the hilt of Antithesis again.

<!-- paragraph:2958 para_id:00000B8E style:Normal -->
It still felt wrong to admit—even in the privacy of his own mind—but the smaller blade helped. Focused his attention. With a sword, the flow was broader, clumsier. But the dagger let him zero in on a single node, a single fracture in the lattice. He didn’t have to stretch his concentration across a wide area. Just one precise twist. One bend in the current.

<!-- paragraph:2959 para_id:00000B8F style:Normal -->
It was, he realized, exactly what daggers were meant for: concentrating force onto a single point to punch through armor. Less mass. More penetration.

<!-- paragraph:2960 para_id:00000B90 style:Normal -->
He frowned.

<!-- paragraph:2961 para_id:00000B91 style:Normal -->
Would it work on a Shroud? What if he didn’t need to overpower one? What if he could bypass it, unravel a thread, push through where the weave was weakest?

<!-- paragraph:2962 para_id:00000B92 style:Normal -->
He decided to keep practicing when he could. It wasn’t easy. Staying crouched over the sphere too long would raise questions, and his team still needed direction. Every time Kolvar misaligned a scaffolding node or Jessa called for clarification, he had to pull back, refocus, move.

<!-- paragraph:2963 para_id:00000B93 style:Normal -->
Still, maybe that was part of the training.

<!-- paragraph:2964 para_id:00000B94 style:Normal -->
If he could sharpen his mind under these conditions—noise, questions, interruptions—it would be even easier when the time came. At least it wasn’t as bad as trying to tune out the chaos of the magus practice courtyard. That thought made him smirk despite himself.

<!-- paragraph:2965 para_id:00000B95 style:Normal -->
Then he remembered why that memory came to mind—her voice cutting through the noise, her movements quick and precise, that frustrating mix of strength and arrogance.

<!-- paragraph:2966 para_id:00000B96 style:Normal -->
His smirk vanished.

<!-- paragraph:2967 para_id:00000B97 style:Normal -->
He exhaled slowly through his nose and reached again for the lattice, directing his mind not at Maya, but at the slow, complex flow of power beneath his fingers.

<!-- paragraph:2968 para_id:00000B98 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:2969 para_id:00000B99 style:Normal -->
The shift ended faster than Davian expected.

<!-- paragraph:2970 para_id:00000B9A style:Normal -->
For hours he’d been adrift in the sphere chamber, body weightless, mind lashed to the work—zä after zä etched or secretly drained, structure humming faint beneath his fingers. It was the closest thing to quiet he could remember. No future to dread, no past to unravel. Just effort, moment to moment.

<!-- paragraph:2971 para_id:00000B9B style:Normal -->
But the silence broke too soon.

<!-- paragraph:2972 para_id:00000B9C style:Normal -->
“Supervisor! We’re out of time,” Tanelle called from across the vault, her voice echoing sharp against the curved walls.

<!-- paragraph:2973 para_id:00000B9D style:Normal -->
Davian blinked, fingers still hovering near a conduit seam connecting two zän. He exhaled hard, twisting midair, and kicked off the sphere to drift down toward the ground. He dreaded the shift back to gravity, the too-real weight pressing him down.

<!-- paragraph:2974 para_id:00000B9E style:Normal -->
The team was already gathering near the floor: Kolvar cracking his knuckles, Jessa hauling the calibration crates with practiced economy. Tanelle was recounting some half-scandalous hallway rumor, voice bright and too loud in Davian’s ears.

<!-- paragraph:2975 para_id:00000B9F style:Normal -->
He forced himself to step in, take the end-of-day reports. Routine. Grounding. One thing, then the next.

<!-- paragraph:2976 para_id:00000BA0 style:Normal -->
Marek intercepted him at the bottom of the stairs. “We’re ahead on the perimeter layers,” the older man said, voice low. “Tanelle does the work of three, although I wish she wouldn’t do the talking of three. Might even gain a day if this keeps up.”

<!-- paragraph:2977 para_id:00000BA1 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded. “Good.”

<!-- paragraph:2978 para_id:00000BA2 style:Normal -->
“You coming with us?” Marek tipped his head toward the hallway, where Tanelle’s voice still floated down. “Supposed to be decent bread tonight.”

<!-- paragraph:2979 para_id:00000BA3 style:Normal -->
The invitation hit wrong—like a hand pressed to a bruise. For an instant Davian thought of Maya, then of Jalen, the weight of both tightening in his chest. His throat felt dry.

<!-- paragraph:2980 para_id:00000BA4 style:Normal -->
“I’ve got something I need to do,” he said, too quickly. “Later.”

<!-- paragraph:2981 para_id:00000BA5 style:Normal -->
Marek frowned, clearly about to say more, but Tanelle called for him and the moment broke. Davian let it.

<!-- paragraph:2982 para_id:00000BA6 style:Normal -->
He ducked into the mess long enough to grab a roll—still warm, sharp with herbs—and left without waiting for anyone else. The corridors were half-lit at this hour, shadows stretched long along the stone.

<!-- paragraph:2983 para_id:00000BA7 style:Normal -->
He should have gone with them. He knew that. Staying too distant would draw eyes, maybe suspicion. But his pulse was already quickening for another reason. He hadn’t stopped moving all day, and it still wasn’t enough. The tightness in his chest was worse now that the work was done.

<!-- paragraph:2984 para_id:00000BA8 style:Normal -->
He needed something to fight. Something that would fight back.

<!-- paragraph:2985 para_id:00000BA9 style:Normal -->
The courtyard was cooler than the halls, wide and ringed with training zän. Old scars marked the stone underfoot, traces of past sessions scored faintly into the floor. A good place. Quiet. Breath echoed sharper here.

<!-- paragraph:2986 para_id:00000BAA style:Normal -->
Harvick was there already, alone in the center of the ring, stretching out his shoulders with deliberate economy. He glanced up at Davian’s approach, one brow lifting—not displeased, but faintly surprised.

<!-- paragraph:2987 para_id:00000BAB style:Normal -->
“You’re early.”

<!-- paragraph:2988 para_id:00000BAC style:Normal -->
Davian took a bite of the roll and swallowed hard, throat dry. “Had time.”

<!-- paragraph:2989 para_id:00000BAD style:Normal -->
Harvick studied him a beat longer, then gave a small nod.

<!-- paragraph:2990 para_id:00000BAE style:Normal -->
“Well. Let’s see what you’ve brought with you today.”

<!-- paragraph:2991 para_id:00000BAF style:Normal -->
Harvick tossed Davian a training blade, not the lightest one — a subtle message. Davian caught it by the grip and gave it a testing arc through the air. Balance was good.

<!-- paragraph:2992 para_id:00000BB0 style:Normal -->
“You look different from yesterday,” Harvick said mildly, stepping into the ring.

<!-- paragraph:2993 para_id:00000BB1 style:Normal -->
Davian shrugged. “The day was good.”

<!-- paragraph:2994 para_id:00000BB2 style:Normal -->
“Oh?”

<!-- paragraph:2995 para_id:00000BB3 style:Normal -->
“Yeah.” He adjusted his stance, right foot slightly behind, blade low. “I didn’t have to think. Just worked.”

<!-- paragraph:2996 para_id:00000BB4 style:Normal -->
Harvick circled left, loose at the knees, hands relaxed. “A quiet mind, then.”

<!-- paragraph:2997 para_id:00000BB5 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, then hesitated. “I wish it could be like that all the time.”

<!-- paragraph:2998 para_id:00000BB6 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a short exhale through his nose. “Maybe I pushed you a little too hard.”

<!-- paragraph:2999 para_id:00000BB7 style:Normal -->
Their blades tapped as they began to circle.

<!-- paragraph:3000 para_id:00000BB8 style:Normal -->
“It’s good to live in the moment,” Harvick continued. “It’s bad to block everything else out.”

<!-- paragraph:3001 para_id:00000BB9 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t answer right away. They traded a pair of light feints — testing range, not intent.

<!-- paragraph:3002 para_id:00000BBA style:Normal -->
He finally said, “Is there a difference?”

<!-- paragraph:3003 para_id:00000BBB style:Normal -->
Harvick’s only reply was to press forward. Davian met the strike, sparks flashing dulled blades clapped against each other. The rhythm started to build — circle, clash, retreat.

<!-- paragraph:3004 para_id:00000BBC style:Normal -->
Davian let his focus slip for a breath, and everything rushed in at once—not calm, not clarity—saltwater, stinging and bitter.

<!-- paragraph:3005 para_id:00000BBD style:Normal -->
He was only here to steal. To lie. To kill. This entire role, this entire life inside the Rezin citadel—it was a trick. A betrayal dressed up in civility.

<!-- paragraph:3006 para_id:00000BBE style:Normal -->
He should hate them. The High Clades. Everyone who served them. Everyone who helped keep the world the way it was: where power floated to the top and left families like his to drown in the mud. It was their world that had forced him to kneel, to beg, to let the Harbingers put a knife to his family’s throat.

<!-- paragraph:3007 para_id:00000BBF style:Normal -->
A flicker of Maya crossed his mind—too fast, too bright— and beneath even that, something worse.

<!-- paragraph:3008 para_id:00000BC0 style:Normal -->
The sense that all of this was wrong. That no excuse, no mission, no memory could make it right.

<!-- paragraph:3009 para_id:00000BC1 style:Normal -->
His next strike came fast, and Harvick turned it easily, redirecting the force with a flick of the wrist.

<!-- paragraph:3010 para_id:00000BC2 style:Normal -->
“If I don’t block it out,” Davian said, “it’s going to drown me.”

<!-- paragraph:3011 para_id:00000BC3 style:Normal -->
Harvick stepped back, not attacking. Watching. “It’s not possible to be focused all the time.”

<!-- paragraph:3012 para_id:00000BC4 style:Normal -->
Davian moved to circle again, jaw tight.

<!-- paragraph:3013 para_id:00000BC5 style:Normal -->
Harvick kept speaking. “Would you even want to be?”

<!-- paragraph:3014 para_id:00000BC6 style:Normal -->
Their blades kissed again.

<!-- paragraph:3015 para_id:00000BC7 style:Normal -->
“Your past and future — they matter. You just can’t live in them. You have to make peace with the things that try to sweep you away.”

<!-- paragraph:3016 para_id:00000BC8 style:Normal -->
Their movements sharpened—rhythm snapping tighter, the space between feints narrowing. Davian's strikes came with more heat now, the edge just shy of real. Harvick didn’t call it out. He just matched the pace.

<!-- paragraph:3017 para_id:00000BC9 style:Normal -->
Davian broke the silence. “Is this even about fighting anymore?”

<!-- paragraph:3018 para_id:00000BCA style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a short, surprised laugh as their blades locked. “Everything is about fighting.”

<!-- paragraph:3019 para_id:00000BCB style:Normal -->
He disengaged smoothly, stepping back. “Being alive is a fight. Every second you stay upright, your body’s winning against something trying to kill it.”

<!-- paragraph:3020 para_id:00000BCC style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled hard, parrying. “I don’t want to fight all the time.”

<!-- paragraph:3021 para_id:00000BCD style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged. “Then die.”

<!-- paragraph:3022 para_id:00000BCE style:Normal -->
Davian faltered a half-step.

<!-- paragraph:3023 para_id:00000BCF style:Normal -->
Harvick’s voice came quieter now, but sharper for it. “The only way to stop struggling is to stop breathing. The rest of us have to learn how to live with the weight. Until you learn how to do that, you’ll always be too distracted to win.”

<!-- paragraph:3024 para_id:00000BD0 style:Normal -->
Davian’s blade caught Harvick’s next strike and slid it aside. “That can’t be true. I’ve beaten you before.”

<!-- paragraph:3025 para_id:00000BD1 style:Normal -->
Another chuckle, almost fond. “You’ve surprised me, sure. You’re one of the most talented I’ve had—raw, fast, vicious. But we’ve never actually fought.”

<!-- paragraph:3026 para_id:00000BD2 style:Normal -->
Davian lunged, and Harvick danced back two steps, light on his heels.

<!-- paragraph:3027 para_id:00000BD3 style:Normal -->
They circled again. Harvick lifted his sword, not in a guard but in a gesture—like pointing a question.

<!-- paragraph:3028 para_id:00000BD4 style:Normal -->
“So make up your mind,” he said. “What kind of man do you want to be? Someone who sees clearly—doesn’t excuse the world, but accepts it for what it is? Or someone who cuts his own eyes out and calls it focus?”

<!-- paragraph:3029 para_id:00000BD5 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t answer. He just ran in.

<!-- paragraph:3030 para_id:00000BD6 style:Normal -->
And Harvick inhaled.

<!-- paragraph:3031 para_id:00000BD7 style:Normal -->
Not loudly, not theatrically. Just a controlled, practiced breath.

<!-- paragraph:3032 para_id:00000BD8 style:Normal -->
But something shifted—subtle as a change in pressure, sudden as a fall. The courtyard felt wrong. Empty. Like the moment before a wave crashes.

<!-- paragraph:3033 para_id:00000BD9 style:Normal -->
Then Harvick was in front of him.

<!-- paragraph:3034 para_id:00000BDA style:Normal -->
Every angle at once—low sweep, shoulder check, elbow toward the ribs. Davian blocked the first two, barely saw the third. His breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:3035 para_id:00000BDB style:Normal -->
The next strike landed flat on his blade, but the force jolted through his arms, ripped his stance wide open. The sword flew from his grip and clattered across the stone.

<!-- paragraph:3036 para_id:00000BDC style:Normal -->
He hit the ground hard, shoulders scraping along the pitted floor, staring up at empty sky.

<!-- paragraph:3037 para_id:00000BDD style:Normal -->
Footsteps scraped across the ground. Harvick crouched beside him, sword lowered, expression open and unguarded. No gloating. No judgment. Just that same quiet calm.

<!-- paragraph:3038 para_id:00000BDE style:Normal -->
“I know you’re carrying something,” he said gently. “And I’m not here to shame you for it. I’m not trying to break you down or beat something out of you.”

<!-- paragraph:3039 para_id:00000BDF style:Normal -->
His voice dropped, softer still. “I can tell you’re trying. Really trying. I just want to help with that.”

<!-- paragraph:3040 para_id:00000BE0 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at the sky, breath coming in shallow draws. His hands were shaking, though not from the fight.

<!-- paragraph:3041 para_id:00000BE1 style:Normal -->
Something in his chest twisted — sharp and unfamiliar, but not bad. Not dangerous. Just… too much.

<!-- paragraph:3042 para_id:00000BE2 style:Normal -->
He closed his eyes and let the feeling come.

<!-- paragraph:3043 para_id:00000BE3 style:Normal -->
That someone could see him, like this, and not assume he was dangerous. Or selfish. Or rotten at the core. That someone could think he was worth helping.

<!-- paragraph:3044 para_id:00000BE4 style:Normal -->
If someone had looked at him like that back then—maybe everything would’ve turned out differently.

<!-- paragraph:3045 para_id:00000BE5 style:Normal -->
“Let your thoughts flow through,” Harvick said. “Like a river. Don’t try to fight the current.”

<!-- paragraph:3046 para_id:00000BE6 style:Normal -->
“They’re not water,” Davian murmured. “They’re brine.”

<!-- paragraph:3047 para_id:00000BE7 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s eyebrows lifted slightly.

<!-- paragraph:3048 para_id:00000BE8 style:Normal -->
“It’s like being submerged,” Davian said, quieter. “Everything burns.”

<!-- paragraph:3049 para_id:00000BE9 style:Normal -->
The older man stood and offered a hand. Davian took it.

<!-- paragraph:3050 para_id:00000BEA style:Normal -->
“Maybe that’s enough with the sword for today,” Harvick said, brushing dust from his palm. “I showed you something. You show me something?”

<!-- paragraph:3051 para_id:00000BEB style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. “What?”

<!-- paragraph:3052 para_id:00000BEC style:Normal -->
Harvick gestured loosely. “You were going through some forms when I first saw you in the courtyard last week. Looked like hand-to-hand work.”

<!-- paragraph:3053 para_id:00000BED style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. “My—my mother taught me. “She didn’t want me relying too much on my…”  he caught the word magic just before it left his mouth “...other skills.”

<!-- paragraph:3054 para_id:00000BEE style:Normal -->
Harvick tilted his head. “She taught you a lot, for someone who died when you were what, eight?”

<!-- paragraph:3055 para_id:00000BEF style:Normal -->
A flicker of panic tore through Davian’s chest. He suddenly felt cold.

<!-- paragraph:3056 para_id:00000BF0 style:Normal -->
Stupid. Stupid. You forgot.

<!-- paragraph:3057 para_id:00000BF1 style:Normal -->
He glanced down—bare arms, bared Ink. The pattern had shifted after the last boon. Had Harvick noticed? Had he already figured it out?

<!-- paragraph:3058 para_id:00000BF2 style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t press. Just folded his arms. “I read your file,” he said casually. “If I’m going to try keeping you around, I like to know who I’m investing in.”

<!-- paragraph:3059 para_id:00000BF3 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, forcing his shoulders to stay still.

<!-- paragraph:3060 para_id:00000BF4 style:Normal -->
“It’s not a style that’s good for sparring,” he said. “It’s made to surprise someone stronger than you. Disable them before they even know you’re going to fight back.”

<!-- paragraph:3061 para_id:00000BF5 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s eyes lit slightly at that — a gleam that meant trouble.

<!-- paragraph:3062 para_id:00000BF6 style:Normal -->
“Well,” he said, stepping back and motioning to a nearby guard. “Now you’ve got me curious.”

<!-- paragraph:3063 para_id:00000BF7 style:Normal -->
He called across the courtyard. “Bring the healer.”

<!-- paragraph:3064 para_id:00000BF8 style:Normal -->
They stood in the stillness that followed a hard fight, the ring of stone and breath slowly fading. Harvick leaned casually against the weapon rack, like they were just two men cooling down from a morning stretch.

<!-- paragraph:3065 para_id:00000BF9 style:Normal -->
Davian crossed his arms, still keyed up. “I don’t think this is a good idea.”

<!-- paragraph:3066 para_id:00000BFA style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a warm chuckle. “Yeah, I figured you’d say that.”

<!-- paragraph:3067 para_id:00000BFB style:Normal -->
He nodded toward the center of the courtyard. “But I’ve heard about this kind of style before—crippling strikes, timing-based breaks. Never seen it properly. Seems like something that could be… educational. What do you call it?”

<!-- paragraph:3068 para_id:00000BFC style:Normal -->
Davian shifted his weight. “I don’t think it even has a name.”

<!-- paragraph:3069 para_id:00000BFD style:Normal -->
“No?”

<!-- paragraph:3070 para_id:00000BFE style:Normal -->
“It’s not like there’s a school or anything. It’s just—my mother knew that you wouldn’t always have a sword. Or armor. That sometimes you’d have to fight someone bigger, stronger, better-equipped. And if you wanted to survive, you had to make it ugly.”

<!-- paragraph:3071 para_id:00000BFF style:Normal -->
Harvick’s face sobered slightly, and he gave a thoughtful nod. “Fair enough.”

<!-- paragraph:3072 para_id:00000C00 style:Normal -->
A side door creaked open behind them.

<!-- paragraph:3073 para_id:00000C01 style:Normal -->
The healer entered without hurry, her crimson robes trimmed in darker piping. A vulpine familiar padded beside her, half-translucent and fluid in its gait, its glowing eyes sweeping the courtyard as if reading it.

<!-- paragraph:3074 para_id:00000C02 style:Normal -->
It broke off from her and curled itself lazily along the stone near the sparring ring—coiled like smoke with a pulse that beat faintly in time with the air.

<!-- paragraph:3075 para_id:00000C03 style:Normal -->
The healer stopped a few paces away, giving Harvick a small incline of her head. She did not speak. She didn’t need to.

<!-- paragraph:3076 para_id:00000C04 style:Normal -->
A second figure followed: a guard with an open expression and a resigned shrug already forming.

<!-- paragraph:3077 para_id:00000C05 style:Normal -->
Harvick smiled and clapped his hands once, brisk. “Perfect. Let’s see what this looks like, then.”

<!-- paragraph:3078 para_id:00000C06 style:Normal -->
Harvick stripped off his overshirt, rolling his shoulders as he stepped to the center of the ring. He looked over at the healer, who gave a slight nod and crouched beside her familiar. The fox-like creature mirrored her stillness, its glowing eyes fixed on the space between the two men.

<!-- paragraph:3079 para_id:00000C07 style:Normal -->
“All right,” Harvick said, loosening his stance. “Show me.”

<!-- paragraph:3080 para_id:00000C08 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t nod. He just moved.

<!-- paragraph:3081 para_id:00000C09 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t a charge or a flourish—it was a step, a pivot, and Harvick’s arm was already being pulled out of position. Davian hooked his foot behind Harvick’s ankle while twisting his wrist back toward his own shoulder. The motion looked clumsy until it landed, and then there was a loud pop. Harvick was on his knees with his elbow pointed the wrong direction.

<!-- paragraph:3082 para_id:00000C0A style:Normal -->
“Damn, that hurts” Harvick muttered.

<!-- paragraph:3083 para_id:00000C0B style:Normal -->
The healer was already moving. She placed one hand lightly on her familiar and one on Harvick’s arm. A quiet glow passed between them.

<!-- paragraph:3084 para_id:00000C0C style:Normal -->
Harvick stood, flexing the joint. “Okay. Again.”

<!-- paragraph:3085 para_id:00000C0D style:Normal -->
This time Davian faked high and moved in low, dropping weight into Harvick’s hip and rotating under his center of balance. A sharp elbow snapped toward the floating ribs. Harvick blocked, barely—just enough to take the edge off—but Davian flowed through, redirecting to the knee.

<!-- paragraph:3086 para_id:00000C0E style:Normal -->
A pop, a grunt, and Harvick hit the floor.

<!-- paragraph:3087 para_id:00000C0F style:Normal -->
The healer was already reaching for him, the familiar curling around Harvick’s leg like mist drawn into a vacuum.

<!-- paragraph:3088 para_id:00000C10 style:Normal -->
After a third round—this one a neck-level feint that ended with a knee locked straight and a palm jabbing the throat lightly, just enough to make the risk obvious—Harvick held up a hand.

<!-- paragraph:3089 para_id:00000C11 style:Normal -->
“All right,” he said, voice dry. “I get it. That’s probably enough.”

<!-- paragraph:3090 para_id:00000C12 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped back, breathing light and even. “There are some forms I didn’t show.”

<!-- paragraph:3091 para_id:00000C13 style:Normal -->
“Oh?” Harvick was still catching his breath.

<!-- paragraph:3092 para_id:00000C14 style:Normal -->
“They’re not the kind the healer could fix.”

<!-- paragraph:3093 para_id:00000C15 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a short, wry exhale. “Well. I appreciate that.”

<!-- paragraph:3094 para_id:00000C16 style:Normal -->
The healer inclined her head after the final check and slipped out, familiar padding at her heels like a living shadow. The courtyard felt quieter without her, emptier in a way Davian couldn’t name.

<!-- paragraph:3095 para_id:00000C17 style:Normal -->
Harvick watched the door close, then turned back to him, arms crossed loosely. “I wanted to see it for myself. Not just the technique—your stance, your angles—but what it tells me about how you think. About the kind of life you’ve lived.”

<!-- paragraph:3096 para_id:00000C18 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t move.

<!-- paragraph:3097 para_id:00000C19 style:Normal -->
“That style…” Harvick gestured toward the ring where they'd just moved like smoke and fracture. “It’s not about outmatching someone. It’s about surviving. About winning because losing isn't an option. Because the consequences of one mistake are too high to imagine.”

<!-- paragraph:3098 para_id:00000C1A style:Normal -->
His voice was steady, but not cold.

<!-- paragraph:3099 para_id:00000C1B style:Normal -->
“The way you move—it’s built on the idea that anyone could turn on you at any second. That pain is inevitable, and the only control you get is how fast you end the threat.”

<!-- paragraph:3100 para_id:00000C1C style:Normal -->
Harvick met his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:3101 para_id:00000C1D style:Normal -->
“So… that’s why your river tastes like brine.”

<!-- paragraph:3102 para_id:00000C1E style:Normal -->
Davian looked away, jaw tight. He didn’t trust his voice enough to speak.

<!-- paragraph:3103 para_id:00000C1F style:Normal -->
Harvick’s tone softened, but he didn’t pull away. “No wonder it feels easier to block it all out.”

<!-- paragraph:3104 para_id:00000C20 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded once. Just once. That was all he could offer.

<!-- paragraph:3105 para_id:00000C21 style:Normal -->
“I meant what I said earlier,” Harvick went on. “About wanting to help. About seeing that you’re trying.”

<!-- paragraph:3106 para_id:00000C22 style:Normal -->
He didn’t sound ashamed, exactly, but Davian could hear the shift—the edge of regret in someone reevaluating their stance.

<!-- paragraph:3107 para_id:00000C23 style:Normal -->
“I can see why this is hard for you now. Harder than I gave credit for.”

<!-- paragraph:3108 para_id:00000C24 style:Normal -->
He let the silence hang a beat, then continued. “But I need you to hear this part too.”

<!-- paragraph:3109 para_id:00000C25 style:Normal -->
Harvick stepped closer, his voice low but firm.

<!-- paragraph:3110 para_id:00000C26 style:Normal -->
“Blocking everything out might help you get through the day. It might make you faster, colder, even sharper. But it won’t help you build a better life. It won’t make you strong in the way that lasts.”

<!-- paragraph:3111 para_id:00000C27 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed, still not trusting himself to say anything that wouldn’t crack something open.

<!-- paragraph:3112 para_id:00000C28 style:Normal -->
Harvick clapped him gently on the shoulder—not hard, not condescending. Just solid. Grounding.

<!-- paragraph:3113 para_id:00000C29 style:Normal -->
“There’s no easy answer to whatever you’re facing. But there is a path forward.”

<!-- paragraph:3114 para_id:00000C2A style:Normal -->
Harvick turned to leave, stepping lightly over the scored stone as if the fight had never touched him. He made it halfway to the courtyard arch before glancing back over his shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:3115 para_id:00000C2B style:Normal -->
“You probably still have time to make the dining hall,” he called. “One roll’s not gonna get you strong enough to win next time.”

<!-- paragraph:3116 para_id:00000C2C style:Normal -->
The comment hit just right—cutting through the fog in Davian’s chest with something almost like light.

<!-- paragraph:3117 para_id:00000C2D style:Normal -->
He huffed out a laugh, the first real one all day. “You talk about rolls a lot. I’m starting to think you’re the one with the problem.”

<!-- paragraph:3118 para_id:00000C2E style:Normal -->
Harvick looked down at his own stomach with exaggerated concern, then laughed and waved him off. “Better than pretending I don’t.”

<!-- paragraph:3119 para_id:00000C2F style:Normal -->
Then he disappeared through the archway, and the courtyard fell quiet again.

<!-- paragraph:3121 para_id:00000C31 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 19

<!-- paragraph:3122 para_id:00000C32 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 19

<!-- paragraph:3123 para_id:00000C33 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3124 para_id:00000C34 style:Normal -->
The numbers didn’t add up.

<!-- paragraph:3125 para_id:00000C35 style:Normal -->
<!-- comment-start:111 --><!-- comment-start:112 -->Maya circled the zä cluster again, then crossed it out entirely and started from scratch. The team assigned to the east wing was either working from an outdated schematic or flat-out incompetent. Either way, the registry-aligned runes were misaligned by half a cadence, which meant they might not trigger properly if the citadel were ever actually under attack. Which, at this rate, felt increasingly likely.<!-- comment-end:111 --><!-- comment-ref:111 --><!-- comment-end:112 --><!-- comment-ref:112 -->

<!-- paragraph:3126 para_id:00000C36 style:Normal -->
Erynn made a soft humming sound from her perch near the window. “You know,” she said without looking up, “you could just fire them.”

<!-- paragraph:3127 para_id:00000C37 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer. Her pen scratched harder than it needed to.

<!-- paragraph:3128 para_id:00000C38 style:Normal -->
The knock on the door came precise and measured. Alden.

<!-- paragraph:3129 para_id:00000C39 style:Normal -->
She didn’t stop working. “Come in.”

<!-- paragraph:3130 para_id:00000C3A style:Normal -->
He entered and closed the door behind him with a quiet click. “Selwin’s awake.”

<!-- paragraph:3131 para_id:00000C3B style:Normal -->
She set her pen down harder than she meant to, fingers tight from hours of writing. “Of course he is.”

<!-- paragraph:3132 para_id:00000C3C style:Normal -->
She stood, fixing her cuffs. “So I get to go reassure him he’s not dangerous. While I coordinate all the ward teams and try to stop the lattice from collapsing under the weight of its own incompetence. And Father?”

<!-- paragraph:3133 para_id:00000C3D style:Normal -->
Alden said nothing.

<!-- paragraph:3134 para_id:00000C3E style:Normal -->
She gave a short laugh. “Of course not. He gets to vanish until it suits him to return—while I hold everything together and somehow thank him for the privilege.”

<!-- paragraph:3135 para_id:00000C3F style:Normal -->
“He’s not doing nothing,” Alden said. “He’s unraveling. Quietly.”

<!-- paragraph:3136 para_id:00000C40 style:Normal -->
Maya turned. “And that’s supposed to make me feel better? That I’m being left to carry the city while the man who built it sinks into himself?”

<!-- paragraph:3137 para_id:00000C41 style:Normal -->
“He built it by turning himself into something no one could challenge,” Alden said calmly. “He’s the knife at their throats. Every High Clade knows it. And they’ve spent years smiling while calculating what it would take to survive the day he falls.”

<!-- paragraph:3138 para_id:00000C42 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at him. “So this is helping? This is keeping us safe?”

<!-- paragraph:3139 para_id:00000C43 style:Normal -->
“He doesn’t know how to stop,” Alden replied. “He’s spent his entire life containing everything—his power, his grief, his family. He trusted one person with the truth, and she died. He killed his father. His daughter may already outmatch him. His son is… fragile. The only other people in the city who could be his peers are the ones most likely to turn on him.”

<!-- paragraph:3140 para_id:00000C44 style:Normal -->
He paused. “He’s terrified. And he doesn’t know how to ask for help.”

<!-- paragraph:3141 para_id:00000C45 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms. Her voice was quieter now, but no less bitter. “He treated me like I was reckless. Like I was dangerous. And now I’m expected to clean up the mess without so much as a word.”

<!-- paragraph:3142 para_id:00000C46 style:Normal -->
Alden’s gaze didn’t waver. “You stepped onto the wall and showed the city what strength looks like. You changed the pattern. That’s not a mess. It’s a beginning.”

<!-- paragraph:3143 para_id:00000C47 style:Normal -->
“But it wasn’t supposed to be like this,” Maya said. “I didn’t ask to lead.”

<!-- paragraph:3144 para_id:00000C48 style:Normal -->
“No one asks to inherit the fire. They either hold it, or they burn.”

<!-- paragraph:3145 para_id:00000C49 style:Normal -->
Maya was silent. Then, more quietly: “I saw what he used to be. I saw it.”

<!-- paragraph:3146 para_id:00000C4A style:Normal -->
“So did I,” Alden said. “And I believe that man is still there. But even weapons take time to be reforged.”

<!-- paragraph:3147 para_id:00000C4B style:Normal -->
She met his eyes. “How much time does he need?”

<!-- paragraph:3148 para_id:00000C4C style:Normal -->
Alden didn’t answer immediately. When he did, his voice was even.

<!-- paragraph:3149 para_id:00000C4D style:Normal -->
“We don’t know. We’re all waiting. And worrying. But this kind of healing doesn’t follow orders.”

<!-- paragraph:3150 para_id:00000C4E style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled slowly.

<!-- paragraph:3151 para_id:00000C4F style:Normal -->
Alden glanced toward the door. “The healer says Selwin’s asking for you.”

<!-- paragraph:3152 para_id:00000C50 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, slowly. “All right.”

<!-- paragraph:3153 para_id:00000C51 style:Normal -->
The corridor to the infirmary was dim and still, the hour too early for most of the Citadel to be stirring. Maya’s boots made soft sounds on the stone as she walked, arms folded tightly across her chest. Erynn padded beside her, more awake than Maya would have thought possible.

<!-- paragraph:3154 para_id:00000C52 style:Normal -->
“He was fine that morning,” Maya murmured. “He didn’t even seem anxious. Just… determined.”

<!-- paragraph:3155 para_id:00000C53 style:Normal -->
Erynn said nothing at first, letting the silence stretch. “You mean Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:3156 para_id:00000C54 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded. “I should have noticed something. Or said something different. Or told Niall—”

<!-- paragraph:3157 para_id:00000C55 style:Normal -->
“Or maybe,” Erynn cut in gently, “you did your best with what you knew.”

<!-- paragraph:3158 para_id:00000C56 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned, her gaze fixed ahead. “I’m supposed to be watching over him.”

<!-- paragraph:3159 para_id:00000C57 style:Normal -->
“You are. You’ve been doing that for years. And you still are.”

<!-- paragraph:3160 para_id:00000C58 style:Normal -->
“But not well enough.” Her voice was sharp. “He could’ve died. No one knew what to do. Not the guards, not the healers, not even me. And I always at least know something. That’s my job.”

<!-- paragraph:3161 para_id:00000C59 style:Normal -->
“No,” Erynn said, a little more firmly. “Your job is to be Maya. Not some omniscient overlord.”

<!-- paragraph:3162 para_id:00000C5A style:Normal -->
They turned a corner, and the door to the infirmary came into view—quiet, no guards, just a thin ribbon of light from beneath the frame.

<!-- paragraph:3163 para_id:00000C5B style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated a moment before pushing the door open.

<!-- paragraph:3164 para_id:00000C5C style:Normal -->
Inside, the ward was hushed. The scent of herbs hung faintly in the air, and the only sounds were the slow crackle of the rune-braziers and the occasional creak of bed linens. At the far end, Selwin sat up, propped against pillows, his hair mussed and his brow faintly furrowed. He looked pale but alert.

<!-- paragraph:3165 para_id:00000C5D style:Normal -->
“Maya,” he said groggily, then squinted. “And Erynn. Did I—am I still—?”

<!-- paragraph:3166 para_id:00000C5E style:Normal -->
“You’re awake,” Maya said, relief and worry tangling in her chest. “That’s what matters.”

<!-- paragraph:3167 para_id:00000C5F style:Normal -->
Selwin’s gaze darted down to his arms. “I didn’t mean to lose control. It just happened. I didn’t even feel it coming.”

<!-- paragraph:3168 para_id:00000C60 style:Normal -->
“You manifested something,” Maya said gently. “A new sigil. No one’s sure what it is yet.”

<!-- paragraph:3169 para_id:00000C61 style:Normal -->
Selwin’s eyes widened slightly. “It was everywhere. I thought—I thought it was going to swallow me.”

<!-- paragraph:3170 para_id:00000C62 style:Normal -->
“You brought down half a building,” Maya said, her voice softer now. “Arrek pulled you out just before it collapsed.”

<!-- paragraph:3171 para_id:00000C63 style:Normal -->
Selwin’s voice went quieter. “Is he okay?”

<!-- paragraph:3172 para_id:00000C64 style:Normal -->
“He’s fine.” Maya offered a small smile. “Bruised, probably, but fine.”

<!-- paragraph:3173 para_id:00000C65 style:Normal -->
Selwin glanced toward the wall, then back at Maya. “Am I in trouble?”

<!-- paragraph:3174 para_id:00000C66 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth to answer, but another voice came from behind her.

<!-- paragraph:3175 para_id:00000C67 style:Normal -->
“No,” it said quietly. “You’re not.”

<!-- paragraph:3176 para_id:00000C68 style:Normal -->
She turned.

<!-- paragraph:3177 para_id:00000C69 style:Normal -->
Niall stood in the doorway, his shoulders hunched deeper into his coat, the shadows beneath his eyes darker than she remembered. But his presence still carried weight—less like thunder now, more like the low hum of something distant, gathering.

<!-- paragraph:3178 para_id:00000C6A style:Normal -->
Selwin straightened instinctively. “I didn’t mean to—”

<!-- paragraph:3179 para_id:00000C6B style:Normal -->
“I know,” Niall said, his voice rasping with disuse but steady enough. He stepped forward with a slow caution that wasn’t quite hesitation. No reprimand. No disappointment. Just the effort of being there. He reached Selwin’s bedside and laid a hand on his son’s shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:3180 para_id:00000C6C style:Normal -->
“You came through it,” he said. “That’s what matters.”

<!-- paragraph:3181 para_id:00000C6D style:Normal -->
Selwin’s eyes dropped, then nodded slowly.

<!-- paragraph:3182 para_id:00000C6E style:Normal -->
Maya watched, arms still folded. Her chest ached with something tangled and uncertain. She didn’t speak. Not yet.

<!-- paragraph:3183 para_id:00000C6F style:Normal -->
Niall glanced down at Selwin, then back up. “I heard what you said,” he murmured. “About how it felt. About me.”

<!-- paragraph:3184 para_id:00000C70 style:Normal -->
Selwin didn’t reply, but his expression shifted—part guilt, part fear.

<!-- paragraph:3185 para_id:00000C71 style:Normal -->
“I’m sorry,” Niall said. “If I gave you the impression that I only care about power, or that I’d given up on you. I haven’t. I wish I could always be the steady foundation you need, but sometimes I’m not. And that isn’t your fault.”

<!-- paragraph:3186 para_id:00000C72 style:Normal -->
There was a moment, just a breath, where the words seemed to cost him something. Maya saw it—the smallest retreat, a folding inward. And then, with deliberate care, he continued. “But I’m going to do better. Starting now.”

<!-- paragraph:3187 para_id:00000C73 style:Normal -->
He looked up then, and across the bed, met Maya’s gaze. It was a quiet, searching look—almost unsure. Like he was asking for something she hadn’t known he needed: her belief.

<!-- paragraph:3188 para_id:00000C74 style:Normal -->
And in that instant, the world tilted. Threads shimmered at the edges of her vision, stretching outward like cracks in glass. She saw him again—not the weary, distant man he had become, but the younger version she’d glimpsed in the vision: proud, certain, still whole.

<!-- paragraph:3189 para_id:00000C75 style:Normal -->
It vanished in the space of a blink.

<!-- paragraph:3190 para_id:00000C76 style:Normal -->
Maya cleared her throat. “Thank you,” she said softly. “For coming.”

<!-- paragraph:3191 para_id:00000C77 style:Normal -->
