Niall stood turned to the arched window of the small chamber, pale morning light framing his silhouette.

<!-- paragraph:3192 para_id:00000C78 style:Normal -->
"I’ve reviewed the records and searched through every sigil catalog I have access to," Niall began, his voice even but quieter than usual. "I don’t recognize what manifested. And I’ve never seen anything like it before."

<!-- paragraph:3193 para_id:00000C79 style:Normal -->
Selwin looked up, his expression tightening.

<!-- paragraph:3194 para_id:00000C7A style:Normal -->
"Which means," Niall continued, turning back toward them, "we have to proceed with caution. You’ll be training in an isolated section of the fortress from now on. Until we understand the nature of your magic, no one else can be exposed to it."

<!-- paragraph:3195 para_id:00000C7B style:Normal -->
Maya watched Selwin's shoulders stiffen.

<!-- paragraph:3196 para_id:00000C7C style:Normal -->
"I had hoped to spare you the kind of training Maya went through," Niall added. "But that’s not a luxury we have anymore."

<!-- paragraph:3197 para_id:00000C7D style:Normal -->
Selwin looked up sharply. "So you’re not disappointed in me?"

<!-- paragraph:3198 para_id:00000C7E style:Normal -->
Niall blinked, clearly caught off guard. "Disappointed? Why would I be?"

<!-- paragraph:3199 para_id:00000C7F style:Normal -->
Selwin’s voice came out quieter, but it was clearer than before. "Because after Maya manifested, you started spending all your time with her. You stopped asking about my casting. You stopped training me."

<!-- paragraph:3200 para_id:00000C80 style:Normal -->
Niall stepped forward slowly. "I thought I was giving you something better. A chance to be a boy for a while longer. To play, to sleep in, to feel like there wasn’t a war at your doorstep."

<!-- paragraph:3201 para_id:00000C81 style:Normal -->
Maya was quiet, watching the exchange. She remembered how it had changed for her—the first time her sigil lit her skin, the way Niall’s focus narrowed. Training, sparring, long nights of ward calculations and sigil drills until her arms ached and her head pounded. She remembered crying and Niall telling her that emotion was a luxury they couldn’t afford. That perfection wasn’t a goal; it was a necessity.

<!-- paragraph:3202 para_id:00000C82 style:Normal -->
Maya stayed quiet for a long moment, watching Selwin’s face as he processed Niall’s words. She could see the hope trying to rise in him, fragile and tentative.

<!-- paragraph:3203 para_id:00000C83 style:Normal -->
But she didn’t entirely believe what Niall had said.

<!-- paragraph:3204 para_id:00000C84 style:Normal -->
Not because it was cruel or dishonest—but because it wasn’t the whole truth. Maybe he had wanted to spare Selwin the weight she’d carried, but it wasn’t the only reason. He’d been pulling back from everything, not just his son. She’d felt it too—in his absences, in the way he'd started treating duty like it was armor rather than anchor. And maybe he didn’t even know why. Maybe that was the part that worried her most.

<!-- paragraph:3205 para_id:00000C85 style:Normal -->
Still, she let it pass—for now. There were other battles to fight.

<!-- paragraph:3206 para_id:00000C86 style:Normal -->
She stepped forward, folding her arms loosely. “We need balance,” she said, not looking at Niall, not looking at Selwin—just speaking it into the space between them. “Not the way it was for me. Not constant drills until you can’t feel your hands. But not nothing either. He needs structure. Guidance. He needs you.”

<!-- paragraph:3207 para_id:00000C87 style:Normal -->
She turned her gaze to her father now. “That’s what keeps us safe.”

<!-- paragraph:3208 para_id:00000C88 style:Normal -->
Selwin shifted in his blankets, then looked up at them both. “I want to go say thank you to Arrek.”

<!-- paragraph:3209 para_id:00000C89 style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression flickered—hesitation, a breath half-held—but he caught himself. His jaw tensed slightly, then eased. “All right,” he said. “I’ll see you after. For training.”

<!-- paragraph:3210 para_id:00000C8A style:Normal -->
Selwin’s face lit up. “Okay!”

<!-- paragraph:3211 para_id:00000C8B style:Normal -->
Maya stepped toward the door. “I know where he’ll be,” she said quietly, offering her brother a small nod.

<!-- paragraph:3212 para_id:00000C8C style:Normal -->
Selwin swung his legs over the side of the bed and followed, moving carefully but with unmistakable eagerness. A bit of color had returned to his cheeks; some of the usual brightness had crept back into his voice.

<!-- paragraph:3213 para_id:00000C8D style:Normal -->
“I met him once before,” he said as they walked. “In the tower. He looked kind of… sad.” Selwin glanced up at Maya. “Not in a scary way. Just like… the way Father looks sometimes. Maybe if I say thank you, it’ll help.”

<!-- paragraph:3214 para_id:00000C8E style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer right away. She just opened the door and waited as he stepped through.

<!-- paragraph:3215 para_id:00000C8F style:Normal -->
But before they could go, Selwin turned and dashed back to Niall’s side. He wrapped his arms around his father in a sudden, tight hug.

<!-- paragraph:3216 para_id:00000C90 style:Normal -->
“Thank you,” he said, voice muffled against Niall’s coat.

<!-- paragraph:3217 para_id:00000C91 style:Normal -->
Then he ran after Maya, sandals slapping softly against the stone.

<!-- paragraph:3218 para_id:00000C92 style:Normal -->
The treasury chamber was unusually quiet. Maya stepped through the archway and paused at the bottom of the stairs, brows knitting. Half a dozen workers lounged on the upper steps, bent over their lunches, talking with the relaxed cadence of midday respite. A few looked up when they noticed her; one elbowed the man next to him.

<!-- paragraph:3219 para_id:00000C93 style:Normal -->
“Looking for Arrek?” a heavyset man called down. “Said something about getting his exercise in. Not a man who likes to sit still.”

<!-- paragraph:3220 para_id:00000C94 style:Normal -->
Maya sighed, pivoted sharply on her heel, and stalked back the way she’d come.

<!-- paragraph:3221 para_id:00000C95 style:Normal -->
Selwin trotted at her side with cheerful obliviousness, bouncing a little with each step. By the time they reached the staircase, he’d already skipped ahead, feet barely touching the ground.

<!-- paragraph:3222 para_id:00000C96 style:Normal -->
“Careful,” Maya muttered as he nearly missed a step.

<!-- paragraph:3223 para_id:00000C97 style:Normal -->
“I’m fine,” he chirped back, already halfway down. She followed at a more measured pace, annoyance simmering under her skin.

<!-- paragraph:3224 para_id:00000C98 style:Normal -->
Back in the main courtyard, a sudden clatter of wood on wood rang out from the eastern side—sharp, rhythmic. Shouts followed. She turned toward the noise just as another thwack sounded, and then laughter.

<!-- paragraph:3225 para_id:00000C99 style:Normal -->
A small crowd had gathered near one of the sparring yards. Maya pushed toward it, Selwin trailing behind. As soon as the onlookers caught sight of her, they parted without a word, a murmured "Lady Maya" rippling through the group. She stepped through the opening and stopped.

<!-- paragraph:3226 para_id:00000C9A style:Normal -->
Harvick and Arrek were sparring in the center of the ring. The two men moved with brutal precision, their wooden training blades a blur as they exchanged blows. Harvick’s stance was grounded and economical, but Arrek fought like a fire barely contained—every movement aggressive, honed, fast.

<!-- paragraph:3227 para_id:00000C9B style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath caught as she watched a particularly rapid exchange. Arrek twisted out of the way of a low strike, shirt clinging to his back, soaked and taut across his shoulders. The flicker of his tattoos—inked lines vanishing beneath damp fabric—stood out against pale skin. Her stomach did something unexpected and entirely unhelpful.

<!-- paragraph:3228 para_id:00000C9C style:Normal -->
She blinked and shook her head hard. “Aren’t you supposed to be in the treasury chamber?” she called, her voice sharper—and louder—than she’d intended.

<!-- paragraph:3229 para_id:00000C9D style:Normal -->
Arrek turned just in time for Harvick’s wooden sword to crack against his ribs.

<!-- paragraph:3230 para_id:00000C9E style:Normal -->
“Every time,” he muttered, dropping to one knee with a grimace.

<!-- paragraph:3231 para_id:00000C9F style:Normal -->
There was a brief silence, then scattered laughter and groans from the onlookers. Coins changed hands. Arguments flared about whether or not the hit counted as a win. Harvick offered Arrek a hand, barely suppressing a grin.

<!-- paragraph:3232 para_id:00000CA0 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms. “I see the treasury’s in good hands.”

<!-- paragraph:3233 para_id:00000CA1 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s gaze locked with hers—steady, unreadable, intense in the way it always was when he looked at her. Maya felt the heat rise to her face and forced herself to keep her eyes firmly on his, refusing to glance down again.

<!-- paragraph:3234 para_id:00000CA2 style:Normal -->
“There’s no rule against exercising during the lunch hour,” he said, brushing sweat-soaked hair back from his brow.

<!-- paragraph:3235 para_id:00000CA3 style:Normal -->
“Oh, is that so?” Maya snapped—sharper than she meant. “And will you be bathing before returning to work, or are your co-workers going to suffer through your noble sacrifice in the form of an over-crowded barracks stench?”

<!-- paragraph:3236 para_id:00000CA4 style:Normal -->
Arrek raised an eyebrow, then discreetly lifted an arm and sniffed. His mouth twitched slightly, a betrayed expression flickering across his face before he smoothed it over. “I didn’t realize your duties extended to scheduling my hygiene alongside my treasury duties.”

<!-- paragraph:3237 para_id:00000CA5 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth, then caught sight of the lingering onlookers still not-so-subtly pretending they weren’t listening. She pressed her lips together, adjusted her posture, and adopted a more diplomatic tone—though her jaw remained tight.

<!-- paragraph:3238 para_id:00000CA6 style:Normal -->
“Selwin wanted to thank you,” she said crisply.

<!-- paragraph:3239 para_id:00000CA7 style:Normal -->
Selwin stepped forward but gave her a curious look, then turned toward Arrek. Instead of the solemn gratitude she’d expected, he blurted, “Can you teach me how to fight with a sword?”

<!-- paragraph:3240 para_id:00000CA8 style:Normal -->
From behind, Harvick gave an offended grunt.

<!-- paragraph:3241 para_id:00000CA9 style:Normal -->
Arrek blinked, clearly caught off guard. He looked down at Selwin, then said slowly, “From what I saw… I don’t think you’re going to need anything like that.”

<!-- paragraph:3242 para_id:00000CAA style:Normal -->
There was a flicker in his eyes—something Maya couldn’t quite place. Not fear, not admiration. Maybe caution. Maybe grief. She couldn’t tell.

<!-- paragraph:3243 para_id:00000CAB style:Normal -->
Selwin squared his shoulders. “One day I’m going to be the most powerful magus ever. Even stronger than Maya. Even stronger than Papa. Then I’ll keep everyone safe. And happy.”

<!-- paragraph:3244 para_id:00000CAC style:Normal -->
Arrek crouched down until he was level with Selwin, meeting his eyes directly.

<!-- paragraph:3245 para_id:00000CAD style:Normal -->
“If that day comes,” he said, voice low but firm, “I hope you’ll still remember what it felt like to be small. To need people to be kind to you.”

<!-- paragraph:3246 para_id:00000CAE style:Normal -->
Selwin nodded solemnly.

<!-- paragraph:3247 para_id:00000CAF style:Normal -->
Arrek stood again. For a moment his gaze lingered on Maya’s, that impossible current tightening between them like a drawn wire. Her heart thudded once, sharp and unwelcome.

<!-- paragraph:3248 para_id:00000CB0 style:Normal -->
Then something shifted.

<!-- paragraph:3249 para_id:00000CB1 style:Normal -->
The courtyard peeled away like paper curling in flame, and Maya was falling—not through space, but through meaning. Through possibility. Her body stood still, but her senses unraveled, stretched thin across a vastness she couldn’t comprehend.

<!-- paragraph:3250 para_id:00000CB2 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s face was still in front of her, but it fractured—no, not fractured, expanded, layered into impossible depth. She saw him as a regal figure wreathed in sunlight, radiating certainty, purpose, his presence like a standard raised against the dark. Then, overlaid and simultaneous, he was cloaked in shadow, eyes alight with rage, ink dripping from his hands like spilled wine.

<!-- paragraph:3251 para_id:00000CB3 style:Normal -->
Time wasn’t moving. Or it was moving in all directions at once.

<!-- paragraph:3252 para_id:00000CB4 style:Normal -->
Everything about him—his power, his danger, his pain—rushed toward her like a tidal wave, but none of it resolved. There was too much. Too many of him. A lattice of futures flickered at the edges of her mind, unreadable and immense. She couldn’t breathe.

<!-- paragraph:3253 para_id:00000CB5 style:Normal -->
Then Selwin suddenly jerked upright as if remembering something important and blurted, “Oh—thank you!”

<!-- paragraph:3254 para_id:00000CB6 style:Normal -->
His voice cut through the distortion like a bell. Maya reeled back into herself.

<!-- paragraph:3255 para_id:00000CB7 style:Normal -->
Arrek turned toward Selwin with a flicker of surprise, then looked back at her—brows slightly drawn, concern slipping past his usual guardedness.

<!-- paragraph:3256 para_id:00000CB8 style:Normal -->
“Are you all right?” he asked, his voice low.

<!-- paragraph:3257 para_id:00000CB9 style:Normal -->
“I’m fine,” she managed, though the word felt like someone else had said it.

<!-- paragraph:3258 para_id:00000CBA style:Normal -->
Arrek didn’t move at first. His brow furrowed slightly, like he wanted to say something more—ask something more—but the moment stretched too long. Their eyes met again, and for just a heartbeat, the tension between them felt like a question neither of them knew how to phrase.

<!-- paragraph:3259 para_id:00000CBB style:Normal -->
Then he broke the gaze, turning to Selwin. “It was good to see you. Don’t be a stranger.” Arrek turned to her and gave an awkward half bow that was a touch too formal. “Lady Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:3260 para_id:00000CBC style:Normal -->
And with that, he turned and walked off—toward the baths, Maya noted with a flicker of satisfaction.

<!-- paragraph:3261 para_id:00000CBD style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3262 para_id:00000CBE style:Normal -->
By the time they returned to the infirmary, Selwin’s steps had slowed, the earlier energy giving way to something quieter—more thoughtful. When Niall appeared at the door, Maya handed her brother off with a few murmured reassurances, watching as Selwin straightened his shoulders and walked inside beside their father.

<!-- paragraph:3263 para_id:00000CBF style:Normal -->
She didn’t follow.

<!-- paragraph:3264 para_id:00000CC0 style:Normal -->
Too many things weren’t adding up. The sigil. Her visions. The codex. Whatever Selwin had manifested, it didn’t match any of the known lineages—not hers, not Niall’s, and she wasn’t going to get anywhere asking Niall about Elric and Alara.

<!-- paragraph:3265 para_id:00000CC1 style:Normal -->
She turned sharply down the outer corridor, already drawing a folded slip of parchment from her sleeve. “Send a runner to find Alden,” she told Erynn, pressing it into her hand. “He’s to meet me in the records annex. Don’t wait for a reply. Just go.”

<!-- paragraph:3266 para_id:00000CC2 style:Normal -->
Erynn hesitated a half-breath, then nodded and turned away.

<!-- paragraph:3267 para_id:00000CC3 style:Normal -->
Maya kept walking. Her boots echoed against the stone, steady and fast.

<!-- paragraph:3268 para_id:00000CC4 style:Normal -->
If there was a pattern, a precedent, a buried piece of lore—she would find it. And if anyone still breathing remembered enough of the old bloodlines and what lay buried in them, it would be Alden.

<!-- paragraph:3269 para_id:00000CC5 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3270 para_id:00000CC6 style:Normal -->
The records annex wasn’t a library so much as a graveyard of good intentions. A dozen different cataloging systems competed for dominance, none victorious, and the scent of dust and beeswax hung thick in the air. Crates were stacked in precarious towers, each bearing hand-scrawled labels like Arcanalysis—Misc. or Clade Histories (Fragmented). Shelves groaned under the weight of books and loose scrolls, and even the better-organized sections had the look of barely-contained chaos.

<!-- paragraph:3271 para_id:00000CC7 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped inside and let the door drift shut behind her. The annex clerk didn’t look up at first—too absorbed in untangling a curled scrap of parchment from a sheaf twice its size. He was ancient in the way some scholars became, all folded skin and stooped spine, with wispy white hair and a quill that trembled when he wrote.

<!-- paragraph:3272 para_id:00000CC8 style:Normal -->
“Genealogical records,” Maya said, striding up to the desk.

<!-- paragraph:3273 para_id:00000CC9 style:Normal -->
The clerk blinked up at her, peering as though her features might arrange themselves into something legible if he stared long enough. Then, with a grunt, he shuffled to his feet and began moving through the clutter with surprising confidence—though his muttered commentary (“no, not that one,” “someone really should fix that bracket,” “should’ve retired fifty years ago”) didn’t inspire much reassurance.

<!-- paragraph:3274 para_id:00000CCA style:Normal -->
Maya glanced back toward the door as they passed through a sagging wooden arch. “Wait outside,” she said to her guards, and when one of them hesitated, she gave the one Erynn had been shamelessly flirting with a wry look. “Erynn will be along soon. You can wait for her.”

<!-- paragraph:3275 para_id:00000CCB style:Normal -->
That seemed to settle it.

<!-- paragraph:3276 para_id:00000CCC style:Normal -->
The clerk led her deeper into the annex, winding past half-collapsed shelving units and crates that looked one tremor away from toppling. The arcane lanterns overhead buzzed faintly, their glow barely illuminating the narrowing corridor. Dust motes danced in the air like pale insects.

<!-- paragraph:3277 para_id:00000CCD style:Normal -->
After what felt like far too long, he stopped beside a shelf groaning under the weight of unmarked wooden boxes. He squinted at one, tapped the label—birth records?—with a nod, and then shuffled back the way he’d come, muttering something about “intercalated lineages” and “the war ruining everything.”

<!-- paragraph:3278 para_id:00000CCE style:Normal -->
Maya eyed the box. It was broader than she expected and deceptively dense. As she stooped to lift it, her arms strained against the weight, and she exhaled sharply. With a controlled breath, she summoned her Shroud—cool force wrapping around her limbs, bracing muscle and bone with invisible reinforcement. The chill of it stirred faintly in the air, but she kept it tamped down, focused on maintaining balance and integrity without freezing the box through. Buoyed by the added strength, she hefted the box and carried it to a worn oak table, setting it down with a heavy, echoing thud.

<!-- paragraph:3279 para_id:00000CCF style:Normal -->
The lid creaked as she opened it, revealing a mess of brittle scrolls, folded records, and bound fragments of leather-stitched ledgers.

<!-- paragraph:3280 para_id:00000CD0 style:Normal -->
The records were worse than she expected.

<!-- paragraph:3281 para_id:00000CD1 style:Normal -->
Maya sat hunched over the warped table, sleeves rolled up, hands blackened with ink and dust. The box had seemed promising, but the contents were little more than a barely sorted mess of parchment slips, brittle name ledgers, and haphazard notations in half a dozen styles of calligraphy. Still, patterns were emerging. Slowly.

<!-- paragraph:3282 para_id:00000CD2 style:Normal -->
She brushed back a loose strand of hair and squinted at the record in front of her. Archeon. A name she recognized. Yet the family’s clade lineage seemed surprisingly short—only a few generations traced, and even those not with the formal notations of a long-established line. Several entries simply ended with a question mark.

<!-- paragraph:3283 para_id:00000CD3 style:Normal -->
Not Presented? one read beside a supposed second son.

<!-- paragraph:3284 para_id:00000CD4 style:Normal -->
Presumed deceased, another noted with no source or verification.

<!-- paragraph:3285 para_id:00000CD5 style:Normal -->
And it wasn’t just Archeon. House after house, name after name, was followed by uncertainty—rumors of births with no matching Presentations, notes of unconfirmed heir, or no surviving issue. Maya leaned back slightly, rubbing her temple.

<!-- paragraph:3286 para_id:00000CD6 style:Normal -->
Either someone had been recording every whispered pregnancy in the citadel, or far more High Clade children died than she’d ever realized.

<!-- paragraph:3287 para_id:00000CD7 style:Normal -->
She shuffled the next handful of papers aside and paused as she reached a ledger inked with unusual density. The top read Drakhal, and the margins were crammed with symbols. The sheer number of question-marked birth notations under their crest was staggering—and beside many of them, short phrases in shorthand: birth contract issued, outside clade, maternal line unconfirmed.

<!-- paragraph:3288 para_id:00000CD8 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned.

<!-- paragraph:3289 para_id:00000CD9 style:Normal -->
She’d known High Clade houses used intermarriage and fertility contracts to maintain power. But the breadth of it… the desperation of it... surprised her. Every contract that didn’t result in a Presented child faded into obscurity. But the moment one did, the balance of status and favor could shift overnight. Those men and women—un-Presented magi who were never officially recognized by their houses—could suddenly become valuable for what they’d produced.

<!-- paragraph:3290 para_id:00000CDA style:Normal -->
A cold, uneasy feeling settled beneath her ribs.

<!-- paragraph:3291 para_id:00000CDB style:Normal -->
She was still staring at the page, absently tracing a smudged Drakhal seal with one inkstained fingertip, when she heard the soft shift of boots on stone.

<!-- paragraph:3292 para_id:00000CDC style:Normal -->
“You know,” came Alden’s quiet voice behind her, “when Elric was younger, he wanted to outlaw those contracts.”

<!-- paragraph:3293 para_id:00000CDD style:Normal -->
Maya turned, surprised—she hadn’t heard him approach.

<!-- paragraph:3294 para_id:00000CDE style:Normal -->
Alden stepped closer, the faintest edge of weariness in his posture as he leaned over the table to look at the records. “He said it was slavery. For the ones offering the contract, and for the children born because of them.”

<!-- paragraph:3295 para_id:00000CDF style:Normal -->
Maya studied his expression, but as usual, it gave nothing away. His voice held no bitterness. Just remembrance.

<!-- paragraph:3296 para_id:00000CE0 style:Normal -->
She glanced back at the records. At all the nameless children and ghost-lineages inked into history, only to disappear again.

<!-- paragraph:3297 para_id:00000CE1 style:Normal -->
Maya glanced up from the records, her brow furrowed. “So why didn’t it happen? If Elric was so respected—why didn’t anyone listen?”

<!-- paragraph:3298 para_id:00000CE2 style:Normal -->
Alden’s gaze didn’t move from the pages in front of him. “Oh, they listened,” he said quietly. “He was one of the most widely read magi of his time. People debated his work in forums, taught it in academies. His writing on this topic was particularly eloquent.”

<!-- paragraph:3299 para_id:00000CE3 style:Normal -->
He paused, running a finger down the edge of one particularly frayed page.

<!-- paragraph:3300 para_id:00000CE4 style:Normal -->
“But the loudest protests didn’t come from the ruling Clades,” he went on. “They came from the ones offering the contracts.”

<!-- paragraph:3301 para_id:00000CE5 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “The ones who were being exploited?”

<!-- paragraph:3302 para_id:00000CE6 style:Normal -->
“They didn’t always see it that way. Some did. But many of them…” He exhaled, slow. “They were more afraid of what might replace the system if it vanished. Without contracts, they had no guarantees. No leverage. No reason for their Clades to protect them if something went wrong. Elric wanted to tear out the rot, but they were afraid it was the only thing keeping the roof from falling in.”

<!-- paragraph:3303 para_id:00000CE7 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the parchment in front of her.

<!-- paragraph:3304 para_id:00000CE8 style:Normal -->
“So he just gave up?”

<!-- paragraph:3305 para_id:00000CE9 style:Normal -->
Alden finally met her eyes. “He couldn’t liberate people who didn’t want to be liberated.” His voice was gentle, but the weight of it landed like a stone. “He thought change had to come from choice, not coercion.”

<!-- paragraph:3306 para_id:00000CEA style:Normal -->
Maya was quiet a long moment. Then, softly, “How did he go from that… to whatever he became? Whatever made Niall—”

<!-- paragraph:3307 para_id:00000CEB style:Normal -->
She didn’t finish the sentence. She didn’t need to.

<!-- paragraph:3308 para_id:00000CEC style:Normal -->
Alden’s eyes dropped again, and for the first time in a long while, she saw something flicker in his expression. Not pain exactly. But the ghost of something worn down to its last thread.

<!-- paragraph:3309 para_id:00000CED style:Normal -->
“It’s never just one thing,” he said. “Sometimes people don’t break. They erode.”

<!-- paragraph:3310 para_id:00000CEE style:Normal -->
The silence stretched between them, taut and strange. Then Alden shifted, pulling them back to the present with a subtle, practiced ease.

<!-- paragraph:3311 para_id:00000CEF style:Normal -->
“You won’t find much here,” he said, tapping the box. “The High Clades don’t publish records of their un-Presented children. And birth contracts are held privately, usually by the receiving party’s archivist.”

<!-- paragraph:3312 para_id:00000CF0 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Then what is all this?”

<!-- paragraph:3313 para_id:00000CF1 style:Normal -->
“A project I had one of my aides work on. I wanted to see if we could predict the rate of Presentations in a Clade based on how many contracts they signed.”

<!-- paragraph:3314 para_id:00000CF2 style:Normal -->
“And?”

<!-- paragraph:3315 para_id:00000CF3 style:Normal -->
He gave a dry smile. “Useless. Too much noise. The data’s full of gaps, inconsistent notations, and, frankly, a lot of fiction.”

<!-- paragraph:3316 para_id:00000CF4 style:Normal -->
She exhaled, frustrated.

<!-- paragraph:3317 para_id:00000CF5 style:Normal -->
Alden added, “And before you ask—no, there’s nothing in there about our line. Niall never used contracts. And there are no un-Presented Rezin children, other than Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:3318 para_id:00000CF6 style:Normal -->
.Maya set the half-curled parchment aside, rubbing her temple with one knuckle. “Do we have anything more than this? Our own family, I mean. You said these are just public records, but if anyone would know more…”

<!-- paragraph:3319 para_id:00000CF7 style:Normal -->
Alden looked at her, his expression unreadable. “What records do you think there are?”

<!-- paragraph:3320 para_id:00000CF8 style:Normal -->
“You said Niall never had any birth contracts. But he married Alara. She was from Naeroth, wasn’t she?”

<!-- paragraph:3321 para_id:00000CF9 style:Normal -->
“She was.”

<!-- paragraph:3322 para_id:00000CFA style:Normal -->
“And?”

<!-- paragraph:3323 para_id:00000CFB style:Normal -->
“And good luck getting anything from Naeroth.” Alden leaned against the edge of the shelving, arms folded. “They barely speak to Elarion now. Haven’t for years. They’re… not isolationist in the traditional sense, just extremely selective. Information, diplomacy, even bloodlines—they hold all of it close.”

<!-- paragraph:3324 para_id:00000CFC style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “So they wouldn’t have sent any documentation when she married in?”

<!-- paragraph:3325 para_id:00000CFD style:Normal -->
“They might have. If they did, Elric would’ve had it.”

<!-- paragraph:3326 para_id:00000CFE style:Normal -->
Maya’s brow furrowed. “You said Elric kept his own records.”

<!-- paragraph:3327 para_id:00000CFF style:Normal -->
“He did. He was fascinated by lineage. Obsessed, even. Traced all sorts of inherited sigils, divergences, strain compatibilities. But after his death, I couldn’t find any of it. He must have destroyed it—or hidden it somewhere I never thought to look.”

<!-- paragraph:3328 para_id:00000D00 style:Normal -->
“Why would he destroy it?”

<!-- paragraph:3329 para_id:00000D01 style:Normal -->
Alden didn’t answer that. His gaze slid to the half-open crate of records on the table. “He had one birth contract, that I know of. But he never shared the details. Not with me.”

<!-- paragraph:3330 para_id:00000D02 style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled through her nose, frustration flickering. “So either we dig up something from Elric’s secret cache, or we find a way into Naeroth’s archives.”

<!-- paragraph:3331 para_id:00000D03 style:Normal -->
“If they even keep them where anyone could find them,” Alden said dryly. “And if they’d let us see them. Alara was one of their best—gifted, sharp, trained for contact outside their borders. But even she never spoke much about Naeroth. Not even to me.”

<!-- paragraph:3332 para_id:00000D04 style:Normal -->
“Niall never speaks of it either,” Maya said, quieter now.

<!-- paragraph:3333 para_id:00000D05 style:Normal -->
“No. I don’t think he’s trying to hide something so much as… trying to leave it buried.”

<!-- paragraph:3334 para_id:00000D06 style:Normal -->
A sudden alarm tone split the air—a deep, oscillating blare that made the stone floor hum beneath their feet.

<!-- paragraph:3335 para_id:00000D07 style:Normal -->
Alden’s head snapped up from the parchment in Maya’s hands. “That’s the Registry. It’s in active mode.”

<!-- paragraph:3336 para_id:00000D08 style:Normal -->
She was already rising. “But no one was scheduled to—”

<!-- paragraph:3337 para_id:00000D09 style:Normal -->
Alden didn’t answer. His face had gone pale.

<!-- paragraph:3338 para_id:00000D0A style:Normal -->
Maya shoved the chair back and bolted for the door. “Come on.”

<!-- paragraph:3339 para_id:00000D0B style:Normal -->
Her guards, already alert from the alarm, fell in step around her as she moved. Erynn caught up just behind, hand brushing the dagger at her hip.

<!-- paragraph:3340 para_id:00000D0C style:Normal -->
All around them, Citadel staff and soldiers were pouring out of offices, rushing for the walls or fallback positions. Maya pushed through the confusion, heading for the Registry chamber at full sprint. Her breath stayed even, her pace relentless. Her Shroud flickered to life as she ran—invisible pressure wrapping her limbs, bolstering her speed and focus. Cold rippled through her skin, but she forced it down again, keeping the signature of her power just below flaring.

<!-- paragraph:3341 para_id:00000D0D style:Normal -->
They reached the Registry chamber moments later. The outer doors were already ajar. Inside, the arcane staff were clustered around the projection dais, a glimmering map of the Citadel glowing overhead in pale blue. A single crimson flicker moved slowly across the map’s courtyard—unnamed, unregistered.

<!-- paragraph:3342 para_id:00000D0E style:Normal -->
“Where’s Niall?” Maya barked, striding to the projection. “Why isn’t he here?”

<!-- paragraph:3343 para_id:00000D0F style:Normal -->
“He’s been summoned,” one of the operators said, eyes wide. “We—we thought it had to be some kind of error.”

<!-- paragraph:3344 para_id:00000D10 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the projection. A single aura. No designation.

<!-- paragraph:3345 para_id:00000D11 style:Normal -->
The Registry should have purged it instantly.

<!-- paragraph:3346 para_id:00000D12 style:Normal -->
Alden appeared behind her. “Unless they have a Shroud,” he muttered. “A powerful one.”

<!-- paragraph:3347 para_id:00000D13 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t wait. She spun and took off again, Shroud flaring more brightly now as she darted through corridors, out past the inner gate, into the central courtyard. The cold clung to her skin like a second breath.

<!-- paragraph:3348 para_id:00000D14 style:Normal -->
She heard it before she saw it—crackling bursts of arcane energy, exploding against something just ahead. A squad of guards were holding position, weapons raised, but not advancing. The sky above the Citadel shimmered as the Registry fired again—pure arcs of energy slamming into something in the courtyard center, only to spark and fizzle as they hit a thin, transparent shimmer. The air around it vibrated with tension, and the blasts left no mark.

<!-- paragraph:3349 para_id:00000D15 style:Normal -->
In the center of the chaos, a man strolled forward at a slow, unbothered pace.

<!-- paragraph:3350 para_id:00000D16 style:Normal -->
He wore fine clothes, tailored and embroidered with subtle Clade markings. His mahogany hair was loose around his shoulders, and his posture was relaxed, almost bored. The high cheekbones, chiseled jawline, and faint smirk left no doubt.

<!-- paragraph:3351 para_id:00000D17 style:Normal -->
Caius Drakhal.

<!-- paragraph:3352 para_id:00000D18 style:Normal -->
He looked directly at Maya and offered a cheerful wave just as another bolt slammed into his Shroud with a noise like thunder on stone.

<!-- paragraph:3353 para_id:00000D19 style:Normal -->
She skidded to a halt. “Get a runner to Alden,” she shouted to one of her guards, eyes locked on the projection behind her. “Have him switch the Registry to passive mode before it burns itself out!”

<!-- paragraph:3354 para_id:00000D1A style:Normal -->
The man saluted and vanished back into the corridor.

<!-- paragraph:3355 para_id:00000D1B style:Normal -->
Maya turned back to the courtyard, her Shroud coiling tighter around her in instinctive defense. “Caius!” she shouted. “What are you doing?”

<!-- paragraph:3356 para_id:00000D1C style:Normal -->
He cupped a hand to his ear theatrically, as another blast of energy detonated just above his shoulder. His coat rippled but he didn’t flinch. He mouthed something at her—unreadable—and shrugged.

<!-- paragraph:3357 para_id:00000D1D style:Normal -->
The bolts were slowing now. One… then a pause… then another.

<!-- paragraph:3358 para_id:00000D1E style:Normal -->
Not a good sign.

<!-- paragraph:3359 para_id:00000D1F style:Normal -->
Maya’s pulse quickened. Either the Registry was being powered down—

<!-- paragraph:3360 para_id:00000D20 style:Normal -->
Or it was nearly depleted.

<!-- paragraph:3361 para_id:00000D21 style:Normal -->
Another flash of arcane fire burst against Caius’s Shroud—and this time, he didn’t even bother to react. He stood there, looking around as if admiring the weather.

<!-- paragraph:3362 para_id:00000D22 style:Normal -->
Then, silence.

<!-- paragraph:3363 para_id:00000D23 style:Normal -->
The Registry ceased firing.

<!-- paragraph:3364 para_id:00000D24 style:Normal -->
Maya stood very still. Her Shroud whispered against her skin.

<!-- paragraph:3365 para_id:00000D25 style:Normal -->
Caius smiled again.

<!-- paragraph:3366 para_id:00000D26 style:Normal -->
The courtyard filled with the sound of wind.

<!-- paragraph:3367 para_id:00000D27 style:Normal -->
“What is wrong with you?” Maya snapped, stalking forward. Her Shroud still shimmered faintly around her, crackling at the edges. “We thought we were under attack.”

<!-- paragraph:3368 para_id:00000D28 style:Normal -->
Caius blinked innocently, hands held just slightly apart, as if to show peaceful intentions. “I sent a letter,” he said lightly. “Days ago. Maybe longer. You never answered.”

<!-- paragraph:3369 para_id:00000D29 style:Normal -->
Maya flushed despite herself. She had forgotten about his letter in the face of her mother’s codex. She’d shoved it in a drawer, intending to get around to it at some point.

<!-- paragraph:3370 para_id:00000D2A style:Normal -->
Caius tilted his head, clearly enjoying himself. His hair caught the sunlight, the soft mahogany waves curling just so against a jaw that looked like it had been chiseled into place by someone with opinions. His sleeveless magus vest clung to his frame, accentuating lean muscle and collarbones that gleamed with a light sheen of effort—or artful sweat.

<!-- paragraph:3371 para_id:00000D2B style:Normal -->
“I assumed your silence meant I could come when I pleased,” he went on. “Besides, didn’t your parents meet on a battlefield? Very dramatic. I was hoping to capture some of the same spirit.”

<!-- paragraph:3372 para_id:00000D2C style:Normal -->
“My parents met during an actual war,” Maya said, her voice rising. “You’re just breaking into my house.”

<!-- paragraph:3373 para_id:00000D2D style:Normal -->
Caius blinked again, as though genuinely surprised she hadn’t found this whole thing charming. A flicker of confusion passed across his expression, quickly replaced with a grin he probably thought would smooth everything over.

<!-- paragraph:3374 para_id:00000D2E style:Normal -->
“My apologies, truly,” he said, bowing his head slightly. “If my grand romantic gesture came across as… incendiary.” He glanced meaningfully at the still-scorched paving stones around him. “I just wanted to talk to you again. That’s all.”

<!-- paragraph:3375 para_id:00000D2F style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth, unsure what to even say, when she felt a familiar pressure on her shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:3376 para_id:00000D30 style:Normal -->
She turned.

<!-- paragraph:3377 para_id:00000D31 style:Normal -->
Niall stood beside her, silent as death. His face was unreadable—blank and carved of stone—but the edges of his irises shimmered with flickers of restrained lightning. His long overcoat flared slightly in the breeze.

<!-- paragraph:3378 para_id:00000D32 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Niall said, voice low and toneless, “you’re here. Talk.”

<!-- paragraph:3379 para_id:00000D33 style:Normal -->
There was nothing welcoming in it.

<!-- paragraph:3380 para_id:00000D34 style:Normal -->
Caius turned slowly to face him. He gave a theatrical blink, then smiled. “I like your coat,” he said. “Is it custom work? Who’s your tailor?”

<!-- paragraph:3381 para_id:00000D35 style:Normal -->
The words were harmless. But the tone… Maya couldn’t pin it exactly—playful, mocking, pointed? There was something layered underneath.

<!-- paragraph:3382 para_id:00000D36 style:Normal -->
To her surprise, Niall seemed caught slightly off guard by the comment. A faint crease appeared between his brows. He didn’t answer.

<!-- paragraph:3383 para_id:00000D37 style:Normal -->
Maya took the opportunity to cut back in. “What do you want, Caius?” she demanded. “Because if this is your idea of diplomacy, it’s a miracle your Clade still has any allies.”

<!-- paragraph:3384 para_id:00000D38 style:Normal -->
For a moment, there was something else in Caius’s eyes—a shadow that passed too quickly to name. Maya almost missed it. Irritation? Resentment? It vanished so fast she might’ve imagined it.

<!-- paragraph:3385 para_id:00000D39 style:Normal -->
Still, his tone remained smooth as glass.

<!-- paragraph:3386 para_id:00000D3A style:Normal -->
“I simply think,” he said, brushing an invisible speck from his vest, “that Rezin and Drakhal—being, after all, the two strongest Clades—might benefit from… warmer relations.” He met Maya’s eyes again, and this time the look was unmistakable: bold, possessive, hungry. “Think of what we could accomplish.”

<!-- paragraph:3387 para_id:00000D3B style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach turned, though whether from anger or unease she couldn’t say. She remembered the look in his eyes during her Presentation ceremony—the flicker of something covetous beneath the applause. Like her victory had cost him something. Or revealed something about him he didn’t want seen.

<!-- paragraph:3388 para_id:00000D3C style:Normal -->
“What would that even mean?” she asked, keeping her voice flat.

<!-- paragraph:3389 para_id:00000D3D style:Normal -->
Caius smiled, but it didn’t reach his eyes. He held up his hands, deflecting. “Let’s not rush the conversation. Perhaps we could speak again soon. In a less… dramatic setting.” He gestured vaguely at the scorch marks still lining the courtyard. “After all, Elric and Evand worked quite well together, didn’t they?”

<!-- paragraph:3390 para_id:00000D3E style:Normal -->
Maya’s breath caught. She opened her mouth, intending to ask—what did he mean by that?

<!-- paragraph:3391 para_id:00000D3F style:Normal -->
But Niall stepped forward instead.

<!-- paragraph:3392 para_id:00000D40 style:Normal -->
“Don’t ever speak Elric’s name again,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:3393 para_id:00000D41 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t loud. It didn’t have to be.

<!-- paragraph:3394 para_id:00000D42 style:Normal -->
The air felt heavier around him, the faint shimmer of lightning barely contained. Even the guards nearby shifted uneasily, as if something under their feet had changed and they couldn’t name it.

<!-- paragraph:3395 para_id:00000D43 style:Normal -->
Caius’s mouth twitched. For just a heartbeat, the mask slipped. Rage flared behind his polished expression—raw and startling—but then it was gone, replaced by an exaggerated calm.

<!-- paragraph:3396 para_id:00000D44 style:Normal -->
He glanced upward. “Perhaps next time,” he said quietly, “we won’t meet beneath the sky.”

<!-- paragraph:3397 para_id:00000D45 style:Normal -->
He gave Maya an elegant, theatrical bow, the kind meant for crowded ballrooms, not scorched courtyards under duress.

<!-- paragraph:3398 para_id:00000D46 style:Normal -->
Then he turned and walked, unhurried, back through the still-smoking gate.

<!-- paragraph:3399 para_id:00000D47 style:Normal -->
After Caius vanished through the gates, Maya turned toward Niall.

<!-- paragraph:3400 para_id:00000D48 style:Normal -->
“What was that all about?”

<!-- paragraph:3401 para_id:00000D49 style:Normal -->
Niall’s gaze remained on the empty space where the Drakhal heir had stood. “The Drakhals have a talent for getting under my skin.”

<!-- paragraph:3402 para_id:00000D4A style:Normal -->
His voice was even, but Maya caught it—something tight in his jaw, the edge of genuine worry etched faintly into the lines around his mouth. That, more than anything, startled her. Not fury. Not hollow resolve. Just concern.

<!-- paragraph:3403 para_id:00000D4B style:Normal -->
Erynn stepped lightly to Maya’s side. “It’s nearly time for the end-of-day supervisor meeting,” she said in a low voice. “You know they’ll be wondering what happened.”

<!-- paragraph:3404 para_id:00000D4C style:Normal -->
Maya nodded absently, then turned back to Niall. “I don’t know what’s going on,” she said quietly. “But go be with Selwin. Don’t lock yourself away again. Don’t brood.”

<!-- paragraph:3405 para_id:00000D4D style:Normal -->
Niall met her eyes. For a moment, the usual weight behind them seemed to recede. He gave a small nod—muted, but real.

<!-- paragraph:3406 para_id:00000D4E style:Normal -->
She turned, gathering her guards—still winded from sprinting across the Citadel in full armor. As they made their way back through the corridors, Maya adjusted her tunic, smoothing her expression as best she could. The weight of command settled back onto her shoulders like a cloak she didn’t remember removing.

<!-- paragraph:3407 para_id:00000D4F style:Normal -->
By the time she entered the meeting chamber, the supervisors were already seated. A low murmur of conversation quieted instantly as she stepped inside.

<!-- paragraph:3408 para_id:00000D50 style:Normal -->
She didn’t sit.

<!-- paragraph:3409 para_id:00000D51 style:Normal -->
“I’m sure you all have the same question,” she said, folding her hands behind her back. “So let’s start there. No—this wasn’t an attack. If it had been, I wouldn’t be standing here having a routine meeting.”

<!-- paragraph:3410 para_id:00000D52 style:Normal -->
There were a few quiet exhales of relief. Some still looked uneasy.

<!-- paragraph:3411 para_id:00000D53 style:Normal -->
She moved briskly into the day’s reports, but the tension lingered like smoke. The numbers, the logistics—none of it was landing. Finally, she paused, eyes scanning the room.

<!-- paragraph:3412 para_id:00000D54 style:Normal -->
“What is it?”

<!-- paragraph:3413 para_id:00000D55 style:Normal -->
Marek, the Group One supervisor, shifted in his seat and spoke up.

<!-- paragraph:3414 para_id:00000D56 style:Normal -->
“My workers aren’t used to things like this,” he said. “Alarms. Lockdowns. They need something to take back with them—something real. Otherwise, they’ll spend all day glancing over their shoulders instead of focusing on the zä lattices.”

<!-- paragraph:3415 para_id:00000D57 style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled. Not irritated—just tired.

<!-- paragraph:3416 para_id:00000D58 style:Normal -->
“We had an… unwanted guest,” she said. “A High Clade magus decided to wander into the Citadel without warning. He set off the Registry.”

<!-- paragraph:3417 para_id:00000D59 style:Normal -->
Marek’s brows rose, but he said nothing.

<!-- paragraph:3418 para_id:00000D5A style:Normal -->
“I assure you,” Maya continued, “both Niall and I made it very clear that it won’t happen again. No one was in any danger—other than the magus himself.”

<!-- paragraph:3419 para_id:00000D5B style:Normal -->
That got a few suppressed smiles. The room eased, just slightly.

<!-- paragraph:3420 para_id:00000D5C style:Normal -->
She picked up the next ledger from the stack before her.

<!-- paragraph:3421 para_id:00000D5D style:Normal -->
“Now. Glyph recalibrations for the outer wards. Let’s get back to it.”

<!-- paragraph:3422 para_id:00000D5E style:Normal -->
The meeting concluded with a few last notes and quiet affirmations, the supervisors rising and filing out in pairs, murmuring reassurances they could bring back to their crews. Maya remained standing, the tension still humming faintly under her skin.

<!-- paragraph:3423 para_id:00000D5F style:Normal -->
Erynn stretched her arms overhead and gave a low sigh. “Did Caius literally come all the way here by himself?”

<!-- paragraph:3424 para_id:00000D60 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, still processing. “He must have. Just to make it a spectacle.” Her mouth twisted. “I can’t believe he thought that would be romantic.”

<!-- paragraph:3425 para_id:00000D61 style:Normal -->
A presence behind her made her spine stiffen. She turned, expecting another lingering supervisor—but it was Arrek, standing in the shadow of the open door. His eyes weren’t on her.

<!-- paragraph:3426 para_id:00000D62 style:Normal -->
He was staring at Erynn.

<!-- paragraph:3427 para_id:00000D63 style:Normal -->
His face was unreadable, expression carved from stone. But his fists were clenched so tightly at his sides that the skin across his knuckles had gone white.

<!-- paragraph:3428 para_id:00000D64 style:Normal -->
“Do you have a question?” Maya asked, careful not to let her voice rise.

<!-- paragraph:3429 para_id:00000D65 style:Normal -->
Arrek didn’t look at her. “No,” he said, the word clipped. Then he turned and walked away, the fabric of his shirt stiff over the tension in his shoulders.

<!-- paragraph:3430 para_id:00000D66 style:Normal -->
Maya watched him go, a frown pulling at her brow.

<!-- paragraph:3431 para_id:00000D67 style:Normal -->
She turned back to Erynn. “But I agree, that still seems weird. He walked all the way here? Did he really think this was going to go well for him?”

<!-- paragraph:3432 para_id:00000D68 style:Normal -->
Erynn shrugged, her voice light. “From what I’ve heard, Caius’s two favorite things are being famous and showing off. I’m sure he loved striding through the city with his Shroud blazing—every awestruck stare from the non-Clades feeding his ego.” She gave Maya a sly look. “Besides… a little bit of sweat looked pretty good on him.”

<!-- paragraph:3433 para_id:00000D69 style:Normal -->
Maya rolled her eyes and punched Erynn lightly on the arm. “He’s going to be a lot warmer than that if he tries something like this again.”

<!-- paragraph:3434 para_id:00000D6A style:Normal -->
Erynn just grinned.

<!-- paragraph:3438 para_id:00000D6E style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 20

<!-- paragraph:3439 para_id:00000D6F style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 20

<!-- paragraph:3441 para_id:00000D71 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3442 para_id:00000D72 style:Normal -->
The moment Davian rounded the corner, he pivoted sharply and picked up speed, striding down the hallway with long, forceful steps. His pulse beat in his throat like a drum. The edges of his vision blurred, thoughts collapsing inward, narrowing until there was only lethal intent.

<!-- paragraph:3443 para_id:00000D73 style:Normal -->
His fists clenched so tightly his knuckles ached. He barely noticed. The corridors were a blur of shadowed stone and torchlight. The calm rhythm he usually kept—his mask of patience and precision—was gone. Anger rippled under his skin, coiling around him like a Shroud, dark and cold.

<!-- paragraph:3444 para_id:00000D74 style:Normal -->
Near the barracks turn-off, a cloak hung from a wooden peg, carelessly dropped by someone off duty. Davian snatched it as he passed. No time to return to his quarters. No time for thought. He had Antithesis tucked at his hip and his boiling rage for armor. The cloak swirled around his shoulders as he pulled it on, too long and not his size, but it would hide his face in the dimming light. Good enough.

<!-- paragraph:3445 para_id:00000D75 style:Normal -->
He stalked toward the gate, eyes scanning the scorched earth ahead. Blackened patches of stone marked the path—residue from the Registry’s arcane retaliation, scattered like smudged charcoal across the outer courtyard. That way.

<!-- paragraph:3446 para_id:00000D76 style:Normal -->
One of the guards near the entrance glanced over as Davian approached. A stocky man with tired eyes and the easy slouch of someone who’d stood post too long, he straightened a little and frowned.

<!-- paragraph:3447 para_id:00000D77 style:Normal -->
“Arrek? You heading out?”

<!-- paragraph:3448 para_id:00000D78 style:Normal -->
Davian forced his mouth into something like a smile. “Just getting a drink.”

<!-- paragraph:3449 para_id:00000D79 style:Normal -->
The guard scratched at his beard. “Couple of us were talking about going out later—Inn of the Brass Moon. Want to come?”

<!-- paragraph:3450 para_id:00000D7A style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head. “I feel like drinking alone.”

<!-- paragraph:3451 para_id:00000D7B style:Normal -->
That got him another once-over. The guard’s gaze lingered on the hood, the obscuring cloak, the tension in his shoulders. He raised an eyebrow, and when he spoke again, his tone was knowingly lecherous. “Ah. Well. If that’s the kind of ‘drink’ you’re looking for, don’t let me stop you.”

<!-- paragraph:3452 para_id:00000D7C style:Normal -->
Davian grimaced. Tried to make it look sheepish. Said nothing.

<!-- paragraph:3453 para_id:00000D7D style:Normal -->
He tugged the hood down farther and stepped past, heart thudding against his ribs—not from nerves, but from the sheer velocity of his fury, driving him forward down the darkened road.

<!-- paragraph:3454 para_id:00000D7E style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t know how much of a lead Caius had, but from what he’d overheard Erynn say, it wouldn’t be long—he’d likely be keeping to the wider thoroughfares, soaking in every glance and whispered word like some strutting peacock. A High Clade magus walking through the common districts with his Shroud active wasn’t subtle. The city would part around him like water around a stone.

<!-- paragraph:3455 para_id:00000D7F style:Normal -->
Davian ducked into a side alley, vaulted a broken cart, and broke into a sprint.

<!-- paragraph:3456 para_id:00000D80 style:Normal -->
The narrow lane twisted between leaning brick walls and hanging washlines, but he mapped the city’s layout in his head—the arteries that fed into the Spoke roads, the places where he could make up ground. His boots pounded the uneven cobbles as he cut through the older parts of the city, the light dimming with each turn, the air growing sour with dust and old cooking grease. Most of these alleys never saw a patrol. His breath rasped in his ears. His heart pumped ice.

<!-- paragraph:3457 para_id:00000D81 style:Normal -->
He reached the edge of the Spoke and flattened himself against the wall, peering around the corner. The crowd there was thicker—marketgoers, messengers, beggars, and hawkers jostling along the thoroughfare. But through it, he saw the pattern he’d expected: a slow, spiraling ripple, as people instinctively moved aside. No screams, no panic. Just that primal need to yield to something bigger, brighter, more dangerous.

<!-- paragraph:3458 para_id:00000D82 style:Normal -->
Caius.

<!-- paragraph:3459 para_id:00000D83 style:Normal -->
He wasn’t far—maybe a hundred yards ahead and drifting casually, as though he was going for a stroll.

<!-- paragraph:3460 para_id:00000D84 style:Normal -->
Davian scanned the vendors lining the Spoke until he saw one he recognized—a blade merchant with far too many “antique” weapons for someone with no forge or smiths in sight. The man’s stand was all brass fittings and ostentatious polish, a front for the real business hidden behind its canvas siding: trade with the Veiled Clades, tools for the Concord, and things better left unnamed.

<!-- paragraph:3461 para_id:00000D85 style:Normal -->
Davian strode up, hood still drawn low. The man squinted at him from under his heavy brows, not moving from where he lounged behind the counter. Davian didn’t speak. He just raised one hand inside the cloak and signed low and fast.

<!-- paragraph:3462 para_id:00000D86 style:Normal -->
I need a mask. Now.

<!-- paragraph:3463 para_id:00000D87 style:Normal -->
The vendor’s eyes flicked over his shoulder toward the crowd. He followed the faint turbulence, the unnatural space being carved through the street like a blade slicing fabric. His fingers signed back under the counter.

<!-- paragraph:3464 para_id:00000D88 style:Normal -->
This is foolish. You will die. Others will die.

<!-- paragraph:3465 para_id:00000D89 style:Normal -->
Davian’s return gesture was sharp, resolute.

<!-- paragraph:3466 para_id:00000D8A style:Normal -->
I’ll do it with or without your help.

<!-- paragraph:3467 para_id:00000D8B style:Normal -->
He dropped three copper-haloed gold coins on the counter, the coins clinking too brightly against the dented brass.

<!-- paragraph:3468 para_id:00000D8C style:Normal -->
The vendor looked at him a moment longer, then cursed under his breath and reached into a box behind the stall wall. He passed a small satchel across the counter, its drawstrings neatly knotted.

<!-- paragraph:3469 para_id:00000D8D style:Normal -->
Don’t do it near here, the man signed.

<!-- paragraph:3470 para_id:00000D8E style:Normal -->
Then, without another word, he moved to the awning's edge, yanked the canvas down, and latched the front closed.

<!-- paragraph:3471 para_id:00000D8F style:Normal -->
Davian turned, walking away with the bag clutched tight beneath the cloak.

<!-- paragraph:3472 para_id:00000D90 style:Normal -->
Caius wasn’t hard to follow. The man moved through the streets like a torch carried high, the crowd parting before him in an instinctive arc of deference and awe. Davian trailed behind at a distance, slipping from doorway to stall to alley’s edge, his hand clenched tight around Antithesis beneath the borrowed cloak. His breath came shallow and fast.

<!-- paragraph:3473 para_id:00000D91 style:Normal -->
The dagger pulsed faintly against his palm—eager, almost.

<!-- paragraph:3474 para_id:00000D92 style:Normal -->
He kept his thoughts narrowed, his awareness razor-edged. He’d spent days learning how to condense his magic into a single point, how to drive it with precision. He didn’t need brute force, just opportunity. Surprise. The will to strike.

<!-- paragraph:3475 para_id:00000D93 style:Normal -->
He imagined it: closing the distance, funneling all his gathered force into the blade, and driving it through the Shroud before Caius even realized he was under attack. A single, clean moment. An end.

<!-- paragraph:3476 para_id:00000D94 style:Normal -->
But then Caius turned.

<!-- paragraph:3477 para_id:00000D95 style:Normal -->
Not sharply. Not in alarm. He simply veered left, still strolling with infuriating ease, down a side road where the buildings leaned closer and the people thinned. His gait didn’t change. He moved like he owned the paving stones beneath his feet—and maybe he did. This sector of the city lay under the long, gilded shadow of the Drakhal citadel.

<!-- paragraph:3478 para_id:00000D96 style:Normal -->
Davian slipped into the next alley, heart hammering.

<!-- paragraph:3479 para_id:00000D97 style:Normal -->
He ducked behind a stack of broken crates and yanked open the satchel. Inside, nestled in cloth, was a smooth, black ceramic mask—featureless save for narrow eye slits. No mouth. No markings. Just anonymity carved into silence.

<!-- paragraph:3480 para_id:00000D98 style:Normal -->
He took a steadying breath, pulled off the cloak, and slid the mask into place.

<!-- paragraph:3481 para_id:00000D99 style:Normal -->
The moment it settled against his face, something in him quieted. Not calmed—but sharpened. He became movement and intent.

<!-- paragraph:3482 para_id:00000D9A style:Normal -->
He slipped from shadow to shadow again, faster now without the encumbrance of the cloak, keeping pace with the figure ahead. Caius hadn’t looked back once. Hadn’t altered his pace or posture. His Shroud still shimmered faintly in the fading light, a constant reminder of power.

<!-- paragraph:3483 para_id:00000D9B style:Normal -->
But there were fewer people now. No crowded stalls. No wagons. Just shuttered buildings and the occasional rustle of trash in the gutter.

<!-- paragraph:3484 para_id:00000D9C style:Normal -->
Davian slowed, eyes narrowing behind the mask.

<!-- paragraph:3485 para_id:00000D9D style:Normal -->
This wasn’t the fastest route to the Drakhal holdings. It wasn’t even a good place to show off.

<!-- paragraph:3486 para_id:00000D9E style:Normal -->
So why was he here?

<!-- paragraph:3487 para_id:00000D9F style:Normal -->
Davian felt the question rise—but shoved it aside.

<!-- paragraph:3488 para_id:00000DA0 style:Normal -->
He wasn’t here for answers.

<!-- paragraph:3489 para_id:00000DA1 style:Normal -->
He stepped out of the alley and onto the street, his fingers tightening on Antithesis. Thirty feet ahead, Caius strolled on, arms loose at his sides.

<!-- paragraph:3490 para_id:00000DA2 style:Normal -->
Unaware.

<!-- paragraph:3491 para_id:00000DA3 style:Normal -->
Unprotected.

<!-- paragraph:3492 para_id:00000DA4 style:Normal -->
Perfect.

<!-- paragraph:3493 para_id:00000DA5 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t breathe so much as inhale the moment—air thick with smoke and sun-warmed stone, the scent of sweat and oil, a breath before eruption.

<!-- paragraph:3494 para_id:00000DA6 style:Normal -->
The world compressed into a single, glinting point.

<!-- paragraph:3495 para_id:00000DA7 style:Normal -->
Antithesis left its sheath with a whisper like steel remembering violence. The weight of it, the shape, felt inevitable in Davian’s grip. He exploded forward, knees coiling and releasing all at once. The mask tunneled his vision, but that only helped. The distance shrank. The space between them became nothing. His mind became one thought, one act.

<!-- paragraph:3496 para_id:00000DA8 style:Normal -->
Kill.

<!-- paragraph:3497 para_id:00000DA9 style:Normal -->
The knife came up low, underhanded, aimed to carve upward into the vulnerable hollow just beneath Caius’s jaw. He could already imagine the way the Shroud would split apart, the shock in Caius’s eyes, the brief flare of realization before—

<!-- paragraph:3498 para_id:00000DAA style:Normal -->
Caius turned.

<!-- paragraph:3499 para_id:00000DAB style:Normal -->
It happened too fast and too slow all at once. A swirl of motion. A pivot on his heel.

<!-- paragraph:3500 para_id:00000DAC style:Normal -->
And then—

<!-- paragraph:3501 para_id:00000DAD style:Normal -->
That smile.

<!-- paragraph:3502 para_id:00000DAE style:Normal -->
Wide. Giddy. Almost delighted.

<!-- paragraph:3503 para_id:00000DAF style:Normal -->
Not surprise. Not fear. Recognition.

<!-- paragraph:3504 para_id:00000DB0 style:Normal -->
Caius’s hand was already rising, the space around his fingers warping, condensing, humming with power. Davian shifted, redirecting Antithesis mid-strike, slashing at the distortion.

<!-- paragraph:3505 para_id:00000DB1 style:Normal -->
He barely made contact.

<!-- paragraph:3506 para_id:00000DB2 style:Normal -->
The air shattered.

<!-- paragraph:3507 para_id:00000DB3 style:Normal -->
A sphere of pressure erupted from Caius’s palm in a perfect ripple, silent for a heartbeat before reality screamed to catch up. The shockwave tore outward, howling through the street like a god’s breath. Pavement cracked. Windowpanes burst in glittering waves. Doors slammed open, then tore from hinges. Bodies lifted—bystanders and hawkers and the unlucky—all flung like rag dolls through the air.

<!-- paragraph:3508 para_id:00000DB4 style:Normal -->
Davian was already reacting. His will snapped into the blade like a coiled whip, commanding cut, divide, part. Antithesis shimmered, an extension of thought—no, of necessity.

<!-- paragraph:3509 para_id:00000DB5 style:Normal -->
The wave met him and split.

<!-- paragraph:3510 para_id:00000DB6 style:Normal -->
Force warped around his body in a v-shaped cleft, the pressure deflecting. Still, it wasn’t enough.

<!-- paragraph:3511 para_id:00000DB7 style:Normal -->
The tail end of the blast slammed into him like a siege engine. Davian flew backward, his limbs slack with helpless momentum. The sky spun. Stone rushed up.

<!-- paragraph:3512 para_id:00000DB8 style:Normal -->
He hit.

<!-- paragraph:3513 para_id:00000DB9 style:Normal -->
Rolled.

<!-- paragraph:3514 para_id:00000DBA style:Normal -->
Pain burst across his ribs, hip, shoulder. He came up low, feet staggered, blade still in hand.

<!-- paragraph:3515 para_id:00000DBB style:Normal -->
Breathing hard.

<!-- paragraph:3516 para_id:00000DBC style:Normal -->
Alive. Somehow.

<!-- paragraph:3517 para_id:00000DBD style:Normal -->
Ahead of him, Caius stood amidst the debris like he’d been born from it. Dust curled around him. His vest fluttered with the recoil of power. His Shroud shimmered.

<!-- paragraph:3518 para_id:00000DBE style:Normal -->
And he was still smiling.

<!-- paragraph:3519 para_id:00000DBF style:Normal -->
Caius turned in a slow circle, surveying the devastation like a painter admiring his finished canvas. Shattered carts, broken masonry, twitching forms pressed against walls—human lives flung aside like leaves in a gale.

<!-- paragraph:3520 para_id:00000DC0 style:Normal -->
He threw his head back and laughed.

<!-- paragraph:3521 para_id:00000DC1 style:Normal -->
A wild, unrestrained howl of joy that echoed off the broken facades around them. The sound sliced through the moans and cries like the edge of a sword.

<!-- paragraph:3522 para_id:00000DC2 style:Normal -->
“Oh, this,” he roared to the sky, “this is what it means to be alive! Can you feel it?” He spun back toward Davian, eyes alight with lunatic thrill. “I held back, you know. Didn’t want to kill our dear little Shade just yet. Not before everyone’s had a good look. And of course, to make sure everyone knows this was all in self-defense.”

<!-- paragraph:3523 para_id:00000DC3 style:Normal -->
Davian staggered to his feet, limbs shaking, Antithesis held low.

<!-- paragraph:3524 para_id:00000DC4 style:Normal -->
Caius grinned wider. “Those weak little Clades you’ve been pruning? Honestly, you’ve done us a favor. Culling the outer herds, keeping the bloodlines pure—it’s practically charity. But it looks bad.”

<!-- paragraph:3525 para_id:00000DC5 style:Normal -->
He tilted his head, still watching Davian with hungry amusement. “And that trick of yours just now? Very interesting. Weak, of course, but… interesting.”

<!-- paragraph:3526 para_id:00000DC6 style:Normal -->
Davian’s grip on the dagger whitened.

<!-- paragraph:3527 para_id:00000DC7 style:Normal -->
This wasn’t just arrogance.

<!-- paragraph:3528 para_id:00000DC8 style:Normal -->
This was bait.

<!-- paragraph:3529 para_id:00000DC9 style:Normal -->
He’d walked into it. Like a fool. Caius had wanted this.

<!-- paragraph:3530 para_id:00000DCA style:Normal -->
And worse, Davian had obliged him.

<!-- paragraph:3531 para_id:00000DCB style:Normal -->
The rage flared again, hot and humiliating—and then, just as suddenly, everything dropped away.

<!-- paragraph:3532 para_id:00000DCC style:Normal -->
Color. Sound. Self.

<!-- paragraph:3533 para_id:00000DCD style:Normal -->
The world narrowed to gray edges and silence. His limbs felt far away. Like he was observing from somewhere else—detached. Cold. Clean. A familiar absence. The air around him grew sharp, and deep inside his mind something moved. The tattoos along his arms prickled, like the ink was alive. Like the swirling marks had mouths now, and they were whispering.

<!-- paragraph:3534 para_id:00000DCE style:Normal -->
Yes. One push. He could end this.

<!-- paragraph:3535 para_id:00000DCF style:Normal -->
The Ink Boon pulsed along his forearms, waiting to be spent. He could reach Caius. The blade could slip through the shimmer of his Shroud before he had time to react. Just one step.

<!-- paragraph:3536 para_id:00000DD0 style:Normal -->
But—

<!-- paragraph:3537 para_id:00000DD1 style:Normal -->
But then he saw the street again.

<!-- paragraph:3538 para_id:00000DD2 style:Normal -->
The twisted limbs of bystanders—ordinary people—slumped in blood against crumbling stone. A woman curled over a child, unmoving. A collapsed vendor stall, its owner’s hand limp beneath it. One of them had been smiling at a customer not a minute ago.

<!-- paragraph:3539 para_id:00000DD3 style:Normal -->
And now—this. Because of him. He’d given Caius the excuse he was looking for.

<!-- paragraph:3540 para_id:00000DD4 style:Normal -->
If he lunged again—if he missed, or even if he struck true—Caius might not hold back this time. And if he didn’t… more people would die.

<!-- paragraph:3541 para_id:00000DD5 style:Normal -->
Davian froze.

<!-- paragraph:3542 para_id:00000DD6 style:Normal -->
His vision cleared just long enough to take in the wreckage—the ruined street, the bloodied figures crumpled against walls—and something inside him cracked. The spell broke.

<!-- paragraph:3543 para_id:00000DD7 style:Normal -->
Davian sucked in a breath like a drowning man surfacing. His fingers loosened on the dagger. His legs trembled.

<!-- paragraph:3544 para_id:00000DD8 style:Normal -->
Caius’s smile faltered. Then it returned, sharper now.

<!-- paragraph:3545 para_id:00000DD9 style:Normal -->
“Oh no,” he said, tilting his head. “Don’t fold on me now. You’ve been playing at hero for so long I think you’ve started to believe it. But we both know what this really is.”

<!-- paragraph:3546 para_id:00000DDA style:Normal -->
His voice lowered, almost fond.

<!-- paragraph:3547 para_id:00000DDB style:Normal -->
“You’re not here to protect anyone. You’re here because you want to kill someone stronger than you. Someone you hate for having what you never could. That’s not justice. That’s jealousy.” He smirked. Magic crackled in his palm again—violent and eager.

<!-- paragraph:3548 para_id:00000DDC style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes widened.

<!-- paragraph:3549 para_id:00000DDD style:Normal -->
The air shifted.

<!-- paragraph:3550 para_id:00000DDE style:Normal -->
Davian felt it first as pressure—rising, rushing—followed by a searing wave of heat that slammed into his back, forcing the breath from his lungs. His ears popped. Then a beam of fire screamed past him, white-hot and blinding, cracking the air as it struck Caius full in the chest.

<!-- paragraph:3551 para_id:00000DDF style:Normal -->
Caius barely had time to twist. His hands snapped up, Shroud flaring to meet the blast. The street lit up like a furnace mouth, stone glowing beneath the impact. At the same time, a sheet of frost rolled in from behind Davian—cracking over the cobbles, hissing as it met lingering heat. The shockwave rocked him where he stood.

<!-- paragraph:3552 para_id:00000DE0 style:Normal -->
The beam didn’t stop. It intensified—tightened—and for a moment, Davian swore he saw something spiraling at its heart.

<!-- paragraph:3553 para_id:00000DE1 style:Normal -->
Then it cut off.

<!-- paragraph:3554 para_id:00000DE2 style:Normal -->
Caius dropped to one knee, chest heaving, steam curling from his shoulders. His vest—embroidered silk and tailored within a breath of vanity—was scorched, the fabric puckered and smoking.

<!-- paragraph:3555 para_id:00000DE3 style:Normal -->
He looked down at it with genuine offense. “What the hell,” he rasped. “I just got this vest.”

<!-- paragraph:3556 para_id:00000DE4 style:Normal -->
Footsteps echoed behind Davian.

<!-- paragraph:3557 para_id:00000DE5 style:Normal -->
Maya swept past him, her presence sharp and freezing, like the leading edge of a stormfront. Her hair whipped in the breeze, and every stone she passed rimed with frost. She didn’t even glance at Davian—as if he were no more than a post in the road.

<!-- paragraph:3558 para_id:00000DE6 style:Normal -->
Caius staggered upright, brushing ash from his front. “So,” he said, voice tightening. “You followed me. I knew you couldn’t stay away.”

<!-- paragraph:3559 para_id:00000DE7 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer. She raised one hand again—calm, effortless—and Davian saw the flicker in Caius’s eyes: that half-second of nerves before he smoothed it over with bravado.

<!-- paragraph:3560 para_id:00000DE8 style:Normal -->
“Actually, no. I’ve just been working on something,” Maya said, her tone flat. “Turns out being fast is pretty useful.”

<!-- paragraph:3561 para_id:00000DE9 style:Normal -->
Caius gave a dramatic shrug, brushing soot from his vest. “So tell me, Maya—what are you even doing here? Changed your mind about that romantic battlefield meeting after all?”

<!-- paragraph:3562 para_id:00000DEA style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond right away. She glanced down the street, eyes tracing the gouges in the stone, the limp forms still not moving.

<!-- paragraph:3563 para_id:00000DEB style:Normal -->
“I realized you were up to something,” she said at last. “No matter how vain you are—and you are—you don’t walk through the entire city, in full regalia, during peak hour traffic, just for a dramatic entrance. That’s a lot of effort for someone who usually expects the world to come to him.”

<!-- paragraph:3564 para_id:00000DEC style:Normal -->
She looked around again, at the ruined stalls and shattered windows.

<!-- paragraph:3565 para_id:00000DED style:Normal -->
“I didn’t think it would be this bad.”

<!-- paragraph:3566 para_id:00000DEE style:Normal -->
Davian felt heat crawl up his neck beneath the mask. She’d figured it out. She’d been ahead of him. And now here he was—sidelined, silent, cloaked in failure while she stood where he should’ve been.

<!-- paragraph:3567 para_id:00000DEF style:Normal -->
Caius let out a laugh, short and sharp. “This is bad? Come now, Maya. I’ve heard the stories. Everyone in the city knows how many bodies you’ve left behind. And your dear father?” He gave an exaggerated wink. “I remember hearing about that little maneuver where he scythed through a few battalions running for their lives. Just to send a message.”

<!-- paragraph:3568 para_id:00000DF0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s gaze snapped back to him. “Those were soldiers. Armed. Dangerous. These people—” she gestured toward the broken street, the bloodied stones “—were civilians. Innocents. The only reason we have the right to rule is if we protect them.”

<!-- paragraph:3569 para_id:00000DF1 style:Normal -->
Caius’s smirk twisted. “Oh? That wasn’t quite the message of your Presentation, was it?” Magic flared again at his fingertips, threads of heat dancing like snakes.

<!-- paragraph:3570 para_id:00000DF2 style:Normal -->
Maya raised her hand, eyes narrowing. “If you remember Presentation,” she said coolly, “then you also remember that I held back. This—” she swept her fingers in a slow, deliberate motion “—was restraint.”

<!-- paragraph:3571 para_id:00000DF3 style:Normal -->
The expression on Caius’s face fractured—momentarily raw and unguarded. Rage. Humiliation. Something almost like fear. And then it was gone, buried under the polished sneer.

<!-- paragraph:3572 para_id:00000DF4 style:Normal -->
“Well,” he said lightly, brushing ash from his shoulder, “maybe if I get to my tailor fast enough, she can salvage this vest.”

<!-- paragraph:3573 para_id:00000DF5 style:Normal -->
Without another word, he turned and strolled away, the wind from the fire-scorched street tugging at his sleeves.

<!-- paragraph:3574 para_id:00000DF6 style:Normal -->
Davian’s grip tightened on the hilt of his dagger again. Just one—

<!-- paragraph:3575 para_id:00000DF7 style:Normal -->
“Don’t even think about it,” Maya said, turning toward him. Her voice was ice and iron. “I could take you in for what you did. For what you almost did.”

<!-- paragraph:3576 para_id:00000DF8 style:Normal -->
She took a step closer, chin lifting. “But I saw you hesitate. I know you don’t want anyone else to die. So let it go.”

<!-- paragraph:3577 para_id:00000DF9 style:Normal -->
He froze. The mask hid his face, the cloak hid his arms, but he was still afraid she might see through him somehow. He couldn’t speak—not without risking everything—so instead, he raised one hand and signed, beneath the folds of fabric:

<!-- paragraph:3578 para_id:00000DFA style:Normal -->
Thank you.

<!-- paragraph:3579 para_id:00000DFB style:Normal -->
She frowned slightly, puzzled.

<!-- paragraph:3580 para_id:00000DFC style:Normal -->
He turned and vanished into the alley before she could ask questions.

<!-- paragraph:3581 para_id:00000DFD style:Normal -->
As soon as Davian was out of sight, he spun to the side and slammed Antithesis into the wall.

<!-- paragraph:3582 para_id:00000DFE style:Normal -->
Once.
 Twice.
 Three times.
 The fourth strike cracked the stone, the vibrations shivering up his arm.

<!-- paragraph:3583 para_id:00000DFF style:Normal -->
He stood there, hunched, panting, staring at the dagger embedded in the wall.

<!-- paragraph:3584 para_id:00000E00 style:Normal -->
He had been suckered. Played. Outmaneuvered by a peacocked heir who practically invited him to try something stupid. And Davian had delivered—had run through the city, mask on, dagger drawn, ready to kill.

<!-- paragraph:3585 para_id:00000E01 style:Normal -->
Ready to kill, and people had died.

<!-- paragraph:3586 para_id:00000E02 style:Normal -->
Not Caius. Not him.

<!-- paragraph:3587 para_id:00000E03 style:Normal -->
Vendors. Shoppers. A child curled against their mother.

<!-- paragraph:3588 para_id:00000E04 style:Normal -->
He hadn’t even hesitated until the bodies started falling.

<!-- paragraph:3589 para_id:00000E05 style:Normal -->
His fingers flexed, loosening and tightening around the hilt. He yanked Antithesis free with a low growl and sheathed it with shaking hands. His breath steamed in the cooling night air, ears still ringing with the pressure of the blast.

<!-- paragraph:3590 para_id:00000E06 style:Normal -->
He leaned back against the wall, trying to slow his pulse. Anger pulsed through him, but it was muddled now—cut through with fear. Cold, creeping fear. He could have died. If Maya hadn’t—

<!-- paragraph:3591 para_id:00000E07 style:Normal -->
He couldn’t even finish the thought.

<!-- paragraph:3592 para_id:00000E08 style:Normal -->
Why would she do that?

<!-- paragraph:3593 para_id:00000E09 style:Normal -->
Why would she save him?

<!-- paragraph:3594 para_id:00000E0A style:Normal -->
She hadn’t even hesitated. Had stepped in between them like it was obvious, like it was nothing. Protected him—the assassin, the Shade, the nobody.

<!-- paragraph:3595 para_id:00000E0B style:Normal -->
And let him go.

<!-- paragraph:3596 para_id:00000E0C style:Normal -->
He scrubbed a hand over his face. It didn’t make sense—not if the High Clades were what he’d always believed. They weren’t supposed to act like that. They didn’t act like that. And yet…

<!-- paragraph:3597 para_id:00000E0D style:Normal -->
That thought lodged like a splinter behind his ribs. She hadn’t gloried in her power like Caius did. She hadn’t threatened or mocked or dismissed him.

<!-- paragraph:3598 para_id:00000E0E style:Normal -->
She’d saved him.

<!-- paragraph:3599 para_id:00000E0F style:Normal -->
She’d been… magnificent. Not just her power, though that had been terrifying in its precision. Not just her control. But the way she walked through chaos like it meant nothing, the way her eyes fixed on Caius with cold fury, the frost curling at her heels as if the world bent to her steps.

<!-- paragraph:3600 para_id:00000E10 style:Normal -->
She’d been the most beautiful thing he’d ever seen.

<!-- paragraph:3601 para_id:00000E11 style:Normal -->
He shut his eyes, fists clenching again—but this time, not from rage.

<!-- paragraph:3602 para_id:00000E12 style:Normal -->
He wasn’t supposed to think like this. Not about her. Not about any of them.

<!-- paragraph:3603 para_id:00000E13 style:Normal -->
But something had shifted.

<!-- paragraph:3604 para_id:00000E14 style:Normal -->
And for the first time in a long while, he didn’t know what he believed anymore.

<!-- paragraph:3605 para_id:00000E15 style:Normal -->
Davian moved through the city like a shadow, half-aware of the streets winding past him. The glow of lanterns blurred in his vision. He kept to the alleys, looping wide to avoid any main roads. His thoughts ran in circles—snarled loops of anger, guilt, confusion. His feet ached, and he barely noticed.

<!-- paragraph:3606 para_id:00000E16 style:Normal -->
Before the final turn toward the citadel gate, he stopped in a narrow, bricked alcove and crouched low. He peeled the black ceramic mask from his face—damp now with sweat—and wrapped it in the cloth from the vendor’s satchel. He tucked it behind a loose stone in the wall, brushing some dust over it. Not perfect, but it would have to do.

<!-- paragraph:3607 para_id:00000E17 style:Normal -->
He checked his appearance. His hands were dirty, his hair sweat-matted. His tunic was rumpled from the roll he'd taken, but thankfully unburned. With a few quick movements, he smoothed himself out and forced his breathing to slow.

<!-- paragraph:3608 para_id:00000E18 style:Normal -->
Then he stepped out of the alley and rounded the bend toward the citadel gate—only to halt.

<!-- paragraph:3609 para_id:00000E19 style:Normal -->
Harvick was sitting on the guard's bench. Alone.

<!-- paragraph:3610 para_id:00000E1A style:Normal -->
Davian’s stomach dropped.

<!-- paragraph:3611 para_id:00000E1B style:Normal -->
