Harvick’s arms were folded, his expression unreadable—but there was a glint of something in his eyes. Something too knowing.

<!-- paragraph:3612 para_id:00000E1C style:Normal -->
Davian fought to stay calm. “Didn’t think someone like you pulled gate duty.”

<!-- paragraph:3613 para_id:00000E1D style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged as he stood. “Usually don’t. But when I heard you left the citadel again—alone—I figured I’d wait around to say hi.”

<!-- paragraph:3614 para_id:00000E1E style:Normal -->
Davian’s throat felt dry. He opened his mouth to deflect, but Harvick was already smirking.

<!-- paragraph:3615 para_id:00000E1F style:Normal -->
“Out paying off your cousins’ debt contracts, I assume?”

<!-- paragraph:3616 para_id:00000E20 style:Normal -->
Davian nearly sagged with relief, managing to twist the sudden exhale into a casual shift of his shoulders. Harvick seemed to take it as confirmation.

<!-- paragraph:3617 para_id:00000E21 style:Normal -->
“Don’t worry,” Harvick added. “I won’t tell the boys. I know it can be... embarrassing.”

<!-- paragraph:3618 para_id:00000E22 style:Normal -->
Davian gave a weak nod.

<!-- paragraph:3619 para_id:00000E23 style:Normal -->
“But next time,” Harvick said, “let me know. If you’re headed to that part of town, you should have someone watching your back.”

<!-- paragraph:3620 para_id:00000E24 style:Normal -->
“I can handle myself.”

<!-- paragraph:3621 para_id:00000E25 style:Normal -->
“I know,” Harvick said easily. “But you shouldn’t have to. There’s no harm in accepting help when it’s offered.”

<!-- paragraph:3622 para_id:00000E26 style:Normal -->
Davian looked at him, brow tight. He hadn’t meant to ask, but it came out all the same.

<!-- paragraph:3623 para_id:00000E27 style:Normal -->
“Why?” he said quietly. “Why would you help me like this? Train me. Stick up for me. Recommend me for a job? You don’t even know me. What are you getting out of this?”

<!-- paragraph:3624 para_id:00000E28 style:Normal -->
Harvick tilted his head, thoughtful. “Few reasons.”

<!-- paragraph:3625 para_id:00000E29 style:Normal -->
He held up a hand and counted them off with his fingers.

<!-- paragraph:3626 para_id:00000E2A style:Normal -->
“One, you remind me of myself when I was younger. I wish someone had watched my back back then.”

<!-- paragraph:3627 para_id:00000E2B style:Normal -->
“Two, I think you’ve got potential. And I hate watching potential rot for lack of opportunity.”

<!-- paragraph:3628 para_id:00000E2C style:Normal -->
“And three…” A faint smile. “I don’t have much else to do these days. Playing busybody keeps me from dying of boredom.”

<!-- paragraph:3629 para_id:00000E2D style:Normal -->
It might’ve been a joke. But it didn’t feel like one.

<!-- paragraph:3630 para_id:00000E2E style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. He thought of Maya again—walking through fire and frost to stop a massacre. Sparing him. Protecting him.

<!-- paragraph:3631 para_id:00000E2F style:Normal -->
“I guess everyone around here’s determined to help me,” he muttered. “I’m just telling you now—I’m going to let you down.”

<!-- paragraph:3632 para_id:00000E30 style:Normal -->
Harvick raised a brow. “Maybe. That’s your choice.”

<!-- paragraph:3633 para_id:00000E31 style:Normal -->
Then he clapped Davian lightly on the shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:3634 para_id:00000E32 style:Normal -->
“But helping you? That’s mine. So deal with it.”

<!-- paragraph:3635 para_id:00000E33 style:Normal -->
Davian shut the door behind him with more care than he felt. The latch clicked home with a dull finality.

<!-- paragraph:3636 para_id:00000E34 style:Normal -->
He didn’t light the lamp.

<!-- paragraph:3637 para_id:00000E35 style:Normal -->
Instead, he crossed the small room and sat at the edge of the bed, the muscles in his legs twitching from the strain of the day—of the fight. Of the run.

<!-- paragraph:3638 para_id:00000E36 style:Normal -->
He pulled up his sleeve.

<!-- paragraph:3639 para_id:00000E37 style:Normal -->
The Ink Boons wrapped his forearm in a tangle of black lines, deceptively ordinary in the low light. Anyone might mistake them for decorative tattoos. But his eyes went straight to the section he knew too well—the slight distortion where Arrek’s aura was bound. A borrowed signature. A counterfeit key. Not glowing. Not moving.

<!-- paragraph:3640 para_id:00000E38 style:Normal -->
But still almost pulsing beneath the skin.

<!-- paragraph:3641 para_id:00000E39 style:Normal -->
He pressed his fingers to it. Nothing. But the sensation remained. Like it was ticking. Fading.

<!-- paragraph:3642 para_id:00000E3A style:Normal -->
He could tell it was losing integrity—faint around the edges now, like a map left out in the rain. Such a thin veil between him and the Registry. He’d seen what it did—those blackened scorch marks on the stone in the courtyard. When the Boon unraveled completely, he would die screaming. And then there would just be another stain on the courtyard stones.

<!-- paragraph:3643 para_id:00000E3B style:Normal -->
His throat tightened. He dropped his hand.

<!-- paragraph:3644 para_id:00000E3C style:Normal -->
He wasn't Arrek. He’d killed Arrek. Left him dead in the street so he could take his place, wrap himself in the man’s name and walk into his life.

<!-- paragraph:3645 para_id:00000E3D style:Normal -->
But sometimes…

<!-- paragraph:3646 para_id:00000E3E style:Normal -->
Sometimes he wished he was.

<!-- paragraph:3647 para_id:00000E3F style:Normal -->
Arrek, the Arrek he was - that man was trusted. Respected. He had a place here. A future. Friends, even. A path toward something better.

<!-- paragraph:3648 para_id:00000E40 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned forward, forearms on his knees, and stared down at the ink again.

<!-- paragraph:3649 para_id:00000E41 style:Normal -->
The lines that gave him a name, a role, a contract.

<!-- paragraph:3650 para_id:00000E42 style:Normal -->
The same lines that bound him to the Harbingers.

<!-- paragraph:3651 para_id:00000E43 style:Normal -->
His voice was low, nearly soundless. “I wish I could be him. Really be him.”

<!-- paragraph:3652 para_id:00000E44 style:Normal -->
But the Boon didn’t care what he wished.

<!-- paragraph:3653 para_id:00000E45 style:Normal -->
It only cared what he owed.

<!-- paragraph:3656 para_id:00000E48 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 21

<!-- paragraph:3657 para_id:00000E49 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 21

<!-- paragraph:3660 para_id:00000E4C style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3662 para_id:00000E4E style:Normal -->
The alley spat her into the open street, but the air here still felt wrong—thinner somehow, as if it remembered what had just happened. No shattered stone, no smoking craters, yet every glance told the same story. A vendor froze mid-gesture over a stack of cloth. A boy darted behind his father’s coat, peering out with wide, skittish eyes. The market seemed to breathe shallowly around her, clearing her path without a word.

<!-- paragraph:3663 para_id:00000E4F style:Normal -->
Her Shroud whispered frost across the edges of her vision. She held her posture the way she’d been taught—measured steps, lifted chin, the mask of someone untouched.

<!-- paragraph:3664 para_id:00000E50 style:Normal -->
Inside, her balance was slipping.

<!-- paragraph:3665 para_id:00000E51 style:Normal -->
One blink and the street was gone. She was still moving, but in her mind she was back in that narrow place with Caius—his eyes locking onto hers in that razor-sharp second before everything broke. Rage and something rawer, almost feral, burning there. The way it had been with that assassin, what seemed a lifetime ago. That last moment when she’d met his gaze, frozen him from the inside out, felt his life shatter under her hands.

<!-- paragraph:3666 para_id:00000E52 style:Normal -->
The two moments braided together, flickering with an edge of unreality. Threads seemed to hum between them, stretching taut as if someone had plucked a line in the fabric of fate. She knew it wasn’t—knew she wasn’t seeing the strands—but the sensation was the same: that pull in her chest, the cold clarity of standing in the hinge-point of a choice that could not be undone.

<!-- paragraph:3667 para_id:00000E53 style:Normal -->
Niall’s words about her Presentation echoed back to her, unbidden: You’ve claimed a seat among monsters, Maya. You’ll have to fight to keep it

<!-- paragraph:3668 para_id:00000E54 style:Normal -->
She didn’t know why she’d done it. She could tell herself it was because she’d read Caius’s intentions, because she’d wanted to head off whatever game he was playing before it reached the Citadel. But that wasn’t what had moved her in the moment. She had stepped in front of someone she didn’t even know.

<!-- paragraph:3669 para_id:00000E55 style:Normal -->
Someone in a black mask, silent as shadow. The Shade Alden had spoken of only with doubt, a rumor given form.

<!-- paragraph:3670 para_id:00000E56 style:Normal -->
And she had let him go.

<!-- paragraph:3671 para_id:00000E57 style:Normal -->
She didn’t know why she had done that either. Maybe it was the way he stood there—just a knife braced against the impossible shimmer of a High Clade Shroud. Maybe it was the mask.

<!-- paragraph:3672 para_id:00000E58 style:Normal -->
Because she wore one too. Every time she left her chambers, she wrapped herself in the same illusion of control: a mask of composure and perfection. On the outside she looked fearless, flawless, untouchable—just as he had looked faceless, impenetrable. But no matter how strong her magic, how precise her movements, how perfect her performance, there was always another voice telling her it wasn’t enough. That safety lived only in control. That she had to be sharper, better, more exact—or risk breaking.

<!-- paragraph:3673 para_id:00000E59 style:Normal -->
So maybe she let the Shade go because she recognized herself in him. Two masks, different shapes, both hiding the same truth: neither of them was what the world demanded they be.

<!-- paragraph:3674 para_id:00000E5A style:Normal -->
The thought came to her then, sharp as a splinter: how had he survived? She’d only seen him thrown back by Caius’s attack from a distance, but the ruin that blast had carved into buildings, into the bodies still lying where they’d fallen—it should have been lethal.

<!-- paragraph:3675 para_id:00000E5B style:Normal -->
She let the question slip away. The edges of her adrenaline had worn off, leaving her drained, and she didn’t have the focus to chase it.

<!-- paragraph:3676 para_id:00000E5C style:Normal -->
The street around her swam back into focus. The crowd parted in a wide, instinctive current around her, their eyes flicking to the faint shimmer of her Shroud and the frost blooming in her footsteps. Awe and fear hung in the air like mist.

<!-- paragraph:3677 para_id:00000E5D style:Normal -->
It felt like hours before the Citadel’s spire loomed above her again. The gates yawned open, and Erynn was there—flanked by her two personal guards, their posture tight with anxiety.

<!-- paragraph:3678 para_id:00000E5E style:Normal -->
“You can’t just leave us like that,” Erynn said, stepping forward, voice taut but quiet. “You know there’s no way we could follow you when you shoot off.”

<!-- paragraph:3679 para_id:00000E5F style:Normal -->
“There wasn’t any other option,” Maya replied. Her voice was steady, though her chest still ached with the strain. “Caius was too far ahead—I had to see what he was doing.”

<!-- paragraph:3680 para_id:00000E60 style:Normal -->
“Was that an explosion?” Erynn hissed, eyes wide. “Because if it was, your father is going to literally kill us.”

<!-- paragraph:3681 para_id:00000E61 style:Normal -->
The words jolted Maya from her exhaustion, adrenaline sparking sharp and sudden. “Did you tell him?”

<!-- paragraph:3682 para_id:00000E62 style:Normal -->
Erynn scoffed. “No. Obviously not. We didn’t want to die. But…” She shifted uneasily. “We did tell someone.”

<!-- paragraph:3683 para_id:00000E63 style:Normal -->
A voice cut in from the shadows beside the gate. “We need to talk.”

<!-- paragraph:3684 para_id:00000E64 style:Normal -->
Maya turned. Alden stepped forward, his hawkish features carved in lamplight, expression unreadable but harder than usual. She opened her mouth to protest—I’m too tired for this—but one look at him stole the excuse from her lips.

<!-- paragraph:3685 para_id:00000E65 style:Normal -->
They walked in silence across the courtyard, her two guards and Erynn trailing behind, all of them tense as bowstrings. When they reached his office, Alden gestured to the escort to wait outside. The heavy door shut with a click, muffling the nervous shifting of boots in the hall.

<!-- paragraph:3686 para_id:00000E66 style:Normal -->
Inside, Alden strode to the far window, the city’s faint glow framing him in silhouette. When he turned back, his gaze was sharp, cutting through her mask as though it wasn’t there.

<!-- paragraph:3687 para_id:00000E67 style:Normal -->
“You know I have to tell your father about this,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:3688 para_id:00000E68 style:Normal -->
Maya’s jaw tightened. “Then what was the point? Why drag me here, if you’re just going to run to him anyway?”

<!-- paragraph:3689 para_id:00000E69 style:Normal -->
Alden’s mouth thinned. “Because you may not realize it yet, but you are a High Clade magus now. And that means you get the courtesy of debriefing with me first before I run off to our head magus.”

<!-- paragraph:3690 para_id:00000E6A style:Normal -->
The words landed harder than she expected. For a second, the room tilted. Maya’s breath caught, and she lowered herself into the nearest chair before her legs could betray her. Everything from the night—the confrontation, Caius’s smirk, the frozen silence of the Shade’s mask—came rushing back all at once, newly framed in the light of Alden’s reminder. It wasn’t that she hadn’t known her role academically. Of course she had. But hearing it spoken aloud made every decision feel heavier, every action suddenly freighted with consequences she hadn’t fully let herself name.

<!-- paragraph:3691 para_id:00000E6B style:Normal -->
Caius’s taunts returned like knives in the ribs. You might have power but you don’t know how to use it.

<!-- paragraph:3692 para_id:00000E6C style:Normal -->
Her chest tightened, and she forced her eyes up to Alden’s. “I couldn’t just let him kill them,” she said, her voice thin with the edge of panic. “I couldn’t.”

<!-- paragraph:3693 para_id:00000E6D style:Normal -->
Something in his expression eased—just slightly. He stepped closer, resting one hand on the desk, his voice lower. “Look. Just tell me what happened. I know you well enough to reserve judgment until I hear the whole story.”

<!-- paragraph:3694 para_id:00000E6E style:Normal -->
She drew a shaky breath and began. The words came haltingly at first, but once she started, the shape of the night tumbled out. Alden listened without interruption, hands folded behind his back. As she went on, his eyebrows crept steadily upward. By the time she finished, they were arched high, a rare, visible crack in his composure.

<!-- paragraph:3695 para_id:00000E6F style:Normal -->
Alden looked like he was about to speak, lips parting—then he stopped. His mouth closed, and he gave a short shake of his head, as if trying to gather his thoughts into some coherent order.

<!-- paragraph:3696 para_id:00000E70 style:Normal -->
“So,” he said at last, his voice slower than usual, “you went after Caius because you thought he was up to… something. And instead he was really fighting this mythical Shade. And then you attacked him—” his hand flicked slightly, as though even saying it out loud strained belief “—and somehow got him to back down. And then…” he paused again, searching for words, “you let the Shade… go?”

<!-- paragraph:3697 para_id:00000E71 style:Normal -->
Alden pulled out a chair and lowered himself into it with a slow exhale. He stared up at the ceiling for what felt like a long time, then finally brought his gaze back down to her.

<!-- paragraph:3698 para_id:00000E72 style:Normal -->
“I understand why you attacked Caius,” he said. “Though I wish you had just let it go. There will be consequences for that, and we’ll need to talk with Niall about next steps.” His eyes narrowed slightly. “But what I really need clarified is why you let the Shade walk away.”

<!-- paragraph:3699 para_id:00000E73 style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated. Her mind skittered back to the half-formed reasons she’d told herself earlier - but she wasn’t about to voice those aloud. Not to Alden. She lifted her chin instead. “He was attacking Caius. And Caius is—” she allowed herself a thin, brittle smile “—well, Caius. How do we even know the Shade is the enemy here?”

<!-- paragraph:3700 para_id:00000E74 style:Normal -->
Alden’s stare was unblinking. “Because someone wiped out the Sorevins,” he said flatly. “And several other Low Clades besides. And with what you’ve just told me, it looks a lot more like that someone was this Shade—not the Drakhals.”

<!-- paragraph:3701 para_id:00000E75 style:Normal -->
Maya had no good answer for that. Her mouth opened, then closed again. What came out instead was the one detail she couldn’t shake. “He took a direct hit from Caius,” she said. “At close range. It should have killed him—anyone else, it would have—but he got back up, ready to keep fighting. And I didn’t see a Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:3702 para_id:00000E76 style:Normal -->
Alden’s eyes bulged, his mouth parting slightly as if words had deserted him. Maya found herself almost savoring the moment, cataloguing each new expression that crossed the usually stone-carved face of the spymaster.

<!-- paragraph:3703 para_id:00000E77 style:Normal -->
“If that’s true,” he said finally, his voice low, “then I have no idea what we’re dealing with. But it sounds extremely dangerous.”

<!-- paragraph:3704 para_id:00000E78 style:Normal -->
Alden rubbed at his temple, then let his hand fall back to the desk. “It doesn’t sound like there’s anything else pertinent for us to cover—unless you’ve discovered another mythical being that’s real, or attacked another incredibly dangerous magus without telling me.” His tone was flat, but the edge of it made her wince. “So. I’m going to have the guards get Niall. We need to discuss what to do about the Drakhals, and he’s the most important voice in that conversation. He’s the one force in this city they might actually fear.”

<!-- paragraph:3705 para_id:00000E79 style:Normal -->
He crossed the room and opened the door. “Fetch Lord Niall,” he told the waiting guards. One of them shifted uneasily, then nodded with the weary resignation of a man dispatched on an errand no one envied.

<!-- paragraph:3706 para_id:00000E7A style:Normal -->
The door closed again, leaving the office heavy with silence. Maya smoothed her palms across her skirt, then folded her hands in her lap. She straightened, shoulders square, face calm. The perfect daughter. The unshakable heir. She could feel the weight of it settling over her like armor.

<!-- paragraph:3707 para_id:00000E7B style:Normal -->
Alden glanced at her, his sharp eyes noting the transformation, but he said nothing. He simply resumed his post near the desk, hands folded behind his back, waiting.

<!-- paragraph:3708 para_id:00000E7C style:Normal -->
When the door opened again, the air itself seemed to tighten. Niall entered without a word, and the space felt suddenly smaller, as if the leading edge of a stormcloud had drifted in with him.

<!-- paragraph:3709 para_id:00000E7D style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes swept the room, flat and intense all at once—the way they always seemed now, as if he were watching everything and nothing at the same time. His voice came out low, steady. “What is going on.”

<!-- paragraph:3710 para_id:00000E7E style:Normal -->
“Sit, my lord,” Alden said smoothly, gesturing toward a chair across from the desk. “I have an important report to deliver—reconnaissance, from Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:3711 para_id:00000E7F style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t react to the phrasing, just crossed the room and settled into the chair, his posture controlled, his gaze finally sliding to Maya for the briefest moment before returning to Alden.

<!-- paragraph:3712 para_id:00000E80 style:Normal -->
Alden began his account, precise and economical as always. Yet Maya noticed, even through her exhaustion, the subtle shading of his words. Every phrase placed her as a neutral observer, almost an incidental witness, while Caius’s behavior emerged sharp-edged and sinister, a deliberate provocation too dangerous to ignore.

<!-- paragraph:3713 para_id:00000E81 style:Normal -->
Niall gave no sign of response as the story unfolded. His face remained carved stone, his silence weighing heavier than any question. But when Alden described the street laid waste—the civilians caught in Caius’s blast—Maya swore she saw it: a brief crackle of light sparking at the corner of his eyes before vanishing.

<!-- paragraph:3714 para_id:00000E82 style:Normal -->
The spymaster concluded. Silence pooled between them, stretching taut. Niall sat with it, unmoving, as if testing the weight of the report in his hands. Finally he spoke, voice clipped and certain.

<!-- paragraph:3715 para_id:00000E83 style:Normal -->
“No one will care what Caius did,” he said. “The Drakhals are too well-positioned. He can claim it was self-defense, and it will stand. Their house has weathered worse scandals without a scratch. You remember what happened with Vorren.”

<!-- paragraph:3716 para_id:00000E84 style:Normal -->
His tone was not bitter, not even resigned. It was pure assessment—cold, clinical, inevitable.

<!-- paragraph:3717 para_id:00000E85 style:Normal -->
Niall’s gaze shifted to Maya fully now, steady and unblinking. “But it was a misstep not to at least attempt to apprehend the Shade,” he said. “The Drakhals may yet find a way to twist this. They’ll say the Rezins attacked one of their heirs, then conveniently let their attacker vanish into the alleys. It could be made to look like collaboration. That we are propping up some nameless assassin to undermine them.”

<!-- paragraph:3718 para_id:00000E86 style:Normal -->
He leaned back slightly, his expression unreadable. “The other houses already strain against our position. They resent our dominance, even when it protects them. This will not ease that resentment.”

<!-- paragraph:3719 para_id:00000E87 style:Normal -->
For a moment, the room was silent but for the faint hum of wards in the stone. Then he added, his voice as even as before: “I agree with your choice to protect civilians. That was correct. But your decisions concerning the Shade…” his jaw tightened fractionally, “leave me somewhat disappointed.”

<!-- paragraph:3720 para_id:00000E88 style:Normal -->
Maya held herself still under the weight of his words, her posture perfectly measured, her face schooled into calm. The disappointment in his tone cut deeper than any anger might have, but she forced her features to remain steady. That was what he expected—what he demanded—of her. Flawless composure. No weakness.

<!-- paragraph:3721 para_id:00000E89 style:Normal -->
And yet, in the silence that followed, her thoughts began to spiral. Niall had been trying harder—she knew it, could see it in the way he lingered a fraction longer in conversations with her, or how he corrected himself when speaking to Selwin. So if he still voiced disappointment now, it wasn’t cold dismissal. It was genuine.

<!-- paragraph:3722 para_id:00000E8A style:Normal -->
The realization sank into her like ice water, pulling her inward. Her thoughts battered at her, each one sharper than the last, until the pressure of them seemed unbearable.

<!-- paragraph:3723 para_id:00000E8B style:Normal -->
And then—she slipped.

<!-- paragraph:3724 para_id:00000E8C style:Normal -->
The room dissolved into a vast, disorienting expanse, as though the floor had fallen away beneath her. Threads—fine, incandescent strands—stretched outward from her, from Niall, from Alden, weaving endlessly into the dark. They extended forward and backward at once, into what was and what could be, the whole sea of it swelling around her. Infinite, and yet deepest black, as if possibility itself was suspended in the ink between the stars.

<!-- paragraph:3725 para_id:00000E8D style:Normal -->
Her breath hitched as the sensation overtook her. She was both here and not. The past and future pressed against her like overlapping shadows. She saw herself again in the alley—Caius’s grin carved into her memory, her own hand raised in fire, the Shade’s faceless mask vanishing into the dark.

<!-- paragraph:3726 para_id:00000E8E style:Normal -->
At the same time, something else intruded. Soldiers swarming at the base of the Citadel’s walls—shields raised, weapons catching light. For a moment she thought it was the day before her Presentation, when she had first stood upon those ramparts. But no, the angles were wrong, the assault sharper. A surge of magic coiled below, then launched upward, slamming against the wall just beside where her vision perched. The impact thundered through her, the concussion throwing her back into herself.

<!-- paragraph:3727 para_id:00000E8F style:Normal -->
She gasped, lurching upright, her hands gripping the chair as if she might be flung again. “They’re going to attack us,” she blurted, her voice cutting through the heavy quiet.

<!-- paragraph:3728 para_id:00000E90 style:Normal -->
“What?” Alden snapped, the sharpness of his voice startling in the hush. “How do you know?”

<!-- paragraph:3729 para_id:00000E91 style:Normal -->
Maya’s chest heaved, the words spilling before she could stop them. “I saw the threads.”

<!-- paragraph:3730 para_id:00000E92 style:Normal -->
The crackle of lightning filled the room. Niall surged to his feet, his chair scraping hard against the stone floor. Sparks flared at the corners of his eyes, leaping across his shoulders like the edge of a storm breaking loose. His voice was a thunderclap. “I told you never to look there.”

<!-- paragraph:3731 para_id:00000E93 style:Normal -->
Maya’s body moved before her mind caught up, rising as if drawn into the current of his fury. Frost leapt from the floor beneath her, webbing outward from her bare feet, climbing the legs of her chair in jagged veins of white. Her hands trembled, then clenched, her whole frame shaking as every piece of tension she’d shoved down all night broke free.

<!-- paragraph:3732 para_id:00000E94 style:Normal -->
“I can’t control it!” she screamed, the mask shattering with the words. The perfect composure, the practiced neutrality, all ripped away. Her voice echoed hard against the stone walls, raw and naked. Frost spiraled outward in a jagged burst, the air stinging with cold.

<!-- paragraph:3733 para_id:00000E95 style:Normal -->
“You have to!” Niall’s shout cracked through the room, lightning flashing in the whites of his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:3734 para_id:00000E96 style:Normal -->
And in that instant, his mask slipped. She saw it—not the carved stoicism, not the war hero or the Clade lord—but the hollow core beneath. Fear. Loss. Grief so deep it seemed bottomless.

<!-- paragraph:3735 para_id:00000E97 style:Normal -->
Her own magic faltered. The frost at her feet hissed and stilled as she released it, her breath ragged. “You always ask the impossible,” she said hoarsely. “From me, from Selwin, from everyone.”

<!-- paragraph:3736 para_id:00000E98 style:Normal -->
The moment broke. His mask drew back into place with practiced precision, sealing away what she had glimpsed. She forced herself to follow, drawing the shards of her composure together, smoothing her face into neutrality.

<!-- paragraph:3737 para_id:00000E99 style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes narrowed, the faintest static still crawling across his shoulders. “When?” he asked, his tone cold and precise.

<!-- paragraph:3738 para_id:00000E9A style:Normal -->
Maya swallowed, forcing herself to meet his gaze. “I don’t know. But soon.”

<!-- paragraph:3739 para_id:00000E9B style:Normal -->
For a moment, silence stretched, taut as a drawn bowstring. Then Niall’s jaw tightened. “The Drakhals could never coordinate something like this so quickly,” he said flatly. His gaze shifted past her, toward the middle distance, voice lowering as if speaking half to himself. “We’ve been politically outmaneuvered since Presentation. I should have seen it.”

<!-- paragraph:3740 para_id:00000E9C style:Normal -->
His expression sealed over again. His voice was calm, hard-edged. “If they’re going to attack, then we need to prepare.” He turned, already moving. “Alden, see to the Registry.”

<!-- paragraph:3741 para_id:00000E9D style:Normal -->
Alden inclined his head, silent.

<!-- paragraph:3742 para_id:00000E9E style:Normal -->
Niall strode to the door, gesturing for one of her guards. “Fetch the wall commanders.” His tone was clipped, final.

<!-- paragraph:3743 para_id:00000E9F style:Normal -->
And then he was gone, the storm he carried receding into the Citadel’s corridors.

<!-- paragraph:3744 para_id:00000EA0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s hands curled, steadying against the chill still rising faintly from the stone. Masks within masks. His. Hers. Would they ever truly see each other? Or were they both destined to live forever behind the armor they had made?

<!-- paragraph:3745 para_id:00000EA1 style:Normal -->
Alden exhaled softly through his nose, his posture relaxing just enough to signal he’d been waiting for Niall to go. “It doesn’t feel like the best time to ask this,” he said, voice level, “but—do you have anything else useful from whatever it was you just saw? Even something small. Was it day or night, at least?”

<!-- paragraph:3746 para_id:00000EA2 style:Normal -->
Maya closed her eyes, pressing her fingers briefly to her temple as she forced herself to recall. Threads, flashes, walls shuddering, the crush of soldiers below. “Day,” she said finally. “That much I know. But nothing else I can trust. It just… felt imminent.”

<!-- paragraph:3747 para_id:00000EA3 style:Normal -->
Alden gave a short nod, his expression unreadable. “Then if that’s accurate, you should focus on getting rest tonight. Tomorrow may not be so forgiving.”

<!-- paragraph:3748 para_id:00000EA4 style:Normal -->
Maya drew a breath, steadying herself. “I’m going to have to stay up and figure out what to do with the ward restoration project, in light of… all this.”

<!-- paragraph:3749 para_id:00000EA5 style:Normal -->
Alden’s gaze sharpened. “Look,” he said quietly, “I respect your ability. Both as a scholar and as a magus. Not because of who you are—but because you deserve it. You’ve earned it.” His eyes flicked—almost imperceptibly—toward the door Niall had stormed through before returning to her. “From everything you’ve endured to be here. But you should rest. You can’t burn yourself out trying to do everything at once.”

<!-- paragraph:3750 para_id:00000EA6 style:Normal -->
Maya’s instinct was to argue, to brace for the debate. But she realized, with a faint twist of surprise, that Alden wasn’t forcing her. He had set the choice in her hands, the way a true advisor should. She could decide. And that meant she didn’t need to fight him.

<!-- paragraph:3751 para_id:00000EA7 style:Normal -->
She rose from her chair instead. “Thank you,” she said. “I’ll take your advice.”

<!-- paragraph:3752 para_id:00000EA8 style:Normal -->
Alden inclined his head, the gesture precise but somehow carrying more respect than before.

<!-- paragraph:3753 para_id:00000EA9 style:Normal -->
She was halfway to the door when his voice stopped her. “One more thing.”

<!-- paragraph:3754 para_id:00000EAA style:Normal -->
She turned.

<!-- paragraph:3755 para_id:00000EAB style:Normal -->
“When we were speaking about Elric,” Alden said slowly, “about how he backed off the birthing contracts because people were concerned—I think he was wrong.”

<!-- paragraph:3756 para_id:00000EAC style:Normal -->
Her brow furrowed. “What does that have to do with this?”

<!-- paragraph:3757 para_id:00000EAD style:Normal -->
“For what it’s worth,” Alden said, meeting her eyes evenly, “I think you did the right thing at Presentation. Sometimes you have to do things people don’t like to change what’s wrong. And you can’t hold back because you’re afraid they’ll retaliate—or won’t agree with you—or won’t like you.”

<!-- paragraph:3758 para_id:00000EAE style:Normal -->
The words lingered, heavier than she expected, as if even Alden knew he’d offered more than his usual rationed counsel.

<!-- paragraph:3759 para_id:00000EAF style:Normal -->
Emotion welled in Maya’s chest, sharp enough she had to bite the inside of her cheek to keep it from showing. This—this was what she had needed to hear. And yet she couldn’t let herself unravel again. Instead, she let the tension soften just enough to offer Alden a real smile. “Thank you,” she said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:3760 para_id:00000EB0 style:Normal -->
She drew in a deep breath, steadying herself, then turned to the door. When she opened it, Erynn and the guards straightened at once. Maya gathered them with a nod and led the way back toward her chambers.

<!-- paragraph:3761 para_id:00000EB1 style:Normal -->
The hallways were hushed, the Citadel heavy with late-night quiet. After a long stretch of silence, Erynn tilted her head and spoke in a deliberately casual tone. “So,” she said, giving Maya a sidelong glance, “I’m guessing a second date with Caius isn’t on the table.”

<!-- paragraph:3762 para_id:00000EB2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s tension snapped in an unexpected burst. She laughed, the sound startling in the quiet. “If there’s a ‘second date,’” she said dryly, “then no matter how nice a vest he shows up in, he’s going back home in an urn.”

<!-- paragraph:3763 para_id:00000EB3 style:Normal -->
Erynn blinked, then her eyes widened as the joke landed. “Ohhhhhh.” She gave an exaggerated sigh. “That is a shame, though. He was really cute.”

<!-- paragraph:3764 para_id:00000EB4 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head, still smiling faintly despite herself, as Erynn helped her prepare for bed. When she finally lay down, the weight of the day pressed into her all at once. Within minutes, sleep took her.

<!-- paragraph:3765 para_id:00000EB5 style:Normal -->
The threads had followed her into her dreams.

<!-- paragraph:3766 para_id:00000EB6 style:Normal -->
Maya drifted in a vast black sea, endless strands shimmering faintly in the dark. They stretched in every direction, humming with futures she couldn’t name, brushing against her skin like spider-silk.

<!-- paragraph:3767 para_id:00000EB7 style:Normal -->
Most fractured when she reached for them—splintering into fire, frost, collapse. Selwin falling. The Citadel walls unraveling. She let them go, but one drew her in.

<!-- paragraph:3768 para_id:00000EB8 style:Normal -->
It pulsed darker than the rest, ink-dark, vibrating steady and low. At the end of it she saw him: the Shade, faceless in his mask, standing silent in shadow. Waiting.

<!-- paragraph:3769 para_id:00000EB9 style:Normal -->
Her chest tightened. She reached, needing to hold onto it, to follow—

<!-- paragraph:3770 para_id:00000EBA style:Normal -->
The thread unraveled in her hands. Ink bled across her palms, winding up her arms in delicate, merciless patterns. It climbed her shoulders, her throat, slid over her eyes.

<!-- paragraph:3771 para_id:00000EBB style:Normal -->
Oblivion swallowed her. Quiet, total, and almost a relief compared to the noise of branching futures.

<!-- paragraph:3772 para_id:00000EBC style:Normal -->
She woke with a gasp. Morning light filtered through her curtains. Erynn was already at her side, gentle and brisk, helping her dress.

<!-- paragraph:3773 para_id:00000EBD style:Normal -->
Erynn slipped into the room just as Maya startled awake. Maya shook her head quickly, as if she could shake loose the remnants of the dream. It was nothing, she told herself. Of course I’d dream about this—after last night, how could I not?

<!-- paragraph:3774 para_id:00000EBE style:Normal -->
“I hope you had a good rest,” Erynn said lightly, already moving toward the wardrobe. “Because I’ve heard today might be exciting.”

<!-- paragraph:3775 para_id:00000EBF style:Normal -->
She was already dressed and polished for the day, every braid and fold precise. Maya blinked at her, still gathering herself as Erynn laid out fresh clothes.

<!-- paragraph:3776 para_id:00000EC0 style:Normal -->
As Erynn helped her dress, Maya murmured, “You always have to go to bed after me and wake up before me, don’t you? Because of your duties.”

<!-- paragraph:3777 para_id:00000EC1 style:Normal -->
“Yes,” Erynn said promptly, her mouth quirking as she fastened a clasp. “Thank you for noticing. It’s the terrible price I pay for not being able to blow up a building with my mind.”

<!-- paragraph:3778 para_id:00000EC2 style:Normal -->
“Well, thank you,” Maya said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:3779 para_id:00000EC3 style:Normal -->
Erynn laughed, fastening the last clasp. “Hey, you pay me for this, remember?”

<!-- paragraph:3780 para_id:00000EC4 style:Normal -->
“Sure,” Maya replied, “but no one is paying you to laugh, to help me bear the tension—and to be my friend.”

<!-- paragraph:3781 para_id:00000EC5 style:Normal -->
That stopped Erynn for a moment. She set the brush aside and pulled Maya into a sudden, firm hug. “Actually,” she whispered near Maya’s ear, “Niall pays me three silver haloes per joke.”

<!-- paragraph:3782 para_id:00000EC6 style:Normal -->
Maya gave her a flat look as she pulled back. “I know that isn’t true. That would be far too much for the quality of your jokes.”

<!-- paragraph:3783 para_id:00000EC7 style:Normal -->
Erynn smirked. “Fair. Also, Niall doesn’t have a sense of humor.”

<!-- paragraph:3784 para_id:00000EC8 style:Normal -->
By the time Maya was dressed, some of the weight pressing on her chest had eased. She and Erynn shared a quiet breakfast in her chambers before she gathered her things and headed out to meet with the ward project supervisors.

<!-- paragraph:3785 para_id:00000EC9 style:Normal -->
Right before entering the meeting, Maya drew a slow breath and fixed her mask into place. She would not stare at Arrek. She would not acknowledge him unless he spoke directly. After the way he’d acted in the last meeting, ignoring him entirely was the only proper course.

<!-- paragraph:3786 para_id:00000ECA style:Normal -->
She addressed the gathered supervisors briskly. “Due to possible upcoming circumstances, the outer wards may be drawing significantly more power.”

<!-- paragraph:3787 para_id:00000ECB style:Normal -->
The room shifted uncomfortably—glances darted, lips pressed tight. They all knew what she was hinting at, though none dared to name it.

<!-- paragraph:3788 para_id:00000ECC style:Normal -->
“We don’t yet know how this will affect the internal wards,” she continued. “So all internal groups are dismissed for today. You’ll remain in the city. The outer ward teams must be prepared to halt and stand down at any time.”

<!-- paragraph:3789 para_id:00000ECD style:Normal -->
She dismissed them with a clipped nod. Chairs scraped, papers shuffled, and the supervisors filed out, murmuring to each other.

<!-- paragraph:3790 para_id:00000ECE style:Normal -->
All but one.

<!-- paragraph:3791 para_id:00000ECF style:Normal -->
Arrek stayed where he was, motionless, lingering like a shadow at the edge of the table. Maya pretended not to notice, flipping deliberately through the papers spread before her. But he didn’t move. He didn’t speak. He just stood there.

<!-- paragraph:3792 para_id:00000ED0 style:Normal -->
Annoyance prickled beneath her composure. Finally she snapped her gaze upward. “What?” she asked, bracing herself for his usual brash insolence.

<!-- paragraph:3793 para_id:00000ED1 style:Normal -->
He was looking at her—but not with arrogance. For a fraction of a second, she caught something else flicker across his face. Surprise, maybe. Something softer. But as soon as her eyes met his, the expression was gone, shuttered behind a neutral mask.

<!-- paragraph:3794 para_id:00000ED2 style:Normal -->
“I want to be reassigned to the outer wards,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:3795 para_id:00000ED3 style:Normal -->
Her brows knit. “That doesn’t make sense. You’re turning down a paid day off.”

<!-- paragraph:3796 para_id:00000ED4 style:Normal -->
“I don’t like being idle.”

<!-- paragraph:3797 para_id:00000ED5 style:Normal -->
She gave him a cool look. “There isn’t time to retrain you.”

<!-- paragraph:3798 para_id:00000ED6 style:Normal -->
“I’m a fast learner,” he replied evenly. “And I doubt the outer ward zän are more complex than the treasury sequences.”

<!-- paragraph:3799 para_id:00000ED7 style:Normal -->
Her irritation sharpened into disbelief. “I’m sure you’ve all figured it out—we’re probably going to be attacked. Why would you volunteer to be out there?”

<!-- paragraph:3800 para_id:00000ED8 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s eyes didn’t waver. “You’re going to be out there, right?”

<!-- paragraph:3801 para_id:00000ED9 style:Normal -->
“Yes,” Maya said, already irritated by the obviousness of the question. “But this is—” The rest spilled out before she could stop it. “This is my whole reason for existing. This is what I was bred for: to kill and kill and kill, to fight, to carry the responsibility of protecting everyone and never make a mistake.”

<!-- paragraph:3802 para_id:00000EDA style:Normal -->
The words hung between them, raw and unpolished, nothing like the perfected mask she wore before the others.

<!-- paragraph:3803 para_id:00000EDB style:Normal -->
Arrek tilted his head slightly. His voice, when it came, was low but certain. “Sometimes it must feel like life just happens to you. Like you’re swept along by responsibility, by family, by everything closing in. Until the endless chaos pours over you and you’re drowning in it.”

<!-- paragraph:3804 para_id:00000EDC style:Normal -->
“I can’t afford to drown,” Maya said, her voice taut, each word fitted into place like stone in a wall. “I am a Rezin. There is strength in my bloodline, and there is no room for error.”

<!-- paragraph:3805 para_id:00000EDD style:Normal -->
Arrek’s gaze sharpened. “So is that it? You’re trying to be your father? Not like he is now, but the legend. The omnipotent Niall—at the apex of all magi, bending nations to his will with perfect strength. The father who was never wrong.” His mouth tightened. “Did that person even exist?”

<!-- paragraph:3806 para_id:00000EDE style:Normal -->
Her mind slid to the memory she had seen in her mother’s journal: Niall standing on the field, lightning cascading from his hands in a single, blinding stroke. The war had ended with that. That slice of perfection, frozen in time, preserved like a painting of glory.

<!-- paragraph:3807 para_id:00000EDF style:Normal -->
But was that his whole life? One stroke? One moment?

<!-- paragraph:3808 para_id:00000EE0 style:Normal -->
She thought of him now, silent and shuttered, lightning flickering at the corners of his eyes. Was that contrast failure—or the cost of being human?

<!-- paragraph:3809 para_id:00000EE1 style:Normal -->
At some point in the exchange, Arrek had edged closer. Maya only realized it when she lifted her gaze and found his eyes fixed on hers, unflinching. The intensity of it stole her breath; thought slipped away, leaving only the warmth that pooled in her chest.

<!-- paragraph:3810 para_id:00000EE2 style:Normal -->
It took her a long moment to swim back through the haze before she found her voice. “Fine. I admit my father is… overbearing at times. He alternates between treating me like an infant and expecting me to do the impossible. Is that what you wanted to hear?”

<!-- paragraph:3811 para_id:00000EE3 style:Normal -->
She meant it to sound sharp, but it came out breathless.

<!-- paragraph:3812 para_id:00000EE4 style:Normal -->
“Yes,” Arrek said, still holding her gaze. “I wanted to understand you better.”

<!-- paragraph:3813 para_id:00000EE5 style:Normal -->
The air between them thickened again, charged and unspoken, until the awareness of how close they stood seemed to break over both of them at once. Arrek shifted back abruptly, bumping the table with his hip. “So… can I work on the outer wards?”

<!-- paragraph:3814 para_id:00000EE6 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, trying to pull herself together. “I’m not sure I understand your argument,” she said at last. “But… fine.”

<!-- paragraph:3815 para_id:00000EE7 style:Normal -->
They looked at each other for one more awkward second, then Arrek inclined his head. “Thanks.” He turned and left, his footsteps echoing in the empty chamber.

<!-- paragraph:3816 para_id:00000EE8 style:Normal -->
Maya stood where she was, pulse quickened, the echo of that moment still lingering like heat beneath her skin.

<!-- paragraph:3820 para_id:00000EEC style:Title -->
# 👫 Chapter 22

<!-- paragraph:3821 para_id:00000EED style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 22

<!-- paragraph:3823 para_id:00000EEF style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3824 para_id:00000EF0 style:Normal -->
Davian left the chamber with his breath oddly short, though he couldn’t say why. He had gotten what he wanted—an assignment to the wall. That should have been enough. Progress. Victory, even.

<!-- paragraph:3825 para_id:00000EF1 style:Normal -->
It makes sense, he told himself as he strode down the corridor. Up on the wall he’d be able to see the attack coming, maybe even slip away in the chaos to get closer to the treasury. If nothing else, it was a chance to scout another piece of the fortress, one more angle for when the time came. Perfectly logical.

<!-- paragraph:3826 para_id:00000EF2 style:Normal -->
But his chest still felt tight, as if some invisible hand had closed around his lungs. His boots rang too loud on the polished stone, each step echoing through the oppressive procession of banners and carved zän. And no matter how hard he tried to focus on tactics and opportunity, his thoughts kept drifting back—to the look on her face, to the moment she’d let the mask slip and spoken of herself as though she were nothing but breeding and duty.

<!-- paragraph:3827 para_id:00000EF3 style:Normal -->
Don’t think about her. He pressed the thought down hard, burying it beneath the weight of the mission. The wards. The vault. The Heart.

<!-- paragraph:3828 para_id:00000EF4 style:Normal -->
When he stepped out into the citadel courtyard, the sunlight felt harsher than it should, glaring off pale stone. Ahead, a small group of zänwrights and guards was already crossing the square toward the outer wall, their conversation drifting in ragged bursts.

<!-- paragraph:3829 para_id:00000EF5 style:Normal -->
“…figured this would happen sooner or later…”
 “…why else pay triple the rate…”
 “…doesn’t mean I signed up to get killed…”

<!-- paragraph:3830 para_id:00000EF6 style:Normal -->
The voices carried the same weary inevitability Davian had grown up with in the Concord: complaints that weren’t meant to change anything, only to make the next step easier. He quickened his pace until he fell in beside them. A few gave him sidelong glances, sizing him up with faint suspicion.

<!-- paragraph:3831 para_id:00000EF7 style:Normal -->
They reached the base of the wall and circled around their supervisor, a broad-shouldered man with cropped gray hair and the permanent squint of someone who spent too much time staring into the sun. The man looked down the line, eyes flicking across each worker, then stopped abruptly when he reached Davian.

<!-- paragraph:3832 para_id:00000EF8 style:Normal -->
“You,” the supervisor said flatly. “What are you doing here?”

<!-- paragraph:3833 para_id:00000EF9 style:Normal -->
Davian kept his posture easy, voice level. “I want to work. If I stayed behind, I’d be bored.”

<!-- paragraph:3834 para_id:00000EFA style:Normal -->
The man blinked at him once, incredulous. Then again, slower, as if trying to decide whether Davian was serious. “That,” he said at last, “is ridiculous.”

<!-- paragraph:3835 para_id:00000EFB style:Normal -->
Davian shrugged, forcing a half-smile. “Look, I already went through all this with Maya. She says it’s fine. Just give me something to do.”

<!-- paragraph:3836 para_id:00000EFC style:Normal -->
The supervisor’s brows shot up. “You mean Lady Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:3837 para_id:00000EFD style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated for the briefest moment, then gave a quick nod. “Right. That’s what I mean.”

<!-- paragraph:3838 para_id:00000EFE style:Normal -->
The man studied him a moment longer, then shook his head with a muttered curse, turning back to the rest of the group.

<!-- paragraph:3839 para_id:00000EFF style:Normal -->
The man studied him a moment longer, then shook his head with a muttered curse, turning back to the rest of the group.

<!-- paragraph:3840 para_id:00000F00 style:Normal -->
“Fine,” he said, throwing up his hands. “I guess the more of us up there, the more bodies to soak up any stray arrows. But hear me now—if you jeopardize me, or my men, by trying to play the hero for whatever silly reason you’ve got rattling around in your head, I’ll toss you off the wall myself.”

<!-- paragraph:3841 para_id:00000F01 style:Normal -->
Davian rolled his eyes, keeping the motion loose and careless. “Look, just let me help, all right? If the Rezins get wiped out, none of us get paid.”

<!-- paragraph:3842 para_id:00000F02 style:Normal -->
The supervisor held his stare, jaw working, as if weighing whether the trouble was worth it. At length he grunted and turned back to the circle.

<!-- paragraph:3843 para_id:00000F03 style:Normal -->
The supervisor barked out assignments, passing around scraps of paper scrawled with rough diagrams of the wall’s weak points. Davian’s slip was hastily drawn, more an afterthought than a plan, and told him to reinforce and double-check existing zän. Busywork. But at least it would keep him where he wanted to be.

<!-- paragraph:3844 para_id:00000F04 style:Normal -->
Soon enough he was on the parapet, wind tugging at his cloak as the city stretched out on one side and open sky yawned on the other. He crouched to trace the glowing edges of wards etched into the stone, running his hand along fine cracks and testing for strain in the zänwork. Now and then he had to sling a rope around one of the crenellations and lower himself down the inside face of the wall, bracing against smooth stone while adjusting zän worn by weather and time.

<!-- paragraph:3845 para_id:00000F05 style:Normal -->
Lightly armored runners darted past every few minutes, eyes sharp as they scanned the horizon, relaying signals to keep command posted on any hint of movement beyond the walls. Down below, in the broad courtyards, companies of soldiers were gathering. But instead of forming ranks in armor, they were stacking weapons into neat rows, organizing shields and supplies into ready depots. They weren’t preparing to stand all day on alert—they were waiting for the signal. They clearly didn’t know exactly when the attack was coming.

<!-- paragraph:3846 para_id:00000F06 style:Normal -->
Davian hauled himself back over the parapet after finishing another stretch of zän, boots scraping against the stone. He straightened—and froze.

<!-- paragraph:3847 para_id:00000F07 style:Normal -->
Harvick was standing a few paces away, arms folded, expression wry.

<!-- paragraph:3848 para_id:00000F08 style:Normal -->
“Oh,” Harvick said, “When did this happen?”

<!-- paragraph:3849 para_id:00000F09 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave him a sidelong look. “I’d ask you to explain yourself,” he said, then glanced down into the courtyard below. Niall was striding across the flagstones, Maya a pace behind him, her Shroud flickering faintly in the sunlight. Harvick’s mouth tugged into a dry half-smile. “But I can guess what you’re up to.”

<!-- paragraph:3850 para_id:00000F0A style:Normal -->
Davian’s jaw tightened. “I don’t know what you mean. I just get bored easily.”

<!-- paragraph:3851 para_id:00000F0B style:Normal -->
Harvick rolled his eyes. “Sure. You’re okay with a sword—”

<!-- paragraph:3852 para_id:00000F0C style:Normal -->
Davian shot him a glare sharp enough to cut steel.

<!-- paragraph:3853 para_id:00000F0D style:Normal -->
“—but don’t get yourself killed by some magus because you want to show off,” Harvick finished, unfazed. “You’ll have plenty of time to get blown up by magic later, if you decide to join our guard.”

<!-- paragraph:3854 para_id:00000F0E style:Normal -->
Davian snorted, resting a hand on the parapet. “You know, we’re on this side of the wall, with two of the most powerful magi who’ve ever lived. What possible temptation could there be for anyone out there to attack? Most of them are mercenaries—it seems insane.”

<!-- paragraph:3855 para_id:00000F0F style:Normal -->
Harvick leaned forward, elbows on the stone, gaze fixed on the hazy horizon. “You’re right about that. Normally mercenaries know they’ll take losses going at a citadel, so you just hope it’s not your company that gets turned into ash. When the magus shows up, you pull back. Best you can do is try to redirect them, limit the exposure of your men to whatever nightmare they’re throwing around that day.”

<!-- paragraph:3856 para_id:00000F10 style:Normal -->
“But attacking the Rezins again,” Davian pressed, “when not only is Niall here, but Maya’s clearly powerful… it must be unbelievably expensive. It doesn’t add up. What could they possibly gain?”

<!-- paragraph:3857 para_id:00000F11 style:Normal -->
For a moment Harvick said nothing. He turned his head, eyes skimming over the sprawl of rooftops and streets on the city side of the wall. Davian caught the subtle shift—the way Harvick’s posture stiffened, the flicker of something he was hiding.

<!-- paragraph:3858 para_id:00000F12 style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” Harvick said at last, voice oddly flat. “I don’t get it either.”

<!-- paragraph:3859 para_id:00000F13 style:Normal -->
Davian let the answer hang. He wasn’t about to push.

<!-- paragraph:3860 para_id:00000F14 style:Normal -->
His eyes drifted to the sword strapped across Harvick’s back. It wasn’t one he’d seen before. The weapon was long and black as onyx, its surface etched with zän that shimmered faintly in the light. At first glance it echoed Antithesis, but heavier, broader through the spine. And unlike Antithesis’s clean taper, this blade swept forward near the end, giving its edge a brutal, hooked weight—less like a duelist’s weapon and more like something meant to hack through armor.

<!-- paragraph:3861 para_id:00000F15 style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes caught a flash of matching onyx on Harvick’s wrist—a broad bracer, its surface etched with the same faint zän as the blade. He frowned. “I’ve never seen you use something like that. What’s it for?”

<!-- paragraph:3862 para_id:00000F16 style:Normal -->
Harvick turned back, unhooking the sword from his back with an easy motion. “This?” He held it out, the weight of it obvious even in his grip. “It’s kind of a toy. I just like to carry it during times like these.”

<!-- paragraph:3863 para_id:00000F17 style:Normal -->
He angled the blade so Davian could see the forward curve of its edge. “The idea is simple enough: a heavier sword, reinforced with zän, has a better chance of punching through a Shroud. More mass means more force spread across the surface. At least, that’s the theory.”

<!-- paragraph:3864 para_id:00000F18 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned closer, eyes narrowing at the runes glowing faintly along its spine. “I’ve never seen those zän before.”

<!-- paragraph:3865 para_id:00000F19 style:Normal -->
“Yeah, well,” Harvick said, giving a crooked grin. “That’s because it’s an expensive toy. They probably didn’t bother teaching you these—etching material costs a fortune, and you need a magus to charge them. One specific magus, actually. This was Niall’s design, back in the day.”

<!-- paragraph:3866 para_id:00000F1A style:Normal -->
Before Davian could reply, Harvick shifted his grip, heaving the sword in an overhand throw. The blade whirled end-over-end, vanishing briefly into the sky beyond the wall. Davian’s mouth twitched in surprise—then the bracer’s zän flared to life. Harvick extended his hand, and with a sudden crack of displaced air the sword curved back, slapping neatly into his palm.

<!-- paragraph:3867 para_id:00000F1B style:Normal -->
He gave Davian a satisfied smile. “There are some other tricks with it, too.”

<!-- paragraph:3868 para_id:00000F1C style:Normal -->
Davian raised an eyebrow. “You’re right. That is a toy.” He paused, tilting his head. “You ever kill a magus with it?”

<!-- paragraph:3869 para_id:00000F1D style:Normal -->
Harvick barked a laugh, shaking his head. “Nah. This isn’t how I’d do it. The idea’s good, but the power just isn’t anywhere near enough to get through even a weak Shroud. If I had to kill a magus, I’d just use a slow poison and a fast horse out of their citadel.”

<!-- paragraph:3870 para_id:00000F1E style:Normal -->
Davian arched a brow. “Would that actually work?”

<!-- paragraph:3871 para_id:00000F1F style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged, lips quirking. “How would I know? It’s not like I’ve ever done it.”

<!-- paragraph:3872 para_id:00000F20 style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes narrowed. “Isn’t it your job to keep them safe? You’d have to consider what you’re trying to protect them from.”

<!-- paragraph:3873 para_id:00000F21 style:Normal -->
“That’s the thing.” Harvick leaned back against the parapet, the black sword balanced casually in his hand. “This kind of information just… doesn’t exist. There are only a handful of High Clades in the whole world, and they sure as hell aren’t going to cooperate to research what can kill them. And if someone did figure it out, they wouldn’t share it. Wouldn’t even write it down.”

<!-- paragraph:3874 para_id:00000F22 style:Normal -->
Davian huffed out a short breath, half a laugh. “Fine. So what’s the plan, then? You up here with your special magic returning sword, planning to throw it at mercenaries?”

<!-- paragraph:3875 para_id:00000F23 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s grin faded, his gaze drifting out toward the horizon. “No. I don’t do that anymore—not like this.” He slung the blade back over his shoulder, the onyx bracer catching the light as he lowered his arm. “I’ll be inside, keeping an eye on the Registry and helping Alden coordinate. Truth is, I don’t think I’d be much help out here.”

<!-- paragraph:3876 para_id:00000F24 style:Normal -->
He rested a hand on the parapet, eyes tracing the jagged line where city met sky. “But I like to walk the wall before a battle. Remind myself that I still can.”

<!-- paragraph:3877 para_id:00000F25 style:Normal -->
The low blare of horns rolled along the wall, cutting through the chatter of runners and the steady hum of wards. Harvick straightened, the easy weight in his posture hardening into something sharper.

<!-- paragraph:3878 para_id:00000F26 style:Normal -->
“Okay,” he said, clapping Davian once on the shoulder. “Remember—don’t be stupid. I know that’s hard for you, but if you die, I’ll have wasted a lot of time teaching you sword work.”

<!-- paragraph:3879 para_id:00000F27 style:Normal -->
Davian smirked. “Promising not to be stupid is an impossible promise coming from a stupid person and irrelevant if it’s coming from a smart one.”

<!-- paragraph:3880 para_id:00000F28 style:Normal -->
Harvick paused, brow furrowing. Then he gave a small snort. “I don’t have time to rebut that, but I’m pretty sure it’s stupid.” He turned and jogged toward the stairwell, cloak snapping in the wind.

<!-- paragraph:3881 para_id:00000F29 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned back against the parapet, the horns still echoing in his chest, and tightened his grip on the rope beside him.

<!-- paragraph:3882 para_id:00000F2A style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3883 para_id:00000F2B style:Normal -->
The horns blared across the courtyard, deep and urgent. Maya felt the sound ripple through her chest, setting her nerves alight. Soldiers snapped to motion around them, runners already sprinting for the stairwells.

<!-- paragraph:3884 para_id:00000F2C style:Normal -->
Beside her, Niall’s expression didn’t change, but his voice cut like a blade. “Harlun—north sector. Don’t over-commit until you’ve confirmed their numbers. I won’t have us caught off guard by a feint.”

<!-- paragraph:3885 para_id:00000F2D style:Normal -->
“Yes, my lord.” The commander bowed sharply before vanishing into the surge of movement.

<!-- paragraph:3886 para_id:00000F2E style:Normal -->
Maya stepped forward, her pulse quickening. “Where do you want me?” she asked. “Tell me where to stand. I can help hold the wall.”

<!-- paragraph:3887 para_id:00000F2F style:Normal -->
For the briefest moment, Niall’s stony mask cracked. Fear flickered across his features, raw and unguarded. “You can’t go out there,” he said, the words too fast, too sharp. “It’s the Drakhals. You’ve never fought other magi—”

<!-- paragraph:3888 para_id:00000F30 style:Normal -->
“Snap out of it,” Maya said, her voice hard. “I already fought Caius. This is my duty. I won’t stand back here and pretend nothing’s at stake. This is what you bred me for. What you trained me for.”

<!-- paragraph:3889 para_id:00000F31 style:Normal -->
Something in him shifted at the words. His gaze flicked away for a breath, and when it returned the panic was gone—buried under iron. His eyes were hard again, his tone colder. “You’re right. This is what I made you for. Today you must be flawless. Show them no weakness. We’ll drive the Drakhals back and prove they were fools to test us.”

<!-- paragraph:3890 para_id:00000F32 style:Normal -->
He set a heavy hand on her shoulder, his grip firm, almost possessive. “I know you can be perfect.”

<!-- paragraph:3891 para_id:00000F33 style:Normal -->
The words landed like a stone on her chest. Outwardly, she straightened, chin high, because that was what he expected. She told herself to take it as encouragement, to let his certainty steady her. And yet the words carried a strange weight, pressing down instead of lifting. If she stumbled—if she failed to be what he demanded—what would be left of her?

<!-- paragraph:3892 para_id:00000F34 style:Normal -->
Still, she nodded. There was no other answer.

<!-- paragraph:3893 para_id:00000F35 style:Normal -->
“I’ll cover the north with Harlun,” Niall said, his voice clipped. Without waiting for reply, he strode across the courtyard, lightning already sparking faintly along the long sleeves of his military coat.

<!-- paragraph:3894 para_id:00000F36 style:Normal -->
Enver lingered behind, his expression tight with uncertainty. Maya straightened, lifting her chin, and forced her posture into something steady, commanding. She imagined what her father would want to see—what he would expect to see—and let that shape her voice.

<!-- paragraph:3895 para_id:00000F37 style:Normal -->
“I’ll cover the south with you,” she told Enver, calm and certain.

<!-- paragraph:3896 para_id:00000F38 style:Normal -->
As if the city itself had been listening, the horns blared again—this time from the southern wall.

<!-- paragraph:3897 para_id:00000F39 style:Normal -->
Maya allowed herself the faintest smile. “See you there.”

<!-- paragraph:3898 para_id:00000F3A style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3899 para_id:00000F3B style:Normal -->
By the time Davian climbed down from the wall, the horns were echoing from both ends of the fortress. He gave the wall supervisor a perfunctory report, then slipped away as the other zänwrights hurried toward their fortified barracks, voices tight with nerves. The south and north both under attack—no one would be sleeping tonight.

<!-- paragraph:3900 para_id:00000F3C style:Normal -->
He paused in the courtyard, weighing the noise rolling down from the walls. From what he knew of the Registry, it would have to be switched into passive mode the moment the attackers reached the stone. No choice—the strain of tracking so many auras at once would burn it out otherwise. Someone would still be monitoring, of course, but not with the same precision. And in chaos like this, a shadow could move farther than it should.

<!-- paragraph:3901 para_id:00000F3D style:Normal -->
His gaze dropped to his forearms. The Boons coiled there like living serpents beneath his skin, faint lines of ink curling tighter each day. Even the renewed one—the mark that let him wear Arrek’s aura—was beginning to fray.

<!-- paragraph:3902 para_id:00000F3E style:Normal -->
If I move now, that’s it. The thought cut through him, cold and certain. If he went for the treasury, if he tried to reach the Heart, he had to assume his cover would burn with it. There’d be no more hiding inside the citadel. No more pretending he belonged here.

<!-- paragraph:3903 para_id:00000F3F style:Normal -->
He drew a slow breath and fixed his course. Not to the zänwrights’ barracks—too far from where he needed to be. Instead, he turned his steps toward the one closest to the central tower. From there, he could watch. See how the night unfolded. He might not get another chance like this.

<!-- paragraph:3904 para_id:00000F40 style:Normal -->
An idle thought curled at the edges of his mind, foolish and impossible. Maybe I could blame it on the Drakhals. Slip my true name into the Registry during the confusion. Stay here. Keep…

<!-- paragraph:3905 para_id:00000F41 style:Normal -->
He cut the fantasy short. Even as it took shape, he knew it wouldn’t hold. The Registry did not lie, and the Clades would never let a Concord bastard nestle into their walls.

<!-- paragraph:3906 para_id:00000F42 style:Normal -->
Still, for a moment, he let himself imagine it. Then he shook it off and kept walking, the barracks looming ahead through the smoke and the horns.

<!-- paragraph:3907 para_id:00000F43 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3908 para_id:00000F44 style:Normal -->
Maya dropped onto the wall, her Shroud flaring outward to catch and soften the landing. The stone beneath her boots cracked with the force, frost spiderwebbing out from her soles before she pulled it back in.

<!-- paragraph:3909 para_id:00000F45 style:Normal -->
A scout at the parapet spun toward her, eyes wide. “How—”

<!-- paragraph:3910 para_id:00000F46 style:Normal -->
“What have you seen?” she cut in, her voice sharper than she meant.

<!-- paragraph:3911 para_id:00000F47 style:Normal -->
He blinked, then pointed southward. “Several columns moving in. They’re hidden by the houses now, but they’ll have to cross the open ground around the citadel. They should be here any—”

<!-- paragraph:3912 para_id:00000F48 style:Normal -->
“Clear the wall around me.”

<!-- paragraph:3913 para_id:00000F49 style:Normal -->
The words carried more weight than her years. He jumped back instantly, scrambling to give her space.

<!-- paragraph:3914 para_id:00000F4A style:Normal -->
Maya drew herself inward, closing her eyes for a breath. She pictured the wall beneath her feet, every vein of stone running down into the earth, and the air above it—thin, restless, hungry. With a single thought, she linked them.

<!-- paragraph:3915 para_id:00000F4B style:Normal -->
Cold crept outward in jagged lines from her boots, racing across the battlements. The nearest soldier stumbled back with a startled cry as ice glazed the stones.

<!-- paragraph:3916 para_id:00000F4C style:Normal -->
Maya opened her eyes. The first enemy column had broken from cover, spilling out into the open ground. The killing field.

<!-- paragraph:3917 para_id:00000F4D style:Normal -->
She began tracing lines with her mind, threads of possibility carved into the fabric of the world. Heat bled from the wall beneath her, flooding into the air before her chest. She held it there, forcing it to pool, to circle, to compress. At the same time, her focus pulled in two directions—holding her Shroud taut around her body, ready to turn aside arrows, to harden her limbs, to keep her alive.

<!-- paragraph:3918 para_id:00000F4E style:Normal -->
Her thoughts accelerated. The exercises she’d practiced for years—delicate balances, steady channels—suddenly became feral, urgent, real. She ripped new lines open with every breath, straining to funnel more and more warmth into the growing sphere. Her mind drew tight loops, folding heat back on itself, containing it, recirculating it until the air in front of her pulsed and writhed with invisible fire.

<!-- paragraph:3919 para_id:00000F4F style:Normal -->
It fought her, straining to obey its nature, to burst and expand. The pressure built, each second heavier, each heartbeat louder.

<!-- paragraph:3920 para_id:00000F50 style:Normal -->
And then the enemy broke into a charge.

<!-- paragraph:3921 para_id:00000F51 style:Normal -->
Maya slashed a line through her construct, carving an outlet. The sphere screamed as the constraint unraveled, all its hunger ripping down the channel she’d drawn.

<!-- paragraph:3922 para_id:00000F52 style:Normal -->
The earth in the killing field convulsed.

<!-- paragraph:3923 para_id:00000F53 style:Normal -->
An eruption of fire tore upward from the soil beneath the charging troops, a white-gold column that swallowed them whole. The soldiers caught at the center vanished instantly, bodies consumed to ash. Those at the edges were hurled screaming through the air, armor glowing, weapons clattering as they spun across the field.

<!-- paragraph:3924 para_id:00000F54 style:Normal -->
The gout subsided in an instant, leaving only scorched ground and the echo of ragged silence before the next wave of troops came into view.

<!-- paragraph:3925 para_id:00000F55 style:Normal -->
Maya’s lungs burned. Her Shroud flickered, demanding more of her. She pulled another breath, drew more lines, and prepared to carve again.

<!-- paragraph:3926 para_id:00000F56 style:Normal -->
Maya’s lungs pulled in air easily enough—her body was steady, her limbs strong within the Shroud—but her mind throbbed with the effort. Every line she’d drawn, every loop she’d forced into place, had taken focus sharpened to a razor’s edge.

<!-- paragraph:3927 para_id:00000F57 style:Normal -->
The enemy soldiers surged forward, but to her eyes they seemed to crawl. Her thoughts moved faster than their charge, faster than their lives could measure. She was already weaving again before they cleared the smoke, gritting her teeth as she traced new channels.

<!-- paragraph:3928 para_id:00000F58 style:Normal -->
Stone bled its warmth into her control. Air thickened with the pressure of redirected energy. She spun it tighter this time, with fewer loops, more precision, fighting the drag in her own mind as though pushing through a storm. Perfection, she told herself. No mistakes. No hesitation. That was what her father demanded. What she had to be.

<!-- paragraph:3929 para_id:00000F59 style:Normal -->
The sphere quivered under her hold, straining against the unnatural stillness she’d forced on it. She released it again with a flick of her will.

<!-- paragraph:3930 para_id:00000F5A style:Normal -->
Fire erupted once more, a pillar tearing the ground open beneath the second column. Men were swallowed whole, others hurled like broken dolls across the field. Screams bled into the thunder of collapsing formations as the survivors scattered. The charge dissolved into a rout, the enemy line fracturing under the weight of what she had unleashed.

<!-- paragraph:3931 para_id:00000F5B style:Normal -->
From below came the shrill cries of panic—“Magus! Magus!”—as the Drakhal troops broke and ran. Their voices carried raw fear, and for a moment Maya only watched, the words striking her like a chorus of recognition.

<!-- paragraph:3932 para_id:00000F5C style:Normal -->
This time, it had taken only a heartbeat to scatter them. A single weave of lines, a single release, and the field was hers. Weeks ago she had stood on the Rezin walls trembling, uncertain, a girl wrapped in lessons and expectations. Now she was something else—something closer to perfect.

<!-- paragraph:3933 para_id:00000F5D style:Normal -->
She lifted her hand again, tracing one more channel into the threads before her. Fire surged downward, exploding amid the retreating soldiers, flinging bodies like leaves in a gale. Maya smiled, her eyes gleaming as the energy coursed through her. The thrill of it burned brighter than the flames below.

<!-- paragraph:3934 para_id:00000F5E style:Normal -->
Another series of horn blasts split the air, signaling a new assault.

<!-- paragraph:3935 para_id:00000F5F style:Normal -->
Maya spun toward the nearby scout, crouched low by the parapet. His face had gone chalk white, his eyes fixed on the distance. “What does that sequence mean?” Maya demanded, striding up to him. “What section of the wall?” He only stared, frozen, the color draining from his knuckles as he gripped the stone.

<!-- paragraph:3936 para_id:00000F60 style:Normal -->
She seized him by the shoulder and shook hard, her voice cutting like steel. “Where is it? I need to be there!”

<!-- paragraph:3937 para_id:00000F61 style:Normal -->
The scout blinked hard, the shock finally breaking. Maya noticed then—the tips of his boots glittered with frost. He’d barely stumbled back, nearly caught in the ice that had spread from her feet.

<!-- paragraph:3938 para_id:00000F62 style:Normal -->
With a shuddering breath, he lifted a trembling finger and pointed.

<!-- paragraph:3939 para_id:00000F63 style:Normal -->
Maya released her grip, her own hands still tight with urgency. “Good,” she said, her voice steady, loud enough for the others on the wall to hear. “I’ll deal with it.”

<!-- paragraph:3940 para_id:00000F64 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3941 para_id:00000F65 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned against the rough stone of the barracks, arms folded. The courtyard seethed with chaos—troops sprinting past in both directions, runners shouting over each other, officers trying to carve order from the noise. In the press of it all, no one had reason to question him.

<!-- paragraph:3942 para_id:00000F66 style:Normal -->
The first explosion shook the south wall, a thunderclap that rattled through the flagstones. A minute later, another erupted on the far side of the citadel, so distant he felt the tremor before he heard it. Then came the crackle—lightning arcing from a cloudless sky, lancing down into the north.

<!-- paragraph:3943 para_id:00000F67 style:Normal -->
Horns blared in dizzying succession, their calls unintelligible to him but sharp enough to send cohorts of soldiers rushing in fresh waves toward the stairwells. Davian stayed where he was, watching, listening. The blasts kept coming—explosions flaring from every quarter of the citadel, one after another, never more than a short pause between them.

<!-- paragraph:3944 para_id:00000F68 style:Normal -->
His brows knit. How is that possible? Was Maya moving this quickly, carving through threat after threat without pause? He’d never heard of a magus respond to so many assaults with such force, in so little time. Even Niall, in all the stories, hadn’t done this.

<!-- paragraph:3945 para_id:00000F69 style:Normal -->
She really is his daughter.

<!-- paragraph:3946 para_id:00000F6A style:Normal -->
A flicker of worry stirred in him—but only for a moment. Whatever else she was, Maya was holding the walls. Clearly. Definitively. If anything, he should be more worried about the Drakhals—whether they’d committed any of their own magi yet. He hadn’t seen a sign of them. Not yet.

<!-- paragraph:3947 para_id:00000F6B style:Normal -->
Time stretched. He stayed leaned against the wall as the battle played out above, the rhythm of horns and detonations carrying through the stone like a second heartbeat.

<!-- paragraph:3948 para_id:00000F6C style:Normal -->
Eventually, a knot of exhausted soldiers stumbled into the courtyard, collapsing onto the ground not far from him. Their armor was scorched, their faces streaked with sweat and ash. One of them let out a bitter laugh.

<!-- paragraph:3949 para_id:00000F6D style:Normal -->
“Never seen an attack like this before,” he muttered. “Every direction, and it just keeps coming.”

<!-- paragraph:3950 para_id:00000F6E style:Normal -->
Another spat into the dirt. “Lady Maya’s giving it to them, though. They’ll think twice before they come near these walls again.”

<!-- paragraph:3951 para_id:00000F6F style:Normal -->
A third leaned back, wiping grime from his brow. “She must be tired, though. Moving that fast—how is she everywhere at once and still tearing through them like that?”

<!-- paragraph:3952 para_id:00000F70 style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse ticked up. If she’s tiring… and Niall too…

<!-- paragraph:3953 para_id:00000F71 style:Normal -->
His gaze drifted toward the central spire. The treasury was there. The Heart was there. And Niall…

<!-- paragraph:3954 para_id:00000F72 style:Normal -->
This might be it—the moment he’d been waiting for. If he moved now, while they were both stretched thin. He might not get another chance like this.

<!-- paragraph:3955 para_id:00000F73 style:Normal -->
The thought burned through him, sharper than any hornblast. Another thought whispered in its wake—Maya, tired and far away, wouldn’t have to see it happen.

<!-- paragraph:3956 para_id:00000F74 style:Normal -->
Davian kept his pace casual, slipping into the shadowed edges of the courtyard as he angled toward the central tower. He moved like a man with purpose but not urgency, head slightly bowed, eyes fixed ahead—close enough to blend in with the chaos around him, distant enough to avoid scrutiny.

<!-- paragraph:3957 para_id:00000F75 style:Normal -->
Every step he rehearsed excuses in his head. Lies stacked on lies, and none of them likely to hold if the guards pressed him. His hand rested on the hilt of Antithesis, thumb brushing the carved zän in its grip. He prayed he wouldn’t need it here—not yet.

<!-- paragraph:3958 para_id:00000F76 style:Normal -->
But as he rounded the final row of buildings, he slowed. The great doors of the spire stood open, their iron-banded wood hanging loose on splintered hinges. No guards. No challenge.

<!-- paragraph:3959 para_id:00000F77 style:Normal -->
A chill ran through him.

<!-- paragraph:3960 para_id:00000F78 style:Normal -->
He crept closer, peering past the threshold. Inside, the entry hall was littered with bodies—Rezin soldiers, broken and bloodied. Some had been hurled into the walls with such force their armor had buckled. Others lay in twisted heaps, limbs bent in ways bones weren’t meant to bend.

<!-- paragraph:3961 para_id:00000F79 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped carefully over the nearest corpse, his breath shallow, each movement a deliberate attempt to melt into the shadows.

<!-- paragraph:3962 para_id:00000F7A style:Normal -->
Then, from a darkened doorway ahead, a figure emerged.

<!-- paragraph:3963 para_id:00000F7B style:Normal -->
A zä flared to life on the man’s cheek as he stepped into view, lines of pale light sketching across rough features. He was young—shaved head gleaming with sweat and blood, his broad face split by a grin too wide for the moment. A sleeveless vest showed thick, scarred arms, and both forearms were sheathed in massive black gauntlets. Runes glimmered along their length, the heavy plating ending in sharp, clawed fingertips.

<!-- paragraph:3964 para_id:00000F7C style:Normal -->
“Well,” the man said, voice rough and easy, “sorry, boy, but now I gotta kill you.”

<!-- paragraph:3965 para_id:00000F7D style:Normal -->
He didn’t look sorry at all.

<!-- paragraph:3966 para_id:00000F7E style:Normal -->
