Davian’s chest hammered. A magus. No—worse. The way the zä blazed, the way the man carried himself, it had to be one of Archeon. A “super-shroud,” one of the breed that boasted they could turn their bodies into weapons strong enough to rival any sorcery.

<!-- paragraph:3967 para_id:00000F7F style:Normal -->
Davian lifted Antithesis, his arms trembling but steady, determination scraping against the fear clawing up his throat. If he was about to die, he’d at least carve one mark into this monster.

<!-- paragraph:3968 para_id:00000F80 style:Normal -->
The man’s grin widened as his gaze dropped to Davian’s forearms. His eyes caught the ink coiling there, and they lit with a sharp, delighted hunger.

<!-- paragraph:3969 para_id:00000F81 style:Normal -->
“Ohhh,” he said, dragging out the sound like a savoring breath. “I’ve heard about you. From our… mutual friends.”

<!-- paragraph:3970 para_id:00000F82 style:Normal -->
Davian froze.

<!-- paragraph:3971 para_id:00000F83 style:Normal -->
“Go along, then,” the magus said, waving one clawed hand toward the inner hall. “I hear you’ve got some way into the treasury. I’ll just keep doing what I’ve been doing. I’ve got the easy job—killing anyone stupid enough to try and leave the Central Spire.”

<!-- paragraph:3972 para_id:00000F84 style:Normal -->
Davian edged sideways, Antithesis still raised between them. The man didn’t move, only pivoted at the waist to keep him in sight. His grin never faltered, those shark-like eyes tracking every step as Davian inched toward the far door.

<!-- paragraph:3973 para_id:00000F85 style:Normal -->
“This feels too good to be true,” Davian said carefully. His voice sounded steadier than he felt. “What’s the point of this? You here just to… help me?”

<!-- paragraph:3974 para_id:00000F86 style:Normal -->
The magus barked a laugh, the sound echoing off the blood-smeared walls. “Help you? No, boy. I’m here to make sure no little Registry runners get out with their warnings. And if that silly little bitch decides to bolt for safety—” he lifted one gauntleted fist, flexing the clawed fingers so the runes shimmered with a hungry light—“then I’ll be waiting. A nice little surprise for her.”

<!-- paragraph:3975 para_id:00000F87 style:Normal -->
He smiled again, and this time Davian noticed the flecks of blood spattered across his teeth.

<!-- paragraph:3976 para_id:00000F88 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:3977 para_id:00000F89 style:Normal -->
The wall trembled under her knees as another blast ripped through the enemy line below. Maya forced herself upright, chest heaving, but her limbs quivered with the strain. She’d lost count of how many times she’d sprinted the parapets, tracing and releasing until her vision blurred and her mind screamed for rest.

<!-- paragraph:3978 para_id:00000F8A style:Normal -->
A horn of water pressed into her hand. She drank greedily, then thrust it back, forcing herself to straighten. The scout’s worried eyes lingered on her, but she gave him nothing—her mask steady, her posture defiant. She couldn’t afford weakness. Not now.

<!-- paragraph:3979 para_id:00000F8B style:Normal -->
She turned back to the killing field, wondering how much longer this tide could keep crashing against the walls. Her thoughts dragged, sluggish, as if each calculation was being pulled through tar.

<!-- paragraph:3980 para_id:00000F8C style:Normal -->
When she looked back, her breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:3981 para_id:00000F8D style:Normal -->
An old woman stood on the wall.

<!-- paragraph:3982 para_id:00000F8E style:Normal -->
Through the haze of exhaustion, Maya felt a strange pull of recognition—like seeing someone from a half-forgotten dream. It was so absurd it almost broke through her fatigue: an elderly woman, thin and stooped, standing among soldiers and blood.

<!-- paragraph:3983 para_id:00000F8F style:Normal -->
The scout noticed her gaze, pivoted, and his face drained of color. “Magus!” he shouted, steel rasping as he drew his sword. A sigil flared across the woman’s cheek, carving her face into a grotesque smile.

<!-- paragraph:3984 para_id:00000F90 style:Normal -->
She moved.

<!-- paragraph:3985 para_id:00000F91 style:Normal -->
In a single motion, the old woman blurred forward, backhanding the scout with enough force to fling him screaming over the parapet. His sword spun after him, glinting once before vanishing.

<!-- paragraph:3986 para_id:00000F92 style:Normal -->
Maya flared her Shroud just as a bare foot swept toward her head. The impact thundered through her, Shroud colliding against Shroud. The force hurled her off the wall, the world tilting into a smear of sky and stone as she flew backward toward the spire.

<!-- paragraph:3987 para_id:00000F93 style:Normal -->
Shock cut through the fog of her fatigue. She twisted, shaping her Shroud again, bracing. Her body smashed through the tiled roof of an outbuilding, debris scattering like shrapnel, then punched through a wall before she tumbled across the cobblestones of a courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:3988 para_id:00000F94 style:Normal -->
Her Shroud dulled the pain, but not the violence. She rolled with the momentum, boots scraping, and forced herself to her feet, gasping, eyes snapping upward.

<!-- paragraph:3989 para_id:00000F95 style:Normal -->
A voice dropped into the courtyard, casual and amused. “Not bad.”

<!-- paragraph:3990 para_id:00000F96 style:Normal -->
She barely had time to leap aside before a gauntleted fist cratered the stone where she’d stood, fragments pelting her face and arms.

<!-- paragraph:3991 para_id:00000F97 style:Normal -->
Maya spun, breath ragged, and saw him—close-cropped hair, the sides of his head shaved to stubble, a sleeveless vest stretched across a broad chest. His arms gleamed with heavy black gauntlets, runes burning faintly along their length.

<!-- paragraph:3992 para_id:00000F98 style:Normal -->
A second impact shook the ground. The old woman landed beside him in a crouch, rising with impossible grace. She smacked the back of his head with a gnarled hand, scolding.

<!-- paragraph:3993 para_id:00000F99 style:Normal -->
“Idiot! I told you to stop giving yourself away. This could have been over already.”

<!-- paragraph:3994 para_id:00000F9A style:Normal -->
The man straightened, rolling one shoulder as though loosening a kink. His grin widened, eyes glittering with manic delight.

<!-- paragraph:3995 para_id:00000F9B style:Normal -->
“Come on,” he drawled, voice carrying the easy confidence of someone who had never lost a fight. “We never get to do this, and you want to make it boring? Plan, plan, plan—that’s all you ever do. Why can’t we just fight and see what happens?”

<!-- paragraph:3996 para_id:00000F9C style:Normal -->
The old woman didn’t even glance at him. Her gaze fixed on Maya, sharp and cold. “She’s too tired to fight—that’s the whole point.” Her words cut with the weight of command. She tilted her head toward the man, her expression sour. “Now do your job and help me kill her, or I’ll give the heir spot to your brother.”

<!-- paragraph:3997 para_id:00000F9D style:Normal -->
The man’s grin twitched, the faintest crack in his bravado, though his fists clenched with eagerness all the same.

<!-- paragraph:3998 para_id:00000F9E style:Normal -->
Maya’s Shroud flickered, straining against the pounding of her heart. Her mind screamed with fatigue, but her body coiled anyway, instinct pulling her into stance.

<!-- paragraph:3999 para_id:00000F9F style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4000 para_id:00000FA0 style:Normal -->
The first thing that hit Davian was sheer disbelief: a High Clade magus working with the Harbingers. His Harbingers. It didn’t make sense. Couldn’t. The Clades fought and schemed, yes, but always alone—each move balanced by the others, a game where the pieces never truly shifted. But if Archeons were standing with Drakhals, if High Clades started ganging up, then the precedent shattered. The whole balance could collapse into open war, the kind that would burn the city to the ground.

<!-- paragraph:4001 para_id:00000FA1 style:Normal -->
But the next thought broke through like a blade: the magus wasn’t here for him. He was here as a trap for Maya.

<!-- paragraph:4002 para_id:00000FA2 style:Normal -->
His chest lurched. His heart went off like a war drum, and suddenly time seemed to stretch thin around him, each second dragging. His body braced on its own, fingers tightening on Antithesis, and beneath his skin the dark strands of ink began to stir. He felt them like fishermen’s lines dangling down into the depths of his mind, tugging at him, waiting for him to seize hold.

<!-- paragraph:4003 para_id:00000FA3 style:Normal -->
The panic rose sharp and blinding. He forced it down, shoved her name, her face, the thought of her running into this butcher out of his head. He narrowed his focus: he’s with the Drakhals - I have to kill him.

<!-- paragraph:4004 para_id:00000FA4 style:Normal -->
The rest of the thought—that it was ridiculous, suicidal, that no one sane rushed at a High Clade shrouded and already watching him—he smothered. He repeated to himself: Kill him. That’s the plan. That’s enough.

<!-- paragraph:4005 para_id:00000FA5 style:Normal -->
Still, his father’s voice surfaced, the sharp reprimand he could never shake. You rush in without thinking. You don’t look. You don’t plan.

<!-- paragraph:4006 para_id:00000FA6 style:Normal -->
So he forced his eyes around the room, mind racing, trying to think instead of only leap. The entry hall: corpses crumpled against walls, broken shields on the floor, the smell of blood still sharp in his nose. Weapons, armor, debris, strewn across the ground at his feet.

<!-- paragraph:4007 para_id:00000FA7 style:Normal -->
The magus tilted his head, watching him. Davian realized too late how erratic his eyes must have looked, darting from corpse to rubble to shadow. The man’s grin bent wider into something puzzled.

<!-- paragraph:4008 para_id:00000FA8 style:Normal -->
“Didn’t you hear me?” he said, voice rough with amusement. “Get out of here.”

<!-- paragraph:4009 para_id:00000FA9 style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse hammered in his throat. He was out of time. Another heartbeat and the moment would be gone, the chance to strike wasted. He seized one of the strands he knew best—one of the two remaining from what his father had given him—and clenched it tight in his mind.

<!-- paragraph:4010 para_id:00000FAA style:Normal -->
The world lurched. Sound smeared into dull echoes, motion slowed to a crawl. His gaze locked on the mace lying abandoned at his feet.

<!-- paragraph:4011 para_id:00000FAB style:Normal -->
Ink flooded him. He felt it coil and seep through every limb, winding through muscle and bone, permeating his body like black veins. His mind split—one part clutching the boon’s tether, the other reaching inside himself to grab hold of the current running through his arm. He dragged it forward, forcing the limb to move faster, harder, than it was built for. Pain flared in his shoulder, his elbow, his wrist, each joint shrieking protest.

<!-- paragraph:4012 para_id:00000FAC style:Normal -->
His fingers closed around the mace’s haft. He didn’t pause. His focus fractured again—legs, torso, spine. He seized the ink there and yanked, hauling his body into a spin.

<!-- paragraph:4013 para_id:00000FAD style:Normal -->
Agony lanced through him as tendons stretched, bones ground against sockets, each movement tearing him closer to the edge of what he could bear. The mace blurred, momentum building as though the air itself bent beneath its arc. For a moment he thought his own arm might tear loose under the weight.

<!-- paragraph:4014 para_id:00000FAE style:Normal -->
Then he let go.

<!-- paragraph:4015 para_id:00000FAF style:Normal -->
The weapon flew from his grasp like a comet flung from orbit, spinning end over end toward the magus’s head with all the force of his tortured body behind it.

<!-- paragraph:4016 para_id:00000FB0 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4017 para_id:00000FB1 style:Normal -->
She barely had time to see him move. One moment the younger Archeon magus was crouched, the next he was hurtling toward her, gauntleted claws outstretched, propelled by a speed that looked impossible.

<!-- paragraph:4018 para_id:00000FB2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s thoughts lurched, frantic, too tired for precision. She hacked channels recklessly into the air before her, dragging heat from the space between them and crash-siphoning it down into the stone. Frost erupted outward, jagged spears forming into a lattice of ice meant to catch and bind him.

<!-- paragraph:4019 para_id:00000FB3 style:Normal -->
He smashed through it without slowing. His gauntlet punched forward—not just with the sheer force of his charge, but with something deeper, the space itself buckling against her threads. Her channels trembled, as though the air refused her command. For an instant their wills collided, her mind clawing to snap the flows into place, his body tearing them apart simply by existing.

<!-- paragraph:4020 para_id:00000FB4 style:Normal -->
Her thoughts splintered under the strain. She threw herself sideways just as the gauntlet tore through the space she had stood. The stone under his strike cracked like thunder. Maya yanked heat from the ground mid-fall, bleeding it out in a frantic burst, and skidded across a sheet of ice that crystallized beneath her.

<!-- paragraph:4021 para_id:00000FB5 style:Normal -->
Caius’s words cut through her exhaustion, sharp as the memory of his smirk: You’ve got power, but no idea how to use it. He had been right. This wasn’t the distant duels of force she’d been trained for. This was close, brutal, claw and fist smashing through spellwork, a violation of everything the High Clades pretended was civilized combat.

<!-- paragraph:4022 para_id:00000FB6 style:Normal -->
She didn’t have time to dwell. A blur filled her vision—the older woman. Recognizing flashed into place through Maya’s fatigue - this was the Archeon High Magus herself, braided white hair streaming, body spinning as her heel arced down toward Maya’s prone chest.

<!-- paragraph:4023 para_id:00000FB7 style:Normal -->
Too fast. Too relentless. I can’t pin them down.

<!-- paragraph:4024 para_id:00000FB8 style:Normal -->
Maya rolled to her feet, teeth gritted, and shoved every scrap of concentration into her forearms. Her Shroud condensed there, a lattice of cold so sharp it burned, just as the High Magus’s kick slammed home.

<!-- paragraph:4025 para_id:00000FB9 style:Normal -->
The collision detonated. Her Shroud ripped the kinetic force into thermal backlash, fire bursting in a wild eruption behind her, the blast flaring so bright it seared her vision.

<!-- paragraph:4026 para_id:00000FBA style:Normal -->
And as she staggered back, the spark of an idea lit through her haze.

<!-- paragraph:4027 para_id:00000FBB style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4028 para_id:00000FBC style:Normal -->
Davian loosened his grip on the ink strand, letting himself slide down its length toward the inevitable end, like a rope with only so much slack. His lungs seized, dragging in ragged air as the strain on his body eased. Time surged forward again—the mace flashing through the air, propelled by the desperation that had nearly torn him apart.

<!-- paragraph:4029 para_id:00000FBD style:Normal -->
It connected with the magus’s skull.

<!-- paragraph:4030 para_id:00000FBE style:Normal -->
The mace shattered instantly, bursting into a storm of shards that buried themselves in the stone behind him. The man didn’t even flinch. His head hadn’t moved. His face was untouched—except for the grin stretching wider across it, his eyes shining with something like ecstasy through the haze of his flared Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:4031 para_id:00000FBF style:Normal -->
“And here I thought,” the magus said, voice thick with delight, “I might have to stand here bored with no one to kill.”

<!-- paragraph:4032 para_id:00000FC0 style:Normal -->
He crouched.

<!-- paragraph:4033 para_id:00000FC1 style:Normal -->
Davian barely had time to seize the Ink Boon again, yanking on it with his mind. The ink strands ripped through him, dragging his body sideways, each joint threatening to burst under the whiplash. Even with the world slowed, he saw the blur of the magus’s body rush past, turning his head mid-flight, smiling as his eyes tracked Davian’s escape with impossible clarity.

<!-- paragraph:4034 para_id:00000FC2 style:Normal -->
The fist smashed into the wall where Davian had been. Stone cratered inward, debris blasting across the hall.

<!-- paragraph:4035 para_id:00000FC3 style:Normal -->
Davian staggered, his muscles and tendons stretched to tearing. He couldn’t hold on. He let up again, slipping farther down the rope, the world jerking faster and faster as the magus wheeled to face him once more.

<!-- paragraph:4036 para_id:00000FC4 style:Normal -->
If that had been him—

<!-- paragraph:4037 para_id:00000FC5 style:Normal -->
The thought struck, vivid and merciless: his body crumpling, bones pulverized, blood spraying the walls as those black gauntlets tore him apart. A grotesque vision painted itself in his mind: his own limbs bent at angles, his face driven into the stone until nothing remained.

<!-- paragraph:4038 para_id:00000FC6 style:Normal -->
And with it, the echo of another memory, just as violent. Selina. Was this what had happened to her? Torn apart, destroyed utterly by a power so far beyond reach it couldn’t even be called a fight?

<!-- paragraph:4039 para_id:00000FC7 style:Normal -->
The image of Selina’s body wouldn’t leave him—her bones breaking under impossible force, her scream cut short, her life ended in a heartbeat by power she could never match. His vision swam with it, his body already anticipating the same end for himself, torn to ruin against the stone.

<!-- paragraph:4040 para_id:00000FC8 style:Normal -->
I could run. The thought slid in unbidden, cold and desperate. He still had slack in the boon—he could let himself fall farther down the rope, seize it again, keep yanking himself aside, faster than the magus could truly follow. The man’s eyes had tracked him, yes, but even so he hadn’t landed a blow. Maybe—just maybe—he could escape.

<!-- paragraph:4041 para_id:00000FC9 style:Normal -->
But then another image cut in, sharper than the rest: Maya walking into this hall, only to meet those black gauntlets. The trap waiting for her. The grin spreading as her Shroud failed under brute force. Her body broken..

<!-- paragraph:4042 para_id:00000FCA style:Normal -->
He tried to shove it away, to tell himself it wasn’t his problem. But the thought clung to him, lodged in his chest like a knife he couldn’t pull free.

<!-- paragraph:4043 para_id:00000FCB style:Normal -->
His heart pounded so hard it hurt. He was trapped—between the memory of Selina’s death, the knowledge that even his strongest strike had been meaningless, and the raw terror of facing this monster again. He could run. He should run.

<!-- paragraph:4044 para_id:00000FCC style:Normal -->
But his body wouldn’t obey. He couldn’t make himself flee. Not while this beast was here, waiting for her.

<!-- paragraph:4045 para_id:00000FCD style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4046 para_id:00000FCE style:Normal -->
Every breath rasped like fire in Maya’s chest. Her thoughts dragged, heavy and slow, but each lunge of her enemies sent a shock of adrenaline slicing through the haze. Every strike came close enough to kill her. Every heartbeat was borrowed time.

<!-- paragraph:4047 para_id:00000FCF style:Normal -->
You must be perfect. Niall’s voice echoed in her head, cold and steady. I trust you. Show them no weakness.

<!-- paragraph:4048 para_id:00000FD0 style:Normal -->
Maya gritted her teeth until her jaw ached. She couldn’t collapse now—not while those words pinned her upright. She forced her thoughts sharper, her reflexes quicker, her mind sparking against the limits of exhaustion.

<!-- paragraph:4049 para_id:00000FD1 style:Normal -->
Both magi came for her at once—two blurs of muscle and claw and impossible speed. She hurled her mind outward, carving channels in one direction, while dragging the lattice of her Shroud tighter around her body. Her skull throbbed with the effort, every new line tearing through her concentration like glass.

<!-- paragraph:4050 para_id:00000FD2 style:Normal -->
A kick hammered into her forearm. Her Shroud held, the ice biting down hard enough to split her skin, but the force still rang through her bones. She screamed hoarsely, half in pain, half in defiance, as her channels snapped into place.

<!-- paragraph:4051 para_id:00000FD3 style:Normal -->
The backlash roared through her. Energy siphoned, twisted, and turned back. It detonated in a plume of fire against the younger magus’s face, the blast hurling him backward. His body slammed into the wall hard enough to crater it, stone dust spilling down in sheets.

<!-- paragraph:4052 para_id:00000FD4 style:Normal -->
The High Magus landed lightly on her toes, her expression sharp with fury. “Ridiculous,” she spat. “There’s no way you should be able to channel like this while this tired.”

<!-- paragraph:4053 para_id:00000FD5 style:Normal -->
Maya’s whole body trembled. Her mind begged her to fold. But she reached inward instead, grasping for the same mask she wore every day for her father—the cold, perfect facade—and forced herself to stand tall.

<!-- paragraph:4054 para_id:00000FD6 style:Normal -->
“Maybe that’s true for lesser magi,” she said, voice raw but steady. “But I am a Rezin. And there is no limit to what I can do.”

<!-- paragraph:4055 para_id:00000FD7 style:Normal -->
For the first time, the High Magus faltered. Her eyes narrowed, and for a heartbeat she looked almost… surprised. Then her features hardened again into a perfect stillness, every trace of emotion pressed flat.

<!-- paragraph:4056 para_id:00000FD8 style:Normal -->
Her gaze flicked toward her son, who had just peeled himself out of the rubble. Blood streaked his face, his nose crooked at an ugly angle, breath hissing ragged through split lips.

<!-- paragraph:4057 para_id:00000FD9 style:Normal -->
“It’s time for a change of strategy,” she said, her voice calm, precise. “Be quick about it. We need what Evand has been calling plausible deniability.”

<!-- paragraph:4058 para_id:00000FDA style:Normal -->
The young magus’s grin returned, blood-smeared and eager, as he straightened to obey.

<!-- paragraph:4059 para_id:00000FDB style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4060 para_id:00000FDC style:Normal -->
The ink strand slid slick through Davian’s mental grip, pulling him down toward its terminus. He clenched harder, paradoxically slowing the fall by sheer will, and each time the magus lunged he yanked himself just far enough aside to survive. No more. No less.

<!-- paragraph:4061 para_id:00000FDD style:Normal -->
It felt like a game to the man. His grin hadn’t faltered, his eyes bright with almost childlike pleasure. Davian realized with a jolt of horror—this wasn’t a fight to him. He was just killing time. Killing Davian would be an afterthought, incidental as snapping a twig.

<!-- paragraph:4062 para_id:00000FDE style:Normal -->
Every tug of the boon left new marks. He could see his joints swelling, purple blooming under the skin of his arms and legs. Each forced motion left his body behind, straining at angles it was never meant to reach.

<!-- paragraph:4063 para_id:00000FDF style:Normal -->
His mind raced, the old habits of focus layering atop the unnatural speed of the ink. Faster and faster, his thoughts cut across one another, desperately trying to find a seam in the impossible. He’d fought Low Clades before, killed them—with Antithesis. But that meant getting close. Too close.

<!-- paragraph:4064 para_id:00000FE0 style:Normal -->
Every lesson drilled into him screamed that was suicide. High Clades could warp their Shrouds in ways no one truly understood. Expand them, sharpen them—turn a single misstep into instant death. He’d seen it. Maya had ended that assassin with a single flare, freezing him solid before he’d even touched her.

<!-- paragraph:4065 para_id:00000FE1 style:Normal -->
That was the gulf. That was the truth. You didn’t fight High Clades. You survived them. If you were lucky.

<!-- paragraph:4066 para_id:00000FE2 style:Normal -->
He’d planned to stab Caius, but only because he’d believed he’d have the element of total surprise. But looking at this magus’s eyes—tracking him, savoring each twitch of muscle—Davian wondered if that had always been a fantasy. What if speed of thought itself had been bred into the High Clades, honed over generations, until someone like him was little more than an insect crawling into the flame?

<!-- paragraph:4067 para_id:00000FE3 style:Normal -->
Maybe Caius’s laughter hadn’t been arrogance. Maybe he really had watched Davian’s blade crawl toward him, slow and helpless, the effort of a child lashing out at a god.

<!-- paragraph:4068 para_id:00000FE4 style:Normal -->
He couldn’t let go now. If he loosened his grip on the ink strand, he’d slide off the end of it, empty-handed and empty-bodied, nothing left to save him. So he held tight, even as each tug frayed his joints and scoured his mind raw.

<!-- paragraph:4069 para_id:00000FE5 style:Normal -->
His thoughts spun back, grasping for anything useful. The mace—its full weight and his desperation behind it—had smashed square across the magus’s face. And the man had smiled. His Shroud must have flared in that instant, dismissing the blow so completely. But then—why had there been no backlash? Davian had felt nothing, no ripple of redirected force, no echo slamming back into his body. The mace had simply destroyed itself on contact, annihilated by the impact alone.

<!-- paragraph:4070 para_id:00000FE6 style:Normal -->
And those gauntlets. Black iron, etched with zän that still shimmered faintly from the last strike. The claws weren’t just for show—they were conduits. Tools. Tools. High Clades scorned reliance on such things, even considered it shameful. If his Shroud was truly that strong, what was the point of them?

<!-- paragraph:4071 para_id:00000FE7 style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse hammered, every breath shallow as the truth pressed at the edge of his mind.

<!-- paragraph:4072 para_id:00000FE8 style:Normal -->
He could run. He should run. But he wouldn’t. He’d already made that choice, somewhere between Selina’s memory and Maya’s face. Running wasn’t an option.

<!-- paragraph:4073 para_id:00000FE9 style:Normal -->
Then maybe—maybe there was something here. Something everyone was missing. The Archeons weren’t legends of brute force alone; they were hiding something in plain sight. Some secret of how their Shrouds actually worked.

<!-- paragraph:4074 para_id:00000FEA style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4075 para_id:00000FEB style:Normal -->
Something had shifted.

<!-- paragraph:4076 para_id:00000FEC style:Normal -->
The Archeons weren’t lunging together anymore. Their rhythm had changed—one striking, then the other, alternating with perfect precision, forcing her back step by step. The constant pressure left her no breath, no time to weave anything elaborate. She could only carve quick, brutal channels and reinforce her Shroud, over and over, her mind screaming at the pace.

<!-- paragraph:4077 para_id:00000FED style:Normal -->
They weren’t trying to overwhelm her. They were buying time. For what, she couldn’t guess.

<!-- paragraph:4078 para_id:00000FEE style:Normal -->
Maya forced her spine straight, her mask of cold control slipping into place even as every nerve begged her to fold. She carved sharper, quicker channels, letting each strike rebound, each flare of energy twist away. She even tried to make it look effortless, almost bored, though her temples throbbed with every exertion.

<!-- paragraph:4079 para_id:00000FEF style:Normal -->
Another clawed strike slammed into her arm. She flared her Shroud, hardening it with cold, and bent the backlash sideways into the younger magus. He snarled as the blast clipped him, giving her a heartbeat of cover.

<!-- paragraph:4080 para_id:00000FF0 style:Normal -->
But in that instant she felt it—something strange. The older woman’s will pressed into hers, heavy and invasive, not just the clash of Shrouds but another current beneath it. A second layer of magic, gnawing at the edges of her defenses like acid, trying to eat through the cold she’d conjured.

<!-- paragraph:4081 para_id:00000FF1 style:Normal -->
The realization jolted her, but she had no time to linger on it. Their claws and feet came again, faster, sharper, and all she could do was keep moving, keep standing. If she faltered for even a second, if she let the mask slip, they’d know. They’d close in and kill her.

<!-- paragraph:4082 para_id:00000FF2 style:Normal -->
So she ground her teeth, drew another ragged breath, and stood taller, daring them to believe she had more left than she did.

<!-- paragraph:4083 para_id:00000FF3 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4084 para_id:00000FF4 style:Normal -->
The explosions had been background thunder, constant and distant—until suddenly Davian realized they were louder. Closer. Moving toward the Spire.

<!-- paragraph:4085 para_id:00000FF5 style:Normal -->
The Archeon magus heard it too. His grin softened into something almost companionable. “Sorry, boy. Can’t keep playing. But I had fun. Hope we meet again. And good luck on your mission for the Harbingers.”

<!-- paragraph:4086 para_id:00000FF6 style:Normal -->
Davian’s gut lurched. He didn’t think—he acted. A stone lay at his feet. He seized the ink rope tight in his mind, muscles screaming as he snapped his arm faster than bone should allow, and hurled it.

<!-- paragraph:4087 para_id:00000FF7 style:Normal -->
The stone struck the magus square in the chest—splintering to powder, the shards cascading harmlessly across his vest.

<!-- paragraph:4088 para_id:00000FF8 style:Normal -->
The man chuckled. “Cute.” He turned for the door.

<!-- paragraph:4089 para_id:00000FF9 style:Normal -->
Davian slumped, letting his shoulders sag, feigning defeat. But inside, his mind clamped harder, white-knuckled around the last fraying fibers of the Ink Boon.

<!-- paragraph:4090 para_id:00000FFA style:Normal -->
As the magus stepped through the threshold, Davian surged forward. He snatched a discarded shield from the rubble and sprinted, boots slapping the stone. The magus half-turned, bemusement flickering across his face, as Davian scanned the sky with frantic eyes.

<!-- paragraph:4091 para_id:00000FFB style:Normal -->
Then he yanked.

<!-- paragraph:4092 para_id:00000FFC style:Normal -->
He tore at the Ink Boon with everything he had left, pulling his body up and outward, bones screaming in protest. The shield slammed forward into the magus’s torso—and suddenly the world lifted.

<!-- paragraph:4093 para_id:00000FFD style:Normal -->
The two of them arced into the air above the courtyard, weightless, shield pressed between them. Wind ripped at Davian’s hair, and for an instant—an impossible, crystalline instant—the flight felt almost peaceful.

<!-- paragraph:4094 para_id:00000FFE style:Normal -->
The magus’s grin vanished, shock flashing across his face as he reached for Davian.

<!-- paragraph:4095 para_id:00000FFF style:Normal -->
Davian let go. He shoved himself backward off the shield, severing the last of his hold on the boon, sending his body streaking away from the magus. Both of them plummeted, two streaks of flesh and iron, hurtling toward the stones below.

<!-- paragraph:4096 para_id:00001000 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4097 para_id:00001001 style:Normal -->
Maya’s boots scraped against the stone as she gave ground, over and over. The first lunges had nearly crushed her, each strike a brush with death, but now—somehow—she found a rhythm. Her arms shook, her thoughts frayed at the edges, but the shock of their assault had dulled into something almost manageable. Her body moved on instinct, carving sharp, simple channels, shaping her Shroud into bulwarks and blades.

<!-- paragraph:4098 para_id:00001002 style:Normal -->
Her mind was still thinning, every line traced like tearing a wound open in her skull, but the rhythm let her breathe. Deflect. Redirect. Retreat. Again.

<!-- paragraph:4099 para_id:00001003 style:Normal -->
She entered into open space and realized she had reached the rally courtyard by the central spire. The massive tower loomed behind her as she backed in, the Archeon magi still pressing. Around them, soldiers had gathered, forming a loose perimeter—yet not one dared close.

<!-- paragraph:4100 para_id:00001004 style:Normal -->
Maya caught their faces: wide eyes, mouths half-open, awe frozen into them. She blinked hard, realization crashing in—barely a minute or two had passed since the Archeon magi had first attacked. A lifetime crammed into seconds.

<!-- paragraph:4101 para_id:00001005 style:Normal -->
Then a volley of arrows hissed through the air, loosed by trembling hands from the courtyard edge.

<!-- paragraph:4102 para_id:00001006 style:Normal -->
The Archeons didn’t so much as blink. The shafts snapped and skittered off their Shrouds, falling harmlessly at their feet. Their eyes never left Maya.

<!-- paragraph:4103 para_id:00001007 style:Normal -->
The older woman’s voice cut through the air, sharp and irritated. Serathis. The name jolted in Maya’s memory, snapping into place.

<!-- paragraph:4104 para_id:00001008 style:Normal -->
“Drosin,” Serathis snapped at her bloodied grandson, who was grinning even through his broken nose. “Where the hell is Brann? This is already stretching the definition of plausible deniability.”

<!-- paragraph:4105 para_id:00001009 style:Normal -->
Arrows clattered uselessly against her Shroud, each one shattering like brittle twigs against ice. She barely spared them a glance.

<!-- paragraph:4106 para_id:0000100A style:Normal -->
Maya straightened, forcing the weight of command into her voice, even though her chest heaved with every breath. “Fall back,” she barked to the soldiers. “Now. Get Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:4107 para_id:0000100B style:Normal -->
They obeyed instantly. None of them hesitated—not when a High Clade magus spoke. The courtyard emptied in heartbeats, the scrape of boots vanishing into the din.

<!-- paragraph:4108 para_id:0000100C style:Normal -->
Serathis rolled her eyes, exhaling a long, put-upon sigh. “Dramatic as ever.”

<!-- paragraph:4109 para_id:0000100D style:Normal -->
Even as Serathis sighed, Maya’s breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:4110 para_id:0000100E style:Normal -->
There it was again—that strange pressure, deep inside her where the strands of fate coiled like hidden threads. She’d felt it before, faintly, as a background hum she’d pushed aside in the chaos. But now it pulled, a sudden tug running through her chest and gut as if something far larger had grasped hold of the loom itself.

<!-- paragraph:4111 para_id:0000100F style:Normal -->
The tug came again, sudden and violent, deep in the place where the strands of fate wound through her. She’d been feeling it all along, faint as a whisper, but now it yanked hard from behind her—straight from the Central Tower. Her breath hitched, but she forced her eyes to stay on Serathis, forcing her body into stillness, refusing to show the stumble.

<!-- paragraph:4112 para_id:00001010 style:Normal -->
Then, as quickly as it surged, the pull was gone. Silence.

<!-- paragraph:4113 para_id:00001011 style:Normal -->
“What the hell just happened to Brann?” Drosin muttered, his grin faltering as he tilted his head back, staring up at the sky.

<!-- paragraph:4114 para_id:00001012 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4115 para_id:00001013 style:Normal -->
Davian and Brann tumbled through the air in a blur of shattered stone and sky. Davian clung to the last ragged fibers of the Ink Boon, wrenching them tight to slow his fall. He slammed into the cobblestones with bone-rattling force, bouncing across the pavement, his body screaming at every impact as his mind at long last completely released the ink.

<!-- paragraph:4116 para_id:00001014 style:Normal -->
Brann hit harder. The Archeon magus crashed down like a meteor, stone cracking under his weight. He rose with a grunt, rolling his shoulders as if he’d only tripped. “What was the point of that?” he asked, exasperated.

<!-- paragraph:4117 para_id:00001015 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t answer. Instead, he threw himself against the nearest wall with theatrical desperation. “Magus!” he shouted, voice cracking as he collapsed to the ground. “Help! They’re attacking me!”

<!-- paragraph:4118 para_id:00001016 style:Normal -->
For an instant, Brann looked honestly confused, brows drawn in a puzzled knot. Then his head snapped upward.

<!-- paragraph:4119 para_id:00001017 style:Normal -->
Lightning.

<!-- paragraph:4120 para_id:00001018 style:Normal -->
It forked across the sky, jagged and ravenous, converging on some hidden point above. Brann’s grin twisted into a snarl. “Son of a bitch.”

<!-- paragraph:4121 para_id:00001019 style:Normal -->
Flat on his back, Davian raised one trembling hand, middle finger extended, before letting it flop back to the ground with all the limp exaggeration of a man fainting on stage. The screaming in his ribs, the raw swell of his joints, and the bruises blooming across his limbs made it an easier performance than he would have liked.

<!-- paragraph:4122 para_id:0000101A style:Normal -->
Brann crouched, muscles coiling, and leapt away in a blur.

<!-- paragraph:4123 para_id:0000101B style:Normal -->
But even as he rose, Niall stepped into the courtyard. His hand dropped.

<!-- paragraph:4124 para_id:0000101C style:Normal -->
The world tore open. Every lightning storm Davian had ever seen, every crackling bolt that had ever split the sky, seemed to converge in that single instant. The air ignited in a furious white blaze, thunder smashing down midair as it swallowed the receding figure of the Archeon magus.

<!-- paragraph:4125 para_id:0000101D style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4126 para_id:0000101E style:Normal -->
Maya bared her teeth in something close to a smile. “You can’t believe I’ll fall for the look-what’s-behind-you trick.”

<!-- paragraph:4127 para_id:0000101F style:Normal -->
The words had barely left her mouth when the world went white. A pulse of light, brighter than the sun, erupted behind her, forcing her to flinch, her eyes seared by the brilliance.

<!-- paragraph:4128 para_id:00001020 style:Normal -->
Instinct betrayed her. She turned.

<!-- paragraph:4129 para_id:00001021 style:Normal -->
A charred body smashed into the courtyard stones, skidding and bouncing grotesquely, pieces sloughing away as it tumbled. It came to rest in a broken heap at Serathis’s feet, the stench of burned flesh filling the air.

<!-- paragraph:4130 para_id:00001022 style:Normal -->
For the first time, Serathis’s mask slipped. She looked down, grim, her features carved in hard lines. But when she lifted her gaze back to Maya, it wasn’t grief in her eyes—it was fury, sharp and personal, the anger of someone thwarted.

<!-- paragraph:4131 para_id:00001023 style:Normal -->
“This is your fault,” Serathis spat, her voice low and cutting. “Your dramatic, silly Presentation. Did you really think the other High Clades would sit content, watching yet another generation of Rezins lording it over us? Enforcing peace. Limiting expansion.”

<!-- paragraph:4132 para_id:00001024 style:Normal -->
She kicked the blackened corpse aside, dismissing it as one might brush away ash.

<!-- paragraph:4133 para_id:00001025 style:Normal -->
“You have this kind of power—” she jabbed a finger at Maya, venom dripping from every word—“and you waste it doing nothing but holding the rest of us down.”

<!-- paragraph:4134 para_id:00001026 style:Normal -->
Drosin wiped the blood from his broken nose with the back of his gauntlet and muttered, “I don’t think we have time for this.”

<!-- paragraph:4135 para_id:00001027 style:Normal -->
“Shut up,” Serathis snapped without looking at him. “There are plenty more grandsons where you came from.”

<!-- paragraph:4136 para_id:00001028 style:Normal -->
Her eyes locked on Maya again, cold and venomous. “You might think you’ve won here. But all it will take is one slip—one moment of weakness—and the rest of us will be there, right at your throat.”

<!-- paragraph:4137 para_id:00001029 style:Normal -->
With a single motion, she and Drosin sprang skyward, vanishing over the rooftops.

<!-- paragraph:4138 para_id:0000102A style:Normal -->
Maya staggered, trying to summon the last spark of strength, hands twitching as she reached for another weave, another channel—but nothing came. Her mind had been overdrawn for too long. The adrenaline that had carried her broke all at once, leaving her hollow. She fell to one knee, her Shroud flickering around her like embers sputtering in the wind.

<!-- paragraph:4139 para_id:0000102B style:Normal -->
Boots thundered back into the courtyard. Her troops returned, voices urgent, faces pale, but she barely saw them.

<!-- paragraph:4140 para_id:0000102C style:Normal -->
“I did it,” she whispered to herself, forcing the words into the air. Her chest ached with every breath, but her spine stayed straight.

<!-- paragraph:4141 para_id:0000102D style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4142 para_id:0000102E style:Normal -->
Davian stayed flat on his back, staring up at the fractured sky. Niall’s boots pounded past, straight toward the Central Tower. He didn’t even glance down. Of course he didn’t. Why would he?

<!-- paragraph:4143 para_id:0000102F style:Normal -->
Davian let out a ragged breath. He couldn’t have taken another step anyway. His body was wrecked, his joints swollen and screaming, his ribs raw with every inhale. He’d done everything he could—and it hadn’t been enough.

<!-- paragraph:4144 para_id:00001030 style:Normal -->
The Ink Boon’s threads lay quiet now, receded into him like embers cooling in ash. He wouldn’t be able to touch another one for a long time. No one knew how long—not really. Ink Boons weren’t toys you wasted on experiments, and those who had tried to force the same kind twice… well, the stories said their bodies tore themselves apart.

<!-- paragraph:4145 para_id:00001031 style:Normal -->
He should have felt satisfaction. Brann was dead, burned from the sky in a storm that cracked the world open. But the knowledge hollowed him instead. Niall had killed the magus. Not him. Davian had been nothing—just a child with a toothpick, raging at a monster, just as the Harbinger leader had once mocked him.

<!-- paragraph:4146 para_id:00001032 style:Normal -->
Even with the boon thrumming through his veins, all he’d managed was survival—barely. If he’d tried to layer his own magic over it, Subversion straining against the ink’s unnatural speed, one slip in focus and he’d have been paste against the wall.

<!-- paragraph:4147 para_id:00001033 style:Normal -->
Selina’s broken body. Maya’s face flashing before him. The certainty of his own death. They had all battered him at once, fear and memory and responsibility clashing so violently inside his skull he’d felt almost paralyzed. He hadn’t fought. He hadn’t even lived. He’d just clung to the edges of survival and hoped not to drown.

<!-- paragraph:4148 para_id:00001034 style:Normal -->
He didn’t move. Just lay there, watching the sky as the horns blared again, sharper now, echoing across the citadel. The signal carried through the stone and into his bones—retreat. The assault was over.

<!-- paragraph:4149 para_id:00001035 style:Normal -->
Troops began to file back through the courtyards, their shouts ragged, their armor clattering in tired rhythm. The chaos was settling, the moment gone.

<!-- paragraph:4150 para_id:00001036 style:Normal -->
Davian closed his eyes. He’d missed it. The chance to slip into the Treasury, to vanish into the storm, to make it all mean something. Instead he was broken on the stones, useless, clinging to a fantasy that he could have taken the Heart and vanished clean, with Maya never knowing it was him.

<!-- paragraph:4151 para_id:00001037 style:Normal -->
But even as he thought it, he felt the lie of it. That was all it had ever been—a fantasy. There was no world where she wouldn’t find out. No world where he could keep the mask forever.

Bootsteps stopped beside him. Davian blinked against the light, squinting up at a stocky man with a shaved head and a scar like a white line down his cheek. He vaguely recognized him—one of the guards who sometimes lingered at the edge of Harvick’s sparring sessions, watching with quiet interest.

<!-- paragraph:4152 para_id:00001038 style:Normal -->
“You all right?” the man asked, crouching down. “Someone said you got punched by a magus, and here you are—looking like shit, but not totally dead.”

<!-- paragraph:4153 para_id:00001039 style:Normal -->
Davian managed a rasp of a laugh. “Yeah. More of a throw, really. I don’t think he was trying to kill me. Just… going somewhere. I was in the way.”

<!-- paragraph:4154 para_id:0000103A style:Normal -->
The guard frowned, confused, but shrugged. “Well, don’t question blessings too much, hey? Can you walk?”

<!-- paragraph:4155 para_id:0000103B style:Normal -->
He offered a hand. Davian hesitated, then took it. His body screamed as he was pulled upright, but the grip steadied him, solid and unpretentious. They half-limped, half-staggered toward the healers’ tents together.

<!-- paragraph:4158 para_id:0000103E style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 23

<!-- paragraph:4159 para_id:0000103F style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 23

<!-- paragraph:4161 para_id:00001041 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4162 para_id:00001042 style:Normal -->
Maya walked through the outer courtyards with her chin high, every step deliberate, every line of her body a mask of poise. The stink of battle still clung to the air, acrid smoke and the iron tang of blood, but she forced herself to breathe it as though it were nothing at all. Soldiers turned their heads as she passed, their faces pale, some streaked with ash, others wet with grief. She gave them nothing back—no stumble, no falter. Only the calm, unshaken mask of a Rezin.

<!-- paragraph:4163 para_id:00001043 style:Normal -->
Inside the spire, the carnage was worse. Men and women she had seen training in these halls now lay silent under blankets, their bodies lined in neat rows while others were dragged toward the healing chambers. She knew this had to be the work of one of the Archeon magi, their brutal style written in the shattered bones and twisted limbs of the fallen. The sight brought an echo of her own work rushing back to her—the moments on the wall when soldiers broke and burned under her hands. How many had she killed? She had hurled death until her mind went raw, but from here, in the silence, she could almost hear their screams again.

<!-- paragraph:4164 para_id:00001044 style:Normal -->
She kept walking, straight-backed, her eyes forward. A Rezin heir did not crumble in front of soldiers. She was so intent on holding the mask that she nearly missed the sound of her name.

<!-- paragraph:4165 para_id:00001045 style:Normal -->
“Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:4166 para_id:00001046 style:Normal -->
Aldira appeared at her side, firm hands closing on her arm, guiding her down a side corridor. She cut off the soldiers trying to follow with a curt order—“No one enters. Not until Niall himself”—and ushered Maya into a narrow conference room. The door shut with a solid thud behind them.

<!-- paragraph:4167 para_id:00001047 style:Normal -->
“You can rest now,” Aldira said, her voice low, leaving no room for argument.

<!-- paragraph:4168 para_id:00001048 style:Normal -->
Maya wanted to deny it, to keep the performance going, but the effort of holding herself upright broke all at once. Her knees bent and she dropped into a chair, her breath escaping in a trembling sigh. The mask slipped from her face as exhaustion roared in, heavier than any blow.

<!-- paragraph:4169 para_id:00001049 style:Normal -->
Even then, part of her clung to her Shroud, mind white-knuckled around the weave of protection she had kept alive for what felt like hours. Releasing it felt dangerous, shameful, like letting the world see too much. But the longer she held, the more the strain blurred her thoughts. She shut her eyes, tried to steady her breath, and felt the edges of her Shroud begin—finally, reluctantly—to fall away.

<!-- paragraph:4170 para_id:0000104A style:Normal -->
She didn’t know how long she sat slumped in the chair, staring blankly at the tabletop. The sound of her own pulse had drowned everything out, her body too heavy to move. Then the muffled voices of the guards outside stirred her back to herself. They were admitting her father.

<!-- paragraph:4171 para_id:0000104B style:Normal -->
Maya forced her spine straight, dragging the mask back over her face. She remembered his hand on her shoulder, his words before the battle—I know you can be perfect. She couldn’t let him see what she had been moments ago. She couldn’t see that fear flicker across his face again.

<!-- paragraph:4172 para_id:0000104C style:Normal -->
Niall entered like a storm contained in a man’s frame, his coat singed at the edges, the air around him faintly charged with the tang of lightning. His eyes cut to her at once. He didn’t look relieved—only furious.

<!-- paragraph:4173 para_id:0000104D style:Normal -->
“What happened?” His voice was sharp, not asking but demanding.

<!-- paragraph:4174 para_id:0000104E style:Normal -->
Maya met his gaze, steady, though her chest still burned with every breath. She told him everything: Serathis and Drosin, the sudden ferocity of their hand-to-hand assault, their relentless coordination, how they had pressed her with close-range strikes unlike anything she had trained for. Her words were clipped, deliberate, trying to make the story sound as efficient and orderly as possible, even as her exhaustion threatened to creep into her voice.

<!-- paragraph:4175 para_id:0000104F style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes never left her, hawkish, searching for weakness. She refused to waver in her chair.

<!-- paragraph:4176 para_id:00001050 style:Normal -->
“You’re exhausted,” he said finally, his mouth tightening. “You know better than this. Throwing soldiers at a magus to wear them down before sending in your own magi—that’s as old as warfare itself. You should have expected it. And the way you used energy was inefficient. That fight could have turned out poorly.”

<!-- paragraph:4177 para_id:00001051 style:Normal -->
The words stung, but Maya inclined her head with deliberate calm. “I was able to deal with them easily. If this is what Archeon has to offer, then the High Clades are right to look down on them.”

<!-- paragraph:4178 para_id:00001052 style:Normal -->
For the first time since entering, Niall’s eyes left her. He turned sharply toward Aldira. “Then explain this. How was she blindsided by magi at all if you were monitoring the Registry?”

<!-- paragraph:4179 para_id:00001053 style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t flinch under Niall’s glare. Her voice was even, almost clipped, as if reeling off a report. “We did see them. Multiple auras, unregistered, appearing all at once. They were moving faster than the Registry could resolve, even in passive mode. It was overwhelmed.”

<!-- paragraph:4180 para_id:00001054 style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes narrowed, but she pressed on.

<!-- paragraph:4181 para_id:00001055 style:Normal -->
“We sent messengers—more than one—but they never arrived. Judging from the bodies in the entryway, the Archeons cut them down before they could reach anyone. The Registry isn’t built for that kind of response. It was designed by paranoid magi to catch assassins creeping in under cover of night, not blitz attacks from multiple High Clade magi moving at impossible speed.”

<!-- paragraph:4182 para_id:00001056 style:Normal -->
She folded her arms. “And we had no reason to anticipate Archeon. Every dossier, every preparation pointed toward Drakhal. Which means we didn’t know half of what they were capable of. You, of all people, should understand that.”

<!-- paragraph:4183 para_id:00001057 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s voice hardened at the end, the faintest bite of reproach. “This was over in minutes. By the time we registered the incursion, the fighting was already here.”

<!-- paragraph:4184 para_id:00001058 style:Normal -->
Niall’s shoulders eased as Aldira finished, some of the fire leaving his eyes. He let out a slow breath and lowered himself into a chair at the table, fingers drumming against the wood.

<!-- paragraph:4185 para_id:00001059 style:Normal -->
“We need to know exactly what happened here,” he said. “The timing alone doesn’t make sense. The mercenaries waited far too long before committing their magi. And mercenary companies don’t come cheap to begin with—certainly not when the contract is to attack us. After the losses they took last time, the rate must have been astronomical.”

<!-- paragraph:4186 para_id:0000105A style:Normal -->
He leaned forward, voice sharpening again. “The Archeons couldn’t have financed this themselves. That means Drakhal is involved. But two High Clades cooperating?” He shook his head. “Unheard-of. The kind of preparation this assault required means it’s been in the works for weeks, maybe months.”

<!-- paragraph:4187 para_id:0000105B style:Normal -->
Maya sat straighter, her exhaustion pressing at her temples, but she forced her voice steady. “Serathis told me the other High Clades are bitter. Bitter about another generation of Rezins lording it over them, enforcing peace through our strength. She said they’ll be right there at our throats at the first sign of weakness.”

<!-- paragraph:4188 para_id:0000105C style:Normal -->
Her voice sharpened. “But why would they think now was the time? My Presentation should have scared them into silence.”

<!-- paragraph:4189 para_id:0000105D style:Normal -->
Silence stretched. Aldira remained stony-faced, unreadable. Niall, though—Niall’s gaze slipped away from hers. He looked down at the table, the faintest flicker of something guilty shadowing his face. For him not to meet her eyes was rarer than lightning in a clear sky.

<!-- paragraph:4190 para_id:0000105E style:Normal -->
“None of this matters,” he said at last, voice low and taut. “They cannot come into our citadel, strike at my daughter, and think to walk away. That is not how these things are supposed to go.”

<!-- paragraph:4191 para_id:0000105F style:Normal -->
Aldira’s expression didn’t change. “And how are they supposed to go, Niall? You have to remember—it was you who set this precedent.”

<!-- paragraph:4192 para_id:00001060 style:Normal -->
Maya’s brow furrowed. “What are you talking about?”

<!-- paragraph:4193 para_id:00001061 style:Normal -->
Niall’s hand curled into a fist on the table. “I don’t want to talk about this.”

<!-- paragraph:4194 para_id:00001062 style:Normal -->
“She should know,” Aldira said evenly, her eyes still fixed on Maya. “She deserves to know why things are the way they are.”

<!-- paragraph:4195 para_id:00001063 style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes flared, cornered again. “Is that why you chose this room?” His voice was low, dangerous.

<!-- paragraph:4196 para_id:00001064 style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t answer.

<!-- paragraph:4197 para_id:00001065 style:Normal -->
“Fine,” he snapped. “But you show her. And don’t show her anything else.” He turned on his heel. “I’m going to get the reports from the wall commanders.” The door shut hard behind him.

<!-- paragraph:4198 para_id:00001066 style:Normal -->
Maya looked from the door back to Aldira, her voice tight. “What is this about?”

<!-- paragraph:4199 para_id:00001067 style:Normal -->
Aldira crossed the room without answering, her fingers trailing lightly across a row of shelves. She stopped at one section, pressed, and with a muted click a hidden compartment slid open. Dust billowed out, undisturbed for years.

<!-- paragraph:4200 para_id:00001068 style:Normal -->
From within, Aldira drew a thick, leather-bound book etched with faint sigils. Maya’s breath caught—the weight of recognition instant. A Codex. Just like her mother’s.

<!-- paragraph:4201 para_id:00001069 style:Normal -->
“I don’t think he’s ever been able to make himself open this again,” Aldira murmured. “But he couldn’t bring himself to destroy it either. He couldn’t destroy her.”

<!-- paragraph:4202 para_id:0000106A style:Normal -->
She carried it to the table, brushing dust from the cover. Opening to the first page, Aldira traced the inscribed sigil, the faint shimmer of its key incantation flaring at her touch. She studied it for a long moment before giving a short nod.

<!-- paragraph:4203 para_id:0000106B style:Normal -->
“It hasn’t been tampered with,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:4204 para_id:0000106C style:Normal -->
Maya’s fingers hovered above the cover. “What am I going to see?”

<!-- paragraph:4205 para_id:0000106D style:Normal -->
Aldira’s gaze was steady. “Words wouldn’t do it justice.”

<!-- paragraph:4206 para_id:0000106E style:Normal -->
The Codex was heavy in her lap, the faint shimmer of the key incantation visible on the inside cover. She already knew what to do—focus, speak, let her aura press into the tether carved there.

<!-- paragraph:4207 para_id:0000106F style:Normal -->
She recited the phrase under her breath, and the sigil flared. The shelves and table dissolved in a rush of light, and then the world tore away, dragging her headlong into Niall’s memory.

<!-- paragraph:4208 para_id:00001070 style:Normal -->
The world slammed into her. Boots striking stone. The uneven rhythm of a Spoke road—though not the one to the Rezin citadel. This one was cracked, pitted, edges crumbling where weeds pushed through.

<!-- paragraph:4209 para_id:00001071 style:Normal -->
She looked down—no, he did—military pants, boots ground down from years of use, a sleeveless vest that left his arms bare. Niall’s chest rose and fell in furious measure, every breath carrying the weight of rage. Maya felt it clawing inside her too, the heat of it, like standing too close to a forge.

<!-- paragraph:4210 para_id:00001072 style:Normal -->
Darkness edged her vision, his vision, as the fury spiked. The channels his mind instinctively traced burst outward in erratic surges, sparks leaping down them like lightning trying to escape a storm. For an instant, she thought they would tear him apart—but then his focus snapped back to the road, the sparks dying as quickly as they had come. One step, then another. Forward. Always forward.

<!-- paragraph:4211 para_id:00001073 style:Normal -->
The fury tunneled his thoughts so deeply into the rhythm of walking that the world shrank to boot, stone, boot, stone—until a cough cut through.

<!-- paragraph:4212 para_id:00001074 style:Normal -->
Maya’s—Niall’s—head jerked to the side.

<!-- paragraph:4213 para_id:00001075 style:Normal -->
Harvick walked there, keeping pace with an easy stride. Younger, his face less lined, hair darker, though the familiar sharpness in his eyes was unchanged.

<!-- paragraph:4214 para_id:00001076 style:Normal -->
“I told you all to stay away,” Niall growled. “You’re the only one who won’t obey me.”

<!-- paragraph:4215 para_id:00001077 style:Normal -->
“I know you don’t need help,” Harvick said easily. “I just happened to be walking this way myself.”

<!-- paragraph:4216 para_id:00001078 style:Normal -->
Niall dropped his eyes back to the ground, forcing his focus onto the rhythm of boots against stone. Harvick didn’t press him. He kept pace, loose and relaxed, though even in his calm stride Maya could see the leather armor he wore and the glyph-wrought sword strapped across his back—the one she rarely glimpsed.

<!-- paragraph:4217 para_id:00001079 style:Normal -->
“There is no other way to do this,” Niall said at last.

<!-- paragraph:4218 para_id:0000107A style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a little shake of his head, as though almost surprised to hear him speak. “Plenty of other ways. In fact, you’re the first to try it like this.”

<!-- paragraph:4219 para_id:0000107B style:Normal -->
“So I should keep doing what everyone else is doing?” Niall’s voice was sharp, anger rising again. He gestured at the broken road around them. “Look. This city is falling apart. I’m going to end it—all of it. The scheming, the backstabbing, the endless refusal to cooperate.”

<!-- paragraph:4220 para_id:0000107C style:Normal -->
Harvick kept walking, his expression skeptical but his tone mild.

<!-- paragraph:4221 para_id:0000107D style:Normal -->
Niall tried to clamp his jaw shut, but the words came boiling out again. “I told her what would happen if she kept opposing my proposal. She said she was ready for me.”

<!-- paragraph:4222 para_id:0000107E style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a low, almost amused snort. “She thinks you’re going to duel her. Set a date in the arena, stand in front of the crowd, and channel at each other until one of you gives in. Keep the Clades satisfied - nobody has to die.”

<!-- paragraph:4223 para_id:0000107F style:Normal -->
Niall’s jaw worked. The shadows at the edge of his vision pulsed with memory. “She doesn’t understand,” he said. The words came rough, like unmade rope. “How do you duel when the rules are what’s killing us? When a duel only shows them how polite we can be while they rot everything else?”

<!-- paragraph:4224 para_id:00001080 style:Normal -->
“I already tried the other way,” Niall said, quieter now, and for a moment the walk narrowed to the scrape of leather. The memory around them felt tight, the air too thin for anything but the next heartbeat. “I spoke at councils. I offered—reform, cooperation, an end to the petty preening. I put my face forward and tried to make a different game. They smiled, they took the favors, and they kept their nets. Then she died.” The name hovered—Alara—soft and jagged in the center of the memory. The color of that instant was salt and rust.

<!-- paragraph:4225 para_id:00001081 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s voice was softer. “You can’t make everyone honest by making yourself cruel.”

<!-- paragraph:4226 para_id:00001082 style:Normal -->
Niall’s laugh was a blade. “No. You can make them cautious. You can bring some of the suffering their choices cause back to them and make them choke on it.”

<!-- paragraph:4227 para_id:00001083 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s step slowed. He looked at Niall as if measuring a distance that wasn’t physical. “This isn’t going to work,” he said quietly. “You know that. I know that. Being furious doesn’t make it a better solution. This isn’t really what—”
 He cut himself off, the thing unsaid hanging between them.

<!-- paragraph:4228 para_id:00001084 style:Normal -->
“She’s dead,” Niall snapped, the words like struck flint. “I don’t care what anyone else wants.”

<!-- paragraph:4229 para_id:00001085 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s gaze didn’t flinch. “What about your children?”

<!-- paragraph:4230 para_id:00001086 style:Normal -->
Niall’s face tightened, a raw thing. “We will just not tell them.” His voice rose, brittle with strain. “Stop throwing out arguments just because you can. You know I’m going to win. The victor writes the history.”

<!-- paragraph:4231 para_id:00001087 style:Normal -->
Harvick shook his head slowly. “That’s not what I mean. You put a lid on a pot, it’s going to boil. This isn’t a mantle you—or your family—can take up and then just walk away from.”

<!-- paragraph:4232 para_id:00001088 style:Normal -->
They looked up. The Lyranth citadel rose before them, its spire spearing into the heavens, so tall that for an instant Niall felt small beneath it. The last row of houses gave way to the bare, open ground that circled its walls. Sentries paced along the battlements, but none seemed alarmed by two men approaching on foot.

<!-- paragraph:4233 para_id:00001089 style:Normal -->
Niall glanced at Harvick. The man had taken up a place against the wall of a nearby building, arms folded, posture loose as if they were out for nothing more than an idle stroll.

<!-- paragraph:4234 para_id:0000108A style:Normal -->
Niall drew a long breath.

<!-- paragraph:4235 para_id:0000108B style:Normal -->
“You know,” Harvick said, his tone easy, “it’s not too late. It’s never too late to turn back from a bad decision.”

<!-- paragraph:4236 para_id:0000108C style:Normal -->
Niall looked at him. The fury was still there, roaring inside, but for one fragile moment it broke against something else—wetness threatening at the corners of his eyes. He shoved it down, forced it into the bubbling pot of anger, and managed only: “Thank you. You didn’t have to come see me like this.”

<!-- paragraph:4237 para_id:0000108D style:Normal -->
Harvick flipped a gold halo into the air and caught it with a faint smile. “Like I said. I just happened to be walking this way.”

<!-- paragraph:4238 para_id:0000108E style:Normal -->
Niall pressed the emotions down hard and turned from him. He stepped out across the open ground, each footfall striking the cobblestones like a drumbeat. The memory of the council chamber burned in him—the endless maneuvering, the smug smiles while others suffered. He stoked that fury, fed it until the shadows crowding his vision burned red.

<!-- paragraph:4239 para_id:0000108F style:Normal -->
This time, he didn’t rein the channels back. He let them spill wide, lightning crackling from his skin in erratic lashes as he strode toward the gates of the citadel.

<!-- paragraph:4240 para_id:00001090 style:Normal -->
His boots struck the cobblestones in steady rhythm, the sound reverberating up through bone and muscle. His Shroud held itself now, reflexive, as his mind stretched wider and wider, climbing skyward until it felt as though he had no limit. Each breath filled his body with the weight of the world—the scrape of leather, the iron taste of air, the hum of every mote of electricity.

<!-- paragraph:4241 para_id:00001091 style:Normal -->
Shouts rose from the battlements. Distant, frantic. He didn’t break stride. Rage kept him moving, the heat of it scouring out anything else that dared to rise—pity for the sentries above, the faint twist of wrongness at what he was about to do, the hollow ache of grief. One by one he shoved them all into the furnace until nothing was left.

<!-- paragraph:4242 para_id:00001092 style:Normal -->
And then it was like he was floating. Detached, unmoored. His body still moved, but from somewhere just outside of it he watched himself raise his arms. His mind carved downward, not with craft or precision but with violence—slashing jagged channels through the air, gouging them raw.

<!-- paragraph:4243 para_id:00001093 style:Normal -->
The clouds answered. Electricity screamed down the paths he had torn open, flooding toward the terminus he’d forced into being.

<!-- paragraph:4244 para_id:00001094 style:Normal -->
The gate ceased to exist. In its place, vapor, and the rain of shattered stone and ironwood blasted outward in all directions. The thunderclap rolled back on him in a brutal wave, hammering his Shroud like a hurricane, rattling his bones even through the armor of power.

<!-- paragraph:4245 para_id:00001095 style:Normal -->
He stepped through the smoldering wreckage of the gate, boots grinding over scorched stone and molten iron. Alarm horns blared from within, their notes clashing and overlapping across the citadel grounds.

<!-- paragraph:4246 para_id:00001096 style:Normal -->
Arrows hissed down at him almost immediately, streaks from the towers that ringed the courtyard. Each one struck his Shroud and vanished in a hiss of vapor, licked away by the current crawling along his skin. He felt their origins like threads tugging against him, and without hesitation he carved open jagged channels above. Lightning answered, screaming down and obliterating the towers in showers of ash and splintered stone.

<!-- paragraph:4247 para_id:00001097 style:Normal -->
He moved forward, his own voice breaking free in a raw bellow—half-yell, half-scream—for the one he had come for. “Selara!”

<!-- paragraph:4248 para_id:00001098 style:Normal -->
The name echoed against stone and smoke as he strode toward the Central Spire.

<!-- paragraph:4249 para_id:00001099 style:Normal -->
Then the world tore open.

<!-- paragraph:4250 para_id:0000109A style:Normal -->
A sound hit him—like one of his thunderclaps, but magnified a hundredfold. His Shroud flared by instinct as the air itself split. A vertical line of force ripped outward from the citadel’s entrance, slamming into him with a hurricane’s fury. He leaned into it, grounding the torrent with practiced focus, turning the assault into a storm of sublimated lightning that screamed past and burned itself out against the stones.

<!-- paragraph:4251 para_id:0000109B style:Normal -->
Then stillness.

<!-- paragraph:4252 para_id:0000109C style:Normal -->
Smoke drifted. His eyes followed the furrow carved by the attack, leading back to the Spire’s steps. A figure emerged—a woman, descending with unhurried grace. She wore a fine gown divided for movement, bare arms gleaming as her sigil blazed, stark and bright against the obsidian tone of her throat.

<!-- paragraph:4253 para_id:0000109D style:Normal -->
“Yes,” she said, her voice smooth, almost amused, like one greeting an unexpected dinner guest. “I heard you, Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:4254 para_id:0000109E style:Normal -->
Selara didn’t stop her descent. Her skirts flared with each step, soldiers and magi spilling out in her wake to form a fan behind her. Their presence only heightened her calm, the mask of authority worn like fine silk.

<!-- paragraph:4255 para_id:0000109F style:Normal -->
“How uncouth,” she said, voice cutting smooth as glass. “To appear at my gates, what’s left of them, uninvited. What is it that you want?”

<!-- paragraph:4256 para_id:000010A0 style:Normal -->
Her composure fanned the flames already boiling inside him. Rage pulsed through his body as he felt himself half-detached, floating above it, watching as his own voice broke loose in a raw scream. “I want you to cooperate! To help me unite this city before it tears itself apart!”

<!-- paragraph:4257 para_id:000010A1 style:Normal -->
Selara’s eyes narrowed just slightly, then she rolled them with exaggerated ease. “We already have a system for this. You don’t like the way it works, so you throw a tantrum.”

<!-- paragraph:4258 para_id:000010A2 style:Normal -->
His throat burned, fury and desperation colliding. “Then help me change it!” The words tore out of him, ragged. “The other Clades listen to you. We could build something different—something that cares for more than just us.”

<!-- paragraph:4259 para_id:000010A3 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat, her composure wavered. A flicker, a ruffle across the mask, as though she had heard the rawness in his plea and felt the truth of it. But with her followers watching, the moment passed. Her spine straightened, her expression smoothed. She resumed her role as Head Magus.

<!-- paragraph:4260 para_id:000010A4 style:Normal -->
With a sweep of her hand toward the blasted gate, she said, “And this is caring? This is your system? How many of my soldiers lie dead today because you can’t handle politics?”

<!-- paragraph:4261 para_id:000010A5 style:Normal -->
Niall’s chest heaved. The words came out low, bitter, final. “So that’s it. You can’t be reformed.”

<!-- paragraph:4262 para_id:000010A6 style:Normal -->
Selara’s lip curled. “The ravings of a child. Leave now, or we will be forced to put you down.”

<!-- paragraph:4263 para_id:000010A7 style:Normal -->
For an instant, Niall’s fury sharpened into clarity. The storm stilled inside him, leaving only the calm before it broke. His training carried him into that space where thought accelerated beyond time, where the world slowed to a crawl.

<!-- paragraph:4264 para_id:000010A8 style:Normal -->
Two magi to the left. Three to the right. Archers scattered through the walls and balconies above. And Selara—calm, certain, her confidence anchoring them all—directly before him.

<!-- paragraph:4265 para_id:000010A9 style:Normal -->
He reached upward. His arms rose from his sides, his hands becoming points to guide the vast sweep of his mind. The sky stretched above him, alive and waiting.

<!-- paragraph:4266 para_id:000010AA style:Normal -->
Selara’s face shifted. Shock. Incredulity. She truly hadn’t believed he would dare. Her sigil flared as her lips formed the first shape of a command—attack—but the word never carried.

<!-- paragraph:4267 para_id:000010AB style:Normal -->
His mind soared, weightless, racing higher and higher until it felt as though it hovered at the spire’s peak. In that instant, rage and calm both vanished, and he hung suspended in silence.

<!-- paragraph:4268 para_id:000010AC style:Normal -->
Then he fell.

<!-- paragraph:4269 para_id:000010AD style:Normal -->
The fury returned with him, crashing down from the clouds. He carved a single colossal channel, then split it—once, twice, again and again—until it splintered into a web of searing threads, straining the very edge of his focus.

<!-- paragraph:4270 para_id:000010AE style:Normal -->
The sky answered.

<!-- paragraph:4271 para_id:000010AF style:Normal -->
Before the Lyranth defenders could even move, the lightning struck. Countless lances of white fire tore through them, piercing armor, flesh, stone. Screams rose, cut short as bodies convulsed and burned, the air choked with smoke and scorched blood.

<!-- paragraph:4272 para_id:000010B0 style:Normal -->
Selara stood at the eye of it, her Shroud blazing brighter with each loss, until it was a barrier of pure light. Still she endured, even as her forces died around her beneath the torrent that did not end.

<!-- paragraph:4273 para_id:000010B1 style:Normal -->
His boots struck the cobblestones, steady, relentless, the sound loud in his ears even over the storm. Lightning still cascaded down around them, lancing through the courtyard in ragged curtains of white fire.

<!-- paragraph:4274 para_id:000010B2 style:Normal -->
Selara clapped her palms together, releasing another line of force. It cracked through the air and slammed into him, but it was too weak; he barely had to split his attention to ground it away. She struck again, then again, each effort thinner, her Shroud guttering as he drew more and more of the storm onto her alone.

<!-- paragraph:4275 para_id:000010B3 style:Normal -->
Niall closed the distance.

<!-- paragraph:4276 para_id:000010B4 style:Normal -->
When her Shroud finally flickered out, he cut the lightning, letting the channels collapse. Selara dropped to one knee, shoulders shaking, her face pale as she looked up at him. Her voice was ragged but clear. “This won’t accomplish anything.”

<!-- paragraph:4277 para_id:000010B5 style:Normal -->
Something sharp tore through him at her words, and in the strike of it he saw Alara—her face on another battlefield, her voice in his ear. The memory seared, unbearable. He shoved it down.

<!-- paragraph:4278 para_id:000010B6 style:Normal -->
“My father was right, damn him,” he spat. “You—and this whole world—are rotten to the core.”

<!-- paragraph:4279 para_id:000010B7 style:Normal -->
He carved one final channel, perfect in its cruelty: from the spire’s peak to the center of Selara’s body. The lightning fell straight through her, burning a path into the earth beneath, its flash burning painful recollection from his mind.

<!-- paragraph:4280 para_id:000010B8 style:Normal -->
Silence. Smoke and the stink of scorched flesh clung to the air.

<!-- paragraph:4281 para_id:000010B9 style:Normal -->
He looked around at the ruin. Corpses, rubble, walls half-melted. And still, a heaviness dragged at him, a bone-deep exhaustion that made no sense. This should have been effortless.

<!-- paragraph:4282 para_id:000010BA style:Normal -->
Not knowing if anyone still lived to hear him, he raised his voice to the ruin. “This is what happens if you don’t listen to me!”

<!-- paragraph:4283 para_id:000010BB style:Normal -->
His words rang hollow in the broken courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:4284 para_id:000010BC style:Normal -->
He turned, walking through the wreckage, each step heavy, until the memory fractured and fell away.

<!-- paragraph:4285 para_id:000010BD style:Normal -->
The wreckage dissolved. Smoke, blood, the echo of his voice—all torn away in a rush of light.

<!-- paragraph:4286 para_id:000010BE style:Normal -->
Maya staggered as the room swam back into place around her, the Codex heavy in her lap, her pulse still racing as though she had walked through the carnage herself. The taste of ozone lingered in her mouth, her skin prickling with phantom heat.

<!-- paragraph:4287 para_id:000010BF style:Normal -->
She blinked hard, forcing the images back into the book, into memory where they belonged.

<!-- paragraph:4288 para_id:000010C0 style:Normal -->
“He was so angry,” she murmured.

<!-- paragraph:4289 para_id:000010C1 style:Normal -->
“Anger is usually a cover for something else,” Aldira said quietly. “It warms us like a fire.”

<!-- paragraph:4290 para_id:000010C2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s eyes lifted. “Is he going to do that again?”

<!-- paragraph:4291 para_id:000010C3 style:Normal -->
“I don’t think so.”

<!-- paragraph:4292 para_id:000010C4 style:Normal -->
“Why?”

<!-- paragraph:4293 para_id:000010C5 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s face was impassive, but Maya thought she saw a flicker behind the mask. “He has his reasons.”

<!-- paragraph:4294 para_id:000010C6 style:Normal -->
Maya let out a sharp breath. “Well, that perfect magus the others rallied behind, all force of will and control—that was a myth. I don’t have time to deal with that right now.”

<!-- paragraph:4295 para_id:000010C7 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the closed door, her father’s memory still burning in the air. Slowly, she turned to Aldira. “So that’s why there’s no more expansion war. It’s not treaties. It’s not compromises. It’s not anyone realizing they were in the wrong. It’s because my father beat them into submission with the threat of death.”

<!-- paragraph:4296 para_id:000010C8 style:Normal -->
Her voice sharpened. “But that doesn’t explain why they’d attack now. My Presentation should have scared them.”

<!-- paragraph:4297 para_id:000010C9 style:Normal -->
“No,” Aldira said, her tone flat. “Your Presentation is exactly why they attacked. The Drakhals must have hoped to see weakness—to prove the Rezins were fading. Instead you showed strength. That upset their plan. So they might have panicked - moved everything forward, hoping to strike before the other High Clades start to wonder if you can carry Niall’s mantle.”

<!-- paragraph:4298 para_id:000010CA style:Normal -->
She leaned back in her chair, her expression carved from stone. “Understand the position we’re in. With your father, no one dares risk a straight confrontation. No one knows what it would look like, and they’re too afraid to find out. So the fighting has stayed covert—assassins in alleys, sabotage, mercenary skirmishes everyone pretends not to notice. That’s how the game has been played since Niall made himself the deterrent.”

<!-- paragraph:4299 para_id:000010CB style:Normal -->
Her eyes narrowed. “But that kind of fear doesn’t last forever. The younger magi are restless. They’ve grown up with their ambitions bottled, watching their elders swallow their pride for over a decade. The lid has been on too long, and the pressure’s at a boiling point. If the Drakhals can prove Niall isn’t untouchable—if they can show he’s even slightly vulnerable—the entire balance falls apart.
