Every grudge, every ambition, every old claim will come rushing back.”

<!-- paragraph:4300 para_id:000010CC style:Normal -->
She let the silence hang a moment before finishing. “That’s the real legacy you’ll have to step into—or die. I thought you should know.”

<!-- paragraph:4301 para_id:000010CD style:Normal -->
Maya’s jaw tightened. She refused to let Aldira’s words settle too deeply, not now, not when the weight of them threatened to crush her already frayed thoughts. “Fine. So every other High Clade magus might decide they want to come for us. Then Niall and I will kill them. What we need is a better strategy for dealing with Archeon.”

<!-- paragraph:4302 para_id:000010CE style:Normal -->
Her mind flashed back to the fight—the blur of limbs, the shock of each impact. “When I was close to the High Magus… when I was deflecting her blows, I could feel it. It was like two layers of magic. Her Shroud was there, but underneath, something else. Something that felt like it was trying to eat away at my defenses.”

<!-- paragraph:4303 para_id:000010CF style:Normal -->
Aldira frowned. “That doesn’t make sense. Everyone knows a Shroud is an extension of a magus’s own power. It’s the first thing they learn, a step toward mastering hereditary magic. It’s not separate.”

<!-- paragraph:4304 para_id:000010D0 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “Two of them wore gauntlets—heavy, black, covered in runes. I thought magi considered it shameful to rely on tools. But the pull of that second magic was stronger from those weapons.”

<!-- paragraph:4305 para_id:000010D1 style:Normal -->
“That is… surprising,” Aldira admitted, her voice measured. “Though if any Clade would ignore convention, it would be Archeon.”

<!-- paragraph:4306 para_id:000010D2 style:Normal -->
Maya pressed her palms to the table. “And there’s more. I felt something else, like a tug on the strands of fate themselves. And how are they so fast, so physically strong? Everyone says they have especially powerful Shrouds, but if that’s true, why would they need gauntlets at all? None of it adds up.”

<!-- paragraph:4307 para_id:000010D3 style:Normal -->
“Plenty of time to unravel the secrets of the universe later,” a dry voice said from the doorway.

<!-- paragraph:4308 para_id:000010D4 style:Normal -->
Maya looked up to see Erynn striding in, her hands on her hips. “Right now you need to eat. And bathe.”

<!-- paragraph:4309 para_id:000010D5 style:Normal -->
Only then did Maya feel it fully—the gnawing ache in her belly, the grime of sweat, soot, and blood caked on her skin. She was starving.

<!-- paragraph:4310 para_id:000010D6 style:Normal -->
Maya ate quickly, scarcely tasting the food, and let Erynn fuss over her as she washed and changed. The simple act of being clean again felt foreign after the chaos of the walls. But as soon as she was able, Maya turned her steps toward the practice arena.

<!-- paragraph:4311 para_id:000010D7 style:Normal -->
Erynn trailed after her, arms folded, exasperation plain. “Is it really necessary to do this right now?”

<!-- paragraph:4312 para_id:000010D8 style:Normal -->
“I thought of techniques during the fight,” Maya said, her stride brisk despite the weariness still heavy in her limbs. “Ways to counter Archeon’s style. But I wasn’t fast enough. They need to be burned into me perfectly until I don’t even think about them. That fluency gap—that hesitation—that’s what kept me from switching to offense.”

<!-- paragraph:4313 para_id:000010D9 style:Normal -->
“There you go again,” Erynn sighed, “saying perfectly like it’s some kind of magic spell. What does that even mean?”

<!-- paragraph:4314 para_id:000010DA style:Normal -->
Maya stopped, meeting her eyes. “Just… you know… perfect. It means no hesitation. No uncertainty. No weakness. Just pure, perfect action.”

<!-- paragraph:4315 para_id:000010DB style:Normal -->
Erynn threw her hands up. “You can’t define a word with itself.”

<!-- paragraph:4316 para_id:000010DC style:Normal -->
For a moment, Maya held her ground. Then her voice softened, the steel still underneath. “What if Archeon come back? I need to be ready.”

<!-- paragraph:4317 para_id:000010DD style:Normal -->
Erynn gave her an incredulous look. “What—you think they’re coming back tonight? That’s ridiculous. If anything, running yourself ragged drilling techniques now will just leave you more vulnerable tomorrow.”

<!-- paragraph:4318 para_id:000010DE style:Normal -->
Maya ground her teeth, then huffed. “You know what? I hate it when you’re right. Fine. But just because I’m not training doesn’t mean I’m not going to be productive. Get Harvick and meet me at the library.”

<!-- paragraph:4319 para_id:000010DF style:Normal -->
“I’ll send someone else for Harvick,” Erynn countered, already crossing her arms. “And I’m not sure this is actually better.”

<!-- paragraph:4320 para_id:000010E0 style:Normal -->
Maya rolled her eyes but gestured to one of her guards, who hurried off. The other fell in behind her as she and Erynn made their way down through the citadel’s interior passages.

<!-- paragraph:4321 para_id:000010E1 style:Normal -->
The corridors cooled as they descended, the echo of their boots swallowed by stone.

<!-- paragraph:4322 para_id:000010E2 style:Normal -->
“I think you like being worried,” Erynn said at last.

<!-- paragraph:4323 para_id:000010E3 style:Normal -->
Maya shot her a sideways glance. “No one likes being worried.”

<!-- paragraph:4324 para_id:000010E4 style:Normal -->
“Then why are you always trying to guess what’s going to happen and prepare for it?” Erynn pressed.

<!-- paragraph:4325 para_id:000010E5 style:Normal -->
“It’s not a guess,” Maya replied flatly. “I can see the future.”

<!-- paragraph:4326 para_id:000010E6 style:Normal -->
Erynn arched a brow. “Then why act on it if you know it’s going to happen? Or is it really the future?”

<!-- paragraph:4327 para_id:000010E7 style:Normal -->
“It never hurts to be prepared,” Maya muttered.

<!-- paragraph:4328 para_id:000010E8 style:Normal -->
“I think,” Erynn said, tilting her head, “it’s more comfortable for you to be a little uncomfortable than it would be to admit you can’t prepare for everything.”

<!-- paragraph:4329 para_id:000010E9 style:Normal -->
“You’re really after me today,” Maya said, half-exasperated. “Can’t you give me a break after everything that just happened?”

<!-- paragraph:4330 para_id:000010EA style:Normal -->
“Give yourself a break,” Erynn shot back, but her smile softened the words.

<!-- paragraph:4331 para_id:000010EB style:Normal -->
The corridors cooled as they descended, the echo of their boots swallowed by stone. By the time they reached the records annex, the air smelled of dust and beeswax, thick enough to coat the tongue. Stacks of crates leaned precariously against groaning shelves, and yellowed scraps of parchment jutted from ledgers as though the place itself were trying to spit them out. The faint buzz of arcane lanterns overhead gave the chaos a tired, amber glow.

<!-- paragraph:4332 para_id:000010EC style:Normal -->
The same old librarian looked up at Maya as she entered. His face was a map of wrinkles, his spine bent almost double, his white hair clinging to his head in sparse wisps. His quill still trembled faintly in one hand as though reluctant to stop writing. He squinted at her without a glimmer of recognition.

<!-- paragraph:4333 para_id:000010ED style:Normal -->
“Any records you have on Archeon,” Maya said curtly, then turned to one of her guards. “Go with him this time. Carry everything back here.”

<!-- paragraph:4334 para_id:000010EE style:Normal -->
The guard gave a sharp nod and followed as the clerk shuffled off, muttering incoherently about brackets and someone fixing things “fifty years too late.” His voice drifted into the labyrinth of shelves and boxes until only the faint buzz of the lanterns remained.

<!-- paragraph:4335 para_id:000010EF style:Normal -->
Maya sat across from Erynn at a battered oak table. For a few moments they were silent. Then Erynn, predictably, broke. “So, isn’t Harvick a little old for you?”

<!-- paragraph:4336 para_id:000010F0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s glare could have frozen stone. She wondered sourly which would arrive first—Harvick or the records.

<!-- paragraph:4337 para_id:000010F1 style:Normal -->
It was the records. Her guard staggered back under the weight of stacked boxes, jaw set with effort. Maya rose smoothly, reinforcing her Shroud just enough to redistribute the load as though it weighed nothing. She caught the flicker of envy in her guard’s eyes as she slid the crates onto the table.

<!-- paragraph:4338 para_id:000010F2 style:Normal -->
Despite the bulk, the contents were thin. Maya flipped through brittle ledgers and scattered notes, her frustration mounting. “There are thousands of records down here,” she muttered, “but never the ones I actually need.”

<!-- paragraph:4339 para_id:000010F3 style:Normal -->
She found the familiar scraps: the Archeons had only been recognized as a High Clade for a single generation. Serathis—the High Magus who had attacked her—was their founder. A few other notes caught her eye: Serathis had once had a husband, also a magus, though he had died several years ago. Scrawled commentary in a sharp, decisive hand she was beginning to recognize as Aldira’s suggested his mind had frayed as he aged, and that Serathis herself had been forced to end him.

<!-- paragraph:4340 para_id:000010F4 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the words, her stomach tightening. Killed by his own wife. Even for a High Clade, it was horrible.

<!-- paragraph:4341 para_id:000010F5 style:Normal -->
Maya lingered over Aldira’s notes, her stomach twisting. The power of a High Magus gone mad could never be contained—not by soldiers, not by walls. For a moment an image flashed, unbidden and horrifying: her father, lightning spilling from his hands, his mind cracked beyond repair, the Citadel itself reduced to ash under his fury.

<!-- paragraph:4342 para_id:000010F6 style:Normal -->
She shoved the thought away, too raw to hold, and forced her eyes back to the scattered parchments. But there was little else here, nothing she hadn’t already seen.

<!-- paragraph:4343 para_id:000010F7 style:Normal -->
To shape her own thoughts, she spoke aloud, her voice low. “What we know is that Archeon has been running some of the most extensive breeding programs outside of Drakhal. They’ve only been a High Clade for a single generation. Serathis claimed she had many grandchildren who could use magic, but only two of them have been Presented—and only one still lives. And because they weren’t a High Clade until recently, there’s almost nothing on them from earlier than a generation ago.”

<!-- paragraph:4344 para_id:000010F8 style:Normal -->
Erynn leaned back in her chair, her expression distant, almost dreamy. “Yeah. And no one really knows what goes on inside those citadels. You can bribe a servant or two, sure, but if they want to keep secrets? They’ll never let anyone who knows anything leave. They can limit who sees what. Lock it all away behind their walls.”

<!-- paragraph:4345 para_id:000010F9 style:Normal -->
Maya closed the ledger in front of her, its thin weight a sharp contrast to the enormity of the questions it left unanswered.

<!-- paragraph:4346 para_id:000010FA style:Normal -->
Maya leaned back in her chair, fingers drumming lightly against the table. “There’s something about those gauntlets. The zänwork—I know zän, and I didn’t recognize a single one they used. Could they have been reinforcing their Shrouds somehow?” She frowned, shaking her head. “But that makes no sense. Serathis wasn’t wearing any equipment, even though her grandsons had the gauntlets.”

<!-- paragraph:4347 para_id:000010FB style:Normal -->
Erynn gave a sharp laugh, without humor. “Of course it doesn’t make sense. That’s the point. They’re hiding something, and they just tried to kill you with it.” She slumped back in her chair, her gaze flicking toward the stack of thin records. “And it’s not like anyone’s going to share what they’re really doing. Not with us. Not with anyone. That’s the way the High Clades have always been.”

<!-- paragraph:4348 para_id:000010FC style:Normal -->
The door creaked open and Harvick stepped in, brushing a layer of dust from his coat.

<!-- paragraph:4349 para_id:000010FD style:Normal -->
“It’s good to see you’re all right,” Maya said. Her voice was steady, but the words felt stilted. After a pause, she added, “And your… friends? Are they—?”

<!-- paragraph:4350 para_id:000010FE style:Normal -->
Harvick’s expression tightened, the weight of memory in his eyes. “Every time this kind of thing happens, I lose friends.”

<!-- paragraph:4351 para_id:000010FF style:Normal -->
The words hit harder than she expected. Panic surged through her chest before she could stop it—an image of Arrek cut down, buried among the faceless dead.

<!-- paragraph:4352 para_id:00001100 style:Normal -->
Harvick must have seen the flicker in her expression, because his gaze softened. He cleared his throat, looking as though the words themselves were an effort. “But, uh… all the zänwrights are fine. From what I heard.” He hesitated, then added awkwardly, “Which I’m telling you because I’m… friends with… one of them.”

<!-- paragraph:4353 para_id:00001101 style:Normal -->
They sat in silence for a moment.

<!-- paragraph:4354 para_id:00001102 style:Normal -->
Maya forced herself to straighten. “Right,” she said at last. “So. I hear you’re some kind of expert in martial arts.”

<!-- paragraph:4355 para_id:00001103 style:Normal -->
Harvick scratched at the back of his neck. “Well, I don’t know if I’d say that. But I guess I know a few things.”

<!-- paragraph:4356 para_id:00001104 style:Normal -->
“From what I heard,” Maya pressed, “you only lose fights when you want to. You’ve got some kind of edge the others don’t.”

<!-- paragraph:4357 para_id:00001105 style:Normal -->
Harvick laughed, but it was thin, his eyes flicking around the room as though searching for an escape. “Hey, people spread rumors. I’m just an old dog with a lot of tricks up his sleeve. And I lose plenty.”

<!-- paragraph:4358 para_id:00001106 style:Normal -->
That nervousness was answer enough. Maya turned to Erynn and her guard. “Wait outside.”

<!-- paragraph:4359 para_id:00001107 style:Normal -->
Erynn gave her a long look but obeyed, ushering the guard out with her. The door clicked shut behind them.

<!-- paragraph:4360 para_id:00001108 style:Normal -->
Maya leaned forward. “I know how this works. Everyone keeps secrets, always looking for an edge. I’m not going to expose yours. But I also know you fought alongside Niall in the war. He wouldn’t keep someone close unless they were more than just competent. And there’s that sword of yours. Something doesn’t add up.”

<!-- paragraph:4361 para_id:00001109 style:Normal -->
“Now that’s just offensive,” Harvick said, forcing a grin. “My experience is worth plenty. I help train all the new ones, don’t I?” But a bead of sweat broke at his brow, betraying him.

<!-- paragraph:4362 para_id:0000110A style:Normal -->
Maya narrowed her eyes. “Fine. Let’s talk hypothetically. I fought those Archeon magi. Their speed, their strength—it doesn’t make sense, even if their Shrouds are supposed to be especially strong. So tell me, hypothetically, is there a way to push physical capabilities beyond the human limit? Training, zän, anything. Something that lets a man—who’s well past his prime—consistently outfight younger, stronger swordsmen?”

<!-- paragraph:4363 para_id:0000110B style:Normal -->
Harvick blew out a breath and rubbed the back of his neck. “Fine. I’ll tell you. But don’t go spreading this around.” He leaned forward, lowering his voice. “Hypothetically, there might be… some special things someone could learn. Things that make you, temporarily, maybe a little stronger or faster. Old tricks from Naeroth—where your mom came from. Like… breathing, and… stuff. But it’s not like you’re going to be smashing through walls. Nothing close.”

<!-- paragraph:4364 para_id:0000110C style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “All right. But what about zän?”

<!-- paragraph:4365 para_id:0000110D style:Normal -->
He shook his head. “zän can do crazy things, sure. But I’ve never heard of one that just makes you strong. There are storage zän—electricity, heat, whatever—and you can let that out later. But strength? No. Not like that.”

<!-- paragraph:4366 para_id:0000110E style:Normal -->
“I know the ones you mean,” Maya said. “But using them in combat isn’t practical. They burn out too quickly, and the materials are too expensive.”

<!-- paragraph:4367 para_id:0000110F style:Normal -->
“Then maybe Archeon figured out a way to make them cheaper. Or more efficient,” Harvick offered, shrugging.

<!-- paragraph:4368 para_id:00001110 style:Normal -->
Maya tapped her fingers against the tabletop. “Why doesn’t Niall want anyone else to know about this? I assume he does.”

<!-- paragraph:4369 para_id:00001111 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a small nod. “He knows. And he’s afraid of exactly what just happened. He’s an army enough by himself, but if techniques like this started getting out, the whole balance shifts. Who knows how long Archeon’s been using those gauntlets? Anyone else who’s seen them is probably dead.”

<!-- paragraph:4370 para_id:00001112 style:Normal -->
Maya’s eyes narrowed. “Well, at least this explains why Drakhal would work with Archeon. They must have more information. Enough that they think they could blackmail Archeon if it came to it.”

<!-- paragraph:4371 para_id:00001113 style:Normal -->
She leaned forward, her voice cooling. “There’s some kind of combination at work here giving them their power. I can’t pin it down, but the summary is this: it’s some kind of technological breakthrough, dressed up to look like pure magical strength. But whatever it is, it isn’t hereditary magic. If that got out—if the others knew they’d lowered themselves with tools and tricks—every other High Clade would gang up on them to protect their status.”

<!-- paragraph:4372 para_id:00001114 style:Normal -->
Harvick leaned back in his chair, his expression caught somewhere between weary and wry. “See, that’s the thing. You all go out there with your Shrouds and lightning and fire—no offense—”

<!-- paragraph:4373 para_id:00001115 style:Normal -->
Maya arched a brow.

<!-- paragraph:4374 para_id:00001116 style:Normal -->
“—and tell yourselves that makes you the right ones to rule,” he finished. “But admitting that it’s possible to break into the High Clades without pure, powerful hereditary magic? That ruins the whole story. Shows it’s not about bloodlines, or training, or any of the rituals you lot cling to. It’s just about having the biggest stick.”

<!-- paragraph:4375 para_id:00001117 style:Normal -->
Maya leaned forward. “Those Archeon magi were brutal. Aggressive in ways I wasn’t prepared for. Do you think you could teach me some of that special stuff, to handle them better?”

<!-- paragraph:4376 para_id:00001118 style:Normal -->
Harvick glanced toward the door, making sure it was still shut, then lowered his voice. “Well… assuming I could, it’s not beginner work. More advanced. And I’m really more of a sword man, anyway.” His mouth tugged into a half-smile. “And I doubt you’re about to start carrying one of those around.”

<!-- paragraph:4377 para_id:00001119 style:Normal -->
Maya tilted her head, unamused.

<!-- paragraph:4378 para_id:0000111A style:Normal -->
He held her look for a moment before adding, quieter still, “But I do know someone who’s good at hand-to-hand. I’ll put in a good word for you.”

<!-- paragraph:4381 para_id:0000111D style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 24

<!-- paragraph:4382 para_id:0000111E style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 24

<!-- paragraph:4384 para_id:00001120 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4385 para_id:00001121 style:Normal -->
Davian stood in the courtyard, hands shoved deep into his pockets, watching the morning bustle churn around him. He’d come early, hoping to throw himself back into the work—anything to quiet his thoughts—but even the replacement zänwright crew he’d volunteered to help hadn’t started. The wall project was still on hold. Too much else came first: restoring order, clearing bodies from the parapets, rearming soldiers for whatever might come next. The fortress was alive with movement, but none of it needed him.

<!-- paragraph:4386 para_id:00001122 style:Normal -->
He could still feel the hooks of the Ink Boon threaded through his mind, what few remained after the fight. For a while they’d loosened, their grip dulled by routine and exhaustion. Now they had sunk back in. It was as if the darkness that once lingered only at the edges of his vision had spread, until the world itself looked like a pinprick of light far down a tunnel he had no right to walk.

<!-- paragraph:4387 para_id:00001123 style:Normal -->
And still, even in that narrowing dark, he felt the weight of eyes. Soldiers passed by with their bundles of gear, their expressions unreadable. Yet when he glanced their way, he could swear he’d caught suspicion in their faces a moment before, like glances meant for an intruder, a fraud. Either way, the moment his gaze touched theirs, the looks were gone, leaving only the echo of mistrust crawling across his skin.

<!-- paragraph:4388 para_id:00001124 style:Normal -->
Davian flexed his sore hands, the memory of Brann’s Shroud still grinding against his nerves. He had wanted to finish it himself, to bring the Archeon down. Needed to, if he was honest. And not just for the mission. Something deeper had driven him—something he didn’t want to name. In the end, he hadn’t done it. He’d stood there bleeding, watching while Niall Rezin ended it with a single gesture.

<!-- paragraph:4389 para_id:00001125 style:Normal -->
He could still see it: lightning tearing down from the sky like judgment, ripping the battlefield apart. Not just power—detachment. A man emptied of everything but violence. That was what true self-sufficiency looked like, wasn’t it? To stand so far above the rest of them that the world became nothing but insects underfoot.

<!-- paragraph:4390 para_id:00001126 style:Normal -->
Niall hadn’t even looked at him. Why would he?

<!-- paragraph:4391 para_id:00001127 style:Normal -->
A flicker of Selwin’s face came unbidden, the boy straining under magic too raw to control. Davian could already see the shape of what he’d become if nothing stopped him. Another monster, another hollow man.

<!-- paragraph:4392 para_id:00001128 style:Normal -->
He might have gone deeper down that path if not for the sound of boots crunching against the stone. Harvick, crossing the courtyard with that same unhurried stride, gaze fixed squarely on him.

<!-- paragraph:4393 para_id:00001129 style:Normal -->
It was uncanny, the way the man seemed to appear whenever Davian’s mind was chewing itself apart. The moment broke like glass. The heat of those phantom stares came rushing back, soldiers’ eyes on him from every direction—harsh, judging, full of suspicion—and then, just as quickly, boiled away under the pressure of his direct glare. No one was looking. No one at all.

<!-- paragraph:4394 para_id:0000112A style:Normal -->
Harvick stopped beside him, arms loose at his sides. “Got something more interesting for you today,” he said, tone mild.

<!-- paragraph:4395 para_id:0000112B style:Normal -->
The urge to snap back rose hard, sharp on his tongue. He wanted to make Harvick flinch, to drive him away, to prove that the darkness was all he had left. But the realization hit just as fast: it wouldn’t work. Harvick would let it roll off him, the way he always did, and keep seeing Davian as someone else—Arrek, the careful, capable contractor. A name borrowed. A lie with an expiration date etched into his skin in black ink.

<!-- paragraph:4396 para_id:0000112C style:Normal -->
He fell into step anyway, the dark tunnel in his head loosening by degrees as they walked. The direction was obvious enough: another training courtyard. Davian couldn’t resist letting the bitterness slip out as they approached.

<!-- paragraph:4397 para_id:0000112D style:Normal -->
“Why do we even do this?” he muttered. “If unkillable magi decide battles in an instant, what good is any of this training?”

<!-- paragraph:4398 para_id:0000112E style:Normal -->
They walked in silence for a stretch, boots clicking against the stone. Harvick finally broke it. “You live in this world, right?”

<!-- paragraph:4399 para_id:0000112F style:Normal -->
Davian shot him a look. “Yeah. Doesn’t mean I need to like it.”

<!-- paragraph:4400 para_id:00001130 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a short chuckle. “You don’t need to like it. Or forgive it. Or say you think it’s great. All you have to do is accept it.”

<!-- paragraph:4401 para_id:00001131 style:Normal -->
Davian stopped walking. The words landed hard, heavier than Harvick seemed to mean them. He stood there in the corridor, pulse spiking. “I can’t accept it. I can’t accept a world where I have no agency, no ability to change anything. Where we have to live under the thumb of people who can erase us with an eyeblink.”

<!-- paragraph:4402 para_id:00001132 style:Normal -->
For the first time, Harvick’s composure cracked. His shoulders shifted, eyes narrowing ever so slightly. A flicker of unease crossed his face, and Davian felt a dark satisfaction coil in his chest. He’d broken him. This was how it was supposed to be—proof that he was unfixable, unsalvageable.

<!-- paragraph:4403 para_id:00001133 style:Normal -->
Instead of arguing, Harvick let out a long breath and sat down heavily on the low stone lip of the corridor wall. He nodded at the space across from him. “Sit.”

<!-- paragraph:4404 para_id:00001134 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated, then lowered himself opposite. His body felt heavier than he wanted to admit, exhaustion settling in as soon as he stopped moving. Harvick leaned back, eyes following the empty length of corridor ahead.

<!-- paragraph:4405 para_id:00001135 style:Normal -->
“I just want you to know,” he said, voice quieter now, “I don’t have all these great answers for you. Not sure I ever did. But I can see the direction you’re walking in, and I know how it ends. The worst part is, it feels like you know too—and you don’t even want to go there.”

<!-- paragraph:4406 para_id:00001136 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at his hands. “I don’t know what I want,” he muttered. “Every direction seems bad.”

<!-- paragraph:4407 para_id:00001137 style:Normal -->
“Maybe that’s the issue.” Harvick’s gaze flicked back toward him. “If you don’t know what you want, you’ll keep hanging on to what’s familiar. And I can tell you hate the High Clades—don’t bother protesting. You’re not even a little sneaky about it.”

<!-- paragraph:4408 para_id:00001138 style:Normal -->
Despite himself, Davian almost smiled. The picture was absurd: this soldier of the Rezins, emblem of everything he loathed, patiently coaching someone who hated everything he represented.

<!-- paragraph:4409 para_id:00001139 style:Normal -->
Harvick rubbed the back of his neck, words coming haltingly. “I don’t really know how to say this right. Never was good at it. But you have to have something to run toward, not just away from. And I know, with absolute certainty, there’s good in you. Some part of you wants to do more than just burn it all down. Otherwise, you’d never have saved Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:4410 para_id:0000113A style:Normal -->
Despite every argument he stacked against it—telling himself he’d only saved Selwin out of selfish calculation, telling himself Harvick was seeing a mirage propped up by the mask of Arrek—Davian still felt something stir. Harvick’s words had reached him. Not all the way, not enough to believe them, but enough to matter. Enough to remind him that Harvick was trying, really trying.

<!-- paragraph:4411 para_id:0000113B style:Normal -->
The question slipped out before he could stop it. “From the way you talk… you’ve been in a position like this before. What was it?”

<!-- paragraph:4412 para_id:0000113C style:Normal -->
Harvick grimaced, then huffed a short laugh at himself. “Guess I couldn’t be the only one asking hard questions all the time.” He pushed himself upright and started walking again. Davian forced his legs to follow, joints stiff as he rose.

<!-- paragraph:4413 para_id:0000113D style:Normal -->
“I’ll keep it brief,” Harvick said, tone quieter now. “Can’t make myself say much more. But—I had a friend, a long time ago. Really close. I was younger, looked up to him. And he went down the same road I see you walking—spiraling into the dark. And I…” His jaw tightened. “I ended up helping kill him.”

<!-- paragraph:4414 para_id:0000113E style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes narrowed. “So that’s what this is? You’re just trying to make up for failing back then?”

<!-- paragraph:4415 para_id:0000113F style:Normal -->
“I don’t really know,” Harvick admitted. “Maybe. But aren’t we all just trying to make up for our past failings?”

<!-- paragraph:4416 para_id:00001140 style:Normal -->
Selina’s face flashed through Davian’s mind, sharp and painful as ever. But this time it didn’t cut as deep. Seeing Harvick’s shadowed expression made it easier to carry.

<!-- paragraph:4417 para_id:00001141 style:Normal -->
He exhaled, then tilted his head with a crooked grin, wriggling out of the heaviness. “So this friend of yours was older than you?”

<!-- paragraph:4418 para_id:00001142 style:Normal -->
“Yeah.” Harvick gave him a side-eye, already bracing for it.

<!-- paragraph:4419 para_id:00001143 style:Normal -->
“So there’s no way he’d still be alive anyway.”

<!-- paragraph:4420 para_id:00001144 style:Normal -->
Harvick rolled his eyes. “He didn’t remind me of you that much. After all, he was smart.”

<!-- paragraph:4421 para_id:00001145 style:Normal -->
Davian swung a mock punch toward his kidney, but Harvick twisted aside with practiced ease, smirking as Davian’s fist cut through air. Their footsteps carried them out of the dim corridor into the open light of the training courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:4422 para_id:00001146 style:Normal -->
Davian scanned the courtyard as they stepped inside. No racks of dulled practice swords, no weighted staves—just a broad expanse of mats rolled smooth across the stone. He frowned. “So what are we doing today?”

<!-- paragraph:4423 para_id:00001147 style:Normal -->
Harvick shifted his weight, oddly hesitant. “Well… I actually thought maybe you could teach someone else some of your… fighting… stuff.”

<!-- paragraph:4424 para_id:00001148 style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes narrowed. He glanced around again, but the courtyard was empty. “Who? And why are you acting weird?”

<!-- paragraph:4425 para_id:00001149 style:Normal -->
Footsteps sounded from the far corridor. Voices carried lightly over the stone, and even before they entered, Davian recognized the cadence of one of them. Maya.

<!-- paragraph:4426 para_id:0000114A style:Normal -->
She stepped through the archway a moment later, Erynn at her side, her voice trailing off into silence when she saw them. She wasn’t in her usual austere gown but in something closer to what Harvick wore—leather armor, cut to move, the dark lines fitted to her frame with an elegance that made Davian’s chest tighten. His eyes traced the curve of her figure before he wrenched them away, heat creeping into his throat.

<!-- paragraph:4427 para_id:0000114B style:Normal -->
He hissed at Harvick, low and sharp. “Why is she here?”

<!-- paragraph:4428 para_id:0000114C style:Normal -->
Harvick’s mouth twitched. “Well, she does own the place.”

<!-- paragraph:4429 para_id:0000114D style:Normal -->
“I knew you were acting suspicious,” Davian muttered. He risked another glance out of the corner of his eye—just to make sure she couldn’t hear—and had to drag his gaze away again almost immediately.

<!-- paragraph:4430 para_id:0000114E style:Normal -->
Harvick smirked. “Think you look like you’re going to enjoy this.”

<!-- paragraph:4431 para_id:0000114F style:Normal -->
Davian rotated slightly, as if adjusting his stance, and caught Harvick in the shin with a quick, hidden kick. Harvick’s eyes bulged, but he swallowed the reaction.

<!-- paragraph:4432 para_id:00001150 style:Normal -->
Davian leaned closer, voice even quieter now, words tumbling out in a rush. “I can’t control it, all right? Everyone knows the High Clades breed their children to be—” he nearly sputtered, “—inhumanly good-looking.”

<!-- paragraph:4433 para_id:00001151 style:Normal -->
Harvick had to choke down a laugh. “I don’t know who you’ve been talking to, but that’s definitely not one of the breeding priorities. Just admit she’s your type.”

<!-- paragraph:4434 para_id:00001152 style:Normal -->
Davian opened his mouth to protest—but a polite cough behind him froze him in place. He turned, forcing a half smile that landed somewhere between polite and pained, and found himself face to face with Maya.

<!-- paragraph:4435 para_id:00001153 style:Normal -->
Her hair was pulled up loosely, a few strands framing her face in a way that made her look both more relaxed and somehow more striking. Davian locked his eyes on hers through sheer force of will.

<!-- paragraph:4436 para_id:00001154 style:Normal -->
“Oh—Lady Maya,” he said, each syllable deliberate. Both her brows rose at the uncharacteristic use of her title. “We were just discussing, uh… some finer points of training.”

<!-- paragraph:4437 para_id:00001155 style:Normal -->
Davian’s mind kicked into overdrive, heat flooding his chest. He felt cornered and couldn’t even name why. Some part of him screamed to get out—to cut this short before it went any further. But another part, treacherous and insistent, wanted nothing more than to stay right here, to let himself be close to her. He had to admit it now: she was striking, disarmingly so. And the thought made his skin crawl, because he knew he shouldn’t take another step closer. He was going to betray her. It was only a matter of time.

<!-- paragraph:4438 para_id:00001156 style:Normal -->
He forced a grin, scrambling for an exit. “Don’t you have any other hand-to-hand experts? Surely I’m not the best you’ve got.” He tried to pitch it as a joke, and hated himself for the flicker of hope that she’d say no—that she did need him here.

<!-- paragraph:4439 para_id:00001157 style:Normal -->
Maya rolled her eyes, her voice smooth with that practiced coolness of hers, though there was the faintest glint in her expression. “No. Our experts here are experts in weapons—because generally, when you want to kill someone, like in a war, you use one of those instead of your fists.” Her gaze lingered on him for a beat, sharp as ice, before she added, “But since Archeon decided to upend years of tradition and turn magus fights into a street brawl, I find myself in desperate need of someone with the same kind of… uncouth talents.”

<!-- paragraph:4440 para_id:00001158 style:Normal -->
Harvick stepped in smoothly, breaking the tension before Davian could claw his way out of it. “Look, I don’t know too much about Shrouds and magi and all that, but Lady Maya thinks she needs to develop some new techniques for when someone gets right up in her face. And you did specifically say you hate being idle and wanted to help out.” He gave Davian a pointed look. “You’ll never get a better chance than this.”

<!-- paragraph:4441 para_id:00001159 style:Normal -->
Davian’s mind spun. He could still make the smart choice—refuse, walk away, keep the distance that had always protected him. Keep the walls up, keep the hatred burning. Rage was ugly, but it was reliable. It kept the emptiness at bay.

<!-- paragraph:4442 para_id:0000115A style:Normal -->
But standing this close to her… it sparked something different. Not the searing fire of hate, not the hollow gnaw of the Boon’s whispers, but a warmth that almost startled him. Comforting. Dangerous.

<!-- paragraph:4443 para_id:0000115B style:Normal -->
He knew what his parents would say. That this choice was selfish, indulgent, a crack in the armor. And maybe it was. But in that moment, he let himself fall toward it.

<!-- paragraph:4444 para_id:0000115C style:Normal -->
He made a show of rolling his eyes, giving an exaggerated sigh. “Okay. Fine. If it’ll help.”

<!-- paragraph:4445 para_id:0000115D style:Normal -->
Harvick looked pleased, clapping his hands once. “Great.”

<!-- paragraph:4446 para_id:0000115E style:Normal -->
Davian led Maya toward the mats, trying to look as though this was nothing unusual. He turned—and found her far closer than he’d expected. The air around her shimmered faintly, the near-invisible distortion of her Shroud hugging her frame. For a moment their eyes locked, and he swore her breathing picked up. He filed it away as some kind of esoteric technique, a way of reinforcing her Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:4447 para_id:0000115F style:Normal -->
He cleared his throat. “All right, before we do this, I need to understand the problem. You can destroy entire battalions from a distance. I saw you freeze an assassin solid with your Shroud in an eyeblink. How are these Archeon magi even a threat to you?”

<!-- paragraph:4448 para_id:00001160 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms, voice cool. “The way I manifest fire requires time to build enough heat. And when I tried to siphon directly from the magus’s body, it was as if the space resisted my will. Then they close the distance in an eyeblink, and their strikes carry so much power that sublimating it leaves me no focus for complex offensive spells.”

<!-- paragraph:4449 para_id:00001161 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “I don’t really understand how Shrouds work. I thought they just made you invulnerable.”

<!-- paragraph:4450 para_id:00001162 style:Normal -->
She exhaled sharply, not quite a sigh, but close. “I don’t have time to educate you fully. But you must at least understand this much: energy—power, force—it has to come from somewhere, and it has to go somewhere. Shrouds function through sublimation. Somehow—no one’s certain exactly how—they take whatever energy could harm you and turn it into something aligned with your hereditary magic. That makes it possible for your own power to disperse it.”

<!-- paragraph:4451 para_id:00001163 style:Normal -->
Davian froze, a jolt running through him. Changing the form of energy into something else—that was exactly what his own family’s Subversion did. Before he could stop himself, the question slipped out. “Why can’t every magic user form a Shroud?”

<!-- paragraph:4452 para_id:00001164 style:Normal -->
Maya gave him an odd look, head tilting slightly. “Well, that’s the question all the Clades have been asking for generations. It just seems certain strains of magic form strong Shrouds, and others don’t.”

<!-- paragraph:4453 para_id:00001165 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “It just seems strange that they’re all so similar. The Shrouds, I mean—the way they look, the way they act.”

<!-- paragraph:4454 para_id:00001166 style:Normal -->
Her eyes sharpened, and she stepped closer, her voice dropping low, almost a purr. “And you’ve seen lots of Shrouds up close, have you? Enough to compare them?”

<!-- paragraph:4455 para_id:00001167 style:Normal -->
Heat surged into his face before he could stop it. His mind scrambled through every Shroud he’d seen, trying to sort which ones wouldn’t seem suspicious if he admitted to them, and whether that would be enough to justify what he’d just said. He cursed himself silently for letting her push him off balance, for forgetting the delicacy of the mask he wore.

<!-- paragraph:4456 para_id:00001168 style:Normal -->
He stepped back quickly, words tumbling out in a half-stammer. “Right—yeah, I get the idea. So you want to train some techniques that’ll let you use that Shroud stuff closer in, faster.”

<!-- paragraph:4457 para_id:00001169 style:Normal -->
Maya’s lips curved in a smile, coy and deliberate. “Now you’re getting the idea.”

<!-- paragraph:4458 para_id:0000116A style:Normal -->
For a moment, the tilt of her body, the glint in her eyes, made him feel like prey under a predator’s gaze. Only the sight of Harvick behind her, struggling to hide a grin, jolted him back to the courtyard and out of the trap of her nearness.

<!-- paragraph:4459 para_id:0000116B style:Normal -->
Davian shot Harvick the sharpest glare he could manage without turning his head, hoping Maya wouldn’t notice. Harvick only raised his brows innocently, the picture of a man who’d done nothing wrong.

<!-- paragraph:4460 para_id:0000116C style:Normal -->
Movement caught at the edge of Davian’s vision. Erynn was watching too, lips tugged into a smirk before she quickly schooled her face, chin lifting primly as if she’d been studying the sky the whole time.

<!-- paragraph:4461 para_id:0000116D style:Normal -->
Davian suppressed a groan. Those two were a dangerous pair.

<!-- paragraph:4462 para_id:0000116E style:Normal -->
Davian cleared his throat, forcing his focus back onto the task. “Well, I still don’t know much about Shrouds. But it sounds like you’re basically asking how to buy some time against an opponent who’s stronger than you so you can do your… magic stuff.”

<!-- paragraph:4463 para_id:0000116F style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression sharpened. “They aren’t stronger than me.”

<!-- paragraph:4464 para_id:00001170 style:Normal -->
“Okay, fine,” Davian said, hands lifting in mock surrender. “An opponent who’s better at punching. Does the phrasing really matter?”

<!-- paragraph:4465 para_id:00001171 style:Normal -->
Her eyes cut sideways at him, a cool glance that said plenty. He pressed on. “Let’s start with the basics then. When you’re up against someone stronger—uh, punchier—you can’t just stand there and try to match them. You’ve got to be flexible. Trick them into wasting their force, or maneuver them into a spot where they can’t use their full strength without hurting themselves. Or, as my mother used to put it: it doesn’t matter how strong someone is, you can always trip them.”

<!-- paragraph:4466 para_id:00001172 style:Normal -->
Maya tilted her head, a flicker of curiosity behind the ice. “Your mother died when you were young, didn’t she?”

<!-- paragraph:4467 para_id:00001173 style:Normal -->
The words hit like a jab to the ribs. Davian scowled and glanced around the courtyard. “Has literally every person here read my file?”

<!-- paragraph:4468 para_id:00001174 style:Normal -->
Erynn, still leaning casually against the wall, was suddenly very interested in the ceiling.

<!-- paragraph:4469 para_id:00001175 style:Normal -->
Davian sighed, rubbing the back of his neck. “You can learn a lot when you’re young. And if you learn it then, it’s easier for it to become second nature.”

<!-- paragraph:4470 para_id:00001176 style:Normal -->
Maya’s posture shifted, some of the stiffness easing. “You know, that’s how magic training works for us too. The conventional wisdom is that if you don’t start basic visualization and processing exercises by the age of five, you’ll never reach your full potential.”

<!-- paragraph:4471 para_id:00001177 style:Normal -->
Davian’s thoughts flicked back, unbidden, to when he was five. By then he already knew how to lift bread without being caught, how to run when a deal soured. Survival lessons, not schooling. And then Kelyra had bargained for the Ink Boon that vaulted the family into the Concord’s leadership, and everything changed.

<!-- paragraph:4472 para_id:00001178 style:Normal -->
He glanced at Maya, and without meaning to, imagined her at the same age—Niall’s shadow looming over her, demanding more, pushing harder. A little girl raised under the weight of a hollow man whose grip on the city was nothing but clenched lightning. Training day after day with the certainty that her power would be honed for killing. Growing up in rooms where no one could truly understand her strain.

<!-- paragraph:4473 para_id:00001179 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat the mask she wore now fell away in his mind’s eye, and he saw the shape of her life clear: isolation, expectation, and the cruel certainty that she would be both weapon and shield for a family already fractured. And then—thrown into the political arena, her Presentation a spark that drew assassins to her door and soldiers to her walls.

<!-- paragraph:4474 para_id:0000117A style:Normal -->
The thought made his stomach twist. Because somewhere in that faceless storm of enemies, he was one of them. He was part of what pressed her mask tighter against her skin. And damn him, but that only made her look more beautiful—like something refined in fire, surviving what should have broken her.

<!-- paragraph:4475 para_id:0000117B style:Normal -->
He forced everything else away—the mission, the Heart, the gnawing hook of the Ink Boon—shoved it all into a box in the back of his mind and slammed the lid shut. He needed to be here, fully here, with her. Even as every lesson of his upbringing screamed that this was weakness, that he was only digging himself deeper, he couldn’t turn from it.

<!-- paragraph:4476 para_id:0000117C style:Normal -->
Maya looked up at him, eyebrows raised. “So,” she said evenly, “are we going to actually do anything?”

<!-- paragraph:4477 para_id:0000117D style:Normal -->
For once, his mouth didn’t twist into a sneer or a mask. The smile he gave her almost felt genuine.

<!-- paragraph:4478 para_id:0000117E style:Normal -->
“All right,” Davian said, forcing steadiness into his voice. “Normally, you’d start with the very beginning—basic forms, footwork, all that—but something tells me we don’t have years to shape you into a street fighter. So let me show you a few moves you can practice. They’ll be especially useful because your enemies won’t expect you to do anything like this. And that surprise is exactly what you want.”

<!-- paragraph:4479 para_id:0000117F style:Normal -->
He reached for her forearms, but the instant his skin brushed hers he recoiled, pulling back like he’d touched a hot stove—except the shock was ice, her Shroud ringing cold across her skin.

<!-- paragraph:4480 para_id:00001180 style:Normal -->
“Sorry,” Maya said quickly. “It’s a reflex.”

<!-- paragraph:4481 para_id:00001181 style:Normal -->
He rubbed his hand, trying to bring the warmth back. “You know you’re going to have to trust me, at least a little, if you want this to be useful.”

<!-- paragraph:4482 para_id:00001182 style:Normal -->
Her eyes held his for a breath. Then the shimmer of her Shroud thinned, faded, until it was almost gone. When he reached again, his fingers passed through a trace of coolness and into the warmth of her bare hands.

<!-- paragraph:4483 para_id:00001183 style:Normal -->
Behind them, Harvick cleared his throat. “Wait a minute—are you about to demonstrate any of the stuff you used on me? Do I need to fetch the healer?”

<!-- paragraph:4484 para_id:00001184 style:Normal -->
Maya’s brows pinched in confusion. “Why would we need the healer?”

<!-- paragraph:4485 para_id:00001185 style:Normal -->
Davian shot Harvick a glare. “Okay, so I wasn’t going to tell you this, but it’s completely possible to demonstrate these techniques safely.”

<!-- paragraph:4486 para_id:00001186 style:Normal -->
Harvick blinked. “So you dislocated my shoulder for no reason?”

<!-- paragraph:4487 para_id:00001187 style:Normal -->
Davian shrugged. “I was mad. And we both know you cheated. Anyway—no time to get into it.” He turned back to Maya, ignoring Harvick’s disgruntled muttering.

<!-- paragraph:4488 para_id:00001188 style:Normal -->
He stepped closer, lifting her arm gently into position. “First lesson: how to redirect force. If someone grabs you here—” he clasped her wrist lightly, guiding her elbow inward—“instead of trying to pull back, you rotate around their pressure.” He demonstrated, drawing her hand across her body. His other hand slid to her shoulder, nudging her weight into a pivot.

<!-- paragraph:4489 para_id:00001189 style:Normal -->
The movements were simple, but every point of contact buzzed. Her forearm beneath his grip was warm now, almost delicate, and when he corrected her stance his palm brushed the line of her waist. The closeness made his pulse stumble; he told himself it was just the mechanics, the need for precision—but his body betrayed him, heat sparking low in his chest. He forced his expression flat, his hands careful, almost tender, as he guided her through the motions.

<!-- paragraph:4490 para_id:0000118A style:Normal -->
“See?” he said, voice lower than he intended. “It doesn’t matter how strong they are. You make them trip over their own weight.”

<!-- paragraph:4491 para_id:0000118B style:Normal -->
“Here’s another one,” Davian said, forcing his voice steady. He caught her wrist gently, turning it outward and down. His body followed, stepping into her blind spot as he guided her arm behind her back. A twist of pressure—not enough to hurt, just enough to show control—put her off balance and left him standing close behind her, her arm pinned between them.

<!-- paragraph:4492 para_id:0000118C style:Normal -->
It was a clean, efficient restraint, exactly what he meant to show her. But the position—the nearness of her, the faint catch of her breath—was something else entirely. He released her quickly, stepping back. His own breathing felt heavier than it should have from a simple drill.

<!-- paragraph:4493 para_id:0000118D style:Normal -->
“Okay,” he muttered, dragging a hand down his face. “Can we take a break?”

<!-- paragraph:4494 para_id:0000118E style:Normal -->
Maya turned, and to his irritation she laughed. Not the brittle, courtly laugh he’d heard in meetings, but a genuine one. “You know better than that. We’ll have to drill this until it’s second nature.”

<!-- paragraph:4495 para_id:0000118F style:Normal -->
Davian groaned inwardly, already envisioning the hours of close contact ahead—torture of the most maddening kind.

<!-- paragraph:4496 para_id:00001190 style:Normal -->
They moved through drills until sweat dampened the mats, the air carrying the faint salt of exertion. Harvick and Erynn lingered on the edge of the courtyard, speaking in low voices that fooled no one—every so often their eyes flicked back, caught by the rhythm of movement.

<!-- paragraph:4497 para_id:00001191 style:Normal -->
Maya learned fast. Faster than Davian had expected. She picked up the wrist pivots and balance breaks quickly, her movements crisp and controlled, not tentative. He shouldn’t have been surprised—someone raised the way she had would only ever learn to excel—but the ease still made something tighten in his chest.

<!-- paragraph:4498 para_id:00001192 style:Normal -->
“Okay,” she said at last, straightening and brushing a strand of hair from her face. “I get that you want me to learn the basics. But I need something a little more aggressive.”

<!-- paragraph:4499 para_id:00001193 style:Normal -->
Davian couldn’t help the smile tugging at his mouth; he knew the feeling too well. “All right. But maybe I should demonstrate on Harvick.”

<!-- paragraph:4500 para_id:00001194 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s head shook so fast it was almost comical. “Not a chance.”

<!-- paragraph:4501 para_id:00001195 style:Normal -->
Maya lifted her chin. “It’s fine. I can control my Shroud enough to keep it from hurting me—without killing you.”

<!-- paragraph:4502 para_id:00001196 style:Normal -->
The image of that assassin’s body flashed in Davian’s head, frozen solid in an instant. He hesitated, every instinct telling him to be cautious.

<!-- paragraph:4503 para_id:00001197 style:Normal -->
Then she stepped in close again, voice low. “I thought we agreed to trust each other.”

<!-- paragraph:4504 para_id:00001198 style:Normal -->
He swallowed, nodding. “Okay. But really—be careful.”

<!-- paragraph:4505 para_id:00001199 style:Normal -->
She rolled her eyes; they both knew he was the one in far more danger here.

<!-- paragraph:4506 para_id:0000119A style:Normal -->
Davian took a steadying breath, forcing his voice to stay calm. “All right. This one’s simple. I’ll show you how to take someone off their feet when retreat isn’t an option. Push or pull up top to break their balance, then hook behind the leg. Gravity does the rest. Don’t just let them fall—drive them down.”

<!-- paragraph:4507 para_id:0000119B style:Normal -->
Maya gave a sharp nod, already stepping in close, her stance square. He placed his hand against her shoulder, guiding her weight just enough to feel the line of tension in her frame. His other hand brushed her arm, a light contact that set the angle. “Now—” He shifted his foot behind her ankle and swept.

<!-- paragraph:4508 para_id:0000119C style:Normal -->
She went down cleanly, body jolting as the mat absorbed the impact. The shimmer of her Shroud flared for a heartbeat, the cold biting against his skin where they touched. But that was all—no blazing defense, no surge of power. He tried not to think about what that meant, how weak his hard-won fighting ability was against the strength of a Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:4509 para_id:0000119D style:Normal -->
Instead he focused on the tangle they’d landed in. His grip on her arm had pulled him down with her, leaving them both horizontal, bodies pressed awkwardly together on the mat. Her hair brushed his jaw, her breath quick against his neck, the faint cold of her Shroud mixing with the warmth of skin underneath.

<!-- paragraph:4510 para_id:0000119E style:Normal -->
For a moment he didn’t move. The drill was supposed to be clinical, efficient—but the closeness carried a charge that had nothing to do with technique. He forced himself to breathe, heart hammering louder than it should have for a simple throw.

<!-- paragraph:4511 para_id:0000119F style:Normal -->
Davian rolled off her awkwardly, forcing space between them. End this. Now. He sat up, brushing sweat from his brow. “Okay, well… now you kind of know the basics. I don’t have a Shroud, and Harvick doesn’t want to be practiced on, so…”

<!-- paragraph:4512 para_id:000011A0 style:Normal -->
Beside him, Maya stayed on the mat, her voice quiet, almost to herself. “I can do this.”

<!-- paragraph:4513 para_id:000011A1 style:Normal -->
He turned his head toward her.

<!-- paragraph:4514 para_id:000011A2 style:Normal -->
“I can use this with my magic,” she continued, still lying down, gaze fixed somewhere far past the ceiling. “Take them by surprise and end them. Kill those Archeon magi.” The words were flat, not triumphant.

<!-- paragraph:4515 para_id:000011A3 style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” Davian said after a beat. “Isn’t that the point? They tried to kill you, so you kill them first. You said before—that’s basically your job.”

<!-- paragraph:4516 para_id:000011A4 style:Normal -->
Maya sat up slowly, turning to look at him. Her eyes were sharp, but her voice trembled at the edges. “They hate me. Because of something I can’t control. They hate me, and now I have to kill them.”

<!-- paragraph:4517 para_id:000011A5 style:Normal -->
She pushed to her feet, taking a step away.

<!-- paragraph:4518 para_id:000011A6 style:Normal -->
Davian rose too, following without meaning to. “They hate you because you’re trying to do something good, right? Make the city better, help people?”

<!-- paragraph:4519 para_id:000011A7 style:Normal -->
She turned back, her expression tight with pain. “But how do I even know I’m right?”

<!-- paragraph:4520 para_id:000011A8 style:Normal -->
“They’re wrong,” Davian said instantly, the words pulled out of him without thought. “The High Clades are all evil. That’s why they hate you.”

<!-- paragraph:4521 para_id:000011A9 style:Normal -->
Maya’s face hardened. “Ohhh, there it is again—the ‘we’re all evil’ thing. I’m one of these High Clades! Maybe they have a good reason to hate me. Maybe I’m evil too?” She took a step closer, eyes flashing. “You can’t just be that person who puts everyone into a box and writes them off as evil. You can’t define yourself only by what you hate.”

<!-- paragraph:4522 para_id:000011AA style:Normal -->
Davian felt the sting of it, but he could tell—beneath the anger, beneath the words—there was something else weighing on her, something she hadn’t said yet.

<!-- paragraph:4523 para_id:000011AB style:Normal -->
“Look,” Davian said quickly, hands spreading as if he could catch the words he’d just thrown out. “I didn’t mean that. I just—” He shook his head, forcing his voice steady. “I just know that if you do the right thing, some people are going to hate you. That’s how it works.”

<!-- paragraph:4524 para_id:000011AC style:Normal -->
Her gaze stayed fixed on him, guarded, waiting.

<!-- paragraph:4525 para_id:000011AD style:Normal -->
“I think you’re someone who does the right thing,” he said. The words came out rougher than he meant, pulled straight from the thought of her standing against Caius, fire in her eyes, saving him when she hadn’t even known who he was. Of her declaring that the High Clades’ right to rule meant nothing if they didn’t protect the innocent.

<!-- paragraph:4526 para_id:000011AE style:Normal -->
“If someone reacts badly to that,” he finished, meeting her eyes, “that’s on them. Not on you.”

<!-- paragraph:4527 para_id:000011AF style:Normal -->
Maya’s shoulders sagged slightly, the edge in her voice giving way to weariness. “I wish I shared your confidence about what the right thing to do even is.”

<!-- paragraph:4528 para_id:000011B0 style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head. “I don’t know what the right thing is most of the time.” His mouth twisted, but he pushed on. “But I know you’re trying to care about the right things. And it’s better to hold to that and deal with people hating you, than to abandon what you care about just to keep them happy.”

<!-- paragraph:4529 para_id:000011B1 style:Normal -->
Maya let out a long breath, halfway between a sigh and a laugh. “You and Aldira must be reading the same books.”

<!-- paragraph:4530 para_id:000011B2 style:Normal -->
From the sidelines, Harvick piped up. “Hey, actually I taught him that like an hour ago.”

<!-- paragraph:4531 para_id:000011B3 style:Normal -->
Maya brushed her palms together as though sealing the end of the session. “Great. I’m glad we all agree on… whatever this is. If we’re not going to keep practicing, then we should be eating—I’m starving.”

<!-- paragraph:4532 para_id:000011B4 style:Normal -->
The sun had lowered toward the courtyard walls, catching her in its glow. It traced every line of her face, highlighting the sheen of sweat under the faint shimmer of her Shroud, catching in strands of hair that had worked loose. Her complexion was flushed from the effort, and when her eyes found his again, she held them expectantly.

<!-- paragraph:4533 para_id:000011B5 style:Normal -->
Davian blinked, unsure what she wanted.

<!-- paragraph:4534 para_id:000011B6 style:Normal -->
“So,” she said, “show me what you eat after doing something like this. I’m not going to follow through only halfway.”

<!-- paragraph:4535 para_id:000011B7 style:Normal -->
He cleared his throat. “I thought you said I smelled like an overcrowded barracks after exercising.”

<!-- paragraph:4536 para_id:000011B8 style:Normal -->
Her gaze swept him up and down with deliberate coolness. “Right. First, go bathe. Then we’ll eat. I’ll meet you at the mess hall.”

<!-- paragraph:4537 para_id:000011B9 style:Normal -->
With that, she turned and strode toward the archway, her Shroud flickering faintly around her like a second skin.

<!-- paragraph:4538 para_id:000011BA style:Normal -->
Harvick sidled up beside Davian and clapped him on the shoulder. “Sounds like a date.”

<!-- paragraph:4539 para_id:000011BB style:Normal -->
This time Harvick wasn’t fast enough—Davian’s elbow caught him square in the ribs.

<!-- paragraph:4543 para_id:000011BF style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 25

<!-- paragraph:4544 para_id:000011C0 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 25

<!-- paragraph:4546 para_id:000011C2 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4547 para_id:000011C3 style:Normal -->
The corridor air was cooler than the training yard, but Maya’s skin still burned from exertion—and from something else she couldn’t quite classify. Her pulse hadn’t fully settled. Arrek had looked at her in a way that wasn’t deferential, or calculating, or fearful. He hadn’t been seeing the heir of a High Clade at all, just… her.

<!-- paragraph:4548 para_id:000011C4 style:Normal -->
It was a strange feeling—unfamiliar, disorienting, but not entirely unpleasant. Like stepping into a space she didn’t know existed and realizing she wanted to linger there a little longer, even if she didn’t understand why.

<!-- paragraph:4549 para_id:000011C5 style:Normal -->
“Wow,” Erynn said suddenly, breaking the silence. “That was really not subtle.”

<!-- paragraph:4550 para_id:000011C6 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, pulled out of her thoughts. “What are you talking about?”

<!-- paragraph:4551 para_id:000011C7 style:Normal -->
“You know,” Erynn drawled, “sometimes he seems a little dense, but I think even he’d notice you literally just asked to eat with him.”

<!-- paragraph:4552 para_id:000011C8 style:Normal -->
Maya shot her a look of pure annoyance, then fixed her eyes forward. “Diet is a key component of any training regimen. Everyone knows that.”

<!-- paragraph:4553 para_id:000011C9 style:Normal -->
Erynn smirked. “You’ve decided not to look at me, but I want you to know I’m rolling my eyes.”

<!-- paragraph:4554 para_id:000011CA style:Normal -->
“What are you implying?”

<!-- paragraph:4555 para_id:000011CB style:Normal -->
Erynn adopted an exaggeratedly prim tone. “What are you implying? Clearly I’m implying you want to spend more time with this guy.”

<!-- paragraph:4556 para_id:000011CC style:Normal -->
“Excuse me,” Maya said, voice going crisp, “but I have to spend time with him if I want to not get murdered by those Archeon thugs. Anyway, he clearly hates every second of it—he could barely get out of there fast enough after showing me a few basic things.”

<!-- paragraph:4557 para_id:000011CD style:Normal -->
“Uh-huh.” Erynn folded her arms. “Then what was all that—” she dropped her voice into an overdone gravelly imitation of Arrek—“‘I know you care about the right things.’”

<!-- paragraph:4558 para_id:000011CE style:Normal -->
Maya spun around, hands thrown up. “I don’t know! Maybe he’s just trying to get on my good side so I promote him again!”

<!-- paragraph:4559 para_id:000011CF style:Normal -->
Erynn laughed, incredulous. “Oh, right. Because he’s been trying so hard to get on your good side. Anyway, that’s basically the first time I’ve ever seen him smile—and he was smiling riiiiiight at you.”

<!-- paragraph:4560 para_id:000011D0 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth, but nothing came out. The image flashed behind her eyes—that smile, rare and unexpected, breaking across his face like sunrise. It had filled her with a strange, fragile warmth, the kind that made it easy to imagine being someone else, someone freer. She felt the heat rise in her cheeks again, traitorous.

<!-- paragraph:4561 para_id:000011D1 style:Normal -->
“Erynn,” she hissed suddenly, “be quiet.”

<!-- paragraph:4562 para_id:000011D2 style:Normal -->
Her two guards, several paces behind, were already pretending to be absorbed by something outside the windows. Very absorbed.

<!-- paragraph:4563 para_id:000011D3 style:Normal -->
By the time they reached her rooms, Maya’s flush still hadn’t faded. Erynn dismissed the guards to their usual posts outside and started helping her bathe and change. Maya spent longer than usual deciding what to wear, which immediately drew commentary.

<!-- paragraph:4564 para_id:000011D4 style:Normal -->
“Trying to impress the help, my lady?”

<!-- paragraph:4565 para_id:000011D5 style:Normal -->
Maya shot her a look, which only made Erynn grin wider. In the end she settled on a sleeveless tunic and fitted leggings—her version of casual, which meant the fabric was finer than most people’s formal wear. Still unsatisfied, she made Erynn redo her hair three times before finally declaring it acceptable.

<!-- paragraph:4566 para_id:000011D6 style:Normal -->
Erynn stepped back, eyeing her reflection critically, then dropped into another imitation of Arrek: “I think you look good in anything, Maya… or maybe nothing.”

<!-- paragraph:4567 para_id:000011D7 style:Normal -->
“Erynn,” Maya warned, but it lacked heat.

<!-- paragraph:4568 para_id:000011D8 style:Normal -->
Normally she would have eaten in the private dining room within the central spire—sometimes with Niall, more often alone, or with Selwin when he wasn’t sulking. Stepping into the main hall felt like entering a different world. The space buzzed with conversation and clatter until her arrival, when the noise cut off almost instantly. Heads turned. Workers froze mid-bite. The bowing and hurried murmurs of Lady Maya multiplied until the entire room seemed to be tripping over itself.

<!-- paragraph:4569 para_id:000011D9 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat Maya wanted to retreat. Then she straightened, smoothed her expression into calm control, and said lightly, “Please, continue. I’m just here to eat.” She gave the words an even, practiced cadence—precisely the tone she used when she wanted to make something strange sound entirely ordinary.

<!-- paragraph:4570 para_id:000011DA style:Normal -->
It worked, mostly. The noise resumed, but a visible bubble of space formed around her and Erynn like an invisible cordon.

<!-- paragraph:4571 para_id:000011DB style:Normal -->
Behind her, a familiar voice: “So, I’m not really sure what you want me to do here.”

<!-- paragraph:4572 para_id:000011DC style:Normal -->
She turned. Arrek stood near the entrance, hair still damp from bathing, sleeves pushed back, looking faintly bemused. The smile he gave her was smaller than before but still startling in its warmth, an expression she wasn’t used to seeing on his face.

<!-- paragraph:4573 para_id:000011DD style:Normal -->
She swallowed before answering, her composure unbroken. “I defer to your expertise in post-training diet regimen.”

<!-- paragraph:4574 para_id:000011DE style:Normal -->
He blinked. “Okay. I guess… follow me?”

<!-- paragraph:4575 para_id:000011DF style:Normal -->
It was only then, watching him move toward the serving tables, that she realized everyone was getting their own food. She’d never done that before. Her armored guards wedged in close, making movement awkward.

<!-- paragraph:4576 para_id:000011E0 style:Normal -->
“It’s all right,” she told them, turning with deliberate poise. “You can wait outside—with Erynn.”

<!-- paragraph:4577 para_id:000011E1 style:Normal -->
“My lady, that’s—”

<!-- paragraph:4578 para_id:000011E2 style:Normal -->
“I just fought off two High Clade magi on my own,” she interrupted, crisp. “What do you think is going to happen in a dining room?”

<!-- paragraph:4579 para_id:000011E3 style:Normal -->
That silenced them. The guards mumbled apologies and withdrew. When Maya turned back, the hall had quieted again. Dozens of eyes were on her, and for once she didn’t know whether she wanted to laugh or vanish.

<!-- paragraph:4580 para_id:000011E4 style:Normal -->
That silenced them. The guards mumbled apologies and withdrew. When Maya turned back, the hall had quieted again. Dozens of eyes tracked her every move.

<!-- paragraph:4581 para_id:000011E5 style:Normal -->
Of course they did. Everyone was always watching her—exhaustingly so. Most of them had probably only heard fragments of what had actually happened with the Archeon attack, enough to inflate the story in every possible direction. She could already imagine the rumors: that she’d leveled half the wall, that she’d gone mad, that she’d single-handedly obliterated an army.

<!-- paragraph:4582 para_id:000011E6 style:Normal -->
Her jaw tightened. You sounded imperious, her inner voice whispered. Too sharp. You should’ve smiled more. Your tone was clipped, your posture stiff—

<!-- paragraph:4583 para_id:000011E7 style:Normal -->
She shut the thought off with a quiet sigh and gestured for Arrek to keep walking. He obeyed easily, moving toward the food line. The workers parted around them like a tide, clearing space so quickly it was almost comical. Conversation resumed in fragments—spoons clinking, a laugh here or there—but the air still felt heavy, like the whole room was holding its breath.

<!-- paragraph:4584 para_id:000011E8 style:Normal -->
Arrek didn’t seem to notice. He just reached for a plate, perfectly calm, as though this were the most natural thing in the world. Maya mimicked him, doing her best to appear equally composed. It wasn’t difficult—holding a plate wasn’t difficult—but her mind still managed to imagine dropping it, scattering food everywhere, being the punch line of a story that would spread through the Citadel by nightfall.

<!-- paragraph:4585 para_id:000011E9 style:Normal -->
Maya trailed after him through the line, watching closely to see what he chose. When Arrek reached the baskets of bread, he stacked roll after roll onto his plate with casual confidence. She hesitated, then matched him—one, two, three, five—because apparently this was what one ate after training. Maybe it was some tradition, an energy-replenishment technique or something.

<!-- paragraph:4586 para_id:000011EA style:Normal -->
“Do you only eat bread after exercise?” she asked.

<!-- paragraph:4587 para_id:000011EB style:Normal -->
Arrek gave her an odd look and, without a word, began stacking several large slabs of meat onto his plate. It seemed physically impossible that the pile wouldn’t topple over.

<!-- paragraph:4588 para_id:000011EC style:Normal -->
Maya blinked at him, lowering her voice. “Are you joking? Is this some kind of joke? You’re going to eat all that?”

<!-- paragraph:4589 para_id:000011ED style:Normal -->
He leaned slightly toward her, voice low enough that only she could hear. “Of course. I’m starving because someone made me skip my mid-afternoon meal so she could learn how to hit people.”

<!-- paragraph:4590 para_id:000011EE style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “Mid-afternoon meal isn’t even a thing,” she whispered back.

<!-- paragraph:4591 para_id:000011EF style:Normal -->
He sighed—long-suffering, exaggerated—and turned toward the tables.

<!-- paragraph:4592 para_id:000011F0 style:Normal -->
She watched his back with narrowed eyes as he walked away. After a moment she added a more reasonable amount of meat to her own plate, determined to at least look like she knew what she was doing.

<!-- paragraph:4593 para_id:000011F1 style:Normal -->
Arrek sat down at a long table already half-occupied by a few zänwrights. They were a mismatched trio: a young woman, small and perpetually animated, her copper hair escaping every attempt at containment; a man, broad-shouldered and cautious, eyes half-lidded as if measuring every word before releasing it; and an older woman, lean and sharp-featured, the quiet sort who radiated competence.

<!-- paragraph:4594 para_id:000011F2 style:Normal -->
Maya settled across from Arrek, careful with the way she placed her plate.

<!-- paragraph:4595 para_id:000011F3 style:Normal -->
“I’d introduce you,” he said dryly, “but I’m guessing they already know who you are.”

<!-- paragraph:4596 para_id:000011F4 style:Normal -->
That was… accurate. All three were staring at her, the frozen politeness of people who had no idea what to do with the person suddenly sitting in their midst.

<!-- paragraph:4597 para_id:000011F5 style:Normal -->
Arrek gestured anyway. “Tanelle, Kolvar, Jessa—Lady Maya Rezin.”

<!-- paragraph:4598 para_id:000011F6 style:Normal -->
She inclined her head, automatic grace slipping into place. She already knew their names, of course—she’d signed their contracts—but she’d never actually attached faces to them.

<!-- paragraph:4599 para_id:000011F7 style:Normal -->
When none of them spoke, she took charge in the only way she knew how. “Please, continue whatever you were discussing before I arrived.”

<!-- paragraph:4600 para_id:000011F8 style:Normal -->
A faint pause. She felt heat rise at the base of her throat. That sounded strange, didn’t it? She looked down at her plate, deciding that starting with bread was safest.

<!-- paragraph:4601 para_id:000011F9 style:Normal -->
“So, uh,” Kolvar ventured after a beat, “how’s… work?”

<!-- paragraph:4602 para_id:000011FA style:Normal -->
Maya picked up a roll, aware of how the coolness followed her fingers where the Shroud met the surface of her skin. When she lifted it toward her mouth, the faint chill spread—her Shroud’s way of sensing the edge between her and the world.

<!-- paragraph:4603 para_id:000011FB style:Normal -->
Across the table, Tanelle was still staring, a half-eaten roll drooping precariously from her hand. The moment their eyes met, Tanelle jolted, swallowed hastily, and burst out, “Sorry! I’m so sorry, my lady—it’s just, I never thought I’d see a High Clade magus up close. This is so fascinating! How do you even eat with a Shroud?”

<!-- paragraph:4604 para_id:000011FC style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, caught off guard by the question. “Well,” she said after a moment, “when I was younger it was actually pretty difficult. As soon as I could manifest my Shroud, I was drilled to keep it up constantly until it became unconscious—like learning to walk a certain way, or to keep your shoulders straight. But it was hard to regulate its strength, so I would sometimes… accidentally freeze something I was trying to eat.”

<!-- paragraph:4605 para_id:000011FD style:Normal -->
Tanelle’s eyes widened. Kolvar gave a low whistle before remembering himself.

<!-- paragraph:4606 para_id:000011FE style:Normal -->
“That was more than a decade ago,” Maya added quickly, smoothing her tone back into control. “Now it’s second nature. I keep it at a low enough intensity not to freeze anything unless I want to.”

<!-- paragraph:4607 para_id:000011FF style:Normal -->
The word want lingered a beat too long. She saw them all shift almost imperceptibly—Tanelle setting her roll down, Kolvar leaning back, Jessa folding her hands in her lap. A tiny, collective recalibration of space. They were remembering, no doubt, what she’d done to the assassin: how fast it had happened, how final.

<!-- paragraph:4608 para_id:00001200 style:Normal -->
Maya lowered her eyes to her plate again, pretending not to notice, though the hollow in her chest told her she had.

<!-- paragraph:4609 para_id:00001201 style:Normal -->
She could feel Arrek’s eyes on her again. When she finally looked up, he wasn’t smirking or skeptical—just watching her with a faint crease between his brows.

<!-- paragraph:4610 para_id:00001202 style:Normal -->
“That sounds like a really terrible thing to deal with when you’re that young,” he said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:4611 para_id:00001203 style:Normal -->
Maya hadn’t expected sympathy. It caught her off guard more than the question itself. For a heartbeat she almost let the mask slip, almost let herself say something true—but she couldn’t. Not here. Not in front of so many eyes. Still, the memory came unbidden: crying in the training yard, her Shroud freezing her tears against her cheeks while Niall told her to stand straighter, to control herself.

<!-- paragraph:4612 para_id:00001204 style:Normal -->
The clank of armor behind her broke the spell. Relief and irritation twisted together in her chest. Of course. Her guards, probably deciding she’d had enough exposure to the common world. She turned, ready to send them away—

<!-- paragraph:4613 para_id:00001205 style:Normal -->
—and froze.

<!-- paragraph:4614 para_id:00001206 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t her guards this time. Two different soldiers were approaching, flanking a boy with pale hair and bright, restless eyes.

<!-- paragraph:4615 para_id:00001207 style:Normal -->
“Selwin?” she breathed.

<!-- paragraph:4616 para_id:00001208 style:Normal -->
He beamed and trotted forward, completely unbothered by the stares rippling through the hall, and dropped onto the bench beside her with a loud thunk. His guards positioned themselves behind him, armor rasping, trying not to meet her eye.

<!-- paragraph:4617 para_id:00001209 style:Normal -->
Maya leaned closer, voice low and sharp. “You’re not really supposed to be out here.”

<!-- paragraph:4618 para_id:0000120A style:Normal -->
“Father said I should spend time with you,” Selwin announced cheerfully, loud enough for half the table to hear. “He had some things to do, so we went to look for you, and Harvick saw us and said you’d be here! So we’re just doing what Father wanted.”

<!-- paragraph:4619 para_id:0000120B style:Normal -->
Maya closed her eyes for a moment. Of course.

<!-- paragraph:4620 para_id:0000120C style:Normal -->
She shot a glare toward his guards, but they were staring determinedly outward, pretending to scan the room. She couldn’t blame them. They had to be terrified of what might happen if something set Selwin off again—and, if she was honest, so was she. Especially here, surrounded by so many people, with her brother smiling like the chaos that followed him didn’t exist.

<!-- paragraph:4621 para_id:0000120D style:Normal -->
Selwin picked up a roll from her plate and turned it over in his hands. “I’m hungry,” he announced. “When will they bring my food out?”

<!-- paragraph:4622 para_id:0000120E style:Normal -->
Arrek smiled at him. “Hey, this is one of those places where we get our own food. But if that sounds fun, I can show you how.” He shot Maya a glance that was almost mischievous. “Don’t worry, your sister didn’t know how it worked out here either.”

<!-- paragraph:4623 para_id:0000120F style:Normal -->
Maya gave him a look that could have frozen steel, but Selwin was already laughing and bounding to his feet. His enthusiasm rippled outward; even the zänwrights couldn’t help smiling a little as Arrek and Selwin walked towards the food tables. Then they seemed to register what Arrek had said—calling her Selwin’s sister—and their expressions shifted to shock. Seeing an un-Presented High Clade child out in public was unheard of. Seeing one so close was worse. Dangerous, even.

<!-- paragraph:4624 para_id:00001210 style:Normal -->
Maya turned toward them, keeping her tone composed. “My apologies. I need someone to carry a message for me.”

<!-- paragraph:4625 para_id:00001211 style:Normal -->
They almost fell over each other volunteering. Tanelle managed to speak first, practically glowing with excitement.

<!-- paragraph:4626 para_id:00001212 style:Normal -->
“Tanelle,” Maya said, “go to my guards at the entrance and tell them to find Harvick. Have him come up with a reason for my brother and me to leave the dining hall and return to the spire. Something that seems natural.”

<!-- paragraph:4627 para_id:00001213 style:Normal -->
Tanelle nodded so vigorously her hair came loose. She looked ready to sprint for the door, then realized what that would look like and forced herself into an unnaturally fast walk instead.

<!-- paragraph:4628 para_id:00001214 style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled slowly, turning her attention back to Arrek and Selwin. Selwin was at the food line now, stacking slices of meat high enough to defy architectural reason. Arrek stood beside him, clearly encouraging it.

<!-- paragraph:4629 para_id:00001215 style:Normal -->
Maya pressed her fingers against her temple. Of course he is.

<!-- paragraph:4630 para_id:00001216 style:Normal -->
By the time Arrek and Selwin came back, Tanelle was already seated again, trying very hard to look casual and failing spectacularly.

<!-- paragraph:4631 para_id:00001217 style:Normal -->
Selwin could barely see over the mound of bread and meat on his own plate.
