<!-- paragraph:4632 para_id:00001218 style:Normal -->
“This is great!” he said brightly. “Arrek taught me that here I can take as much bread and meat as I want—and if there’s any left over, he can take care of it for me!”

<!-- paragraph:4633 para_id:00001219 style:Normal -->
Arrek winced and gave Maya a sheepish, guilty smile that somehow managed to be both apologetic and proud of himself at once.

<!-- paragraph:4634 para_id:0000121A style:Normal -->
Maya stared at the plate, then at Arrek, then at the faintly horrified expressions of the zänwrights. “How generous of you,” she said dryly.

<!-- paragraph:4635 para_id:0000121B style:Normal -->
Selwin was happily demolishing a roll roughly the size of his fist when his attention seemed to snag on the last thing anyone wanted him to bring up. “So… was Archeon really here?” he asked, voice bright. “They said you beat them. That’s good, right? …But aren’t they our allies? I thought all the High Clades were allies.”

<!-- paragraph:4636 para_id:0000121C style:Normal -->
Maya’s throat went tight. For a heartbeat she was back on the wall—shimmering gauntlets, the impossible acceleration, that fist arcing toward her face hard enough to break bone through a Shroud. She smelled scorched stone. She heard the crack of someone’s spine hitting the parapet.

<!-- paragraph:4637 para_id:0000121D style:Normal -->
She forced it down. “This isn’t good mealtime conversation,” she said, aiming for light but landing closer to brittle. “And sometimes things are more complicated than ‘allies’ or ‘enemies,’ Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:4638 para_id:0000121E style:Normal -->
She thought she’d smoothed it over, but when she glanced up she caught Arrek looking at her—brows faintly drawn, like he’d seen the flash of something he wasn’t supposed to see. She looked away, annoyed with herself.

<!-- paragraph:4639 para_id:0000121F style:Normal -->
Then a sharp crash split the room.

<!-- paragraph:4640 para_id:00001220 style:Normal -->
The sound jolted through her like an electric strike. Her Shroud flared on instinct—cold rippling over her skin—and her mind snapped automatically into battle processing. The world thickened, slowed. Voices stretched. Movement became lines and vectors.

<!-- paragraph:4641 para_id:00001221 style:Normal -->
Arrek was already moving. In her slowed perception she saw him twist off the bench in a clean pivot, one hand going behind his back toward something tucked into his waistband.

<!-- paragraph:4642 para_id:00001222 style:Normal -->
Her eyes tracked the source: a worker halfway across the hall, slipping on spilled soup, tray flying. Pottery spun in the air, then shattered across the floor in a spray of fragments.

<!-- paragraph:4643 para_id:00001223 style:Normal -->
No threat. Just clumsiness.

<!-- paragraph:4644 para_id:00001224 style:Normal -->
She began to ease off, willing her senses to release, Shroud thinning back to a soft halo. As she let go, time seemed to speed up again. Arrek landed in front of her and Selwin, shoulders squared toward the hall, body between them and the broken crockery, one hand out and low, holding something dark and matte he clearly meant to use.

<!-- paragraph:4645 para_id:00001225 style:Normal -->
He looked, for one ridiculous second, like he was warding off an assassin.

<!-- paragraph:4646 para_id:00001226 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat nobody moved. Even the background noise of the hall had dropped away, everyone processing what had just happened several steps behind her.

<!-- paragraph:4647 para_id:00001227 style:Normal -->
Then Tanelle blurted, “What on earth are you doing?” She gestured at the object in Arrek’s hand. “Is that your weird zan chisel? Do you always carry that around? Why? What were you going to do with it?”

<!-- paragraph:4648 para_id:00001228 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s face colored; he slid the small dark tool back into his belt with studied nonchalance. “We were talking about Archeon,” he muttered. “It made me jumpy.”

<!-- paragraph:4649 para_id:00001229 style:Normal -->
Maya found her voice again. “And what were you planning to do, exactly? Stab them with a chisel?”

<!-- paragraph:4650 para_id:0000122A style:Normal -->
That only made him redder. “It’s all I have, all right? You never know—”

<!-- paragraph:4651 para_id:0000122B style:Normal -->
Before he could dig the hole deeper, a man in a chef’s apron rushed up, bowing so low his forehead nearly brushed the floor. “My lady, please forgive us! For this to happen after you honor us with your presence—such shame!”

<!-- paragraph:4652 para_id:0000122C style:Normal -->
“It’s fine,” Maya said quickly, lifting a hand, but he’d already been joined by the poor worker who’d tripped. The man was pale and sweating, clutching his cap, bowing repeatedly.

<!-- paragraph:4653 para_id:0000122D style:Normal -->
“I’m so sorry, my lady! I should have—”

<!-- paragraph:4654 para_id:0000122E style:Normal -->
“It’s really no problem,” she said. “In fact, it’s more intrusive to have everyone apologizing for me.”

<!-- paragraph:4655 para_id:0000122F style:Normal -->
The man hesitated, still bent at the waist. “Then… I—ah—should I apologize for—” He trailed off, eyes wide, clearly trapped in the logic.

<!-- paragraph:4656 para_id:00001230 style:Normal -->
Maya winced inwardly. Perfect. Terrify the kitchen staff. Well done. She drew herself up, finding her polished tone again. “You’re dismissed,” she said smoothly. “Thank you for your service.”

<!-- paragraph:4657 para_id:00001231 style:Normal -->
That seemed to satisfy him; he backed away in relief.

<!-- paragraph:4658 para_id:00001232 style:Normal -->
Only then did she notice the rim of frost spreading from where her hands had braced on the table. The wood glittered with a thin sheen of ice. Jessa, pretending to adjust her sleeve, was covertly checking whether the surface had cracked.

<!-- paragraph:4659 para_id:00001233 style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach turned. At least no one was hurt. She sent a quick, silent thanks to the Creator that she’d managed to pull her Shroud back before it had lashed out at anyone nearby.

<!-- paragraph:4660 para_id:00001234 style:Normal -->
And then, mercifully, Harvick arrived—his voice cutting through the awkward quiet like a rescue rope.

<!-- paragraph:4661 para_id:00001235 style:Normal -->
“Hey, so someone a lot smarter than me has been taking a look at those Archeon gauntlets we pulled in—there’s some odd stuff going on with them. Thought you might want to see for yourself.”

<!-- paragraph:4662 para_id:00001236 style:Normal -->
Maya seized the lifeline. “Yes,” she said, standing. “That sounds… productive.” She turned to Selwin. “You should come too.”

<!-- paragraph:4663 para_id:00001237 style:Normal -->
Selwin frowned down at his plate. “But I still have so much food left.”

<!-- paragraph:4664 para_id:00001238 style:Normal -->
Before she could answer, the chef reappeared, bowing so low his apron brushed the floor. “If the young lord wishes, I can have it all wrapped and delivered to his chambers immediately!”

<!-- paragraph:4665 para_id:00001239 style:Normal -->
Selwin brightened at the title young lord, nodding enthusiastically. “Okay! But Arrek has to come too.”

<!-- paragraph:4666 para_id:0000123A style:Normal -->
Maya froze. So did Arrek.

<!-- paragraph:4667 para_id:0000123B style:Normal -->
“That’s not—” he started, but Selwin’s face was already falling, shoulders hunching in that telltale way that made every guard in the room tense.

<!-- paragraph:4668 para_id:0000123C style:Normal -->
Arrek looked helpless for half a heartbeat, then sighed. “Fine. I’ll come.”

<!-- paragraph:4669 para_id:0000123D style:Normal -->
Harvick set a brisk pace through the inner corridors of the Citadel, long strides echoing off stone. The others followed in a loose file—Maya a half-step behind him, Selwin darting ahead whenever something shiny caught his eye, and Arrek keeping a wary distance as though unsure whether he was being punished or conscripted.

<!-- paragraph:4670 para_id:0000123E style:Normal -->
They passed from the polished marble of the administrative wing into rougher hallways where the air smelled faintly of oil and hot metal. “This way,” Harvick said over his shoulder. “One of the zanwright supply bays. The foremen were kind enough to let us borrow it.”

<!-- paragraph:4671 para_id:0000123F style:Normal -->
When they reached the door, it was obvious that “borrowed” wasn’t quite the right word. Two armed guards stood outside. From inside, she could hear the faint clatter of tools.

<!-- paragraph:4672 para_id:00001240 style:Normal -->
Maya stared. “You’ve been busy.”

<!-- paragraph:4673 para_id:00001241 style:Normal -->
“Well, I try to avoid work when possible, but Aldira is really bad about taking no for an answer,” Harvick said. He gave the guards a nod; they opened the heavy door and stepped aside. The sharp scent of ozone and metal hit Maya immediately. The storage bay had been stripped of its normal clutter, the floor cleared and worktables pulled into a broad semicircle around a reinforced dais. At its center sat two battered metal gauntlets—sable under the white worklights, their surfaces webbed with faint cracks that pulsed like veins of dark glass.

<!-- paragraph:4674 para_id:00001242 style:Normal -->
A figure was already bent over them. Aldira, hunched in a posture that made Maya’s own back ache, was prodding at the gauntlets with thin, za-inscribed tools. Her long black-and-gray hair was tied back, and a pair of thick goggles magnified her eyes until she looked like some sort of predatory insect.

<!-- paragraph:4675 para_id:00001243 style:Normal -->
Without looking up she said, “If you’re going to hover, at least do it quietly.” Then she lifted her head, pushing the goggles to her forehead, and smirked. “Ah. Good, you’re here. It’s nice to have something to do that isn’t reading reports all day.”

<!-- paragraph:4676 para_id:00001244 style:Normal -->
Selwin edged up to the table, standing on his toes to see the gauntlets better, eyes bright with fascination. The faint hum of latent magic made his hair stand on end, but it didn’t seem to dampen his enthusiasm in the slightest.

<!-- paragraph:4677 para_id:00001245 style:Normal -->
“Don’t touch them,” Maya said, sharper than she meant to. He froze mid-motion, looking up at her with wide, hurt eyes.

<!-- paragraph:4678 para_id:00001246 style:Normal -->
She sighed inwardly. During the day, Selwin was supposed to be training—and technically, she and Niall hadn’t agreed on how to divide that responsibility. But apparently, Niall had decided to send him off to her without so much as a word. It would be more effort than it was worth to try to fob Selwin back off on him.

<!-- paragraph:4679 para_id:00001247 style:Normal -->
“Here,” she said, keeping her tone even. “Do one of your Shroud exercises instead. Focus on how your body feels—the movement of your breath, the air on your skin, your weight on the floor. Everything.”

<!-- paragraph:4680 para_id:00001248 style:Normal -->
Selwin straightened, nodding eagerly. “Okay!”

<!-- paragraph:4681 para_id:00001249 style:Normal -->
He began to breathe in careful, exaggerated motions, concentrating fiercely, his brow furrowing. He still couldn’t form a Shroud at all, but at his level the exercises were complex enough to distract him from the Archeon gauntlets.

<!-- paragraph:4682 para_id:0000124A style:Normal -->
Aldira gave Maya a sidelong look, her eyes flicking briefly toward Arrek. “Maybe,” she said, voice deceptively mild, “he shouldn’t be here for this.”

<!-- paragraph:4683 para_id:0000124B style:Normal -->
Maya followed her gaze. “Selwin wants him here,” she said, her tone calm but edged just enough to make the point. “It’s fine.”

<!-- paragraph:4684 para_id:0000124C style:Normal -->
Aldira’s eyes lingered on Selwin for half a second—measuring, calculating—but she wisely dropped it. “Fine,” she said at last. “But if he breaks anything, I’m not cleaning it up.”

<!-- paragraph:4685 para_id:0000124D style:Normal -->
Harvick had drifted closer, curiosity written all over him. He reached toward the nearest gauntlet, and Aldira swatted his bracer hard enough to make a metallic clang.

<!-- paragraph:4686 para_id:0000124E style:Normal -->
“What is it with you men?” she snapped. “You see something humming ominously on a worktable and your first instinct is to poke it?”

<!-- paragraph:4687 para_id:0000124F style:Normal -->
Harvick rubbed the back of his hand, unoffended. “Research.”

<!-- paragraph:4688 para_id:00001250 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “It’s amazing men survive as long as they do.”

<!-- paragraph:4689 para_id:00001251 style:Normal -->
“Luck,” Aldira said dryly, before turning back to the gauntlets. “Anyway, it shouldn’t surprise you that these were very expensive. Look here.” She pointed with the tip of one thin, insulated probe.

<!-- paragraph:4690 para_id:00001252 style:Normal -->
Arrek had edged a bit closer despite himself. Aldira caught the movement and shot him a warning glare.

<!-- paragraph:4691 para_id:00001253 style:Normal -->
He raised his hands in mock surrender. “I’m not touching them.”

<!-- paragraph:4692 para_id:00001254 style:Normal -->
“Good,” Aldira said.

<!-- paragraph:4693 para_id:00001255 style:Normal -->
He leaned in slightly anyway, eyes narrowing at the patterns. “I don’t recognize most of those zan.”

<!-- paragraph:4694 para_id:00001256 style:Normal -->
“That’s because there’s no point in general zanwrights learning them,” Maya said. “They’re too specialized—and expensive—to waste time putting into books. Plus, the etching order is difficult enough that you’d only want a specialized craftsman to deal with them, or you’d risk wasting a lot of money.”

<!-- paragraph:4695 para_id:00001257 style:Normal -->
Aldira bent closer to the gauntlets again, peering through her lenses. “These look almost like kizän to me,” she said, tapping a fingertip beside one of the etched lines, “the type used for storing energy. But the pattern isn’t quite right. It’s denser—tighter.” She straightened, rubbing the bridge of her nose. “I don’t recognize this configuration. You’ve been spending more time with zän lately than I have, Maya. Any thoughts?”

<!-- paragraph:4696 para_id:00001258 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped up beside her, studying the web of etchings under the harsh white light. “Most of the zän we use in general applications are simplified derivations of older patterns,” she said slowly. “Standardized, efficient, easy to produce. But I’ve heard of some zanwrights working the old way—miniaturizing the archaic etchings into compact lattices like this.” She traced one of the lines in the air above the metal, careful not to touch. “It requires rare alloys and perfect craftsmanship. Even a small mistake would make the whole array collapse.”

<!-- paragraph:4697 para_id:00001259 style:Normal -->
She frowned, eyes scanning deeper into the pattern. “But there are others here I don’t recognize at all. It’s possible they’re archaic variants that never made it into modern catalogs.”

<!-- paragraph:4698 para_id:0000125A style:Normal -->
Aldira gave a low whistle. “Or new ones someone doesn’t want in the catalogs.”

<!-- paragraph:4699 para_id:0000125B style:Normal -->
Arrek leaned in again, brow furrowed. “What’s the point of something like that?” he asked. “Even if you could make miniature kizän, what would it do?”

<!-- paragraph:4700 para_id:0000125C style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms, studying the intricate etchings. “It doesn’t really make sense. You could pour a fortune into crafting a small array like this, sure—but it couldn’t store that much energy. If Archeon was using these to amplify their strength, the stored power would be gone after a single strike. It’s… inefficient.”

<!-- paragraph:4701 para_id:0000125D style:Normal -->
Aldira adjusted her goggles and gave a thoughtful hum. “You did say earlier you felt something else in them—a second current of magic, separate from the Shroud. Maybe that’s where the efficiency comes from.”

<!-- paragraph:4702 para_id:0000125E style:Normal -->
Arrek was still staring at the gauntlets, expression unusually focused. “All right,” he said slowly, “then how do Shrouds actually work?”

<!-- paragraph:4703 para_id:0000125F style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated, glancing at Selwin. He had stopped his exaggerated breathing and was sneaking looks at the gauntlets through the corners of his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:4704 para_id:00001260 style:Normal -->
“Since you’re both clearly fascinated,” she said dryly, “we’ll review. It’ll be good practice for your Shroud exercises, Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:4705 para_id:00001261 style:Normal -->
He straightened guiltily. “I was listening!”

<!-- paragraph:4706 para_id:00001262 style:Normal -->
Maya folded her hands behind her back, slipping easily into the cadence of instruction. “A Shroud isn’t just a layer of magic you wear,” she said. “It’s a constant awareness of the space around your body—and an instantaneous mental ability to sublimate energy through your hereditary magic. Sublimation is the process that converts any form of energy into one aligned with your own power, so you can disperse or redirect it as needed. It happens in fractions of a second.”

<!-- paragraph:4707 para_id:00001263 style:Normal -->
Arrek tilted his head, frowning. “So… since you’ve got your Shroud up all the time, that means you’re thinking about it all the time? Protecting yourself every moment?”

<!-- paragraph:4708 para_id:00001264 style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated. “I suppose I am,” she admitted. “But that’s just what it means to be a High Clade magus. You never know when an enemy might strike. You can’t trust anyone completely.”

<!-- paragraph:4709 para_id:00001265 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s expression shifted; some flicker of guilt or shame passed through his eyes. She assumed it was because he’d interrupted her and pressed on.

<!-- paragraph:4710 para_id:00001266 style:Normal -->
“Anyway,” she continued briskly, “the traditional dogma says we ‘split focus’—that we think about the Shroud and everything else simultaneously. But Elric disproved that decades ago. He showed that it’s impossible to truly focus on more than one thing at a time. What powerful magi actually do is use accelerated cognition to switch attention back and forth very rapidly.”

<!-- paragraph:4711 para_id:00001267 style:Normal -->
She turned toward Selwin, who was staring at her with wide eyes. “That’s why we talk so much about focus, Selwin. For your Shroud to work, you have to make the process reflexive—so ingrained that the mental effort to maintain it is minimal. That leaves room to think, to fight, to live.”

<!-- paragraph:4712 para_id:00001268 style:Normal -->
When she looked back, Arrek was watching her again—too intently. She felt a flush threaten at the base of her throat and fought to keep her expression neutral.

<!-- paragraph:4713 para_id:00001269 style:Normal -->
“If you don’t master that reflex by the end of adolescence,” she went on, “your mind becomes too rigid, too fully formed. You’ll never reach the level of instinctive control you need to be safe as a magus, to fight and protect yourself at the same time.”

<!-- paragraph:4714 para_id:0000126A style:Normal -->
Arrek’s gaze flicked between her and Selwin, his brow furrowing.

<!-- paragraph:4715 para_id:0000126B style:Normal -->
“What?” she asked. “I can tell you have a question.”

<!-- paragraph:4716 para_id:0000126C style:Normal -->
“It’s horrible,” he said. “Always having to worry about threats in the back of your mind. Even when you’re young.”

<!-- paragraph:4717 para_id:0000126D style:Normal -->
“That’s not a question,” Maya said, voice cool. “And that’s just the way things are.”

<!-- paragraph:4718 para_id:0000126E style:Normal -->
Maya turned back to the gauntlets but could still feel Arrek’s eyes on her. The words he’d said echoed in her head and she couldn’t help thinking about the way he’d spoken of the High Clades, the venom in his voice when he called them monsters. People like her.

<!-- paragraph:4719 para_id:0000126F style:Normal -->
He must be thinking about that now, she told herself. About how she embodied everything he hated. He was only here because Selwin had asked him to be, and even then, he’d tried to get out of it. The thought made her chest tighten in a way that was part hurt, part anger.

<!-- paragraph:4720 para_id:00001270 style:Normal -->
They were still locked in that uneasy silence when Harvick’s voice cut in, merciful and loud.

<!-- paragraph:4721 para_id:00001271 style:Normal -->
“So the important thing here,” he said cheerfully, “is that I was basically right. These gauntlets do some kind of magic thing, and that’s the answer.”

<!-- paragraph:4722 para_id:00001272 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, the spell between her and Arrek snapping. “First off,” she said dryly, “I’m fairly certain you said the opposite of that when we discussed it. And second, the Archeon Head Magus wasn’t even wearing them.”

<!-- paragraph:4723 para_id:00001273 style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a wounded shrug. “Okay, fine, you got me. But—hear me out.” He leaned a little closer, glancing at Arrek and lowering his voice to a stage whisper. “What if those tricks I told you about—”

<!-- paragraph:4724 para_id:00001274 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s eyes narrowed immediately. Harvick coughed. “—you know, the ones we talked about, were combined with energy from these things? Maybe that could do what you saw on the battlefield.”

<!-- paragraph:4725 para_id:00001275 style:Normal -->
Maya considered it. “That doesn’t solve the issue of where the energy is coming from,” she said. “You can’t amplify power without a source, and you can’t reuse what’s already spent. The math doesn’t work.”

<!-- paragraph:4726 para_id:00001276 style:Normal -->
Aldira straightened, pushing her goggles back down around her neck. “Let’s assume, for the sake of argument, that you’re both right. That Archeon’s using two kinds of magic at once. Suppose the second type is generating energy somehow. That would explain why the Head Magus is strong without these gauntlets, while her brood needs the extra help.”

<!-- paragraph:4727 para_id:00001277 style:Normal -->
Maya folded her arms, eyes still on the gauntlets. “We know that Archeon has the most extensive breeding programs of any Clade,” she said. “But it’s not supposed to be possible to manifest two hereditary magics at once. People have tried. Both of my parents had different affinities—my father with Energetics, my mother with Convergence. I only inherited hers. The magical arrays etched into the brain just don’t have the capacity to process more than one at a time.”

<!-- paragraph:4728 para_id:00001278 style:Normal -->
Aldira hummed, still peering through her goggles. “That’s true—at least for the types of hereditary magic we know. But there’s a lot about the world we don’t understand. What if there’s another kind entirely? Something that doesn’t follow the same rules?”

<!-- paragraph:4729 para_id:00001279 style:Normal -->
Arrek broke in abruptly. “Another kind of magic? What does that mean? What would it look like?”

<!-- paragraph:4730 para_id:0000127A style:Normal -->
Aldira straightened and gave him a long, irritated look. “Well,” she said, “I just said no one knows. That’s the whole point of a mystery.”

<!-- paragraph:4731 para_id:0000127B style:Normal -->
Maya straightened, her thoughts already spinning ahead. “Thank you for letting me know, Aldira. See if you can get any more information about Archeon’s breeding programs. With how far they’ve taken this, there have to be people who were involved—someone we can track down. Maybe a few broke ties and went into hiding. Also, see if you can find out what kind of zanwright could make something like this.”

<!-- paragraph:4732 para_id:0000127C style:Normal -->
Aldira raised an eyebrow over her goggles. “You know, Maya, I don’t tell you how to do your job,” she said—her tone dry but the corner of her mouth quirking.

<!-- paragraph:4733 para_id:0000127D style:Normal -->
Maya gave her a smile. “Of course not. We’ll leave you alone.”

<!-- paragraph:4734 para_id:0000127E style:Normal -->
“Good,” Aldira said. “Because you’re very distracting. Now, unless you want to take over cataloguing these lovely murder weapons, I’ll get back to work.”

<!-- paragraph:4735 para_id:0000127F style:Normal -->
Maya inclined her head. “Please, do. Let me know if you find out anything else.”

<!-- paragraph:4736 para_id:00001280 style:Normal -->
Aldira waved a hand in dismissal, already lowering her goggles again as the rest of them turned to go.

<!-- paragraph:4737 para_id:00001281 style:Normal -->
Out in the hallway, Maya’s mind churned. The more she turned over the conversation, the more she wanted to dig back through the old codices in the library. There had to be something—some mention of multiple magics coexisting, or something to explain what was going on.

<!-- paragraph:4738 para_id:00001282 style:Normal -->
Selwin, of course, was already tugging at her sleeve. “So what do we do now?”

<!-- paragraph:4739 para_id:00001283 style:Normal -->
Harvick glanced between them, reading her expression with that uncanny ease of his. “Why don’t I take Selwin out to one of the outer courtyards,” he said. “We can run some focus exercises for his Shroud. Practice won’t hurt.”

<!-- paragraph:4740 para_id:00001284 style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated. She should spend more time training Selwin herself, but she had too much to process—and Harvick was one of the few people she trusted to keep him safe. “That’s fine,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:4741 para_id:00001285 style:Normal -->
Selwin brightened. “Okay! But Arrek needs to come too! He can show me how to use swords!”

<!-- paragraph:4742 para_id:00001286 style:Normal -->
Arrek looked caught, but recovered quickly. “Actually, Selwin,” he said lightly, “why don’t you teach me about Shrouds? You can show me the exercises you do—they sound interesting.”

<!-- paragraph:4743 para_id:00001287 style:Normal -->
There was something in his tone Maya couldn’t quite read. She frowned, trying to pin it down, then decided she didn’t have the energy. Her thoughts were too tangled where he was concerned—curiosity, irritation, something else she didn’t want to name.

<!-- paragraph:4744 para_id:00001288 style:Normal -->
She watched as the three of them headed down the corridor, Selwin chattering, Harvick half-listening, Arrek matching his pace. For a moment, the image blurred—the dark chisel flashing in Arrek’s hand back in the dining hall, the way he’d planted himself between her and the crash of pottery.

<!-- paragraph:4745 para_id:00001289 style:Normal -->
In her mind’s eye, the chisel and the gauntlets overlapped, both the same deep, black sheen. Ridiculous, she told herself. Just exhaustion.

<!-- paragraph:4746 para_id:0000128A style:Normal -->
It had been a very long day.

<!-- paragraph:4749 para_id:0000128D style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 26

<!-- paragraph:4750 para_id:0000128E style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 26

<!-- paragraph:4752 para_id:00001290 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4753 para_id:00001291 style:Normal -->
Davian walked a half-step behind the others, his mind running hotter than his pulse. The words another kind of magic kept looping in his head, tangled with Maya’s voice explaining Shrouds—how they sublimated energy, how they converted it. Converted, redirected, absorbed, redirected again. And the gauntlets… their etchings had looked wrong and right at the same time, familiar in the way a half-forgotten scar was.

<!-- paragraph:4754 para_id:00001292 style:Normal -->
But the most pressing thing in his head wasn’t that. It was the whisper Harvick had made back in the bay. Davian flicked a glance at him. “So,” he said quietly, “what are those tricks you were whispering about?”

<!-- paragraph:4755 para_id:00001293 style:Normal -->
Harvick stiffened. “Ah. Those. Right.” He scratched the back of his neck. “That’s, uh—code. For secret magic stuff.”

<!-- paragraph:4756 para_id:00001294 style:Normal -->
Davian’s stare flattened into a deadpan.

<!-- paragraph:4757 para_id:00001295 style:Normal -->
Selwin had skipped a few paces ahead, lost in some humming rhythm of his own. Harvick used the moment to jerk his chin in the boy’s direction. “Look, I know you had it rough,” he said, low. “But there’s more than one way for people to suffer growing up.”

<!-- paragraph:4758 para_id:00001296 style:Normal -->
Davian’s jaw flexed. “You’re just trying to distract me from the question.”

<!-- paragraph:4759 para_id:00001297 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s expression shifted into something sly, almost boyish. “If I really wanted to distract you,” he said under his breath, “Selwin isn’t the Rezin I’d be pointing at.”

<!-- paragraph:4760 para_id:00001298 style:Normal -->
Davian blinked, then flushed hard, eyes snapping forward. “Okay. I get it.”

<!-- paragraph:4761 para_id:00001299 style:Normal -->
Harvick chuckled under his breath.

<!-- paragraph:4762 para_id:0000129A style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled sharply through his nose. “You’re right, though,” he said after a beat. “Maya didn’t have it easy just because she’s rich and powerful. We both… we both know what it’s like to feel like you have to do more. Like one mistake means catastrophe. Or death.”

<!-- paragraph:4763 para_id:0000129B style:Normal -->
Harvick nodded. “Yeah. For her it’s not just figurative. She literally has to keep her Shroud up every second. And that’s not the only mask she’s been trained to wear.”

<!-- paragraph:4764 para_id:0000129C style:Normal -->
Davian narrowed his eyes. “I don’t know what you’re trying to do here, Harvick, but it’s not a good idea.”

<!-- paragraph:4765 para_id:0000129D style:Normal -->
Harvick’s grin was all innocence. “Me? I’m not trying to do anything. I’m just an innocent bystander in all this.”

<!-- paragraph:4766 para_id:0000129E style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” Davian muttered. “That’s never true when someone says it out loud.”

<!-- paragraph:4767 para_id:0000129F style:Normal -->
They walked in silence after that, Selwin skipping a few paces ahead. The kid clearly knew where he was going, though the route wound on longer than Davian expected—across narrow interior bridges, down two levels, and through a series of low archways that opened finally into a secluded courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:4768 para_id:000012A0 style:Normal -->
It was smaller than the main training yards, enclosed by high walls and half-covered in creeping ivy. Only two guards waited there, both wearing the lighter runner’s leathers that Harvick favored—practical, scuffed, and far removed from the polished plate of the citadel elite. The guards were older too, maybe a few years on Harvick, lounging on the stones with the comfortable slouch of men who’d long since stopped pretending to stand on ceremony.

<!-- paragraph:4769 para_id:000012A1 style:Normal -->
Harvick raised a hand. “Afternoon, gents.”

<!-- paragraph:4770 para_id:000012A2 style:Normal -->
One of them tipped his chin up. “Afternoon, Harv.”

<!-- paragraph:4771 para_id:000012A3 style:Normal -->
“This is Arrek,” Harvick said, clapping Davian lightly on the shoulder. “These two are a couple of my old buddies. They’ve been around since the days when Niall was first learning to throw lightning—and when Lady Maya was still a terror-in-training. So they know the drill.”

<!-- paragraph:4772 para_id:000012A4 style:Normal -->
Both men nodded to Davian, taking him in with the sort of casual scrutiny only veterans could manage.

<!-- paragraph:4773 para_id:000012A5 style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” one of them said, dryly. “If by ‘the drill’ you mean knowing to sit somewhere no stray shrapnel can hit you, and being ready to sprint for Lord Niall— or I guess Lady Maya, now—if something… happens, then sure. We know it.”

<!-- paragraph:4774 para_id:000012A6 style:Normal -->
Selwin stepped confidently into the middle of the courtyard and announced, “If anything happens, I’ll be here to protect you!”

<!-- paragraph:4775 para_id:000012A7 style:Normal -->
One of the older guards laughed and reached out to ruffle the boy’s hair. “I believe it, lad,” he said, good-naturedly.

<!-- paragraph:4776 para_id:000012A8 style:Normal -->
Selwin grinned wide, then waved at Davian. “Come on!”

<!-- paragraph:4777 para_id:000012A9 style:Normal -->
Davian started forward, but one of the guards caught his attention with a quiet voice. “You know,” the man said, leaning slightly closer, “I’ve already lived a lot longer than I should have in this world—this close to High Clade magi. But you’re young, got your whole life ahead of you. Be careful out there.”

<!-- paragraph:4778 para_id:000012AA style:Normal -->
He nodded because that was what you did when an old soldier gave you advice. He walked into the open and tried to imagine the sentence as a map: my whole life ahead of me. Up until now his life had been a string of discrete operations—get ink, take a face, lie well, break in, get out with the prize. Tomorrow was another task. Next week another. He’d never let his mind stray past the immediate checklist; the future was a luxury that smelled like risk.

<!-- paragraph:4779 para_id:000012AB style:Normal -->
What do I even want? The thought surprised him by arriving so quietly, as if it had been waiting for when the noise around him softened. If he stole the Heart—if he handed it over, felt the ink tighten around his mind—what then? Home would be a hollow place with a darker version of him sitting at the table. If he killed Niall, if he made that long-renowned name into ash and rumor, what would he be? A man who had finally satisfied a ledger? A murderer with a better story? He tried not to picture Maya in the calculus—her face at the mess hall, the way she moved in the training yard, the odd fragile moments between them that lodged like a splinter in his mind. But the images slid in anyway, slippery and unwanted.

<!-- paragraph:4780 para_id:000012AC style:Normal -->
If I go home victorious… hide forever? Keep killing until the thing inside me stops being a hole? The questions came like knives with no handles. He didn't have the language for what he feared he might become; only an appetite he couldn't name, a hunger sharpened by years of doing what he'd been told would make the scales even.

<!-- paragraph:4781 para_id:000012AD style:Normal -->
Selwin was calling to him again, voice bright, breaking his train of thought. “Arrek! You said you’d practice with me!”

<!-- paragraph:4782 para_id:000012AE style:Normal -->
Davian blinked, forced a small grin, and walked out toward him. He saw Harvick out of the corner of his eye. His gaze followed Davian with a look that implied knowledge of the whispers and the charms and the small, dangerous things men carried.

<!-- paragraph:4783 para_id:000012AF style:Normal -->
Davian lowered himself onto the stones across from Selwin, mirroring the boy’s straight-backed posture. The kid looked deadly serious, legs crossed, hands resting on his knees like a miniature monk.

<!-- paragraph:4784 para_id:000012B0 style:Normal -->
“Usually it’s Father or Maya who sits across from me and tells me what to do,” Selwin said, puffing out his chest a little. “But this time I’m going to show you what to do!”

<!-- paragraph:4785 para_id:000012B1 style:Normal -->
“Alright,” Davian said, settling in. “I’ll follow your lead.”

<!-- paragraph:4786 para_id:000012B2 style:Normal -->
“You have to sit and feel your whole body,” Selwin instructed. “Your hands and arms and feet and legs and stomach and back and head and… you just have to feel it.”

<!-- paragraph:4787 para_id:000012B3 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded. “Okay. Should we be closing our eyes?”

<!-- paragraph:4788 para_id:000012B4 style:Normal -->
Selwin looked scandalized. “No! I need to keep them open. Because if I can only make a Shroud with my eyes closed, then how could I fight Archeon like Maya did?”

<!-- paragraph:4789 para_id:000012B5 style:Normal -->
“Fair point,” Davian said. “She probably had her eyes open.”

<!-- paragraph:4790 para_id:000012B6 style:Normal -->
Selwin gave a satisfied nod and then fixed his gaze on the far wall with such intensity that Davian half-expected it to start cracking. Dutifully, he tried to imitate him, staring at the same spot until his vision blurred. His thoughts started wandering almost immediately—to Maya, how she had looked in her tunic and leggings, how when her eyes met his for a few moments it made him feel like the sun was rising inside his chest… How he had agreed to steal from her…

<!-- paragraph:4791 para_id:000012B7 style:Normal -->
He exhaled, breaking the silence. “So, uh, is this it? Am I supposed to be doing something?”

<!-- paragraph:4792 para_id:000012B8 style:Normal -->
Selwin didn’t move. “It’s only been a minute,” he said seriously. “Father says I have to do this for at least a few hours before I can make a Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:4793 para_id:000012B9 style:Normal -->
Davian tried not to grimace. “Hours. Right.”

<!-- paragraph:4794 para_id:000012BA style:Normal -->
“Oh, yeah—I forgot!” Selwin perked up. “You also have to be thinking about anything that’s touching you. Or could be touching you. Or trying to hurt you. And then you think about it… going away somewhere else. Like, away from your body.”

<!-- paragraph:4795 para_id:000012BB style:Normal -->
Davian blinked, looking at him. “Just like that, huh?”

<!-- paragraph:4796 para_id:000012BC style:Normal -->
Selwin nodded earnestly. “Uh-huh. That’s what Maya says. You just have to make it not happen.”

<!-- paragraph:4797 para_id:000012BD style:Normal -->
Davian stared back at the wall again, wondering if anyone in the world—child or adult—actually understood how this was supposed to work.

<!-- paragraph:4798 para_id:000012BE style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “So you just sit here for hours thinking about how to stop things from hurting you?”

<!-- paragraph:4799 para_id:000012BF style:Normal -->
Selwin didn’t even blink. “But I need to! Otherwise how could I help Father and Maya keep everyone safe? And how could I keep myself safe so they don’t have to worry about me?”

<!-- paragraph:4800 para_id:000012C0 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated, watching the boy’s earnest face. “I guess you’re right,” he said quietly. “Nothing’s more important than being powerful enough to protect yourself—and your family.”

<!-- paragraph:4801 para_id:000012C1 style:Normal -->
Selwin smiled, satisfied, and went back to staring at the wall like it had personally offended him.

<!-- paragraph:4802 para_id:000012C2 style:Normal -->
Davian looked down at his own hands. Would it even be possible for me to make a Shroud? His family’s magic didn’t work like that; it bent energy, rerouted it. But Maya had said the Shroud converted energy—sublimated it. That sounded awfully close to Subversion.

<!-- paragraph:4803 para_id:000012C3 style:Normal -->
A new thought stirred uneasily. If I tried… and if it actually worked… would my sigil flare?

<!-- paragraph:4804 para_id:000012C4 style:Normal -->
He shifted, pretending to stretch out his shoulders. Out of the corner of his eye, he glanced back—the guards were leaning against the wall, talking easily with Harvick, all of them relaxed but watchful. No one seemed too interested in him.

<!-- paragraph:4805 para_id:000012C5 style:Normal -->
He looked forward again. Selwin hadn’t moved, still staring straight ahead, posture rigid with concentration.

<!-- paragraph:4806 para_id:000012C6 style:Normal -->
Davian took a slow breath. Alright, he thought. I’ll try it. Just once. If I feel anything strange, I’ll stop. No risks. Not here.

<!-- paragraph:4807 para_id:000012C7 style:Normal -->
He closed his eyes for a fraction of a second, then opened them again, fixing on the same patch of stone Selwin had chosen. At first, he only felt stillness. His breath evened, the sound of the courtyard dulled, and the warmth of the afternoon flattened into silence. The edges of the world softened—Harvick’s voice, the guards, even Selwin’s steady breathing—until all of it slipped beneath a dark, slow tide.

<!-- paragraph:4808 para_id:000012C8 style:Normal -->
The Ink hummed in his skin. The threads of it ran beneath his tattoos like roots, coiling up through his veins, winding into the back of his skull. He could feel it now—not as power, but as pressure, a steady sinking pull. His field of vision narrowed; the wall ahead of him stretched away, a single bright patch of stone receding down a long corridor of shadow. The light flickered, then dimmed, as though swallowed.

<!-- paragraph:4809 para_id:000012C9 style:Normal -->
The sensation wasn’t frightening. It was familiar. He had been here before—when the real Arrek had bled out beneath his hands, when he’d looked down and seen nothing left in himself but reflection. The place between things. The ink-sea.

<!-- paragraph:4810 para_id:000012CA style:Normal -->
He sank further, until even the memory of weight was gone. The darkness pressed in, thick as water, cool and formless, and then the voice came—soft, deliberate, each word ringing clear in the emptiness. You’ve come again to the still place between breaths. The world above is sharp and full of teeth, but here the water remembers no wounds.

<!-- paragraph:4811 para_id:000012CB style:Normal -->
It wasn’t spoken aloud. It arrived as thought, sliding under his own. You’ve carried too much, little shadow. The ledger of what you’ve done and what you’ll never undo—let it rest. You are tired, and tired men deserve the quiet.

<!-- paragraph:4812 para_id:000012CC style:Normal -->
Davian knew that tone—the young man from the Syndicate, the one who had marked him. He didn’t remember his name, only that impossible calm, the sense that every word carried its own echo. You need not think. Thought is the oldest wound. Let it close. Let the noise go silent, and the skin of the world grow smooth again.

<!-- paragraph:4813 para_id:000012CD style:Normal -->
Davian felt the rest of himself dissolve—the guilt, the mission, the faces. Even his name thinned to a thread. All that remained was his body, perfectly outlined, every limb distinct, every breath a pulse of cold awareness.

<!-- paragraph:4814 para_id:000012CE style:Normal -->
There was something he needed to do. Something instinctive. To protect himself.

<!-- paragraph:4815 para_id:000012CF style:Normal -->
He looked down. His hands glimmered faintly, a sheen like oil on water tracing the ridges of his skin. For an instant it looked alive, spreading upward like frost climbing glass. His heart slammed once, hard. A Shroud. I made a Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:4816 para_id:000012D0 style:Normal -->
The thought alone was enough to send him lurching backward. His body moved before the rest of him did—bolting upright, scraping across the stone, trying to get away from his own hands.

<!-- paragraph:4817 para_id:000012D1 style:Normal -->
But it was like moving through the Ink again, as if the air itself had thickened. His heartbeat echoed from far away. Selwin turned toward him at an impossible slowness, his eyes wide, his mouth just starting to form a question. The shimmer was already fading, draining away like water poured over coals, and Davian could see the world speeding up again—sound returning, time snapping into place.

<!-- paragraph:4818 para_id:000012D2 style:Normal -->
He stumbled, catching himself on his knees, and the realization hit like a blade against bone. If the sigil flares—if it glows—they’ll see.

<!-- paragraph:4819 para_id:000012D3 style:Normal -->
His hands went to his face instinctively, covering it, pressing hard enough that his vision sparked.

<!-- paragraph:4820 para_id:000012D4 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s shout came from behind him. “Davian! What’s going on—?”

<!-- paragraph:4821 para_id:000012D5 style:Normal -->
For one sick heartbeat Davian imagined looking up and seeing Harvick’s blade coming down. Or worse—his own hand jerking forward, red on steel, Harvick’s eyes going glassy. Then the ink in his mind twisted the image: Harvick’s face melting, his own face forming beneath it.

<!-- paragraph:4822 para_id:000012D6 style:Normal -->
The thought brought bile to his throat.

<!-- paragraph:4823 para_id:000012D7 style:Normal -->
But then—slowly—the panic drained. His breathing steadied, his pulse cooled. He knew this. He knew that Maya’s and Niall’s sigils didn’t flare when they held their Shrouds; the glow was only for power projection, not defense. He hadn’t exposed himself. Not yet.

<!-- paragraph:4824 para_id:000012D8 style:Normal -->
He let his hands drop, his body shaking, and the courtyard seemed to tilt under him. The adrenaline left him hollow, boneless. “Sorry,” he said, his voice rough. “Guess I… fell asleep sitting up. Had some kind of nightmare.”

<!-- paragraph:4825 para_id:000012D9 style:Normal -->
Selwin responded instantly. “Oh, I get those all the time when I fall asleep! Sometimes there are big eyes, or fire, or both.”

<!-- paragraph:4826 para_id:000012DA style:Normal -->
Davian forced a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Yeah. Something like that.”

<!-- paragraph:4827 para_id:000012DB style:Normal -->
Harvick was watching him closely. “You sure you’re not overdoing it?” he said, tone mild. “Wouldn’t be the first time someone nodded off mid-training. Maybe you just need more sleep.”

<!-- paragraph:4828 para_id:000012DC style:Normal -->
Davian tried to laugh, but it came out thin. “Yeah. Sleep. That’d fix everything.”

<!-- paragraph:4829 para_id:000012DD style:Normal -->
He could still feel it—the ghost of the shimmer, the pulse of it under his skin. The sense that something was on him, just beyond sight. He ran a hand across his arm, over his chest, but there was nothing—no warmth, no hum. Still, every time he blinked, he thought he saw faint ripples at the edges of his vision, like heat distortion.

<!-- paragraph:4830 para_id:000012DE style:Normal -->
The courtyard felt smaller now. The air too close. Out of the corner of his eye, the two guards were still lounging by the wall, but their faces were shadowed—he couldn’t quite make out their expressions. Were they watching him? Their eyes seemed like dark pits, bottomless, evil in some way he couldn’t name.

<!-- paragraph:4831 para_id:000012DF style:Normal -->
He swallowed hard. They have a good reason to look at you, they aren’t planning anything. But it didn’t help. His pulse picked up again, and the back of his neck prickled as if something unseen had fixed its gaze there.

<!-- paragraph:4832 para_id:000012E0 style:Normal -->
He shook his head, once, twice—trying to clear the residue of the Ink, of the voice, of that perfect empty calm. Breathe. Control it. He forced his breath to even out, counting silently, making his hands unclench.

<!-- paragraph:4833 para_id:000012E1 style:Normal -->
When he looked again, the guards’ eyes were just eyes. Human. Uninterested. The world steadied—but only just.

<!-- paragraph:4834 para_id:000012E2 style:Normal -->
Harvick exhaled through his nose, half a sigh, half a tired laugh. “Well, after what’s been going on the last few days, I suppose none of us have been sleeping all that well.”

<!-- paragraph:4835 para_id:000012E3 style:Normal -->
He glanced toward Selwin, then back to Davian. “Sorry, kid,” he said to the boy, “but I’m vetoing Arrek being here for now.”

<!-- paragraph:4836 para_id:000012E4 style:Normal -->
Selwin frowned, shoulders sagging. “But I wanted him to teach me swords!”

<!-- paragraph:4837 para_id:000012E5 style:Normal -->
Harvick crouched a little to meet his eye. “You’ll have to settle for learning from me—the undisputed best swordsman in the citadel.” He looked up at Davian, smirking. “Right?”

<!-- paragraph:4838 para_id:000012E6 style:Normal -->
Davian forced a grin. “Sure. Wouldn’t want to steal your title.”

<!-- paragraph:4839 para_id:000012E7 style:Normal -->
Harvick clapped him on the shoulder. “Then go rest. That little episode was embarrassing enough. You wouldn’t want something like that happening in front of Lady Maya, would you?”

<!-- paragraph:4840 para_id:000012E8 style:Normal -->
The words hit harder than Harvick meant them to. For a second, Davian saw it—Maya’s face, soft and open the way it had been in training, then hardening into revulsion. Her Shroud flaring white-blue, frost crawling up his skin, the last thing he’d ever see before she shattered him.

<!-- paragraph:4841 para_id:000012E9 style:Normal -->
He blinked, forcing the image back into the dark. He knew the look on his face must’ve slipped, so he ducked his head, muttering, “Yeah… thanks. I guess I could use some more rest.”

<!-- paragraph:4842 para_id:000012EA style:Normal -->
He turned and started down the corridor before anyone could say anything else. Selwin’s voice trailed after him—protesting, pleading—but it blurred into the background, distant and harmless.

<!-- paragraph:4843 para_id:000012EB style:Normal -->
Step by step, the world grew quieter again. His heartbeat slowed. The halls stretched long and dim, the light turning thick and liquid at the edges. He felt himself drifting, the air itself softening into a slow, viscous calm.

<!-- paragraph:4844 para_id:000012EC style:Normal -->
Like he was sinking once more into that sea of Ink—where everything was muted, everything still, and the worries of the day dissolved into black water.

<!-- paragraph:4845 para_id:000012ED style:Normal -->
When he reached his room, he shut the door and leaned against it for a long breath. The world steadied, but only a little. He looked down at his hands. No shimmer. No light. No Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:4846 para_id:000012EE style:Normal -->
But something had happened. He could still feel it under his skin—a reflex that hadn’t been learned, just waiting there. Like the motion of breathing, or blinking. Buried. Submerged in the Ink behind his mind.

<!-- paragraph:4847 para_id:000012EF style:Normal -->
In the dim light, his tattoos seemed to move. The black lines along his arms swirled faintly, almost alive. He lifted one arm closer to the lamp, half expecting the patterns to shift again, but they lay still. Only his heartbeat seemed to pulse through them.

<!-- paragraph:4848 para_id:000012F0 style:Normal -->
His breath caught. A thought, quick and unwelcome, surfaced from the dark. With a shaking hand he reached into his waistband and pulled out Antithesis. The knife caught the weak light, its blade webbed with fine, delicate zan-etchings—so much like the ones on the Archeon gauntlets that it made his stomach twist.

<!-- paragraph:4849 para_id:000012F1 style:Normal -->
His mother had told him the blade was an heirloom, a sacred relic of their line. A tool to protect the innocent from the horrors born of the High Clades. A secret inheritance, proof of their calling.

<!-- paragraph:4850 para_id:000012F2 style:Normal -->
But now… he stared at the blade, tracing the lines with his thumb. If that was a Shroud he had made—if it hadn’t been a dream—then everything she had told him was wrong.

<!-- paragraph:4851 para_id:000012F3 style:Normal -->
He could feel his pulse hammering in his throat. He reached toward his desk, to put pen to paper, to draft a letter to contact her, to demand answers. To know if she’d lied to him, or if she even knew the truth. His hand hovered there, shaking.

<!-- paragraph:4852 para_id:000012F4 style:Normal -->
Too risky, he told himself. They’ll trace it, someone will notice.

<!-- paragraph:4853 para_id:000012F5 style:Normal -->
But the lie felt thin, brittle. The real reason was heavier. He didn’t want to hear her voice. Didn’t want to be dragged back down. Because every time he thought of her, of home, of the mission—the bright world he’d found here seemed to shrink, fading toward that same narrow, distant light.

<!-- paragraph:4854 para_id:000012F6 style:Normal -->
The same one he’d seen down the tunnel in the Ink. He set the knife down, slowly, and sat on the edge of the bed.

<!-- paragraph:4855 para_id:000012F7 style:Normal -->
You’re being selfish, he thought. The words came automatically—his mother’s voice, his own, both at once. Everything I’m doing, I’m doing for my family. For everyone. To keep them safe from the High Clades. To break their hold.

<!-- paragraph:4856 para_id:000012F8 style:Normal -->
He dragged a hand down his face, staring at the dark between his fingers. “I deserve to be selfish for a moment,” he whispered into the quiet. “Just one. To feel what it’s like in the sun before I’m dragged back into the shadow.”

<!-- paragraph:4857 para_id:000012F9 style:Normal -->
For an instant, he let the thought unfurl. The memory of warmth—not the sterile kind that came from fire or magic, but the kind that seeped through skin, slow and golden. Maya’s warmth. She filled that place in his mind without him meaning to—bright, steady, terrifying in her clarity. The only light that hadn’t asked him to hide.

<!-- paragraph:4858 para_id:000012FA style:Normal -->
He felt a pull then, sharp and strange. A need to go to her, to be near her, to speak her name aloud. But he didn’t know how to start—didn’t even know what he would say if he did.

<!-- paragraph:4859 para_id:000012FB style:Normal -->
So instead he sat. He let the impulse pass, or at least pretended to. The bed creaked under him as he leaned forward, elbows on his knees. One by one, the thoughts unraveled, dissolving back into the Ink behind his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:4860 para_id:000012FC style:Normal -->
It came easily now—that floating sensation, the paradox of heaviness and weightlessness, as though his body were sinking while the rest of him rose above it. The silence of it was perfect. Safe.

<!-- paragraph:4861 para_id:000012FD style:Normal -->
He looked down at his hands. They were steady on his knees, every line and vein clear in the dimness. He could feel them so precisely, every joint and tendon, yet they didn’t quite seem like his.

<!-- paragraph:4862 para_id:000012FE style:Normal -->
And then, slowly, the air around them stirred.

<!-- paragraph:4863 para_id:000012FF style:Normal -->
A faint shimmer—colorless, rippling, bending the lamplight that wasn’t there—spread from his palms to his fingers, outlining them in ghostlight.

<!-- paragraph:4864 para_id:00001300 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4865 para_id:00001301 style:Normal -->
Davian drifted weightless beside the vast curve of the Treasury Sphere, his gloved fingertips brushing its surface as he rotated gently in the air. He’d circled the entire thing again searching for an entrance. Every seam, every zan array, every panel. Nothing. No door, no latch, no hidden channel. Just endless lattices of interconnected zan.

<!-- paragraph:4866 para_id:00001302 style:Normal -->
He pushed himself off the surface, floating backward through the emptiness of the chamber. He found himself thinking of her again. In the cafeteria, jumping in front of her like a fool without even realizing what he was doing. “You’re an idiot.” he whispered into the echoing silence.

<!-- paragraph:4867 para_id:00001303 style:Normal -->
He could still see her as she had been in that moment: her eyes wide, her Shroud flaring, spreading crystals across the table in an unconscious display of immeasurable power. Maya Rezin, the woman who should have been his enemy. The woman who was his enemy. But when Caius had cornered him, she hadn’t hesitated. She’d stepped between them—between him and certain death—because she’d seen something worth saving.

<!-- paragraph:4868 para_id:00001304 style:Normal -->
Even when she didn’t know who he was. Even when he hid behind a mask of iron and secrets.

<!-- paragraph:4869 para_id:00001305 style:Normal -->
He wanted her to see him without the mask. And he wanted to see her without hers—the perfect heir, the faultless magus, the one everyone watched but no one really saw.

<!-- paragraph:4870 para_id:00001306 style:Normal -->
The thought cut too deep and he instinctively closed his eyes, needing to escape, to block out the pain of living within this lethal contradiction. The Ink in his mind stirred and his consciousness sunk down into it, slow and soft, a sable haven of stillness and lack of meaning.

<!-- paragraph:4871 para_id:00001307 style:Normal -->
Something had happened last night, and he wasn’t sure what it was. But it had become too easy to sleep between states now, and the Ink was always waiting to soothe him by blurring the world at the edges. And in that space he could find his way to that alien instinct, the invitation to protect himself by pushing everything else away. He opened his eyes again. Is that what a Shroud is? The negation of connection, of meaning, of value? The Sphere still floated before him, a vast glowing surface that made his desires feel small and impotent.

<!-- paragraph:4872 para_id:00001308 style:Normal -->
He sighed. He was running out of time. The Ink Boon that shielded him, that held his sin within itself and let him pass as someone else, was fading. He pulled on his tether, drifting back towards the surface of the sphere. Tanelle was anchored “below” him, or at least closer to the ground, tools floating lazily around her in the weightless environment. She was painstakingly re-etching a za, her tongue protruding from between her teeth in her concentration.

<!-- paragraph:4873 para_id:00001309 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. He’d been convinced that he could find an entrance point himself, especially after his promotion to supervisor, that there had to be something on the surface of the sphere that would give away a location for ingress. But he couldn’t afford to be careful any more.

<!-- paragraph:4874 para_id:0000130A style:Normal -->
Davian pushed off from a brace and drifted toward her, moving carefully, the slow precision of someone pretending not to be in a hurry. Tanelle didn’t notice him until he was close enough to cast a shadow across her work-light. She flinched, twisting slightly in her harness, the chisel slipping from her hand and floating between them.

<!-- paragraph:4875 para_id:0000130B style:Normal -->
“Skag, Arrek—don’t do that,” she said, grabbing it out of the air. “You scared me half to death.”

<!-- paragraph:4876 para_id:0000130C style:Normal -->
“Sorry.” He glanced over his shoulder, checking that the nearest workers were several spans away, busy with their own sections of the outer lattice. Their murmurs were faint through the hum of the lattice. Satisfied, he turned back and lowered his voice while trying not to sound too furtive. “Have you seen anything that looks like it could be an entrance? A seam, a panel, something hidden?”

<!-- paragraph:4877 para_id:0000130D style:Normal -->
Tanelle blinked at him, momentarily thrown by the question. “An entrance? No. It’s actually really interesting—”

<!-- paragraph:4878 para_id:0000130E style:Normal -->
Davian recognized that tone: the prelude to one of her lectures.

<!-- paragraph:4879 para_id:0000130F style:Normal -->
“The zan lattice is remarkably homogenous across the whole surface,” she went on, her hands already sketching shapes in the air. “No distortions, no interruptions in the flow. Whoever designed this thing was a genius.”

<!-- paragraph:4880 para_id:00001310 style:Normal -->
“So nowhere different,” Davian pressed. “Nothing that stands out? How do they even get in if they want to take something out?”

<!-- paragraph:4881 para_id:00001311 style:Normal -->
That stopped her. Her expression went blank for a moment as the idea worked its way through. “Huh. I… actually never thought about that. Well, there are a few lattice sections that look like they were replaced more recently—recently meaning ten years ago instead of fifty.” She tapped her chisel against her palm, thinking. “So maybe there used to be an entrance and they blocked it up?”

<!-- paragraph:4882 para_id:00001312 style:Normal -->
“That doesn’t make sense,” Davian said. “It’s a treasury. What’s the point of a treasury you can’t use?”

<!-- paragraph:4883 para_id:00001313 style:Normal -->
Tanelle shrugged, a quick, sheepish motion. “You’re asking me like I built it. But—” She leaned closer, lowering her voice. “Here’s something odd.”

<!-- paragraph:4884 para_id:00001314 style:Normal -->
She pulled a folded sheet of parchment from her tool pouch and pressed it flat against the Sphere’s surface, scribbling fast, drawing out a small network of zan. “Look at this!” She said in almost a stage whisper.

<!-- paragraph:4885 para_id:00001315 style:Normal -->
Davian squinted. “Yeah, that’s one of the repeating patterns—mostly kizan, for storage, with some pulzan to regulate the output. The same one that’s all over the shell.”

<!-- paragraph:4886 para_id:00001316 style:Normal -->
Tanelle smiled in a way that made him nervous—half triumphant, half conspiratorial. “Exactly. This version—the one I just drew—would push energy out from the sphere. But look.” She flipped the paper over and smoothed it out over a section of the etched lattice. It matched almost exactly.

<!-- paragraph:4887 para_id:00001317 style:Normal -->
“See?” she whispered. “The lattice on the surface of the sphere is backwards. At least the parts that matter. These zan don’t push energy out—they’re holding it in.”

<!-- paragraph:4888 para_id:00001318 style:Normal -->
Davian just stared at the mirrored diagram, trying to make sense of what she’d said.

<!-- paragraph:4889 para_id:00001319 style:Normal -->
“So if that’s true,” he said slowly, “how come no one else has brought it up?”

<!-- paragraph:4890 para_id:0000131A style:Normal -->
Tanelle gave a quick, distracted laugh. “Oh, come on, Arrek. You’ve met the others. Most low-level zanwrights don’t care about theory. You learn the strokes, the sequence, how not to blow your hand off—then you follow the design someone smarter than you made a century ago. Nobody’s paying us to get creative.”

<!-- paragraph:4891 para_id:0000131B style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “Still. You’d think someone would’ve noticed.”

<!-- paragraph:4892 para_id:0000131C style:Normal -->
She shrugged, the movement easy and unbothered. “They probably did what they always do—hire people who specialize in grunt work. Re-etching, maintenance, copying existing zan. No one who actually looks at the why.” She caught his expression and added with a grin, “No offense.”

<!-- paragraph:4893 para_id:0000131D style:Normal -->
“None taken,” he said. “I have no idea what to make of any of this. You should have been the supervisor.”

<!-- paragraph:4894 para_id:0000131E style:Normal -->
Her eyes went wide, horrified. “Oh, no. Absolutely not. Then I’d just stand around watching other people do the work. Can you imagine? I’d lose my mind in a week.” She made a face, mock-shuddering. “I’d get so bored I’d probably chew my own hands off.”

<!-- paragraph:4895 para_id:0000131F style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. “That’s… graphic.”

<!-- paragraph:4896 para_id:00001320 style:Normal -->
Tanelle smiled unabashedly. “Practical truth. The fun part’s in the etching, not the oversight.”

<!-- paragraph:4897 para_id:00001321 style:Normal -->
He studied her for a moment, still floating a few feet away. “So I guess they accidentally hired someone a lot smarter than they expected.”

<!-- paragraph:4898 para_id:00001322 style:Normal -->
She snorted. “Not smarter. Just someone who gets obsessed with patterns.”

<!-- paragraph:4899 para_id:00001323 style:Normal -->
Davian floated there a moment longer, the glowing surface of the Sphere curving away into infinity. “I don’t really know what to do with this,” he said finally.

<!-- paragraph:4900 para_id:00001324 style:Normal -->
Tanelle shrugged, unconcerned. “Well, everything’s going fine, right? I’m getting to etch plenty of zan, we’re being paid well, nobody’s exploded yet. So it’s just an interesting puzzle. The Rezins have… a lot of secrets.”

<!-- paragraph:4901 para_id:00001325 style:Normal -->
He gave a humorless laugh and started to push away, but something in her tone made him pause. “Wait,” he said, turning back. “If the zan here aren’t pushing energy out to make us weightless… what is?”

<!-- paragraph:4902 para_id:00001326 style:Normal -->
She looked at him, then pointed to the Sphere itself. “Something in there, I guess. Maybe a second layer of zan on the inside. Though I don’t know how they’d maintain it.”

<!-- paragraph:4903 para_id:00001327 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at the surface for another heartbeat, then nodded. “Thanks.”

<!-- paragraph:4904 para_id:00001328 style:Normal -->
He kicked off gently, drifting upward until the tether caught his momentum. Below, the other zanwrights floated across the surface of the sphere, their tools glinting in the dim light.

<!-- paragraph:4905 para_id:00001329 style:Normal -->
He wasn’t lying—he really didn’t know what to make of what she’d told him. But one thing was certain: if Tanelle couldn’t find a way in, he had no chance.

<!-- paragraph:4906 para_id:0000132A style:Normal -->
His hand brushed against the hilt of Antithesis, the etched blade at his side. The patterns there—so like the zan on the Sphere—seemed to hum faintly beneath his touch. I’ll just have to take my chances. Cut my way in if I have to.

<!-- paragraph:4907 para_id:0000132B style:Normal -->
Maybe, he thought, he could use the Ink Boon somehow—merge it with whatever he’d done in the courtyard, that half-formed Shroud. If he could reinforce his body, blunt the backlash—just long enough.

<!-- paragraph:4908 para_id:0000132C style:Normal -->
He knew he would only get one shot. And time was draining away with the Ink.

<!-- paragraph:4909 para_id:0000132D style:Normal -->
His thoughts began to spiral—Maya’s voice, her eyes, the way she looked at him like he was someone worth saving. Guilt bit deep, then dissolved as his mind slipped down into the waiting Ink.

<!-- paragraph:4910 para_id:0000132E style:Normal -->
The noise, the light, the weight of the Citadel—all gone. In its place: that still, thick silence. He drifted inside it, feeling his body fade, until there was only simplicity. No choices to make, no impossible lines to walk—only reflexes carved into him long ago. The old pattern. Hate them. Break them. Burn the rot out of the world. It wasn’t belief anymore, just muscle memory of the soul.

<!-- paragraph:4911 para_id:0000132F style:Normal -->
He drifted down until there was nothing left—no light, no sound, not even the thought of himself. And in that perfect absence, something moved. Not a sound, not a beat—the opposite of a vibration, rippling through the nothingness like breath drawn backward.

<!-- paragraph:4912 para_id:00001330 style:Normal -->
He felt it without words, a knowing that wasn’t thought: the Heart. The silence around it bent, vast and patient. He understood: the threads have been woven, the patterns have been set, the bargains have been made. It was inevitable, the thing that had been etched into the Skein of reality for him. When the time came, there would be no escape from the Heart.

<!-- paragraph:4913 para_id:00001331 style:Normal -->
He would take it.

<!-- paragraph:4914 para_id:00001332 style:Normal -->
Then—against the black—a spark.

<!-- paragraph:4915 para_id:00001333 style:Normal -->
A single line of gold, burning down through the dark like a thread of living fire. Her face in its light—Maya’s—bright, defiant, impossibly alive. He seized the thread without thinking, clutching the warmth that cut through the ink like an exposed nerve.

<!-- paragraph:4916 para_id:00001334 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t salvation, just direction. A reminder that somewhere above the void, something still burned—and that he was already bound to it.

<!-- paragraph:4917 para_id:00001335 style:Normal -->
He pulled himself toward it, hand over hand, following that thread out of the dark.

<!-- paragraph:4920 para_id:00001338 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 27

<!-- paragraph:4921 para_id:00001339 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 27

<!-- paragraph:4923 para_id:0000133B style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:4924 para_id:0000133C style:Normal -->
Maya still had a headache from her visit to the library yesterday that had gone too far into the evening. She’d combed through the genealogical ledgers, the bloodline archives, even the old census ledgers from the Drakhal alliance years—but none of it helped. Every thread she tried to pull on unraveled into blank pages and burned seals. Whatever Archeon was doing with their breeding programs, it was buried deep, and the Library’s polite silences only made that clearer.

<!-- paragraph:4925 para_id:0000133D style:Normal -->
And then there had been the morning’s supervisor meeting. She’d seen Arrek across the table, and he’d barely looked at her. His posture had been stiff, his expression unreadable, his gaze fixed anywhere but in her direction. Tense, distracted, like being in the same room with her was a punishment. Maybe it was. He’d left yesterday to “train” with Selwin, and she realized now that the excuse had been exactly that—an escape.

<!-- paragraph:4926 para_id:0000133E style:Normal -->
By the time she reached Aldira’s office, her nerves were already frayed. The spymaster didn’t look up from the spread of reports on her desk, her gray-streaked hair tied back in its usual precise knot.

<!-- paragraph:4927 para_id:0000133F style:Normal -->
Maya cleared her throat. “Anything new?”

<!-- paragraph:4928 para_id:00001340 style:Normal -->
Aldira turned a page with deliberate calm. “No.”

<!-- paragraph:4929 para_id:00001341 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Nothing at all?”

<!-- paragraph:4930 para_id:00001342 style:Normal -->
“These things take time,” Aldira said evenly. “You can’t expect an entire network to yield results on command.”

<!-- paragraph:4931 para_id:00001343 style:Normal -->
“I’m not expecting miracles, just—”

<!-- paragraph:4932 para_id:00001344 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s looked up from her desk with maddening slowness. “Checking in several times a day isn’t going to make it seem like things are moving any faster, you know.”

<!-- paragraph:4933 para_id:00001345 style:Normal -->
Maya folded her arms, trying to ignore the heat creeping into her face. “I’m aware.”

<!-- paragraph:4934 para_id:00001346 style:Normal -->
Aldira leaned back in her chair, watching Maya for a moment before speaking. “You know,” she said, her voice softer now, “some things are always going to be out of your control.”

<!-- paragraph:4935 para_id:00001347 style:Normal -->
Maya’s jaw tightened. “I know that.”

<!-- paragraph:4936 para_id:00001348 style:Normal -->
“You might say you do,” Aldira said, “but I think it’s hard for you to truly believe. It’s a perennial problem for magi.” She picked up a quill, rolled it between her fingers, then set it down again. “When you can bend the world to your will in one area, it’s easy to start believing you can do it everywhere. That if you just think harder, plan better, push longer, you’ll force the world to obey.”

<!-- paragraph:4937 para_id:00001349 style:Normal -->
Maya looked away, tracing the grain of the desk with her eyes. “Isn’t that what we’re supposed to do? Improve the world? Fix what’s broken?”

<!-- paragraph:4938 para_id:0000134A style:Normal -->
Aldira gave a small, almost fond smile. “It’s a good instinct. But the stronger and smarter a magus is, the harder it is not to let that instinct consume them.” She let the words hang for a moment. “And you, Maya, are both very strong and very smart.”

<!-- paragraph:4939 para_id:0000134B style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated for a long moment before saying quietly, “Tell me what happened to Elric.”

<!-- paragraph:4940 para_id:0000134C style:Normal -->
Aldira’s head snapped up. Her eyes flicked to the closed door, then to the corners of the room, as if half-expecting Niall himself to materialize out of the air. “You know your father doesn’t want anyone to mention him,” she said at last. Her tone was calm, but there was steel under it. “And we’ve already talked about what happened.”

<!-- paragraph:4941 para_id:0000134D style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “You’ve told me warnings about him—about not following his path, not reaching too far. But that’s not the same as what he did. What did he do that made my father kill him?”

<!-- paragraph:4942 para_id:0000134E style:Normal -->
Even speaking the words sent a faint chill through the air, as if the Citadel itself disapproved.

<!-- paragraph:4943 para_id:0000134F style:Normal -->
Aldira’s gaze softened, though her posture stayed guarded. “To be honest, I don’t know,” she said. “The only people who were there were Niall, Alara…” She paused, reluctant. “…and Harvick.”

<!-- paragraph:4944 para_id:00001350 style:Normal -->
Maya straightened. “Then I’ll ask Harvick.”

<!-- paragraph:4945 para_id:00001351 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s hand came up before she could move. “Don’t.”

<!-- paragraph:4946 para_id:00001352 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Why not?”

<!-- paragraph:4947 para_id:00001353 style:Normal -->
“Because whatever else he is, Harvick’s still human. He might look unshakeable—calm, grounded—but this… this is the one thing I don’t think he’ll ever talk about.” Aldira leaned forward, lowering her voice. “Please, Maya. Let it rest.”

<!-- paragraph:4948 para_id:00001354 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms but didn’t argue. “Fine,” she said. “But you must at least know why he wouldn’t tell me.”

<!-- paragraph:4949 para_id:00001355 style:Normal -->
Aldira hesitated, the lines around her mouth tightening. “Elric had a presence about him—overwhelming, magnetic. No one before or after ever managed to make the High Clades work together the way he did. He could make you believe in whatever he wanted.”

<!-- paragraph:4950 para_id:00001356 style:Normal -->
She exhaled through her nose. “Harvick fell under that spell, too. He was young when they met. I wouldn’t call Elric a father figure, but he was more than a mentor. Someone Harvick believed in.”

<!-- paragraph:4951 para_id:00001357 style:Normal -->
Maya said quietly, “And then?”

<!-- paragraph:4952 para_id:00001358 style:Normal -->
“Then whatever happened between Elric and your father changed everything,” Aldira said. “It ended suddenly, and badly. And whatever the truth is, Harvick blames himself for Elric’s death. All that discipline, all that quiet philosophy—it’s him trying to make sure it never happens again.”

<!-- paragraph:4953 para_id:00001359 style:Normal -->
Maya sank into the chair in the corner of Aldira’s office, the weight of the conversation finally pressing her down. She pressed her palms against her eyes. “Why does everything have to be wrapped in secrets?” she murmured. “Layer after layer—mystery, intrigue, half-truths. Even my own family. The people who work for me.”

<!-- paragraph:4954 para_id:0000135A style:Normal -->
A face flickered through her mind unbidden—Arrek, his guarded expression, his silences—and her stomach tightened. “Everyone,” she said quietly, “wears a mantle of secrets.”

<!-- paragraph:4955 para_id:0000135B style:Normal -->
Her thoughts began to accelerate, spinning faster and faster until they outran her breath. Aldira was saying something, her mouth moving slowly, but the sound stretched thin and warped. The air around Maya thickened, the way it always did right before she fell into that other place—the place of threads and meaning and unbearable light.

<!-- paragraph:4956 para_id:0000135C style:Normal -->
Not here. Not here.

<!-- paragraph:4957 para_id:0000135D style:Normal -->
She clung to something solid: the memory of Aldira’s voice, patient and steady; her faith in Maya even when she stumbled; the quiet trust that she would find her way. It was enough. She pulled herself back, drawing the world into focus again.

<!-- paragraph:4958 para_id:0000135E style:Normal -->
Aldira was watching her with raised eyebrows. To her, it had been no more than a blink.

<!-- paragraph:4959 para_id:0000135F style:Normal -->
Maya let out a slow breath, but then saw it—the faint frost curling outward from her boots, spreading in delicate tendrils across the stone floor. She’d reached instinctively for the Shroud again, gripping it too tightly, her body’s reflexive answer to fear.

<!-- paragraph:4960 para_id:00001360 style:Normal -->
“I’m sorry,” she said, forcing the Shroud down, willing warmth back into her fingers.

<!-- paragraph:4961 para_id:00001361 style:Normal -->
“It’s fine,” Aldira said mildly, leaning back in her chair. “Ice melts.” Her mouth raised slightly at the edges. “I’ll just count my blessings that it wasn’t the fire.”

<!-- paragraph:4962 para_id:00001362 style:Normal -->
Maya was just starting to recover her breath when she heard muffled voices outside—Erynn’s quick, polite tone, one of her guards responding, then a third voice, higher and breathless. A moment later the door opened, and a young runner all but stumbled inside.

<!-- paragraph:4963 para_id:00001363 style:Normal -->
He stopped short when he saw both women, his eyes darting uncertainly between them. “I—uh—Lady Rezin, Spymaster Aldira—”

<!-- paragraph:4964 para_id:00001364 style:Normal -->
“Just deliver the message,” Maya said, a little sharper than she meant to. She straightened in her chair, forcing calm back into her posture. “What is it?”

<!-- paragraph:4965 para_id:00001365 style:Normal -->
The boy swallowed and fumbled with a slip of paper. “A coded report just came through,” he said quickly. “One of our informants in the Drakhal district says they’ve called a meeting of the High Clades. At the Central Spire.”

<!-- paragraph:4966 para_id:00001366 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “That doesn’t make sense. We haven’t received a notice.”

<!-- paragraph:4967 para_id:00001367 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s expression hardened. “Unless,” she said slowly, “the Drakhals are excluding us intentionally.”

<!-- paragraph:4968 para_id:00001368 style:Normal -->
For a moment, Maya couldn’t move. The words hung in the air without meaning, as if her mind refused to assemble them into anything real.

<!-- paragraph:4969 para_id:00001369 style:Normal -->
Aldira was already on her feet. “Fetch the Head Magus,” she told the boy. “Now. Run.”

<!-- paragraph:4970 para_id:0000136A style:Normal -->
He nodded, pale, and fled the room. The door slammed behind him.

<!-- paragraph:4971 para_id:0000136B style:Normal -->
Aldira exhaled slowly, her composure tight but intact. “Nothing like this has happened in all my years with the Rezins,” she said, voice low. “And that’s longer than I care to admit.”

<!-- paragraph:4972 para_id:0000136C style:Normal -->
Maya found her voice at last. “What could this even mean?”

<!-- paragraph:4973 para_id:0000136D style:Normal -->
“Nothing good,” Aldira said flatly. “First Archeon’s magi siding with Drakhal, and now this. Drakhal has always been ambitious, but calling a meeting and excluding us?” She shook her head. “They must have something big planned to risk a provocation like that.”

<!-- paragraph:4974 para_id:0000136E style:Normal -->
Niall arrived quickly, his military coat already thrown over his shoulders. He looked less like he was preparing for a meeting and more like he was marching to war. Maybe he was.

<!-- paragraph:4975 para_id:0000136F style:Normal -->
His eyes—those storm-gray eyes—fixed on Aldira first. “Where did this come from?”

<!-- paragraph:4976 para_id:00001370 style:Normal -->
“One of our hidden agents inside the Drakhal citadel,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:4977 para_id:00001371 style:Normal -->
“Could it be false?”

<!-- paragraph:4978 para_id:00001372 style:Normal -->
