Aldira shook her head. “Very unlikely. It was sent in our cipher, and our operative wouldn’t risk transmission unless they were certain.”

<!-- paragraph:4979 para_id:00001373 style:Normal -->
Niall’s jaw tightened. “Maybe it’s possible we simply haven’t received the invitation yet.”

<!-- paragraph:4980 para_id:00001374 style:Normal -->
Aldira sighed. “You and I both know that’s extremely implausible.”

<!-- paragraph:4981 para_id:00001375 style:Normal -->
The silence in the room swelled until Maya couldn’t stand it. “Then what do we do?”

<!-- paragraph:4982 para_id:00001376 style:Normal -->
Both of them turned toward her, as if they’d forgotten she was there. Niall’s face was unreadable. “If we weren’t invited,” he said finally, “then we have no obligation to go.”

<!-- paragraph:4983 para_id:00001377 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at him, disbelief curdling into anger. “So we just let Drakhal meet with every other High Clade—scheme under our noses—after allying with Archeon and attacking us?”

<!-- paragraph:4984 para_id:00001378 style:Normal -->
For a moment, she saw fear flicker in his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:4985 para_id:00001379 style:Normal -->
She stepped forward until she was close enough to see the fine tremor in his jaw. “I’ve seen what you can do,” she said, her voice low and sharp. “If we go there—together—we could kill every magus in that Spire, and they couldn’t stop us.”

<!-- paragraph:4986 para_id:0000137A style:Normal -->
Niall looked away. “Maybe,” he said, too quietly. “Maybe I don’t want to rule by fear anymore.”

<!-- paragraph:4987 para_id:0000137B style:Normal -->
Something in his tone didn’t ring true, and Maya pressed harder. “They attacked us, Father. They started this—the day of my Presentation. We go there and tell everyone what Drakhal’s been doing, and they’ll support us. They have to.”

<!-- paragraph:4988 para_id:0000137C style:Normal -->
Aldira exhaled slowly. “The problem is, you don’t have proof.”

<!-- paragraph:4989 para_id:0000137D style:Normal -->
Maya turned toward her. “All the other High Clades have intelligence networks just like we do. They know what really happened.”

<!-- paragraph:4990 para_id:0000137E style:Normal -->
Niall’s voice was flat. “It doesn’t work like that.”

<!-- paragraph:4991 para_id:0000137F style:Normal -->
Maya’s anger flared white-hot. “Fine. If you’re too much of a coward to go, then I’ll go myself.”

<!-- paragraph:4992 para_id:00001380 style:Normal -->
She turned for the door—but the air behind her snapped, sharp and metallic.

<!-- paragraph:4993 para_id:00001381 style:Normal -->
Lightning hissed through the room, crawling from the corners of Niall’s eyes and branching down his cheeks like veins of light. Arcs crackled from his clenched fists, grounding against the metal fixtures, the scent of ozone filling the air.

<!-- paragraph:4994 para_id:00001382 style:Normal -->
Maya spun back toward him. “I don’t believe this. You’ll fight me, but not them?”

<!-- paragraph:4995 para_id:00001383 style:Normal -->
Aldira had already wisely retreated to the corner.

<!-- paragraph:4996 para_id:00001384 style:Normal -->
Niall’s voice was raw, the sound of thunder strangled into human shape. “I would never fight you. But you don’t know what you’re walking into.”

<!-- paragraph:4997 para_id:00001385 style:Normal -->
He took several long, deliberate breaths, the arcs dimming with each exhale. The lightning receded to a faint corona around his eyes, glimmering under the disheveled white of his hair.

<!-- paragraph:4998 para_id:00001386 style:Normal -->
Maya’s tone softened, but only barely. “We can stay here and do nothing, but the world will keep moving out there—whether or not we permit it.”

<!-- paragraph:4999 para_id:00001387 style:Normal -->
For a long time, Niall said nothing. Then, almost too quietly to hear, “Your mother used to say things like that.”

<!-- paragraph:5000 para_id:00001388 style:Normal -->
He looked up, and the spark in his eyes steadied into resolve. “Fine,” he said. “We may be going to our deaths… but I’m tired of hiding.”

<!-- paragraph:5001 para_id:00001389 style:Normal -->
Within an hour, the courtyard was a flurry of motion—armored guards forming ranks, runners relaying orders, the air thick with the low thrum of tension. The escort they’d assembled looked impressive enough on the surface, but everyone knew the truth. If a battle broke out between the High Clades, these soldiers were nothing but offerings—bodies to be swept aside in the opening exchange.

<!-- paragraph:5002 para_id:0000138A style:Normal -->
In the center of the yard, the Rezin chariot waited—drawn not by horses but by four armored guards. Animals would panic in a fight between magi; at least the guards would hold formation.

<!-- paragraph:5003 para_id:0000138B style:Normal -->
Harvick appeared from the far gate, jogging toward them with his sword slung across his back and his black bracers catching the gray light. “I’m coming with you,” he said, not out of deference but out of certainty.

<!-- paragraph:5004 para_id:0000138C style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t hesitate. “No.”

<!-- paragraph:5005 para_id:0000138D style:Normal -->
Harvick’s brow furrowed. “You’ll need—”

<!-- paragraph:5006 para_id:0000138E style:Normal -->
“You’d be of no use there,” Niall cut in, his tone flat. “If anything happens to us, I want you to get Selwin out of the city. You’re the only one I trust to do this.”

<!-- paragraph:5007 para_id:0000138F style:Normal -->
Harvick’s jaw tightened, but he said nothing. The protest died before it reached his lips.

<!-- paragraph:5008 para_id:00001390 style:Normal -->
Maya turned at the sound of movement—the gleam of helmets weaving through the crowd. Selwin’s guards, leading him forward. He looked oddly calm amid the commotion, as if this were just another unpredictable day in his life of unpredictable days.

<!-- paragraph:5009 para_id:00001391 style:Normal -->
Niall looked almost startled. Before he could speak, Aldira stepped forward, unruffled as always. “I sent for him,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:5010 para_id:00001392 style:Normal -->
Selwin waved cheerfully as he approached. “Hi! What’s going on?”

<!-- paragraph:5011 para_id:00001393 style:Normal -->
Maya crouched slightly, forcing a smile. “Father and I are going to a special meeting today, but we’ll be back soon, all right?”

<!-- paragraph:5012 para_id:00001394 style:Normal -->
Selwin grinned. “Sure! I hope I can come to special meetings soon too.”

<!-- paragraph:5013 para_id:00001395 style:Normal -->
She looked at Niall, but he was staring past them. Then, slowly, he knelt in front of his son. The movement seemed to drain the air from the courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:5014 para_id:00001396 style:Normal -->
“You know,” Niall said quietly, “I can’t remember the last time I told my children I love them.”

<!-- paragraph:5015 para_id:00001397 style:Normal -->
It didn’t sound like regret so much as a confession—a simple, damning truth.

<!-- paragraph:5016 para_id:00001398 style:Normal -->
Selwin didn’t seem to notice the weight of it. He just smiled and threw his arms around his father’s neck. “That’s okay,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:5017 para_id:00001399 style:Normal -->
Niall’s arms tightened around him briefly before he stood again. “Goodbye, Selwin.” The words were quiet, but something final trembled beneath them. He gave Harvick a long, significant look, and Harvick nodded once.

<!-- paragraph:5018 para_id:0000139A style:Normal -->
Then Niall climbed into the chariot. Maya followed, her hand brushing the cold metal of the rail as she stepped up beside him.

<!-- paragraph:5019 para_id:0000139B style:Normal -->
The guards took their places at the harnesses. With a single word of command, they began to pull.

<!-- paragraph:5020 para_id:0000139C style:Normal -->
As the chariot rumbled toward the gates, Maya glanced at her father. “We are not going to our deaths,” she said. “So stop acting like it.”

<!-- paragraph:5021 para_id:0000139D style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t look at her. His eyes stayed fixed on the horizon.

<!-- paragraph:5022 para_id:0000139E style:Normal -->
The city passed around them in a blur of stone and motion. Maya barely saw it—only the rhythm of her own breathing and the quiet hum of her Shroud in the back of her mind. She kept it close but ready, the cold of it whispering under her skin. Each turn of the chariot’s wheels was another beat to focus on, another moment to reinforce her mental lattice—channels for rapid attack, patterns of heat and redirection. If it comes to a fight, it has to be instant. No hesitation.

<!-- paragraph:5023 para_id:0000139F style:Normal -->
Beside her, Niall’s hands were locked on the chariot rail, the veins in his forearms drawn tight. Tiny arcs of lightning jumped between his knuckles and the metal, each one fading as quickly as it came. He hadn’t spoken once since they’d left the citadel.

<!-- paragraph:5024 para_id:000013A0 style:Normal -->
As they neared the Central Spire, the gates loomed—carved with the icons of every High Clade. The sentries standing above them were statues in armor: silent, unmoving, their eyes shadowed. No one challenged the Rezins’ approach. The gates began to open before the chariot even reached them, groaning on unseen hinges.

<!-- paragraph:5025 para_id:000013A1 style:Normal -->
The market beyond was half-empty. Where her Presentation had drawn crowds and banners, now there were only small clusters of soldiers and robed attendants moving with grim efficiency. The air that had once been filled with music and heraldry was stripped bare—today was not a day for spectacle, but for calculation.

<!-- paragraph:5026 para_id:000013A2 style:Normal -->
When the chariot halted before the wide stone steps, Niall stood first, scanning the plaza. “Leave the escort,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:5027 para_id:000013A3 style:Normal -->
The guards exchanged uneasy glances but obeyed. Only three followed them—two flanking their sides, one taking the rear as they crossed the courtyard toward the Spire’s entrance.

<!-- paragraph:5028 para_id:000013A4 style:Normal -->
The light outside was harsh and clear as they entered the Spire. Maya reflected on the fitting nature of this location for this meeting - a place named for what had been rather than what was. Her feet rang on the steps down into the circle of pale stone sunk into the conflux between cities, the surface worn smooth by centuries of ceremony.

<!-- paragraph:5029 para_id:000013A5 style:Normal -->
Niall followed her down the polished stairway. Around the circular tiers, every High Clade was represented—less spectacle than her Presentation had been, but somehow far more dangerous.

<!-- paragraph:5030 para_id:000013A6 style:Normal -->
Most wore the practical garb of battle, robes divided for movement or light tabards over leggings. The fabrics were still rich, expensive, needing to showcase elegance while preparing for bloodshed. She wondered, with a sinking feeling, whether every Presented magus in Elarion was here.

<!-- paragraph:5031 para_id:000013A7 style:Normal -->
At the heart of the arena, on the raised stone dais, stood Evana Drakhal. She was as immaculate as ever—her red hair drawn back in a braid wound like a coronet, her white coat traced with gold filigree that caught the sun. Her voice carried easily, polished and precise.

<!-- paragraph:5032 para_id:000013A8 style:Normal -->
“We have all suffered under tyranny for long enough,” she was saying. “We can no longer allow one family to dictate all of our destinies.”

<!-- paragraph:5033 para_id:000013A9 style:Normal -->
The crowd rippled with low approval, the sound like distant surf.

<!-- paragraph:5034 para_id:000013AA style:Normal -->
As Maya and Niall descended the final steps, Evana’s eyes caught theirs. Her speech faltered—then she smiled, perfect and sharp, and turned her body slightly so the whole arena could see.

<!-- paragraph:5035 para_id:000013AB style:Normal -->
“Oh,” she said, projecting her voice with effortless poise. “How good of you to deign to join us. Did you come late to make sure every last straggler who dares defy your will had already arrived?”

<!-- paragraph:5036 para_id:000013AC style:Normal -->
Maya kept walking, the frost curling faintly around her heels, her Shroud whispering in readiness. “You—and everyone else here—know we’re late because you didn’t invite us.”

<!-- paragraph:5037 para_id:000013AD style:Normal -->
She stopped at the edge of the arena floor, unwilling to turn her back on so many gathered magi. Her eyes swept the tiers—Drakhal red, Archeon silver, Cryndor black, Lyranth blue, all watching, all waiting.

<!-- paragraph:5038 para_id:000013AE style:Normal -->
Evana tilted her head, rolling her eyes in a gesture that should have seemed childish, yet somehow still looked like theater. “Oh, what a convenient excuse.”

<!-- paragraph:5039 para_id:000013AF style:Normal -->
Maya’s gaze caught on a familiar figure lounging among the Drakhal delegation. Caius, of course—wearing an ostentatiously new vest of silver-trimmed scarlet, smirking like this was all a play staged for his amusement.

<!-- paragraph:5040 para_id:000013B0 style:Normal -->
Histrionics clearly run in the family, Maya thought bitterly.

<!-- paragraph:5041 para_id:000013B1 style:Normal -->
Evana’s smile sharpened until it was a blade. “Well, you brought at least one of your family’s killers today,” she said, jerking a finger toward Niall. “Will you let me finish, or will you call down your wrath from the sky? Or is your plan to have me killed by a knife in the dark—like you’ve been doing to the Low Clades?”

<!-- paragraph:5042 para_id:000013B2 style:Normal -->
Maya’s mouth opened in protest—what are you talking about—but the name hung in the air. Evana had meant the Shade. The assassin who had walked away from the Citadel. The assassin Maya had let go.

<!-- paragraph:5043 para_id:000013B3 style:Normal -->
Evana pounced on the hesitation. “Oh, you can try to deny it,” she said, “but my own son saw your alliance with that underworld rat, that assassin, that Shade. Will you deny it in front of all these magi?”

<!-- paragraph:5044 para_id:000013B4 style:Normal -->
Maya wanted to answer, to strip the accusation down to its bones, but Caius’s name fell from her lips instead. “Caius was killing civilians—wantonly.”

<!-- paragraph:5045 para_id:000013B5 style:Normal -->
“In self-defense!” Evana snapped. “So you admit you attacked my son.”

<!-- paragraph:5046 para_id:000013B6 style:Normal -->
Something in Maya tightened; the world narrowed to the bareness of the accusation and the heat of too many eyes. Her mind slid into the rapid, clinical calculations of combat—threat, exposure, counterstrike—though there was nothing to strike back with here except words and the thin paper of right. She felt Evana’s gaze like a flame near kindling; the woman wanted this to burn, and the hunger in her eyes was unmistakable.

<!-- paragraph:5047 para_id:000013B7 style:Normal -->
A hand settled on Maya’s shoulder. She looked up. Niall had stepped forward, the faint corona of lightning still flickering around his eyes. He scanned the arena as if measuring the weight of every face.

<!-- paragraph:5048 para_id:000013B8 style:Normal -->
“Our family has no affiliation with this Shade,” he said, voice low but carrying. “My daughter followed Caius to ensure he didn’t disrupt activities in our city. She stopped the fight between him and this Shade because she is kind-hearted and wanted to avoid further casualties. This is all perfectly legal; it occurred within our own city limits.”

<!-- paragraph:5049 para_id:000013B9 style:Normal -->
Evana’s scoff ran through the tiers like a ripple. “You expect us to believe that?”

<!-- paragraph:5050 para_id:000013BA style:Normal -->
Niall’s reply was quiet and hard. “You should all know that if I wanted to kill one of you, I wouldn’t do it in the dark with an assassin.”

<!-- paragraph:5051 para_id:000013BB style:Normal -->
Evana’s eyes narrowed to slits. “Oh—so that’s why you came today?” She spread her arms wide, head tilted back toward the glaring sky. “All right then—do it here, in front of everyone!” Her voice rang across the stone. “At least then my death will show them what a monster you are.”

<!-- paragraph:5052 para_id:000013BC style:Normal -->
The arena held its breath.

<!-- paragraph:5053 para_id:000013BD style:Normal -->
Niall just looked at her, and for a heartbeat Maya thought he might actually do it—that the air would crack open and lightning would scour everything clean. But then his mouth twisted into something that might have been a smile, if not for how wrong it looked on his face.

<!-- paragraph:5054 para_id:000013BE style:Normal -->
“I forgot what a fantastic actor you are, Evana,” he said softly. “You and I both know I’m not going to kill you today.”

<!-- paragraph:5055 para_id:000013BF style:Normal -->
He turned from her, scanning the ranks of magi, his gaze pausing on the Lyranth delegation before sweeping on. When he spoke again, his voice carried—not loud, but deliberate, shaped for every ear.

<!-- paragraph:5056 para_id:000013C0 style:Normal -->
“I made mistakes when I was younger—when I was angry, and grieving. I formally apologize to all of you. And I will not make the same mistakes in the future.”

<!-- paragraph:5057 para_id:000013C1 style:Normal -->
That was all. He turned his back on Evana and began walking up the steps, each measured footfall echoing in the stunned silence.

<!-- paragraph:5058 para_id:000013C2 style:Normal -->
Maya stood frozen, half expecting the sky to split, the ground to burn. When it didn’t, when there was only the sound of boots on stone, she followed, keeping her Shroud coiled tight around her body, ready for the attack that never came.

<!-- paragraph:5059 para_id:000013C3 style:Normal -->
Behind them, Evana’s voice cut through the quiet. “That’s it? You think the kind of mantle you’ve been wearing is just something you can take off when you feel like it?”

<!-- paragraph:5060 para_id:000013C4 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t look back. She climbed after Niall, the air behind them buzzing with whispers.

<!-- paragraph:5061 para_id:000013C5 style:Normal -->
Outside the arena, when the noise of the crowd had faded, she caught his arm. “What was that?” she demanded. “You let her call us out. You all but legitimized everything she said, and then you just walked away?”

<!-- paragraph:5062 para_id:000013C6 style:Normal -->
Niall stared down at her, the storm in his eyes banked but not gone. “I misjudged the situation,” he said. “She was expecting us—or at least prepared for it. I’m not going to throw our lives away in an unwinnable battle.”

<!-- paragraph:5063 para_id:000013C7 style:Normal -->
Maya clenched her fists, frost prickling against her palms. Unwinnable battle. He didn’t believe she was strong enough, didn’t see her as an equal. Just another fragile piece to protect.

<!-- paragraph:5064 para_id:000013C8 style:Normal -->
“I need you to trust me,” she said, her voice shaking with anger. “I’m strong enough to help you.”

<!-- paragraph:5065 para_id:000013C9 style:Normal -->
“I do trust you,” he said simply—and climbed into the chariot.

<!-- paragraph:5066 para_id:000013CA style:Normal -->
The ride back through the city was silent. The only sound was the rattle of wheels on stone and, somewhere above, the faint, distant growl of thunder that never broke.

<!-- paragraph:5067 para_id:000013CB style:Normal -->
In the citadel, every word Maya tried with Niall slid off the silence he carried like rain on glass. He walked beside her without speaking, the faint corona of lightning still faintly alive around his hands. When they reached his meditation room, he made it clear he didn’t want company and shut the door on her. It rang behind him with a hollow finality.

<!-- paragraph:5068 para_id:000013CC style:Normal -->
For a long moment she stood there, staring at the engraved wood, feeling the cold weight of it between them. Then the air tightened in her chest and she turned sharply on her heel.

<!-- paragraph:5069 para_id:000013CD style:Normal -->
Her boots struck the marble too loudly as she descended through the citadel’s corridors. Servants and guards stepped aside without a word. The weight of their glances pressed between her shoulder blades—deference, fear, curiosity, all of it unwanted. She barely noticed Erynn’s quick footsteps falling in behind her, or the two guards trailing like obedient shadows.

<!-- paragraph:5070 para_id:000013CE style:Normal -->
By the time they reached the training courtyard, the sun had already started sinking behind the walls, turning the sky a colorless gray. The air here always smelled faintly of ozone and scorched stone.

<!-- paragraph:5071 para_id:000013CF style:Normal -->
Erynn raised a hand to the guards. “Wait here,” she said quietly, and then crossed the yard to stand beside Maya.

<!-- paragraph:5072 para_id:000013D0 style:Normal -->
“I can tell something happened,” she said, voice soft but steady. “Can we please talk about it?”

<!-- paragraph:5073 para_id:000013D1 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer. Her pulse was already rising, her breathing thinning. Erynn’s voice stretched, slowed, the syllables warping until they sounded underwater. The courtyard light bent at the edges of her vision and she began to see it - that place outside of time and space, where the Skein of fate diverged into countless strands. The place that showed how little control she had in the face of the infinite weight of reality.

<!-- paragraph:5074 para_id:000013D2 style:Normal -->
She reached for her magic the way a drowning woman grabs for air, forcing her focus downward to seize the heat within the stones. It rose eagerly, a living thing. The air shimmered and folded, flame coalescing in front of her palms—a writhing, molten sphere straining against its confines. The sound of her heartbeat drowned everything else.

<!-- paragraph:5075 para_id:000013D3 style:Normal -->
Faster. Just focus.

<!-- paragraph:5076 para_id:000013D4 style:Normal -->
The fire swelled. Her arms shook with the effort of containing it but she held on to the very edge of her strength. The moment before catastrophe she ripped open the far side of the construct, and the built pressure released in one violent instant.

<!-- paragraph:5077 para_id:000013D5 style:Normal -->
A beam of fire tore across the yard, white-hot, obliterating the opposite wall with a thunderous crack. Stone burst outward in a rain of debris, slowly spiraling outward through the thickened perception of her racing mind. For a moment she just stared, breath ragged, the taste of copper in her mouth.

<!-- paragraph:5078 para_id:000013D6 style:Normal -->
A shout cut through the ringing silence. She turned—Erynn was on the ground, scrambling away from the circle of frost, shards of stone raining down around her. Maya ran towards her and threw her Shroud over them both, a translucent shell of frost between them and the sudden hammer blows of falling rock.

<!-- paragraph:5079 para_id:000013D7 style:Normal -->
Erynn looked up at her, eyes wide, face pale. “Maya—” she gasped, pulling herself back. “I know you’re stressed, but you almost killed me!”

<!-- paragraph:5080 para_id:000013D8 style:Normal -->
The words hit harder than any falling stone.

<!-- paragraph:5081 para_id:000013D9 style:Normal -->
“I’m sorry,” Maya managed, but her voice fractured on the words. The Skein pressed in again at the edges of her sight—dozens of futures, all ending in horror: Erynn frozen solid, Erynn burned to ash, Erynn broken beneath the rubble. The threads tangled, all of them leading back to her hands.

<!-- paragraph:5082 para_id:000013DA style:Normal -->
She squeezed her eyes shut. “Go,” she said, the word coming out hoarse. “Get out of here.”

<!-- paragraph:5083 para_id:000013DB style:Normal -->
Erynn hesitated, and so did the guards at the gate.

<!-- paragraph:5084 para_id:000013DC style:Normal -->
“Go!” Maya shouted, and the Shroud flared with her voice—frost spiraling outward from her boots, crawling up the courtyard walls. The air itself seemed to flinch from her.

<!-- paragraph:5085 para_id:000013DD style:Normal -->
They didn’t wait for a second order. Boots pounded against stone as they fled, leaving her standing in the center of the wreckage.

<!-- paragraph:5086 para_id:000013DE style:Normal -->
She stood there for a while as the threads of fate spun and bloomed around her. All I ever see is horror and destruction, she thought bitterly. Maybe that’s because that’s all there is for me. It’s not the future’s fault that I’m trapped in this farce.

<!-- paragraph:5087 para_id:000013DF style:Normal -->
The idea settled in her like lead, heavy enough to crush breath from her lungs. Her body moved on reflex, defensive, sliding back into stance, mind tracing the familiar channels of control. She carved the air again with sharp, practiced technique, forming offensive constructs again and again, aiming them into the air instead of at the walls that suddenly seemed far too fragile. Each time she released, the energy tore upward in a column of gold-white flame that vanished into the gray sky. The next rose higher, brighter, hotter—her penance written in fire across the clouds.

<!-- paragraph:5088 para_id:000013E0 style:Normal -->
She didn’t care who saw. The citadel, the city, the Spire—it didn’t matter. Let them look. Let them whisper about the mad Rezin piercing the sky with her anger.

<!-- paragraph:5089 para_id:000013E1 style:Normal -->
Another column roared upward, scattering embers into the wind. The recoil sent her stumbling back, half-blind from the glare. She fired again, and again, until her limbs felt carved from ice and her breath rasped like sand in her throat.

<!-- paragraph:5090 para_id:000013E2 style:Normal -->
When she finally stopped, the world seemed to tilt around her. The edges of the courtyard blurred, every sound distant and slow. She made it to the steps before her legs gave way and she dropped onto the cold stone, leaning back until she could see the open sky again.

<!-- paragraph:5091 para_id:000013E3 style:Normal -->
The air smelled of ash and frost. Sweat beaded on her skin, freezing almost instantly.

<!-- paragraph:5092 para_id:000013E4 style:Normal -->
For a while there was nothing—just the thrum of her pulse and the faint hiss of cooling stone. Then a voice came from somewhere beyond the ring of frost.

<!-- paragraph:5093 para_id:000013E5 style:Normal -->
“You’ve gotten really good at that.”

<!-- paragraph:5094 para_id:000013E6 style:Normal -->
Her head jerked toward the sound.

<!-- paragraph:5095 para_id:000013E7 style:Normal -->
Arrek was sitting on the edge of the ruined courtyard, outside the reach of the frost spreading from her body, one knee drawn up, gaze tilted toward the sky. The evening light caught on his hair and the faint ink under his collar.

<!-- paragraph:5096 para_id:000013E8 style:Normal -->
“I don’t think anyone knows where the Creator actually is,” he said. “But if he’s up there, I think you got him.”

<!-- paragraph:5097 para_id:000013E9 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat she just stared at him, the words hanging in the thin air. Then she almost laughed—quiet, disbelieving, because of how absurd it all was.

<!-- paragraph:5098 para_id:000013EA style:Normal -->
“I thought this scared you,” she said. “Or made you jumpy. Or… intimidated you. I saw you when Harvick made you come here to spar before—you could barely stand—” she caught herself, “—stand it.”

<!-- paragraph:5099 para_id:000013EB style:Normal -->
He didn’t look away from the sky. “Yeah,” he said after a moment. “It used to scare me. And intimidate me. And all that.”

<!-- paragraph:5100 para_id:000013EC style:Normal -->
He turned then, eyes catching the last of the light. “But that was before I knew you.”

<!-- paragraph:5101 para_id:000013ED style:Normal -->
A pause, faint steam still curling between them.

<!-- paragraph:5102 para_id:000013EE style:Normal -->
“But I know it’s part of you now,” he said. “Part of who you are.”

<!-- paragraph:5103 para_id:000013EF style:Normal -->
Maya’s laugh died as quickly as it came. “I almost killed Erynn on accident earlier,” she said, the words scraping her throat. “Just because she was near me. That’s who I am—too dangerous and too uncontrolled for anyone to get close to.”

<!-- paragraph:5104 para_id:000013F0 style:Normal -->
Arrek shifted a little closer on the stair, the sound of his movement soft against the stone. “I don’t think you’ll kill me,” he said quietly. “And I think you’ve got a lot more control than you give yourself credit for.”

<!-- paragraph:5105 para_id:000013F1 style:Normal -->
Their gazes caught and held. The heat still rising from the courtyard seemed to pulse between them. Maya felt her heartbeat climbing again, too fast.

<!-- paragraph:5106 para_id:000013F2 style:Normal -->
He’d moved a little closer without seeming to realize it. His breathing was quicker now too. Then he looked away suddenly, swallowed, and said, “Uh… do you want to spar?”

<!-- paragraph:5107 para_id:000013F3 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “What? What makes you think about that? How could we even do that safely?”

<!-- paragraph:5108 para_id:000013F4 style:Normal -->
He turned back toward her, the last light of evening catching his face, and took on an almost teasing tone. “I’ll let you throw me.”

<!-- paragraph:5109 para_id:000013F5 style:Normal -->
“I thought that was too dangerous with my Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:5110 para_id:000013F6 style:Normal -->
“You know what?” he said, standing and brushing frost from his pants. “I think you can figure something out with your magic. It’ll be a good exercise.”

<!-- paragraph:5111 para_id:000013F7 style:Normal -->
“I don’t know…”

<!-- paragraph:5112 para_id:000013F8 style:Normal -->
He extended a hand to her, the ink on his arm stark against his pale skin. “I trust you not to hurt me.”

<!-- paragraph:5113 para_id:000013F9 style:Normal -->
Maya took his hand. His grip was warm and steady, unbothered by the rim of frost that always came with her magic.

<!-- paragraph:5114 para_id:000013FA style:Normal -->
He drew her toward the open center of the courtyard, steps unhurried, and for a strange moment it felt almost like a dance.

<!-- paragraph:5115 para_id:000013FB style:Normal -->
“Remember,” he said quietly. “Push up top and hook the leg. I know you can stop arrows and all that with your Shroud, so you should be able to keep me from hitting the ground too hard. You did it on yourself when we were practicing earlier.”

<!-- paragraph:5116 para_id:000013FC style:Normal -->
Maya gave a small, uncertain laugh. “You’re asking me to do a lot of things I’m not that experienced with—all at once—when you know I’m already not at my best. Do you really want to do this?”

<!-- paragraph:5117 para_id:000013FD style:Normal -->
She looked up, expecting the guarded look she was used to seeing from him—doubt, calculation, regret—but instead his eyes met hers and he smiled, broad and genuine. “If it wasn’t risky,” he said, “then it wouldn’t be fun.”

<!-- paragraph:5118 para_id:000013FE style:Normal -->
She blinked. “You’re not one of those weird people who do dangerous things on purpose to get a thrill, right?”

<!-- paragraph:5119 para_id:000013FF style:Normal -->
He raised his eyebrows and tilted his head in a manner that wasn’t exactly reassuring.

<!-- paragraph:5120 para_id:00001400 style:Normal -->
She stepped in close—just to set her foot in position—and the heat from his body met the chill of her Shroud, a fine layer of vapor coiling around them. She tried to think of his body as an extension of her own, to pull him under the same shimmering veil that covered her skin. The edges of the world seemed to blur as the Shroud expanded, enclosing them both in a faint haze that muffled the cold air and their breath alike.

<!-- paragraph:5121 para_id:00001401 style:Normal -->
She set her hand on his shoulder, feeling the tension of his stance, the exact line where his balance would break. For a heartbeat it really did feel like they were the same person—two currents inside one field.

<!-- paragraph:5122 para_id:00001402 style:Normal -->
He leaned close enough that she could hear the rasp of his breath. “Remember,” he said, his voice low. “You use this when there’s no retreat.”

<!-- paragraph:5123 para_id:00001403 style:Normal -->
She moved on instinct—pushing high at his shoulder to unbalance him while her foot swept behind his ankle. The Shroud flexed with her motion, energy flowing in the direction of the throw. His body lifted, weightless inside the shared field, and she caught the fall before it could become impact. The shimmer of her Shroud flared once, cushioning them both, so that when he hit the ground it was with the muffled sound of displaced air instead of bone and stone.

<!-- paragraph:5124 para_id:00001404 style:Normal -->
For a moment they stayed like that—still inside the shared glow, her hand on his shoulder, the world outside their Shroud hushed and distant.

<!-- paragraph:5125 para_id:00001405 style:Normal -->
Maya turned her head toward him. “Why do you always seem to show up near me?”

<!-- paragraph:5126 para_id:00001406 style:Normal -->
He stared upward for a moment, watching the shimmer overhead. “I just… notice things about you,” he said finally. “I don’t know.”

<!-- paragraph:5127 para_id:00001407 style:Normal -->
She studied his face—the angle of his jaw, the line of concentration that always seemed to live between his brows—and something in her chest twisted.

<!-- paragraph:5128 para_id:00001408 style:Normal -->
“You don’t know what it’s like to be a magus,” she said softly. “You can’t rely on me. Niall doesn’t trust me. I couldn’t even kill those Archeon magi, and he knows I’m too slow and weak and unreliable.”

<!-- paragraph:5129 para_id:00001409 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s mouth twitched, almost a smile. “You’re right,” he said. “I don’t know about all that stuff. But the nice thing is that I get to pick the risks I take myself.”

<!-- paragraph:5130 para_id:0000140A style:Normal -->
Maya felt her heartbeat catch against the stillness. He didn’t fit into any of the patterns she lived by. Everything in her world had rules, hierarchies, disciplines of perfection—things to be earned, proven, justified. But he moved outside all of that, as if the weight she carried wasn’t something to overcome but something he simply accepted.

<!-- paragraph:5131 para_id:0000140B style:Normal -->
For the first time in longer than she could remember, she didn’t feel like the Rezin heir, or a magus, or a weapon barely under control. She just felt seen.

<!-- paragraph:5132 para_id:0000140C style:Normal -->
And then the mask she always wore—the precision, the control, the impossible standards—slipped completely away.

<!-- paragraph:5133 para_id:0000140D style:Normal -->
He was still looking at her when she reached for him.

<!-- paragraph:5134 para_id:0000140E style:Normal -->
The space between them closed as though the Shroud itself drew them together. His breath brushed her cheek, warm against the cold rising from her skin, and when their mouths met it was sharp and desperate and absolutely real. For a moment there was no duty, no lineage, no fear—only the raw, impossible relief of connection, the sense that for once she wasn’t alone inside herself.

<!-- paragraph:5136 para_id:00001410 style:Title -->
# 👫 Chapter 28

<!-- paragraph:5138 para_id:00001412 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 28

<!-- paragraph:5140 para_id:00001414 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5141 para_id:00001415 style:Normal -->
Davian couldn’t believe any of it—what he’d done, where he was, what he’d let himself feel. He had followed that thin golden thread out of the darkness of his own mind and straight through the citadel, pulled by something he didn’t have the strength or the will to resist. He’d thrown away every ounce of self-regulation, every plan, every lie, knowing full well it was selfish—but needing that connection to keep the dark from swallowing him whole.

<!-- paragraph:5142 para_id:00001416 style:Normal -->
And then she had kissed him. Or he’d kissed her. He couldn’t tell anymore.

<!-- paragraph:5143 para_id:00001417 style:Normal -->
All he knew was the shock of it—her mouth against his, the heat of her breath, the cold shimmer of her Shroud brushing his skin. It felt like her kiss was turning him into a real person, anchoring him to the world, forcing his mind back into his body. The courtyard, the damage, the danger—everything fell away under the weight of her, the reality of her.

<!-- paragraph:5144 para_id:00001418 style:Normal -->
When they broke apart for air, he was smiling—actually smiling—up at her. A real one, without the mask, without the fear, with all his responsibilities and obligations knocked clean out of his head. He could almost see the golden thread again, a line of fire between them that didn’t belong to anything he’d ever understood. It felt like it ran straight through her chest into his, something bright enough to cut the void open.

<!-- paragraph:5145 para_id:00001419 style:Normal -->
She smiled back.

<!-- paragraph:5146 para_id:0000141A style:Normal -->
And then—Darkness pulsed at the edge of his vision. Like a hand closing over his eyes. A voice—too close, too real—spoke out of the night beside him:

<!-- paragraph:5147 para_id:0000141B style:Normal -->
Will you deal with her first… or leave her until the end—when it hurts the most?

<!-- paragraph:5148 para_id:0000141C style:Normal -->
Davian jerked violently, scrambling back on the stone as if something had grabbed him. His heart slammed against his ribs; the world tilted, cold and black. He whipped his head around, searching for the source, breath stuttering, the taste of ink and iron sharp on his tongue.

<!-- paragraph:5149 para_id:0000141D style:Normal -->
Maya pushed up onto her elbows, confusion knitting her brow. “Arrek—what’s wrong?”

<!-- paragraph:5150 para_id:0000141E style:Normal -->
The name cut into him like the edge of a knife. Too sharp. Too wrong. It tangled with the echo of that voice still curling out of the dark behind his ear. He flinched as if she’d struck him. A name that wasn’t his. A name he’d stolen. A name belonging to someone he’d killed.

<!-- paragraph:5151 para_id:0000141F style:Normal -->
A sick, wrenching twist went through him. What am I doing?

<!-- paragraph:5152 para_id:00001420 style:Normal -->
The darkness surged again, encircling the edges of his vision. She felt suddenly far away, like he was staring down a corridor stretching longer and longer with every breath, her shape distorted by distance and fear. His heart was hammering hard enough to bruise his ribs.

<!-- paragraph:5153 para_id:00001421 style:Normal -->
This is all a lie. None of this is real. He didn’t realize he’d spoken until his own voice tore out of him: “He said this would happen—he knew this would happen—”

<!-- paragraph:5154 para_id:00001422 style:Normal -->
“Arrek—who are you talking about?” Maya’s concern sharpened. “Harvick?”

<!-- paragraph:5155 para_id:00001423 style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head in a frantic, tiny motion and raised his hands as though trying to block her out, to shut out her face before it carved him open. His vision pulsed black at the edges, his breath too shallow, too fast.

<!-- paragraph:5156 para_id:00001424 style:Normal -->
He stared at his hands—at the faint, fading lines of the Ink Boon etched along his skin. The marks were almost invisible in the half-light now, decaying hour by hour. The clock was ticking down, every beat closer to annihilation—his, his family’s—his throat tightened until he couldn’t swallow. He couldn’t breathe.

<!-- paragraph:5157 para_id:00001425 style:Normal -->
Davian lurched to his feet. “I can’t do this,” he said, voice shaking. “This is what he wants—this is all connected somehow—”

<!-- paragraph:5158 para_id:00001426 style:Normal -->
Maya stood quickly too. “What are you talking about?”

<!-- paragraph:5159 para_id:00001427 style:Normal -->
He looked at her like she was suddenly a stranger, or worse—like she was part of the trap closing around him. “This is all… you’re just making it worse.”

<!-- paragraph:5160 para_id:00001428 style:Normal -->
A flicker of hurt crossed her face. “What is wrong with you? Let me help you.”

<!-- paragraph:5161 para_id:00001429 style:Normal -->
For one breath—one suspended, trembling heartbeat—he almost told her. It rose in his throat like something trying to escape. The truth. His name. The whole fragile, rotting mess of it.

<!-- paragraph:5162 para_id:0000142A style:Normal -->
And then the voice came again, a whisper curling out of the darkness behind him, sliding cold along his spine: You’re a thief. A murderer. She’ll hate you. She’ll kill you.

<!-- paragraph:5163 para_id:0000142B style:Normal -->
He squeezed his eyes shut and shouted louder than he meant to—desperate to blot the voice out— “Why does everyone keep trying to help me? You can’t fix everything!”

<!-- paragraph:5164 para_id:0000142C style:Normal -->
Maya recoiled as though struck. The hurt wasn’t masked this time. It sat raw on her face, bright and bewildered.

<!-- paragraph:5165 para_id:0000142D style:Normal -->
Davian staggered back, and the ink swallowed him again—soft and numbing this time, a dark tide rising up to carry him away from her, away from the truth. It was almost refreshing, that distance. Like sinking into cold water. I’ll be gone, and she’ll never know who I really was… She doesn’t even know who I am now.

<!-- paragraph:5166 para_id:0000142E style:Normal -->
“I don’t know what’s happening to you,” Maya said, her voice tight but steady, “but anything can be fixed.”

<!-- paragraph:5167 para_id:0000142F style:Normal -->
The words hit him like claws. She didn’t understand. She couldn’t.

<!-- paragraph:5168 para_id:00001430 style:Normal -->
His breathing stuttered. Paranoia threaded itself through the ink—she knows, she knows, she’s going to kill me like she killed that assassin, like she burned those soldiers—

<!-- paragraph:5169 para_id:00001431 style:Normal -->
He heard himself yelling, but it felt distant, not quite attached to his mouth: “You Rezins think you can fix everything with magic and power and fire and lightning, but everything is still broken out there—everything—and some things are too broken to be fixed!”

<!-- paragraph:5170 para_id:00001432 style:Normal -->
And then he was running. Just turning and running, boots scraping against the cracked courtyard stone, the air cold on his teeth.

<!-- paragraph:5171 para_id:00001433 style:Normal -->
Behind him Maya shouted, “Arrek!” That name again—like a knife twisting—“For the Creator’s sake, you’re not making any sense!”

<!-- paragraph:5172 para_id:00001434 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5173 para_id:00001435 style:Normal -->
Maya stood there for a moment, staring after him as he disappeared into the shadows of the courtyard. The echo of his footsteps faded, leaving only the faint cold of her Shroud around her.

<!-- paragraph:5174 para_id:00001436 style:Normal -->
One moment he had been right there with her—genuine, open in a way she’d never seen from him or almost anyone else. She’d felt the connection, like they had been sharing the same breath, the same pulse. And the next… the next he was saying things that didn’t make sense, looking at her with a darkness in his eyes she couldn’t decipher.

<!-- paragraph:5175 para_id:00001437 style:Normal -->
It was too much. Far too much for the day she’d already lived.

<!-- paragraph:5176 para_id:00001438 style:Normal -->
She felt the weight of exhaustion settle on her shoulders and sank back down onto the cold stones. Her legs folded under her with none of her usual precision, and she let herself sit there, hands braced on her knees, breath unsteady.

<!-- paragraph:5177 para_id:00001439 style:Normal -->
“What am I doing today that’s making everyone run away from me?” she whispered to no one. “Niall, Erynn… Arrek…”

<!-- paragraph:5178 para_id:0000143A style:Normal -->
The courtyard was silent again, broken stone still scattered from her earlier outburst. She stared down at the paving stones between her boots, the small cracks and flecks of ice still clinging to them. She didn’t move. She didn’t blink. She just… stared. Long enough that her breath’s condensation had time to disperse and gather again in slow cycles.

<!-- paragraph:5179 para_id:0000143B style:Normal -->
Aldira would know what to say. Probably she’d say it wasn’t Maya’s fault, that she shouldn’t take things personally, that other people’s choices weren’t a judgment on her worth or competence.

<!-- paragraph:5180 para_id:0000143C style:Normal -->
But then Arrek’s voice replayed in her mind—raw and angry: You Rezins think you can fix everything…

<!-- paragraph:5181 para_id:0000143D style:Normal -->
She closed her eyes. “I can fix everything,” she murmured. “I should be able to.”

<!-- paragraph:5182 para_id:0000143E style:Normal -->
If she just worked harder, thought faster, pushed herself further—then all of this chaos, this unraveling, would fall back into order. She could solve it. She had to.

<!-- paragraph:5183 para_id:0000143F style:Normal -->
The thought made her feel steadier. Directed. Like she had a reason to stand up. So she did. Her legs were trembling, but she forced herself upright, brushing frost from her palms.

<!-- paragraph:5184 para_id:00001440 style:Normal -->
Before she could think too hard about any of it, she turned toward the citadel interior and began walking. One foot in front of the other, through the empty halls still echoing from the day’s confrontation, toward Aldira’s office.

<!-- paragraph:5185 para_id:00001441 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5186 para_id:00001442 style:Normal -->
Davian was already in the city by the time he realized where his feet had taken him. Not the streets—those were too exposed, too visible—but the alleys between the stacked stone buildings, where the lantern light didn’t quite reach and shadows layered thick enough to hide in.

<!-- paragraph:5187 para_id:00001443 style:Normal -->
He barely remembered speaking to the guards at the gate. Their faces blurred together in his mind. He couldn’t recall what he’d said to put them off—just a smear of words, a half-constructed excuse spoken through shaking breath.

<!-- paragraph:5188 para_id:00001444 style:Normal -->
None of it mattered. He was out. He was away.

<!-- paragraph:5189 para_id:00001445 style:Normal -->
But every time he blinked he saw Maya’s face again—her expression collapsing from connection to confusion to hurt. And he kept telling himself it was better this way. Better that he ended it now, ripped it apart early, before it could get any worse. Before he could do something unforgivable or she could look at him and understand what he was.

<!-- paragraph:5190 para_id:00001446 style:Normal -->
He shuddered. He had almost told her. Actually told her everything.

<!-- paragraph:5191 para_id:00001447 style:Normal -->
And he could imagine it too clearly—her hurt snapping into rage, that cold Shroud flaring bright enough to scour him from existence. And it wouldn’t just be his death. The Rezins would retaliate. The Harbingers would retaliate. The ripples would spread until everything he cared about was ash.

<!-- paragraph:5192 para_id:00001448 style:Normal -->
What is it that I care about again? The ink gathered around him, thick and familiar, numbing. He let himself drift inside it, footfalls soft on the uneven ground.

<!-- paragraph:5193 para_id:00001449 style:Normal -->
Not himself. I should have just let her kill me.

<!-- paragraph:5194 para_id:0000144A style:Normal -->
The ink pulsed around him, lapping at the edges of his thoughts like it wanted to pull him under again, dissolve him back into the quiet where nothing hurt and nothing mattered. He forced himself to think.

<!-- paragraph:5195 para_id:0000144B style:Normal -->
I care about… my family… my brother and sister. And my parents. And Selwin and Maya… He stopped walking. A cold tremor went up his spine.

<!-- paragraph:5196 para_id:0000144C style:Normal -->
Wait. That’s not right.

<!-- paragraph:5197 para_id:0000144D style:Normal -->
He shook his head hard, pressing a hand to the rough stone wall beside him, trying to breathe through the static rising in his chest. He started walking again, the alleys narrowing, the darkness thickening until it felt almost physical against his skin. Then he looked up and froze.

<!-- paragraph:5198 para_id:0000144E style:Normal -->
The mismatched, weather-stained manor rose out of the dark like a crooked silhouette—patchwork stone, sagging balcony, the faint glint of old nails hammered into the frame.

<!-- paragraph:5199 para_id:0000144F style:Normal -->
Am I already home?

<!-- paragraph:5200 para_id:00001450 style:Normal -->
A voice called down from the wall, barely a shadow cut out against the sky. “Who’s that?”

<!-- paragraph:5201 para_id:00001451 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t need to see the shape to know a crossbow was leveled at him—center mass, steady, waiting for a wrong move.

<!-- paragraph:5202 para_id:00001452 style:Normal -->
He didn’t speak. He just raised both hands slowly, sleeves falling back to expose the faint remains of his Ink Boons—still banded in uneven threads across his forearms. Antithesis gleamed cold in one hand, catching the strip of moonlight that slipped through the cloud cover.

<!-- paragraph:5203 para_id:00001453 style:Normal -->
A muffled curse came from the wall. “—wait there.” Then the slap of boots running off.

<!-- paragraph:5204 para_id:00001454 style:Normal -->
Davian lowered his hands and stood almost perfectly still. He didn’t know how long he waited—seconds, minutes—just the hollow thumping of his heart and the distant hum of the city’s night air.

<!-- paragraph:5205 para_id:00001455 style:Normal -->
The gate yawned open at last, and Kelyra shot out, a narrow flare of movement in the dark.
 “What are you doing?” she hissed, grabbing his sleeve and dragging him inside with more strength than her frame suggested.

<!-- paragraph:5206 para_id:00001456 style:Normal -->
He let her. There was something strangely easy about being pulled instead of choosing.

<!-- paragraph:5207 para_id:00001457 style:Normal -->
When the inner door clicked shut, she spun him around to face her. Her eyes were wide, bloodshot from exhaustion or relief—he couldn’t tell.

<!-- paragraph:5208 para_id:00001458 style:Normal -->
“I haven’t heard from you in weeks, Davian—we thought you were dead—and now you just show up at the gate? Is someone following you? Do you have it?”

<!-- paragraph:5209 para_id:00001459 style:Normal -->
“It’s fine,” he said. His voice sounded wrong even to his own ears. “No one followed me. I don’t have it. I just needed… answers.”

<!-- paragraph:5210 para_id:0000145A style:Normal -->
“Answers?” she snapped. “What answers could you possibly—”

<!-- paragraph:5211 para_id:0000145B style:Normal -->
Davian reached inward, through the ink—and the ink reached back. Not the comforting numbness this time, but the reflexive need to shield himself, to brace for impact, to be something other than breakable.

<!-- paragraph:5212 para_id:0000145C style:Normal -->
With a small, trembling motion, he lifted his arm. It was encased in shimmering force.

<!-- paragraph:5213 para_id:0000145D style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5214 para_id:0000145E style:Normal -->
Aldira’s office was one of the few places in the citadel that still showed signs of life at this hour. Light spilled out in a thin, warm line from under the door—proof Maya wasn’t the only one still awake, still working, still trying to hold the day together.

<!-- paragraph:5215 para_id:0000145F style:Normal -->
She didn’t bother knocking. She pushed the door open, and Aldira actually startled—just for a heartbeat, her quill pausing mid-stroke, eyes sharpening. Then her composure snapped back into place, fast and precise as a blade sheathing. Maya would have missed it if she hadn’t been watching her closely. Though she was fairly certain Aldira had shoved a few papers under a larger stack.

<!-- paragraph:5216 para_id:00001460 style:Normal -->
Those hawklike eyes swept over her in an instant, reading posture, affect, Shroud, everything. “What’s wrong?” Aldira asked.

<!-- paragraph:5217 para_id:00001461 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t have an answer. Not one she could form. Her throat felt scraped raw, her thoughts half-fogged, half-burning.

<!-- paragraph:5218 para_id:00001462 style:Normal -->
She closed the door behind her, crossed the room without thinking, and sank into the chair in the far corner. For a moment she just stared at the floor, grounding herself in the sensation of sitting, of breathing.

<!-- paragraph:5219 para_id:00001463 style:Normal -->
It was only then that she realized she was still wearing her ceremonial battle garments from earlier—embellished hem, embroidered crest, stiff collar meant to project authority. The same clothes she’d worn into the High Clade meeting believing she might have to bring fire and frost down on all of them. The same clothes in which Niall had judged her and found her lacking.

<!-- paragraph:5220 para_id:00001464 style:Normal -->
She felt like a child playing dress-up. A child pretending at power, pretending at certainty, pretending she wasn’t unraveling.

<!-- paragraph:5221 para_id:00001465 style:Normal -->
Aldira hadn’t repeated the question. She was waiting, silent and steady.

<!-- paragraph:5222 para_id:00001466 style:Normal -->
So Maya just said the first thing that surfaced, the thought that cut through everything else like a blade:

<!-- paragraph:5223 para_id:00001467 style:Normal -->
“Do you still have Arrek’s files?” Her voice was thin, almost hoarse. “The ones from Sorevin, when you vetted him?”

<!-- paragraph:5224 para_id:00001468 style:Normal -->
Aldira studied her for a moment, the tiniest shift of her eyes—the calculation, the concern, the quiet understanding she never voiced.

<!-- paragraph:5225 para_id:00001469 style:Normal -->
“I think I have an idea why you might want those…” she said slowly. “But what is this about?”

<!-- paragraph:5226 para_id:0000146A style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “Can I just… please see them?”

<!-- paragraph:5227 para_id:0000146B style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t press. She didn’t ask a single follow-up question. She simply rose from her chair, moved to a side cabinet, and began sorting through one of the heavy drawers with practiced efficiency. Papers rustled, folders slid, metal clips clinked softly.

<!-- paragraph:5228 para_id:0000146C style:Normal -->
Finally she drew out a leather-bound stack tied with plain twine—the Sorevin vetting packet. She paged through it with the same careful thoroughness she applied to everything, then selected a single sheet of paper. She placed it in Maya’s hands.

<!-- paragraph:5229 para_id:0000146D style:Normal -->
It was written in the neat, deliberate hand of a Low Clade house scribe:

<!-- paragraph:5230 para_id:0000146E style:Normal -->
Arrek Hadrin. Twenty-six years of age. Male. Brown hair. Average height. Glyphwright, guild-licensed, with approximately fourteen years of experience including his apprenticeship. References provided by Rellen Jastor and Sera Vadrin, both affiliated with the Sorevin contractor network.

<!-- paragraph:5231 para_id:0000146F style:Normal -->
No reference available from the master under whom he apprenticed; master deceased six years. No criminal history on record, though one bar altercation was reported seven years ago with no charges filed. No surviving primary relatives; mother deceased eighteen years, father unlisted. Two surviving secondary relatives, cousins, presently debt-contracted to Jorra Halvek, fulfilling obligations through standard post-disbursement wage garnishment procedures.

<!-- paragraph:5232 para_id:00001470 style:Normal -->
Subject displays no indications of financial instability beyond ordinary Low Clade precarity and no evidence of entanglements that would render him especially vulnerable to bribery, coercion, or subversive influence.

<!-- paragraph:5233 para_id:00001471 style:Normal -->
At the bottom of the page, in a slanted, hurried scrawl—barely legible in places, ink unevenly pooled—another hand had added:

<!-- paragraph:5234 para_id:00001472 style:Normal -->
good at hitting things w. a sword but doesn’t really know what he’s doing. kind of moody but seems like a nice guy. i’ll keep an eye on him. — harvick

<!-- paragraph:5235 para_id:00001473 style:Normal -->
Maya looked up from the papers, throat tight. Aldira was already watching her, expression steady.

<!-- paragraph:5236 para_id:00001474 style:Normal -->
“As you know,” Aldira said, her voice calm and even, “I asked Harvick to talk to him casually after the assassination attempt. We trusted the Registry—its readings are flawless—and we knew the assassin only slipped in during the brief window before it was re-enabled. But I still did a second pass through the Sorevin documents to flag anyone who stood out.”

<!-- paragraph:5237 para_id:00001475 style:Normal -->
She tapped the edge of the dossier with one finger.

<!-- paragraph:5238 para_id:00001476 style:Normal -->
“This Arrek had the fewest references. The only one without a master’s recommendation—though, in fairness, there was a reasonable explanation for that. My initial guess was that he’d gotten someone who owed him a favor to bump him forward in Sorevin’s queue. That’s not unusual. That’s how business works. And Sorevin would never recommend someone truly unqualified.”

<!-- paragraph:5239 para_id:00001477 style:Normal -->
Aldira leaned back slightly, her eyes narrowing in thought.

<!-- paragraph:5240 para_id:00001478 style:Normal -->
“But we were all a little on edge after that assassin appeared. So I asked Harvick to check in on him. Harvick said he seemed like a nice kid, a bit moody, nothing concerning. And that was that.”

<!-- paragraph:5241 para_id:00001479 style:Normal -->
Maya stared down at the papers again, something cold and unsettled twisting beneath her ribs.

<!-- paragraph:5242 para_id:0000147A style:Normal -->
“But Sorevin’s dead now,” she said quietly. “Someone killed them the day after they submitted their recommendations. They could have gotten this information—slipped someone in.”

<!-- paragraph:5243 para_id:0000147B style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t flinch. “Of course we thought of that. That’s how they got the assassin in at all. But the Registry ensures that as long as everyone is properly recorded—names, auras, signatures—then it simply isn’t possible for someone to be anyone other than who they claim to be.”

<!-- paragraph:5244 para_id:0000147C style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “What if they got in earlier? What if they changed information in the packet before Sorevin sent it?”

<!-- paragraph:5245 para_id:0000147D style:Normal -->
Aldira gave her a long, measured look. “And then they come back around and kill the Sorevins the next day? To what end? The point of killing them was to sever the trail, not to make it conspicuous.” She tapped the dossier again. “Regardless, all the names here were cross-referenced with the glyphwright guild. Even if something in Sorevin’s notes had been altered, the registered workers are still the registered workers.” A slight tilt of her head. “And you and I both know it’s not possible to give a fake name to the Registry.”

<!-- paragraph:5246 para_id:0000147E style:Normal -->
She spread her hands in a small, final gesture. “This is a dead end. Arrek is who he says he is.”

<!-- paragraph:5247 para_id:0000147F style:Normal -->
The words hit Maya like a wall. Her chest tightened with the same frustration that had been building all day—layers of it, stacking and twisting until she could barely breathe around it.

<!-- paragraph:5248 para_id:00001480 style:Normal -->
“Then this doesn’t make any sense,” she said, her voice sharper than she meant.

<!-- paragraph:5249 para_id:00001481 style:Normal -->
She stood abruptly, the legs of the chair scraping across the floor. That restless, urgent need to fix something—to solve something—snapped back into her with painful clarity.

<!-- paragraph:5250 para_id:00001482 style:Normal -->
“I’m going to the Registry,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:5251 para_id:00001483 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s brows rose, but she didn’t stop her—she simply watched as Maya strode toward the door, the dossier still trembling faintly in her hand.

<!-- paragraph:5252 para_id:00001484 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5253 para_id:00001485 style:Normal -->
Kelyra had pulled Davian into one of the back rooms—one of the little bolt-holes that could be used when trouble came too close. She shut the door fast, barred it, and then turned back to him with a look that was equal parts fear and fury.

<!-- paragraph:5254 para_id:00001486 style:Normal -->
Davian was still standing, still shaking faintly, the glow along his arm guttering unevenly.

<!-- paragraph:5255 para_id:00001487 style:Normal -->
“You don’t seem as surprised by this as you should be,” he said. His voice scraped out low. “What are you keeping from me?”

<!-- paragraph:5256 para_id:00001488 style:Normal -->
Something flashed across her face—too quick to name. Then she shoved it down and let anger cover it.

<!-- paragraph:5257 para_id:00001489 style:Normal -->
“I told you what you needed to know,” she said sharply. “So you could become what you needed to be.”

<!-- paragraph:5258 para_id:0000148A style:Normal -->
“And what do I need to be?” Davian asked.

<!-- paragraph:5259 para_id:0000148B style:Normal -->
Kelyra stepped toward him, jaw tight. “Someone who can stand up for all of us against the Clades. Someone who can bring power back to the people they’ve trampled for generations. That’s what our family is. That’s what we’ve been training for.”

<!-- paragraph:5260 para_id:0000148C style:Normal -->
He reached into his coat, pulled out Antithesis, and drove it down with a hard motion. The blade punched straight through the flimsy wooden table, the surface splitting around it.

<!-- paragraph:5261 para_id:0000148D style:Normal -->
“Yeah?” he said, breath ragged. “And what about this? Some sacred weapon passed down through our noble line?” He shook his head. “I saw Archeon tools up close. Their zanwork looks like it could’ve come from the same bloody workshop.”

<!-- paragraph:5262 para_id:0000148E style:Normal -->
Kelyra went still.

<!-- paragraph:5263 para_id:0000148F style:Normal -->
Davian kept going, voice shaking with exhaustion and disgust and fear. “And when they saw the Ink Boon—when they saw me—they acted like they recognized me. Like I was one of theirs.” His hand clenched around the hilt. “How are we supposed to be fighting the High Clades when it looks like we’re working for them?”

<!-- paragraph:5264 para_id:00001490 style:Normal -->
That landed. Kelyra’s expression cracked open—shock, real shock, widening her eyes.

<!-- paragraph:5265 para_id:00001491 style:Normal -->
“You saw Archeon magi?” she whispered. “And they knew who you were?”

<!-- paragraph:5266 para_id:00001492 style:Normal -->
“Stop keeping secrets,” Davian said, the words tearing out of him before he could temper them. “How am I supposed to know I’m on the right side if I don’t even know what side I’m on?”

<!-- paragraph:5267 para_id:00001493 style:Normal -->
A sharp knock hit the door.

<!-- paragraph:5268 para_id:00001494 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stiffened, shot Davian a warning look, then moved to the bolt. She cracked it open.

<!-- paragraph:5269 para_id:00001495 style:Normal -->
Jalen stepped inside.

<!-- paragraph:5270 para_id:00001496 style:Normal -->
Davian’s father looked… smaller than usual. Drawn-out. Hollow-eyed. The kind of exhaustion that didn’t come from lack of sleep but from months—years—of bearing too much.

<!-- paragraph:5271 para_id:00001497 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s gaze drifted to the dagger driven straight through the table. He walked over, wrapped his hand around the hilt, and pulled it free with a quiet scrape of wood fibers. For a moment he just looked at it—the familiar blade, the weight of it—before lowering himself into a chair with a long, strained breath.

<!-- paragraph:5272 para_id:00001498 style:Normal -->
“Look,” Jalen said, voice low. “I understand that you’re angry. I understand why. But there are some secrets too dangerous to tell anyone. Even you.” He rubbed a hand across his face. “It isn’t your job to piece this puzzle together. It’s your job to trust that we’re doing the work to keep everyone safe.”

<!-- paragraph:5273 para_id:00001499 style:Normal -->
Davian barked out something that might’ve been a laugh if it hadn’t cracked in the middle. “Keeping everyone safe? What about the… the people who work for the Rezins?”

<!-- paragraph:5274 para_id:0000149A style:Normal -->
Kelyra turned on him, sharp and incredulous. “You spend a few weeks in their citadel and suddenly you care about them?” She jabbed a finger toward the lines of ink along his arm. “You feel like you belong there?”

<!-- paragraph:5275 para_id:0000149B style:Normal -->
Davian looked at the Ink Boon that held Arrek’s aura. It was faded, its integrity coming apart at the edges. No amount of illusion or will could hide the truth now.

<!-- paragraph:5276 para_id:0000149C style:Normal -->
“You can’t keep that identity up forever,” Kelyra said, her voice brittle with anger—no, fear. “You aren’t actually Arrek.”

<!-- paragraph:5277 para_id:0000149D style:Normal -->
She stepped closer, looking him dead in the eyes.

<!-- paragraph:5278 para_id:0000149E style:Normal -->
“You killed Arrek,” she said, quietly brutal. “And you did it so you could save your real family.”

<!-- paragraph:5279 para_id:0000149F style:Normal -->
Davian staggered back a half-step, the room closing around him—Kelyra’s words, the glow of the failing Boon, the blade on the table—all blurring together. Before he could find something solid to hold onto, Jalen spoke again, quieter this time, but somehow heavier.

<!-- paragraph:5280 para_id:000014A0 style:Normal -->
“There have been murmurs,” he said. “From our own membership.”

<!-- paragraph:5281 para_id:000014A1 style:Normal -->
Davian’s stomach dropped.

<!-- paragraph:5282 para_id:000014A2 style:Normal -->
“Murmurs that we aren’t fit to lead anymore. That all the danger we’ve brought them—these last few jobs, these close calls—hasn’t come with enough payout. Enough protection.” Jalen’s mouth tightened. “And when we last met with the Harbingers… they made it clear. Again. How easy it would be for them to support someone else if a Trial of Dominion were called against your mother.”

<!-- paragraph:5283 para_id:000014A3 style:Normal -->
The words detonated inside Davian before he could brace for them. He knew exactly what that meant. Someone else receiving a Boon. Someone else given the power to challenge his mother. Someone else killing her and taking leadership for themselves—just like Kelyra had done herself so many years ago.

<!-- paragraph:5284 para_id:000014A4 style:Normal -->
Jalen exhaled, steady but brittle. “If we don’t deliver results—”

<!-- paragraph:5285 para_id:000014A5 style:Normal -->
Before he knew what he was doing, Maya’s voice flickered back through his mind—her face twisted with concern, reaching for him—let me help you—and the words tumbled out of him raw and unplanned: “Maybe the Rezins can help us.”

<!-- paragraph:5286 para_id:000014A6 style:Normal -->
Silence snapped across the room. Both of his parents stared at him, shock and incredulity written across their faces.

<!-- paragraph:5287 para_id:000014A7 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stepped in close again, eyes sharp with disbelief. “Don’t make me laugh. The High Clades only work for themselves. They manipulate and crush whoever’s beneath them. That’s their nature.”

<!-- paragraph:5288 para_id:000014A8 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed. “Maybe the Rezins are different.”

<!-- paragraph:5289 para_id:000014A9 style:Normal -->
“Oh yes,” Kelyra said, voice cutting. “The Rezins. The family whose patriarch’s reign of terror is so oppressive that even the other High Clades at least pretend to support whatever he proposes. And he Presented a daughter who made it clear she will be exactly like him.”

<!-- paragraph:5290 para_id:000014AA style:Normal -->
She jabbed a finger into his chest.

<!-- paragraph:5291 para_id:000014AB style:Normal -->
“You’re going to stake your life—my life—your father’s life—your brothers’ and sisters’ lives—on them being ‘different’? On some fantasy you cooked up because you’ve been in their halls breathing their air?”

<!-- paragraph:5292 para_id:000014AC style:Normal -->
Davian flinched, but she didn’t stop.

<!-- paragraph:5293 para_id:000014AD style:Normal -->
“They’ll fool you like Selina was fooled,” she hissed. “Deception and manipulation are in their blood. And anyone who falls for it—deserves it.”

<!-- paragraph:5294 para_id:000014AE style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t have an answer. His mind was a blur of jagged edges—Selina, Maya, Arrek’s name on Maya’s lips, the Trial of Dominion, Kelyra’s words like arrows punching through him one after another.

<!-- paragraph:5295 para_id:000014AF style:Normal -->
He dragged in a breath, too sharp, too thin. Then he reached inward—deep into that now-familiar inky darkness where sensation cut out, where thought dissolved, where only raw reflex lived. The protective reflex he’d used more and more these past weeks. The one that severed every connection to the world so he could survive it.

<!-- paragraph:5296 para_id:000014B0 style:Normal -->
His Shroud snapped into place around him. Dim, wavering light refracted off the walls of the small room, shimmering across Jalen’s tired face and Kelyra’s set jaw. It wasn’t cold like Maya’s. It was something else—like warped glass, like a shield pulled from a void he didn’t fully understand.

<!-- paragraph:5297 para_id:000014B1 style:Normal -->
He shoved the memory of her Shroud away—of the warmth of her body against the cold of it, that impossible moment in the courtyard. He didn’t know what it meant. He couldn’t know.

<!-- paragraph:5298 para_id:000014B2 style:Normal -->
“Fine,” he said, voice low, distorted by the shimmer around him. “Deception and manipulation might be in their blood, but it’s clearly in ours too.”

<!-- paragraph:5299 para_id:000014B3 style:Normal -->
He looked down at himself—at the impossible glow wrapping his frame.

<!-- paragraph:5300 para_id:000014B4 style:Normal -->
“Why can I do this?” Davian demanded. “It’s not even supposed to be possible for someone my age.”

<!-- paragraph:5301 para_id:000014B5 style:Normal -->
Jalen let out a long, frayed breath. “You always knew magic was in your blood…”

<!-- paragraph:5302 para_id:000014B6 style:Normal -->
“But you told me it was different,” Davian snapped. “Special. That we were some kind of noble bloodline destined to resist the High Clades.”

<!-- paragraph:5303 para_id:000014B7 style:Normal -->
Kelyra threw her hands up, furious and cornered all at once. “Fine,” she spat. “I’m from Archeon. Is that what you wanted to hear?”

<!-- paragraph:5304 para_id:000014B8 style:Normal -->
The shock of it slammed into Davian so hard he lost his grip on the Shroud. It flickered—then collapsed in on itself, melting away in a slow, dim ripple.

<!-- paragraph:5305 para_id:000014B9 style:Normal -->
Kelyra looked suddenly older, her anger burning down to something rawer.

<!-- paragraph:5306 para_id:000014BA style:Normal -->
“I can’t believe you’re making me re-live this,” she said quietly. “But I was part of their breeding programs.”

<!-- paragraph:5307 para_id:000014BB style:Normal -->
Davian’s breath caught.

<!-- paragraph:5308 para_id:000014BC style:Normal -->
“I never manifested a Shroud,” she continued, voice sharp but trembling at the edges. “No magic at all. Even when I knew it was there, and I could feel it in my mind, I shoved it down and locked it away. So eventually they stopped watching me as closely, and I ran. They didn’t even know I was pregnant with Selina.” Her eyes flicked away. “But I knew if they found out, they’d lock me up and take her from me. So I ran, and I built a life here, away from their towers, safe in the shadows.”

<!-- paragraph:5309 para_id:000014BD style:Normal -->
She laughed once—a bitter, broken sound. “But that wasn’t enough. The High Clades took Selina from me anyway. And that’s when I knew— the only way any of us would ever be safe is if I had power.”

<!-- paragraph:5310 para_id:000014BE style:Normal -->
Her gaze sharpened, fierce and bright and desperate.

<!-- paragraph:5311 para_id:000014BF style:Normal -->
“So I did what I had to do. I took the devil’s bargain. I carved out a place for us, and I waited.” She stepped closer, gripping the edge of the ruined table. “And now we’re so close. The Harbingers can give us the power we need—enough to burn it all down and build something new, where we don’t have to look over our shoulders every day.”

<!-- paragraph:5312 para_id:000014C0 style:Normal -->
The room went dead silent but for Davian’s uneven breathing. The ink around him lapped at his senses like a tide.

<!-- paragraph:5313 para_id:000014C1 style:Normal -->
Jalen rubbed his forehead, looking older than Davian had ever seen him. “I know this is a lot to take in. And just because we told you doesn’t mean it was a good idea.” He paused, choosing his words. “You and your mother can both use Subversion. And now you can manifest a Shroud. If Archeon really does know about this, we need to move quickly.”

<!-- paragraph:5314 para_id:000014C2 style:Normal -->
“Archeon would never let a prize like our family slip through their fingers if they knew where we were,” Jalen continued. “And that includes your brother and sister.” His jaw tightened. “The Harbingers might be using Archeon as another lever against us, but the Harbingers always fulfill their bargains—if they get exactly what they want. They’re the only reason we’ve gotten this far.”

<!-- paragraph:5315 para_id:000014C3 style:Normal -->
He looked Davian dead in the eyes, voice lowering. “You can’t stay long. You shouldn’t have come at all. But since you’re here… you should see Kael and Lirin. Remind yourself who your real family is.”

<!-- paragraph:5316 para_id:000014C4 style:Normal -->
Davian felt that line pierce somewhere deep—but he couldn’t tell whether it anchored him or split him open.

<!-- paragraph:5317 para_id:000014C5 style:Normal -->
Jalen stood, unbolted the door, and motioned for him to follow. Davian moved after him automatically, footsteps echoing in narrow hallways washed in low lamplight. The familiar creak of the floorboards, the scent of woodsmoke and dried herbs—it all felt strange, like returning to a memory instead of a home.

<!-- paragraph:5318 para_id:000014C6 style:Normal -->
They turned a corner and stepped into the living quarters. Kael and Lirin were in front of the small stone fireplace—Kael swinging a wooden sword wildly, Lirin blocking each sloppy strike with the calm, precision of someone two years older who knew she was better. Sparks of firelight flickered across their faces.

<!-- paragraph:5319 para_id:000014C7 style:Normal -->
Kael saw Davian first.

<!-- paragraph:5320 para_id:000014C8 style:Normal -->
His sword clattered to the floor as he gasped, eyes going huge. He didn’t even flinch when Lirin’s blade smacked into his arm.

<!-- paragraph:5321 para_id:000014C9 style:Normal -->
“Davian!” he yelled, voice cracking with joy.

<!-- paragraph:5322 para_id:000014CA style:Normal -->
He barreled across the room and latched onto Davian’s leg, burying his face against him. Lirin followed, more controlled, her steps small and careful—but the moment she reached him, a shy, relieved smile broke across her usually serious face. She wrapped her arms around him too.

<!-- paragraph:5323 para_id:000014CB style:Normal -->
Kael tilted his head up, eyes wide and bright. “You’ve been gone so long—Lirin thought maybe you'd never come back!”

<!-- paragraph:5324 para_id:000014CC style:Normal -->
Lirin crossed her small arms, cheeks coloring faintly. “I didn’t actually think that,” she muttered. “But I did feel worried.”

<!-- paragraph:5325 para_id:000014CD style:Normal -->
Kael immediately spoke over her, bouncing on the balls of his feet. “So you’re done with your mission? It must have been really important and big to take so long! Tell me—did you beat lots of bad guys? I hope so.”

<!-- paragraph:5326 para_id:000014CE style:Normal -->
His grin was huge, earnest, trusting.

<!-- paragraph:5327 para_id:000014CF style:Normal -->
And Davian felt the memory hit him—the shock in Arrek’s eyes as the knife slid between his ribs, how the body had slumped, heavy and warm and horribly human against him.

<!-- paragraph:5328 para_id:000014D0 style:Normal -->
His stomach twisted.

<!-- paragraph:5329 para_id:000014D1 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Davian said gently, “some missions aren’t just about beating bad guys. And you always have to be careful how you do it… because it’s not something you can take back.”

<!-- paragraph:5330 para_id:000014D2 style:Normal -->
Lirin nodded seriously, as though this made perfect sense. “I’m sure you did it right,” she said. “We’re glad you’re back.”

<!-- paragraph:5331 para_id:000014D3 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed. “Well… I actually still have something to do. But I wanted to visit you. Just in case…” He stopped himself, shook his head. “I just wanted to visit you. And I’ll be back for good soon.”

<!-- paragraph:5332 para_id:000014D4 style:Normal -->
Kael’s face fell for a heartbeat—small, honest sadness flickering through it—before he bolted away toward the fireplace. He snatched up his wooden sword and held it above his head with great ceremony.

<!-- paragraph:5333 para_id:000014D5 style:Normal -->
“Do you need a sword?” he asked proudly. “You can borrow mine!”

<!-- paragraph:5334 para_id:000014D6 style:Normal -->
For a moment, Davian saw another boy—Selwin—running across a different courtyard, offering thanks, offering trust he never should’ve given.

<!-- paragraph:5335 para_id:000014D7 style:Normal -->
Davian knelt down, meeting Kael’s excited eyes. “I wish this was a mission I could do with a wooden sword,” he said softly. “But I think I’ll have to stick with my knife.”

<!-- paragraph:5336 para_id:000014D8 style:Normal -->
Jalen stepped forward then, voice gentle but firm. “All right. Davian has to go now. But like he said—he’ll be back soon.” He rested a hand on Kael’s shoulder and guided both children away.

<!-- paragraph:5337 para_id:000014D9 style:Normal -->
Davian was still kneeling on the floor, the warmth of Kael’s hug fading from his leg, when Kelyra stepped quietly back into the room. Her expression had settled into something cooler now—focused, deliberate.

<!-- paragraph:5338 para_id:000014DA style:Normal -->
“I have something for you,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:5339 para_id:000014DB style:Normal -->
He looked up mutely, still half in the past, half in the ink.

<!-- paragraph:5340 para_id:000014DC style:Normal -->
She held out a small cloth bag, its weight slight but unmistakably rigid inside. Davian hesitated before taking it. The fabric felt too familiar beneath his fingers.

<!-- paragraph:5341 para_id:000014DD style:Normal -->
He loosened the drawstring and slid the object into his palm.

<!-- paragraph:5342 para_id:000014DE style:Normal -->
