A mask. His mask. The one that had been cracked during the fight with the Low Clade magus weeks ago. But now—smooth, mended, renewed. And marked with a symbol he didn’t recognize.

<!-- paragraph:5343 para_id:000014DF style:Normal -->
“I fixed it for you,” Kelyra said. Her voice was almost gentle. “You were right to mark yourself out as special. It’s fate that you are where you are, with the strength you need to finish your task.”

<!-- paragraph:5344 para_id:000014E0 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at the symbol, a knot tightening in his chest.

<!-- paragraph:5345 para_id:000014E1 style:Normal -->
“The symbol is different,” he said slowly. He remembered how self-important he’d been, carving the za for equilibrium—zhëza—onto the cheek of the mask, thinking balance meant something. Thinking it defined him.

<!-- paragraph:5346 para_id:000014E2 style:Normal -->
But the za on this mask wasn’t anything he recognized.

<!-- paragraph:5347 para_id:000014E3 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stepped closer and tapped the symbol with her finger. “This is who you are. You don’t belong with them.”

<!-- paragraph:5348 para_id:000014E4 style:Normal -->
Davian turned the mask in his hands, studying the unfamiliar strokes. “Why did you change it?”

<!-- paragraph:5349 para_id:000014E5 style:Normal -->
“Because we can’t bring about equality inside the system that crushed us,” she said. “Balance is a lie they sell to keep us obedient.” She pointed again to the za, her expression sharpening. “That is nïza. The za of negation.”

<!-- paragraph:5350 para_id:000014E6 style:Normal -->
Her voice lowered. “The only way people like us can have equality is if we bring everything down.”

<!-- paragraph:5351 para_id:000014E7 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5352 para_id:000014E8 style:Normal -->
The walk to the Registry felt like it took forever.

<!-- paragraph:5353 para_id:000014E9 style:Normal -->
Maya kept having to drag her attention back into her body—back to the corridor under her feet, the cold air brushing her skin—anything to stop her thoughts from spiraling back to the moment Arrek turned and ran from her. Each time the memory surfaced, her vision threatened to flicker at the edges, a pull toward that bright, unbearable place where the world unraveled.

<!-- paragraph:5354 para_id:000014EA style:Normal -->
The paper in her hand crumpled. She looked down and realized she had crushed it into a tight knot. She forced her fingers open and smoothed the page against her palm.

<!-- paragraph:5355 para_id:000014EB style:Normal -->
So little information. What did I really know about him?

<!-- paragraph:5356 para_id:000014EC style:Normal -->
She reached the Registry doors. The two guards stationed there straightened when they saw her—surprised, uncertain.

<!-- paragraph:5357 para_id:000014ED style:Normal -->
Maya drew herself up—trying to banish the thought of how Niall must see her like a child playing dress-up in her formal battle garb—and said, “Open the doors.”

<!-- paragraph:5358 para_id:000014EE style:Normal -->
They hesitated. Just for a heartbeat. And in that heartbeat she had a quick, sharp fantasy of them telling her to fetch Niall, to go get permission, to remember her place.

<!-- paragraph:5359 para_id:000014EF style:Normal -->
But after that heartbeat they moved to the heavy bronze handles. The doors groaned open. She stepped inside the Registry.

<!-- paragraph:5360 para_id:000014F0 style:Normal -->
The chamber spread out in a wide circle of pale stone, the walls etched with faintly luminescent sigils that pulsed in slow intervals. The desks were arranged in two arcs around the central platform that housed the aurametric array—usually buzzing with readings and soft magical resonance. At this hour only a handful of scribes remained, their lamps burning low, the ambient hum of the machinery quiet and steady. The guards along the perimeter glanced at her with confusion, then quickly looked away.

<!-- paragraph:5361 para_id:000014F1 style:Normal -->
“Out,” Maya said.

<!-- paragraph:5362 para_id:000014F2 style:Normal -->
The scribes froze. Then, after a few uneasy glances at one another, they began gathering their papers and withdrawing from the room.

<!-- paragraph:5363 para_id:000014F3 style:Normal -->
She kept her shoulders square, her chin lifted, every part of her posture rigid with willpower. She could not afford to look shaken—not here, not now.

<!-- paragraph:5364 para_id:000014F4 style:Normal -->
The guards lingered, uncertain.

<!-- paragraph:5365 para_id:000014F5 style:Normal -->
“All of you,” she added. “Leave.”

<!-- paragraph:5366 para_id:000014F6 style:Normal -->
They obeyed. Reluctantly, but without protest. The heavy Registry doors swung closed with a deep, echoing thud that rattled along the stone.

<!-- paragraph:5367 para_id:000014F7 style:Normal -->
Maya stood alone in the vast chamber, the intricate zanwork casting faint gold and silver light across the floor, her breath sounding louder than she wanted it to.

<!-- paragraph:5368 para_id:000014F8 style:Normal -->
She stepped up onto the central platform, the circular stone warm beneath her boots where the residual aurametric channels pulsed faintly. She drew in a breath and said—quieter than she meant to— “Arrek Hadrin. Locate.”

<!-- paragraph:5369 para_id:000014F9 style:Normal -->
The projection sphere at the platform’s center flickered—just a soft pulse of light through the citadel’s ghost-map—but nothing shifted, no aura-thread lit up, no signature pinpricked across the miniature city.

<!-- paragraph:5370 para_id:000014FA style:Normal -->
She frowned, straightening. “Arrek Hadrin,” she said again, louder this time. “Locate.”

<!-- paragraph:5371 para_id:000014FB style:Normal -->
Nothing.

<!-- paragraph:5372 para_id:000014FC style:Normal -->
Her pulse stuttered. She looked down at the dossier still trembling in her hand. Had he been using a fake name this whole time? But no—she’d repeated the name exactly as written, exactly as logged.

<!-- paragraph:5373 para_id:000014FD style:Normal -->
“Arrek Hadrin,” she said, jaw tightening. “Verify.”

<!-- paragraph:5374 para_id:000014FE style:Normal -->
A small light blinked on one of the wall stones to her right—confirmation of a matching entry. She crossed the room briskly, boots echoing in the empty Registry, and looked up at the inscribed name. Arrek Hadrin. Perfectly recorded. Perfectly valid.

<!-- paragraph:5375 para_id:000014FF style:Normal -->
So he was Registered—but not locatable within the citadel. He wasn’t here.

<!-- paragraph:5376 para_id:00001500 style:Normal -->
She let out a slow, exhausted breath and leaned back against the wall. Technically, there was nothing unusual about that. One of the quiet advantages of working for the Rezins was that you weren’t locked inside the citadel until your assignment ended; trusted contractors had freedom of movement.

<!-- paragraph:5377 para_id:00001501 style:Normal -->
It was one of the things that made the Rezins powerful—security without strangling their own efficiency. The other High Clades coveted it, envied it, resented it. A citadel with free-flowing movement and near-perfect magical surveillance was something no one else had managed.

<!-- paragraph:5378 para_id:00001502 style:Normal -->
For a moment—a foolish, wistful moment—she wished the Registry stretched across the whole city. Across all the High Clades. All their citadels. A single system, clean and total, where no one could vanish into an alley or cloak themselves behind a false name. Where she could find him. Where she could make sense of all of this.

<!-- paragraph:5379 para_id:00001503 style:Normal -->
She sank down onto the edge of the platform, the empty map of the citadel humming faintly above her like a heartbeat she couldn’t hear.

<!-- paragraph:5380 para_id:00001504 style:Normal -->
Maya lifted her hand and carved a small channel in the air with her mind, hardly more than a line of intent. A thin trickle responded—heat, drawn from the stone beneath her feet, rising in a faint shimmering thread and collecting in a small hovering sphere above her palm.

<!-- paragraph:5381 para_id:00001505 style:Normal -->
The shimmer snaked upward in coils, slowly pooling into a denser glow. She shaped a looping channel around it, coaxing the heat to fold back into itself, balancing the diffusion with the pull from below. A child’s exercise… or at least an exercise she’d made for herself when she was a child. Something to train precision, patience, control.

<!-- paragraph:5382 para_id:00001506 style:Normal -->
She kept the loop steady and looked down. A patch of condensation had formed on the stone floor—just cool enough to draw water from the air, not yet cold enough to freeze.

<!-- paragraph:5383 para_id:00001507 style:Normal -->
She lifted her other hand and began a second draw—another trickle of heat rising from the stone, spiraling into a second sphere above her palm. Two separate channels. Two separate structures of intent. Two loops. Two balances.

<!-- paragraph:5384 para_id:00001508 style:Normal -->
Maintaining both while holding her Shroud close but not flaring it took real effort—enough effort that it forced her fully into the present, every sense sharpened to keep the threads from tangling.

<!-- paragraph:5385 para_id:00001509 style:Normal -->
But the turmoil sat just outside her awareness, pressing against her concentration like rising pressure. The moment she acknowledged any of it—Niall’s distrust. Arrek’s sudden reversal. The High Clades turning against us—the thoughts slammed into her, a torrent breaking through a fragile dam.

<!-- paragraph:5386 para_id:0000150A style:Normal -->
Both heat-spheres burst outward in a muffled pop, scattering warm air across her face and sending her hair stirring.

<!-- paragraph:5387 para_id:0000150B style:Normal -->
She stared at her empty palms.

<!-- paragraph:5388 para_id:0000150C style:Normal -->
“I can’t even do a child’s exercise anymore,” she said aloud, voice low and shaking. “No wonder Niall is afraid to rely on me.”

<!-- paragraph:5389 para_id:0000150D style:Normal -->
Maya pressed both hands against her knees, trying to steady her breath, but it only made the trembling in her chest sharper. Her heart was climbing too fast, each beat landing higher than the last, and her breathing followed—quick, thin pulls of air she couldn’t quite slow.

<!-- paragraph:5390 para_id:0000150E style:Normal -->
She knew where this led. She knew the shape of the fall. The rising pressure, the narrowing of her vision, the sense of being pushed out of her own skin—And then it broke.

<!-- paragraph:5391 para_id:0000150F style:Normal -->
The world tilted, snapped sideways, folded inward—and she was hovering again. Outside her body.

<!-- paragraph:5392 para_id:00001510 style:Normal -->
The Registry chamber fell away beneath her like a hollow shell of stone and light. Around her and through her and beyond her stretched the threads—countless strands of shimmering potential, branching and twining in every direction that wasn’t a direction at all. They braided into spirals, diverged into rivers of possibility, intersected like lines on a sphere that wasn’t a sphere. Time, choice, consequence—everything lay layered atop everything else.

<!-- paragraph:5393 para_id:00001511 style:Normal -->
Her mind skidded across the comprehension of it, like a hand slipping on polished glass.

<!-- paragraph:5394 para_id:00001512 style:Normal -->
But she knew this place. This impossible vantage. This terrible clarity. The Skein.

<!-- paragraph:5395 para_id:00001513 style:Normal -->
The same place she’d glimpsed in dreams, in moments of terror, in the corners of the corrupted memories Aldira had given her—the ones stained with her mother’s unraveling thoughts.

<!-- paragraph:5396 para_id:00001514 style:Normal -->
And from somewhere in that shimmering lattice, from a memory that wasn’t hers but lived inside her anyway, she heard Elric’s voice—soft, lilting, too-close and too-far all at once: Every choice is a thread... Some are taut. Some are frayed. Some have already snapped. And some… some have teeth.

<!-- paragraph:5397 para_id:00001515 style:Normal -->
The threads around her pulsed, twisting in colors she had no names for.

<!-- paragraph:5398 para_id:00001516 style:Normal -->
And Maya felt herself sway in that vast, impossible space—her own thread vibrating like a plucked wire, flickering between futures she couldn’t hold onto long enough to understand.

<!-- paragraph:5399 para_id:00001517 style:Normal -->
She told herself—fiercely, breathlessly—that this was the place she could finally understand. Finally control what was happening. Here, nothing could escape her sight. Here, she could see everything.

<!-- paragraph:5400 para_id:00001518 style:Normal -->
The threads shimmered around her, endless, shifting, vibrating in patterns she wasn’t meant to perceive—but she reached anyway. She pushed her mind outward along the lattice, instinctively slipping between strands, flickering through branching possibilities with that speed of thought she’d been trained to sharpen since childhood. Faster. Faster. The branching futures blurred, blending into ribbons of motion.

<!-- paragraph:5401 para_id:00001519 style:Normal -->
She swept past a thousand versions of herself—speaking, fighting, falling, dying—so quickly they left only impressions. She skimmed along timelines where the citadel burned, where Niall fell, where darkness swallowed the High Clades whole. Too much, too fast.

<!-- paragraph:5402 para_id:0000151A style:Normal -->
Her vision strained. Her mind snapped taut like string. Then—a thread caught her mind’s eye. Gold-streaked, warm, vibrating in a pattern she recognized without knowing how.

<!-- paragraph:5403 para_id:0000151B style:Normal -->
She seized it.

<!-- paragraph:5404 para_id:0000151C style:Normal -->
And the blur resolved: Her and Arrek in the courtyard, the shared Shroud, the heat of his breath, the moment their mouths met. The pure, impossible connection that had made the whole world feel real again.

<!-- paragraph:5405 para_id:0000151D style:Normal -->
The thread pulsed—once, twice—And then it split. A second line veered away from it, dark and jagged, pulling violently into shadow. Arrek wrenched from her. Arrek running. Arrek swallowed by a darkness that felt alive.

<!-- paragraph:5406 para_id:0000151E style:Normal -->
Maya reached for it without thinking. She couldn’t lose him—not again, not without understanding, not without fixing it.

<!-- paragraph:5407 para_id:0000151F style:Normal -->
The moment her mind touched that dark thread, it snapped tight around her and she was pulled into the torrent.

<!-- paragraph:5408 para_id:00001520 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5409 para_id:00001521 style:Normal -->
Davian trudged through the tight, winding alleys, boots scraping against uneven stone, the air damp with night fog. The small cloth bag tied to his belt tapped lightly against his leg with each step—the mask inside shifting, the hard contours brushing through the fabric like a reminder. Or a weight.

<!-- paragraph:5410 para_id:00001522 style:Normal -->
Ahead, through gaps between leaning buildings, he could see it:
 the Rezin citadel tower spearing the sky, a black silhouette cutting through the moonlight. His vision tunneled toward it. The ink was creeping into his periphery again, blurring everything except that single, sharp objective. Every breath felt like it pulled him deeper into the narrowing corridor of necessity.

<!-- paragraph:5411 para_id:00001523 style:Normal -->
He reached the edge of the alley network, about to step out onto the road that led toward the gate—

<!-- paragraph:5412 para_id:00001524 style:Normal -->
Footsteps. Behind him. He spun instantly, Antithesis raised, his reflexes snapping taut.

<!-- paragraph:5413 para_id:00001525 style:Normal -->
A man stepped out of a recessed doorway Davian swore had been empty when he passed it moments earlier. A hood hid most of the man’s face, but a wide smile curved beneath the shadow, stretched too easily, too comfortably across his features.

<!-- paragraph:5414 para_id:00001526 style:Normal -->
Davian’s pulse thudded hard and fast.

<!-- paragraph:5415 para_id:00001527 style:Normal -->
“Who’s there?” he demanded.

<!-- paragraph:5416 para_id:00001528 style:Normal -->
The man tilted his head back into the moonlight. The hood slipped down. Davian froze. He knew this face—had seen it across a table weeks ago when he first volunteered, when he’d been searching for a way to save his family. And then impossibly, again, inside the Rezin citadel. The young Harbinger leader. But his face wasn’t the same now. Ink covered his skin in intricate, spiraling patterns, curling around his eyes, sweeping down his cheeks, webbing across every exposed inch like a living script. Beautiful, horrible, consuming.

<!-- paragraph:5417 para_id:00001529 style:Normal -->
Some part of Davian wanted to step back. Another part couldn’t move at all.

<!-- paragraph:5418 para_id:0000152A style:Normal -->
“You don’t need to threaten me,” Davian said sullenly, lowering Antithesis a fraction. “I’m already going to do what you want.”

<!-- paragraph:5419 para_id:0000152B style:Normal -->
The man’s smile widened, serene and bright as a false dawn. “I’m not here to threaten you,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:5420 para_id:0000152C style:Normal -->
His voice slipped through the air like something half-sung, half-whispered—too soft for the street, too certain for the dark. Davian felt it more than he heard it, a vibration that thrummed along the ink in his veins.

<!-- paragraph:5421 para_id:0000152D style:Normal -->
“I’m here to reward you,” the man went on, stepping closer with that slow, predatory grace. “To share in my joy. Do you feel it? The turning of the world? The thread you’ve plucked still singing in your hands?”

<!-- paragraph:5422 para_id:0000152E style:Normal -->
Davian ground his teeth. “I don’t need you to reward me. I need you to keep your end of the deal.”

<!-- paragraph:5423 para_id:0000152F style:Normal -->
When the man spoke again, the words didn’t feel like sound—they resonated through the Ink in Davian’s body, vibrating in the marrow behind his teeth. The sensation crawled up his spine, a sick echo telling him that what he heard was right, correct, the natural order of things.

<!-- paragraph:5424 para_id:00001530 style:Normal -->
“A deal?” he echoed. “Sweet child, there are no deals. There are only currents. And you’ve stepped into one far older than either of us. You aren’t negotiating with me. You’re drifting downstream.”

<!-- paragraph:5425 para_id:00001531 style:Normal -->
Davian’s grip tightened on Antithesis, the blade quivering. “Promise me,” he said. “If I do this—we’ll be able to destroy the High Clades.”

<!-- paragraph:5426 para_id:00001532 style:Normal -->
The man tilted his head back, inhaling the night as though tasting the shape of Davian’s fear.

<!-- paragraph:5427 para_id:00001533 style:Normal -->
“They are already ruined,” he murmured. “Hollowed. Rotted through the spine. A tower that still stands only because no one has breathed hard enough against it.”

<!-- paragraph:5428 para_id:00001534 style:Normal -->
His gaze dropped, sharp as a blade sliding free of its sheath.

<!-- paragraph:5429 para_id:00001535 style:Normal -->
“And she,” he added softly, “the girl of threads and fire… she is undone already. You felt it when she kissed you. She is breaking along every line.”

<!-- paragraph:5430 para_id:00001536 style:Normal -->
Davian staggered as though the words struck him physically. “You’re wrong,” he rasped. “I’m not going to hurt her. I won’t.”

<!-- paragraph:5431 para_id:00001537 style:Normal -->
The man stepped closer, almost tender in his amusement.

<!-- paragraph:5432 para_id:00001538 style:Normal -->
“You agreed to slit the storm’s throat,” he said. “To break the father—such a proud, fragile construct of lightning and fear—and to bring me the Heart. The world’s heart, the one locked in their vault like a caged animal.”

<!-- paragraph:5433 para_id:00001539 style:Normal -->
He leaned in, voice a whisper-lilt between music and threat.

<!-- paragraph:5434 para_id:0000153A style:Normal -->
“Do it, and the board clears. The game ends. You will have the power to grind the Clades to dust. To make the world quiet again. A sweet, clean silence.” His smile curved, reverent. “The last dance has already begun. I merely came to tell you the steps.”

<!-- paragraph:5435 para_id:0000153B style:Normal -->
Davian let out a shaky breath. “It doesn’t give me a lot of confidence,” he said, “that the fate of the world apparently rests on the shoulders of a lunatic.”

<!-- paragraph:5436 para_id:0000153C style:Normal -->
The man’s smile didn’t falter. If anything, it warmed.

<!-- paragraph:5437 para_id:0000153D style:Normal -->
“I take that as a compliment,” he murmured, stepping closer. “For what is madness, little shadow, but seeing the world as it truly is? Not the soft skin you cling to—but the deeper world beneath it.” He tilted his head, the ink on his cheeks rippling like script shifting positions. “Like biting into an apple.”

<!-- paragraph:5438 para_id:0000153E style:Normal -->
Before Davian could react, the man reached out and touched his temple with two fingers.

<!-- paragraph:5439 para_id:0000153F style:Normal -->
Davian’s breath locked in his throat. His vision narrowed instantly—sharply—until the man’s smile was the only thing left in the world, bright and serene and horrible in its certainty. Everything else receded into a tunnel of darkness.

<!-- paragraph:5440 para_id:00001540 style:Normal -->
And then something moved in that dark.

<!-- paragraph:5441 para_id:00001541 style:Normal -->
A thread. Thin, black, unmistakable. It wound forward through the narrow corridor of his sight, pulsing with each beat of his heart.

<!-- paragraph:5442 para_id:00001542 style:Normal -->
“Follow the thread,” the man whispered. “The dark thread that is you. And tug, tug, tug—until the whole tapestry unravels.”

<!-- paragraph:5443 para_id:00001543 style:Normal -->
Davian shut his eyes against it, but that made it worse. The thread remained. Coiled behind his eyelids. Reaching. When he opened his eyes again, gasping—the alley was empty.

<!-- paragraph:5444 para_id:00001544 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5445 para_id:00001545 style:Normal -->
Maya clung to the dark thread with clenched mental fingers, forcing her focus along the thin, sable line that represented Arrek moving through the city. It felt like running along a taut wire in a hurricane. Every second, torrents of possibility slammed against her—sideways branches whipping past her, dying futures grabbing at her ankles, eddies that tried to pull her off the path entirely.

<!-- paragraph:5446 para_id:00001546 style:Normal -->
Follow him. Find him. Fix it. The thread shuddered. Arrek—Davian—heard something in the alley and turned, his hand closing around the handle of that strange black chisel he always carried. The moment his fingers touched it—the thread detonated. A violent, cataclysmic rupture of fate—raw, primordial, wrong.

<!-- paragraph:5447 para_id:00001547 style:Normal -->
It struck her like a blow, flinging her sideways. Her consciousness spiraled off the line and into the roaring storm of the Skein. The world became an ocean in mid-tempest. She fell—tumbled—spun through it—and then she was someone else.

<!-- paragraph:5448 para_id:00001548 style:Normal -->
She was masked. Her body was smaller. Younger. Her lungs burned from running. Her heartbeat jackhammered against her ribs. Her legs coiled and uncoiled with trained precision as she sprinted toward a man’s unguarded back.

<!-- paragraph:5449 para_id:00001549 style:Normal -->
The long, black blade spun into her hand—heavy and perfectly familiar, like an extra bone sliding into place. She didn’t question it. She didn’t think.

<!-- paragraph:5450 para_id:0000154A style:Normal -->
She launched herself upward, knife raised for the kill.

<!-- paragraph:5451 para_id:0000154B style:Normal -->
The magus ahead of her wore sleeveless High Clade garb. A perfect target. A perfect moment. The blade arced down toward the soft spot at his neck—

<!-- paragraph:5452 para_id:0000154C style:Normal -->
A Shroud erupted around him. Not a simple protective flare. A detonation. Omnidirectional. Blinding. A ring of force so intense it cracked the stone under his feet and vaporized the air in a circular pulse.

<!-- paragraph:5453 para_id:0000154D style:Normal -->
Her body struck the ground like a thrown doll. Agony. Then nothing. Her limbs twisted at angles that meant death. Her lungs wouldn’t fill. Her vision went red, then black. And as she died, the man turned.

<!-- paragraph:5454 para_id:0000154E style:Normal -->
His face was unfamiliar. But the expression—wide-eyed glee, ecstatic hunger—made something cold spool through Maya’s soul. And the sigil across his cheekbones—Drakhal.

<!-- paragraph:5455 para_id:0000154F style:Normal -->
The terror wasn’t hers alone. It belonged to the girl whose body she was wearing. But it ripped through Maya anyway. Before she could breathe—she was ripped into another body.

<!-- paragraph:5456 para_id:00001550 style:Normal -->
Into him. The magus, younger—skin fever-warm with unspent power, hands tingling with force, every nerve tuned toward violence and triumph. Maya felt his strength like it was her own—feral, unpredictable, intoxicating. She felt his laughter bubbling beneath her tongue as she stared at the terrified boy in front of her:

<!-- paragraph:5457 para_id:00001551 style:Normal -->
Caius Drakhal. Younger. Pallid. Caius lifted his hand and blasted force toward her - scrambling for dominance. It hit like a gust of warm air. She brushed it aside. And the joy that surged through her was so bright and vicious it nearly made Maya choke.

<!-- paragraph:5458 para_id:00001552 style:Normal -->
She leapt. Force exploded outward in a radial shockwave. Attendants flew. Debris shattered. Caius collapsed under him.

<!-- paragraph:5459 para_id:00001553 style:Normal -->
Maya felt his hands around Caius’s jaw. Felt the rush of absolute dominance. Felt the sick, sweet pleasure of smashing Caius’s head into the stone—Once. Twice. Again.

<!-- paragraph:5460 para_id:00001554 style:Normal -->
Stone cracked. Blood sprayed. Her breath came fast, ecstatic.

<!-- paragraph:5461 para_id:00001555 style:Normal -->
“Vorren, enough!” Ivana Drakhal’s voice cut through the carnage—but Vorren didn’t listen. Maya felt that refusal like a drug as she slammed Caius’s head down again.

<!-- paragraph:5462 para_id:00001556 style:Normal -->
A force blast hit her, hard and precise—Ivana’s beam cutting the air like a burning thread, striking her straight in the chest. Her body lifted, weightless for a blink—then slammed into the courtyard wall. Stone cratered under the impact. Fractures spiderwebbed outward, radiating like a mirror breaking.

<!-- paragraph:5463 para_id:00001557 style:Normal -->
The pain hit late—sharp and brilliant and delicious - hurling her deeper into the dark

<!-- paragraph:5464 para_id:00001558 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5465 para_id:00001559 style:Normal -->
Davian followed the dark thread as if it were a physical cord pulling him forward—through the gate, past the guards who waved him through without a second thought. He could practically hear their easy laughter echoing behind him: easiest posting in the citadel; who would be stupid enough to try sneaking in unregistered?

<!-- paragraph:5466 para_id:0000155A style:Normal -->
Inside the Spire, the hallways were silent. Too silent. His footsteps felt loud even though he was barely making a sound. The ink’s pressure behind his eyes steered him forward, turning corner after corner until he reached the hall leading to the Treasury stair.

<!-- paragraph:5467 para_id:0000155B style:Normal -->
He pressed his back to the wall and peeked around the corner.

<!-- paragraph:5468 para_id:0000155C style:Normal -->
The usual guards—heavy plate, spears braced, crests gleaming—were gone.

<!-- paragraph:5469 para_id:0000155D style:Normal -->
In their place, two older men in worn leather armor slouched against the stone. One had his arms crossed. The other looked… asleep?

<!-- paragraph:5470 para_id:0000155E style:Normal -->
Davian stared, genuinely confused. His mind whispered—no, the Ink whispered—take this chance. Get close. Kill them.

<!-- paragraph:5471 para_id:0000155F style:Normal -->
He recognized the men. Harvick’s friends. The ones who had helped watch Selwin during training. He’d never gotten their names.

<!-- paragraph:5472 para_id:00001560 style:Normal -->
And then the “sleeping” one lifted his head and said, “Hey! You’re here!”

<!-- paragraph:5473 para_id:00001561 style:Normal -->
Davian froze. He assumed the man was calling to someone else, but then—“Yeah, Arrek, I can see you there,” the man said, waving lazily. “Harvick told us you’d be coming by.”

<!-- paragraph:5474 para_id:00001562 style:Normal -->
Davian’s stomach dropped. He had no choice but to step out from behind the wall. He tried to arrange his face into something natural. He doubted he succeeded.

<!-- paragraph:5475 para_id:00001563 style:Normal -->
“Oh. Yeah. Uh… where is he?”

<!-- paragraph:5476 para_id:00001564 style:Normal -->
The man jabbed a thumb upward at the stairs. “Up there.”

<!-- paragraph:5477 para_id:00001565 style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. “Alone?”

<!-- paragraph:5478 para_id:00001566 style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” the man said, shrugging. “We sent the normal guards away. They were glad for it—must suck standing around all day with ten stones of steel strapped on, guarding a treasury everyone knows can’t be robbed.”

<!-- paragraph:5479 para_id:00001567 style:Normal -->
He let out a chuckle, shaking his head. “Anyway, go do whatever weird thing you two have set up.”

<!-- paragraph:5480 para_id:00001568 style:Normal -->
Davian managed a nod. His body felt hollow, unreal, but he forced himself to move. He climbed the stairs in a suffocating silence, the sound of his own footsteps drowned out by the pulse of the Ink in his head. The dark thread pulled him upward like a cord coiled through his mind, its cool temperature the only steady thing left—soothing in a grim, narrowing way. He clung to it. Let it tell him where to go.

<!-- paragraph:5481 para_id:00001569 style:Normal -->
When he rounded the last bend, he stopped. Harvick was there. Sitting on the landing with a wooden practice sword across his lap, as casually as if they’d arranged this. For a long moment, neither spoke.

<!-- paragraph:5482 para_id:0000156A style:Normal -->
Then Harvick sighed. “You know,” he said, “I thought it’d be a lot more dramatic if I was up here sharpening a real sword. But you can’t really sharpen a wooden one without looking stupid.”

<!-- paragraph:5483 para_id:0000156B style:Normal -->
He tossed the wooden sword toward Davian. Davian caught it reflexively, fingers closing around the familiar grip before he realized what he was doing.

<!-- paragraph:5484 para_id:0000156C style:Normal -->
Harvick produced a second wooden sword from beside him and stood up.

<!-- paragraph:5485 para_id:0000156D style:Normal -->
“Why are we doing this?” Davian asked, his voice low, strained.

<!-- paragraph:5486 para_id:0000156E style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged as if this were obvious. “Because you’ve been distracted by your ‘job’—that oh-so-important gig you get ‘paid’ for—and you’re slacking off on what really matters.”

<!-- paragraph:5487 para_id:0000156F style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed, fingers tightening around the practice blade. “I can’t do this right now. I have… work to do.”

<!-- paragraph:5488 para_id:00001570 style:Normal -->
Harvick raised an eyebrow. “Oh? What work do you have to do by yourself, in the middle of the night, in the treasury?”

<!-- paragraph:5489 para_id:00001571 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped up the final stairs until he stood level with Harvick on the landing, the wooden sword still clutched tight in his shaking hand. The dark thread pulsed behind his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:5490 para_id:00001572 style:Normal -->
Harvick moved first.

<!-- paragraph:5491 para_id:00001573 style:Normal -->
No warning—just a blur of motion and the crack of wood cutting the air. Davian barely got his sword up in time. The impact shocked up his arm, harder than any of their usual drills, and knocked his balance sideways. His heel skidded on the stone; he stumbled, breath catching.

<!-- paragraph:5492 para_id:00001574 style:Normal -->
Another hiss of air—

<!-- paragraph:5493 para_id:00001575 style:Normal -->
He twisted just in time. Harvick’s blade swept low from the right, fast enough that Davian felt the breeze skim his leg, close enough to raise gooseflesh along his skin. He jerked back, weight pitching unevenly as he reset his feet.

<!-- paragraph:5494 para_id:00001576 style:Normal -->
His heart hammered. The Ink buzzed at the back of his skull, urging him forward, urging him to break the pattern.

<!-- paragraph:5495 para_id:00001577 style:Normal -->
Harvick had already backed off, stance light, eyes sharp. So Davian lunged. Stone under his boots. Air dragging past his face. The wooden sword cutting a rough line as he aimed for Harvick’s shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:5496 para_id:00001578 style:Normal -->
But Harvick wasn’t there.

<!-- paragraph:5497 para_id:00001579 style:Normal -->
He slipped around Davian’s left side with a pivot that felt impossible for a man his size, and suddenly Davian saw the swing coming—a low horizontal arc aimed at his legs, too fast to dodge cleanly.

<!-- paragraph:5498 para_id:0000157A style:Normal -->
Davian gritted his teeth, braced—And let the blow land. Wood thudded against his thigh, pain blooming sharp and hot. At the same instant, he brought his own blade around like a bat, all muscle and instinct, and slammed it into Harvick’s shoulder. The sound was ugly—wood on bone—and they both staggered from the collisions.

<!-- paragraph:5499 para_id:0000157B style:Normal -->
Harvick hopped back, rubbing his shoulder with a wince.

<!-- paragraph:5500 para_id:0000157C style:Normal -->
“Well,” he said, “that’s gonna bruise for sure.” He eyed Davian’s stance, his technique—if it could even be called that. “Also, I’m pretty sure that’s not, like, a good way to fight with a sword. Which is kind of the whole point of what we’re doing here.”

<!-- paragraph:5501 para_id:0000157D style:Normal -->
Davian panted once, twice, the ache in his thigh throbbing in time with his heartbeat. “I got you, though.”

<!-- paragraph:5502 para_id:0000157E style:Normal -->
Harvick blinked at him. “I mean… we got each other.” His brow furrowed as he gestured vaguely between them. “So good job? I guess we’re both dead?”

<!-- paragraph:5503 para_id:0000157F style:Normal -->
He tilted his head, baffled.

<!-- paragraph:5504 para_id:00001580 style:Normal -->
“I thought I trained you to try to win, Arrek. So you could beat lots of other guys. Not give up on the first exchange for a quick tie.”

<!-- paragraph:5505 para_id:00001581 style:Normal -->
Davian lunged again before Harvick even finished talking, swinging hard—too hard, too wide—his muscles screaming from the last exchange.

<!-- paragraph:5506 para_id:00001582 style:Normal -->
“You don’t even know my real name!” Davian heard himself yelling, having to block out a sudden memory of Maya calling him that name tenderly.

<!-- paragraph:5507 para_id:00001583 style:Normal -->
Harvick jerked back, parrying reflexively this time instead of attacking. “Oh—now that actually is a surprise.”

<!-- paragraph:5508 para_id:00001584 style:Normal -->
Davian’s blade crashed down on Harvick’s again, jarring both their arms.

<!-- paragraph:5509 para_id:00001585 style:Normal -->
Harvick kept talking, breath steady—infuriatingly steady. “I always say, ‘Hey, you can’t rely on this big magical Registry thing all the time, you need real security,’ and they’re all ‘Harvick, it’s magic, you don’t understand, it’s perfect, and also we do have real security.’”

<!-- paragraph:5510 para_id:00001586 style:Normal -->
Davian hammered down again. And again. The wooden swords cracked together so hard his palms stung.

<!-- paragraph:5511 para_id:00001587 style:Normal -->
The impacts vibrated up into his elbows, his shoulders. His bones felt hollowed out. The ache in his thigh had spread, turning into a slow burn that threatened to buckle his stance.

<!-- paragraph:5512 para_id:00001588 style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t relent. He kept parrying, kept talking, as though Davian wasn’t coming apart at the seams.

<!-- paragraph:5513 para_id:00001589 style:Normal -->
“I mean, like…” Harvick continued, blocking three rapid blows in a row, “at this point I’d have to be pretty dumb to think you were one hundred percent who you said you were, but I figured maybe it was gambling debts or something.”

<!-- paragraph:5514 para_id:0000158A style:Normal -->
Davian grunted with effort, slamming down again.

<!-- paragraph:5515 para_id:0000158B style:Normal -->
“And you’re telling me you fooled the Registry somehow?” Harvick suddenly shoved Davian’s sword back with a sharp twist of his wrists. Davian stumbled, off-balance—and Harvick stepped in, executing a clean, precise flurry of strikes.

<!-- paragraph:5516 para_id:0000158C style:Normal -->
One—Davian barely deflected.
Two—his sword slipped in his grip.
Three—wood cracked against his ribs, the impact stunning his breath.

<!-- paragraph:5517 para_id:0000158D style:Normal -->
Davian staggered back, lungs burning, his entire body protesting.

<!-- paragraph:5518 para_id:0000158E style:Normal -->
“You don’t know who I am,” he panted. “You have no idea what I’ve been through.” He swung again, a desperate arc powered more by rage than training.

<!-- paragraph:5519 para_id:0000158F style:Normal -->
Harvick met it, grunting with the force of it.

<!-- paragraph:5520 para_id:00001590 style:Normal -->
“Yeah, of course I don’t,” Harvick shot back. “You just told me you were lying about it!” He pivoted, deflecting another desperate swing.

<!-- paragraph:5521 para_id:00001591 style:Normal -->
“So tell me?” Harvick barked, meeting Davian’s eyes with that same grounding, infuriating steadiness. “What is going on with you?”

<!-- paragraph:5522 para_id:00001592 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped back, chest heaving, sweat stinging the cut on his rib where Harvick’s last strike had landed. The world swam for a second—Harvick’s outline blurring—as the memory of the man in the alley slid back into his mind. There are only currents… and you have stepped into one older than either of us.

<!-- paragraph:5523 para_id:00001593 style:Normal -->
Davian saw the dark thread again—right in front of him—leading directly up the staircase toward the Treasury chamber. Toward his “task.” Toward the Heart.

<!-- paragraph:5524 para_id:00001594 style:Normal -->
He lowered the wooden sword.

<!-- paragraph:5525 para_id:00001595 style:Normal -->
“I can’t tell you,” he said. His voice was barely more than a whisper, raw from shouting and rage and fear. “But I have to do this.”

<!-- paragraph:5526 para_id:00001596 style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted, brushing his thumb across the grain of his practice blade. “You know, that’s exactly what Niall says. ‘I have to do this. I have to do that.’ It’s like a family motto.”

<!-- paragraph:5527 para_id:00001597 style:Normal -->
He shrugged. “Actually? You don’t have to do anything.”

<!-- paragraph:5528 para_id:00001598 style:Normal -->
Davian felt something snap inside him—frustration, panic, guilt, all tangled into one jagged surge. “You’re not being helpful,” he said sharply. “You’re just patronizing me to make yourself feel better.”

<!-- paragraph:5529 para_id:00001599 style:Normal -->
He lifted the wooden sword a few inches, felt how uneven his grip was, how badly his arm shook. “And we both know that with your tricks I won’t be able to beat you with this.”

<!-- paragraph:5530 para_id:0000159A style:Normal -->
He let it fall. The wooden sword clattered on the stone between them. Then he reached to his waistband and drew Antithesis. The metal felt colder than the air, as if it had been waiting.

<!-- paragraph:5531 para_id:0000159B style:Normal -->
Harvick’s face went still. He let out a long, sad sigh and shook his head. “You clearly never got it.” He shifted his stance slightly—not aggressive, not afraid, just… ready. Disappointed. “I don’t fight you to win,” he said quietly. “That’s not the point of sparring.”

<!-- paragraph:5532 para_id:0000159C style:Normal -->
Davian let himself fall backward—into the Ink, into the cold quiet beneath thought. It rose up instantly, swallowing him whole, muting everything except the tight, bright thread of self-preservation. His senses narrowed and he felt—more than saw—his Shroud skin over him like a thin second membrane, humming with unstable energy.

<!-- paragraph:5533 para_id:0000159D style:Normal -->
Harvick gave a low whistle. “Wow. Now that’s a trick I didn’t expect from you.” His grin was crooked, impressed. “But I’ll be honest, it looks like it could use a little work.”

<!-- paragraph:5534 para_id:0000159E style:Normal -->
Harvick dropped his wooden sword. It clattered once on the stone. Then he extended his right hand out to the side, palm open. The zan etched into his obsidian bracer flared, bright and sharp.

<!-- paragraph:5535 para_id:0000159F style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t wait. He leapt forward, driving Antithesis toward Harvick’s chest, the blade vibrating in his grip as if it could sense the Shroud around him. Time stretched thin—his awareness accelerating, sharpening until each fraction of movement felt like its own moment.

<!-- paragraph:5536 para_id:000015A0 style:Normal -->
A flash flickered at the edge of his vision. Davian’s head snapped toward it—just enough to see Harvick’s curved sword spinning through the air toward him, the zan along its spine blazing like a strip of molten gold. He ducked on instinct.

<!-- paragraph:5537 para_id:000015A1 style:Normal -->
The blade whistled over his head and landed perfectly in Harvick’s waiting hand—right as Harvick’s body turned, already descending into a low, slicing arc aimed at Davian’s legs.

<!-- paragraph:5538 para_id:000015A2 style:Normal -->
How can he move so fast?

<!-- paragraph:5539 para_id:000015A3 style:Normal -->
Davian barely managed to wrench Antithesis downward in time. The blades collided with a metallic crack, the force blasting through his arms, knocking him backward a full step.

<!-- paragraph:5540 para_id:000015A4 style:Normal -->
He reset his feet—pain shooting through his thigh from the earlier blow—right as Harvick came barreling at him again. Sword-first. No hesitation. Fast.

<!-- paragraph:5541 para_id:000015A5 style:Normal -->
Davian saw the zan gleaming along the curved blade again. Recognition slammed into him—familiarity. Zan like those on Antithesis. A weapon built to cut through him.

<!-- paragraph:5542 para_id:000015A6 style:Normal -->
He panicked. Slashed sideways at the sword, trying to knock it off-line—

<!-- paragraph:5543 para_id:000015A7 style:Normal -->
The blades collided. But the shock of the block reverberated through his entire body, a concussive jolt that ran up his arms and into his jaw. His grip spasmed. Antithesis flew out of his hands.

<!-- paragraph:5544 para_id:000015A8 style:Normal -->
He watched it in the stretched-out seconds—spinning end over end into the Treasury chamber beyond, crossing the threshold into weightlessness. It slammed into the far wall with an echoing metallic clang.

<!-- paragraph:5545 para_id:000015A9 style:Normal -->
The shock jarred Davian so hard he dropped his Shroud—a reflex he hadn’t mastered, something he still barely knew how to hold. Time came back all at once.

<!-- paragraph:5546 para_id:000015AA style:Normal -->
Harvick hopped from foot to foot, flushed with adrenaline, sword raised. “Oh man,” he crowed, grinning like a kid. “I still got it! You’re disarmed!”

<!-- paragraph:5547 para_id:000015AB style:Normal -->
Davian raised his fists. Harvick groaned theatrically. “Oh come on, you don’t even have a—” He paused, blinked, and sighed. “Right. This is kinda your thing.”

<!-- paragraph:5548 para_id:000015AC style:Normal -->
Davian barely heard him. The dark thread was stronger now—cutting straight through Harvick’s chest, passing into the Treasury chamber like a blade of night.

<!-- paragraph:5549 para_id:000015AD style:Normal -->
“Harvick,” Davian said, voice cracking. “Please. I don’t want to hurt you.”

<!-- paragraph:5550 para_id:000015AE style:Normal -->
Harvick barked a laugh that didn’t hide how tense he was. “Imagine how I feel—I’m the one holding a sword!” He waved it vaguely. “Kid, why do you have to be so hard to help sometimes?”

<!-- paragraph:5551 para_id:000015AF style:Normal -->
Memories whispered through Davian’s mind, flashing like sparks thrown into the Ink:

<!-- paragraph:5552 para_id:000015B0 style:Normal -->
Maya’s voice as she stepped between him and Caius—
 Harvick’s dry, steady encouragement in the training yard—
 Selwin’s eyes looking up at him, naive and mature all at once—
 Marek and Tanelle and Kolva and Jessa ribbing him and still calling him their friend—
 Maya again, her voice breaking as she stood in the courtyard—

<!-- paragraph:5553 para_id:000015B1 style:Normal -->
Let me help you!

<!-- paragraph:5554 para_id:000015B2 style:Normal -->
The Ink surged. Davian’s breath came in jagged pulls. “Why does everyone keep trying to HELP me?” he shouted, fists shaking. “I don’t need HELP—I just need you to get out of my way!”

<!-- paragraph:5555 para_id:000015B3 style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t flinch. Didn’t even raise his sword. “You know,” he said calmly, “I think everyone keeps trying because they see what I see. That you really, truly want to do what’s right. You’re just making yourself block out the things that matter for some reason.”

<!-- paragraph:5556 para_id:000015B4 style:Normal -->
“You have no idea what I care about,” Davian hissed. “I am focusing on what matters.”

<!-- paragraph:5557 para_id:000015B5 style:Normal -->
Harvick shook his head. “Oh yeah? Well, you saved that kid—Selwin—and you definitely didn’t have time to think about it beforehand. Then you spend all this time thinking just to twist your mind around.”

<!-- paragraph:5558 para_id:000015B6 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed hard. “I don’t deserve it, Harvick. I don’t deserve any of this, okay? Just… let it go.”

<!-- paragraph:5559 para_id:000015B7 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s expression softened—then hardened, regret knitting across his features.

<!-- paragraph:5560 para_id:000015B8 style:Normal -->
“Oh, because of what? The things you’ve done?” he asked. “That’s a terrible excuse to keep doing bad things.” He lifted his sword slightly, leveling it but not threatening. “I swear, I am going to help you, whether you like it or not.”

<!-- paragraph:5561 para_id:000015B9 style:Normal -->
He exhaled. “So just know that I pre-emptively feel really bad about this.” And then Harvick suddenly lunged forward— fast, low, precise— his blade sweeping toward Davian’s legs in a blur of motion.

<!-- paragraph:5562 para_id:000015BA style:Normal -->
Davian seized the Ink Boon with his mind—or did it seize him?

<!-- paragraph:5563 para_id:000015BB style:Normal -->
The tendrils of it burrowed down into him, invasive and intimate, threading through brain and skin, muscle and marrow. A cold numbness spread from his center outward, swallowing sensation until only clarity remained—clarity sharpened to a blade.

<!-- paragraph:5564 para_id:000015BC style:Normal -->
His sight narrowed. His breath thinned. Time thickened like syrup. Harvick’s blade was still coming—too slowly. Far too slowly. In the dilation, Davian could see the intention behind the strike, could read its shape: A disabling blow. Nonlethal. The kind of attack you used on someone weaker, someone you could overpower even if you left yourself exposed.

<!-- paragraph:5565 para_id:000015BD style:Normal -->
Even now… Even with the Shroud he’d manifested, even with the knife he’d drawn, even with the Ink—Harvick still didn’t see him as a threat. A familiar anger seeped through the numbness. Hot. Shameful. Contaminated by fear.

<!-- paragraph:5566 para_id:000015BE style:Normal -->
It’s just like the High Clades, Davian thought bitterly. Relying on their power to push us down. Knowing they’ll always have the advantage. Knowing we can’t touch them—because they don’t believe we ever could.

<!-- paragraph:5567 para_id:000015BF style:Normal -->
He couldn’t stand it. The Ink surged through his left arm—before he even chose to command it.

<!-- paragraph:5568 para_id:000015C0 style:Normal -->
He slammed the side of his hand into the flat of Harvick’s blade at a speed that felt alien—sickeningly fast. The impact cracked through his bones like dry twigs snapping underfoot. Pain didn’t reach him through the numbness.

<!-- paragraph:5569 para_id:000015C1 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s face froze—shock widening his eyes—as the sword was knocked wide, hit hard enough to twist Harvick’s grip open, sending the sword clattering down the unending staircase. Davian heard himself shouting—far away, as though echoing up from underwater:

<!-- paragraph:5570 para_id:000015C2 style:Normal -->
“GET OUT OF MY WAY!”

<!-- paragraph:5571 para_id:000015C3 style:Normal -->
The Ink surged again, this time flooding his right arm. He drove his fist into the right side of Harvick’s chest—wild, unrestrained, powered by something beyond muscle. He felt the ribs crunch under his knuckles. Felt another sickening pop inside his own hand. Felt Harvick’s breath tear out of him in a gasp that was barely a sound.

<!-- paragraph:5572 para_id:000015C4 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s arm went limp. The curved sword slipped from his fingers, clattering uselessly on the stone. He folded—not collapsing so much as crumpling, the strength disappearing out of his legs all at once.

<!-- paragraph:5573 para_id:000015C5 style:Normal -->
And then Davian was catching him with mangled hands.

<!-- paragraph:5574 para_id:000015C6 style:Normal -->
Time didn’t resume. It stayed stretched thin—pulled like taffy—every second widening into an unbearable eternity. Davian couldn’t let go of the Ink Boon. Or maybe it wouldn’t let go of him.

<!-- paragraph:5575 para_id:000015C7 style:Normal -->
It crawled deeper into his nerves, threading into his veins, slowing each heartbeat until even the rise and fall of Harvick’s chest seemed to take forever. The world drooped at the edges like soft wax melting.

<!-- paragraph:5576 para_id:000015C8 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s lips moved. Sound bled out of him in a slow, dragging smear:

<!-- paragraph:5577 para_id:000015C9 style:Normal -->
“...guess that’s… what I get… for talking… too much…”

<!-- paragraph:5578 para_id:000015CA style:Normal -->
The words slid through the syrup of time, each syllable breaking apart before it reached Davian fully.

<!-- paragraph:5579 para_id:000015CB style:Normal -->
“…should have… asked more… questions…”

<!-- paragraph:5580 para_id:000015CC style:Normal -->
Davian couldn’t answer. He couldn’t speak. He couldn’t breathe properly. The numb rage still held him—ice seeping into his ribs, his spine, the sides of his face. His cheeks were wet.

<!-- paragraph:5581 para_id:000015CD style:Normal -->
Harvick’s body shifted against him in a small, twitching spasm. His voice came again, even slower than before

<!-- paragraph:5582 para_id:000015CE style:Normal -->
“I… just want… you… to really… live…”

<!-- paragraph:5583 para_id:000015CF style:Normal -->
The last syllable dragged out into silence.

<!-- paragraph:5584 para_id:000015D0 style:Normal -->
Davian stayed kneeling there; his hands wouldn’t unclench and his mind wouldn’t release whatever this moment had become. The Ink was seeping deeper into the bones of his fingers, crawling into the fractures, snapping and sealing, restoring somehow. He knew it should be excruciating, but the sensation arrived muted—dull, distant, like someone else’s pain traveling to him through too many walls. He didn’t know how long he was there—seconds or hours or something between—because the Ink held time in its teeth..

<!-- paragraph:5585 para_id:000015D1 style:Normal -->
Then—Footsteps. Sharp. Measured. Climbing the stairs. Davian’s head jerked up.

<!-- paragraph:5586 para_id:000015D2 style:Normal -->
Through the haze, through the warping edges of the slowed world, he saw Niall Rezin coming up the staircase. His long-sleeved military coat swayed with each step, and the faint crackle of static around his shoulders distorted the air like heat above a pyre.

<!-- paragraph:5587 para_id:000015D3 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5588 para_id:000015D4 style:Normal -->
Maya tumbled through the dark strands, the Skein’s pressure battering her from all directions. She couldn’t even pretend she was following Arrek anymore—she had lost him, lost the thread, lost everything. She spun helplessly, flung from one vision to the next like debris caught in a storm.

<!-- paragraph:5589 para_id:000015D5 style:Normal -->
She was hurtling down Vorren’s lineage—his story—dragged after him through branching timelines she couldn’t control. Am I going backward? Each vision felt earlier. Earlier than the last. Further up the terrible spiral of cause and consequence.

<!-- paragraph:5590 para_id:000015D6 style:Normal -->
Another vision slammed into her. She became Vorren again.

<!-- paragraph:5591 para_id:000015D7 style:Normal -->
She felt the cool touch of a mask as Vorren placed it on his face. The Shade’s style. Sleek. Predatory. A hunter’s disguise meant to hide a smile that was too feral for polite society.

<!-- paragraph:5592 para_id:000015D8 style:Normal -->
His body moved through underground tunnels with a confident, unhurried gait. Her breath echoed off stone. She felt the weight of power at her fingertips, the reckless thrill that came only from knowing you could slaughter everyone you met if you chose.

<!-- paragraph:5593 para_id:000015D9 style:Normal -->
A figure waited in the dark. Young. Unmasked. Pale skin. A smile that shone like a lantern—brimming with excitement, matching Vorren’s mania perfectly. When their hands clasped, it was like two storms greeting each other. Twin derangements. Twin ambitions.

<!-- paragraph:5594 para_id:000015DA style:Normal -->
“So I hear you’re the one who can help kill Niall Rezin.” Vorren’s voice was full of delight. The predatory surge was intoxicating. Maya felt the thrill of hunting something powerful, something legendary. Turning an apex predator into prey.

<!-- paragraph:5595 para_id:000015DB style:Normal -->
The young man’s smile widened— Too wide— Impossibly wide. Maya fell into that smile, into that darkness—and she was ripped backward in time again. She could feel it now. The pull. Like the echo of her father’s name shoving her through the strands, hurling her into—

<!-- paragraph:5596 para_id:000015DC style:Normal -->
She became Niall Rezin. Young. Angry. Standing in a room that smelled like old books and colder secrets. His father—Elric—stood across from him, skin thin as paper over a face too alive, too bright. Eyes lit with unnatural vitality. They were arguing.

<!-- paragraph:5597 para_id:000015DD style:Normal -->
She felt Niall’s frustration, the fury burning under his ribs. He turned away—And something else hit him. Lightning ripped through his chest from behind. Agony. Shock. He collapsed to the floor, knowing instantly he was dying. Alara—where did she come from?—rushed to him, screaming, catching his body before it hit stone fully, her voice shattering into something animal.

<!-- paragraph:5598 para_id:000015DE style:Normal -->
Maya felt Niall’s last thought: confusion, terror, and the sense of his own thread snapping—

<!-- paragraph:5599 para_id:000015DF style:Normal -->
And then—The scene snapped taut, like a thread jerked backward through time, and she was in it again—still Niall, still turning away from Elric—But this time some deep instinct, some primal spark, flared inside him. He turned. He turned.

<!-- paragraph:5600 para_id:000015E0 style:Normal -->
Lightning speared toward her—and Niall’s Shroud erupted in the same instant. Blinding, violent. The strike hit the Shroud and her mind twisted it back along its own path like a serpent. Elric’s eyes widened as his own current drove into his chest.

<!-- paragraph:5601 para_id:000015E1 style:Normal -->
There was a brilliant, catastrophic flash. The last thing she saw before Elric became ash was his smile. And as that moment ended Maya was hurled outward—clear of the Skein entirely—and into nothingness.

<!-- paragraph:5602 para_id:000015E2 style:Normal -->
She drifted weightless in the void, unmoored from past or present, staring at the impossible shape of the Skein hanging before her. She could see all of it. Every path. Every thread. All at once.

<!-- paragraph:5603 para_id:000015E3 style:Normal -->
Her mind couldn’t reconcile it—couldn’t force the infinite into a human skull— but she still saw it: A colossal tapestry of reality with a gap torn through it, the place she had been thrown clear. A hole. A nothingness. And from that wound—dark threads spilled in every direction.

<!-- paragraph:5604 para_id:000015E4 style:Normal -->
She focused—adjusting her sense of time as a direction, stretching it like muscle—and she sensed the origin: Far back. Farther. So far back that everything she had just seen felt like the space of a single breath.

<!-- paragraph:5605 para_id:000015E5 style:Normal -->
She reached out with her mind towards the wound. She seized the threads. Tried to channel them. Tried to force them to align, to converge, to FIX themselves the way she molded heat or force or stone. She pulled. And pulled. I should be able to do this— I should— I should—

<!-- paragraph:5606 para_id:000015E6 style:Normal -->
She didn’t even know what she was trying to fix. Only that she was failing. And then—A voice. Far away. Real. Human.

<!-- paragraph:5607 para_id:000015E7 style:Normal -->
“Maya—MAYA!”

<!-- paragraph:5608 para_id:000015E8 style:Normal -->
Hands grabbed her shoulders—And she slammed back into her body as Erynn shook her hard enough to rattle her teeth. “Maya, please—wake up!”

<!-- paragraph:5609 para_id:000015E9 style:Normal -->
Maya gasped—a sharp, tearing breath—and the world didn’t return all at once. The Skein still hung over her sight like an afterimage burned into her retinas, a bright, impossible web superimposed over reality.

<!-- paragraph:5610 para_id:000015EA style:Normal -->
She couldn’t shake the feeling that she wasn’t here. That she was still in someone else’s body—still wearing the lives she’d slipped into. That Erynn’s hands on her shoulders weren’t hers, that the cold stone beneath her bare feet wasn’t actually under Maya’s feet.

<!-- paragraph:5611 para_id:000015EB style:Normal -->
Erynn leaned back, eyes wide with panic. “Oh thank the Creator. Maya—everyone heard the Registry making noises, and nobody wanted to come in here, and they couldn’t find Niall so they asked me to—” She stopped. “Maya, you’re looking at me really weird. What’s happening?”

<!-- paragraph:5612 para_id:000015EC style:Normal -->
Maya stared at her. Erynn’s face was right there. Right in front of her. Present and real. But layered over it—like a ghost-image—was the vision of Erynn frozen solid, ice creeping up her neck. Her body crumpled in the courtyard. Her throat torn open by debris. Erynn dying because Maya lost control.

<!-- paragraph:5613 para_id:000015ED style:Normal -->
Maya blinked hard—once, twice—trying to shake the image loose, shaking her head as if she could rattle the futures out of her skull. Her stomach lurched. Then she noticed the sound.

<!-- paragraph:5614 para_id:000015EE style:Normal -->
The Registry. A soft, intermittent chiming, not the sharp alarm of an intruder but the steady pulse of location. Maya turned. One name blinked on the Spire projection: Arrek Hadrin. Her breath seized. “Refine!”

<!-- paragraph:5615 para_id:000015EF style:Normal -->
The map shuddered and zoomed inward, the layers of the citadel folding away until the Treasury corridor glowed bright. Three auras pulsed there. Arrek. And—fuzzing into existence, two more names: Niall Rezin. Harvick Arelon.

<!-- paragraph:5616 para_id:000015F0 style:Normal -->
Erynn said something—still talking—but her voice was split and distant, like Maya was hearing her through water. Then—Harvick’s aura flickered. Dimmed. And faded out.

<!-- paragraph:5617 para_id:000015F1 style:Normal -->
“No…” Maya whispered. Erynn grabbed her arm. “Maya, what is going on? What are you seeing?”

<!-- paragraph:5618 para_id:000015F2 style:Normal -->
Maya barely heard her. Her mind was still half in the Skein, half here, the threads pushing at her, whispering about convergence and collapse and endings she couldn’t parse.

<!-- paragraph:5619 para_id:000015F3 style:Normal -->
“There must have been… an accident,” she murmured. “Harvick’s hurt. Arrek must be helping Niall—he works in the treasury after all—he must be helping—” She reached out—not physically but with the part of her mind that still floated in the space-between-reality, trying to grasp the strands again, trying to see, to fix, to pull meaning—

<!-- paragraph:5620 para_id:000015F4 style:Normal -->
Suddenly warm arms wrapped around her from behind. Erynn. She didn’t hesitate, didn’t flinch despite the frost flaring from Maya’s Shroud. She just hugged tighter, like she meant to anchor Maya to the ground by force alone. The Skein tore away. The cold receded in a rush. Reality—solid, immediate, painful—slammed back into her chest.

<!-- paragraph:5621 para_id:000015F5 style:Normal -->
Maya muted the Shroud instantly to keep from hurting her. Erynn’s voice was next to her ear, shaking but fierce. “I don’t know where you’ve been going, or what you think you have to do in your mind, but something is happening and we need you here, now.”

<!-- paragraph:5622 para_id:000015F6 style:Normal -->
Maya swallowed hard. “Thank you,” she said, voice rougher than she meant. “I… needed you here now, too.”

<!-- paragraph:5623 para_id:000015F7 style:Normal -->
Erynn released her after another long moment. When Maya turned, Erynn met her eyes and gave her a steady, confident smile—small, but impossibly grounding.

<!-- paragraph:5624 para_id:000015F8 style:Normal -->
“Okay,” Erynn said. “Things are scary right now. But I know you can help us.”

<!-- paragraph:5625 para_id:000015F9 style:Normal -->
Something steadied inside Maya at that. Something clicked into place. She strode out into the hallway, her steps already gathering speed. Three guards and two Registry assistants lurked near the door, startled by her sudden appearance.

<!-- paragraph:5626 para_id:000015FA style:Normal -->
Maya drew in a sharp breath—purging the last vestiges of the Skein’s from her lungs—and said, clear and commanding: “Clear the hall. Now. I need to get somewhere very, very fast.”

<!-- paragraph:5627 para_id:000015FB style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5628 para_id:000015FC style:Normal -->
Davian dropped Harvick without meaning to—his arms simply let go—and Harvick’s body hit the stone with a dull, final sound. Davian scrambled backward, his palms sliding on cold floor. His vision pulsed. His breath tore in and out of him too fast. He searched wildly for Antithesis—before remembering he’d lost it. It was still floating somewhere inside the Treasury room, weightless and unreachable.

<!-- paragraph:5629 para_id:000015FD style:Normal -->
Harvick’s curved sword lay at Niall’s feet. Too far. Too late. Too many things happening at once, even with time dilated, even with the Ink stretching out each moment until it felt hollow and echoing.

<!-- paragraph:5630 para_id:000015FE style:Normal -->
Niall barely glanced at Davian. Why would he? Davian felt the thought coil inside him like venom. I’m nothing to him. An insect. The numb rage rose to smother the guilt.

<!-- paragraph:5631 para_id:000015FF style:Normal -->
Niall walked toward Harvick’s body. He stood over it - it, not him, now - raised a hand, and fired a single bolt of electricity into Harvick’s chest. There was a crack of ozone. A twitch of muscle.

<!-- paragraph:5632 para_id:00001600 style:Normal -->
“What are you doing?” Davian screamed.

<!-- paragraph:5633 para_id:00001601 style:Normal -->
Niall finally looked up—Not at him. Through him.

<!-- paragraph:5634 para_id:00001602 style:Normal -->
His voice came out flat, dulled, like someone speaking in their sleep.

<!-- paragraph:5635 para_id:00001603 style:Normal -->
“This kind of thing,” Niall said, “doesn’t really surprise me anymore.”

<!-- paragraph:5636 para_id:00001604 style:Normal -->
Davian’s throat tore as he yelled, “I hate you! LOOK at me! This is all your fault! You made me do this!”

<!-- paragraph:5637 para_id:00001605 style:Normal -->
That finally got Niall’s focus. His eyes sharpened—And Davian saw it. The same inky darkness. The same drowning void. Niall’s voice was barely a whisper, thick and exhausted, like each syllable cost him something enormous. “I can’t make anyone do anything,” he said. “Except die.”

<!-- paragraph:5638 para_id:00001606 style:Normal -->
He raised his hand. His sigil flared on his face as bright as the sun.

<!-- paragraph:5639 para_id:00001607 style:Normal -->
Davian felt the charge in the air—the static building, the way the currents gathered, the inevitability of it. The lightning no Shroud could withstand. The kind of lightning that would unmake him.

<!-- paragraph:5640 para_id:00001608 style:Normal -->
The dark thread whispered to him. A pull. A promise. A command. Ink flooded into his legs, cold and hungry. And Davian launched himself backward—away from Niall, away from the bolt, after the thread—into the Treasury chamber.

<!-- paragraph:5641 para_id:00001609 style:Normal -->
Lightning tore through the space where Davian had just been—a living thing, too fast to be outrun, too bright to be seen, too violent to survive. It split and branched midair, serpentine, snaking around the curvature of the doorway like it was searching for him, hunting him, tasting the air he left behind.

<!-- paragraph:5642 para_id:0000160A style:Normal -->
Davian hurtled toward the opposite wall of the Treasury chamber, weightless in the Heart’s field, body spinning end over end. He tried to brace—His feet slammed into the wall. Tendons popped. Bones jarred—And the Ink surged through him, sealing the damage instantly, repairing even as he felt the intense shock of pain as if it was happening to someone else.

<!-- paragraph:5643 para_id:0000160B style:Normal -->
The Ink was no longer a trickle; it was a torrent. Before, the Boon had felt like dipping his fingers into cold water. Now it felt like plunging into a freezing river that grabbed him by the spine and dragged him under. The second Boon—the one the pale smiling man had etched into him hours ago—was burning awake, flooding him with strength and narrowing his vision into a single, dark corridor.

<!-- paragraph:5644 para_id:0000160C style:Normal -->
He pushed off the wall, spinning through the weightless chamber just as more lightning arced inward, splitting wildly around him. It hissed through the space like angry vipers, seeking him, lashing at the air with crackling fingers.

<!-- paragraph:5645 para_id:0000160D style:Normal -->
He caught a glimpse of Niall standing in the doorway of the chamber, lightning streaming from his hand in a steady cascade. Davian blinked—Shouldn’t there be… more? Niall’s lightning could obliterate an army in an instant. But this—this looked… wild. Scattered.

<!-- paragraph:5646 para_id:0000160E style:Normal -->
No time to think.

<!-- paragraph:5647 para_id:0000160F style:Normal -->
Davian another wall and launched upward, shoving himself toward the ceiling by sheer force of will and Ink-fed speed. He followed the dark thread with tunnel-vision certainty, putting the massive Treasury Sphere between himself and the lightning storm behind him.

<!-- paragraph:5648 para_id:00001610 style:Normal -->
Lightning slammed into the Sphere’s surface, breaking apart into smaller arcs that ricocheted hungrily around the chamber.

<!-- paragraph:5649 para_id:00001611 style:Normal -->
Davian flipped again—boot soles planting against the ceiling—And saw it. Antithesis floated ahead of him, perfectly aligned with the path of the dark thread. He reached out and snatched it from the air, the blade vibrating with the chamber’s strange weightlessness.

<!-- paragraph:5650 para_id:00001612 style:Normal -->
He looked down. The thread led directly to the surface of the treasury sphere. A moment of confusion: What am I supposed to do?

<!-- paragraph:5651 para_id:00001613 style:Normal -->
But the Ink answered before he could. If he followed— If he obeyed— If he completed what it demanded— he would finally have the power they needed. The power his mother told him would save them all. Power that didn’t depend on anyone else. Power that didn’t leave him begging for help, for mercy, for forgiveness. Power that silenced the pain of connection.

<!-- paragraph:5652 para_id:00001614 style:Normal -->
The Ink was everywhere now. His nerves. His bones. His thoughts. It numbed him while filling him with strength so sharp it felt like clarity. Like purpose. He shoved off the ceiling with even greater force—felt the world blur around him, felt the weightlessness twist into acceleration that his body should never have been able to survive. But something steered him. The Ink. The dark thread. He couldn’t tell anymore where his choice ended and the Boon began.

<!-- paragraph:5653 para_id:00001615 style:Normal -->
He raised Antithesis over his head—both hands trembling, bones sealed with Ink, feeling the air on his face as he cut through it—And he flew. Straight at the Sphere. This time the strike was nothing like his futile stab through a wooden table. This time he hit with power.

<!-- paragraph:5654 para_id:00001616 style:Normal -->
Antithesis slammed into the Sphere’s surface and Davian felt his magic burst awake. Subversion. Raw. Violent. That impossible, forbidden power he had never been able to use without a mask. But now it flooded out of him unhindered. And he felt it: This was the same force that produced a Shroud—but also older. Deeper. Balanced in a way that made his teeth ache.

<!-- paragraph:5655 para_id:00001617 style:Normal -->
His awareness spiked outward across the Sphere’s surface—like his mind had smeared across the metal. He felt every za etched into the containment shell, felt the lattice of stored power humming beneath it. Waiting. Begging. Wanting to be released. So he released it.

<!-- paragraph:5656 para_id:00001618 style:Normal -->
With perfect certainty, with the feeling of acting out something already written—he tore the energy free and redirected it in a single catastrophic surge.

<!-- paragraph:5657 para_id:00001619 style:Normal -->
The Sphere, a structure larger than a house, launched. Straight toward Niall.

<!-- paragraph:5658 para_id:0000161A style:Normal -->
The backlash hit Davian all at once. The force that hurled the Sphere tore back through him like a shockwave, shattering every bone in both of his arms. Antithesis flew out of his grip, spinning away into the weightless dark. At the same instant, the Sphere struck the threshold of Niall’s lightning. The impact was blinding—light exploded outward as the lightning ripped through the Sphere’s core and shattered it into a spray of metallic fragments.

<!-- paragraph:5659 para_id:0000161B style:Normal -->
Then gravity returned.

<!-- paragraph:5660 para_id:0000161C style:Normal -->
Davian registered “down” a split second too late. The anti-gravity field collapsed around him and he dropped like a stone. Instinct told him to brace, to twist, to land—none of it mattered. His arms were useless. He only had time to look up helplessly, watching the debris and the crackling afterimage of lightning flare above him before he hit the ground.

<!-- paragraph:5661 para_id:0000161D style:Normal -->
Pain flared white, bursting through his ribs as more bones cracked on impact.

<!-- paragraph:5662 para_id:0000161E style:Normal -->
The Ink surged into his injuries, but something was wrong. It felt slower. Thicker. Struggling. Each bone in his arms began to grind and reset, cracking back into alignment with sickening precision. He knew the healing was happening fast, but it felt stretched out, like time itself resisted.

<!-- paragraph:5663 para_id:0000161F style:Normal -->
I should have used my Shroud, he thought. Frustration tightened in his throat. How was he supposed to do any of this? Use a Shroud he had barely learned to manifest—one he wasn’t even supposed to be able to make in the first place. Fight with a knife from his mother. Use an Ink Boon he didn’t understand, and another that felt like it was thinking for him. Fight with his fists when nothing else worked. Hold all of it together at once.

<!-- paragraph:5664 para_id:00001620 style:Normal -->
It’s impossible, he murmured as he dragged himself upright. His limbs trembled. His hands still twitched with phantom pain. It’s unfair. The High Clades have all of this handed to them just for being born, and I’m supposed to cobble everything together from their scraps and hope it works.

<!-- paragraph:5665 para_id:00001621 style:Normal -->
He forced his feet under him. Wavering. Hurt. The Ink humming unevenly through his bones. He could still see the dark thread. It pulsed in front of him, guiding him forward, tugging at his senses like a magnet buried beneath the world. He followed it, stumbling, until his foot struck something with a sharp metallic clang.

<!-- paragraph:5666 para_id:00001622 style:Normal -->
He looked down. Antithesis.

<!-- paragraph:5667 para_id:00001623 style:Normal -->
He crouched—slowly, every joint complaining—and closed his fingers around the hilt. The blade felt heavier now, resonant, as if it recognized what he’d just done.

<!-- paragraph:5668 para_id:00001624 style:Normal -->
He dragged himself deeper into the wreckage of the shattered Sphere. Metal fragments littered the chamber, jagged pieces of the internal lattice scraped across the floor like scattered bones. He barely processed any of it; his mind was a funnel, narrowing around the thread’s pull and nothing else.

<!-- paragraph:5669 para_id:00001625 style:Normal -->
The thread grew stronger as he crawled, weaving through broken slabs, twisted glyph-plates, splintered arcs of zan. He followed until he reached what had been the inner core—a smaller metal sphere split wide open, its surface etched in intricate zan he didn’t recognize.

<!-- paragraph:5670 para_id:00001626 style:Normal -->
Except—Some he did recognize.The same shape his mother had carved into his mask: versions of za, bent into patterns her hands had known without teaching him. The sight made something tighten in his chest. He felt the weight of the mask against his thigh. Still there. Still intact. He hadn’t put it on before fighting Harvick—too distracted. Or maybe he hadn’t wanted to.

<!-- paragraph:5671 para_id:00001627 style:Normal -->
His hand slid through the jagged opening of the core. The thread guided it. His fingers closed around something cool. Not cold—cool in a way that made his skin prickle, like touching a shadow made solid.

<!-- paragraph:5672 para_id:00001628 style:Normal -->
He pulled out a perfectly dark sphere. Not dark like absence—dark like it pulled light into it, the world bending slightly around its edges. He put it into the same pouch as his mask. The sphere’s weight settled there like an extra heartbeat. He looked again at the broken core it had been encased in—the lattice of Nizan zan twisting over its surface. Negation. Unweaving. Transformation. Designs far beyond his understanding. “I should ask Tanelle,” he muttered. The thought floated up before he could stop it.

<!-- paragraph:5673 para_id:00001629 style:Normal -->
Then he realized he would never see Tanelle again. Or Marek, or Jessa, or Kolvar.. Or… anyone else here. The Ink dulled the grief immediately—but he felt its shape behind the numbness. He turned toward the entrance of the Treasury, still following the strand. He had to climb over chunks of shattered stone and plates of warped metal, each movement stiff and shaky, his arms barely functioning.

<!-- paragraph:5674 para_id:0000162A style:Normal -->
Right, he thought, detached and drifting in the Ink. I forgot somehow… Niall… He remembered the blast, the Sphere’s explosion, the shockwave. He must be dead.

<!-- paragraph:5675 para_id:0000162B style:Normal -->
Davian clambered out of the ruined chamber, dragging himself over the last ridge of rubble. His fingers slipped on dust and shattered zan-plates, his arms trembling from the still-unfinished healing. The air outside the chamber was thick with settling debris and the metallic scent of lightning burn.

<!-- paragraph:5676 para_id:0000162C style:Normal -->
Two bodies lay ahead.

<!-- paragraph:5677 para_id:0000162D style:Normal -->
Niall, slumped on his side, half-curled around Harvick’s body. The wreckage had parted around them in an uncanny sweep, as though Niall had forced a path through the collapsing metal by sheer will or by the momentum of the lightning blast. Rubble fanned outward in a pattern like the wake of a ship cutting through water.

<!-- paragraph:5678 para_id:0000162E style:Normal -->
Niall’s coat—the one Davian had never seen him without, the one that made him look invulnerable and distant—was shredded. His shirt too, ripped open down the front and clinging to him in ragged strips. It exposed his upper body for the first time.

<!-- paragraph:5679 para_id:0000162F style:Normal -->
Davian froze. Niall’s arms were covered in scars. Burns that curled in branching patterns like lightning etching itself permanently into flesh. But it wasn’t just scars. There were strips of metal. Long, thin bands wound around both of Niall’s arms—twisting up from wrist to shoulder, spidering across his chest, disappearing under the ruined remains of his trousers only to emerge again lower on his legs. They clung to him like veins, glinting whenever dust shifted over them.

<!-- paragraph:5680 para_id:00001630 style:Normal -->
Davian stumbled closer, squinting. Each metal strip was carved with zanwork—so intricate, so impossibly fine that he didn’t know how anyone could etch something like that by hand. It was as delicate as jewelry, but as dense and complex as the zan arrays on the Archeon gauntlets. More complex, maybe.

<!-- paragraph:5681 para_id:00001631 style:Normal -->
He thought, half-sick, Are those strips… silver? He crouched, breath catching in his throat. And then Niall’s chest rose. Just barely—but enough. He was breathing. Davian’s heart lurched painfully. The numbness wavered for a moment, a jagged line of panic breaking through.

<!-- paragraph:5682 para_id:00001632 style:Normal -->
Then, like a whisper against his cheek, he felt it. The dark thread pulsed from Niall’s body like a heartbeat he could feel in his own bones. It whispered to him—cold, certain, inevitable. Davian tightened his grip on Antithesis until his knuckles ached. The knife’s edge glimmered faintly, catching the dim ambient light of the broken corridor. You’ve done so much. You can finally end it. Be free. Be safe. Be powerful.
