<!-- paragraph:5683 para_id:00001633 style:Normal -->
His hand trembled.

<!-- paragraph:5684 para_id:00001634 style:Normal -->
A sound broke the stillness. A deep, distant boom. Then another. And another.

<!-- paragraph:5685 para_id:00001635 style:Normal -->
Davian jerked his head up. The vibrations shuddered through the floor. Dust drifted from the ceiling. The explosions sounded like they were coming from far below—but each one was louder than the last.

<!-- paragraph:5686 para_id:00001636 style:Normal -->
Were they getting closer?

<!-- paragraph:5687 para_id:00001637 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5688 para_id:00001638 style:Normal -->
Maya hurtled upward through the open column of the spiral staircase, using the hollow center as a direct vertical path. The world blurred around her. Her mind wove faster than her body moved, faster than gravity could pull her down. She siphoned heat indiscriminately in every direction—walls, lantern sconces, the stone itself—anything her mind could latch onto. She pulled that heat into a concentrated sphere beneath her, shaping it with split focus, channeling energy with the precision she no longer had time to question.

<!-- paragraph:5689 para_id:00001639 style:Normal -->
Then she released it.

<!-- paragraph:5690 para_id:0000163A style:Normal -->
The air exploded beneath her feet, an expanding burst that bent the world upward around her. She seized her Shroud an instant later, shaping the reaction, molding the backlash into a shield that wrapped her and propelled her higher. Her mind strained. Every channel she carved felt like a step closer to losing control. But she needed to reach the treasury now. Arrek. Niall. Harvick. She couldn’t be too late. She couldn’t.

<!-- paragraph:5691 para_id:0000163B style:Normal -->
The staircase fell away beneath her in a dizzying blur. Suddenly she burst free of the endless winding ascent, flying above the last landing. For a heartbeat she hung there in midair—and saw everything at once.

<!-- paragraph:5692 para_id:0000163C style:Normal -->
Rubble. Smoke. The twisted remnants of the Sphere’s lattice scattered across the floor. Niall, shirt and coat ripped to shreds, collapsed on his side. Harvick unmoving beside him. And Arrek—standing, shaken, next to them.

<!-- paragraph:5693 para_id:0000163D style:Normal -->
Maya flipped in the air, twisting her body with instinctive precision. She hit the stone hard enough that bone would have shattered. Her Shroud sublimated the force into heat, sending a scorch of steam curling out from under her boots.

<!-- paragraph:5694 para_id:0000163E style:Normal -->
Her mind raced to make sense of the scene. An accident, it had to be—some kind of breakdown in the treasury arrays—Arrek was the supervisor here, he must have rushed in—Niall came to help—Harvick must have followed—

<!-- paragraph:5695 para_id:0000163F style:Normal -->
She sprinted toward Arrek. “What’s going on?” she demanded. “I came as fast as I could—”

<!-- paragraph:5696 para_id:00001640 style:Normal -->
Arrek wouldn’t look at her. He stood there, shoulders tight, breath shallow, eyes fixed on the ground instead of her. It hit her like a blow. He wasn’t relieved to see her. He wasn’t grateful she’d come.

<!-- paragraph:5697 para_id:00001641 style:Normal -->
He was afraid.

<!-- paragraph:5698 para_id:00001642 style:Normal -->
“I wish you wouldn’t have come,” he said.

<!-- paragraph:5699 para_id:00001643 style:Normal -->
Maya stopped short. Only then did she really see his right hand. Not empty. Holding a knife. Not a tool. Not a chisel. A weapon meant for killing from shadows. The same dark shape she’d seen in her vision.

<!-- paragraph:5700 para_id:00001644 style:Normal -->
Arrek’s voice came out low, almost like he was speaking to himself. “I have to kill him. That’s what has to happen. I can’t do anything about it.”

<!-- paragraph:5701 para_id:00001645 style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach dropped. “What are you talking about?” she yelled. “You’re being an idiot. Of course you don’t have to kill him!”

<!-- paragraph:5702 para_id:00001646 style:Normal -->
Arrek didn’t look at her. He simply lifted the knife over Niall.

<!-- paragraph:5703 para_id:00001647 style:Normal -->
Maya screamed. She lunged. Her Shroud flared—cold burning along her arm—and she struck his wrist with everything she had. She felt the bones in his forearm snap like brittle sticks. The knife flew out of his hand, spinning, and vanished over the railing—falling back down the long shaft she had just flown up. Davian staggered backward, his arm crooked at a sick angle.

<!-- paragraph:5704 para_id:00001648 style:Normal -->
Maya followed his movement—and her eyes caught the treasury chamber behind him. Empty. The field inside was dead. Nothing floated. No containment. No Sphere. Her blood ran cold.

<!-- paragraph:5705 para_id:00001649 style:Normal -->
“You took it,” she whispered. Then louder, “You took it, didn’t you? The Heart? Is that what this has all been about—just stealing from us? And killing?” She shook her head, disbelief burning into fury. “You act like you have no control over yourself, like you’re being dragged along by fate—but you had to plan this. You had to keep secrets and shut everyone out and lie every day. You don’t do something like that by accident. You have to want to do it.”

<!-- paragraph:5706 para_id:0000164A style:Normal -->
Arrek reached for the bag at his hip with his broken arm. She saw the bones in his shattered forearm beginning to crack and grind back into place, Ink patterns crawling and fading under his skin. He still wouldn’t look at her.

<!-- paragraph:5707 para_id:0000164B style:Normal -->
As his arm reset, he pulled out a mask. “I’m sorry,” he said, placing the mask over his face. “This is the only way I know how to live.”

<!-- paragraph:5708 para_id:0000164C style:Normal -->
She barely caught the subtle shift of his weight—his heel turning, his center dropping, that telltale Ink ripple across his skin—And then he launched himself past her in a burst of motion, barreling through the air, too fast for normal eyes to track, bursting into the open space beyond the landing.

<!-- paragraph:5709 para_id:0000164D style:Normal -->
Maya sprinted to the railing and looked down just in time to see Arrek plummeting, limbs tucked, his body already turning as if anticipating the impact. At the same time, two figures in leather armor rounded the corner of the staircase—Harvick’s friends—the ones whose shock she’d barely registered when she’d blasted past them moments earlier.

<!-- paragraph:5710 para_id:0000164E style:Normal -->
“One of you get a healer!” she shouted, voice cracking. And then she vaulted over the railing after Arrek. Gravity seized her like a fist. Her stomach lurched into her throat. The acceleration was brutal—far faster than she’d expected—and she saw Arrek strike the ground far below her in a flash of light, a telltale flare snapping around him. A heartbeat later, he leapt forward out of her field of view with that same impossible speed.

<!-- paragraph:5711 para_id:0000164F style:Normal -->
She hit the stone a moment after him, smashing down hard enough that her Shroud had to flare instinctively, the impact blooming into a burst of heat. The ground trembled beneath her boots.

<!-- paragraph:5712 para_id:00001650 style:Normal -->
How did he—was that a Shroud? Her mind barely had time to form the thought before she saw him again—just the streak of motion as he turned down a corridor and vanished. She blasted off after him, siphoning heat from floor and walls, propelling herself forward with rapid-fire bursts of expanding air, her Shroud shaping the backlash. He was headed for the entrance.

<!-- paragraph:5713 para_id:00001651 style:Normal -->
I can’t lose sight of him. If he gets over the outer wall, I’ll never find him again. She chased him through the hallways, turn after turn, the citadel a blur around her. Every corridor, every corner pushed her to her limits. She could feel her heartbeat cracking against her ribs, her Shroud flickering dangerously as she accelerated.

<!-- paragraph:5714 para_id:00001652 style:Normal -->
Then she saw the front entrance ahead—Arrek flying through it, mask gleaming under the moonlight. She launched herself upward at an angle, heat exploding from under her boots. She rose above him, a hawk tracking prey across open ground, watching the straight, predictable vector of his movement. She knew exactly where he would leap next.

<!-- paragraph:5715 para_id:00001653 style:Normal -->
She blasted forward and cut him off, landing in a burst of fire that scorched the stone. Arrek barreled toward her, unable to stop in time. He skidded hard, heels digging into the earth as he tried to redirect, dust pluming behind him. Through his mask, she couldn’t see his face—but she could feel his shock.

<!-- paragraph:5716 para_id:00001654 style:Normal -->
She reached deep—too deep—and pulled every scrap of heat she could from the ground. It surged into her hands, into the air before her, into a roiling sphere of pressure that grew faster than she could shape it. She wasn’t sure what she meant to do with it—not arrest him, not kill him, not anything she could name—just stop him. Her anger flared, and the sphere swelled in response, ballooning out of her control.

<!-- paragraph:5717 para_id:00001655 style:Normal -->
“You need to stop running,” she yelled.

<!-- paragraph:5718 para_id:00001656 style:Normal -->
The orb ignited. Heat roared outward and Davian threw his arms up to shield himself. The sphere kept growing, far too large, far too unstable. It hummed with a will of its own, fueled by her rage, expanding faster than she could contain. Realizing she had moments at most, Maya carved a single, desperate channel straight down to dump the energy into the earth

<!-- paragraph:5719 para_id:00001657 style:Normal -->
The ground erupted. An explosion blasted upward from beneath her feet. She barely had time to flare her Shroud before she was thrown into the air, tossed like a leaf in a wildfire. The world spun in a smear of stone and flame. She couldn’t focus enough to carve a channel, couldn’t orient herself—just tumbling, helpless—clinging to her Shroud.

<!-- paragraph:5720 para_id:00001658 style:Normal -->
She slammed into the outer wall. A blast of flame shot out of her Shroud on impact, wild and uncontrolled, scattering in erratic arcs across the courtyard. Stone burned beneath her palms as she slid down the wall, hitting the ground in a daze.

<!-- paragraph:5721 para_id:00001659 style:Normal -->
Get up.

<!-- paragraph:5722 para_id:0000165A style:Normal -->
She forced herself to her feet. Her vision was washed out, everything burned white by the afterimage of the explosion. She blinked, but nothing came into focus. Shapes wavered.

<!-- paragraph:5723 para_id:0000165B style:Normal -->
It was too much. The entire day—every moment of strain, every frayed nerve, every betrayal—compounded with the tearing agony of the Skein and the overuse of her magic. Her limbs trembled.

<!-- paragraph:5724 para_id:0000165C style:Normal -->
She sank to one knee as guards ran toward her, shouting her name.

<!-- paragraph:5725 para_id:0000165D style:Normal -->
The last words she heard herself say before darkness took her were barely a whisper: “I need to check on Niall…”

<!-- paragraph:5728 para_id:00001660 style:Title -->
# 🤱 Chapter 29

<!-- paragraph:5729 para_id:00001661 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 29

<!-- paragraph:5731 para_id:00001663 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5732 para_id:00001664 style:Normal -->
Kelyra sat across from Jalen at the scarred wooden table, though sat was generous; she couldn’t get comfortable, couldn’t stop shifting in her seat. The documents they’d stolen from Sorevin—once their most valuable tool—were spread between them, useless now, like relics from a life that had belonged to someone else.

<!-- paragraph:5733 para_id:00001665 style:Normal -->
They’d already argued themselves hoarse. About Davian’s appearance—wild-eyed, rambling, covered in fading Ink. About telling him the truth. About the Harbingers. About Archeon.

<!-- paragraph:5734 para_id:00001666 style:Normal -->
Jalen stared at nothing, elbows on the table, hands entwined. The tension in his jaw was visible even in the dim firelight. They had sent Kael and Lirin to bed against a small rebellion of tears and questions, and now there was nothing to do except sit in the wreckage of their choices.

<!-- paragraph:5735 para_id:00001667 style:Normal -->
Kelyra dragged her knife across the table. Wood curled away in pale ribbons. She didn’t remember starting to carve, or what she meant to carve, but she could feel the rough grooves under her palm.

<!-- paragraph:5736 para_id:00001668 style:Normal -->
“I’m telling you,” she said—again—“we should have asked him for a timeline. Who knows how long he’ll waste in there, confusing himself again, wandering in circles—”

<!-- paragraph:5737 para_id:00001669 style:Normal -->
Jalen exhaled, long and slow, the sound of someone refusing the invitation into another argument. They fell back into silence. After a while, Kelyra realized she had carved the same shape into the wood over and over. The Nizan. Its sharp angles looked like an accusation.

<!-- paragraph:5738 para_id:0000166A style:Normal -->
She stared at it. Then lifted her head sharply. “You know,” she said, voice too bright, too brittle, “he volunteered for this.”

<!-- paragraph:5739 para_id:0000166B style:Normal -->
Jalen didn’t look up. “I know.”

<!-- paragraph:5740 para_id:0000166C style:Normal -->
They sat another minute. The fire popped. Kelyra’s skin crawled with the need to move. She stood abruptly and began pacing the cramped room.

<!-- paragraph:5741 para_id:0000166D style:Normal -->
“There weren’t even any suitable women for me to impersonate,” she said, throwing the words over her shoulder. “They were all too well-established.” She flicked a hand toward the papers on the table.

<!-- paragraph:5742 para_id:0000166E style:Normal -->
Jalen finally lifted his eyes. “That’s why you go those out? And what were you going to do? Read through them again and second-guess what’s already happened?” His voice was gentle. The attempt at comfort made her want to scream. She didn’t answer. She paced instead, feet carrying her in tight, angry circuits across the floor.

<!-- paragraph:5743 para_id:0000166F style:Normal -->
“The Harbingers are on our side,” she snapped. “You might not like them, but we need them. They’re the only ones powerful enough to bring real change. The only ones willing.”

<!-- paragraph:5744 para_id:00001670 style:Normal -->
Jalen gestured toward the table—the thousands of tiny cuts she’d carved deep into its surface, the repeated Nizan glaring up at them from the wood.

<!-- paragraph:5745 para_id:00001671 style:Normal -->
“I agree,” he said mildly. “Negation is certainly a kind of change.”

<!-- paragraph:5746 para_id:00001672 style:Normal -->
Kelyra turned on Jalen so fast her chair scraped back across the floorboards. “You weren’t there,” she said, knife still in her hand. “In the Archeon breeding program. You don’t know what it was like.”

<!-- paragraph:5747 para_id:00001673 style:Normal -->
Jalen didn’t flinch. “You’re right,” he said. “I believe we’ve previously established that thoroughly.” His tone—calm, steady, non-reactive—only made the pressure in her chest spike harder. She wanted to scream, to shake him, to make him feel it - they’re monsters, we need to kill them.

<!-- paragraph:5748 para_id:00001674 style:Normal -->
She knew exactly what Jalen’s response would be. Not argument—worse. That quiet, maddening refusal to escalate. That constant redirection toward strategy, consolidation, patience. Build the Masked Clades. Gather strength. Plan for years. No mass killing. No uprisings. No violence unless necessary. He had no idea what “necessary” meant.

<!-- paragraph:5749 para_id:00001675 style:Normal -->
He had never been locked in one of Archeon’s clean rooms while they measured her like livestock. Had never been shoved into a training pit with men twice her size, told to “prove viability,” while Serathis watched from above with that thin, clinical smile. Her breath stuttered. The room shrank. She was there again. In the breeding block. In the pit. Her fist cracking against another man’s jaw as he slammed into her ribs. The taste of blood.

<!-- paragraph:5750 para_id:00001676 style:Normal -->
“There’s no running from something like that,” she said. Her voice felt far away, like it belonged to someone else. “There’s no hiding.”

<!-- paragraph:5751 para_id:00001677 style:Normal -->
Jalen stood—slowly, deliberately—and crossed the small space between them. He placed a hand on her shoulder, grounding her with a warmth she hadn’t expected. The room snapped back into focus. She blinked up at him, disoriented. He looked like he was about to speak—maybe something reassuring, maybe something she’d have hated—

<!-- paragraph:5752 para_id:00001678 style:Normal -->
A deafening crash tore through the hideout. The walls shook. Dust rained from the beams overhead. Kelyra’s heart stopped. Archeon. Smashing through the gate. Pulverizing Concord members like children. Dragging her back to finish what they started—

<!-- paragraph:5753 para_id:00001679 style:Normal -->
Her knives were already in her hands before the thought finished. She sprinted for the door, Jalen right behind her, calling her name. She ran harder. If they’re coming, she thought, let them kill me before they realize who I am.

<!-- paragraph:5754 para_id:0000167A style:Normal -->
She shook her head violently, trying to shove the memories back into whatever pit they crawled from. Her breath felt too sharp, too thin. The courtyard loomed ahead, lit by the flicker of torches.

<!-- paragraph:5755 para_id:0000167B style:Normal -->
She forced the memories down—jammed them into the corner of her mind where she kept everything poisonous—and suddenly the adrenaline wasn’t dragging her; she was riding it. Her thoughts snapped into brutal clarity. Even without a Shroud, she still had Archeon blood, and the old conditioning sharpened her senses, accelerated her mind until all the motion in the courtyard slowed to a crawl.

<!-- paragraph:5756 para_id:0000167C style:Normal -->
Half the wooden gate hung crooked on its hinges, the splintered edge blown inward by something with tremendous force. The sentries on the wall had only just begun to react—shouting, scanning the darkness beyond the breach, fumbling to take positions. Their movements looked slow to her, unbearably slow, like wading through syrup.

<!-- paragraph:5757 para_id:0000167D style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s mind analyzed the entire courtyard in a single instant. No movement. No sigils glowing. No refracted Shroud-light dancing in the dark.

<!-- paragraph:5758 para_id:0000167E style:Normal -->
Just a single figure slumped in the center of the open space. A man? Unmoving.

<!-- paragraph:5759 para_id:0000167F style:Normal -->
Her eyes weren’t yet adjusted enough to see his features, but she saw no aura, no radiance of power, no telltale shimmer of a Shroud. Not Archeon?

<!-- paragraph:5760 para_id:00001680 style:Normal -->
The relief almost knocked her off her feet. Jalen reached her side, breath uneven, but she didn’t look at him. She couldn’t. She forced her spine straight, pulled her shoulders back, and slipped the mask of Concord leadership over her fear like armor.

<!-- paragraph:5761 para_id:00001681 style:Normal -->
“Light!” she barked. “Bring light, now!”

<!-- paragraph:5762 para_id:00001682 style:Normal -->
For several agonizing seconds, nothing. Then hurried footsteps, and someone rushed into the courtyard with a torch held high. They normally avoided flame outside at night—light drew eyes, and eyes brought death—but tonight, caution had already failed.

<!-- paragraph:5763 para_id:00001683 style:Normal -->
When the torchlight hit the man’s body, Kelyra’s mind devoured the details all at once—brown hair matted with blood, clothing torn to ribbons, limbs bent at angles that made no anatomical sense. She took it in like a starving animal, every detail slotting into place before she consciously registered it. And then she saw the ink.

<!-- paragraph:5764 para_id:00001684 style:Normal -->
Black spirals. Crawling. Flickering. Webbing his arms all the way to the shoulders. Her breath left her in one violent exhale.

<!-- paragraph:5765 para_id:00001685 style:Normal -->
“Davian!” She was already moving—knees hitting the packed dirt, hands sliding under his shoulders, pulling him up and into her arms. His head lolled back, eyes unfocused and glassy. His mouth moved before his mind seemed to catch up.

<!-- paragraph:5766 para_id:00001686 style:Normal -->
“I got it,” he murmured.

<!-- paragraph:5767 para_id:00001687 style:Normal -->
The words were slurred, drifting, a child’s voice coming out of a broken man. Without his mask, without the hard façade he wore like skin, he looked younger than he had in years. He looked like the baby she had held against her chest, the perfect weight of him, the warmth of something that was hers. He looked like the child she’d sworn she would protect from every monster in the world, especially the ones that looked human. And now his body was broken because of her choices. Just like Selina—No. She crushed the thought beneath sheer force of will.

<!-- paragraph:5768 para_id:00001688 style:Normal -->
“Someone bring healing supplies!” she shouted, voice cracking with urgency, with fury at whoever wasn’t fast enough. “Now!” Davian’s eyelids fluttered. His breathing was thin, ragged. His face had gone ashy, lips pale. He sagged in her arms, unconscious or close to it. She held him tighter, terrified that if she let go he would simply break further.

<!-- paragraph:5769 para_id:00001689 style:Normal -->
Jalen crouched beside them, his normally unreadable expression cracking open with something that looked almost like horror. “Kelyra—look.”

<!-- paragraph:5770 para_id:0000168A style:Normal -->
She followed his gaze to Davian’s right hand. The shattered bones were…moving. No—twisting. Grinding against each other with faint, wet cracking sounds. Ink surged along the fractures, pooling, knitting, forcing the structure back into alignment in slow, grotesque jerks. The fingers spasmed, then elongated, then snapped back into shape.

<!-- paragraph:5771 para_id:0000168B style:Normal -->
A figure sprinted across the courtyard, a Concord woman clutching a small bundle of supplies—bandages, rough splints, a waterskin. Tessa, the youngest of their members, half-trained as a field medic. She skidded to a halt the moment the torchlight revealed Davian’s twisting hand.

<!-- paragraph:5772 para_id:0000168C style:Normal -->
“By the Creator…” she breathed, the words cracking in her throat.

<!-- paragraph:5773 para_id:0000168D style:Normal -->
None of them moved. They watched—horrified, riveted—as the ink pulsed again, the bones in Davian’s forearm jerking back into place one by one with sickening snaps. His shoulder rolled in its socket, spine arching as the realignment spread up his body. The air itself felt wrong, too light, as if gravity were waiting to reverse itself.

<!-- paragraph:5774 para_id:0000168E style:Normal -->
Kelyra couldn’t take her eyes off him. “So he’s going to be okay,” she said to Jalen, voice shaking. “He has to be okay.”

<!-- paragraph:5775 para_id:0000168F style:Normal -->
Jalen didn’t answer. Not right away. His face—normally a mask of steady calm—was open now, stunned. “I…don’t know,” he said quietly. “Kelyra, I’ve never seen—”

<!-- paragraph:5776 para_id:00001690 style:Normal -->
Davian convulsed. The ink on his arms flared with a dim, sickly light. His chest heaved—and he bolted upright, gasping like someone dragged back from drowning. His head fell back, eyes rolling white. “He won’t let me die,” Davian gasped. “Not until I reach the end of the dark thread and it snaps. I have to follow it… follow it like water running down a channel…”

<!-- paragraph:5777 para_id:00001691 style:Normal -->
His fingers twitched. His hand fumbled toward the pouch at his waist, as if guided by something that wasn’t entirely him. Kelyra steadied him with one arm while he clumsily pulled free a dark, perfectly smooth marble.

<!-- paragraph:5778 para_id:00001692 style:Normal -->
He held it out to her with a shaking hand. “And I left her behind,” he whispered, voice cracking. “The only bright thread. The thread of flame.”

<!-- paragraph:5779 para_id:00001693 style:Normal -->
Kelyra swallowed hard and took the sphere from him—not because she chose to, but because something in her muscles obeyed before thought arrived. It was cool in her palm. Not cold with temperature—cool as if it was something that existed apart from this world, could never be warmed by its heat.

<!-- paragraph:5780 para_id:00001694 style:Normal -->
“What are you saying?” she demanded. “Who? Davian—who?” But the instant the sphere fully passed into her hand, his body went slack. He slumped back, unconscious.

<!-- paragraph:5781 para_id:00001695 style:Normal -->
Kelyra didn’t move at first. Her mind had been hurled outward—the weight of her past, every choice that had led to this courtyard, to Davian broken in her arms, flaring behind her like a pair of burning wings. And ahead of her, a future she could no longer see the shape of. It was too much. Too vast. Paralyzing.

<!-- paragraph:5782 para_id:00001696 style:Normal -->
She shoved it down. Again. Hard. The courtyard came back into focus. The cold night air. The jagged half-gate. The smear of torchlight across Davian’s ink-wreathed skin. The weight of his body in her arms. She forced her jaw to unclench. “Tessa,” she said, voice rough. “Get a cot. Put him on it. Bring it inside.”

<!-- paragraph:5783 para_id:00001697 style:Normal -->
Tessa ran as though chased, two more Concord members stumbling after her. Others backed away—not in organized motion but like animals startled by lightning—fear making their bodies move before thought. Kelyra stayed kneeling beside Davian, her breath seesawing in and out of her lungs—too deep, too long, too shaky. Every inhale made her feel like she was going to choke on her own heartbeat.

<!-- paragraph:5784 para_id:00001698 style:Normal -->
Jalen, standing behind her, exhaled through his nose. “I can tell what you’re going to say.”

<!-- paragraph:5785 para_id:00001699 style:Normal -->
She didn’t look at him. She didn’t need to. “We need to go to the Harbingers,” she said. “Tonight.”

<!-- paragraph:5786 para_id:0000169A style:Normal -->
Jalen closed his eyes for half a second, like bracing against a blow. Kelyra pressed on. “We don’t know what happened out there, but we can’t risk that someone followed him. Or that this—” she lifted the dark marble slightly “—gave us away. We need what the Harbingers can offer us, and then we move. We change locations. Immediately.”

<!-- paragraph:5787 para_id:0000169B style:Normal -->
“They’re not going to like that,” Jalen murmured.

<!-- paragraph:5788 para_id:0000169C style:Normal -->
“I know they’re not going to like that,” she snapped, whirling on him. “That’s the point of being the leader—I don’t have to care whether they like it. We don’t have a choice. And they’re going to understand that, one way or another.”

<!-- paragraph:5789 para_id:0000169D style:Normal -->
She jabbed a hand toward the ruined gate—the shattered boards, the splinters scattered like bones. “That’s all the proof we need. The Harbingers can give us the power to defend ourselves. To finally be safe.”

<!-- paragraph:5790 para_id:0000169E style:Normal -->
Jalen’s gaze lingered on her. “Just to defend ourselves?” She didn’t answer. Because the truth was a flame too hot to be spoken aloud.

<!-- paragraph:5791 para_id:0000169F style:Normal -->
Tessa came running back into the courtyard with three others carrying a cot. The interruption snapped the moment. Kelyra turned away from Jalen’s question, from his eyes that saw too clearly. She focused on Davian. Very gently, she helped guide his limp body onto the waiting canvas. She brushed a strand of blood-stiff hair back from his forehead.

<!-- paragraph:5792 para_id:000016A0 style:Normal -->
And without saying a word, she shot Jalen a look sharp enough to cut wood—because she knew he was still thinking about the question she hadn’t answered. And he knew she had answered it anyway.

<!-- paragraph:5793 para_id:000016A1 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stood, legs unsteady for a moment, clutching the dark orb in a white-knuckled grip. “Let’s go,” she said to Jalen.

<!-- paragraph:5794 para_id:000016A2 style:Normal -->
She turned to the Concord members still gathered in the courtyard—wide-eyed, unsettled, waiting for her to fail or to lead—and forced steel into her voice. “This is the biggest job we’ve ever done. The Harbingers will reward us—all of us—beyond anything we’ve received before.”

<!-- paragraph:5795 para_id:000016A3 style:Normal -->
The words tasted thin, but the effect rippled through the group: a sharp inhale here, a faint murmur there. Hope or fear—she didn’t care which, as long as it kept them aligned.

<!-- paragraph:5796 para_id:000016A4 style:Normal -->
One woman stepped forward - tall and blunt with a powerful frame - Karra. “Let me come with you. Davian saved my life during the Sorevin job,” she said, squaring her shoulders. “Let me help you out.”

<!-- paragraph:5797 para_id:000016A5 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s first instinct was to say no. Because she could see the undercurrent in her stance—the flicker of mistrust. The fear she and Jalen might take whatever reward the Harbingers gave and vanish into the night, leaving the rest of them to rot. She could feel the eyes of the others on her like knives. Waiting. Measuring. Judging.

<!-- paragraph:5798 para_id:000016A6 style:Normal -->
She bought herself a heartbeat by sliding the orb into her pouch and bending to retrieve her twin knives from the dirt. She picked them up in a single fluid motion—practiced, natural, but unmistakably threatening. Karra flinched. Kelyra tightened her grip on the hilts, feeling the familiar balance, the reassuring weight. Her whole body was a taut wire humming with adrenaline. For a razor-thin moment, one thought flashed through her mind with terrible clarity: I could kill them all in a moment.

<!-- paragraph:5799 para_id:000016A7 style:Normal -->
And they knew it. But the next thought followed hard on the heels of the first: I need them. I can’t do this alone. Jalen’s hand touched her shoulder. Grounding. Supportive. A reminder that whatever storm she brought down on the world, they would face its consequences together. He might question her. He might temper her. But when the moment came to act, he stood with her—always.

<!-- paragraph:5800 para_id:000016A8 style:Normal -->
Kelyra let out a tense exhale and forced herself to relax a fraction. “Fine,” she said at last. “Just you, Karra.”

<!-- paragraph:5801 para_id:000016A9 style:Normal -->
Karra released a breath she clearly hadn’t realized he’d been holding and gave her a small, grateful smile. She didn’t return it. Instead, she spun on her heel and strode out through the shattered gate.

<!-- paragraph:5802 para_id:000016AA style:Normal -->
Behind her she heard Jalen and Karra jog to close the distance, their footsteps sharp in the quiet street. She slid her knives back into their sheaths, but the tension didn’t leave her muscles. Her body stayed coiled, alert, as it always did - as if every shadow held Archeon hands waiting to drag her back.

<!-- paragraph:5803 para_id:000016AB style:Normal -->
They walked in silence for several moments, the night thick around them. Then Jalen tapped her shoulder. She didn’t stop walking; she only angled her body slightly so she could see him in her periphery. His hands moved low at his chest—silent, precise, both following nighttime protocol and keeping Karra from reading over his shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:5804 para_id:000016AC style:Normal -->
How do we do this? he signed. We don’t have a way to meet the Harbingers directly.

<!-- paragraph:5805 para_id:000016AD style:Normal -->
Kelyra signed back immediately, sharp movements betraying her irritation. We use the dead drop. Their network will have picked up that something happened. They’ll be waiting.

<!-- paragraph:5806 para_id:000016AE style:Normal -->
Jalen frowned. We’re not supposed to monitor the dead drop. Protocol says we leave it alone until they contact us.

<!-- paragraph:5807 para_id:000016AF style:Normal -->
Her answering sign was almost a jab, fast and clipped with frustration she barely managed to hold in check. Sheol take the protocol. This is different. They’ll have to make an exception.

<!-- paragraph:5808 para_id:000016B0 style:Normal -->
She tapped her pouch—the weight of the dark sphere pressing coldly against her hip. This is valuable enough. They’ll want it immediately.

<!-- paragraph:5809 para_id:000016B1 style:Normal -->
Jalen’s shoulders sagged with a reluctant exhale, but he didn’t argue. They passed the rest of the journey in total silence. The quiet wasn’t companionable; it was the taut silence of people carrying too much fear and too many secrets. The streets shifted gradually as they walked, from the mostly abandoned outer sector into a narrower, grimier artery where the city’s unwanted gathered. Here the walls rose high and close, trapping the stale smell of ale and wet stone. Here the few lanterns sputtering behind barred windows did nothing to touch the deeper dark.

<!-- paragraph:5810 para_id:000016B2 style:Normal -->
Thugs lounged in the mouths of alleys, faces half-lit, eyes predatory. They looked up as the three approached. And then they looked away. The way Kelyra, Jalen, and Karra carried themselves communicated something in the unspoken language of Masked Clades: Interference with us means your death.

<!-- paragraph:5811 para_id:000016B3 style:Normal -->
Even the sparse foot traffic parted around them without a word. Kelyra scanned the street automatically, marking exits, choke-points, potential lookouts. She picked a tavern at the bend of the road—the kind of place too poor to attract officials and too mean to attract attention. A few rough crates served as seating, stacked outside where the stone could leech the cold into them. Heavy, cracked, and stained: too cheap to be worth stealing.

<!-- paragraph:5812 para_id:000016B4 style:Normal -->
Iron mugs—massive, ugly things—were chained to brackets hammered into the tavern wall. None of them had been washed in months. Maybe years. Perfect.

<!-- paragraph:5813 para_id:000016B5 style:Normal -->
Kelyra sat on one of the crates. The wood gave slightly under her weight but held. The alley was so narrow the walls seemed to press inward, funneling the night into something almost solid. The darkness muted even torch-glow from nearby windows, muffling the world into an intimate hush. This was the kind of dark she understood. The kind she trusted.

<!-- paragraph:5814 para_id:000016B6 style:Normal -->
A huge man—almost Karra’s equal in mass, bald head gleaming faintly in the dim streetlight—stepped out of the tavern’s doorway. His gaze swept over the three of them, lingering on Karra, then Kelyra.

<!-- paragraph:5815 para_id:000016B7 style:Normal -->
“You have to drink to sit,” he grunted. Kelyra didn’t bother responding aloud. She just tipped her chin toward Jalen. He flicked a silver halo in a smooth underhand arc. The coin spun once in the air before the barkeep caught it against his palm. At the same time, Jalen signed low and sharp at waist level: Just us. Understand?

<!-- paragraph:5816 para_id:000016B8 style:Normal -->
The man’s eyes widened. He took them all in again, some level of recognition flickering, and gave a hurried nod. He disappeared back inside as if he was fleeing a wild animal. Kelyra pried up the edge of her crate seat with her fingertips, working loose a sliver of wood. She didn’t bother to hide the cracking sound as it snapped free. With the small knife she kept in her boot, she carved a za into its surface—quick, efficient strokes.

<!-- paragraph:5817 para_id:000016B9 style:Normal -->
She tossed it to Karra, who caught it one-handed. Jalen tapped Karra’s shoulder and signed: Down the alley across the way. Three doorways. Loose flagstone on the second step.

<!-- paragraph:5818 para_id:000016BA style:Normal -->
Karra nodded and left without a sound. Despite her size, she moved with uncanny quiet, absorbed effortlessly by the darkness. Once she was gone, Jalen signed to Kelyra: What did you write?

<!-- paragraph:5819 para_id:000016BB style:Normal -->
Kelyra leaned back slightly, letting the shadows swallow the edges of her face. Her gestures were small, economical. It doesn’t matter what I write. They’ll understand.

<!-- paragraph:5820 para_id:000016BC style:Normal -->
Jalen exhaled slowly, then angled himself against the wall beside her. To anyone passing by, he looked like a man resting after a long day. But Kelyra saw the truth in the tiny flickers of his eyes—constantly scanning the alley in both directions, marking every movement, every potential threat.

<!-- paragraph:5821 para_id:000016BD style:Normal -->
Karra emerged from the alley sooner than Kelyra expected—moving quickly, but not hurried—and beside her walked a hooded figure. Dark-bearded. Broad-shouldered. Moving with that loose, predatory confidence the Harbingers cultivated like a second skin. Kelyra didn’t bother masking her exhale. It always has to be this guy.

<!-- paragraph:5822 para_id:000016BE style:Normal -->
He stopped beside them in the narrow street, didn’t bother pulling back his hood. The torchlight from a distant window flickered off the hard line of his jaw, his expression unreadable.

<!-- paragraph:5823 para_id:000016BF style:Normal -->
He raised his hands and signed, sharp and unambiguous: You have the Heart.

<!-- paragraph:5824 para_id:000016C0 style:Normal -->
Not a question. Kelyra met his shadowed eyes. He smiled. Follow, he signed. Then he turned, already walking away. Kelyra fell in behind him without looking at Jalen, because she didn’t need to. She heard his footsteps fall into place a half-beat later, steady and loyal. Karra brought up the rear, a silent wall of muscle whose breathing barely disturbed the air. The Harbinger didn’t look back as he led them into the alley.

<!-- paragraph:5825 para_id:000016C1 style:Normal -->
They followed him through a twisting snarl of alleys, the kind of path only someone born to shadows could memorize. Even Kelyra—who prided herself on mapping spaces instinctively—lost the thread of their route. At least twice she was certain they took the same turn more than once, looping back only to turn again. The streets were a maze, folding into themselves, lit only by the occasional sickly lamp guttering behind shutters.

<!-- paragraph:5826 para_id:000016C2 style:Normal -->
Then, without warning, a doorway yawned open in front of them—nothing but blackness inside. The Harbinger guide was already descending into it, swallowed by the dark before she could blink. Kelyra hesitated at the threshold. One hand drifted to the hilt of each knife. She waited for a sound—movement, breath, the shift of boots behind walls. An ambush. Something. They won’t betray you, she told herself. They never break a deal. Not with those who still have value.

<!-- paragraph:5827 para_id:000016C3 style:Normal -->
She stepped into the doorway. A sharp crack echoed off stone—flint struck against steel—and a torch flared to life ahead of her. The guide held it at arm’s length, already walking deeper into the tunnel, the flame bobbing with his steps as though he’d lit it mid-stride.

<!-- paragraph:5828 para_id:000016C4 style:Normal -->
They followed. They had no choice. The tunnel wound and doubled, spiraled down and then sideways, the walls narrowing and widening unpredictably. Kelyra’s breath quickened; even her honed instincts couldn’t orient her. It felt like being led in circles, or knots, or patterns meant to confuse the mind into submission. Then the corridor turned sharply and spat them into a vast chamber.

<!-- paragraph:5829 para_id:000016C5 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stopped short. Delayed familiarity seeped into her mind like a tendril of ink. She had been here, once. The first time she met the Harbingers. When she was younger—so much younger—hungry with rage, wild from grief, newly unmoored after Selina’s death. She had been ready to bargain away anything, everything, to make someone pay. The Harbingers had found her then—no, chosen her—and brought her into this room.

<!-- paragraph:5830 para_id:000016C6 style:Normal -->
A flash tore through her mind:

<!-- paragraph:5831 para_id:000016C7 style:Normal -->
Ink seeping into her skin like a hundred burning needles. Her scream echoing against stone. Then waking to power—real power—for the first time. Speed. Precision. Strength. Her elbow slamming into the throat of the man who led the Concord then… The crack of something important breaking. Vindication washing through her like heat, as if killing him somehow erased his failure to protect them.

<!-- paragraph:5832 para_id:000016C8 style:Normal -->
She inhaled sharply. Too fast. Her heart sped. Her hands trembled, though she kept them hidden at her sides. The vastness of the chamber pressed in, just as it had the first time, its stone columns disappearing upward into darkness. She told herself—sternly, silently—This room is for momentous meetings. This is an honor.

<!-- paragraph:5833 para_id:000016C9 style:Normal -->
Their guide’s torch sputtered—and went out. At the same instant, another light flared ahead of them, white-hot and clean, as if summoned from nothing. It washed across the chamber and revealed a low stone table, and seated behind it—A young man. Familiar. Impossible.

<!-- paragraph:5834 para_id:000016CA style:Normal -->
The Harbinger leader.

<!-- paragraph:5835 para_id:000016CB style:Normal -->
A second figure sat to his left, face completely masked, posture so still it read as inhuman. Kelyra blinked in disorientation, her vision swimming for a beat—because their guide, the bearded man, had vanished. Not slipped into shadow. Not stepped away. Gone.

<!-- paragraph:5836 para_id:000016CC style:Normal -->
She tightened her grip on her knives but forced herself forward. Jalen walked at her shoulder; Karra’s steps were heavier behind them, echoing faintly in the vast chamber.

<!-- paragraph:5837 para_id:000016CD style:Normal -->
The young man smiled. Smooth, elegant features. Unnaturally bright eyes. Sharp cheekbones exaggerated by the deep shadows under the new patterns of ink spiraling across his skin—patterns she had not seen last time she met him. Patterns that crawled from his jaw into his hairline, around his eyes like a crown of thorns carved from darkness. Something was wrong with him. Deeply wrong. And yet the aura he radiated—calm, amused, delighted—pressed against her senses like heat from a forge.

<!-- paragraph:5838 para_id:000016CE style:Normal -->
She had heard of him before she ever met him. Everyone in the underworld had. That nameless youth who ran a small, clever smuggling ring. Bright. Promising. But ultimately irrelevant. Until one day he started handing out power.

<!-- paragraph:5839 para_id:000016CF style:Normal -->
Ink Boons. Real power—raw, intoxicating, unstoppable—etched directly into the skin. A shortcut from obscurity to dominance. And the people had flocked to him. Because no one else could offer such things. In a matter of months, his syndicate - now calling themselves the Harbingers - had devoured the underworld. Every Masked Clade. Every slum. Every shadow economy the High Clades pretended not to see, as if all it was dust beneath their gilded boots. All of it rearranged around him like iron filings around a magnet.

<!-- paragraph:5840 para_id:000016D0 style:Normal -->
He smiled as they approached, and for a moment—just a flicker—the shifting torchlight made it seem like the ink on his face moved. Writhing, like something alive beneath his skin. A chill worked its way down Kelyra’s spine. She cursed herself for ever dealing with this man. Not because she regretted the choice—she didn’t—but because she had always known this was the road she would walk. Power was the only path to safety, and this contract with the devil the only path to power. She had told herself she could bear the cost.

<!-- paragraph:5841 para_id:000016D1 style:Normal -->
And so she had lied. Lied to Davian. Lied to her younger children. Lied that their bloodline was noble, blessed, destined for greatness. Lied that they were part of some heroic lineage instead of descendants of Archeon’s cruelty. Lied while training Davian to be the weapon she wasn’t, the weapon Selina never could be. Lied while pressing a dagger into his hands—a stolen Archeon tool with a false name, Antithesis—and calling it an heirloom.

<!-- paragraph:5842 para_id:000016D2 style:Normal -->
Now her lies had led here.

<!-- paragraph:5843 para_id:000016D3 style:Normal -->
She stepped into the circle of torchlight, Jalen and Karra falling into position behind her like shadows. She reached into her pouch. The calloused fingers that had carved so many lives out of the Skein trembled as she grasped the cool, dark orb. Too cool. Too still. A thing that didn’t belong in this world.

<!-- paragraph:5844 para_id:000016D4 style:Normal -->
She held it out in front of her like a charm—one of those cheap, zan-inscribed pendants hawked in street markets to ward off spirits. A child’s toy. A lie. But the weight of it made her arm shake. The man’s smile widened further, impossibly, and Kelyra had the sickening thought: How can he still look so young?

<!-- paragraph:5845 para_id:000016D5 style:Normal -->
She forced her voice steady. “We did it,” she said. “We fulfilled the impossible contract you forced on us. Now hold up your end of the bargain.”

<!-- paragraph:5846 para_id:000016D6 style:Normal -->
The Harbinger leader lifted one hand. Not a gesture. A summons. And she felt it—an impulse tugging through muscle and bone—as if her body belonged to the command, not to her. Before she could stop herself, she tossed the orb. He caught it without looking at it, without effort, and only then raised it to the light—holding it delicately, almost reverently, as though it were a jewel that refracted light instead of swallowing it whole. His smile sharpened as the darkness inside the sphere seemed to pulse in recognition.

<!-- paragraph:5847 para_id:000016D7 style:Normal -->
He stopped turning the orb. His smile didn’t fade, exactly—it simply collapsed into disinterest, like a puppet letting its strings go slack. Without looking at Kelyra or Jalen or Karra, he handed the Heart to the masked figure beside him. Not gently. Not reverently. As if passing off a rock he’d grown tired of examining.

<!-- paragraph:5848 para_id:000016D8 style:Normal -->
Then he leaned back on his elbows and sighed, almost theatrically. “Ah yes,” he said, “the bargain… the contract. I was just discussing that little fiction with your lovely little Shade.”

<!-- paragraph:5849 para_id:000016D9 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s whole body went rigid. Jalen’s hand brushed her sleeve—a warning—but she didn’t relax. The man’s grin spread, ink shifting faintly across his cheeks like shadows trying to flee.

<!-- paragraph:5850 para_id:000016DA style:Normal -->
“As we discussed,” he continued, “there are no bargains. Only inevitability. But if—” he wiggled his fingers lazily, as if flipping a coin in the air—“theoretically there were a bargain, it would involve that hollow man. The one full of lightning and despair.” He flicked his gaze toward the ceiling, toward some imaginary point far above them. “Snipped clean from the Skein.”

<!-- paragraph:5851 para_id:000016DB style:Normal -->
Kelyra felt her stomach twist. He wasn’t done. “But his thread—mmm, that thread of bright lightning, the one that binds and binds and binds—” he breathed the word out like a lover’s name—“I can still see it.”

<!-- paragraph:5852 para_id:000016DC style:Normal -->
His eyes unfocused. His head tilted. “And the bright thread…” He squinted, leaning forward as if peering straight into a blinding sun. “Still shining. Still burning. Too bright to look at.”

<!-- paragraph:5853 para_id:000016DD style:Normal -->
Kelyra snapped. “So look somewhere else!” she shouted, voice cracking with the desperation she could no longer disguise. “The deal was for the Heart. Niall was an extra, an add-on—give me what you promised!”

<!-- paragraph:5854 para_id:000016DE style:Normal -->
For the first time, he looked at her directly. “Oh,” he said, almost sweetly, “what did I promise?” He tapped his ink-stained cheek with one finger. “The strength? The vitality? The Ink?”

<!-- paragraph:5855 para_id:000016DF style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s pulse hammered. “I already gave that,” he said, smile stretching thin. “I already leaned on the scales for your Shade. I pushed and pushed—” His voice sharpened, a sudden blade—“and pushed and pushed—”

<!-- paragraph:5856 para_id:000016E0 style:Normal -->
Kelyra flinched. Not from the volume, but the emotion. He was angry. She had never seen him angry. “And I pulled,” he hissed. “I pulled the threads taut, wove them, snapped others, twisted them into knots—and the threads are still there!”

<!-- paragraph:5857 para_id:000016E1 style:Normal -->
His hands slammed flat on the table. The masked figure next to him didn’t move. Then, as abruptly as it came, the anger vanished. His posture softened. His smile returned, placid, eerie, like ripples smoothing out over deep water after something enormous has slipped beneath the surface.

<!-- paragraph:5858 para_id:000016E2 style:Normal -->
He spread his hands, palms up, as though offering something invisible to the air. “But like I said,” he murmured, “there are no bargains. Only channels and currents and rivers and oceans and threads and lightning and ink and—”

<!-- paragraph:5859 para_id:000016E3 style:Normal -->
His smile split wider. “—FLAME.”

<!-- paragraph:5860 para_id:000016E4 style:Normal -->
He screamed the last word. A single, sharp detonation of sound that hit the stone walls and ricocheted through Kelyra’s bones. She jerked back instinctively. And then he was calm again. Perfectly. Effortlessly. As if the outburst had simply evaporated. He lifted one finger and pointed at Kelyra.

<!-- paragraph:5861 para_id:000016E5 style:Normal -->
“There are no pacts either,” he said. “But I did make one with her.” His finger drifted sideways. Kelyra followed its trajectory, every muscle in her body icing over. He was pointing at Karra.

<!-- paragraph:5862 para_id:000016E6 style:Normal -->
“What are you talking about?” she asked, but the words were hollow even as they left her mouth. She already knew. She had known the instant his finger moved. Karra lifted her sleeve. Ink rippled beneath it—familiar, dangerous, unmistakable. The same shifting patterns Kelyra had once begged for, the same ink that had once given her strength enough to kill the Concord’s former leader and take his place.

<!-- paragraph:5863 para_id:000016E7 style:Normal -->
Karra met her gaze, jaw tight. “Sorry,” she said. “But what you’re doing is too dangerous.”

<!-- paragraph:5864 para_id:000016E8 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s hands went instantly to her knives. The man laughed, sharp and delighted.

<!-- paragraph:5865 para_id:000016E9 style:Normal -->
“Ah, wait!” he said, holding up one finger. “There are… forms. Channels. Little grooves in the mountain your water must flow through for the pact that is not a pact. You know them.”

<!-- paragraph:5866 para_id:000016EA style:Normal -->
He lifted his other hand. Ink writhed across his skin—truly writhed, twisting in patterns that were deeper, darker, and more alive than anything she had ever seen. It moved like something trying to escape his flesh, or sink deeper into it.

<!-- paragraph:5867 para_id:000016EB style:Normal -->
“And another pact that is not a pact,” he purred. “The scales must tip. They must always be tipping, tipping, tipping… and the Ink will flow downhill to anoint the one they tip toward.”

<!-- paragraph:5868 para_id:000016EC style:Normal -->
Kelyra couldn’t look away. The Ink pulsed, and for one raw, humiliating second she felt hunger coil inside her. Old hunger. Power was right there, crawling across his hand like a living promise.

<!-- paragraph:5869 para_id:000016ED style:Normal -->
Then she looked back at Karra. Kelyra drew her knives. Held them loosely at her sides. Then she let them fall.

<!-- paragraph:5870 para_id:000016EE style:Normal -->
Metal clattered across stone. “Fine,” she said. “I’ll accept this as a penalty for not delivering the full contract.”

<!-- paragraph:5871 para_id:000016EF style:Normal -->
She smiled then. A slow, predatory smile. The smile of something starving that had just spotted its first meal in weeks. Karra flinched. “After all,” Kelyra said softly, eyes never leaving her, “she’ll barely slow me down.”

<!-- paragraph:5872 para_id:000016F0 style:Normal -->
The man clapped his hands together, sharp and delighted, almost childlike. “Yes! The forms, the preparations… the patterns.” His voice rose and fell as if reciting something ancient and obscene. “The dark, dark, deep channels that lead to inevitability.”

<!-- paragraph:5873 para_id:000016F1 style:Normal -->
Beside him, the masked figure stood. The Heart was nowhere in sight—he must have concealed it the instant the light shifted. As he stepped away, he spoke in a low voice, barely audible over the murmur of the torches. “This is of no interest to me,” he said. “Watching the weak kill each other.”

<!-- paragraph:5874 para_id:000016F2 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stiffened. Something in the cadence tugged at her memory, but the voice was too low, too distorted by the mask. Recognition hovered just out of reach. She shook her head. She couldn’t afford distraction. The Harbinger leader did not respond - or even look at - the departing figure. He seemed entranced by something only he could see, gaze fixed somewhere far beyond the chamber walls.

<!-- paragraph:5875 para_id:000016F3 style:Normal -->
Jalen was suddenly at her side. Efficient. Steady. Wordless as he knelt and tugged off her boots. The cold stone kissed her bare feet, grounding her and sharpening her senses. He helped her strip down to her tight wrap across her chest and short breeches. Across the firelit circle, Karra was doing the same, her silhouette huge and trembling faintly.

<!-- paragraph:5876 para_id:000016F4 style:Normal -->
Jalen leaned in, speaking quickly and low—faster than sign, safer than a whisper in a room where nothing was private.

<!-- paragraph:5877 para_id:000016F5 style:Normal -->
“We both know Karra,” he murmured, voice smooth but clipped with tension. “She’s young. She’s strong. She has weight and reach on you. But you’re faster. Your reflexes are better. And your unarmed work—Kelyra, she has no chance against you. She has to know that.”

<!-- paragraph:5878 para_id:000016F6 style:Normal -->
He squeezed her shoulder—hard, grounding her. She looked up at him. “You’re right,” she said. “But we don’t know how much of that the Ink will make irrelevant.”

<!-- paragraph:5879 para_id:000016F7 style:Normal -->
She glanced past him, eyes narrowing at the ink patterns twisting across Karra’s shoulders. Strange placement. Why the shoulders? Just to hide them? How long had Karra had them?

<!-- paragraph:5880 para_id:000016F8 style:Normal -->
Jalen continued, gentle but insistent. “You both have Ink. She might have more now, but she got it recently. She hasn’t learned to use it—not in a fight, not under pressure. You have. That experience gives you another edge.”

<!-- paragraph:5881 para_id:000016F9 style:Normal -->
Kelyra looked into Jalen’s eyes and, for a moment, saw him the way she had all those years ago—the too-bold street rat who tried to cut her purse. Who yelped when she twisted his arm behind his back so sharply that something snapped. She flicked a glance at that arm now and saw the slight, permanent bump where the bone had never entirely healed flat.

<!-- paragraph:5882 para_id:000016FA style:Normal -->
He followed her gaze and huffed a breath that was almost a laugh. “For what it’s worth,” he murmured, “I’d do it all over again. It hurt really, really bad though.” For him, that was practically a joke. He must be terrified.

<!-- paragraph:5883 para_id:000016FB style:Normal -->
She reached up and put her hand on Jalen’s face, fingers cold from the stone but steady. “You know I love you,” she said. “And nothing—nothing, not threads, not Ink, not inevitability—will stop me from carving out a place for us. Even if I have to burn this whole world down to ash.”

<!-- paragraph:5884 para_id:000016FC style:Normal -->
Jalen only nodded once, sharp and sure. “I believe you.” He kissed her, brief and fierce, then stepped back out of the circle.

<!-- paragraph:5885 para_id:000016FD style:Normal -->
Across the chamber, Karra was pounding her fist into her open palm, working herself into a frenzy—a physical mantra, strength gathering in each strike. The Harbinger leader watched with ecstatic delight, hands clasped before him like a child at a puppet show. The torchlight flickered across his ink-slick face, making his grin look carved into something too flexible.

<!-- paragraph:5886 para_id:000016FE style:Normal -->
Kelyra shut all of it out.

<!-- paragraph:5887 para_id:000016FF style:Normal -->
Her pulse surged, the harsh drumbeat she had lived with since Archeon ripped her childhood apart. Memories clawed at the edges, trying to pour through—Serathis’s laugh, the breeding pits, Selina’s blood, the years spent killing down in the dark. She seized those memories with her mind, wrenched them into the void, and sealed the door on them.

<!-- paragraph:5888 para_id:00001700 style:Normal -->
Her eyes snapped left. For a moment she considered the Harbinger—so open, so unguarded-looking in his ecstasy. Could she kill him now? Strike before anyone expected it? No. If he freely handed out power like a god with pockets full of lightning, he had to have enough of it to protect himself.

<!-- paragraph:5889 para_id:00001701 style:Normal -->
She snapped her focus forward. Karra. Massive. Dense. Shoulders like sculpted stone. Strong in a way few women - even few men - were. Ink swirling across her upper chest and deltoids in thick, predatory arcs. Kelyra refused to read the patterns—no advantage in trying to decipher them now. Ink only mattered once it was in motion.

<!-- paragraph:5890 para_id:00001702 style:Normal -->
She thought instead of Archeon. The only lesson they ever taught well. Tactics didn’t matter if you struck too fast and too hard for anyone to respond.

<!-- paragraph:5891 para_id:00001703 style:Normal -->
She locked eyes with Karra. The young woman’s gaze burned with the same intensity—raw determination mixed with fear. She was holding tight to her new Ink Boon, bracing herself behind it like a shield.

<!-- paragraph:5892 para_id:00001704 style:Normal -->
So Kelyra seized her own. She reached for the dark thread in her mind, felt the Ink thrum in her veins, and shoved it into her legs all at once. Her tendons screamed, her bones vibrated, but she knew exactly how far she could push before her body would break.

<!-- paragraph:5893 para_id:00001705 style:Normal -->
And she launched herself forward—at the exact, perfect speed her frame could survive. A blur of bare feet against cold stone. The chamber smeared around her—stone, torchlight, the Harbinger’s delighted grin—reduced to streaks as she closed the distance in a single instant. Her arm chopped across her body in a telegraphed arc, wide and crude, practically begging to be blocked.

<!-- paragraph:5894 para_id:00001706 style:Normal -->
Karra’s eyes flared. Her guard snapped up with Ink-fed speed, forearms rising like twin pillars of stone. The impact never came. Kelyra had already dropped her arm, letting the force of her feint sluice through her spine as she spun, heel whipping toward Karra’s neck.

<!-- paragraph:5895 para_id:00001707 style:Normal -->
The world stretched. Her senses sharpened to a needlepoint. She saw the pulse in Karra’s throat, the bead of sweat tracking down her collarbone, the fractional hitch in her posture as instinct caught up to danger.

<!-- paragraph:5896 para_id:00001708 style:Normal -->
Karra twisted, bringing her guard up just in time. Kelyra’s heel crashed into her forearms. A thunderclap. Shock radiated up Kelyra’s leg—dense, immovable muscle meeting precision and momentum. Karra grunted, feet staggered, but she did not fall. The woman was a fortress.

<!-- paragraph:5897 para_id:00001709 style:Normal -->
Then the counterstrike came. A haymaker the size of a battering ram tore toward Kelyra’s head—so fast it blurred even through the dilation of Kelyra’s Ink-heightened perception. Air warped around the fist.

<!-- paragraph:5898 para_id:0000170A style:Normal -->
Kelyra was already beneath it. She dropped low, spine folding like a hinge, the fist ripping past her hair with a rush of displaced heat. Stone dust shook loose from the floor under the force of the miss.

<!-- paragraph:5899 para_id:0000170B style:Normal -->
Her spin didn’t stop. Momentum curled along her leg, gathering behind her knee. She swept at Karra’s legs. This time she didn’t aim high. She aimed for the joint. Her shin slammed into the side of Karra’s knee. Kelyra felt—not heard—the pop. A sickening give. Karra’s leg folded inward like a felled trunk.

<!-- paragraph:5900 para_id:0000170C style:Normal -->
Karra fell. In Kelyra’s hyperfocused, Ink-honed vision, the fall stretched out—a slow, tilting avalanche, the huge woman’s arms pinwheeling, mouth opening around a sound that wouldn’t reach air until a heartbeat later.

<!-- paragraph:5901 para_id:0000170D style:Normal -->
Karra hit the stone like a collapsing cliff—but even before her full weight landed, instinct pulled her arms up, shielding her head and neck from the killing blow she knew was coming. Even half-fallen, the woman still fought like a survivor, body reacting faster than thought.

<!-- paragraph:5902 para_id:0000170E style:Normal -->
Fine. Kelyra didn’t go for the head. She pivoted around Karra’s falling mass, driving a brutal kick into her exposed ribs. She felt the cracks through her heel—heat, vibration, the familiar sick lurch of bone giving way beneath her strike. Karra’s guard faltered. Her right arm dropped instinctively to shield her flank.

<!-- paragraph:5903 para_id:0000170F style:Normal -->
That was all Kelyra needed.

<!-- paragraph:5904 para_id:00001710 style:Normal -->
She seized Karra’s wrist, yanking herself inside the collapsing guard just as the Ink flooded her right hand. Fingers tightened into a spearpoint. She drove it into the base of Karra’s neck with perfect, lethal precision.

<!-- paragraph:5905 para_id:00001711 style:Normal -->
A snap. A full-body shudder. Karra’s eyes rolled back as her body went slack. Dead weight. Unmoving. The fight ended in a single, decisive moment. Kelyra staggered back, nausea twisting through her stomach. The sensation was too familiar—too reminiscent of the Archeon pits, the days she measured her own survival by the broken bodies she left behind. The room felt smaller, tighter, full of old ghosts.

<!-- paragraph:5906 para_id:00001712 style:Normal -->
She spun—not sure if she was looking toward the Harbinger leader or away from Karra’s corpse—and screamed at him, louder and more broken than she intended. “Fine! I did it! Again! Are you happy? Are you ready to finally hold up your end of the bargain?”

<!-- paragraph:5907 para_id:00001713 style:Normal -->
He only smiled, serene as moonlight on ink. She forced the Ink Boon to ebb, letting time begin to normalize, her heartbeat slowing as her perception loosened. His lips moved—slow, then quicker—as the dilation unraveled. “You… haven’t… tipped… the scales… enough.”

<!-- paragraph:5908 para_id:00001714 style:Normal -->
Sense flared—instinct, survival, the wolfish intuition Archeon had beaten into her as a child. She didn’t think. She seized the Ink Boon again, every vein flaring cold, and leapt. Not toward any target. Just away. Anything to vacate the spot she stood on.

<!-- paragraph:5909 para_id:00001715 style:Normal -->
A massive arm tore through the air she had occupied a heartbeat earlier. It struck her side with a glancing blow instead of pulverizing her spine. Pain exploded across her ribs—white-hot, cracking, tearing—before she was flung sideways like a ragdoll.

<!-- paragraph:5910 para_id:00001716 style:Normal -->
The world cartwheeled. Reflex forced her to twist midair, landing hard on her feet, knees bending to absorb the shock, but as she straightened her rib shrieked in agony. Her breath hitched.

<!-- paragraph:5911 para_id:00001717 style:Normal -->
Karra was standing. Not injured. Not dead. Whole. Her shoulders were squared, her neck unmarred, her ribs smooth beneath unbroken skin. Only the Ink across her upper body throbbed faintly, like a second heartbeat.

<!-- paragraph:5912 para_id:00001718 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s teeth ground together. So that’s what he meant by leaning on the scales. Naive. She had been naive to think he’d ever offer a fair fight. Karra’s eyes gleamed with something wild and unsteady—fear and borrowed confidence tangled together. Kelyra inhaled, ribs burning, and shifted her stance. Fine, she thought. Let’s see how far the scales can tip before they break.

<!-- paragraph:5913 para_id:00001719 style:Normal -->
She couldn’t say it out loud—not in the slowed, stretched-second universe she fought in—but she said it with her eyes, straight across the circle to Karra - bright and razor-edged as a drawn blade: I will break you until your Ink runs out.

<!-- paragraph:5914 para_id:0000171A style:Normal -->
And then she moved. Launched herself into Karra again, ripping past the pain in her ribs and shoving it into the void where she kept everything she refused to feel. Her body was screaming, but she outran the scream, outpaced it with pure velocity.

<!-- paragraph:5915 para_id:0000171B style:Normal -->
She didn’t try to win. She tried to hurt. A fist to the short ribs—an explosion of impact under her knuckles. An elbow into the abdomen—she felt Karra’s diaphragm spasm. A stomp to the instep—bone meeting stone under Karra’s weight. A knee in the hollow beneath the ribs—a white-hot impact that would have folded any normal human in half.

<!-- paragraph:5916 para_id:0000171C style:Normal -->
Karra reeled, flailing wide-armed haymakers that tore the air. Each swing could have crushed a skull if it landed. Kelyra let them pass inches—sometimes less—from her face, dancing through the arcs like she knew the script ahead of time. Because she did. Archeon had taught her well.

<!-- paragraph:5917 para_id:0000171D style:Normal -->
Karra staggered, and Kelyra hammered another heel kick into the side of her knee—the same knee she’d taken out before. Karra buckled, dropping to one knee with a sound between a groan and a snarl. She threw one arm up near her head in a desperate attempt to ward off the inevitable.

<!-- paragraph:5918 para_id:0000171E style:Normal -->
Kelyra felt a cold, vicious satisfaction slide through her. Weak. Pathetic. No Ink on earth could save you from me. And beneath that thought burned something hotter, older, more poisonous: This world is built on injustice. All the way down. Even here. Even now. And I will carve out justice with my hands and my teeth and my fury. I will take power because no one ever gave it, and no one ever will.

<!-- paragraph:5919 para_id:0000171F style:Normal -->
She felt the killing power gather in her hand. That vindicating power that washed clean every sin—past and future—under the lie of necessity. The blow she’d landed a thousand times in the pits. The ending that would quiet everything. She struck. Her fist dropped like a guillotine toward the base of Karra’s skull.

<!-- paragraph:5920 para_id:00001720 style:Normal -->
And then—Her speed vanished. Her strength vanished. The dilation collapsed, time slamming back into place all at once.

<!-- paragraph:5921 para_id:00001721 style:Normal -->
Karra’s eyes snapped open.

<!-- paragraph:5922 para_id:00001722 style:Normal -->
In that instant Kelyra understood—with a sick, sinking horror—that her Ink had run out. She had pushed too far past her limits, burned through every drop, forgotten what she had given away to Davian.

<!-- paragraph:5923 para_id:00001723 style:Normal -->
Then a blur—motion without shape—and Karra was gone from where she’d been. Kelyra only felt it: the weight of a mountain slamming into her side, tossing her like scrap along the stone. Her body bounced, slid, scraped—every impact flaring agony through her ribs, across her back, into her teeth. She skidded into darkness beyond the ring of torchlight, into a place that did not feel hidden or safe anymore but cold and yawning.

<!-- paragraph:5924 para_id:00001724 style:Normal -->
Karra appeared again—standing over her—huge, immovable, blotting out the torchlight like an eclipse. Kelyra waited for the killing blow. Waited for the reversal that she had always known, somewhere deep down, would find her eventually. This is what happens to those who dare cut threads from the Skein.

<!-- paragraph:5925 para_id:00001725 style:Normal -->
She closed her eyes. Nothing happened. She opened them. Karra still stood there. “What are you waiting for?” Kelyra screamed, voice cracking. “Just do it already!”

<!-- paragraph:5926 para_id:00001726 style:Normal -->
Then—something horrible. A laugh. Melodic. The Harbinger. Laughing so hard tears streaked down his cheeks, madness bubbling out of him like a spring. Kelyra pushed herself upright, swaying, ribs shrieking at every movement. Her body was a map of fresh breaks and spreading bruises. Karra backed away from her, shame flickering across her massive features. “I’m sorry,” she said, breathless. “But I did what I had to.”

<!-- paragraph:5927 para_id:00001727 style:Normal -->
And then she was gone—running, fleeing the circle like she couldn’t bear to stay another second. “What’s so funny?” Kelyra screamed, staggering toward the light, clutching her side. She looked around frantically for Jalen. The Harbinger wiped his eyes, still smiling with a too-wide, too-bright joy.

<!-- paragraph:5928 para_id:00001728 style:Normal -->
“Oh,” he breathed, voice lilting and cracked with delight, “such a dark, dark, DARK thread… the thread that comes out of twilight and creates a shadow not its own. Bind it up—tie the cities up, tie the High Clades up with night, tie the Low Clades up with Ink, tie the Masked Clades up with nothingness…”

<!-- paragraph:5929 para_id:00001729 style:Normal -->
Kelyra shook her head. The words slurred in and out, distorted by pain. She blinked hard until her vision steadied—

<!-- paragraph:5930 para_id:0000172A style:Normal -->
A figure stepped into the light beside the Harbinger. Her mind took too long to understand what she was seeing. And then—Whoever she thought she was in that moment vanished.

<!-- paragraph:5931 para_id:0000172B style:Normal -->
Serathis. She reeled backward with a ragged scream. “No—NO—!”

<!-- paragraph:5932 para_id:0000172C style:Normal -->
She turned to run. A man stepped from the shadows. Dark gauntlets. Shroud flickering off him in harsh, prismatic light. He grabbed her like she was weightless. She pounded against him, clawing, thrashing with everything she had left. Futile.

<!-- paragraph:5933 para_id:0000172D style:Normal -->
He tossed her back into the circle. The impact shattered across her ribs—white, blinding pain. Somewhere, the Harbinger’s voice drifted to her as if from underwater. “Take it… take the thread that is so dark… and burn it all with an obsidian flame—the fire that is cold, the light that comes from the void…”

<!-- paragraph:5934 para_id:0000172E style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s vision flickered, doubled, collapsed inward. And Serathis was suddenly kneeling before her. Smiling.

<!-- paragraph:5935 para_id:0000172F style:Normal -->
“I missed you, daughter.”

<!-- paragraph:5936 para_id:00001730 style:Normal -->
Kelyra screamed.

<!-- paragraph:5939 para_id:00001733 style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 30

<!-- paragraph:5940 para_id:00001734 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 30

<!-- paragraph:5942 para_id:00001736 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:5943 para_id:00001737 style:Normal -->
Maya woke with a gasp.

<!-- paragraph:5944 para_id:00001738 style:Normal -->
Early morning light bled through the infirmary windows in pale sheets, too bright, too sharp, catching on the afterimages still seared into her mind. Her dreams had been filled with catastrophic re-imaginings of the night before—Niall lying dead at her feet, Arrek’s scorched body slumped against a wall, Vorren’s hands closing around her throat, a dozen futures she had seen or imagined or feared braided into a single nauseating loop.

<!-- paragraph:5945 para_id:00001739 style:Normal -->
She pressed her palms hard against her eyes, as if that could block it out—but the threads were still there. Bright. Insistent. Winding through her thoughts like razor-fine wire, tugging her toward spirals into the dark.

<!-- paragraph:5946 para_id:0000173A style:Normal -->
A voice cut through the static. “I could have woken you after the healer came,” Aldira said, “but I think you were desperately in need of sleep.”

<!-- paragraph:5947 para_id:0000173B style:Normal -->
Maya’s hands dropped slowly. Aldira sat in a chair dragged close to the bed, a small table pulled beside her and already buried under orderly stacks of reports. She looked like she had been sitting there for hours, posture straight, expression neutral, quill tapping against the rim of an inkpot.

<!-- paragraph:5948 para_id:0000173C style:Normal -->
It was only then Maya realized the infirmary was empty. Every cot, every chair. Just her and Aldira. Her voice felt thin. “Where’s my father?”

<!-- paragraph:5949 para_id:0000173D style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t look up from the page she was annotating. “Niall is fine. He left several hours ago. That man never could learn to sleep when he needs to.”

<!-- paragraph:5950 para_id:0000173E style:Normal -->
Relief hit Maya hard enough to sting—but it didn’t steady her. The image of him crushed under the Sphere, lightning guttering out, flickered again behind her eyelids, bright thread twisting tight. She swallowed. Then—unable to stop herself—she forced out the next question. “And… Arrek?”

<!-- paragraph:5951 para_id:0000173F style:Normal -->
Aldira set her quill down. Very carefully. “Well,” she said, “assuming that is his real name—which, at this point, seems highly unlikely, despite all evidence to the contrary provided by our supposedly flawless magical security—our soldiers have been sweeping the courtyards and the surrounding districts.”

<!-- paragraph:5952 para_id:00001740 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at her, dread pooling cold in her stomach. Aldira met her eyes at last. “There has been no sign of him.”

<!-- paragraph:5953 para_id:00001741 style:Normal -->
A sick, traitorous relief washed through Maya before she could stop it. Despite everything she’d witnessed—the impossible healing, the speed, the flash of what might have been a Shroud—her dreams had ended the same way every time: Arrek’s body burned black, limbs broken at angles no one could survive. Over and over. A fate as inevitable as gravity.

<!-- paragraph:5954 para_id:00001742 style:Normal -->
“He’s not dead,” she whispered. “He’s not dead.” Then, louder, harsher: “This is all my fault.”

<!-- paragraph:5955 para_id:00001743 style:Normal -->
It came out like a verdict, not a thought. Aldira’s quill paused mid-stroke. She let out a long, seasoned sigh. “I suppose I shouldn’t be surprised,” she said. “You’ve been awake for less than a minute and we’ve already arrived at that conclusion. You really are his daughter.”

<!-- paragraph:5956 para_id:00001744 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “What?”

<!-- paragraph:5957 para_id:00001745 style:Normal -->
“Would it surprise you,” Aldira continued dryly, “to know that the first thing Niall said, upon waking, was essentially the same?”

<!-- paragraph:5958 para_id:00001746 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head, anger and shame tangling hot behind her ribs. “You don’t know what you’re saying. It is my fault. I—” Her breath hitched. “I knew him better than Niall did. I should have seen it coming. He was acting strange, so strange I even came to you about it. I should have followed him. Or talked to him. Or made him listen. I just should have been—”

<!-- paragraph:5959 para_id:00001747 style:Normal -->
She stopped. The word enough hovered like a blade between them, too sharp to touch. “…more vigilant,” she said instead, voice thin, brittle.

<!-- paragraph:5960 para_id:00001748 style:Normal -->
Aldira sighed again and set her quill down. “Maya. You are not a citadel sentry. ‘Vigilance’ is not the state a human being is meant to live in.”

<!-- paragraph:5961 para_id:00001749 style:Normal -->
But Maya couldn’t hear her. Not really. The bright threads of the Skein were crowding in again, tugging at every uncertainty, every imagined catastrophe, pulling her toward futures in which every misstep was her fault, every loss predetermined by her failure to anticipate it. She pressed the heels of her hands against her brow.

<!-- paragraph:5962 para_id:0000174A style:Normal -->
“You don’t understand,” Maya said. Her voice was shaking now. “I can see things. I can see everything. And I’ve trained so hard—every day—to be fast, and strong, and prepared, and I was right there. I should have stopped him.”

<!-- paragraph:5963 para_id:0000174B style:Normal -->
Her breath hitched. “I don’t even… I don’t even know his real name.”

<!-- paragraph:5964 para_id:0000174C style:Normal -->
The memory hit her like a blow: the courtyard, the heat, the shards of frost on her skin, his hand in hers—his mouth on hers. The look in his eyes that had felt real, true, something she couldn’t have invented even with all the force of her imagination. Why did he do it? Why be there for me at all? What did I say? What did I do?

<!-- paragraph:5965 para_id:0000174D style:Normal -->
The bright threads flashed across her vision—blinding, white-hot, threading backward and forward through time. She saw the courtyard again, saw him break away from her kiss, saw his eyes widen—and instead of running, he stayed. He told her… something. She reached for that thread with her mind, the way she’d learned to reach for channels of heat: precise, quick, intuitive. She felt the moment warp, stretch, the past-future pulling open like a doorway.

<!-- paragraph:5966 para_id:0000174E style:Normal -->
“It should have been different…” she whispered. “What did I do? What did I—”

<!-- paragraph:5967 para_id:0000174F style:Normal -->
“Maya!” Aldira’s voice cut through the vision like a blade. The thread snapped. The courtyard dissolved. Reality crashed back in around her, flat and gray and unbearably small. Maya jerked upright, breath ragged.

<!-- paragraph:5968 para_id:00001750 style:Normal -->
Aldira set down her quill with deliberate force. “Look,” she said, “I know you need to recover from this, and I am here to support you. It was not easy to drag this desk all the way over here, in case that wasn’t clear.”

<!-- paragraph:5969 para_id:00001751 style:Normal -->
