Maya blinked at her.

<!-- paragraph:5970 para_id:00001752 style:Normal -->
“But,” Aldira continued, gesturing pointedly at Maya’s vacant stare, “sitting in a cot, mumbling self-indictments while you stare off into nowhere is not going to get us anywhere.”

<!-- paragraph:5971 para_id:00001753 style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth to answer Aldira’s question—and the infirmary door slammed open hard enough to rattle the hinges. Harvick strode in.

<!-- paragraph:5972 para_id:00001754 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” he said cheerfully, “Niall told me to come check on—” He stopped because Maya was staring at him like a ghost had walked in.

<!-- paragraph:5973 para_id:00001755 style:Normal -->
“You were…” she breathed. “You were dead. The Registry—your name— it was—”

<!-- paragraph:5974 para_id:00001756 style:Normal -->
Harvick brightened, pleased. “See, this is the reaction I was going for. Aldira pretended she wasn’t worried at all, even though I’m sure she absolutely was.” Aldira did not look up from her papers.

<!-- paragraph:5975 para_id:00001757 style:Normal -->
Harvick stared plaintively at Aldira for a second before continuing. “Anyway! Niall didn’t actually tell me to check on you. He’s too busy being super, super angsty in his meditation chamber like he always is these days. You know how he gets.”

<!-- paragraph:5976 para_id:00001758 style:Normal -->
Maya could only stare. Harvick was casually inspecting the front of his shirt like he’d misplaced a button. “Oh right,” he said. “I should probably explain. Except I don’t really understand it super well.”

<!-- paragraph:5977 para_id:00001759 style:Normal -->
Aldira muttered, “Shocking.”

<!-- paragraph:5978 para_id:0000175A style:Normal -->
“You are essentially correct!” Harvick replied, gesturing vaguely at his chest, “Niall said something to me about, uh, ‘heart-muscle physicality’ or whatever. Apparently there’s electricity in there? Who knew. Anyway, I got punched so hard that the electricity… went away? Or got scrambled? Or misbehaved? I don’t know.”

<!-- paragraph:5979 para_id:0000175B style:Normal -->
He looked down at himself, brows knitted in deep, earnest confusion. “But then Niall restarted it. With lightning.”

<!-- paragraph:5980 para_id:0000175C style:Normal -->
Maya’s jaw fell open. Harvick shrugged. “So, problem solved! Pretty sure that answers any questions anyone will ever have about this.”

<!-- paragraph:5981 para_id:0000175D style:Normal -->
Maya said, “That answers almost none of my questions. The first of which is: what were you doing in the treasury with Niall and… Arrek?”

<!-- paragraph:5982 para_id:0000175E style:Normal -->
Harvick winced. “Yeah, I guess we should keep calling him Arrek for now, huh.”

<!-- paragraph:5983 para_id:0000175F style:Normal -->
Maya fixed him with a stare sharp enough to cut stone.

<!-- paragraph:5984 para_id:00001760 style:Normal -->
He rubbed the back of his neck, looking deeply embarrassed. “Okay, fine. I thought he had some kind of money issue because of the debt contracts—which I only knew about because Aldira gave me his file—and I figured he was going to steal something. And I wanted to, you know… stop him.”

<!-- paragraph:5985 para_id:00001761 style:Normal -->
Harvick kept going, words tumbling out faster now that he felt cornered. “I mean, it made sense. He’s a good guy, but he’s been weird lately - well, all the time, but even weirder than normal - and I didn’t want him getting himself killed over money. Or getting anyone else killed. Or triggering some kind of treasury defense system that would blow us all up. You know, normal concerns.”

<!-- paragraph:5986 para_id:00001762 style:Normal -->
Maya dragged a hand down her face. “So you decided to confront him alone.”

<!-- paragraph:5987 para_id:00001763 style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged helplessly. “Well, I brought two of my friends. Although I did leave them outside to make sure no one else came and interrupted us. I thought maybe I could just uh… spar with him to calm him down.”

<!-- paragraph:5988 para_id:00001764 style:Normal -->
Maya buried her face in both hands. “Harvick. You can’t solve every problem by hitting people with wooden swords.”

<!-- paragraph:5989 para_id:00001765 style:Normal -->
“That’s true,” Harvick agreed quickly. “I know that now. Also, for the record, I tried pretty hard to hit him with a real sword too, but then there was this crazy Ink stuff, and a Shroud, and—look, it got complicated.”

<!-- paragraph:5990 para_id:00001766 style:Normal -->
Maya’s head snapped up. “So he really does have a Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:5991 para_id:00001767 style:Normal -->
Aldira nodded. “Yes. We’ve been going over this ever since Harvick ‘woke up.’” Harvick muttered, “From being dead,” under his breath. Aldira ignored him. “It narrows down the possibilities significantly. Niall told me everything he remembers—which isn’t much. Just that ‘Arrek’ moved extremely quickly to avoid his lightning, and then suddenly the entire treasury Sphere was coming straight at him.”

<!-- paragraph:5992 para_id:00001768 style:Normal -->
Harvick lifted a hand. “He definitely manifested a Shroud during our fight, but it looked pretty unpracticed. Kinda like how yours looked back when you were… eight or nine?”

<!-- paragraph:5993 para_id:00001769 style:Normal -->
Aldira reached for another sheet of paper already marked with a few notes and lifted her quill, pausing over the blank space. “What can you add, Maya? Did you see a sigil?”

<!-- paragraph:5994 para_id:0000176A style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth—then froze as the memory hit her like a blow. Arrek—standing over her father with that knife. Her smashing his wrist aside. The audible crack as his arm snapped. The sickening sight of it knitting back together moments later, Ink crawling through it, his eyes avoiding hers as if looking at her would shatter him.

<!-- paragraph:5995 para_id:0000176B style:Normal -->
“I didn’t see a sigil,” she said finally. “And I didn’t really see his Shroud directly either. But there was something strange. I broke his arm, and he healed almost instantly. And then he moved so fast. And his tattoos…” She swallowed. “It was like they were moving. Seeping into him. Into his body. Into his… soul, I guess.”

<!-- paragraph:5996 para_id:0000176C style:Normal -->
Harvick perked up. “So he moved super fast. That sounds like Archeon.”

<!-- paragraph:5997 para_id:0000176D style:Normal -->
Aldira shook her head. “Except Archeon magi always show a sigil flare when they accelerate—visible, distinct.” She made another note on her paper, lips pressed thin. “Well, I can’t say any of this matches our records for any High or Low Clade abilities. And the lack of a visible sigil is… extremely confusing.”

<!-- paragraph:5998 para_id:0000176E style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Basic Shroud manifestation is supposed to be sub-luminescent in terms of energy requirement, so it’s not unusual that we wouldn’t see one if he was just forming a Shroud. But the other abilities—the speed, the healing—they seemed so powerful. How could any Clade keep something like that secret? Or even want to?”

<!-- paragraph:5999 para_id:0000176F style:Normal -->
Aldira hesitated, just long enough that Maya looked up. “There is one other kind of Clade we haven’t discussed yet,” she said quietly. “The Masked Clades.”

<!-- paragraph:6000 para_id:00001770 style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted. “Oh come on. Those aren’t real Clades. They just call themselves that because it sounds cooler than ‘normal criminals who wear masks when they stab people.’”

<!-- paragraph:6001 para_id:00001771 style:Normal -->
Maya murmured, “He did put on a mask. Right before he ran away.” She could see it now—just for an instant, before he’d leapt past her—dark material, an unfamiliar symbol carved into the cheek. “There was something etched into it, but I didn’t get a good look.”

<!-- paragraph:6002 para_id:00001772 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s eyes sharpened. “So: a criminal who wears a mask and might actually have supernatural abilities. Remind you of anyone?”

<!-- paragraph:6003 para_id:00001773 style:Normal -->
Maya felt the blood drain from her face. “That man. The Shade. The one I saved from Caius… and the one the Drakhals claim we’re conspiring with.” Her voice thinned, spiraling toward panic. “Could the whole thing have been a setup? Did he play me from the beginning? How could I have fallen for this? I’ve let them outmaneuver us at every turn.”

<!-- paragraph:6004 para_id:00001774 style:Normal -->
Aldira said, “You’re saying it was a reasonable standard for you to have disentangled an incredibly complex political scheme—one involving types of magic that seem to fall entirely outside the formal Clade system—in the moment when you saw Caius slaughtering civilians for spectacle?”

<!-- paragraph:6005 para_id:00001775 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at her. “You want me to say ‘no.’”

<!-- paragraph:6006 para_id:00001776 style:Normal -->
Aldira lifted both hands slightly. “I’m just asking an innocent question.”

<!-- paragraph:6007 para_id:00001777 style:Normal -->
Maya looked away. “Niall wouldn’t have fallen for it. He would have killed the Shade right there and prevented any of this from happening. No wonder he doesn’t trust me.”

<!-- paragraph:6008 para_id:00001778 style:Normal -->
Her mind flashed to her father’s sheer, uncontested power—how effortlessly he had dominated every Clade for more than a decade, how he held them all at bay through force of will and force of lightning. How at the meeting yesterday he had only held back because he was worried about her, about whether she could keep herself safe.

<!-- paragraph:6009 para_id:00001779 style:Normal -->
But then—Maya paused. “Wait. This all doesn’t make sense.” Aldira’s eyes narrowed slightly, attentive.

<!-- paragraph:6010 para_id:0000177A style:Normal -->
“So… sure. The Shade somehow threw a massive stone Sphere at Niall.” Maya’s voice picked up momentum. “But even to get to that point, he would have had to dodge Niall’s lightning. Maybe he’s fast, but Niall should be able to fill an entire chamber with lightning in an eyeblink. And if his Shroud is unpracticed, like Harvick said, there’s no way he’d survive that. Even with healing. Niall obliterated one of the Archeon magi through his Shroud in an instant.”

<!-- paragraph:6011 para_id:0000177B style:Normal -->
Aldira and Harvick exchanged a look. A very uncomfortable one.

<!-- paragraph:6012 para_id:0000177C style:Normal -->
Maya’s irritation flared. “So even you two don’t trust me? What’s going on with Niall? And what were those silver strips covering his body under his coat? How am I supposed to keep us all safe if I don’t even know what’s happening with my own father?”

<!-- paragraph:6013 para_id:0000177D style:Normal -->
Harvick raised both hands. “We do trust you. Really. But he’s the boss around here and also I, uh… don’t want to die—” he shot a meaningful look at Aldira “—again.”

<!-- paragraph:6014 para_id:0000177E style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t bother dignifying that with a response. She looked up at Maya instead. “I’ll tell you about Niall if you tell me to,” she said. “But it would be better if it came from him—rather than him feeling like you went behind his back.”

<!-- paragraph:6015 para_id:0000177F style:Normal -->
Maya opened her mouth, then shoved herself upright—and the room immediately pitched sideways.

<!-- paragraph:6016 para_id:00001780 style:Normal -->
Harvick lunged forward and caught her under the arms. “Okay, yeah, maybe don’t explode half a courtyard and then sprint off without breakfast next time.”

<!-- paragraph:6017 para_id:00001781 style:Normal -->
Aldira was already there, pressing a cup of water into Maya’s hand. “Drink.”

<!-- paragraph:6018 para_id:00001782 style:Normal -->
Maya drank, breathing through the dizziness until the infirmary stopped trying to spin out from under her. She pulled away from Harvick and forced herself fully upright. “Fine,” she said, “but I’m telling you now—if there are any other terrible hidden secrets lurking out there, you tell me before they almost kill me.”

<!-- paragraph:6020 para_id:00001784 style:Normal -->
Neither Aldira nor Harvick argued.

<!-- paragraph:6021 para_id:00001785 style:Normal -->
Erynn was waiting just outside the infirmary doors with Maya’s guards, pacing in a tight little loop. The moment Maya stepped out, Erynn sprang forward and wrapped her in a fierce hug that made the hallway tilt again.

<!-- paragraph:6022 para_id:00001786 style:Normal -->
“Hey—” Maya said, breathless, steadying herself.

<!-- paragraph:6023 para_id:00001787 style:Normal -->
“They wouldn’t let me in,” Erynn said immediately, still clinging to her, then pulling back just enough to stare daggers at the two armored guards. “They said it was Aldira’s orders. But of course they let Harvick waltz right on by.” She turned back to Maya, concern flooding her face. “What happened? How are you?”

<!-- paragraph:6024 para_id:00001788 style:Normal -->
Maya felt a flicker of warmth at that—real, grounding—but it didn’t last. “I can’t really talk about it right now,” she said. “I need to go talk to Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:6025 para_id:00001789 style:Normal -->
Erynn huffed. “Okay, fine. I guess I can’t interrogate someone who’s literally just leaving her sick room.”

<!-- paragraph:6026 para_id:0000178A style:Normal -->
They started down the corridor. Maya focused on placing one foot in front of the other, on the familiar stone under her boots, on not letting the world tilt again. She was acutely aware that she probably should have eaten something, or drunk more water, or sat down for another five minutes—but the thought of delaying made her chest tighten.

<!-- paragraph:6027 para_id:0000178B style:Normal -->
They walked in silence for a bit, Erynn falling half a step behind her. Not crowding. Watching. Ready, Maya realized, to catch her if she stumbled.

<!-- paragraph:6028 para_id:0000178C style:Normal -->
After a few turns, Erynn cleared her throat. “So,” she said, almost under her breath, “uh… what happened to Arrek?”

<!-- paragraph:6029 para_id:0000178D style:Normal -->
Maya’s mind lurched toward the Skein on reflex, bright threads flaring into view, tugging her backward toward moments full of possibility she couldn’t afford to relive. She blinked hard, forcing her focus back into the hallway, into the present. She didn’t want to talk about him. Not now.

<!-- paragraph:6030 para_id:0000178E style:Normal -->
“What have people been saying?” Maya asked instead.

<!-- paragraph:6031 para_id:0000178F style:Normal -->
Erynn replied quickly, words tumbling over each other now that she’d started. “No one really told us anything. Aldira just kept saying there was an accident, but everyone heard the explosions. People were saying maybe some kind of magus attacked the treasury and killed everyone, which obviously makes no sense because you and Niall are both here, and no one could ever beat both of you.” She shook her head, clearly still rattled. “But then no one could find either of you, and I started getting really scared.”

<!-- paragraph:6032 para_id:00001790 style:Normal -->
Maya kept her eyes forward, letting the rhythm of her steps anchor her.

<!-- paragraph:6033 para_id:00001791 style:Normal -->
“And then Niall just showed up this morning,” Erynn went on, lowering her voice. “Looking like his normal angry self, like nothing happened. Except there were rumors a healer had to be brought in for him during the night. And you were still in the infirmary. So I guess… all that to say…” She hesitated. “I don’t think anyone really knows what happened. Just you, and Niall, and maybe Aldira and Harvick.” She glanced at Maya again. “…And Arrek?”

<!-- paragraph:6034 para_id:00001792 style:Normal -->
Maya was acutely aware of the guards a few paces behind them, of how sound carried in the stone corridors. She kept her voice level and careful. “Did Niall give directions about what to do with the zanwork?”

<!-- paragraph:6035 para_id:00001793 style:Normal -->
Erynn nodded. “Yeah. He said everything’s suspended until we know the source of the accident.”

<!-- paragraph:6036 para_id:00001794 style:Normal -->
Maya kept walking, her expression neutral. So he’s trying to slow it down, she thought. Trying to keep anyone from realizing just how badly the Registry had been compromised. It wouldn’t work for long. Too many people monitored it, too many systems leaned on it every hour of the day. It was the backbone of their defenses.

<!-- paragraph:6037 para_id:00001795 style:Normal -->
“Good,” Maya said aloud. “That’s what I would’ve recommended too. But I need to talk to him. Figure out… where we stand.”

<!-- paragraph:6038 para_id:00001796 style:Normal -->
Erynn hesitated, then dropped her voice even lower. “I don’t know what’s going on. Obviously. But if something’s wrong with the Registry… that’s okay, right? We can fix it. Or work around it.” She glanced at Maya, earnest and a little scared. “What we really need is you and Niall. You’re the Rezins. People trust you to figure this out.”

<!-- paragraph:6039 para_id:00001797 style:Normal -->
They stopped in front of the doors to Niall’s meditation chambers. The corridor was quiet here, the air cooler, the stone worn smooth by generations of pacing.

<!-- paragraph:6040 para_id:00001798 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded, because there was nothing else she could do. She couldn’t say what pressed against the inside of her chest—that something was wrong with Niall, that something was wrong with her, that trusting them was dangerous or foolish. She knew, with a certainty that chilled her more than fear, that it was the one thought she could never voice.

<!-- paragraph:6041 para_id:00001799 style:Normal -->
Maya forced herself back into the posture she’d practiced since childhood, spine straight, shoulders squared, the calm certainty of the Rezin heir settling over her like armor. “I promise,” she said to Erynn, steady and clear. “We’ll figure this out.”

<!-- paragraph:6042 para_id:0000179A style:Normal -->
Erynn gave her a small, relieved smile. Behind her, the two guards unconsciously straightened, as if the words themselves carried weight. Maya turned away before any of them could see the doubt flicker across her face. As she reached for the doors, a thought slipped through the cracks of her composure, sharp and unwelcome: Who’s the liar now?

<!-- paragraph:6043 para_id:0000179B style:Normal -->
She pushed the doors open and stepped inside.

<!-- paragraph:6044 para_id:0000179C style:Normal -->
Niall sat on the stone floor beneath the open iris of the ceiling, perfectly centered beneath it. Morning light poured down in a single, clean shaft, catching in his white hair and leaving the rest of his face in shadow. He wore his long military coat, dark and immaculate, as he always did. He must have replacements, Maya realized distantly. She’d never really thought about the quiet, practical work it took to maintain the image he projected—how much of his authority was stitched together from discipline and habit rather than raw power.

<!-- paragraph:6045 para_id:0000179D style:Normal -->
She closed the doors behind her and let her Shroud flare just enough to feel solid again. The added energy surged through her arm as she shoved the heavy door shut. It slammed into place with a resonant thud, a thin crust of frost blooming where her palm had touched it.

<!-- paragraph:6046 para_id:0000179E style:Normal -->
Maya walked toward Niall until she reached the edge of the circle of light. She stopped there, just outside it, and looked down. Niall didn’t react. With the way the light cut across him and the angle of his head, she couldn’t even tell if his eyes were open or closed. Even wrapped in the long military coat, even with the breadth of his shoulders and the weight of presence he usually carried without effort, he looked… small. Frail, in a way she had no vocabulary for and didn’t want to examine too closely.

<!-- paragraph:6047 para_id:0000179F style:Normal -->
She held onto the mask anyway. The Rezin heir. The apex magus. The version of herself he had shaped and demanded. Because that was what he wanted. What he needed.

<!-- paragraph:6048 para_id:000017A0 style:Normal -->
“So,” she said, keeping her voice level and authoritative, “what is going on with you?”

<!-- paragraph:6049 para_id:000017A1 style:Normal -->
No response. She pressed on, the words sharper now. “How did that thief get away from you? And what is the silver on your body?”

<!-- paragraph:6050 para_id:000017A2 style:Normal -->
Still nothing. The silence stretched, heavy and unyielding, and something in her chest tightened. She stood there cloaked in the authority he had taught her to project, the same authority he used to silence rooms, to cow High Clades, to end arguments before they began. It felt hollow in her mouth.

<!-- paragraph:6051 para_id:000017A3 style:Normal -->
Her frustration spiked, heat flaring behind her eyes. “What are we going to do to fix this?” she demanded.

<!-- paragraph:6052 para_id:000017A4 style:Normal -->
Niall rose to his feet without looking at her. He shrugged out of the military coat and let it fall where it landed, a dark shape on the stone. Beneath it he wore a sleeveless vest, and Maya’s breath caught as the silver was revealed fully. Cords of it spiraled up both arms, dense with intricate zan lattices so fine they almost seemed etched in light rather than metal. They disappeared beneath the fabric at his shoulders, and she knew without needing to see that they continued across his chest, down his torso, into his legs. Everywhere. A cage worn as skin.

<!-- paragraph:6053 para_id:000017A5 style:Normal -->
He lifted his arms into a posture she had seen a hundred times before, in training, in battle, in her mother’s memories. Lightning kindled along the silver, not exploding outward as it once had, but spiraling through the lattices, channeled, constrained, dissipating into the air above his hands in thin, ragged arcs.

<!-- paragraph:6054 para_id:000017A6 style:Normal -->
“It can’t be fixed,” he said. His voice was flat. “We built everything on my power. And now it’s gone.”

<!-- paragraph:6055 para_id:000017A7 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “What are you talking about?” she said, too quickly. “That’s not true. We were just on the walls. You killed one of the Archeon magi.”

<!-- paragraph:6056 para_id:000017A8 style:Normal -->
He lowered his arms. When he finally looked at her, there was no anger in his expression—just exhaustion. “Don’t you get it?” he said. “I’m weak now. Inefficient. I can’t do what I did before. That’s why you ended up on the wall before your Presentation. Because I was too slow.” His jaw tightened. “If I’d worked harder. If I’d healed faster. If I’d been stronger sooner, you wouldn’t be in this position. You wouldn’t have had to go through any of this.”

<!-- paragraph:6057 para_id:000017A9 style:Normal -->
The words landed hard enough that Maya felt momentarily unmoored. Could that be true? Her mind raced, assembling fragments. She took a step closer without realizing it. Into the light. “I don’t understand,” she said. “What’s happening to you? What is this silver?”

<!-- paragraph:6058 para_id:000017AA style:Normal -->
Niall laughed once, sharp and humorless. Lightning spat from his fingers, uncontrolled, snapping into the air before guttering out. “We’re taught from the time we’re children,” he said, bitterness threading his voice, “put fear in a box. Put shame in a box. Slam the lid shut. Split your focus. Lock it all away so you can kill, and kill, and kill.”

<!-- paragraph:6059 para_id:000017AB style:Normal -->
He dragged a hand through his hair. “But focus can’t be split. It never could be. I put everything I couldn’t bear into that box, buried it in the shadows, and now there’s something there.” His gaze unfocused, as if he were looking past her. “Something in the dark, eating at my mind.”

<!-- paragraph:6060 para_id:000017AC style:Normal -->
Maya felt something inside her click into place with terrible clarity. If this is true, she thought, then now I really can help him. The idea flared bright and urgent, almost a relief. He would finally see it. See her.

<!-- paragraph:6061 para_id:000017AD style:Normal -->
“Then let me help you,” she said. “You taught me. You gave me your strength. We can get through this together.”

<!-- paragraph:6062 para_id:000017AE style:Normal -->
Niall shook his head. “I did all of this so you wouldn’t have to,” he said quietly. “And even then I failed. I failed as a ruler, and I failed as a father.” His mouth twisted. “I don’t even know how to teach you what to do, because I don’t know how to do it myself.”

<!-- paragraph:6063 para_id:000017AF style:Normal -->
He looked away again. “That’s the downside of a fast mind. The painful thoughts are faster too. You inherited that from me.”

<!-- paragraph:6064 para_id:000017B0 style:Normal -->
The words left her reeling. She couldn’t reconcile this man—tired, stripped bare, wrapped in silver scaffolding and regret—with the Niall she knew from her mother’s codex. That Niall had been pure conviction, decisive and whole, lightning given human form.

<!-- paragraph:6065 para_id:000017B1 style:Normal -->
“You’re the one who’s supposed to tell me what to do,” she said, her voice cracking despite herself. “You’re supposed to guide me. Teach me. I know that’s who you are—I saw it in the codex.”

<!-- paragraph:6066 para_id:000017B2 style:Normal -->
Niall tilted his head back, staring up into the shaft of light, and laughed. It wasn’t loud. It wasn’t amused. It was hollow. “The codex,” he said. “You never even considered—after everything I told you—that it was twisted. That it was designed by him.” He dropped his gaze back to her. “I don’t even know why. Only that it corrupts memories. Nudges them. Pushes you toward… something.”

<!-- paragraph:6067 para_id:000017B3 style:Normal -->
Maya’s thoughts spun, the ground shifting under every assumption she’d built herself on. Too many secrets. Too many lies she hadn’t even known were lies. “So you just let me think this was my fault?” she demanded. “My weakness. My bad decisions?” Her hands curled into fists. “You couldn’t trust me enough to tell me the truth, even though not knowing could get me killed?”

<!-- paragraph:6068 para_id:000017B4 style:Normal -->
She barely noticed when she said the name. “What does—what did—Elric want? I saw him kill you. And then I saw you kill him.”

<!-- paragraph:6069 para_id:000017B5 style:Normal -->
Niall’s eyes snapped to her face. “I told you not to look at the strands of fate.”

<!-- paragraph:6070 para_id:000017B6 style:Normal -->
Maya let out a short, incredulous laugh. “Oh,” she said, the sound sharp with exhaustion and fury, “we are way past that, Father.”

<!-- paragraph:6071 para_id:000017B7 style:Normal -->
Niall drew a breath like he meant to say something and then seemed to think better of it. “You don’t understand,” he said instead. “That’s how your mother—” He stopped himself, jaw tightening. “You just can’t do it.”

<!-- paragraph:6072 para_id:000017B8 style:Normal -->
“Oh, great,” Maya snapped. “Yet another terrible secret that could kill me.” She dragged a hand through her hair. “When does it end? When do you finally start treating me like the heir you forced me to be?”

<!-- paragraph:6073 para_id:000017B9 style:Normal -->
Niall’s gaze dropped to the stone floor.

<!-- paragraph:6074 para_id:000017BA style:Normal -->
“Fine,” she said, the word sharp and brittle. “Then I guess I’ll just figure everything out myself and try to dodge lethal secrets whenever you randomly decide to mention them.” She took a breath, steadied herself, and her voice shifted—cooler, more focused. “But I’m not blind. There’s something going on with your mind that you think is sapping your power, and this silver is supposed to help somehow.”

<!-- paragraph:6075 para_id:000017BB style:Normal -->
She stepped closer, eyes tracking the spiraling cords. Up close, the work was extraordinary—and damaged. Whole runs of zan were scorched dark, others warped or kinked, as if they’d been wrenched out of alignment by a massive force. Some lattices looked burned out, inert where they should have been alive with flow.

<!-- paragraph:6076 para_id:000017BC style:Normal -->
Niall shifted under her scrutiny, uncomfortable—and, beneath that, faintly relieved, like someone who had been hiding a wound for too long.

<!-- paragraph:6077 para_id:000017BD style:Normal -->
“Where did you get these?” Maya asked quietly. “They’re beyond anything I’ve seen, except for the Archeon gauntlets and…” She stopped, jaw tightening, the rest of the thought lodged somewhere she wasn’t ready to touch. “But they’re in desperate need of repair.”

<!-- paragraph:6078 para_id:000017BE style:Normal -->
Niall exhaled slowly. “They’re from a zanwright,” he said. “She’s not affiliated with any guild. Or any High Clade.”

<!-- paragraph:6079 para_id:000017BF style:Normal -->
“That’s impossible,” Maya said immediately. “Someone skilled enough to make this wouldn’t be able to stay independent. They’d be swallowed whole.”

<!-- paragraph:6080 para_id:000017C0 style:Normal -->
Niall almost smiled. It was faint but real. “That’s because you’ve never met her.”

<!-- paragraph:6081 para_id:000017C1 style:Normal -->
He still looked worn down, but some of the tightness had eased, like he’d finally set down a weight he’d been bracing against. Maya caught it, the subtle shift, and the realization landed: he was really worried about what I would think.

<!-- paragraph:6082 para_id:000017C2 style:Normal -->
She straightened. “Then I think it’s probably time to remedy that.”

<!-- paragraph:6083 para_id:000017C3 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6084 para_id:000017C4 style:Normal -->
An hour later they were outside the Citadel, winding through the shadowed, narrow streets where the stone felt older and the air carried the low murmur of the city. Niall wore a long-sleeved shirt beneath a plain cloak, the silver hidden, his presence muted in a way Maya had never really seen before. She realized, a little uncomfortably, that she had never wondered why he was so fashion-averse. Like everyone else, she’d been blinded by the ferocity of his reputation, by the assumption that power like his didn’t need concealment.

<!-- paragraph:6085 para_id:000017C5 style:Normal -->
She wore a cloak as well, its hood pulled low. Harvick walked with them, unarmed and relaxed in that irritating way of his that suggested he didn’t think anything here could actually surprise him.

<!-- paragraph:6086 para_id:000017C6 style:Normal -->
“I still think we could have brought a few guards,” Maya said quietly. “Just in case.”

<!-- paragraph:6087 para_id:000017C7 style:Normal -->
Niall shook his head. “Harvick is plenty. We’re not going into a combat situation. We’re disguised, and our Shrouds will be more effective than anything else we could bring.” After a beat he added, “And this zanwright is… very picky about who she’ll meet with.”

<!-- paragraph:6088 para_id:000017C8 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “I still don’t understand how a single non-magus can have that much influence.”

<!-- paragraph:6089 para_id:000017C9 style:Normal -->
Harvick cleared his throat. “I asked Aldira about this a few years ago—”

<!-- paragraph:6090 para_id:000017CA style:Normal -->
Maya shot him a look sharp enough to cut. Harvick realized what he’d given away and winced. “Right. Uh. Anyway.” He pressed on, hands lifting in a placating gesture. “She has… special magic stuff. Things that can kill a magus. Through a Shroud. There’s a rumor that some Low Clade idiot tried to lean on her and she dropped him where he stood.”

<!-- paragraph:6091 para_id:000017CB style:Normal -->
“A rumor,” Maya said flatly.

<!-- paragraph:6092 para_id:000017CC style:Normal -->
Niall nodded. “The High Clades who know about her are split between thinking it’s nonsense and thinking there’s just enough chance it’s true that it isn’t worth testing.” His voice hardened slightly. “If a High Clade magus were even injured by a non-magus, the loss of reputation alone would collapse their house.”

<!-- paragraph:6093 para_id:000017CD style:Normal -->
He glanced back at Maya as they turned down a narrower street, the light thinning. “You’ll see when you meet her.”

<!-- paragraph:6094 para_id:000017CE style:Normal -->
They walked in silence a while longer, the streets narrowing and decaying as they went. The stonework grew uneven, patched with crude repairs or not repaired at all. Half the buildings were abandoned outright; the rest sagged under makeshift additions, their lower levels claimed by squatters who watched from doorways and broken windows with flat, assessing eyes. Thugs lounged in the mouths of alleys, conversations trailing off as the three of them passed.

<!-- paragraph:6095 para_id:000017CF style:Normal -->
Maya leaned a fraction closer to Harvick and murmured, “It’s horrible out here.”

<!-- paragraph:6096 para_id:000017D0 style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t look at her. “You know this is still technically your city.”

<!-- paragraph:6097 para_id:000017D1 style:Normal -->
The words stunned her. She’d assumed, without ever really thinking about it, that they’d crossed some invisible boundary—out of Rezin territory, into someplace else. Her gaze slid back over the street, the collapsed roofs, the scavenged doors, the people watching them with open resentment.

<!-- paragraph:6098 para_id:000017D2 style:Normal -->
No wonder he—they—hate us, she thought, a tight, uncomfortable realization settling in her chest.

<!-- paragraph:6099 para_id:000017D3 style:Normal -->
She was about to say something to Niall when he abruptly slowed, glanced both ways, and turned sharply into a narrow alley barely wide enough for two people abreast. She followed without thinking, Harvick falling in behind her.

<!-- paragraph:6100 para_id:000017D4 style:Normal -->
Niall stopped at what looked like a long-abandoned doorway, its wood split and warped, the frame reinforced with ugly bands of iron. Set into the stone beside it was a dull metal cone, dark with age, its surface scarred and pitted.

<!-- paragraph:6101 para_id:000017D5 style:Normal -->
He leaned in close to it and said quietly, “Zirene. It’s Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:6102 para_id:000017D6 style:Normal -->
After speaking, Niall leaned back against the opposite wall and waited.

<!-- paragraph:6103 para_id:000017D7 style:Normal -->
Maya glanced between him and the door. “So what are we doing?”

<!-- paragraph:6104 para_id:000017D8 style:Normal -->
“She’ll answer when she’s ready,” Niall said. “Give her time. She’s the only one who can do this kind of thing.”

<!-- paragraph:6105 para_id:000017D9 style:Normal -->
They stood there in the narrow alley, the stone pressing in on all sides, the air stale and damp. Seconds stretched. Maya became acutely aware of how exposed they were, how little she liked waiting without understanding the rules.

<!-- paragraph:6106 para_id:000017DA style:Normal -->
Then the metal cone crackled, and a warped female voice spilled out of it, distorted and hollow. “Niall who?”

<!-- paragraph:6107 para_id:000017DB style:Normal -->
Niall sighed and leaned forward again. “You know which Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:6108 para_id:000017DC style:Normal -->
A pause. Then: “I only ask because it’s been so long since anyone named ‘Niall’—if that’s even your real name—came for maintenance that I assume someone is making it up so they can lure me out and kill me.”

<!-- paragraph:6109 para_id:000017DD style:Normal -->
“Very funny, Zirene,” Niall said. “We all know you probably have something set up so you can see me.”

<!-- paragraph:6110 para_id:000017DE style:Normal -->
“Oh, sure,” the voice replied. “But you’ve gotten so much uglier that I almost didn’t recognize you.”

<!-- paragraph:6111 para_id:000017DF style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted. Niall shot him an unamused look.

<!-- paragraph:6112 para_id:000017E0 style:Normal -->
“Just kidding,” Zirene added smoothly. “You’re as handsome as ever. And I recognize that oaf Harvick with you. But who’s the woman? You know I don’t deal with people I don’t know.”

<!-- paragraph:6113 para_id:000017E1 style:Normal -->
“This is my daughter,” Niall said. “Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:6114 para_id:000017E2 style:Normal -->
A sharp, distorted whistle burst from the cone. Maya flinched despite herself.

<!-- paragraph:6115 para_id:000017E3 style:Normal -->
“Sorry,” Zirene said. “I forget I shouldn’t do that from so close to the receiver. I was trying to indicate that I’m impressed… or shocked. Pardon the pun.” A brief pause. “All right. Fine. Come on in.”

<!-- paragraph:6116 para_id:000017E4 style:Normal -->
The door creaked open on its own, hinges groaning softly, revealing an unlit interior that swallowed the light behind it. Before Maya could comment, the decrepit floor inside shifted, and a hatch split open down the middle, its edges scraping against stone as it lifted.

<!-- paragraph:6117 para_id:000017E5 style:Normal -->
Maya summoned a small flame above her hand, just enough to see the rough ladder descending into darkness below.

<!-- paragraph:6118 para_id:000017E6 style:Normal -->
Niall hesitated for only a moment. Then he stepped inside and began his descent.

<!-- paragraph:6119 para_id:000017E7 style:Normal -->
Maya exchanged a look with Harvick and followed. She kept a small flame hovering near her head as she climbed downward, its light catching on damp stone and the rough rungs beneath her hands. Below, a faint arc of electricity flickered now and then, briefly outlining Niall’s shoulders and the ladder beneath him. The air grew cooler as they descended, heavy with the smell of metal and old dust.

<!-- paragraph:6120 para_id:000017E8 style:Normal -->
A dull, final sound echoed above them as the hatch slid shut. It wasn’t a slam, just a solid, deliberate closure, the kind that made Maya very aware of how far underground they now were.

<!-- paragraph:6121 para_id:000017E9 style:Normal -->
The ladder kept going. Longer than it had any right to be. Her legs started to ache, the repetition settling into her muscles, her mind filling the empty space with too many thoughts she didn’t want.

<!-- paragraph:6122 para_id:000017EA style:Normal -->
“I’ll bet she’s got all kinds of super nasty stuff in here,” Harvick said from above her, his voice carrying oddly in the shaft. “You know. Super sharp knives. Spikes. Things that pop out of walls.”

<!-- paragraph:6123 para_id:000017EB style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t look back. “Is that supposed to be funny?”

<!-- paragraph:6124 para_id:000017EC style:Normal -->
“Hey, I’m just saying. Preparedness.”

<!-- paragraph:6125 para_id:000017ED style:Normal -->
“Joking about literally everything isn’t a very mature way to deal with stress.”

<!-- paragraph:6126 para_id:000017EE style:Normal -->
There was a brief pause as Harvick adjusted his grip. “Well, after you’ve died once, you realize not to take everything so seriously.”

<!-- paragraph:6127 para_id:000017EF style:Normal -->
Maya felt irritation spark, “Shouldn’t that make you take things more seriously? Realizing that any day could be your last?”

<!-- paragraph:6128 para_id:000017F0 style:Normal -->
Harvick huffed. “Huh. That’s a good point. I guess I’ll start moping about everything instead of being funny.”

<!-- paragraph:6129 para_id:000017F1 style:Normal -->
Maya sighed. Before she could respond, her foot met solid ground. The ladder ended abruptly, opening into a low stone chamber that swallowed her flame’s light almost immediately. She stepped off and straightened, her flame lifting higher on instinct as Niall turned ahead of her.

<!-- paragraph:6130 para_id:000017F2 style:Normal -->
The dark didn’t vanish so much as reorganize itself. Za etched into the walls and floor flared one by one, lines of pale light chasing each other outward until the space resolved into something coherent. The patterns were dense and layered, not the clean, declarative zan Maya was used to seeing in formal Clade work. These felt older, more pragmatic, like tools that had been modified and re-modified until only their usefulness mattered.

<!-- paragraph:6131 para_id:000017F3 style:Normal -->
The room stretched long and wide, its ceiling lost in shadow even with the zan lit. At the far end, at a distance that felt deliberate, a woman sat on a chair turned backward, arms draped over the top rail. She had fiery red hair pulled back carelessly, wisps escaping around her face, and freckles scattered across her nose and cheeks. Her posture was slouched, almost petulant, chin resting on her forearms as she regarded them with open skepticism. She looked younger than Maya had expected—too young, given the things attributed to her—but there was something in her eyes that didn’t match the impression.

<!-- paragraph:6132 para_id:000017F4 style:Normal -->
“Hello, Zirene,” Niall said. “It’s been a while.”

<!-- paragraph:6133 para_id:000017F5 style:Normal -->
Zirene stood in one smooth motion. She was taller than Maya had guessed, broad through the shoulders, built like someone who worked with heavy things every day. As she straightened, Maya noticed the odd metal wands strapped across her chest—curved, asymmetrical, their surfaces etched with fine zan that glowed faintly, as if idling.

<!-- paragraph:6134 para_id:000017F6 style:Normal -->
“And whose fault is that?” Zirene said. “Don’t answer. We both know it’s yours.” She tilted her head, studying him, then added, “Anyway. Before we do anything else, swear you’re not here to kill me. Or to try to kill me.”

<!-- paragraph:6135 para_id:000017F7 style:Normal -->
Niall sighed. “Zirene. If someone were going to kill you, wouldn’t they just lie when you asked them?”

<!-- paragraph:6136 para_id:000017F8 style:Normal -->
She considered that, lips pursed. “That’s a good point.” Then, brightly, “Okay, then promise you won’t kill me or try to kill me.”

<!-- paragraph:6137 para_id:000017F9 style:Normal -->
Niall looked like there were several responses fighting to escape him. “I promise,” he said, carefully, “that I am not going to kill you or try to kill you.”

<!-- paragraph:6138 para_id:000017FA style:Normal -->
Zirene’s gaze flicked to Maya, then to Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:6139 para_id:000017FB style:Normal -->
“I promise,” Maya said, after a beat.

<!-- paragraph:6140 para_id:000017FC style:Normal -->
“Same,” Harvick added, lifting a hand.

<!-- paragraph:6141 para_id:000017FD style:Normal -->
Zirene’s expression transformed instantly, suspicion melting into something almost cheerful. “Great! Come on in.”

<!-- paragraph:6142 para_id:000017FE style:Normal -->
She reached down and pressed something with her foot. More zan ignited along the walls, warmer this time, and a heavy iron door behind her swung open with surprising ease. As they walked the length of the room, Maya felt the space watching them—not hostile, exactly, but attentive, as if every surface was tuned to notice what passed through it.

<!-- paragraph:6143 para_id:000017FF style:Normal -->
Beyond the door was a workshop that felt almost jarringly comfortable. The ceiling was low, the light warm and steady, cast by zan arrays that seemed designed to mimic sunlight. The walls were covered entirely in tools and devices Maya didn’t recognize. Nothing looked decorative. Everything looked used.

<!-- paragraph:6144 para_id:00001800 style:Normal -->
Zirene grinned in Niall’s direction. “It’s so good to see you again.”

<!-- paragraph:6145 para_id:00001801 style:Normal -->
Niall blinked at her, clearly wrong-footed. “It’s… good to see you too?”

<!-- paragraph:6146 para_id:00001802 style:Normal -->
She laughed, sharp and delighted. “Oh no, I was talking to your silver. I can’t actually see it yet, but I’m very excited. Come on, let me see.”

<!-- paragraph:6147 para_id:00001803 style:Normal -->
Niall hesitated, then pulled off his cloak and tugged his shirt over his head. The silver latticework was immediately visible, cords and spirals crisscrossing his arms and torso, disappearing under the waistband of his trousers. In the warm zanlight it looked less like armor and more like something invasive, threaded through him with intimate precision.

<!-- paragraph:6148 para_id:00001804 style:Normal -->
Zirene gasped theatrically and hurried over, crouching in front of him as if he were an injured child. “Oh, my poor baby. What did this to you?” She leaned in close, eyes scanning the etched surfaces, fingers hovering just short of touching.

<!-- paragraph:6149 para_id:00001805 style:Normal -->
Niall stared up toward the ceiling, jaw tight. “Well,” he said flatly, “someone threw a building at me.”

<!-- paragraph:6150 para_id:00001806 style:Normal -->
Zirene snapped upright. “Oh no you don’t. I did very good work on this. Some of the best I have ever done. This is not my poor baby’s fault.” She narrowed her eyes at him. “Now I have a very, very important question. And before I ask it, I want you to remember that I didn’t promise not to kill you. Did you ever have anyone look at this? Maintain it?”

<!-- paragraph:6151 para_id:00001807 style:Normal -->
Niall muttered something that might have included the word “Aldira.”

<!-- paragraph:6152 para_id:00001808 style:Normal -->
Zirene shrieked and leapt backward as if burned. “Aldira? She’s not even a zanwright! What is wrong with you?”

<!-- paragraph:6153 para_id:00001809 style:Normal -->
Niall opened his mouth, then closed it again, apparently out of explanations. Maya stepped forward before the silence could stretch any further. “Look. It’s been extremely important that no one find out about this at all. I think you know why.”

<!-- paragraph:6154 para_id:0000180A style:Normal -->
Zirene whirled on her, eyes blazing. “Oh, I know exactly why. And when the most powerful magus in the known world asks me to make him a special weapon so he can stay the most powerful magus in the known world, maybe—just maybe—he should also think, ‘This super special secret weapon that the genius Zirene made for me at great personal effort might need literally any maintenance at all in order to keep working.’”

<!-- paragraph:6155 para_id:0000180B style:Normal -->
Maya nodded. “I think maybe we’ve all let some things go on a lot longer than they should have. But we’re here now. Can you fix this?”

<!-- paragraph:6156 para_id:0000180C style:Normal -->
Zirene’s irritation evaporated almost instantly, as if it had never been there. “Of course,” she said brightly. “I’d be thrilled to.” She cocked her head. “Assuming you brought payment.”

<!-- paragraph:6157 para_id:0000180D style:Normal -->
Niall glanced at Harvick, who reached into his belt and tossed a small pouch across the room. Zirene caught it one-handed, peeked inside, and let out a delighted giggle that sounded wildly out of place given the circumstances, like a child receiving an unexpected holiday gift. Without another word she darted to the back of the workshop and, through a series of movements Maya couldn’t follow—levers, pressure plates, something that slid and something else that clicked—opened a heavy iron safe and tucked the pouch inside. Then she was back, sweeping a long worktable clean with one decisive motion. Tools clattered to the floor in a cacophony of metal as she gestured for Niall to lie down.

<!-- paragraph:6158 para_id:0000180E style:Normal -->
He did, stiffly. Zirene dragged over a chair, pulled a pair of goggles down over her eyes, and produced several slender tools etched with zan so fine Maya could barely resolve them. She set to work immediately, hands steady and precise as they traced along the silver cords embedded in Niall’s skin. As she worked she added, almost casually, “You know you’re going to have to take those pants off later too, right? I’m assuming it’s not just your upper body maintenance you’ve been completely neglecting.”

<!-- paragraph:6159 para_id:0000180F style:Normal -->
Niall made a low, noncommittal sound and closed his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:6160 para_id:00001810 style:Normal -->
Minutes passed in silence, broken only by the faint scrape of metal on metal and the occasional soft hum. Maya watched, fascinated despite herself, the way Zirene’s hands seemed to know exactly where to go without hesitation. Finally she asked, “How long is this going to take?”

<!-- paragraph:6161 para_id:00001811 style:Normal -->
Zirene snorted. “Oh, excellent idea, distract the person doing extremely delicate work with a sharp knife.” She didn’t look up. “Just kidding. This part’s easy for me. But all told? Probably all day.”

<!-- paragraph:6162 para_id:00001812 style:Normal -->
Maya leaned closer despite herself. “What kind of zan are those? They look sort of like fozan.”

<!-- paragraph:6163 para_id:00001813 style:Normal -->
Zirene burst out laughing. “Oh no, that’s too funny. You actually know some zan, huh?” She kept working, clearly delighted now. “I find the naming system you all came up with hysterical. You take a normal word, slap ‘zan’ on the end, and then everyone repeats it for a few centuries until it sounds mysterious and inevitable. Fozan is a great example.”

<!-- paragraph:6164 para_id:00001814 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Zan have names because they represent what they are.” She realized, even as she said it, how flimsy that sounded.

<!-- paragraph:6165 para_id:00001815 style:Normal -->
Zirene grinned. “Right, sure. But fozan conduct energy, constrain it inside the metal they’re etched into, yeah? They used to call them flow zan. Then flozan. And then people repeat it and because it’s got a foreign word on the end other people assume it’s something else and we end up dropping part of the pronunciation and end up with ‘fozan.’ Ridiculous.”

<!-- paragraph:6166 para_id:00001816 style:Normal -->
She adjusted one of the silver cords, a faint blue-white glow flickering and then stabilizing. “Language decays. So does equipment.”

<!-- paragraph:6167 para_id:00001817 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Okay, so they’re not fozan, or flow-zan, or whatever. Then what are they?”

<!-- paragraph:6168 para_id:00001818 style:Normal -->
Zirene paused just long enough to shift tools, then shrugged. “Honestly? I don’t know how to pronounce them.” She flicked her chin toward Niall. “His dad gave me some very old books as payment for some… work. Extremely interesting books. But when they talked about zan, they didn’t name them. No phonetics, no labels. Just the symbols themselves, drawn out over and over again.” She tapped lightly along one of the silver cords. “So I know the order to etch them in, I know what they do, and I know how they interact. But I don’t actually know how anyone used to say them.”

<!-- paragraph:6169 para_id:00001819 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “They didn’t write the names down?”

<!-- paragraph:6170 para_id:0000181A style:Normal -->
Zirene laughed softly. “Why would they? They must have just… known. Language is fragile like that. If we all died tomorrow, someone could dig up our notes and still have no idea how any of our words were supposed to sound.” She leaned back slightly, eyes unfocusing as she thought. “Symbols survive better than pronunciation.”

<!-- paragraph:6171 para_id:0000181B style:Normal -->
She glanced up at Maya again. “So in my head, I don’t use names. I think in shapes. In relationships. In how one curve leans into another.” Her fingers moved again, quick and sure. “And since I work alone, it’s not like I need to explain it to anyone else.”

<!-- paragraph:6172 para_id:0000181C style:Normal -->
There was a brief hum as a zan settled into place, the silver brightening and then dimming to a steady sheen. Zirene nodded to herself, satisfied. “But if you’re asking conceptually—yeah. I get the sense these are the foundations. The stuff modern zan are built on. All the changes and simplifications in pursuit of making them easier, or cheaper… it just dilutes them out a bit.” She smiled faintly. “These ones are closer to what they actually want to be.”

<!-- paragraph:6173 para_id:0000181D style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “I don’t think zan want anything. They’re just components.”

<!-- paragraph:6174 para_id:0000181E style:Normal -->
Zirene snorted. “Oh? Then why do all of you High Clades keep coming to me when you need things that actually work?” She didn’t look up from her work. “I think my zan are just happier than yours, so they behave better.” Her fingers traced another short arc along the silver. “Look at your father. He’s forcing himself into a shape he doesn’t even want to hold, shaving off anything that doesn’t fit that polished little mold you all worship. Is it working?”

<!-- paragraph:6175 para_id:0000181F style:Normal -->
Niall, eyes still closed, said quietly, “It’s not about what I want to be. It’s about what I need to be.”

<!-- paragraph:6176 para_id:00001820 style:Normal -->
Zirene hummed. “Right. And what you ‘need’ to be is the biggest, sharpest sword in the room. So bright and terrifying that everyone else falls in line whether they agree with you or not.” She finally looked up, her gaze sliding past him to Maya. “Is that what you want to be too? I always thought sword-size obsession was mostly a man thing. But after your Presentation, I had so many visitors asking about ‘custom work.’ You really kicked the hornet’s nest.”

<!-- paragraph:6177 para_id:00001821 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “Wait. You make weapons for other High Clades too?”

<!-- paragraph:6178 para_id:00001822 style:Normal -->
From behind her, Harvick said, “Of course she does. She only cares about money.” As if on cue, he shifted his weight and knocked something metal off a nearby table. It hit the floor with a sharp, echoing clang.

<!-- paragraph:6179 para_id:00001823 style:Normal -->
Zirene winced. “See? This is why I called you an oaf.” She shot Harvick a glare. “Not only are you clumsy with your hands, you’re imprecise with your words.” She turned back to Maya, eyes bright. “I don’t care about money at all. Money is just a means to an end.”

<!-- paragraph:6180 para_id:00001824 style:Normal -->
She tapped the silver again, softer this time. “What I actually care about is finding out what little surprises the Creator hid in the universe when he put it together. The rules everyone assumes are fixed. The places they bend. The places they break.” A thin smile crossed her face. “I care a lot more about that than which man gets to tell everyone else what to do.”

<!-- paragraph:6181 para_id:00001825 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “It’s not just men. Most of the Head Magi in the High and Low Clades are women.”

<!-- paragraph:6182 para_id:00001826 style:Normal -->
Zirene didn’t even glance up. “Oh, I know.” Her voice stayed light as her fingers continued their careful work. “But this man is the one they all have to listen to, no matter how much they hate it.” She nodded toward Niall with her chin. “Same on the other side too. One man above, another one below, both pulling strands and calling it order.”

<!-- paragraph:6183 para_id:00001827 style:Normal -->
She shifted, leaning closer to her work. “After all, it’s basically what you said at your Presentation. The real government is just whoever can kill everyone else. You sit up in your tower and say you’ll kill them if they don’t obey, so they obey. And all these people down here?” She gestured vaguely toward the ceiling, toward the city above them. “They could never kill any of you. So why would anyone listen to them?”

<!-- paragraph:6184 para_id:00001828 style:Normal -->
Zirene went on, conversational. “Your father had the brilliant idea of sticking up for them right around the same time he started losing the ability to really back up his murder threats. And ever since then”—she clicked her tongue softly—“he’s been busy trying to hide his weakness.”

<!-- paragraph:6185 para_id:00001829 style:Normal -->
She poked Niall lightly in the ribs with one finger, not very gently. “Hey. You do realize none of this silver is going to save you unless you figure out what shape you actually want to be, right?”

<!-- paragraph:6186 para_id:0000182A style:Normal -->
Maya stared at her. “So you’d rather the men in Drakhal be the ones ordering everyone around?”

<!-- paragraph:6187 para_id:0000182B style:Normal -->
Zirene hummed, still working, metal whispering softly under her tools. “You never know. Could be Serathis.” A corner of her mouth twitched. “I liked her. Nice old lady.”

<!-- paragraph:6188 para_id:0000182C style:Normal -->
“She kicked me through a building,” Maya said flatly.

<!-- paragraph:6189 para_id:0000182D style:Normal -->
Zirene glanced sideways at her, eyes flicking over her from head to toe before returning to the silver. “You look all right to me.”

<!-- paragraph:6190 para_id:0000182E style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled sharply. “So you’re the one who made their gauntlets. The ones they tried to kill me with.”

<!-- paragraph:6191 para_id:0000182F style:Normal -->
Zirene’s head snapped up this time, delighted. “Oh! Did they work well?” Then she paused, considering. “Wait—no, I guess since you’re still standing there’s probably room for improvement.” She shook her head, almost fond. “Serathis always hated the whole thing anyway. Breeding for generations, pouring resources into it, and they still needed to come to me for help.” Zirene clicked her tongue. “And she has to be on the front lines herself to make up for how weak her grandchildren are. Must drive her insane. Especially knowing that even with the gauntlets they couldn’t touch you.”

<!-- paragraph:6192 para_id:00001830 style:Normal -->
She gestured vaguely toward Niall. “Of course, they can’t afford the level of work your father can. Not even close.”

<!-- paragraph:6193 para_id:00001831 style:Normal -->
Harvick had been circling the workshop, hands clasped behind his back like he was afraid to touch anything else. He stopped near one of the benches and said, casually, “Speaking of your level of work—who’d you make this for?”

<!-- paragraph:6194 para_id:00001832 style:Normal -->
Maya’s stomach dropped as she saw what he was pulling out. The dark dagger. Arrek’s knife. The one that had been raised over her father.

<!-- paragraph:6195 para_id:00001833 style:Normal -->
Zirene didn’t even look up. “I don’t give out that kind of information.”

<!-- paragraph:6196 para_id:00001834 style:Normal -->
“You just told me you made those Archeon gauntlets,” Maya shot back.

<!-- paragraph:6197 para_id:00001835 style:Normal -->
Zirene snorted. “No, you told me I made them, and then I accidentally heavily implied that you were correct because I was excited.” She finally lifted her head and glanced at the blade, eyes sharpening for a brief, focused moment. Then she made a dismissive noise. “But I can tell you right now—I didn’t make that.”

<!-- paragraph:6198 para_id:00001836 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “You didn’t?”

<!-- paragraph:6199 para_id:00001837 style:Normal -->
Zirene leaned back in her chair, studying the dagger with open disdain. “No. Someone was trying to save money.” She wagged one finger at it. “They copied some of my designs. Older ones, too. You can always tell when someone is copying the superficial shape of a thing without understanding why it’s shaped that way.”

<!-- paragraph:6200 para_id:00001838 style:Normal -->
Harvick said, “Who do you think copied it?”

<!-- paragraph:6201 para_id:00001839 style:Normal -->
Zirene shrugged without looking up. “How would I know?”

<!-- paragraph:6202 para_id:0000183A style:Normal -->
Harvick pulled out another small pouch.

<!-- paragraph:6203 para_id:0000183B style:Normal -->
Zirene’s head snapped up. “You brought more?” She squinted at him, then sighed. “Ugh. Fine. But only because I’m mad that someone tried to steal my designs without paying me. And I’m only guessing.” She glanced at the dagger again. “Based on how old the designs are, and the times at which different High Clades approached me… my best guess would be Archeon.”

<!-- paragraph:6204 para_id:0000183C style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled sharply. “That doesn’t help us much. We already know Archeon has it out for us.”

<!-- paragraph:6205 para_id:0000183D style:Normal -->
Zirene rolled her eyes. “Again, only saying this because you’re paying me and I’m annoyed—but it’s hard to believe that someone who’s currently in Archeon would be using that for anything serious.” She tilted her head. “Where did you get it?”

<!-- paragraph:6206 para_id:0000183E style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated. “We got it from an assassin who tried to kill Niall.” She didn’t add how close he’d come, or what he’d taken.

<!-- paragraph:6207 para_id:0000183F style:Normal -->
Zirene laughed. “That makes no sense. Why would you send someone with an old weapon on the most dangerous mission possible? And a weapon that requires Archeon magic—” She stopped short. “Oh. Wait. I said too much.”

<!-- paragraph:6208 para_id:00001840 style:Normal -->
“Why would someone do that?” Maya asked.

<!-- paragraph:6209 para_id:00001841 style:Normal -->
Harvick answered instead. “Because whoever they’re sending is someone they consider extremely expendable.”

<!-- paragraph:6210 para_id:00001842 style:Normal -->
Maya suddenly saw Arrek’s face beneath the open sky—seeing her for who she was, accepting her. Was that all a lie?

<!-- paragraph:6211 para_id:00001843 style:Normal -->
She snatched the dagger from Harvick’s hand and turned back to Zirene. “The assassin—he was covered in tattoos. They seemed to give him magical abilities. Please.” Her voice tightened. “Tell me where those would come from.”

<!-- paragraph:6212 para_id:00001844 style:Normal -->
Zirene finally looked directly at her. “Hey. Like I said before.” She gestured upward, toward Niall. “One man above.” Then she pointed down at the stone floor beneath their feet. “And one man below.”

<!-- paragraph:6213 para_id:00001845 style:Normal -->
Maya collapsed back onto a stool, the knife still clenched in her hand. “So that’s it,” she said, almost without meaning to. “I’ll never find him.”

<!-- paragraph:6214 para_id:00001846 style:Normal -->
Zirene snorted. “Hey, don’t worry too much.” She wiped her hands on a rag and glanced sideways at Niall. “I’m putting some things together from what Serathis was saying, and I’m pretty sure this expendable tattoo-knife man is a loose end they’re about to tie up.” She snatched the pouch from Harvick and tucked it away. “And that’s all I’m going to say about that. Don’t tell anyone or you’re dead.”

<!-- paragraph:6215 para_id:00001847 style:Normal -->
Panic flared hot and sharp in Maya’s chest. “But I need to find him.”

<!-- paragraph:6216 para_id:00001848 style:Normal -->
“This actually makes things easier,” Niall said. “Instead of tracking down a nameless man in the slums, we can just go to the Archeon citadel.”

<!-- paragraph:6217 para_id:00001849 style:Normal -->
Maya was already moving. She bolted out of the workshop into the long room, barely hearing Niall calling after her or Zirene snapping that she wasn’t finished yet, followed by the slapping sound of Niall being pushed none-too-gently back down onto the table. She stopped at the bottom of the ladder in the dim zanlight, staring down at the knife in her hand.

<!-- paragraph:6218 para_id:0000184A style:Normal -->
Harvick caught up to her, breathing hard. “Hey.”

<!-- paragraph:6219 para_id:0000184B style:Normal -->
“I can’t let him die,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:6220 para_id:0000184C style:Normal -->
Harvick tilted his head. “Why not?”

<!-- paragraph:6221 para_id:0000184D style:Normal -->
Her mind skidded along the strands of the Skein, snapping back to moments before he’d run from her. “It’s my fault,” she said. “I should have done something different.”

<!-- paragraph:6222 para_id:0000184E style:Normal -->
“That’s not a good reason,” Harvick said.

<!-- paragraph:6223 para_id:0000184F style:Normal -->
She swallowed. “Fine. It would just hurt if he was dead.”

<!-- paragraph:6224 para_id:00001850 style:Normal -->
“Why?”

<!-- paragraph:6225 para_id:00001851 style:Normal -->
“Why this, why that,” she snapped. “Why are you always asking so many questions?” She squeezed her eyes shut, the memory forcing itself forward anyway—the courtyard, the kiss, the way he’d looked at her. “I just want to know,” she said more quietly. “Was what happened real? Can I be with someone who sees me for who I am, instead of who I’m supposed to be?”

<!-- paragraph:6226 para_id:00001852 style:Normal -->
Harvick broke into a wide grin. “See? Now that’s a reasonable question.”

<!-- paragraph:6227 para_id:00001853 style:Normal -->
Maya tightened her grip on the knife. Instead of being swept helplessly into the torrent of the Skein, she focused her thoughts. Let me find him. Let me save him. Give me the chance to know who he really is.

<!-- paragraph:6228 para_id:00001854 style:Normal -->
A bright thread snapped into her vision, running straight from the knife and up the ladder.

<!-- paragraph:6229 para_id:00001855 style:Normal -->
She looked toward Harvick. “Tell Zirene I’m really sorry about—”

<!-- paragraph:6230 para_id:00001856 style:Normal -->
He was already halfway across the room, giving her an enthusiastic thumbs-up.

<!-- paragraph:6231 para_id:00001857 style:Normal -->
Maya stretched her mind outward. Heat answered her call, cords of it writhing up from stone and metal and air as frost spread beneath her feet. She slammed the gathered heat into the ground, reinforcing her Shroud, and launched herself upward. Faster and faster, the tunnel rushing past as she shifted focus mid-flight, bracing her Shroud again—

<!-- paragraph:6232 para_id:00001858 style:Normal -->
—and then she was smashing through the hatch, exploding out through the roof in a storm of splintered wood and flying debris. She tore heat from everything she passed—stone, timber, air—and hurled it downward to propel herself higher, harder.

<!-- paragraph:6233 para_id:00001859 style:Normal -->
The city spread out beneath her in all directions: the slums, the central Spire, the seven citadels. And beyond them, spearing out across the distance, she saw it—the golden thread pulling her onward toward the Archeon city.

<!-- paragraph:6234 para_id:0000185A style:Normal -->
She followed it.

<!-- paragraph:6237 para_id:0000185D style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 31

<!-- paragraph:6238 para_id:0000185E style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 31

<!-- paragraph:6240 para_id:00001860 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6241 para_id:00001861 style:Normal -->
Davian woke up already knowing something was wrong. Light pressed against his closed eyes—thin, insistent. Morning. That alone told him too much time had passed.

<!-- paragraph:6242 para_id:00001862 style:Normal -->
His body felt wrecked. Heavy. Not injured in any one place, but wrong all over, like he’d been wrung out and put back together without care. His mouth tasted of metal and ash. He lay still, trying to find the edge of memory, and found only a blur of motion—speed without direction, panic without shape.

<!-- paragraph:6243 para_id:00001863 style:Normal -->
Running.

<!-- paragraph:6244 para_id:00001864 style:Normal -->
That part came first. Sprinting through the city in a daze, feet striking stone so fast it barely registered as impact. He’d known he had to get to the Concord. Not why. Just that it was the next step, the one the dark thread pulled him toward with brutal certainty. Everything else had sunk into the Ink, the world narrowing to a single line of inevitability. The last clear image: the gates. Hurling himself at them without slowing. No hesitation. No plan beyond through.

<!-- paragraph:6245 para_id:00001865 style:Normal -->
He swallowed and lifted his arms, squinting against the light. He remembered heat—searing, consuming—burning that had felt like punishment rather than injury. But his skin was unmarked. No blisters. No scars. Just fading ink, dark veins spidered across his forearms, already thinning, retreating, like something that had finished its work and lost interest. He flexed his fingers slowly. They obeyed.

<!-- paragraph:6246 para_id:00001866 style:Normal -->
He stared at his hands, trying to force the memory back into place. The sprint fractured into flashes—stone, breath tearing at his chest, a sudden swerve. Skidding wildly, boots scraping as he fought momentum he couldn’t afford to lose—He sucked in a sharp breath and sat bolt upright.

<!-- paragraph:6247 para_id:00001867 style:Normal -->
Maya.

<!-- paragraph:6248 para_id:00001868 style:Normal -->
Her face slammed into his mind with painful clarity, like an afterimage burned into his vision after staring too long at the sun. Anger and hurt carving her features, sharper than the edge of any blade.

<!-- paragraph:6249 para_id:00001869 style:Normal -->
“Oh,” he breathed.

<!-- paragraph:6250 para_id:0000186A style:Normal -->
His hands came up on their own, pressing into his eyes, his palms against his face like he could block the memory out by force. His shoulders hunched forward, and for a moment he stayed like that, breathing hard, the room tilting around him. Daylight changed everything. The dark thread that had carried him so cleanly through the night—so sure, so merciless—was already thinning, its heat fading in the presence of ordinary light. With it went the certainty. The numb clarity. The sense that there had been only one possible path.

<!-- paragraph:6251 para_id:0000186B style:Normal -->
“I did the right thing,” he said into his hands. The words sounded wrong the instant he heard them..

<!-- paragraph:6252 para_id:0000186C style:Normal -->
“I did what they told me.” That was worse.

<!-- paragraph:6253 para_id:0000186D style:Normal -->
He shifted, becoming aware of the sheet draped over him, of the bed beneath his weight. A bed. He didn’t remember lying down. He didn’t remember choosing to rest. The idea felt foreign, almost obscene, after how hard he’d run. Davian swung his legs over the side of the bed and sat there, hunched, staring at the floorboards. They were plain. Real. Unimpressed by him.

<!-- paragraph:6254 para_id:0000186E style:Normal -->
He breathed slowly, deliberately, and reached inward for the ink—for that familiar cold, that comfort that wasn’t comfort so much as erasure. It should come easily. Last night it had wrapped around his thoughts and smoothed the edges down until nothing was sharp enough to matter. Now it felt far away. Faded. Like a voice heard through thick walls.

<!-- paragraph:6255 para_id:0000186F style:Normal -->
He couldn’t stop the night from running backward in his head, scenes snapping into place with a clarity that turned his stomach. Standing over Niall, the knife in his hand, its weight familiar and wrong all at once. Maya’s movement—fast, instinctive—batting his arm away, not striking him, just stopping him, like she still believed there was something left to stop.

<!-- paragraph:6256 para_id:00001870 style:Normal -->
He remembered picking up the Heart before that. How cold it had been in a way that had nothing to do with temperature. Alien. Like holding a piece of something that had never agreed to belong to this world. And before that - flight, striking the sphere with power that felt right. Too right; the kind of rightness that didn’t allow questions. Inevitability crystalized into action.

<!-- paragraph:6257 para_id:00001871 style:Normal -->
And before that—

<!-- paragraph:6258 para_id:00001872 style:Normal -->
His hands curled into fists so hard his nails bit through skin. He welcomed the sting. A thin line of warmth seeped out beneath his fingernails.

<!-- paragraph:6259 para_id:00001873 style:Normal -->
I killed Harvick. He hadn’t wanted to. The wanting had mattered, once. He let the rest line up, mercilessly honest. I let Niall live. And I betrayed Maya.

<!-- paragraph:6260 para_id:00001874 style:Normal -->
His throat tightened. She’ll never forgive me.

<!-- paragraph:6261 para_id:00001875 style:Normal -->
He had wanted control, to matter. Desperately. He’d wrapped everything else around that desire like a Shroud, layering justifications around himself in an impenetrable shield. But when it really had mattered, when it had come down to it, he hadn’t felt like the one deciding anything.

<!-- paragraph:6262 para_id:00001876 style:Normal -->
His breathing started to speed up, shallow and sharp, his chest pulling tight. He pushed himself to his feet before it could tip into something worse. Movement. He needed movement.

<!-- paragraph:6263 para_id:00001877 style:Normal -->
Find them. That was the only plan that still felt solid. His parents. His brother. His sister. Everyone else blurred into irrelevance, faces without weight. None of these other people mattered. They never had. It had all been fake anyway—roles, bargains, obligations piled on top of him until he couldn’t tell which parts were his.

<!-- paragraph:6264 para_id:00001878 style:Normal -->
After what felt like an eternity, he reached the door and pulled it open.

<!-- paragraph:6265 para_id:00001879 style:Normal -->
The hallway beyond was silent. Not empty—quiet. The kind of quiet that felt intentional, like the building itself was holding its breath, waiting to see what he would do. Light pooled along the floor in clean lines. No voices. No footsteps. No distant hum of activity he would have expected.

<!-- paragraph:6266 para_id:0000187A style:Normal -->
He stepped out and started down the hall, boots soft against stone. Harvick’s last words surfaced without warning, sharp as a fresh wound. I just want you to really live.

<!-- paragraph:6267 para_id:0000187B style:Normal -->
They burned, refusing to fade. He let out a breath through his nose, half laugh, half wince.

<!-- paragraph:6268 para_id:0000187C style:Normal -->
“I don’t understand,” he muttered to himself, the words barely disturbing the quiet. The weight crept back in, familiar and unwelcome. Guilt, grief, something tighter and harder to name. And then—absurdly—it shifted. Almost like he could hear Harvick laughing, easy and infuriating.

<!-- paragraph:6269 para_id:0000187D style:Normal -->
If I have to die, the imagined Harvick said, I’m at least going to say something really vague and annoying to the guy who kills me so I can live forever in his mind.

<!-- paragraph:6270 para_id:0000187E style:Normal -->
“You’re the worst,” Davian mumbled to the imaginary Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:6271 para_id:0000187F style:Normal -->
It felt stupid, talking to himself in an empty hallway, but the words took a little of the edge off. The tightness in his chest loosened by a fraction, enough to let him keep moving.

<!-- paragraph:6272 para_id:00001880 style:Normal -->
He reached the central dining and meeting area and slowed. It was empty. There should have been people—at least a handful lingering over breakfast, voices overlapping, movement, noise. Instead there were abandoned bowls and utensils scattered across the tables. A chair lay half overturned. The room was out of order in a way that suggested interruption.

<!-- paragraph:6273 para_id:00001881 style:Normal -->
Like people had left quickly. The hairs on the back of his neck rose. He stopped fully now, scanning the space, senses stretching outward despite himself. Nothing moved. No immediate threat made itself known.

<!-- paragraph:6274 para_id:00001882 style:Normal -->
Then he saw the note. It was pinned to one of the tables with a knife, the blade driven cleanly through the parchment and into the wood beneath. He approached slowly, each step measured. The writing was in the Concord’s cipher—pared-down zan, stripped of flourish, used phonetically and rearranged in a precise sequence.

<!-- paragraph:6275 para_id:00001883 style:Normal -->
It took him a moment to decipher.

<!-- paragraph:6276 para_id:00001884 style:Normal -->
Arc has parents.
Harb deal sorry.
Bro sis with us safe.
Arc today arrive.
Leave now.
Burn note.
-Ka

<!-- paragraph:6277 para_id:00001885 style:Normal -->
Davian stood there, staring at the words as they settled into place. Something had gone wrong with the Harbingers. The thought hit hard, disorienting. After everything he had done. Everything he had given up. A sense of panic rose in his chest. There wasn’t time to sit with it.

<!-- paragraph:6278 para_id:00001886 style:Normal -->
He reached out, pulled the knife free of the table in one smooth motion, and took the note with it. Crossing to the hearth, he dropped the parchment onto the smoldering ashes. The edges curled and blackened almost immediately, the cipher collapsing into meaningless burn marks.

<!-- paragraph:6279 para_id:00001887 style:Normal -->
As he turned the knife in his hand, he noticed the wooden hilt.

<!-- paragraph:6280 para_id:00001888 style:Normal -->
Two small zan were carved there, subtle enough to miss if you weren’t looking for them. Concord cipher again. Good luck. His grip tightened around the handle.

<!-- paragraph:6281 para_id:00001889 style:Normal -->
Then he heard something. He wasn’t sure what it was—pressure, displacement, the wrongness of air—but his body reacted before his mind caught up. Davian threw himself sideways just as the doors to the dining hall exploded inward. Not torn from their hinges, but shattered outright, bursting into a storm of splinters and jagged fragments that raked across the room.

<!-- paragraph:6282 para_id:0000188A style:Normal -->
He caught the edge of a table and flipped over it on instinct, muscles moving without deliberation. The table crashed down on its side between him and the blast, becoming a makeshift barrier as shards of wood ricocheted off it in sharp, staccato impacts.

<!-- paragraph:6283 para_id:0000188B style:Normal -->
Silence followed. Brief. Loaded. Davian lifted his head just enough to look over the upended table.

<!-- paragraph:6284 para_id:0000188C style:Normal -->
A woman and a man walked calmly into the room through the wreckage, unhurried, as if they hadn’t just reduced a reinforced door to kindling. They wore leather jerkins and the light from their facial sigils cast chaotic shadows across the room as they moved. Dark metal gauntlets encased their forearms, the surfaces etched with fine zan lattices that glowed faintly as they moved.

<!-- paragraph:6285 para_id:0000188D style:Normal -->
Archeon. Though something about them - their sigils, their gauntlets - felt off from the ones he’d seen in the Rezin Citadel.

<!-- paragraph:6286 para_id:0000188E style:Normal -->
The woman, her dark hair cut close to her scalp, tilted her head and glanced at him. “Hey,” she said to the man beside her, casual as anything. “Isn’t that the guy?” She squinted. “I thought he was supposed to be drugged or something.”

<!-- paragraph:6287 para_id:0000188F style:Normal -->
The man shrugged. He was bald, his nose so thoroughly broken and rebroken it looked less injured than reshaped. “I don’t know,” he said. “Nobody tells me anything.”

<!-- paragraph:6288 para_id:00001890 style:Normal -->
Davian rose slowly from behind the table. His hand tightened around the knife, suddenly and painfully aware of how small it felt. The zan carved into the hilt pressed against the tiny puncture wounds in his palm where his nails had broken skin earlier, a dull, insistent reminder of how little margin he had left.

<!-- paragraph:6289 para_id:00001891 style:Normal -->
“Who are you?” he asked. His voice came out steadier than he felt. “I thought Archeon only had two living magi right now.”

<!-- paragraph:6290 para_id:00001892 style:Normal -->
The woman smiled, bright and interested. “Oh, you know about Brann?” she said. “I heard old Niall cooked him right out of the sky.”
