<!-- paragraph:6291 para_id:00001893 style:Normal -->
The man’s face lit up. “Did you see?” he asked eagerly. “Was it funny?”

<!-- paragraph:6292 para_id:00001894 style:Normal -->
“Wasn’t he your brother?” Davian said, buying time. “I’d think you’d be more torn up about it.”

<!-- paragraph:6293 para_id:00001895 style:Normal -->
The woman laughed, sharp and unbothered. “None of us like magi who get Presented,” she said. “They get to wander around the city, acting all hoity-toity like they’re real High Clades at parties.”

<!-- paragraph:6294 para_id:00001896 style:Normal -->
She tilted her head, considering. “Besides, if Brann is dead, that means maybe Serathis will Present me.”

<!-- paragraph:6295 para_id:00001897 style:Normal -->
The man barked a laugh. “Keep dreaming,” he said. “Nire, if Serathis hasn’t picked you out as a favorite already, you’ve got no chance.”

<!-- paragraph:6296 para_id:00001898 style:Normal -->
Davian breathed in the way his mother had taught him and tried to let the moment stretch. It didn’t work. His thoughts were already spiraling—his parents, his siblings, Harvick, Maya—each one pulling at him at the same time, too loud, too close. He wanted to fall into the Ink, to let it take over and quiet everything, but it felt distant now. Faded. Uninterested in him for the moment, even as it lingered at the edges of his awareness, patient and predatory, as if stalking him for reasons he didn’t understand.

<!-- paragraph:6297 para_id:00001899 style:Normal -->
He breathed out. Then he breathed in again, the way Harvick had taught him. Put the burden down for now, not forever. Focus on the moment so you can do what matters. Don’t use the Ink as a crutch.

<!-- paragraph:6298 para_id:0000189A style:Normal -->
Something clicked, like it had the first time Harvick had tried to teach him to put his load down for a moment. The rushing noise of his thoughts dropped away, and time seemed to slow—not stopping, not bending, just giving him enough space to think. The world sharpened. His eyes darted with preternatural speed as he took stock.

<!-- paragraph:6299 para_id:0000189B style:Normal -->
He cast his mind outwards. The two magi by the destroyed doors. Their gauntlets. The disarrayed furniture scattered across the room, bowls and utensils skidding where they’d been abandoned. The knife in his hand, which might as well be a butter spreader against a Shroud. The exits. The adjoining rooms—did any of them have windows? The streets surrounding the hideout.

<!-- paragraph:6300 para_id:0000189C style:Normal -->
Then inward. The Ink, dried up and distant. His hand-to-hand training. His poorly understood magic, the parts of it he’d always relied on Antithesis to mediate safely. His gaze snapped back to the magi. The space around them felt wrong—distorted, resistant. Their Shrouds.

<!-- paragraph:6301 para_id:0000189D style:Normal -->
It was supposed to be a reflex. But for him it felt like being a child again, learning martial forms by rote, thinking about every limb and angle because the instinct hadn’t been burned in yet. Can I use it? He had to.

<!-- paragraph:6302 para_id:0000189E style:Normal -->
Davian focused on the defensive reflex and felt the Shroud snap into place around him—an invisible pressure, a warped skin of space—but in doing so he lost his grip on speed of thought. The moment stretched too far, then collapsed, and reality crashed back in all at once.

<!-- paragraph:6303 para_id:0000189F style:Normal -->
“You see that, Gyre?” the woman said. “He was just doing it. And now he’s got a Shroud?”

<!-- paragraph:6304 para_id:000018A0 style:Normal -->
She clicked her tongue. “Drosin said this was supposed to be easy.”

<!-- paragraph:6305 para_id:000018A1 style:Normal -->
The man—Gyre—rolled his eyes. “That’s barely a Shroud,” he said. “Please don’t hit him so hard he dies.”

<!-- paragraph:6306 para_id:000018A2 style:Normal -->
Nire rolled her shoulders. “Well, if Serathis isn’t going to Present me, I don’t know what reward I’m supposed to get for doing missions right.”

<!-- paragraph:6307 para_id:000018A3 style:Normal -->
“Not having Serathis kill you is a pretty good reward,” Gyre said.

<!-- paragraph:6308 para_id:000018A4 style:Normal -->
Davian shifted into a defensive stance. He could feel his Shroud flickering, unstable, the effort of holding it colliding with the effort of thinking. His mind wasn’t trained for this.

<!-- paragraph:6309 para_id:000018A5 style:Normal -->
Nire sighed. Then she moved. She didn’t reach Brann’s speed—not that impossible, overwhelming acceleration—but she still crossed the length of the dining hall in an instant. Davian threw his guard up, Shroud flashing weakly, just as she kicked the table in front of him.

<!-- paragraph:6310 para_id:000018A6 style:Normal -->
The table shattered against the Shroud over his forearms. Energy dispersed wildly—am I supposed to be doing something with that?—and the force flung him backward, spinning through the air. His Shroud collapsed completely as his mind snapped to orientation, to landing, to not breaking something important.

<!-- paragraph:6311 para_id:000018A7 style:Normal -->
The hesitation cost him. He hit hard, rolled, and slammed into the wall, breath driven out of him in a sharp grunt.

<!-- paragraph:6312 para_id:000018A8 style:Normal -->
He looked up. Nire was already airborne again. Davian abandoned the Shroud entirely and scrambled to his feet, tumbling away as she cratered the floor where he’d been a moment before. Stone cracked outward in a shallow burst.

<!-- paragraph:6313 para_id:000018A9 style:Normal -->
He glanced sideways. Gyre stood near the main exit, looking up at the ceiling, bored—but positioned perfectly to block the way out. The memory surfaced unbidden: Brann toying with him at the Rezin Citadel. Young. Eager. Not burned out like Gyre.

<!-- paragraph:6314 para_id:000018AA style:Normal -->
“So they really don’t let you all out much, huh?” Davian called to Nire, forcing breath back into his lungs. “You must get restless, cooped up in that citadel all the time. Hiding so no one realizes Archeon’s got un-Presented magi lying around.”

<!-- paragraph:6315 para_id:000018AB style:Normal -->
Nire straightened and began to circle him. Davian turned with her, careful, measured.

<!-- paragraph:6316 para_id:000018AC style:Normal -->
“Yeah,” she said. “Poor Granny Serathis can’t bear the thought that someone might see us walking around and think we don’t live up to the Archeon name she’s so obsessed with polishing.”

<!-- paragraph:6317 para_id:000018AD style:Normal -->
The pause bought him a second. I can’t maintain a full Shroud while fighting, he thought. But could I use just part of it? Narrow the focus. Do something with the energy instead of trying to hold everything at once.

<!-- paragraph:6318 para_id:000018AE style:Normal -->
Davian breathed in again. Time stretched. He studied Nire’s stance - her weight distribution. The angle of her shoulders. He studied her the way his mother had taught him to study an opponent. This is the same thing, he realized. Just faster. And far more lethal.

<!-- paragraph:6319 para_id:000018AF style:Normal -->
The martial art she’d drilled into him as a child wasn’t about strength. It was about redirection. About letting an opponent commit, then using their own momentum against them. The Shroud wasn’t different. It converted force, gave its magus control over it for a fraction of a moment. Like Maya. The way she had slammed into stone in front of him and the impact had spilled outward as heat and flame.

<!-- paragraph:6320 para_id:000018B0 style:Normal -->
Make their strength their weakness. Nire tensed. Davian knew what was coming before she moved. A rapid leap forward, weight driving in, power coiled through the right shoulder into a heavy swing. He mapped it once, cleanly, in his mind.

<!-- paragraph:6321 para_id:000018B1 style:Normal -->
Then time snapped back to speed. Nire surged toward him. Davian focused everything on his left forearm. The Shroud flared into being there alone—no wider, no more than he could hold—and he spun his arm into the block just as her gauntleted fist swept past the side of his head. The contact wasn’t physical so much as conceptual.

<!-- paragraph:6322 para_id:000018B2 style:Normal -->
A sudden contest of wills, his Shroud grinding against hers in that narrow intersection where reality bent under competing claims. Pressure screamed up his arm. His teeth clenched as he forced his focus tighter, tighter, holding the Shroud together by sheer intent.

<!-- paragraph:6323 para_id:000018B3 style:Normal -->
The force changed. Not vanished—changed. The impact lost shape, becoming something amorphous, unstable, raw. Energy without direction.

<!-- paragraph:6324 para_id:000018B4 style:Normal -->
Now.

<!-- paragraph:6325 para_id:000018B5 style:Normal -->
He grabbed for it with his mind, desperately, and felt reality tear in a way that felt familiar. He carved a pathway by instinct alone, the torrent of captured force gratefully screaming downwards. His right hand was already moving. He released the Shroud on his left forearm and snapped it into place around his right fist instead, shoving all the excess energy into it as he drove his knife upward in an underhand strike.

<!-- paragraph:6326 para_id:000018B6 style:Normal -->
It hit like a punch more than a slice. His fist slammed into Nire’s chin, Shroud to Shroud, and the stored force detonated outward. Davian’s knife shattered as it was ground to pieces between the two forces of nature. Nire was launched up and back, flipping end over end before crashing onto a table that exploded beneath her in a spray of wood and debris.

<!-- paragraph:6327 para_id:000018B7 style:Normal -->
Davian dropped to one knee. His breath came in ragged gasps, his vision swimming. His mind felt fuzzy, overstretched—like he’d used muscles he hadn’t known existed and torn half of them in the process. The effort left a hollow ache behind his eyes, deep and disorienting.

<!-- paragraph:6328 para_id:000018B8 style:Normal -->
Nire was already pulling herself out of the wreckage. She brushed splintered wood from her jerkin, rolled her jaw once, then looked at him with something like reluctant respect.

<!-- paragraph:6329 para_id:000018B9 style:Normal -->
“To be honest,” she said, “I did not expect that at all.” She winced, pressing her tongue briefly to her teeth. “And it also hurt.”

<!-- paragraph:6330 para_id:000018BA style:Normal -->
Gyre straightened from where he’d been leaning against the ruined doorframe. He sighed, as if mildly inconvenienced, and pushed off the stone. “Yeah. No offense, Nire” he said, “but I’m gonna help you out here.”

<!-- paragraph:6331 para_id:000018BB style:Normal -->
He flexed his gauntleted hands once, zan lattices flaring faintly. “This was supposed to be easy. And if you come back looking like you got knocked around, it’ll look bad on me too.”

<!-- paragraph:6332 para_id:000018BC style:Normal -->
Davian forced himself upright. His legs trembled, the effort of standing feeling disproportionate to the motion, like his body was lagging behind his intent.

<!-- paragraph:6333 para_id:000018BD style:Normal -->
She didn’t react, he realized, watching Nire reset her stance. Not until it hit her.

<!-- paragraph:6334 para_id:000018BE style:Normal -->
Brann would have seen it. Would have adjusted. Physically faster, yes—but more than that, cognitively. Nire and Gyre hadn’t been Presented for a reason. Serathis didn’t want anyone to notice that some Archeon magi were closer to Low Clade than High. But why?

<!-- paragraph:6335 para_id:000018BF style:Normal -->
What was the difference, really? The thought slipped as soon as it formed. His mind felt scraped thin, exhausted, the edges of his focus fraying. He knew it with sudden, cold clarity: he couldn’t beat two of them like this. Not now.

<!-- paragraph:6336 para_id:000018C0 style:Normal -->
I just need more time, he thought. The idea came with a rush of bitter regret. He could have told Maya everything. Could have asked her to train him. To help him understand what he was doing instead of stumbling through it blind—

<!-- paragraph:6337 para_id:000018C1 style:Normal -->
It didn’t matter. Now he was going to die. Or be taken back to Archeon for something worse than death. He had been pulled by the dark thread all the way here. And this was where it ended.

<!-- paragraph:6338 para_id:000018C2 style:Normal -->
Then something clicked—not gently, but decisively. That’s not right. He opened his eyes. The dark thread hadn’t pulled him anywhere. It never had. He had followed it. Chosen it, step by step, because it felt easier than admitting he was deciding.

<!-- paragraph:6339 para_id:000018C3 style:Normal -->
Maya’s voice cut through him, sharp with remembered anger. You act like you have no control over yourself… but you had to plan this.

<!-- paragraph:6340 para_id:000018C4 style:Normal -->
And Harvick, quieter, steadier. You don’t HAVE to do anything.

<!-- paragraph:6341 para_id:000018C5 style:Normal -->
Davian tipped his head back and stared up at the ceiling. A laugh burst out of him—ragged, surprised, almost hysterical.

<!-- paragraph:6342 para_id:000018C6 style:Normal -->
“I get it now,” he said softly.

<!-- paragraph:6343 para_id:000018C7 style:Normal -->
There was no inevitability. No script he couldn’t deviate from. There was always a choice. Always a plan, even if it was ugly, even if it was desperate. No one could force him to do anything—not the Ink, not Archeon, not some dark strand of fate.

<!-- paragraph:6344 para_id:000018C8 style:Normal -->
He would change his choices. He would get to her. And then would figure out what came next.

<!-- paragraph:6345 para_id:000018C9 style:Normal -->
He dropped his gaze. Nire was watching him with narrowed eyes. She flicked a glance sideways at Gyre.

<!-- paragraph:6346 para_id:000018CA style:Normal -->
Gyre frowned. “Okay,” he said slowly, “I’m not sure what kind of philosophical awakening we’re intruding on here, but you know you’re being really weird, right?”

<!-- paragraph:6347 para_id:000018CB style:Normal -->
Davian smiled. “It doesn’t matter,” he said. “I’m going to kill both of you and get out of here.”

<!-- paragraph:6348 para_id:000018CC style:Normal -->
The shock hadn’t fully reached their faces before he moved.

<!-- paragraph:6349 para_id:000018CD style:Normal -->
He launched himself forward, mind screaming bluff, bluff, bluff. They had to hesitate. They would hesitate—he’d just done something neither of them expected, and they clearly weren’t trained to work as a team.

<!-- paragraph:6350 para_id:000018CE style:Normal -->
He split the space between them and twisted toward Gyre, throwing an obvious, telegraphed haymaker. Gyre dodged back on instinct, gauntlets flaring as he reacted, uncertainty written all over his posture.

<!-- paragraph:6351 para_id:000018CF style:Normal -->
Come on, Nire, Davian thought. I hit you. You’re angry. My back is to you.

<!-- paragraph:6352 para_id:000018D0 style:Normal -->
He didn’t know if the timing was right. He had nothing to measure by except instinct and the faint prickle rising along the back of his neck. He feinted right. Then leapt left and forward.

<!-- paragraph:6353 para_id:000018D1 style:Normal -->
Nire came flying past his right shoulder, her strike committed fully, too fast to pull back. Davian barely twisted out of the way as she slammed straight into Gyre. Shrouds collided. Bodies tangled. They went down together in a snarl of limbs, force and distorted space erupting between them.

<!-- paragraph:6354 para_id:000018D2 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t wait to see how it resolved. He turned and sprinted out the nearest doorway.

<!-- paragraph:6355 para_id:000018D3 style:Normal -->
The adjoining hallway blurred past and he took the first turn hard, breaking line of sight with the dining hall. Then another. Angry shouts echoed behind him, followed by the concussive crack of something giving way—stone or wood, he couldn’t tell—close enough that the sound chased him down the corridor.

<!-- paragraph:6356 para_id:000018D4 style:Normal -->
He zigzagged through the house, letting memory take over. The Concord’s hideout was a maze by necessity: an abandoned house expanded again and again over the years, rooms grafted onto rooms, corridors narrowed or widened depending on what materials could be scavenged without drawing attention. Old stone met newer timber. Load-bearing beams sat where they probably shouldn’t. Nothing about it had ever been elegant.

<!-- paragraph:6357 para_id:000018D5 style:Normal -->
Davian slowed just enough to lighten his footfalls, bare feet whispering over uneven floors. He was suddenly grateful he hadn’t thought to put his boots on—grateful for the grip, for the silence—as he curved his path through the structure, angling toward the back of the house.

<!-- paragraph:6358 para_id:000018D6 style:Normal -->
Once I’m outside, I’m exposed. The thought came clean and sharp. So I leave by the quietest route. Out a back window. Over the wall. Into the alleys before they could get eyes on him.

<!-- paragraph:6359 para_id:000018D7 style:Normal -->
Another impact reverberated through the house, harder this time. The house shuddered around him, beams groaning, dust sifting down from somewhere overhead. The Concord’s hideout had questionable structural integrity at the best of times. With Archeon magi using their own fists as battering rams… Davian lengthened his stride again, threading the line between speed and silence. Not that it mattered much—they wouldn’t hear him over the destruction they were causing—but there was a real risk now that the house might simply collapse around him if he lingered.

<!-- paragraph:6360 para_id:000018D8 style:Normal -->
He rounded a last corner and he saw it - a window - narrow, but open - looking out into the thin, dark corridor between the rear of the manor and the outer wall. Normally it would have been a security risk. Right now it felt like a passageway into paradise.

<!-- paragraph:6361 para_id:000018D9 style:Normal -->
Another violent shudder ran through the house. Davian accelerated and leapt, catching the edge of the frame with one hand and vaulting out through the opening just as something inside the house finally gave up. Part of the structure collapsed with a thunderous crack, dust and splinters spewing out through the window behind him as he cleared it.

<!-- paragraph:6362 para_id:000018DA style:Normal -->
He hit the ground in a crouch—and immediately heard a woman’s voice shout, “Hey! He’s back here!”

<!-- paragraph:6363 para_id:000018DB style:Normal -->
He twisted his head sharply to the right. At the corner of the house stood another figure in a jerkin and gauntlets, long hair spilling out from beneath a broad-brimmed hat. Already turning toward him.

<!-- paragraph:6364 para_id:000018DC style:Normal -->
Idiot, he thought. You should have known they’d post someone at the back.

<!-- paragraph:6365 para_id:000018DD style:Normal -->
His mind raced. No time. No space. The wall was too high—there was no way he could climb it before they were on him. He snapped his gaze left. Another Archeon magus, male, was already charging down the narrow corridor toward him, boots pounding, gauntlets glowing faintly as they closed the distance.

<!-- paragraph:6366 para_id:000018DE style:Normal -->
Davian made a decision without letting himself second-guess it. He ran toward the man.

<!-- paragraph:6367 para_id:000018DF style:Normal -->
Confusion flickered across the magi’s face—just a fraction of a second—and Davian used it. He veered sharply right and leapt, forcing his attention into a single point. Focus. Focus on the right foot. He wrapped the Shroud tight around his foot and kicked, converting the impact into propulsion. The force hurled him left and upward, slamming him onto the roof in a wild arc.

<!-- paragraph:6368 para_id:000018E0 style:Normal -->
He flew farther than he’d intended. His landing was ugly. He hit hard, skidded, and scrambled for purchase as the roof shifted beneath him, tiles and timbers sliding loose. The manor groaned and sagged, the collapse spreading, and Davian clawed his way across the unstable surface, breath ragged, fighting to stay upright as the house continued to come apart under him. Somewhere inside, someone was still tearing the place apart—too deep in their own spree of destruction to notice what was happening above them.

<!-- paragraph:6369 para_id:000018E1 style:Normal -->
Davian risked a glance over his shoulder. The two magi had leapt onto the roof behind him, landing with a grace far greater than he’d managed, their entire bodies wrapped in distorting force.

<!-- paragraph:6370 para_id:000018E2 style:Normal -->
A section of roof gave way under his foot. He snapped his gaze forward again and dropped low, using hands and legs together, claws and momentum, hauling himself upward toward the apex as the surface shifted and slid beneath him.

<!-- paragraph:6371 para_id:000018E3 style:Normal -->
What’s the plan? The thought came sharp and insistent. Come on. You can do this. He pushed his speed of thought as far as it would go, trying to sprint and calculate at the same time, his mind skidding between options. Double back and leap for the wall? Too risky with two magi behind him. Fight? Impossible—weaponless, exhausted, against multiple Archeon magi. Go back into the house, try to lose them inside the collapsing structure? Untenable.

<!-- paragraph:6372 para_id:000018E4 style:Normal -->
Right as he crested the apex, the answer snapped into place. Run for the gate. Get out of the hideout’s grounds. Take a gamble on losing them in the alleys he knew better than they did.

<!-- paragraph:6373 para_id:000018E5 style:Normal -->
And at the same moment, he saw the courtyard. The wrinkle in his plan he’d known would be there—only he’d guessed the number wrong. Not two, but four more Archeon magi, spread out in loose defensive positions, already oriented toward the house.

<!-- paragraph:6374 para_id:000018E6 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat, the thought of giving up pressed in hard and sudden. How can Archeon possibly have this many un-Presented magi? His teeth clenched. No. He could still do this. He had to. Change the plan again—faster, better. He sprinted down the far slope of the roof, dodging collapsing sections as beams snapped and tiles slid away beneath him. Focus. Focus on both legs. Somehow. Jump. Clear the wall.

<!-- paragraph:6375 para_id:000018E7 style:Normal -->
At the edge of the roof, he shoved the Shroud outward around both legs and launched himself as hard as he could. For an instant, he could see it. Clearing the wall. Hitting the pavement. Blunting the fall with the Shroud. Vanishing into the alleys—gone.

<!-- paragraph:6376 para_id:000018E8 style:Normal -->
Then he felt it. The roof couldn’t take it - maybe it never could have. The structure failed beneath the sudden force, shattering as he pushed off. The loss of resistance killed his upward momentum, turning the jump into something wrong and shallow. His horizontal speed carried him forward anyway, spinning, flailing, the arc collapsing beneath him. He barely managed to throw a weak Shroud around his body.

<!-- paragraph:6377 para_id:000018E9 style:Normal -->
He hit the cobblestones hard. Bounced. Rolled. Skidded straight through the midst of the bemused Archeon magi - and slammed into the outer wall with bone-rattling force. He had no focus left to do anything useful with the energy. The Shroud dropped. Davian slid down the stone and slumped there, breath knocked out of him, the world ringing as the courtyard closed in. His vision blurred as the world seemed to speed up around him. He staggered, then forced himself back upright, shaking the fuzz out of his head, setting his feet, trying to at least look dangerous.

<!-- paragraph:6378 para_id:000018EA style:Normal -->
The two magi from the roof dropped down to the courtyard and spread out with the others. Four became six, forming a loose half-moon around him as he backed into the wall, arms raised in a defensive posture. Their voices carried, low and overlapping. He has a Shroud - they didn’t tell us that. Why does Serathis want him anyway? What happened to Nire?

<!-- paragraph:6379 para_id:000018EB style:Normal -->
Behind them, the rest of the house finally gave up. The structure collapsed inward with a roar of splintering wood and stone. Dust rolled outward, and from the wreckage Nire and Gyre, wrapped in their Shrouds, smashed their way free like they were wading through rough water. Ungraceful. Angry.

<!-- paragraph:6380 para_id:000018EC style:Normal -->
Davian’s head cleared a little more. Come on, he thought. You just promised you’d get yourself out of this. That you’d change. That you’d do whatever you had to.

<!-- paragraph:6381 para_id:000018ED style:Normal -->
That you’d apologize for being an idiot.

<!-- paragraph:6382 para_id:000018EE style:Normal -->
Eight magi. He looked at them properly now. Similar sigils. None of them moved like Brann had. Their matching gauntlets didn’t burn with the same level of arcane force - cheaper, corners cut. You can use that, he told himself. Somehow. His thoughts raced, scrabbling for leverage.

<!-- paragraph:6383 para_id:000018EF style:Normal -->
Nire pushed through the line of six, Gyre falling in behind her. Davian noticed it immediately—yet another hierarchy layered inside the others.

<!-- paragraph:6384 para_id:000018F0 style:Normal -->
Nire rolled her shoulders, flexed her neck, clenched and unclenched her hands inside the gauntlets. “You know,” she said, “it’s very annoying when you keep running away like that.”

<!-- paragraph:6385 para_id:000018F1 style:Normal -->
She smiled without humor. “Serathis said she wanted you alive. She didn’t say we couldn’t smash all the bones in your legs first.”

<!-- paragraph:6386 para_id:000018F2 style:Normal -->
Davian knew the truth of it as she spoke. He wasn’t getting away. He couldn’t beat all of them like this. But he had made his choice. He drew the Shroud around himself again. It came weakly, flickering, held together by nothing but stubborn will. It felt like gripping something slippery with numb fingers. Nire sneered and tensed to leap—and something fell out of the sky.

<!-- paragraph:6387 para_id:000018F3 style:Normal -->
It came down like a meteor, slamming into Nire and driving her straight into the stone with a thunderous impact. The ground cracked outward. Davian barely had time to throw his arms up and brace his Shroud before a blast of flame erupted from the point of contact. The heat was overwhelming. White. Blinding. For a moment there was nothing but noise and fire.

<!-- paragraph:6388 para_id:000018F4 style:Normal -->
When his vision cleared, his heart lurched. A woman stood where Nire had been. Brown hair. Bare arms. Her facial sigil flared with quiet, terrible intensity. At her feet lay a charred corpse, still smoking.

<!-- paragraph:6389 para_id:000018F5 style:Normal -->
The remaining seven Archeon magi staggered back, stunned, looking at one another, regrouping. She didn’t look at them. She walked straight toward Davian.

<!-- paragraph:6390 para_id:000018F6 style:Normal -->
His heart began to pound harder, his breath catching painfully in his chest. He straightened without meaning to as she approached, every step shrinking the space between them until there was almost none left at all.

<!-- paragraph:6391 para_id:000018F7 style:Normal -->
She stopped inches from him. Looked at him. Almost expectantly. His mouth was dry. His mind fuzzed again, overloaded. “I’m sorry—” he started.

<!-- paragraph:6392 para_id:000018F8 style:Normal -->
Her expression hardened instantly. “Sorry doesn’t even begin to cover it,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:6393 para_id:000018F9 style:Normal -->
He froze. She lifted a hand. Davian looked down. Antithesis rested gently against his chest, held there with precise, terrifying care. As he stared, she leaned closer, close enough that he could feel the cool pressure of her Shroud brushing his skin. Her mouth was at his ear when she whispered, quiet and absolute.

<!-- paragraph:6394 para_id:000018FA style:Normal -->
“If you even think about running away again, I will kill you.”

<!-- paragraph:6395 para_id:000018FB style:Normal -->
He looked up. She meant it. “Now,” she said, “make yourself useful.”  She drew back and tossed the knife to him. Then she turned toward the Archeon magi, who were pulling themselves back into an uneven formation.

<!-- paragraph:6396 para_id:000018FC style:Normal -->
Davian strode to stand beside Maya, clutching the knife in an underhand grip. He stayed close—close enough that their shoulders almost touched. She didn’t look at him.

<!-- paragraph:6397 para_id:000018FD style:Normal -->
“What are we going to do?” he asked quietly. “There’s eight—” He flicked a glance at the charred corpse on the ground. “I mean. Seven of them.”

<!-- paragraph:6398 para_id:000018FE style:Normal -->
“I’ll take the four on the left,” Maya said evenly. “You take the three on the right.”

<!-- paragraph:6399 para_id:000018FF style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t want to say it. Didn’t want to admit it, not now, not like this. He reached inward anyway, poking and pulling at the Ink, trying to coax it awake, to make it do something. Nothing answered.

<!-- paragraph:6400 para_id:00001900 style:Normal -->
He let out a breath. “Yeah, I, uh… I don’t think I can do that.”

<!-- paragraph:6401 para_id:00001901 style:Normal -->
Maya hissed softly, irritation bleeding through. “Why?” she demanded. “You’re super fast, you heal, you almost beat Niall. I shouldn’t have to come save you at all.”

<!-- paragraph:6402 para_id:00001902 style:Normal -->
He winced. “I mean,” he whispered back, “I did beat Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:6403 para_id:00001903 style:Normal -->
Maya darted a glance at him, and it felt like all the heat of her flame was compressed into that look alone.

<!-- paragraph:6404 para_id:00001904 style:Normal -->
“I mean—” he rushed, “I can’t do that stuff anymore. It’s hard to explain, but it just… ran out. Or something.”

<!-- paragraph:6405 para_id:00001905 style:Normal -->
Across the courtyard, the Archeon magi finished forming up around Gyre. He stepped forward slightly, hands open, voice measured.

<!-- paragraph:6406 para_id:00001906 style:Normal -->
“Look,” he said, “Drosin told us all that only having Niall around saved you from him and Serathis. We’re just here to get that one.” He pointed directly at Davian. “So would you mind letting us have him and avoiding any further trouble?”

<!-- paragraph:6407 para_id:00001907 style:Normal -->
Gyre tilted his head. “You can’t sneak up on us anymore, and I’m pretty sure the seven of us can take you. But I’d prefer to avoid additional casualties. Or injuries.”

<!-- paragraph:6408 para_id:00001908 style:Normal -->
“Excuse me,” Maya said sharply, “but we are trying to have a conversation.”

<!-- paragraph:6409 para_id:00001909 style:Normal -->
Davian felt it before he fully saw it. Maya’s eyes moved rapidly, tracking possibilities, threads of motion. Then strands of heat distortion rose from the ground, from the air, from everywhere between them. The space in front of them froze - solidifying into a wall of crystalline cold that cut off the Archeon magi from view.

<!-- paragraph:6410 para_id:0000190A style:Normal -->
At the same time, the gathered strands collapsed inward, coalescing into an orb that ignited from within. It hovered over Maya’s outstretched hand, bright and contained, humming with restrained violence.

<!-- paragraph:6411 para_id:0000190B style:Normal -->
Davian gasped—half from the sudden cold, half from recognition. The same energy transfer he’d barely managed earlier, the one that had nearly emptied him out—only she’d done it effortlessly. Faster. Cleaner. A hundred times more controlled.

<!-- paragraph:6412 para_id:0000190C style:Normal -->
“How can you do that,” he breathed, “and not be exhausted?”

<!-- paragraph:6413 para_id:0000190D style:Normal -->
Maya shot him an odd look. “What, this?” she said. “It’s basically a child’s exercise.”

<!-- paragraph:6414 para_id:0000190E style:Normal -->
His stomach dropped. “I did, like, one one-hundredth of that a minute ago,” he said, “and it took everything I had. I still feel mentally exhausted.”

<!-- paragraph:6415 para_id:0000190F style:Normal -->
He could feel her eyes on him then—not angry, not dismissive, but measuring. Recalculating. Weighing his value in the fight all over again. It didn’t feel good.

<!-- paragraph:6416 para_id:00001910 style:Normal -->
Voices carried from the other side of the ice wall—muffled, overlapping, rising in pitch as the Archeon magi worked themselves toward action.

<!-- paragraph:6417 para_id:00001911 style:Normal -->
Davian pointed toward the charred corpse on the ground. “Can’t you just do that again?”

<!-- paragraph:6418 para_id:00001912 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t even look at it. “Sure,” she said flatly. “I’ll fly way up into the air while you fight seven magi by yourself, and then I’ll surprise-attack them one at a time when they already know I’m coming.”

<!-- paragraph:6419 para_id:00001913 style:Normal -->
He opened his mouth, then closed it.

<!-- paragraph:6420 para_id:00001914 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped closer. The orb still hovered above her hand, firestorm contained so tightly Davian couldn’t feel a hint of heat from it despite the violence packed inside. She leaned in until her voice was low and sharp.

<!-- paragraph:6421 para_id:00001915 style:Normal -->
“So. Apparently I have about five seconds to teach you how to be a competent magus. Listen closely.”

<!-- paragraph:6422 para_id:00001916 style:Normal -->
She tapped his chest with her free hand.

<!-- paragraph:6423 para_id:00001917 style:Normal -->
“You know how you know where your legs are without looking at them?” she said. “You should have a better sense of that than most, because of how you fight. Shrouds work off that sense.”

<!-- paragraph:6424 para_id:00001918 style:Normal -->
Davian started, “Then why—”

<!-- paragraph:6425 para_id:00001919 style:Normal -->
Maya cut him off with a sharp gesture, “Didn’t I tell you not to talk?”

<!-- paragraph:6426 para_id:0000191A style:Normal -->
“No,” he muttered.

<!-- paragraph:6427 para_id:0000191B style:Normal -->
“Anyway,” she continued without missing a beat, “you shouldn’t be able to do this at all. So stop worrying about why and figure it out.”

<!-- paragraph:6428 para_id:0000191C style:Normal -->
She fixed him with a look. “Last thing. The mental effort for carving depends on how complex the shape is, and how far from your body it is. That means for you—right now—you carve in lines. And you carve right next to yourself.”

<!-- paragraph:6429 para_id:0000191D style:Normal -->
A heavy pounding slammed into the ice wall behind her.

<!-- paragraph:6430 para_id:0000191E style:Normal -->
Maya took a step back. Davian immediately regretted the space.

<!-- paragraph:6431 para_id:0000191F style:Normal -->
“You’re just going to stand there?” she said. “I need more room.”

<!-- paragraph:6432 para_id:00001920 style:Normal -->
He backed up. For a heartbeat, their eyes met—something unspoken and taut passing between them. Then the ice wall shattered.

<!-- paragraph:6433 para_id:00001921 style:Normal -->
Seven magi struck it at once, force collapsing inward in a deafening explosion of fractured crystal and distorted air.

<!-- paragraph:6434 para_id:00001922 style:Normal -->
“You can do this,” Maya said. She turned. The orb in her hand detonated outward into a beam of searing light, leaving a burning afterimage stamped across Davian’s vision as he twisted away—straight toward the Archeon magi bearing down on him.

<!-- paragraph:6435 para_id:00001923 style:Normal -->
He seized the spike of adrenaline, and the moment stretched again. Only two magi were coming at him. His mind still tumbled with everything he’d just done, everything Maya had just said. For a heartbeat it felt like too much, an impossible stack of instructions and sensations, threatening to lock him in place.

<!-- paragraph:6436 para_id:00001924 style:Normal -->
Then he felt her. Maya’s presence burned at the edge of his awareness like a steady flame. She’d come to help him. She thought he could do this.

<!-- paragraph:6437 para_id:00001925 style:Normal -->
One thing at a time, he told himself. Keep moving.

<!-- paragraph:6438 para_id:00001926 style:Normal -->
He stepped forward with his right foot. Focus on the body. The weight of limbs. The pull of muscle and balance. He closed his eyes for a fraction of a second and felt himself as a whole—his stance, the ground beneath him, the alignment his mother had drilled into him since childhood. He linked that awareness to the reflex he’d been brushing against all morning, and suddenly it felt natural.

<!-- paragraph:6439 para_id:00001927 style:Normal -->
Davian opened his eyes. The Shroud wrapped his body in a single, coherent skin.

<!-- paragraph:6440 para_id:00001928 style:Normal -->
You know how to do this, he thought, steadying. It’s hand to hand combat - just at a deadlier scale.

<!-- paragraph:6441 para_id:00001929 style:Normal -->
Both magi came in with driving blows, committed and predictable - like Nire had been. Not quite synchronized.

<!-- paragraph:6442 para_id:0000192A style:Normal -->
I can do this. He ducked low under the first swing. His Shroud thinned as his attention waned—but that was fine. He didn’t need it constantly - only when attacking or being hit.

<!-- paragraph:6443 para_id:0000192B style:Normal -->
He snapped his focus back, lunged forward and past the first magus, leaned narrowly away from the second’s strike, and drove Antithesis up into her neck. For a split second, he could feel his Shroud - somehow expanded around the knife - grinding against hers—a brief, violent contest—then she staggered back a few steps.

<!-- paragraph:6444 para_id:0000192C style:Normal -->
But she didn’t go down. The blow that would have killed a normal opponent barely slowed her. Davian threw himself sideways on instinct, dropping low and rolling under a strike from behind, and sprang back up into a crouch, breath sharp in his lungs.

<!-- paragraph:6445 para_id:0000192D style:Normal -->
Okay, he thought. Not exactly the same. Shrouds didn’t have weak points the way bodies did. And magi recovered far faster from impacts. That meant neutralizing a numbers advantage was much harder than he’d hoped.

<!-- paragraph:6446 para_id:0000192E style:Normal -->
A spike of panic flared at the thought of Maya fighting the other five, but he crushed it down. He couldn’t afford to look away.

<!-- paragraph:6447 para_id:0000192F style:Normal -->
How do Maya and Niall fight? The memories came quickly. Maya crashing down into Nire, the impact erupting into flame that caused death in an instant. Niall calling down a torrent of lightning to annihilate Brann outright.

<!-- paragraph:6448 para_id:00001930 style:Normal -->
They set up a moment—and ended the fight in one decisive strike. Everything else was preparation. Forcing an opening. In that split second, a plan formed. He didn’t have time to evaluate it.

<!-- paragraph:6449 para_id:00001931 style:Normal -->
Step one: control the fight. Control the positioning.

<!-- paragraph:6450 para_id:00001932 style:Normal -->
Davian sprinted straight at his opponents. He feinted toward the magus on the left, then changed direction without warning and slammed his shoulder into the one on the right. The impact staggered her backward. She swung blindly in response, a heavy, predictable haymaker.

<!-- paragraph:6451 para_id:00001933 style:Normal -->
He slid low beneath it and reached down, fingers closing around her ankle. It was something he would never do in a normal fight. But the Shroud made him stronger. Bolder. He planted his foot and yanked, spinning his body hard. The magus was ripped off balance and thrown aside, her body slamming into the ground and bouncing, rolling away as Davian turned back toward the second opponent.

<!-- paragraph:6452 para_id:00001934 style:Normal -->
Barely fast enough. He twisted around a jab from a gauntleted fist, the blow whistling past his face. He didn’t even consider blocking—he knew better than to test that.

<!-- paragraph:6453 para_id:00001935 style:Normal -->
Step two: break their balance.

<!-- paragraph:6454 para_id:00001936 style:Normal -->
He had only moments before the first magus recovered. He kicked at the inside of his opponent’s knee. The reaction was instinctive—eyes dropping, weight shifting, a half-step back. Davian didn’t give him time to correct. He drove a left hook toward the man’s head, Shroud slamming into Shroud in a jarring, disorienting impact. The magus reeled, his guard collapsing for just a heartbeat.

<!-- paragraph:6455 para_id:00001937 style:Normal -->
Step three, Davian thought grimly. Hope I learned enough in five seconds to finish this.

<!-- paragraph:6456 para_id:00001938 style:Normal -->
The counterstrike came wild and unfocused. Davian read it and moved at the same time, throwing out his left hand as his mind locked onto a single, simple image.

<!-- paragraph:6457 para_id:00001939 style:Normal -->
A line. Two points. Impact to blade.

<!-- paragraph:6458 para_id:0000193A style:Normal -->
He tore a path through reality along that line, redirecting the force—his own, his Shroud’s, and his opponent’s—into Antithesis. For an instant, everything went impossibly clear. Antithesis ground against the opposing Shroud, resistance screaming through his arm. Then it gave way with a sensation like wrenching iron.

<!-- paragraph:6459 para_id:0000193B style:Normal -->
The blade sliced through. Through the Shroud. Through skin. Through bone. It tore a lethal gash across the magus’ chest. The man looked down, then up, shock spreading across his face as the knife shuddered to a stop against his sternum. His Shroud winked out entirely. For a brief, unbearable moment, their eyes met. And in that instant of dying recognition, Davian saw it—the reflection he hadn’t expected. This man could have been him. Training in secret. Striving for legitimacy that would never come. Fighting to matter, only to die unrecognized. Then the body sagged, and the moment was gone.

<!-- paragraph:6460 para_id:0000193C style:Normal -->
Davian flared his Shroud instinctively just before something slammed into his back. The impact hurled him forward in a disorienting spiral. He hit the ground hard, protected from the worst of it but still sent skidding, his grip failing as Antithesis tore free from his hand and clattered away out of reach.

<!-- paragraph:6461 para_id:0000193D style:Normal -->
His vision fuzzed at the edges. He tried to push himself up and failed, collapsing again, barely catching himself with one hand before his face met the stone. His breath came in short, useless gasps.

<!-- paragraph:6462 para_id:0000193E style:Normal -->
I can’t do this. The thought landed with crushing certainty. I’m too slow. Too weak. I didn’t get the training I needed.

<!-- paragraph:6463 para_id:0000193F style:Normal -->
And beneath that, sharper and uglier: Why am I even doing this? To kill people who are just like me? Through the blur, he saw the remaining female Archeon magus standing over the body of the one he’d just killed. She stared down at it for a moment, then nudged it with her foot—not gently. Not respectfully.

<!-- paragraph:6464 para_id:00001940 style:Normal -->
Explosions rippled across the courtyard. Heat and force shook the ground in uneven pulses. Somewhere beyond her, the other fight was still raging. Davian felt a pull he couldn’t quite name—warm, insistent, like sunlight breaking through clouds. It had nothing to do with the ideals he’d been raised on and now realized he’d never really understood.

<!-- paragraph:6465 para_id:00001941 style:Normal -->
But he knew—without words, without logic—that there was something he needed from Maya. Something unfinished, unresolved. An apology he hadn’t made. An explanation he hadn’t given. Until he did, he couldn’t let himself die. He couldn’t let himself be taken.

<!-- paragraph:6466 para_id:00001942 style:Normal -->
The female magus glanced between him and the far side of the courtyard, attention clearly split.

<!-- paragraph:6467 para_id:00001943 style:Normal -->
I have to keep her here, he thought. I can’t let her join the others.

<!-- paragraph:6468 para_id:00001944 style:Normal -->
Davian dragged himself up to one knee. Pain screamed through him, white and insistent, and he nearly collapsed again. He forced himself upright inch by inch, swallowing a groan, using the sheer force of that need—that pull—to burn through the fog of pain and despair. The Shroud came back, thin but present, wrapped around him by stubborn will alone. He lifted his head and met her gaze, forcing his voice to steady.

<!-- paragraph:6469 para_id:00001945 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” he said, trying for confidence and not quite finding it. “I’m just getting started.”

<!-- paragraph:6470 para_id:00001946 style:Normal -->
He swallowed. “Maybe you should call for some help.”

<!-- paragraph:6471 para_id:00001947 style:Normal -->
She cast one last look toward the far side of the courtyard. Then she ran at him.

<!-- paragraph:6472 para_id:00001948 style:Normal -->
Davian felt the shift in himself immediately—not panic this time, not scrambling calculation, but something cleaner snapping into place. The fear was still there, the exhaustion still screaming in the background, but they no longer had the reins.

<!-- paragraph:6473 para_id:00001949 style:Normal -->
Wring it out, he told himself. Push it out. As long as you’re alive, there’s still more left.

<!-- paragraph:6474 para_id:0000194A style:Normal -->
He read her attack almost before she committed to it. The same opening, the same driving momentum. Archeon had trained them all from the same book, drilled the same assumptions into their bodies.

<!-- paragraph:6475 para_id:0000194B style:Normal -->
He stepped aside easily. Too easily. He moved past her as she overextended, the motion smooth enough it almost felt like floating—not the hollow dissociation of the Ink, but the opposite. Reality burned bright and sharp, so intense it stripped everything else away.

<!-- paragraph:6476 para_id:0000194C style:Normal -->
He bent as he moved, scooped Antithesis up from the stone without breaking stride, and spun. The knife came up in a perfect arc, catching and deflecting a gauntleted strike before he consciously registered it.

<!-- paragraph:6477 para_id:0000194D style:Normal -->
He heard his own voice, surprised by how steady it sounded. “I get it now,” he said, breath tight but controlled. “You’re supposed to be the physical clade. The dirty fighters.”

<!-- paragraph:6478 para_id:0000194E style:Normal -->
She snarled and pressed in harder.

<!-- paragraph:6479 para_id:0000194F style:Normal -->
“But you fell into the same trap as the High Clades,” Davian continued, circling, forcing her to turn. “You focused on magic. Techniques. Reputation.”

<!-- paragraph:6480 para_id:00001950 style:Normal -->
He slipped another blow, redirected a third with a flicker of Shroud and wrist.

<!-- paragraph:6481 para_id:00001951 style:Normal -->
“And you forgot to keep honing the basics everything else is built on.”

<!-- paragraph:6482 para_id:00001952 style:Normal -->
She attacked faster, frustration bleeding through discipline. The strikes came in a wild rush, power without structure, gauntlets hammering down in a storm meant to overwhelm through sheer force.

<!-- paragraph:6483 para_id:00001953 style:Normal -->
Davian danced around it. With his Shroud dampening the impacts and his focus stripping away the shock and awe, the barrage lost its menace. The fearsome Archeon techniques, once terrifying, collapsed into something raw and undirected.

<!-- paragraph:6484 para_id:00001954 style:Normal -->
It felt like watching a child’s tantrum. Davian didn’t need to force an opening; she gave it to him. Off-balance, overcommitted, attacking with more rage than coordination. He stepped into it, stole her momentum, and drove Antithesis forward. The blade met resistance for the briefest instant as it ground against her Shroud—then punched through. She fell.

<!-- paragraph:6485 para_id:00001955 style:Normal -->
Davian staggered back a step as her body hit the stone, breath coming hard, the knife still trembling in his hand. The courtyard had gone silent. He looked around, disoriented, and then up. Maya was sitting on top of the wall, one leg dangling over the edge, like she had been watching a play she’d seen before. When she saw him looking, she pushed off and dropped down, landing in a burst of flame that flared from her heels as they struck the ground.

<!-- paragraph:6486 para_id:00001956 style:Normal -->
She walked toward him through the wreckage. Charred bodies lay scattered across the courtyard. As she passed them, Davian noticed details he couldn’t quite reconcile—her hair still perfect, pulled back into a single tail at the nape of her neck, a few loose strands framing her face as if placed there on purpose. Had she redone her hair? Or had the fight really been that easy for her? He glanced around again. One of the bodies lay facing the gate, posture frozen mid-flight, burned out from behind.

<!-- paragraph:6487 para_id:00001957 style:Normal -->
Maya kept coming until she was only a few feet away. Davian realized, absurdly, that he hoped she’d only seen the parts of his fight where he’d looked competent. When their eyes met, his head went light again. His grip loosened on Antithesis without him meaning it to.

<!-- paragraph:6488 para_id:00001958 style:Normal -->
“None of this makes sense,” Maya said. “So you’re coming with me. You’re going to give me answers.”

<!-- paragraph:6489 para_id:00001959 style:Normal -->
“Wait,” Davian said quickly. “They took my parents. We need to find them—go after them.”

<!-- paragraph:6490 para_id:0000195A style:Normal -->
Maya tilted her head. “So you’re saying you won’t come back with me?”

<!-- paragraph:6491 para_id:0000195B style:Normal -->
She took a step closer. Davian’s breath picked up. “I don’t know,” he said. “I want to go with you. I just don’t know what Archeon is doing to them.”

<!-- paragraph:6492 para_id:0000195C style:Normal -->
She closed the remaining distance. “How does your Shroud feel?” she asked. “Put it up. Let me see it.”

<!-- paragraph:6493 para_id:0000195D style:Normal -->
He did, reflexively. “It’s actually pretty good right now,” he said. “Why—” That last word came out wrong, off-balance, as she stepped right up to him.

<!-- paragraph:6494 para_id:0000195E style:Normal -->
She reached out. Her arms went around him.

<!-- paragraph:6495 para_id:0000195F style:Normal -->
“What are you doing?” he asked, frozen.

<!-- paragraph:6496 para_id:00001960 style:Normal -->
“Shh,” Maya said quietly. “Focus on your Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:6497 para_id:00001961 style:Normal -->
Her body pressed against his. He felt the cool edge of her Shroud brushing his skin, the contrast sharp and disorienting. Their faces were close—too close—and his mind flickered with memory: warmth, breath, the feeling of kissing her. Is that what she wants?

<!-- paragraph:6498 para_id:00001962 style:Normal -->
“Sorry,” she said softly, right by his ear. “But I can’t trust you not to run away again.”

<!-- paragraph:6499 para_id:00001963 style:Normal -->
The ground exploded beneath them.

<!-- paragraph:6500 para_id:00001964 style:Normal -->
Force hurled them upward in a violent surge. Davian gasped as gravity vanished and then inverted, his instincts screaming as he fought to keep his Shroud intact.

<!-- paragraph:6501 para_id:00001965 style:Normal -->
They were flung into the air together, rising fast, the courtyard dropping away below them. Davian clung to his focus, to her, to the thin skin of distorted reality holding him together as everything else fell away.

<!-- paragraph:6508 para_id:0000196C style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 32

<!-- paragraph:6509 para_id:0000196D style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 32

<!-- paragraph:6511 para_id:0000196F style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6512 para_id:00001970 style:Normal -->
Maya flew hard. Explosive propulsion ignited behind her in controlled bursts, each one punching her higher and faster through the air as she carried Davian’s unconscious body against her chest. He was heavier than he looked. He stirred once, a faint, reflexive movement, and she adjusted her grip without slowing.

<!-- paragraph:6513 para_id:00001971 style:Normal -->
Good, she thought. Still breathing.

<!-- paragraph:6514 para_id:00001972 style:Normal -->
She suspected she might have hit him a little harder than was strictly necessary.

<!-- paragraph:6515 para_id:00001973 style:Normal -->
From above, the Rezin Citadel resolved into sharp geometry. Guards along the wall were already reacting—pointing upward, shouting, weapons half-raised before recognition caught up with urgency. A runner peeled away from the wall and sprinted toward the central spire.

<!-- paragraph:6516 para_id:00001974 style:Normal -->
Efficient, Maya noted distantly. She angled downward and came in fast, shedding velocity at the last possible moment. Flame sublimated outward as she landed in front of the central spire, heat venting into the stone in a controlled bloom. By the time she straightened, Aldira was already coming out of the entrance with the runner.

<!-- paragraph:6517 para_id:00001975 style:Normal -->
Aldira looked at her for a long second. Took in Davian’s limp form. Then she sighed.

<!-- paragraph:6518 para_id:00001976 style:Normal -->
“I’m not even going to ask what happened right now,” Aldira said. “What do you need?”

<!-- paragraph:6519 para_id:00001977 style:Normal -->
Maya shifted her grip slightly. “He needs a healer.”

<!-- paragraph:6520 para_id:00001978 style:Normal -->
Aldira turned and snapped an order to the runner, who took off immediately, boots striking stone hard as he disappeared down the corridor. Then Aldira looked back at Davian, eyes narrowing just a fraction.

<!-- paragraph:6521 para_id:00001979 style:Normal -->
“You know,” she said dryly, “it’s really not good for someone to get knocked out like that.”

<!-- paragraph:6522 para_id:0000197A style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond.

<!-- paragraph:6523 para_id:0000197B style:Normal -->
“I know Harvick was sort of his mentor,” Aldira went on, “but if he keeps taking blows to the head, he’s going to end up a little too similar to Harvick.”

<!-- paragraph:6524 para_id:0000197C style:Normal -->
Maya tightened her jaw and adjusted her hold again as Davian stirred faintly.

<!-- paragraph:6525 para_id:0000197D style:Normal -->
The healer and her attendants arrived quickly, hands efficient as they took Davian’s weight from Maya without ceremony. She let go reluctantly, watching his head loll as they adjusted him, noticing how vulnerable he looked when he wasn’t fighting.

<!-- paragraph:6526 para_id:0000197E style:Normal -->
Aldira watched them for a beat before speaking. “I do have to ask,” she said. “What’s the plan? Are we going to be able to contain him safely?”

<!-- paragraph:6527 para_id:0000197F style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled. “Davian told me most of his abilities had ‘run out,’ somehow.” She gestured briefly, indicating his arms as the healers adjusted their grip. “They were tied to the ink in these tattoos, but those have faded.”

<!-- paragraph:6528 para_id:00001980 style:Normal -->
She paused, choosing precision over reassurance. “He still has a Shroud. And some Archeon-style abilities. But they didn’t seem especially powerful. A well-constructed cell should be able to hold him.”

<!-- paragraph:6529 para_id:00001981 style:Normal -->
Aldira nodded once, considering. “I see. I also see that ‘Davian’ told you a new name. Is this his real one?”

<!-- paragraph:6530 para_id:00001982 style:Normal -->
“I can’t be sure,” Maya said. “I asked him and he seemed sincere when he said his name was Davian Virellis.”

<!-- paragraph:6531 para_id:00001983 style:Normal -->
That caught Aldira’s attention, but she didn’t comment on it immediately. Instead, she turned back to the healers. “Set up a recovery cot in the underground cells,” she said. “Close him in first. Then heal him through the bars.”

<!-- paragraph:6532 para_id:00001984 style:Normal -->
The healers acknowledged and moved off with Davian. When they were gone, Maya looked back at Aldira. “What was that reaction?” she asked.

<!-- paragraph:6533 para_id:00001985 style:Normal -->
Aldira gestured for Maya to follow her. “The name stirred something,” she said as they started toward her office. “A report from a few years ago. I can’t recall the details right now.”

<!-- paragraph:6534 para_id:00001986 style:Normal -->
When they reached the office, Maya took a seat as Aldira rummaged with practiced ease, opening drawers, sliding stacks of paper aside, pulling folders from shelves and replacing them just as quickly. Maya idly wondered if the apparent lack of clear organization was merely another safety measure Aldira employed.

<!-- paragraph:6535 para_id:00001987 style:Normal -->
After Aldira had been searching for a moment, she briefly paused and shot Maya a glance, “I assume Niall and Harvick are all right.”

<!-- paragraph:6536 para_id:00001988 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded. “It’s a long story, but yes. I got some information that let me track Davian down, and at least when I left them, they were safe.”

<!-- paragraph:6537 para_id:00001989 style:Normal -->
Aldira gave a small nod and returned to thumbing through her files. After another minute, she paused, then pulled a single sheet of paper from a thin folder.

<!-- paragraph:6538 para_id:0000198A style:Normal -->
“Ah,” she said. “This is what I was looking for.”

<!-- paragraph:6539 para_id:0000198B style:Normal -->
She brought it over and set it on the desk between them. It was densely covered in neat, compact handwriting. Names ran down the page in orderly lines. Many had been crossed out or amended, some heavily faded, others sharp and dark, marking different points in time.

<!-- paragraph:6540 para_id:0000198C style:Normal -->
Aldira tapped one entry. “Here,” she said. The line read: Concord org. followed by a crossed-out name. Beneath it, in fresher ink, was written: K. Virellis.

<!-- paragraph:6541 para_id:0000198D style:Normal -->
“This is just my index sheet,” Aldira said. “But it jogs my memory for the full report. I received it ten or twelve years ago, I think. Don’t get old—you end up needing things like this to keep everything straight.”

<!-- paragraph:6542 para_id:0000198E style:Normal -->
Maya thought that remembering a detail like this after a decade was hardly a sign of failing memory, but she didn’t say it. Instead, she asked, “Okay. So what does this mean?”

<!-- paragraph:6543 para_id:0000198F style:Normal -->
“Oh. Right.” Aldira leaned back slightly. “I try to keep track of the heads of various criminal organizations. The ones that like to style themselves as members of the ‘Masked Clades.’”

<!-- paragraph:6544 para_id:00001990 style:Normal -->
She gestured at the page. “There’s a lot of turnover, as you can see. Makes it a difficult task.”

<!-- paragraph:6545 para_id:00001991 style:Normal -->
Her finger returned to the entry. “Anyway, back then I received a report that leadership of this Concord organization—there’s some other word that goes with it, Midnight or Darkness or something equally dramatic—had changed hands.”

<!-- paragraph:6546 para_id:00001992 style:Normal -->
She tapped K. Virellis again.

<!-- paragraph:6547 para_id:00001993 style:Normal -->
“And as you know,” Aldira said, “that’s just polite language for this person removing the prior leader and taking ownership.”

<!-- paragraph:6548 para_id:00001994 style:Normal -->
Maya looked at the paper again. “If this was ten years ago,” she said, “it was probably one of Davian’s parents. When I went to capture him, he said Archeon had taken them.”

<!-- paragraph:6549 para_id:00001995 style:Normal -->
Aldira studied the entry for a moment. “At this point,” she said, “maybe you should catch me up.”

<!-- paragraph:6550 para_id:00001996 style:Normal -->
Maya did—briefly, selectively. She described the number and coordination of the many Archeon magi and that they had been defeated. She omitted the level of Davian’s involvement in the fight - or that she had coached him into improving his magic - and skirted the topic of how he had ended up unconscious. Aldira listened without interrupting. Maya had the distinct sense that Aldira could see the gaps in the story, but chose not to push into them.

<!-- paragraph:6551 para_id:00001997 style:Normal -->
When Maya finished, Aldira leaned back slightly and furrowed her brow. “It’s… alarming,” she said, “that Archeon has managed to keep this many un-Presented magi concealed. And the way they were deployed suggests this wasn’t an isolated group. There are likely more.”

<!-- paragraph:6552 para_id:00001998 style:Normal -->
Aldira looked up at Maya. “Serathis isn’t going to like what happened. She may accuse you of interfering in her Clade’s affairs without cause. Siding with a criminal organization against High Clade magi won’t look good.”

<!-- paragraph:6553 para_id:00001999 style:Normal -->
Aldira paused briefly, contemplative. “Of course, that would require acknowledging testimony from un-Presented magi. Which comes with its own complications.”

<!-- paragraph:6554 para_id:0000199A style:Normal -->
“It’s a moot point,” Maya said. “Serathis won’t find out.”

<!-- paragraph:6555 para_id:0000199B style:Normal -->
Aldira raised an eyebrow. “And why is that?”

<!-- paragraph:6556 para_id:0000199C style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t hesitate. “They’re all dead.”

<!-- paragraph:6557 para_id:0000199D style:Normal -->
Aldira blinked. “Oh,” she said. “And how did that happen?”

<!-- paragraph:6558 para_id:0000199E style:Normal -->
Maya threw up her hands in frustration. “Why ask the obvious? I killed them. To prevent the exact things you just said might happen.”

<!-- paragraph:6559 para_id:0000199F style:Normal -->
Aldira folded her hands slowly. She studied Maya for a long moment.

<!-- paragraph:6560 para_id:000019A0 style:Normal -->
“What happened,” she asked quietly, “to the girl who screamed at Niall to stop cutting down retreating soldiers? Who asked for an enemy soldier to be healed? Who let Alra live after she let an assassin into the citadel?”

<!-- paragraph:6561 para_id:000019A1 style:Normal -->
“That girl,” Maya said, “didn’t have the strength to keep her family safe. You told me those were bad decisions.”

<!-- paragraph:6562 para_id:000019A2 style:Normal -->
Aldira shook her head. “No. I told you the consequences. So you could choose with all the information. It’s my job to be the pessimist.”

<!-- paragraph:6563 para_id:000019A3 style:Normal -->
Maya’s jaw tightened. “How is this any different from killing attacking mercenaries who couldn’t even defend themselves from me?”

<!-- paragraph:6564 para_id:000019A4 style:Normal -->
Aldira didn’t answer immediately. “You’ll have to decide that yourself,” she said. “I’m not saying you’re wrong this time. But I see abrupt changes, and I want to be sure you’re making them with your eyes open.”

<!-- paragraph:6565 para_id:000019A5 style:Normal -->
Maya stood to leave. “I can’t take all this second-guessing. It’s done. Just let it go.”

<!-- paragraph:6566 para_id:000019A6 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s expression softened, regret creeping in. “I’m sorry, Maya. I don’t want to add pressure. Creator knows you’re under enough already. It’s not my job to crush your spirit—or to turn you into a copy of Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:6567 para_id:000019A7 style:Normal -->
Maya paused at the door. She remembered Niall on the table. The silver lattice that looked like a cage holding together a hollow man made of lightning.

<!-- paragraph:6568 para_id:000019A8 style:Normal -->
“I’m not going to be a copy of Niall,” she said. “I can see now that his methods don’t work. I’m going to be better. Stronger.”

<!-- paragraph:6569 para_id:000019A9 style:Normal -->
She left. Outside the office, Erynn was waiting with her two guards. Erynn tried to ask questions as they walked, but Maya cut her off almost immediately.

<!-- paragraph:6570 para_id:000019AA style:Normal -->
“I’m not in the mood,” she said, and kept moving.

<!-- paragraph:6571 para_id:000019AB style:Normal -->
Erynn took the hint—for once—and followed her to Maya’s office without pressing further. When they arrived, Maya waved the guards away and dropped into her chair.

<!-- paragraph:6572 para_id:000019AC style:Normal -->
“Let me know when Niall and Harvick return,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:6573 para_id:000019AD style:Normal -->
Then, more quietly, angled so the guards outside wouldn’t hear: “And in the meantime, bring me the interim plans for maintaining security while the Registry is down.”

<!-- paragraph:6574 para_id:000019AE style:Normal -->
Erynn nodded and left briefly, returning with a bundle of documents.

<!-- paragraph:6575 para_id:000019AF style:Normal -->
Maya worked. She annotated the security plans Aldira had already drawn up—thorough, methodical, and clearly prepared in advance. Of course Aldira had been ready for something like this. Maya adjusted patrol routes, reassigned coverage, made marginal notes where assumptions would need to be revisited once more information came in.

<!-- paragraph:6576 para_id:000019B0 style:Normal -->
Erynn lounged idly in the chair across from her desk, watching.

<!-- paragraph:6577 para_id:000019B1 style:Normal -->
“So,” she said eventually, “are you going to fire the zanwrights?”

<!-- paragraph:6578 para_id:000019B2 style:Normal -->
“No,” Maya said without looking up. “Not for now. Dismissing them would signal that something major has gone wrong. It would make us look weak.”

<!-- paragraph:6579 para_id:000019B3 style:Normal -->
She paused, pen hovering. I have to assume we can get the Heart back. The thought landed automatically—and she immediately cursed herself. She should have gotten more information from Davian before she “restrained” him.

<!-- paragraph:6580 para_id:000019B4 style:Normal -->
Erynn tilted her head. “It’s hard to imagine any High Clade with an intelligence network worth mentioning doesn’t already know something big happened.”

<!-- paragraph:6581 para_id:000019B5 style:Normal -->
Maya’s pen resumed moving. “I can tell you’re fishing for information,” she said. “You need to stop.”

<!-- paragraph:6582 para_id:000019B6 style:Normal -->
Erynn huffed. “Fine. Then can I fish for information about that hot guy I heard you flew back with today?”

<!-- paragraph:6583 para_id:000019B7 style:Normal -->
“I’m not in the mood, Erynn.”

<!-- paragraph:6584 para_id:000019B8 style:Normal -->
Erynn leaned back and stared up at the ceiling. “I hear he got taken down to the prison cells.”

<!-- paragraph:6585 para_id:000019B9 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t respond. She focused on the page in front of her.

<!-- paragraph:6586 para_id:000019BA style:Normal -->
“I guess,” Erynn added, still not looking at her, “you like bad boys.”

<!-- paragraph:6587 para_id:000019BB style:Normal -->
Maya looked up. She wanted to be angry. She really did. But the comment landed wrong—ridiculous against the weight of the day, against burned courtyards and broken plans and everything that had nearly gone catastrophically worse.

<!-- paragraph:6588 para_id:000019BC style:Normal -->
She laughed. The sound surprised her as much as it did Erynn. Erynn blinked, then started laughing too. Something about it—about the timing, the release—punctured the tension all at once. Maya laughed harder, tears stinging her eyes as the absurdity of the day finally caught up with her.

<!-- paragraph:6589 para_id:000019BD style:Normal -->
They kept laughing until it hurt. Until there was nothing left to wring out. Maya finally leaned forward and rested her cheek on the desk, the cool wood pressing against her skin. Her body felt weak, emptied.

<!-- paragraph:6590 para_id:000019BE style:Normal -->
Erynn had gone boneless in her chair, slumped and breathless. She spun herself lazily toward Maya until they were facing each other—Maya flat against the desk, Erynn sideways in her chair.

<!-- paragraph:6591 para_id:000019BF style:Normal -->
Their eyes met. “So,” Erynn said, “are we all going to die?”

<!-- paragraph:6592 para_id:000019C0 style:Normal -->
Maya closed her eyes for a moment. “Probably,” she said.

<!-- paragraph:6593 para_id:000019C1 style:Normal -->
A knock interrupted them. Maya jerked upright, swiping at her face and straightening instinctively, trying to look alert and composed. The door opened and one of the guards stepped in, wearing an odd expression. For a brief, mortifying second, Maya wondered if the door had been thick enough to contain the sound of their laughter.

<!-- paragraph:6594 para_id:000019C2 style:Normal -->
“Niall and Harvick are back,” the guard said. “And Niall wants to see you.”

<!-- paragraph:6595 para_id:000019C3 style:Normal -->
Maya groaned. “Can’t he come to my office for once?”

<!-- paragraph:6596 para_id:000019C4 style:Normal -->
The guard stared at her, visibly startled. Maya caught herself, smoothed her expression, and exhaled. “All right,” she said more evenly. “Take me to him.”

<!-- paragraph:6597 para_id:000019C5 style:Normal -->
She followed the guard out, leaving Erynn and the others behind. When they reached Niall’s office, Maya stepped inside alone. Niall stood stiff-backed, posture immaculate, radiating uncompromising authority as if nothing had ever shaken him. The sight made Maya falter for half a step. How does he do that? she wondered, remembering him on Zirene’s worktable. In contrast, Harvick lounged with one leg draped over the arm of a chair.

<!-- paragraph:6598 para_id:000019C6 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Maya said, breaking the silence, “apparently today is the day I tour every office in the Citadel. What did you want to see me for?”

<!-- paragraph:6599 para_id:000019C7 style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted. Niall shot him a sidelong look, then fixed his gaze back on Maya. “The workmen I dispatched to find the location where you originally discovered the Codex have reported in.”

<!-- paragraph:6600 para_id:000019C8 style:Normal -->
Maya blinked, momentarily confused—then remembered. The underground passage. The dream. It felt impossibly distant now.

<!-- paragraph:6601 para_id:000019C9 style:Normal -->
“It took them considerable time,” Niall continued. “Your description was vague. They examined every relevant wall in the area, testing for concealment. They believe they’ve identified a location where there is an empty space behind a wall.”

<!-- paragraph:6602 para_id:000019CA style:Normal -->
He paused. “The wall itself is unusually thick. Without working from the assumption that a passage existed, they would not have found it.”

<!-- paragraph:6603 para_id:000019CB style:Normal -->
Harvick clapped his hands together and bounced to his feet. “Right! So that was the boring part. Let’s go take a look.”

<!-- paragraph:6604 para_id:000019CC style:Normal -->
He strode past Maya toward the door and leaned in as he went by, lowering his voice. “Sorry. I guess we could’ve just met there. But you know how Niall gets when he summons people to his office.”

<!-- paragraph:6605 para_id:000019CD style:Normal -->
Niall pretended not to hear. Maya followed Harvick out, watching as he led the way down the corridor, navigating confidently by a series of sloppy ink arrows drawn across his hand. They descended deeper into the Citadel, the air cooling as stone closed in around them.

<!-- paragraph:6606 para_id:000019CE style:Normal -->
“This part of the Citadel is old,” Niall said to Maya as they walked. “Much of the aboveground structure has been expanded or rebuilt over the years. But the base of the main spire—and the tunnels beneath it—have been here for as long as anyone can remember.”

<!-- paragraph:6607 para_id:000019CF style:Normal -->
He continued evenly, “The Rezins are a long line of warlords. Some more than others. But we’ve held this Citadel for more generations than any other Clade can claim. Far enough back that the records become sparse.”

<!-- paragraph:6608 para_id:000019D0 style:Normal -->
Harvick glanced around and snorted. “Yeah, it’s spooky down here. Who knows what kind of weird things those old Rezins bricked up and forgot about?”

<!-- paragraph:6609 para_id:000019D1 style:Normal -->
They reached a dark tunnel where two workers stood waiting, shoulders tense. A nondescript section of wall ahead of them had been marked off in charcoal.

<!-- paragraph:6610 para_id:000019D2 style:Normal -->
One of the workers stepped forward and bowed awkwardly, clearly uncertain how to address two High Clade magi at once. “My lords… uh—my lord and… lady?” he said, then hurried on. “We believe there’s a space behind this section of wall.”

<!-- paragraph:6611 para_id:000019D3 style:Normal -->
He gestured at the marked stone.

<!-- paragraph:6612 para_id:000019D4 style:Normal -->
Niall studied it briefly. “Why haven’t you opened it yet?”

<!-- paragraph:6613 para_id:000019D5 style:Normal -->
The man swallowed. He couldn’t quite meet Niall’s eyes, sweat beading on his upper lip. “It’s at least three feet of solid stone,” he said. “And it’s hard stone. It would take days at a minimum. Possibly weeks.”

<!-- paragraph:6614 para_id:000019D6 style:Normal -->
“We can blast through it with magic,” Niall said. “Stand back.”

<!-- paragraph:6615 para_id:000019D7 style:Normal -->
“Wait.” Maya stepped forward. “I don’t think that’s a good idea. We’d need a tremendous amount of force, and this is an enclosed space. We don’t know the structural stability down here.”

<!-- paragraph:6616 para_id:000019D8 style:Normal -->
“We have our Shrouds,” Niall said. “And the workmen can leave.” He paused, then added, “Harvick too.”

<!-- paragraph:6617 para_id:000019D9 style:Normal -->
“If all the rock above collapses on us,” Maya said evenly, “our Shrouds will just keep us from being crushed while we’re buried.”

<!-- paragraph:6618 para_id:000019DA style:Normal -->
She looked at the wall again, thinking. “I don’t remember it that clearly, but when I came down here before, I was able to walk straight through this wall. Or—there wasn’t a wall here at all.”

<!-- paragraph:6619 para_id:000019DB style:Normal -->
She turned to the workers. “Have you identified anything that could be a pressure plate? A switch? Any mechanism that might open it?”

<!-- paragraph:6620 para_id:000019DC style:Normal -->
One of the workers spoke up, still visibly nervous—especially at the idea that a High Clade magus might start tearing the tunnel apart with magic.

<!-- paragraph:6621 para_id:000019DD style:Normal -->
“As far as we can tell,” he said carefully, “it’s a single, solid piece of stone. No joints. No articulation. Nothing that suggests it opens.”

<!-- paragraph:6622 para_id:000019DE style:Normal -->
He hesitated, then added, “It’s almost like there was never a tunnel here.”

<!-- paragraph:6623 para_id:000019DF style:Normal -->
Maya stopped listening. Not deliberately. The voices just slid past her, indistinct, as she turned her attention inward. The tunnel, the wall, the people behind her—all of it faded to background pressure. She closed her eyes and remembered the dream that had led her here.

<!-- paragraph:6624 para_id:000019E0 style:Normal -->
The dream had led her through pathways that were beyond the typical geometry of the world. But when she had followed the memory, the movements had felt natural. Even describing it that way seemed wrong - it was more of a feeling, a way for her body to exist. She let herself recall that feeling. The way the world had felt layered, permissive, full of choices that weren’t choices at all. You didn’t go through it.

<!-- paragraph:6625 para_id:000019E1 style:Normal -->
Maya breathed once and turned. Not to her left, not to her right. She simply hadn’t been facing the correct direction, and now she was. She took a step and placed her foot in the place it belonged.

<!-- paragraph:6626 para_id:000019E2 style:Normal -->
The entire world instantly went dark. Maya’s breath hitched, sharp and immediate, and instinct took over. A small orb of flame bloomed above her hand, light snapping into existence before she’d fully registered the fear. Stone walls. A narrow hallway stretching ahead into shadow.

<!-- paragraph:6627 para_id:000019E3 style:Normal -->
She spun, heart racing, and found stone behind her as well—solid, unmarked. Her breathing came fast as the moment caught up with her, panic flaring before control reasserted itself.

<!-- paragraph:6628 para_id:000019E4 style:Normal -->
I’m on the other side. She didn’t know how she knew. She just did. For a brief, unsettling instant, she wondered if she’d trapped herself—if this was one of those mistakes you only made once. But the panic ebbed as quickly as it had come. Having done it again, there was a residue of understanding left behind. Like muscle memory. Like copying a zan over and over until your hand could etch it without thought.

<!-- paragraph:6629 para_id:000019E5 style:Normal -->
Maya closed her eyes and stepped back. A shriek cut the air. She opened them to see Harvick leaping backward, nearly tripping over himself before he caught his balance. His face flushed red as he forced himself still.

<!-- paragraph:6630 para_id:000019E6 style:Normal -->
“Oh, Creator,” he blurted. “That was terrifying. It’s already creepy down here, and then you were just gone—and then you come back with fire out of nowhere!” He exhaled hard, shaking his head.

<!-- paragraph:6631 para_id:000019E7 style:Normal -->
Niall studied Maya with a controlled intensity that didn’t quite mask the tension underneath. “What happened?” he asked.

<!-- paragraph:6632 para_id:000019E8 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “I can’t really explain it. I just… understand how the world works there.” She searched for words and settled on the least wrong ones. “It’s like the tunnel is a flat drawing. And instead of going through it, I can step over it.”

<!-- paragraph:6633 para_id:000019E9 style:Normal -->
Niall considered that for a moment. “Then show us how to do it.”

<!-- paragraph:6634 para_id:000019EA style:Normal -->
“I don’t think I can,” Maya said. “It would be like trying to explain color to a blind man well enough that he could paint his own portrait.”

<!-- paragraph:6635 para_id:000019EB style:Normal -->
Harvick let out a weak snort. “Well, some of the paintings I’ve seen look like that’s exactly where they came from.” The joke landed without conviction. He still looked rattled.

<!-- paragraph:6636 para_id:000019EC style:Normal -->
Niall’s jaw tightened. “Then we’re back to the plan of blowing a hole through the wall.”

<!-- paragraph:6637 para_id:000019ED style:Normal -->
“That’s not an acceptable risk,” Maya said immediately. She met his gaze. “And it would be my magic we’d need to use anyway.”

<!-- paragraph:6638 para_id:000019EE style:Normal -->
She gestured toward the hidden passage. “This is a security problem. We don’t know who else might be capable of coming through here. And we need answers. So I’m going back. Alone.”

<!-- paragraph:6639 para_id:000019EF style:Normal -->
