“Okay,” Harvick said, “that’s very dramatic, and I appreciate how you’re really leaning into being your own person now, but I do have a quick question.”

<!-- paragraph:6640 para_id:000019F0 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t look at him.

<!-- paragraph:6641 para_id:000019F1 style:Normal -->
“So,” Harvick continued, “you said there was stuff back there. Right?”

<!-- paragraph:6642 para_id:000019F2 style:Normal -->
Maya exhaled. “Harvick. I know you enjoy pretending to be stupid, but for all of our sakes, could you either phrase your question more intelligently—or just say what you’re actually thinking?”

<!-- paragraph:6643 para_id:000019F3 style:Normal -->
He held up a finger. “I have a very important point. Stuff requires people to build that stuff.” He gestured toward the two workers hovering nervously nearby. “You had these guys looking around for weeks because you had other things to do. There’s no way that someone with enough magical ability to jump through walls is the same person who built whatever’s back there.”

<!-- paragraph:6644 para_id:000019F4 style:Normal -->
Niall frowned slightly. “It could be connected elsewhere. But we don’t have time to search the entire Citadel.”

<!-- paragraph:6645 para_id:000019F5 style:Normal -->
“No,” Maya said slowly. “Harvick’s right.”

<!-- paragraph:6646 para_id:000019F6 style:Normal -->
She looked back at the wall. “There is a way through here. Even if I’m the only one who can use it right now, there has to be a way to make it accessible—at least temporarily.”

<!-- paragraph:6647 para_id:000019F7 style:Normal -->
“That’s a stretch,” Niall said.

<!-- paragraph:6648 para_id:000019F8 style:Normal -->
Maya had already closed her eyes and stopped listening again - and reached for the thing she hadn’t realized she’d been using before. She saw it in her mind’s eye. A strand of the Skein she’d followed without knowing it, the one that had let her step through the wall.

<!-- paragraph:6649 para_id:000019F9 style:Normal -->
It wasn’t aligned. That wasn’t quite the right word. There was no angle, no direction, not here, in the mind-space outside of reality. But the thread was skewed relative to the way the world flowed.

<!-- paragraph:6650 para_id:000019FA style:Normal -->
She touched it with her mind. It moved. Her breath caught. I can change this.

<!-- paragraph:6651 para_id:000019FB style:Normal -->
Maya grabbed the thread and pulled—not harder, but truer—yanking it into the orientation that felt correct. For an instant, everything fell away. She was floating in an infinite ocean of dark, spreading outward in all directions—not just through space, but through time, through possibility.  The sensation was vast and terrifying and intimate all at once, and it tore a sharp breath from her chest as she wrenched herself back into her body.

<!-- paragraph:6652 para_id:000019FC style:Normal -->
When her eyes opened, the wall was gone. A tunnel stood in its place. She turned, disoriented, to find Harvick, Niall, and the two workmen staring at her. They were all standing in slightly different places than she remembered. Not far—just enough to be wrong.

<!-- paragraph:6653 para_id:000019FD style:Normal -->
Harvick broke first. “Wow,” he said. “I did not like that.”

<!-- paragraph:6654 para_id:000019FE style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression was controlled, but his eyes were sharp. “So we all felt it.”

<!-- paragraph:6655 para_id:000019FF style:Normal -->
He turned to Maya. “This is not the kind of thing you should be playing with on a whim.”

<!-- paragraph:6656 para_id:00001A00 style:Normal -->
“I used my powers to solve a problem,” Maya said. Her voice came out harder than she meant it to. “That’s what you taught me to do. If you don’t like that now, it’s way too late.”

<!-- paragraph:6657 para_id:00001A01 style:Normal -->
Niall held her gaze for a moment without responding.

<!-- paragraph:6658 para_id:00001A02 style:Normal -->
Then he turned to the workmen. “You may go.”

<!-- paragraph:6659 para_id:00001A03 style:Normal -->
They didn’t need to be told twice. Both of them hurried away, almost breaking into a run as soon as they were clear.

<!-- paragraph:6660 para_id:00001A04 style:Normal -->
Niall stepped past Maya and into the tunnel.

<!-- paragraph:6661 para_id:00001A05 style:Normal -->
Harvick followed, falling in beside her just long enough to murmur, “I’ll make sure they get some extra pay.”

<!-- paragraph:6662 para_id:00001A06 style:Normal -->
Maya turned and went after them.

<!-- paragraph:6663 para_id:00001A07 style:Normal -->
As they walked deeper into the passage, she heard Harvick muttering under his breath, half to himself. “Though I don’t think there are enough haloes or booze in the world to drown out that darkness.”

<!-- paragraph:6664 para_id:00001A08 style:Normal -->
They descended deeper into the tunnel, the passage stretching on longer than Maya remembered. Something about the depth itself encouraged quiet, as if the stone absorbed sound and left words feeling intrusive. Even Harvick seemed content to let the silence hold.

<!-- paragraph:6665 para_id:00001A09 style:Normal -->
When they finally reached the alcove where Maya had found the Codex, it appeared unchanged. The single block of stone still sat slightly ajar, revealing the narrow space behind it, just as it had before. Maya raised light and examined the area carefully, running her attention across every surface, every shadowed edge. There were no new markings. No hidden mechanisms. No second layer waiting to be uncovered.

<!-- paragraph:6666 para_id:00001A0A style:Normal -->
“This is where it was,” she said. “When I was here last, it felt like there was… a presence. Like someone wanted me to have the Codex.” She hesitated, then added more quietly, “I thought it was my mother.”

<!-- paragraph:6667 para_id:00001A0B style:Normal -->
Niall nodded slowly, his face ashen. “Now I understand,” he said. “It must have been the one who gave her the Codex in the first place.”

<!-- paragraph:6668 para_id:00001A0C style:Normal -->
Maya straightened and continued forward in step with Niall, their footsteps muted against the ancient stone. After a few paces, Maya realized something was wrong. She slowed, then stopped entirely.

<!-- paragraph:6669 para_id:00001A0D style:Normal -->
Harvick wasn’t following.

<!-- paragraph:6670 para_id:00001A0E style:Normal -->
She turned. He stood a short distance back, fists clenched so tightly his knuckles had gone white, his posture rigid as if he were holding himself in place through sheer force of will.

<!-- paragraph:6671 para_id:00001A0F style:Normal -->
“So there’s some way that…” he said, then stopped, swallowing hard. “Somehow… he might still be alive?”

<!-- paragraph:6672 para_id:00001A10 style:Normal -->
Niall turned back as well. Maya was surprised to find that his expression was softer than she’d expected.

<!-- paragraph:6673 para_id:00001A11 style:Normal -->
“No,” Niall said. “You and I both saw him die.”

<!-- paragraph:6674 para_id:00001A12 style:Normal -->
Harvick shook his head slightly. “I didn’t want to do it,” he said. “And I never got to tell him… that I was sorry.”

<!-- paragraph:6675 para_id:00001A13 style:Normal -->
Niall didn’t answer immediately. When he did, his voice was steady.

<!-- paragraph:6676 para_id:00001A14 style:Normal -->
“Harvick, he had been driven to madness by his studies. I killed him. Not you.” He met Harvick’s eyes without flinching. “And I don’t regret it.”

<!-- paragraph:6677 para_id:00001A15 style:Normal -->
“Then what are you talking about?” Harvick said. “What did she feel?”

<!-- paragraph:6678 para_id:00001A16 style:Normal -->
Maya heard it then—the off-kilter note of hope in his voice, fragile and unguarded.

<!-- paragraph:6679 para_id:00001A17 style:Normal -->
Niall answered. “If there is anything left, it isn’t him. It would be an echo. A residue. A ghost made of the worst parts of his aura. I was a fool to accept that the Codex he gave us was meant only for us—to preserve our memories, nothing more. He had a plan for them from the beginning.”

<!-- paragraph:6680 para_id:00001A18 style:Normal -->
Niall glanced at Maya briefly. “And we’re down here to find out what that plan was. And why he would have wanted you to find what you did.”

<!-- paragraph:6681 para_id:00001A19 style:Normal -->
Niall walked on, then spoke again over his shoulder. His voice softened just enough that Maya noticed. “Harvick—if I find anything else his aura could cling to, I’m going to destroy it. If you don’t want to see that, you should turn back now.”

<!-- paragraph:6682 para_id:00001A1A style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t answer right away. He swallowed, scrubbed at his face with the back of his sleeve, and drew a steadying breath.

<!-- paragraph:6683 para_id:00001A1B style:Normal -->
“No,” he said finally. “I… I want to see what there is.”

<!-- paragraph:6684 para_id:00001A1C style:Normal -->
He followed after them, the tunnel closing in once more as they went deeper.  The stone here was older than the upper levels, worked smooth by hands that hadn’t cared about ornament or comfort, only endurance. Even Harvick stopped talking as they went, the quiet pressing in with the sense that this place had never been meant for frequent visitors.

<!-- paragraph:6685 para_id:00001A1D style:Normal -->
When the passage finally opened out, it did so without ceremony. The chamber beyond was broad but low, its ceiling supported by shallow arches carved directly from the rock. Zanwork was embedded everywhere—running along the walls, branching across the floor, disappearing into the ceiling in dense, overlapping lattices. The patterns were unfamiliar, layered beyond easy parsing. Some strokes still faintly flickered, giving the whole room an uneven, uneasy illumination.

<!-- paragraph:6686 para_id:00001A1E style:Normal -->
Harvick slowed, eyes scanning. “These are still active,” he said quietly.

<!-- paragraph:6687 para_id:00001A1F style:Normal -->
Maya felt it too. The zan here weren’t modular or symbolic in the way modern systems were. They were structural, intertwined so deeply with the stone that it was hard to tell where architecture ended and magic began. The logic reminded her of Zirene’s work—older, heavier, less concerned with scalability than with persistence. Whatever powered this place had been designed to last.

<!-- paragraph:6688 para_id:00001A20 style:Normal -->
They spread out through the chamber, careful without quite knowing what they were being careful of. The zan lattices pulled the eye in too many directions at once, and the uneven light made depth hard to judge. Maya moved more slowly than the others, a faint pressure building behind her eyes as the space began to feel familiar in a way she didn’t like.

<!-- paragraph:6689 para_id:00001A21 style:Normal -->
Then she felt it. The same low pull she’d felt when she found the Codex, a suggestion at the back of her mind. She followed it without fully deciding to, her steps quiet as she crossed toward the furthest corner of the room.

<!-- paragraph:6690 para_id:00001A22 style:Normal -->
The basin sat slightly off-center, sunk into the stone as if it had always belonged there. Its surface was perfectly dark. Zan rings encircled it, their light steady but muted, failing to penetrate whatever filled the bowl.

<!-- paragraph:6691 para_id:00001A23 style:Normal -->
Maya lifted her hand, the small flame she held brightening reflexively as she leaned closer. The light didn’t reflect. It vanished at the surface, swallowed whole, as though the darkness were inverted—less an absence than a substance that consumed illumination.

<!-- paragraph:6692 para_id:00001A24 style:Normal -->
Her breath slowed. It was almost like—Ink.

<!-- paragraph:6693 para_id:00001A25 style:Normal -->
She leaned in further, drawn despite herself, and for a fleeting moment thought she saw her own reflection forming in the surface. The thought barely had time to settle before unease sharpened into recognition.

<!-- paragraph:6694 para_id:00001A26 style:Normal -->
That wasn’t her face.

<!-- paragraph:6695 para_id:00001A27 style:Normal -->
Maya recoiled just as the surface broke.

<!-- paragraph:6696 para_id:00001A28 style:Normal -->
Ink surged upward, gathering itself with deliberate cohesion, rising into shape as if following instructions written long ago. Limbs resolved first, then shoulders, then a head whose features were close enough to human to be deeply wrong. The figure stepped free of the basin, dark liquid trailing back into the pool as it straightened, its attention already fixed on the room.

<!-- paragraph:6697 para_id:00001A29 style:Normal -->
Maya jumped back on instinct as the simulacrum emerged, the chamber’s flickering light catching on its surface without ever quite touching it.

<!-- paragraph:6698 para_id:00001A2A style:Normal -->
Lightning cracked through the chamber as Niall struck without hesitation, the bolt tearing straight through the simulacrum’s torso and blasting a jagged hole through its chest. Ink splattered outward, then flowed back in on itself, sealing the damage as if it had never been there.

<!-- paragraph:6699 para_id:00001A2B style:Normal -->
The figure looked down at the closing wound, then back up at them.

<!-- paragraph:6700 para_id:00001A2C style:Normal -->
“That,” it said evenly, “is a very rude way to greet your father.”

<!-- paragraph:6701 para_id:00001A2D style:Normal -->
The lightning struck again. Maya’s vision seared white as Niall drove the strike harder this time, the bolt leaping from his outstretched hand and refusing to dissipate. It split into branching threads that clawed across the corner of the chamber, tearing into stone, zan lattices, anything they touched. The air screamed. Heat slammed outward in waves.

<!-- paragraph:6702 para_id:00001A2E style:Normal -->
Maya flared her Shroud and staggered back, raising an arm to shield her eyes as the light became unbearable. Niall didn’t stop. The lightning kept pouring out of him in wild, inefficient arcs, each one less controlled than the last, as if whatever restraint he usually exercised had been stripped away by sheer fury.

<!-- paragraph:6703 para_id:00001A2F style:Normal -->
She shouted his name, but the sound vanished into the crackle and roar. Then, abruptly, the arcs faltered.

<!-- paragraph:6704 para_id:00001A30 style:Normal -->
The lightning thinned, broke apart, and winked out, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. The smell hit her next—ozone and scorched stone, the sharp, metallic tang that always lingered after a lightning strike. The air itself felt wrong, overworked, trembling as if it hadn’t decided yet whether to collapse or burn.

<!-- paragraph:6705 para_id:00001A31 style:Normal -->
Maya lowered her arm slowly, blinking spots from her vision. The chamber was dim again, lit only by the uneven flicker of the ancient zanwork. She forced herself to focus and shaped a ball of flame in her palm, careful and deliberate this time, drawing heat from the superheated air and constraining it within the inverted surface. Light returned in a muted glow.

<!-- paragraph:6706 para_id:00001A32 style:Normal -->
She turned toward Niall. He was on one knee, one hand braced against the stone, the other hanging uselessly at his side. His shoulders rose and fell as he dragged in breath, face tight with exertion and rage.

<!-- paragraph:6707 para_id:00001A33 style:Normal -->
Maya took a step toward him—and stopped. Something was wrong. She turned.

<!-- paragraph:6708 para_id:00001A34 style:Normal -->
An ink-dark face met her gaze from inches away.

<!-- paragraph:6709 para_id:00001A35 style:Normal -->
The shock kicked her mind into overdrive as she scrambled back, the moment stretching thin and sharp while she took inventory of everything at once.

<!-- paragraph:6710 para_id:00001A36 style:Normal -->
Her Shroud came first, reflex flaring it without conscious effort. At the same time, she tightened the boundary conditions on the sphere of flame in her hand, compressing its output and holding the excess energy in reserve, ready to release it instantly if she needed to.

<!-- paragraph:6711 para_id:00001A37 style:Normal -->
A split moment later she analyzed the thing standing beside her. The ink-dark figure wore the shape of a man, but its posture was neutral—balanced, unthreatening. There was no forward lean, no tension in its limbs, no telltale distortion in the air that would suggest channels being carved for an attack.

<!-- paragraph:6712 para_id:00001A38 style:Normal -->
Her awareness widened again, sweeping the room. The corner where Niall’s lightning had struck was still smoking, stone cracked and zän mechanisms shattered beyond repair. And yet the simulacrum stood untouched, its form uninterrupted, as if the violence had passed through a different layer of reality entirely.

<!-- paragraph:6713 para_id:00001A39 style:Normal -->
Maya steadied her breathing, keeping her Shroud tight and the flame poised as she looked directly at the figure.

<!-- paragraph:6714 para_id:00001A3A style:Normal -->
“You’re not Elric,” she said carefully. “You’re what’s left of him. A fragment. A remnant.”

<!-- paragraph:6715 para_id:00001A3B style:Normal -->
Maya could have sworn the figure smiled, though it was hard to be certain. Its surface distorted the light unevenly, drinking it in rather than reflecting it, obscuring subtle changes in its shape. When it spoke, its voice carried a faint, intimate warmth that made her skin crawl.

<!-- paragraph:6716 para_id:00001A3C style:Normal -->
“Very good, granddaughter,” it said. “And I will say—it’s so much nicer to see you somewhere other than a dream. Or a memory.”

<!-- paragraph:6717 para_id:00001A3D style:Normal -->
Lightning tore through its face again. The bolt struck cleanly, blasting a hole straight through the simulacrum’s head. Ink scattered outward, then flowed back into place almost immediately, features reforming without haste or resistance. Niall’s arm was extended, breath coming in harsh pulls as the last of the charge bled out of him.

<!-- paragraph:6718 para_id:00001A3E style:Normal -->
“Maya, get back!” He yelled with a hoarse voice. “Don’t let him near you!”

<!-- paragraph:6719 para_id:00001A3F style:Normal -->
The simulacrum turned its attention to Niall and lifted a hand, pointing. “I saw a righteous man,” it said calmly, “who died of his righteousness.”

<!-- paragraph:6720 para_id:00001A40 style:Normal -->
Its finger shifted, indicating itself. “And an evil man who prolonged his life through evil.”

<!-- paragraph:6721 para_id:00001A41 style:Normal -->
It spread its arms wide, the motion slow and deliberate. “There is no good that cannot cause ill. There is no straight path the righteous can walk. When I listen to the threads, all I hear is corruption.”

<!-- paragraph:6722 para_id:00001A42 style:Normal -->
Maya heard Harvick shout behind her, raw and angry. “What are you saying? Why do you always have to talk in riddles?”

<!-- paragraph:6723 para_id:00001A43 style:Normal -->
He was already moving, striding past her toward the figure.

<!-- paragraph:6724 para_id:00001A44 style:Normal -->
The simulacrum lowered its arms. “Ah, Harvick,” it said. “One of the few things I genuinely regret about being dead is losing your counsel.” It tilted its head slightly. “Would you do me a favor? Please kill Niall. It’s something I’m not entirely sure I finished last time.”

<!-- paragraph:6725 para_id:00001A45 style:Normal -->
Harvick swung without thinking, an open-handed strike aimed at the figure’s face. His hand passed through with a wet splash of Ink, leaving his arm slick and trembling.

<!-- paragraph:6726 para_id:00001A46 style:Normal -->
“Why do you have to be like this?” he demanded. “What was wrong with how things were before?”

<!-- paragraph:6727 para_id:00001A47 style:Normal -->
The simulacrum didn’t react. “So many questions,” it said, almost fondly. “You know, I used to love them. I believed that if I climbed high enough—if I reached the apex of knowledge—there would be answers waiting.”

<!-- paragraph:6728 para_id:00001A48 style:Normal -->
Its gaze drifted, unfocused, as if looking past the chamber entirely.

<!-- paragraph:6729 para_id:00001A49 style:Normal -->
“But there was nothing. There is no answer to the evils of this world, because we live in an evil world.” Its voice lowered. “An evil reality. You may curse me to Sheol, but the words ring hollow coming from one who already exists there.”

<!-- paragraph:6730 para_id:00001A4A style:Normal -->
“Then why stay here at all?” Maya asked. “If existence is as terrible as you claim, why do any of this?” She gestured at the chamber—the mechanisms, the Ink, the careful preservation of something that should have been allowed to end.

<!-- paragraph:6731 para_id:00001A4B style:Normal -->
The simulacrum regarded her for a moment. “This,” it said, “was an accident. I had hoped you would come sooner to destroy me.”

<!-- paragraph:6732 para_id:00001A4C style:Normal -->
It extended one darkened hand toward the basin it had emerged from. “The mathematics were… difficult. I may have erred by adding too much of a safety factor in the quantity of Ink I prepared.”

<!-- paragraph:6733 para_id:00001A4D style:Normal -->
Maya raised her hand and widened the boundary conditions on her sphere. Light spilled outward, harsh and deliberate, flooding the corner of the chamber. Cracks became visible in the basin, hairline fractures running through the stone as Ink seeped out and began to spread across the floor in slow, glossy tendrils.

<!-- paragraph:6734 para_id:00001A4E style:Normal -->
The simulacrum watched this with something like relief. It drew its hands together loosely. “You’ve done me two favors,” it said. “Once the Ink dissipates sufficiently, I will finally be destroyed. And you’ve brought together everything I needed—every piece, even the broken ones. Even the backups.”

<!-- paragraph:6735 para_id:00001A4F style:Normal -->
It looked back at her. “So. I will answer one question. You should ask it quickly.”

<!-- paragraph:6736 para_id:00001A50 style:Normal -->
Maya saw Harvick draw breath beside her and didn’t hesitate.

<!-- paragraph:6737 para_id:00001A51 style:Normal -->
“How do we fix reality?” she asked.

<!-- paragraph:6738 para_id:00001A52 style:Normal -->
The simulacrum’s form was already losing cohesion, its edges softening as the Ink spread across the stone like a dark pool of blood. When it spoke again, its voice wavered slightly, stretched thin.

<!-- paragraph:6739 para_id:00001A53 style:Normal -->
“A question as close to perfect as this reality permits,” it said. “Exactly what I would expect from my granddaughter.”

<!-- paragraph:6740 para_id:00001A54 style:Normal -->
It tilted its head as the lower half of its body began to dissolve. “Don’t worry. I have already set in motion the scheme that will correct what is wrong. The blood in the water that leads to true perfection.”

<!-- paragraph:6741 para_id:00001A55 style:Normal -->
The voice shifted again as the figure continued to melt, words slurring as if pulled from different moments at once. “Or the arrow of fate. And the third coffin… though I never found what I needed for that plan - in myself, or in the world…”

<!-- paragraph:6742 para_id:00001A56 style:Normal -->
The simulacrum collapsed in on itself, Ink losing its shape entirely.

<!-- paragraph:6743 para_id:00001A57 style:Normal -->
Then it was gone.

<!-- paragraph:6744 para_id:00001A58 style:Normal -->
Harvick dropped to his knees in the spreading Ink, hands slack at his sides as he stared down at the dark sheen coating the stone. For a moment he didn’t seem to register where he was, or that the simulacrum was gone at all.

<!-- paragraph:6745 para_id:00001A59 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped closer and rested a hand on his shoulder. He didn’t pull away.

<!-- paragraph:6746 para_id:00001A5A style:Normal -->
“Your question was probably better than mine,” Harvick said quietly. “But I still would have liked to ask it.”

<!-- paragraph:6747 para_id:00001A5B style:Normal -->
They stayed there a moment longer, letting the room settle around them, before turning back to the work at hand. Together they searched the chamber methodically, following every line that suggested continuation rather than termination.

<!-- paragraph:6748 para_id:00001A5C style:Normal -->
It was Harvick who found the exit. A narrow passage opened at the far end of the chamber, its mouth half-concealed by shadow, sloping away into darkness. The tunnel beyond was too long to gauge at a glance, its destination unclear.

<!-- paragraph:6749 para_id:00001A5D style:Normal -->
They didn’t linger over the decision. Guards would have to be stationed here and at the tunnel’s mouth, rotated frequently. Others would be sent to explore further, carefully and in stages. Collapsing it wasn’t an option—not with the Citadel built above them and too many unknowns buried in the stone.

<!-- paragraph:6750 para_id:00001A5E style:Normal -->
When they finally turned back, the shape of it had settled into something undeniable.

<!-- paragraph:6751 para_id:00001A5F style:Normal -->
“There’s a link here,” Maya said. “Between Elric and the assassin.”

<!-- paragraph:6752 para_id:00001A60 style:Normal -->
She looked down at the dark liquid still seeping slowly across the floor. “One drawn in Ink.”

<!-- paragraph:6753 para_id:00001A61 style:Normal -->
No one argued.

<!-- paragraph:6756 para_id:00001A64 style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 33

<!-- paragraph:6757 para_id:00001A65 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 33

<!-- paragraph:6759 para_id:00001A67 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6760 para_id:00001A68 style:Normal -->
Something about the world felt wrong as Davian drifted back toward consciousness. Not like pain or danger—subtler than that. As he oriented himself, the sensation refused to resolve into anything specific, only a certainty that the shape of things around him had shifted. It felt as though he were perceiving the room through new senses layered awkwardly over old ones, or perhaps through familiar senses that had learned to take in more than they used to.

<!-- paragraph:6761 para_id:00001A69 style:Normal -->
The first thing that grabbed his attention was impact. Footsteps, descending, registered in his awareness a heartbeat before sound reached his ears. A moment later came the slap of leather boots against stone, sharp and rhythmic. Davian frowned and pushed himself upright, the motion sending a brief wave of dizziness through him as the stone floor, the unnaturally stable zanlight, and the iron bars of his cell blurred together before settling again.

<!-- paragraph:6762 para_id:00001A6A style:Normal -->
He swung his legs off the bed just as the footsteps drew closer. Each step landed twice—once in his ears, once somewhere deeper—echoing through him like the steady thump of a heart. The sensation was overwhelming enough that he closed his eyes, breathing through it until the rhythm slowed.

<!-- paragraph:6763 para_id:00001A6B style:Normal -->
Then stopped. The sudden absence jolted him. A heartbeat stopped, his mind supplied automatically. Death. No—he forced himself back. Don’t be morbid. Someone stopped walking. He opened his eyes and the shock snapped him fully awake.

<!-- paragraph:6764 para_id:00001A6C style:Normal -->
Davian lurched to his feet and grabbed the bars, pressing his face between them as if proximity alone could confirm what he was seeing. “But you were dead,” he said, the words tumbling out raw and disbelieving.

<!-- paragraph:6765 para_id:00001A6D style:Normal -->
Harvick blinked at him. Then his expression shifted, thoughtful in a way that immediately felt dangerous.

<!-- paragraph:6766 para_id:00001A6E style:Normal -->
“Ohhh,” he said slowly. “Right. Yeah.” His eyes widened theatrically. “That’s because I’m a ghost now.” Davian froze, mind racing.

<!-- paragraph:6767 para_id:00001A6F style:Normal -->
Harvick leaned forward, lowering his voice. “Very scary. I haunt people. Mostly I just show up at inconvenient moments and make them feel bad about their life choices.”

<!-- paragraph:6768 para_id:00001A70 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at him, his mind struggling to parse what this apparition was saying. “How…” He stopped, unable to form a coherent question.

<!-- paragraph:6769 para_id:00001A71 style:Normal -->
Harvick watched Davian’s face for a second, then relented. “Okay, no. I’m not actually a ghost.”

<!-- paragraph:6770 para_id:00001A72 style:Normal -->
He straightened and grinned, sheepish again. “Sorry. At this point I’ve explained what happened so many times I kind of forgot there might still be people who didn’t know.” He waved a hand vaguely. “Lightning. Heart stuff. Now I’m alive again. Or—” he tilted his head, considering, “—un-deaded, I guess.”

<!-- paragraph:6771 para_id:00001A73 style:Normal -->
Davian’s vision swam. His face felt wet.

<!-- paragraph:6772 para_id:00001A74 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Harvick said quickly, stepping closer. “Don’t cry. It’s not that bad of an explanation.”

<!-- paragraph:6773 para_id:00001A75 style:Normal -->
Davian’s voice came out rougher than he expected. “I thought I’d never get to tell you I was sorry. That you died thinking it was all your fault.”

<!-- paragraph:6774 para_id:00001A76 style:Normal -->
His head dipped forward, shoulders slumping. Harvick reached through the bars and awkwardly patted the top of his head. “Oh, don’t worry about that. Most of what I said back then was just… problem solving.” He winced. “I mean, yes, I definitely said some poorly thought-out things while we were fighting, but I’m not going to beat myself up over that since you were actively trying to kill me at the time. And I am absolutely sure the part where you punched me so hard I sort of died was your fault.”

<!-- paragraph:6775 para_id:00001A77 style:Normal -->
Davian huffed out something that might have been a laugh and might not. Then the weight came back in.

<!-- paragraph:6776 para_id:00001A78 style:Normal -->
“What am I supposed to do?” he asked. “How do I make up for something like that? Or for everything else?”

<!-- paragraph:6777 para_id:00001A79 style:Normal -->
Harvick drew his hand back and rubbed at the back of his neck. “Oh, I don’t want to give you the impression I have any idea how to answer that.”

<!-- paragraph:6778 para_id:00001A7A style:Normal -->
“But everyone wants something from me,” Davian said. “Everyone wants to use me. Just tell me—what do I need to do?”

<!-- paragraph:6779 para_id:00001A7B style:Normal -->
Harvick sighed and leaned in closer, lowering his voice. “Okay. First of all, you absolutely cannot let Niall or Maya hear this part, because I told them I’d come down here first to smooth things over and get you to cooperate. Which, by the way, is apparently necessary because Maya blasted you through a wall when you tried to run off after your parents.”

<!-- paragraph:6780 para_id:00001A7C style:Normal -->
He paused, then continued quietly. “Second. You’re not supposed to just do what I tell you. That’s kind of the point. The Creator made you for a reason. Maybe that reason is robbing us and sitting in a cell, but I don’t think that’s the whole story.”

<!-- paragraph:6781 para_id:00001A7D style:Normal -->
Davian shook his head. “I don’t trust myself to figure out what that reason is. Or if it’s even real. I hate the High Clades—I despise them. And then I meet them, and they’re just… people. Children. People trying to get by, same as me.”

<!-- paragraph:6782 para_id:00001A7E style:Normal -->
His voice tightened. “I killed to get in here. I killed before that. What does any of it mean if it was all built on the wrong way of thinking?”

<!-- paragraph:6783 para_id:00001A7F style:Normal -->
Harvick shrugged helplessly. “I don’t have a good answer for that. Probably most of the killing in this world gets done for just as little reason.”

<!-- paragraph:6784 para_id:00001A80 style:Normal -->
“There’s no forgiveness for what I did,” Davian said. “I’m broken. Rotten.”

<!-- paragraph:6785 para_id:00001A81 style:Normal -->
Harvick rolled his eyes. “Okay, sure. If you really want to define yourself entirely by what you’ve already done instead of what you might still do.”

<!-- paragraph:6786 para_id:00001A82 style:Normal -->
Davian looked up, heat flaring. “So you won’t tell me what to do, and you won’t even give me something real? Just empty platitudes?”

<!-- paragraph:6787 para_id:00001A83 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s expression hardened. He stepped closer to the bars, voice rising despite himself. “You think I don’t feel any of this? That you’re so unique and special no one else could understand what you’re going through? That there’s some secret you’ll crack and then life will be easy and everything will go your way?”

<!-- paragraph:6788 para_id:00001A84 style:Normal -->
He shook his head sharply. “There is no secret. Everything is hard.”

<!-- paragraph:6789 para_id:00001A85 style:Normal -->
The anger drained out of Davian, leaving him hollow. “It’s not fair,” he said quietly. “I have all these things inside me—senses, urges, layers pulling in different directions. It feels like they’re going to tear me apart before I even understand what they’re for. I can’t keep doing this.”

<!-- paragraph:6790 para_id:00001A86 style:Normal -->
Harvick didn’t answer right away. When he did, his voice was steadier. “What other choice is there for people like us? Who do you think is going to come along and make us whole? You either get up or you stay in the gutter. But that’s your choice. Not mine or anyone else’s.”

<!-- paragraph:6791 para_id:00001A87 style:Normal -->
He went distant for a moment. “Do you want me to tell you it can’t be fixed? That none of this can be fixed?” He shook his head slowly. “Someone told me that. I still don’t understand why anyone would want to believe that.”

<!-- paragraph:6792 para_id:00001A88 style:Normal -->
Davian felt the footsteps again before he heard them, the impact of boots descending stone stairs. His head lifted automatically. Harvick noticed a beat after him. He straightened, expression shifting, and leaned in close to the bars again. “Okay,” he whispered quickly, “do me a favor and just pretend I convinced you to cooperate.”

<!-- paragraph:6793 para_id:00001A89 style:Normal -->
“What—” Davian started.

<!-- paragraph:6794 para_id:00001A8A style:Normal -->
Harvick cut him off with a sharp shushing sound and turned away from the cell, his voice rising into something forced and casual. “Yeah, uh, so it’s really great that you forgive Maya for that time she knocked you out.”

<!-- paragraph:6795 para_id:00001A8B style:Normal -->
Niall and Maya stepped into view at the bottom of the stairs. Niall came first, white hair stark against the dark stone, his long military coat hanging straight and severe around him. His expression was closed and thunderous, the kind of composure that didn’t invite conversation so much as compliance.

<!-- paragraph:6796 para_id:00001A8C style:Normal -->
Maya followed just behind him. Davian’s breath caught despite himself. Brown hair, pulled back neatly, face lit by the cold zanlight in a way that made her seem unreal for a moment. He was so focused on the movement of her mouth—on the remembered warmth of it—that he almost missed what she was saying.

<!-- paragraph:6797 para_id:00001A8D style:Normal -->
“…don’t need him to forgive me,” she finished evenly. “We need him to give us information.” Her eyes met his through the bars, sharp and unreadable, and the space between them felt suddenly much smaller than the room allowed.

<!-- paragraph:6798 para_id:00001A8E style:Normal -->
Niall stepped forward, placing himself squarely between Maya and the bars. He looked down at Davian with a cold, assessing stare, and something in that gaze was uncomfortably familiar. The darkness behind it wasn’t just authority or judgment—it carried the same depth Davian felt when the Ink Boon stirred, the same sense of a vast, lightless pressure that promised power and erasure in equal measure.

<!-- paragraph:6799 para_id:00001A8F style:Normal -->
The Ink answered, shifting at the back of his mind, waking with a slow, predatory awareness, and for a moment it felt bottomless—an infinite well opening beneath his thoughts. A dark thread unfurled in front of him, stretching forward with terrible clarity, and along it came a whisper that didn’t need words.

<!-- paragraph:6800 para_id:00001A90 style:Normal -->
Kill Niall.

<!-- paragraph:6801 para_id:00001A91 style:Normal -->
Davian’s hands tightened on the bars, fingers digging into the cold metal as if they belonged to someone else. His breath went shallow. He squeezed his eyes shut, fighting the pull with everything he had.

<!-- paragraph:6802 para_id:00001A92 style:Normal -->
It’s my choice, he told himself. I get to pick. You don’t tell me what to do. The words slipped out under his breath before he realized he was speaking.

<!-- paragraph:6803 para_id:00001A93 style:Normal -->
He heard Niall’s voice, edged with irritation and something sharper underneath. “How are we supposed to get information from him? He’s barely sane. Are you certain the healers examined him properly?”

<!-- paragraph:6804 para_id:00001A94 style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes snapped open. Niall was still staring at him—but so was Maya. Her expression softened for a moment before snapping back to cool appraisal when she noticed Davian looking at her. Heat rushed to his face, embarrassment and shame flaring all at once, and the dark thread was burned away by the sudden intensity of it. The decision came to him all at once, sharp and undeniable. If he didn’t take the moment now, it would be gone.

<!-- paragraph:6805 para_id:00001A95 style:Normal -->
“The Harbingers did something to me,” Davian said quickly. “With Ink. It tries to tell me what to do.” His fingers flexed against the bars as he forced the words out. “I don’t want to do what it says. I want to help.”

<!-- paragraph:6806 para_id:00001A96 style:Normal -->
Maya stepped forward. “Fine,” she said. “Then help. Tell us what you know—about the Ink, about the Harbingers.”

<!-- paragraph:6807 para_id:00001A97 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed and made himself meet her eyes. He focused on keeping his breathing steady. “No. I mean I want to help.” He hesitated, then pushed on. “You saw it. I have a Shroud. I have magic. My mother says she escaped from Archeon. I want to fight with you.”

<!-- paragraph:6808 para_id:00001A98 style:Normal -->
His voice tightened. “Train me. Let me stand with you. And I’ll give you everything I know.”

<!-- paragraph:6809 para_id:00001A99 style:Normal -->
Niall’s expression didn’t change. “First,” he said calmly, “you are in no position to bargain. Second, young men full of rage and marginal magical talent are not in short supply. If you want consideration, you’ll need to offer something more valuable.”

<!-- paragraph:6810 para_id:00001A9A style:Normal -->
Davian forced himself to look away from Maya and toward Niall, his thoughts racing. “I have what the Ink tells me,” he said. “In my head. That has to be useful - for figuring out what it wants, how to stop it. And I’ve seen the Harbingers. Archeon sent eight magi to bring me in—they want me for a reason.”

<!-- paragraph:6811 para_id:00001A9B style:Normal -->
“All I hear,” Niall replied, “is that you suffer from violent, distracting hallucinations, possess limited knowledge of a non–High Clade organization, and have attracted Archeon’s interest as a destabilizing factor.”

<!-- paragraph:6812 para_id:00001A9C style:Normal -->
Lightning flickered between his fingers. “Most of that could be resolved immediately.”

<!-- paragraph:6813 para_id:00001A9D style:Normal -->
Davian leaned forward, heart pounding—not afraid of dying, but terrified of being dismissed, of being left here with no chance to make any of this mean something. “They may not be High Clade,” he said, “but they gave me the power to steal the Heart. To beat you in a fight—even if it was only for a moment. You can’t afford to lose the only lead you have on them.”

<!-- paragraph:6814 para_id:00001A9E style:Normal -->
Harvick laughed, sharp and brief. Niall turned on him with a searing look.

<!-- paragraph:6815 para_id:00001A9F style:Normal -->
“Well,” Harvick said, raising his hands slightly, “he’s not wrong.”

<!-- paragraph:6816 para_id:00001AA0 style:Normal -->
Niall’s jaw tightened. “Perhaps I won’t kill him,” he said. “But there is no world in which we trust him. And I will not train someone who will only become more dangerous—and harder to kill—when he inevitably betrays us again.”

<!-- paragraph:6817 para_id:00001AA1 style:Normal -->
“I vouch for him,” Harvick said immediately.

<!-- paragraph:6818 para_id:00001AA2 style:Normal -->
Maya cut in before Niall could respond. “Harvick,” she said, “you should know that isn’t enough, for a very large number of reasons.”

<!-- paragraph:6819 para_id:00001AA3 style:Normal -->
She paused, eyes shifting briefly—already thinking ahead.

<!-- paragraph:6820 para_id:00001AA4 style:Normal -->
“But,” she continued, “I think this is a problem that could be mitigated by our mutual friend.”

<!-- paragraph:6821 para_id:00001AA5 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6822 para_id:00001AA6 style:Normal -->
Davian wasn’t sure where they were. They were outside the citadel, but Niall and Maya and Harvick had insisted on concealing cloaks and following back alleys that even he hadn’t seen before. Now he was climbing down a ladder, Harvick ahead of him and Maya and Niall behind.

<!-- paragraph:6823 para_id:00001AA7 style:Normal -->
The ladder led into darkness that smelled faintly of oil and hot metal. At the bottom, the space opened into a long, low room, its far end illuminated by a single beam of steady zanlight.

<!-- paragraph:6824 para_id:00001AA8 style:Normal -->
A woman sat in that light.

<!-- paragraph:6825 para_id:00001AA9 style:Normal -->
She had long, fiery red hair pulled back into a high ponytail and a posture that suggested she was perfectly at ease exactly where she was. As the four of them entered, she stood and lazily raised a curved metal wand, pointing it directly at Davian.

<!-- paragraph:6826 para_id:00001AAA style:Normal -->
“Okay,” she said, voice bright and unbothered, “I’m going to assume that your promise not to try to kill me is still in effect—except for whoever this guy is.”

<!-- paragraph:6827 para_id:00001AAB style:Normal -->
She squinted at Davian, then shifted her weight and tapped something with her foot. The room brightened incrementally as additional lights flickered on.

<!-- paragraph:6828 para_id:00001AAC style:Normal -->
“Oh,” she added, visibly reassessing. “He’s very handsome. He gets a pass. Handsome guys never try to kill me.”

<!-- paragraph:6829 para_id:00001AAD style:Normal -->
Maya stepped smoothly in front of Davian. “Zirene,” she said dryly, “I thought you said Niall was handsome too. And you still made him promise not to kill you. Aren’t you going to ask who this is?”

<!-- paragraph:6830 para_id:00001AAE style:Normal -->
Zirene lowered the wand with a scoff. “My standards were lower then. That was hours ago. Everyone knows standards go up as the day drags on.”

<!-- paragraph:6831 para_id:00001AAF style:Normal -->
She waved the wand vaguely around the room. “But you clearly want to tell me who he is. And I’m assuming you’re not just here to apologize for breaking my hatch—and my ceiling, though let’s be honest, that ceiling didn’t have much life left in it. So. Get on with it.”

<!-- paragraph:6832 para_id:00001AB0 style:Normal -->
Harvick couldn’t help himself. “This is the assassin who threw a building at Niall,” he said eagerly.

<!-- paragraph:6833 para_id:00001AB1 style:Normal -->
Maya shot him a sharp look. Zirene, on the other hand, brightened immediately. “Oh! And you want me to make something that lets him throw a bigger building.” She sounded delighted. “That’s great. The last one was clearly undersized.” She was already moving, pushing open a door into an adjacent workshop that was fully lit and immensely cluttered. “Let me see what I’ve got.”

<!-- paragraph:6834 para_id:00001AB2 style:Normal -->
They followed her in as she began rummaging through piles of metal, half-finished constructs, and tools whose purpose Davian couldn’t begin to guess. Maya cleared her throat. Zirene didn’t react. Maya cleared it again, louder.

<!-- paragraph:6835 para_id:00001AB3 style:Normal -->
Zirene popped back into view, hair askew, holding what looked like an iron club covered in an intricate zan lattice. “What?” she said. “I’m busy.”

<!-- paragraph:6836 para_id:00001AB4 style:Normal -->
“Actually,” Maya said carefully, “we don’t want something that makes the assassin who almost killed Niall stronger.”

<!-- paragraph:6837 para_id:00001AB5 style:Normal -->
From behind her, Davian heard Niall mutter, “That’s not exactly what happened.”

<!-- paragraph:6838 para_id:00001AB6 style:Normal -->
Zirene frowned. “Well that’s extremely disappointing.” She set the club aside. “Now you’re definitely paying for the hatch and my ceiling.”

<!-- paragraph:6839 para_id:00001AB7 style:Normal -->
She stepped closer to Davian and leaned in, peering at him with open curiosity. He leaned back instinctively.

<!-- paragraph:6840 para_id:00001AB8 style:Normal -->
Maya moved between them. “We need something that will let us… trust him.”

<!-- paragraph:6841 para_id:00001AB9 style:Normal -->
Zirene’s face lit up. “Ahhh. Like a device that forces him to always tell the truth?”

<!-- paragraph:6842 para_id:00001ABA style:Normal -->
“Oh,” Maya said immediately. “That would be perfect.”

<!-- paragraph:6843 para_id:00001ABB style:Normal -->
Davian began to sweat.

<!-- paragraph:6844 para_id:00001ABC style:Normal -->
“I can’t make that,” Zirene said flatly. “Too hard.”

<!-- paragraph:6845 para_id:00001ABD style:Normal -->
“Then why did you suggest it?” Davian asked.

<!-- paragraph:6846 para_id:00001ABE style:Normal -->
Zirene looked him up and down again. “Oh. His voice is kind of weird.” She shrugged. “Not really my type.”

<!-- paragraph:6847 para_id:00001ABF style:Normal -->
She looked bored. Davian swallowed, suddenly self-conscious.

<!-- paragraph:6848 para_id:00001AC0 style:Normal -->
“Zirene,” Maya said, her voice strained, “you must have something that can help us here.”

<!-- paragraph:6849 para_id:00001AC1 style:Normal -->
Zirene considered this seriously for several long moments. Harvick opened his mouth, then thought better of it when Maya glanced his way.

<!-- paragraph:6850 para_id:00001AC2 style:Normal -->
“Okay,” Zirene said at last. “Yes. I have the perfect solution.” She smiled brightly. “How about something that lets you kill him instantly if he does anything you don’t like?”

<!-- paragraph:6851 para_id:00001AC3 style:Normal -->
“What?” Davian said. “That’s insane.”

<!-- paragraph:6852 para_id:00001AC4 style:Normal -->
Zirene waved him off. “Shh. Less talking. More standing silently so I can appreciate your looks without the commentary.”

<!-- paragraph:6853 para_id:00001AC5 style:Normal -->
Niall stepped forward. “It’s a fine idea,” he said. “Though I can already kill him instantly.”

<!-- paragraph:6854 para_id:00001AC6 style:Normal -->
Zirene ignored him and grabbed Davian’s arm, lifting it into the light. The faded Ink wound across his skin toward the back of his hand. She leaned in close and sniffed once.

<!-- paragraph:6855 para_id:00001AC7 style:Normal -->
“Huh,” she said. “Looks like this ran out.”

<!-- paragraph:6856 para_id:00001AC8 style:Normal -->
She traced the pattern with one finger. “But look at this. See it?”

<!-- paragraph:6857 para_id:00001AC9 style:Normal -->
Davian didn’t see anything. Maya and Niall exchanged a look. Harvick had drifted to the wall and was pretending not to listen.

<!-- paragraph:6858 para_id:00001ACA style:Normal -->
Zirene dropped Davian’s arm with a sigh. “Just because you only see lattices shining on the face doesn’t mean they aren’t present everywhere. Magic isn’t just head-stuff. Arms, legs, hands—everyone uses their body to focus, whether they admit it or not.”

<!-- paragraph:6859 para_id:00001ACB style:Normal -->
She tilted her head. “This Ink was layered over an Archeon pattern, like it was supposed to activate it somehow. Meaning this guy must be one of Serathis’s… great-something grandchildren. So, you need a way to kill him even if he suddenly learns how to use a Shroud and magic properly.”

<!-- paragraph:6860 para_id:00001ACC style:Normal -->
Maya hesitated. “I have a lot of questions. But I suppose that’s… not the worst idea.”

<!-- paragraph:6861 para_id:00001ACD style:Normal -->
“I don’t know how I feel about setting myself up to be killed on command,” Davian said.

<!-- paragraph:6862 para_id:00001ACE style:Normal -->
Maya stepped closer, lowering her voice. He felt suddenly cornered, the table pressing into his back. “I’m not going to force you,” she said. “But I want to work with you. And I - we - can’t trust you enough yet to train you.”

<!-- paragraph:6863 para_id:00001ACF style:Normal -->
Davian looked away, breath coming shallow. In that moment, he would have agreed to anything to stay close to her.

<!-- paragraph:6864 para_id:00001AD0 style:Normal -->
“…Fine,” he said quietly. “I can’t undo what I’ve already done, but I will do whatever is necessary to earn your trust again.”

<!-- paragraph:6865 para_id:00001AD1 style:Normal -->
Zirene clapped her hands together. “Yay!”

<!-- paragraph:6866 para_id:00001AD2 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6867 para_id:00001AD3 style:Normal -->
A minute later, Davian was lying on his back on a worktable that Zirene had cleared with a single sweeping motion, sending a precarious pile of tools and scrap clattering to the floor. He stared up at the ceiling while she moved around him, measuring his arms and chest with brisk efficiency, muttering to herself as she worked.

<!-- paragraph:6868 para_id:00001AD4 style:Normal -->
At some point she shifted position so that her back was to the others, her body blocking their view of the table. Her hands flickered. do [you] really want this [?]

<!-- paragraph:6869 para_id:00001AD5 style:Normal -->
The signs were slightly off but clear enough. Davian’s breath caught. He turned his head just enough to look at her and answered with minimal movement, keeping his arm close to his side.

<!-- paragraph:6870 para_id:00001AD6 style:Normal -->
how do [you] know Concord sign [?]

<!-- paragraph:6871 para_id:00001AD7 style:Normal -->
Zirene didn’t even look surprised. She signed back easily. silly [question]. all masked codes [more/less] same. learn [one], easy learn rest.

<!-- paragraph:6872 para_id:00001AD8 style:Normal -->
She smiled faintly and added, [and] based on zan.

<!-- paragraph:6873 para_id:00001AD9 style:Normal -->
Davian felt a rush of questions he didn’t have time to ask. He swallowed, then signed back. [yes]. [I] need to prove [myself].

<!-- paragraph:6874 para_id:00001ADA style:Normal -->
Zirene’s shoulders lifted in a small shrug. She turned away, reached into a drawer, and pulled out thin strips of dark metal along with tools so fine they looked like needles.

<!-- paragraph:6875 para_id:00001ADB style:Normal -->
“Well then,” she said aloud, as if the silent exchange had never happened, “let’s get things set up for you. Lay still.”

<!-- paragraph:6876 para_id:00001ADC style:Normal -->
Zirene laid the thin metal strips across Davian’s arms and chest, shifting them by fractions of an inch as she worked. She pulled a pair of heavy goggles down over her eyes and began rotating the metal, tapping and prodding at it with her tapered chisels.

<!-- paragraph:6877 para_id:00001ADD style:Normal -->
Davian watched her hands move. “What are the goggles for?”

<!-- paragraph:6878 para_id:00001ADE style:Normal -->
She didn’t look up. “Magnification,” she said, clearly pleased to be asked. “There’s no real limit to how many times you can take a component za and turn it into a fully functional za at a smaller scale. Smaller and smaller and smaller. The limit is the zanwright’s tooling ability.”

<!-- paragraph:6879 para_id:00001ADF style:Normal -->
She tapped the side of the goggles. “And vision.”

<!-- paragraph:6880 para_id:00001AE0 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “I don’t understand. A za is a za. You etch it and it does what it does.”

<!-- paragraph:6881 para_id:00001AE1 style:Normal -->
Zirene laughed, sharp and delighted. “See? That right there tells me you have no idea what you’re doing.” She adjusted one of the metal strips and continued. “A za means something. It represents something. That meaning—the representation—is what gives it power.”

<!-- paragraph:6882 para_id:00001AE2 style:Normal -->
She paused, studying the alignment. “I’m going to have to draw your za to make this work. That’s why I’m being careful.”

<!-- paragraph:6883 para_id:00001AE3 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. “People don’t have za.”

<!-- paragraph:6884 para_id:00001AE4 style:Normal -->
Zirene looked up at him. “Then what do they have? Names?” Her gaze flicked to the faded Ink patterns along his arm. “Auras?”

<!-- paragraph:6885 para_id:00001AE5 style:Normal -->
She tilted her head. “If you change your name and your aura, are you the same person? Or a different one?”

<!-- paragraph:6886 para_id:00001AE6 style:Normal -->
Before Davian could answer, he noticed Maya had stepped closer to the table. “Did you have to do this for Niall too?” she asked.

<!-- paragraph:6887 para_id:00001AE7 style:Normal -->
“Yes,” Zirene said easily. “If you want something to work at its highest level. And if you didn’t want that, you could have saved a lot of money and gone somewhere else.” She smiled faintly. “It needs to understand the person it’s attached to.”

<!-- paragraph:6888 para_id:00001AE8 style:Normal -->
Maya sighed. “It’s just a lattice. It doesn’t understand anything.”

<!-- paragraph:6889 para_id:00001AE9 style:Normal -->
Zirene snorted. “You’re the one whose brain tears holes in reality, so of all people you should get that you need to understand something to influence it.”

<!-- paragraph:6890 para_id:00001AEA style:Normal -->
She leaned back over Davian, goggles catching the light, looking for all the world like some bright, predatory insect. “I already told you I don’t know the old za names. But I do use some of the newer ones to help remember the strokes.”

<!-- paragraph:6891 para_id:00001AEB style:Normal -->
With precise, minimal movements, she began etching into the metal laid across his chest.

<!-- paragraph:6892 para_id:00001AEC style:Normal -->
“First,” she said, repeating a sharp, linear motion several times, “the Blade.”

<!-- paragraph:6893 para_id:00001AED style:Normal -->
She shifted slightly and etched again, slower and more rounded this time. “Then, the Deep.” Twice.

<!-- paragraph:6894 para_id:00001AEE style:Normal -->
“And last,” she said, tracing a line that seemed to oscillate as it formed, “the Crown.”

<!-- paragraph:6895 para_id:00001AEF style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed. “Are those… city-wedge names?”

<!-- paragraph:6896 para_id:00001AF0 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned. “Only two of them are,” she said. “But it is an odd coincidence.”

<!-- paragraph:6897 para_id:00001AF1 style:Normal -->
Zirene didn’t stop etching. “You know your writing system comes from these,” she said casually. “And your hand language.”

<!-- paragraph:6898 para_id:00001AF2 style:Normal -->
The metal under her hands began to hum, faintly, as the work continued.

<!-- paragraph:6899 para_id:00001AF3 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6900 para_id:00001AF4 style:Normal -->
A few hours later, Davian stood with black iron bands locked around both of his upper arms.

<!-- paragraph:6901 para_id:00001AF5 style:Normal -->
They sat just beneath the shoulder, seamless against his skin, their surface covered in intricate zanwork. He could feel them constantly, a pressure he couldn’t quite shift out of awareness.

<!-- paragraph:6902 para_id:00001AF6 style:Normal -->
Zirene circled him, gesturing animatedly as she spoke to Maya and Niall. “See? I anchored them to the skin the same way I did Niall’s metals.” She gave one band an affectionate tap. “They’re not coming off. Or, I guess, they’d be really hard to get off.”

<!-- paragraph:6903 para_id:00001AF7 style:Normal -->
She leaned in and poked Davian in the arm. “Just so you know, if you try to remove them, they’ll activate and you’ll die.”

<!-- paragraph:6904 para_id:00001AF8 style:Normal -->
Davian said nothing.

<!-- paragraph:6905 para_id:00001AF9 style:Normal -->
Zirene poked him again. “You could give me, like, an affirmative grunt or something. I like a nice, manly grunt.”

<!-- paragraph:6906 para_id:00001AFA style:Normal -->
Still nothing.

<!-- paragraph:6907 para_id:00001AFB style:Normal -->
She wound up to poke him a third time, then lost interest mid-motion and turned back to the others. “Anyway, when they activate, they’ll do the opposite of what Niall did to Harvick’s heart. Instant death.”

<!-- paragraph:6908 para_id:00001AFC style:Normal -->
She brightened. “Do you want to test it?”

<!-- paragraph:6909 para_id:00001AFD style:Normal -->
Maya blinked. “If we test it, won’t that kill him?”

<!-- paragraph:6910 para_id:00001AFE style:Normal -->
Zirene deflated. “Yeah, I guess.” She waved a hand. “Fine.”

<!-- paragraph:6911 para_id:00001AFF style:Normal -->
She resumed pacing. “Activation is simple. You just pass electricity through either band—it doesn’t matter which one. Doesn’t need to be much. I designed them so they’re very hard to fully cover with a Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:6912 para_id:00001B00 style:Normal -->
She glanced at Maya. “You can also activate them by rapidly cooling either one.”

<!-- paragraph:6913 para_id:00001B01 style:Normal -->
“Why not heating?” Maya asked. “That would be easier.”

<!-- paragraph:6914 para_id:00001B02 style:Normal -->
Zirene shook her head. “Cooling is harder. More specific. I don’t want him standing too close to a fire and just—” she snapped her fingers and shrugged. “Also, since his Shroud barely covers the bands, you should still be able to do it from a distance. The zan lattice amplifies electrical or cooling input.”

<!-- paragraph:6915 para_id:00001B03 style:Normal -->
Davian cleared his throat. “What if I stand next to something too cold?”

<!-- paragraph:6916 para_id:00001B04 style:Normal -->
Zirene sighed. “Obviously, just don’t do that.” She glanced back at him. “I put some failsafes in so activation is specific to Niall and Maya, but don’t push your luck.”

<!-- paragraph:6917 para_id:00001B05 style:Normal -->
Harvick frowned. “If it’s meant to affect his heart, why are they on his arms instead of his chest?”

<!-- paragraph:6918 para_id:00001B06 style:Normal -->
Zirene sighed again, longer this time, raising her eyes to the ceiling as if searching for divine fortitude to withstand the idiocy of the question. “Because it looks cooler.”

<!-- paragraph:6919 para_id:00001B07 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6920 para_id:00001B08 style:Normal -->
They walked back through the city with Niall and Harvick a few paces ahead of them, their cloaks blending into the evening foot traffic. Davian adjusted his stride deliberately, slowing just enough that he drifted back until he was walking beside Maya.

<!-- paragraph:6921 para_id:00001B09 style:Normal -->
For a while, neither of them spoke. The city noise filled the space between them—voices, carts, distant machinery—but it all felt strangely muted compared to the awareness humming just under Davian’s skin. He stole a glance at her from the corner of his eye and immediately regretted it. All he could see beneath the edge of her hood was the clean line of her chin and the curve of her mouth, composed and unreadable as ever. The edge of her omnipresent Shroud shimmered in outline of her features, only visible because he knew to look for it.

<!-- paragraph:6922 para_id:00001B0A style:Normal -->
Heat crept up his neck. He swallowed and fixed his gaze straight ahead.

<!-- paragraph:6923 para_id:00001B0B style:Normal -->
After a few steps, he cleared his throat. “So,” he said, trying for casual and missing slightly, “do the bands really look cool?”

<!-- paragraph:6924 para_id:00001B0C style:Normal -->
She didn’t answer right away.

<!-- paragraph:6925 para_id:00001B0D style:Normal -->
“They’re…” Maya said at last. “All right.”

<!-- paragraph:6926 para_id:00001B0E style:Normal -->
“Really?” Davian asked.

<!-- paragraph:6927 para_id:00001B0F style:Normal -->
She was quiet again for a moment, then huffed softly. “Let’s just say Zirene shouldn’t switch careers to clothier.” There was a faint lightness to her tone, enough that Davian felt himself relax.

<!-- paragraph:6928 para_id:00001B10 style:Normal -->
He smiled before he could stop himself.

<!-- paragraph:6929 para_id:00001B11 style:Normal -->
“Since I woke up,” he said after a beat, “back in your dungeon, I mean—there’s been this weird sensation. Like things echo.” He gestured vaguely. “Footsteps, for example. It’s like I hear them twice.”

<!-- paragraph:6930 para_id:00001B12 style:Normal -->
“That’s normal,” Maya said. “It happens to every magus, one way or another.”

<!-- paragraph:6931 para_id:00001B13 style:Normal -->
He glanced at her again, more carefully this time. She was still looking forward as she spoke. “For me, if something nearby is especially hot or cold, it shows up like a bright light or a patch of darkness layered over everything else. Not literally, but… close enough.”

<!-- paragraph:6932 para_id:00001B14 style:Normal -->
She paused. “It was terrifying at first. I thought something was wrong with me. But Niall recognized it immediately and taught me how to work with it.”

<!-- paragraph:6933 para_id:00001B15 style:Normal -->
Davian nodded slowly, absorbing that. The echoing sensation was still there—footsteps doubling, motion arriving before sound—but it had dulled, receding into the background as his mind learned where to place it.

<!-- paragraph:6934 para_id:00001B16 style:Normal -->
Maya glanced at the sparse figures moving along the street and lowered her voice. “The way you perceive forces around you is tied to the alignment of your hereditary magic,” she said. “Which means it could tell us something about your grandmother. We still don’t have a good understanding of how Archeon’s hereditary magic works.”

<!-- paragraph:6935 para_id:00001B17 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned slightly. “But you don’t have the same magic as your father,” he said. “And none of the other High Clades he could have contracted with have anything like what you can do.” He hesitated. “Doesn’t that mean things can change a lot between generations? How can we know that whatever I’m sensing has anything to do with Serathis?”

<!-- paragraph:6936 para_id:00001B18 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t answer right away. They walked in silence for several long steps. When Davian looked at her again, her expression had settled into something composed and distant, the same mask he’d seen her wear in public more times than he could count.

<!-- paragraph:6937 para_id:00001B19 style:Normal -->
“I do have the same magic that my mother did,” she said finally. “My father didn’t have a birthing contract.”

<!-- paragraph:6938 para_id:00001B1A style:Normal -->
The implications landed all at once. Davian remembered the things he’d said to Harvick, the way he’d tried to rationalize his attraction to Maya as something artificial, something engineered. He felt a flush of embarrassment at how confidently wrong he’d been.

<!-- paragraph:6939 para_id:00001B1B style:Normal -->
Davian cleared his throat, trying to step carefully. “I’m sorry,” he said, the simple words forced out abruptly by the pressure of his desire to recant his misconceptions.

<!-- paragraph:6940 para_id:00001B1C style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t bristle. “My mother died a long time ago,” she said evenly. “I barely remember her.”

<!-- paragraph:6941 para_id:00001B1D style:Normal -->
Another realization struck him. “So you had to figure everything out on your own?” he asked. “I thought your magic was… I don’t know. Like Niall’s. I assumed he could teach you… stuff.”

<!-- paragraph:6942 para_id:00001B1E style:Normal -->
There was the faintest hint of a smile at the corner of her mouth. “You thought heat transfer and electricity were basically the same thing?”

<!-- paragraph:6943 para_id:00001B1F style:Normal -->
Davian flushed. “Well. They both… make things hot.”

<!-- paragraph:6944 para_id:00001B20 style:Normal -->
Maya laughed before she could stop herself. The sound was bright enough that Niall and Harvick both glanced back in surprise. She composed her face almost instantly and gave them a cool, lifted brow until they turned forward again.

<!-- paragraph:6945 para_id:00001B21 style:Normal -->
They walked another stretch in silence before she spoke again.

<!-- paragraph:6946 para_id:00001B22 style:Normal -->
“I shouldn’t laugh,” she said. “Niall was able to teach me a few things. And maybe explaining why what you said is funny will teach you something useful.”

<!-- paragraph:6947 para_id:00001B23 style:Normal -->
Her tone shifted—not colder, but more precise.

<!-- paragraph:6948 para_id:00001B24 style:Normal -->
“All magic works by mental carving. You cut spaces into reality that change how basic energies move between places. But each type of energy behaves differently, so the visualizations required are completely different between magi. I don’t have Niall’s instinctive grasp of lightning. I can’t see the world the way he does when he’s looking at electrical potential.”

<!-- paragraph:6949 para_id:00001B25 style:Normal -->
She glanced ahead toward Niall’s back. “His ability is powerful because it’s simple in structure. He finds something with a high electrical charge and connects it to something with less—which is almost everything—and that difference makes the lightning want to travel. The difficulty is in seeing and maintaining connections over long distances. But decisive, long-range attacks are what most magi aspire to. In that sense, his method is efficient.”

<!-- paragraph:6950 para_id:00001B26 style:Normal -->
Maya looked back and forth as they followed Niall and Harvick onto an empty side street. She lifted one hand and the air above her palm distorted. A shimmer gathered there, then condensed and burst into flame—a tight, contained sphere of fire no larger than an apple, but bright enough to cast hard shadows across the cobblestones.

<!-- paragraph:6951 para_id:00001B27 style:Normal -->
“It’s different for me,” she continued. “Cold is simply the absence of heat. Heat spreads naturally—from warm to cold. When I carve, I reverse that tendency. I pull heat toward heat.”

<!-- paragraph:6952 para_id:00001B28 style:Normal -->
The small sun hovered, its surface roiling but perfectly contained.

<!-- paragraph:6953 para_id:00001B29 style:Normal -->
“But heat disperses unless it’s constrained. So I have to gather it, compress it, and release it in a controlled way that preserves its intensity. That requires more layered visualizations. More structure. Especially at a distance.”

<!-- paragraph:6954 para_id:00001B2A style:Normal -->
The flame winked out, leaving only the faint afterimage behind Davian’s eyes.

<!-- paragraph:6955 para_id:00001B2B style:Normal -->
Davian found that he didn’t mind her laughter, her explanation. If anything, listening to her was more comforting than listening to his own dark thoughts.

<!-- paragraph:6956 para_id:00001B2C style:Normal -->
“So the complicated part,” he said carefully, “the shapes you have to hold in your head, the way heat and cold behave—you had to figure all of that out yourself. When you were a child.”

<!-- paragraph:6957 para_id:00001B2D style:Normal -->
He glanced toward Niall’s back and lowered his voice further. “And I can imagine… having him as your father didn’t make that easier.”

<!-- paragraph:6958 para_id:00001B2E style:Normal -->
Maya considered that. “Having a father like him makes some things easier and other things harder. That’s just the way things are.” Her tone was even, not defensive. “He was—like I assume most High Clade parents are—very focused on me manifesting my Shroud as early as possible. It protects against backlash. Against accidents.” She paused. “I don’t really want to talk about that.”

<!-- paragraph:6959 para_id:00001B2F style:Normal -->
Davian nodded, even though she wasn’t looking at him. He could still just barely see the faint distortion of her Shroud along the edge of her cheek, a subtle shimmer under the hood.

<!-- paragraph:6960 para_id:00001B30 style:Normal -->
“My mother made me learn her martial arts when I was very young,” he said after a moment. “I think she was worried about me protecting myself too. I didn’t understand why at the time.” He hesitated. “Now I think she must have learned them at Archeon. She mixed them with other techniques later, but the core was probably from there.”

<!-- paragraph:6961 para_id:00001B31 style:Normal -->
The thought tightened something in his chest. Archeon had his mother now. Why? For leverage? For knowledge? For punishment?

<!-- paragraph:6962 para_id:00001B32 style:Normal -->
“I get why you didn’t let me go after Serathis,” he continued. “I wasn’t thinking clearly. She’s had over a hundred years to refine whatever she does. But we need to move soon. Before something terrible happens to my mother and father.”

<!-- paragraph:6963 para_id:00001B33 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t slow her pace. “Archeon wanted her alive,” she said. “Now they’ve sent magi after you who ended up dead. That makes her more valuable, not less.” Her voice was cool. “We don’t know what Archeon is doing. Or the Drakhals. Or the Harbingers. Or my grandfather. Charging in without information or a solid plan puts her in greater danger than she is now.”

<!-- paragraph:6964 para_id:00001B34 style:Normal -->
At the mention of Maya’s grandfather, the Ink stirred inside Davian. For an instant, the city faded. A dark thread unfurled in his vision, not visible to his eyes but undeniable in his mind, stretching forward with terrible clarity—straight to Niall’s back. A whisper coiled around his thoughts.

<!-- paragraph:6965 para_id:00001B35 style:Normal -->
kill Niall. His hands twitched.

<!-- paragraph:6966 para_id:00001B36 style:Normal -->
He blinked hard and shook his head once, sharply, as if to dislodge something physical. The thread wavered and thinned, dissolving back into the background murmur.

<!-- paragraph:6967 para_id:00001B37 style:Normal -->
Maya had turned toward him. The composure on her face cracked just enough to show concern. “What is it?”

<!-- paragraph:6968 para_id:00001B38 style:Normal -->
Davian glanced ahead to make sure Niall and Harvick hadn’t heard. He lowered his voice again. “Ever since that night—” He faltered. “You know. The night we… I mean, the night I stole the Heart.” Heat rushed to his face. He forced himself to continue. “I keep feeling like I have to kill Niall. Like it’s inevitable.”

<!-- paragraph:6969 para_id:00001B39 style:Normal -->
He swallowed.

<!-- paragraph:6970 para_id:00001B3A style:Normal -->
“That’s what the Harbinger told me. That everything is already laid out. That we’re all just walking the last steps of a dance that’s already been choreographed.”

<!-- paragraph:6971 para_id:00001B3B style:Normal -->
The citadel rose ahead of them, its central spire cutting into the darkening sky. The sight of it tightened something in Davian’s chest. Maya stopped walking for half a step and turned toward him. There was no softness left in her expression now.

<!-- paragraph:6972 para_id:00001B3C style:Normal -->
“So you’re still thinking like that?” she said. “Like you don’t have a choice in what you do?”

<!-- paragraph:6973 para_id:00001B3D style:Normal -->
“No, I’m just—” Davian began.

<!-- paragraph:6974 para_id:00001B3E style:Normal -->
She didn’t let him finish. “Until you admit that you do have a choice,” she said evenly, “and that you did have a choice before, you’re going to keep feeling that way. You’re going to keep looking for something else to blame.”

<!-- paragraph:6975 para_id:00001B3F style:Normal -->
Her gaze held his for a long moment. Then she turned and walked quickly to join Niall and Harvick, leaving Davian a step behind.

<!-- paragraph:6976 para_id:00001B40 style:Normal -->
He stared after her, the echo of her words settling in a place he didn’t want to examine too closely.

<!-- paragraph:6977 para_id:00001B41 style:Normal -->
A moment later Harvick’s voice drifted back. “Someone has to keep an eye on him,” he said lightly, and dropped back to walk beside Davian again.

<!-- paragraph:6978 para_id:00001B42 style:Normal -->
As they approached the gates, Harvick glanced sideways at him. “So. That didn’t go super well, huh?”

<!-- paragraph:6979 para_id:00001B43 style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled. “She’s a Rezin,” he said. “I can’t exactly live up to that.”

<!-- paragraph:6980 para_id:00001B44 style:Normal -->
Harvick rolled his eyes so dramatically Davian could almost hear it. “Don’t be a wimp. She’s not just a symbol of High Clade magic.” He leaned in and lowered his voice to a conspiratorial whisper. “She’s a girl. And you like her.”

<!-- paragraph:6981 para_id:00001B45 style:Normal -->
Davian shoved him in the shoulder, harder than necessary. “Hey. Stop that.”

<!-- paragraph:6982 para_id:00001B46 style:Normal -->
The noise drew a glance from ahead. Niall and Maya both looked back briefly. Davian and Harvick straightened instantly, faces carefully neutral.

<!-- paragraph:6983 para_id:00001B47 style:Normal -->
When the two turned forward again, Harvick looked at Davian and mouthed, slowly and deliberately: deal with it.

<!-- paragraph:6986 para_id:00001B4A style:Title -->
# 🔥 Chapter 34

<!-- paragraph:6987 para_id:00001B4B style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 34

<!-- paragraph:6989 para_id:00001B4D style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:6990 para_id:00001B4E style:Normal -->
The guards did not look at Maya as she passed through the gates. Their eyes were lowered, shoulders stiff, the silence around them strained in a way that told Maya something was wrong before she even lifted her gaze. The reason became obvious a moment later.

<!-- paragraph:6991 para_id:00001B4F style:Normal -->
Niall stood in the open courtyard beneath the darkening sky, his long coat hanging still around him. There was no lightning dancing across his skin, no overt display of power, but the air around him felt charged in a way that was worse than visible fury. It held itself in readiness, as if anticipating catastrophe.

<!-- paragraph:6992 para_id:00001B50 style:Normal -->
“So,” he said, voice cold but edged with fury, “even though I expressly stated that we would not negotiate with this attempted assassin”—the word came out sharp, almost spat—“you, my daughter, chose to go behind my back and free him. For what purpose?”

<!-- paragraph:6993 para_id:00001B51 style:Normal -->
Davian stepped forward at Maya’s side, opening his mouth to speak. She stopped him with a firm sweep of her arm across his chest.

<!-- paragraph:6994 para_id:00001B52 style:Normal -->
“I am a full Presented magus of the Rezin Clade,” she said, her voice steady despite the pressure in the air, “and your heir. I am entitled to make my own decisions.”

<!-- paragraph:6995 para_id:00001B53 style:Normal -->
Niall’s composure cracked. “I am the Head Magus of this Clade,” he thundered, the words echoing off stone, “and I will remain so until the day I die. Your decision is overruled.”

<!-- paragraph:6996 para_id:00001B54 style:Normal -->
Maya drew breath to respond—but Niall moved first.

<!-- paragraph:6997 para_id:00001B55 style:Normal -->
His arms rose, and Maya’s mind snapped into speed an instant before the sky answered him. She shoved Davian backward with one hand and threw herself in front of him. The strike came too fast for her eyes to follow, a jagged arc tearing downward from the cloud cover—or was it upward from the ground?—so sudden that there was no time for thought.

<!-- paragraph:6998 para_id:00001B56 style:Normal -->
The lightning hit. Her Shroud flared into existence at full strength, catching the strike in a blinding explosion of white. The impact drove into her bones, into her teeth, into the back of her skull. She forced her mind to hold shape, to carve space, to redirect.

<!-- paragraph:6999 para_id:00001B57 style:Normal -->
The backlash tore free in sheets of sublimated flame that erupted behind her in a roaring arc, skimming wide of Davian and Harvick by a margin as thin as a blade. The pressure forced Maya down to one knee.

<!-- paragraph:7000 para_id:00001B58 style:Normal -->
The lightning intensified, no longer a single strike but a sustained torrent, a continuous assault that carried more than raw energy. She could feel Niall’s will threaded through it, urgent and implacable, pressing against her defenses and searching for weakness. The current did not simply want to burn; it wanted a path, a target. It wanted Davian. Behind her, she heard him scrambling to his feet.

<!-- paragraph:7001 para_id:00001B59 style:Normal -->
She widened her awareness and spread her Shroud outward, forcing it to hold its shape under the strain. The impact felt like standing beneath a waterfall of molten glass. Every instinct told her to contract, to harden, to endure. Instead she had to do the opposite—carve, open channels, give the sublimated energy somewhere else to go.

<!-- paragraph:7002 para_id:00001B5A style:Normal -->
Sheets of flame burst outward at her sides as she redirected the sublimated backlash, the stone of the courtyard blackening under the heat. The air screamed around her.

<!-- paragraph:7003 para_id:00001B5B style:Normal -->
Niall took a step forward. The pressure intensified instantly. More arcs forked downward, feeding into the stream already hammering against her defenses. Through the crackling roar she barely caught his voice.

<!-- paragraph:7004 para_id:00001B5C style:Normal -->
“Do you have any idea what this means?” he shouted. “The law of the High Clades is the law of strength—of blood. Would you kill me to take my place?”

<!-- paragraph:7005 para_id:00001B5D style:Normal -->
Behind her, Davian strained against Harvick’s grip. His Shroud flickered erratically, flaring and collapsing in unstable bursts.

<!-- paragraph:7006 para_id:00001B5E style:Normal -->
“Stay back, idiot,” Harvick barked over the noise. “She can handle herself. This is way beyond you.”

<!-- paragraph:7007 para_id:00001B5F style:Normal -->
Maya clenched her teeth and forced herself upright, muscles trembling as she dragged her feet beneath her. The lightning pressed down harder in response, but she rose through it.

<!-- paragraph:7008 para_id:00001B60 style:Normal -->
When she stood fully, Niall’s eyes widened. Another flash illuminated his face, and for an instant she saw not the storm, but the man he had been—hopeful, fierce, devoted. The fury overlaying him now did not erase that memory; it only sharpened it.

<!-- paragraph:7009 para_id:00001B61 style:Normal -->
She knew stepping forward would give his mind more leverage. She did it anyway.

<!-- paragraph:7010 para_id:00001B62 style:Normal -->
The lightning raked across her Shroud as she pushed forward a single step, her carvings stretching to their limit to keep the force from collapsing inward.

<!-- paragraph:7011 para_id:00001B63 style:Normal -->
