“This isn’t the world my mother wanted,” she shouted back, her voice breaking through the roar by sheer will. “And I know it’s not the world you wanted either.”

<!-- paragraph:7012 para_id:00001B64 style:Normal -->
Maya felt it before she saw it—the subtle faltering in the current, the slight loosening of the pressure against her Shroud. Niall’s strength was ebbing. The lightning thinned, its arcs shortening, the relentless downward force no longer quite as absolute.

<!-- paragraph:7013 para_id:00001B65 style:Normal -->
“But this is the world that we have,” he called over the diminishing crackle. “The weak are devoured by the strong. And even being the strongest isn’t enough to fix the order of things.”

<!-- paragraph:7014 para_id:00001B66 style:Normal -->
Maya gathered what remained of the energy he had forced into her, refusing to let it dissipate. She carved sharply, connecting her hands to the stone beneath her feet. Ice spiraled outward from where she stood, thin fractal veins racing across the courtyard stones as she seized control of the balance. She remembered her Presentation—the moment the world had seemed to bend beneath her will—and lifted one hand.

<!-- paragraph:7015 para_id:00001B67 style:Normal -->
All the collected force, all the heat she had trapped and redirected, all the intent she had been holding back, she sent upward. A pillar of flame erupted from her palm, a brilliant spear of fire that tore through the dark clouds overhead and burned a path through the night.

<!-- paragraph:7016 para_id:00001B68 style:Normal -->
The courtyard blazed in its light. Niall’s lightning died completely. His arms fell to his sides. For a moment he only stared upward, face illuminated in gold and white, looking younger than he had seconds before—stripped of fury, stripped of fear. There was something almost like wonder there.

<!-- paragraph:7017 para_id:00001B69 style:Normal -->
Maya lowered her arm slowly. “That’s because you’re trying to do it alone,” she said, her voice steadier now. “You have us too. All of us.” She pointed back toward Davian without looking away from Niall. “Even him. Don’t you think it’s time you let me actually help you?”

<!-- paragraph:7018 para_id:00001B6A style:Normal -->
Silence stretched between them and doubt crept back in. What if he said she was still too weak? What if this had only proven that she could defy him, not stand beside him?

<!-- paragraph:7019 para_id:00001B6B style:Normal -->
Niall exhaled slowly, the sound heavy with years. “Fine,” he said at last, the word forced through clenched restraint. “We can… try it your way. For now.”

<!-- paragraph:7020 para_id:00001B6C style:Normal -->
He turned sharply before she could read anything more on his face and walked away across the courtyard, coat snapping behind him. When Maya turned back, Harvick was grinning openly.

<!-- paragraph:7021 para_id:00001B6D style:Normal -->
Davian was not. He was staring at her with an intensity that made the aftermath of lightning feel simple by comparison. For a heartbeat she held his gaze, and heat rose to her cheeks despite herself.

<!-- paragraph:7022 para_id:00001B6E style:Normal -->
“What?” she asked.

<!-- paragraph:7023 para_id:00001B6F style:Normal -->
He coughed and looked abruptly away, shoulders tightening. “Uh… that was just really… cool.”

<!-- paragraph:7024 para_id:00001B70 style:Normal -->
Maya replayed the last few moments in her mind—the lightning splitting the sky, her voice carrying across the courtyard, the towering column of flame. In the moment it had felt necessary, inevitable. Now, standing under the quiet dark with only a few guards pretending not to stare, it felt… theatrical.

<!-- paragraph:7025 para_id:00001B71 style:Normal -->
Her cheeks warmed further.

<!-- paragraph:7026 para_id:00001B72 style:Normal -->
“…Thanks,” she muttered.

<!-- paragraph:7027 para_id:00001B73 style:Normal -->
Silence settled over them. Harvick looked from one of them to the other, his grin widening with each passing second.

<!-- paragraph:7028 para_id:00001B74 style:Normal -->
Maya narrowed her eyes at him. “All right. Why do you look so…”

<!-- paragraph:7029 para_id:00001B75 style:Normal -->
“Like a child who just got an unexpected present?” Davian offered, still not quite meeting her gaze.

<!-- paragraph:7030 para_id:00001B76 style:Normal -->
Harvick smirked. “Oh, I’m just so excited about how awkward things are going to be around here.”

<!-- paragraph:7031 para_id:00001B77 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7032 para_id:00001B78 style:Normal -->
By the time they reached Maya’s office, the necessary introductions had been made and the guards dismissed. Aldira stood near the desk, hands folded behind her back, studying Davian with open skepticism. Harvick leaned against the wall as if he had wandered in by accident. Erynn stood behind Maya’s chair, silent but clearly listening to every word.

<!-- paragraph:7033 para_id:00001B79 style:Normal -->
Aldira shook her head slowly. “I can’t believe that even after I suspected something was off, I still accepted the story that you were a professional zanwright. You look far more like the sort of back-alley thug who robs other back-alley thugs.”

<!-- paragraph:7034 para_id:00001B7A style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. “...Thanks?”

<!-- paragraph:7035 para_id:00001B7B style:Normal -->
“It’s not a compliment. Harvick, this is your fault.”

<!-- paragraph:7036 para_id:00001B7C style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Harvick shot back, straightening. “You’re the one who said the Registry was infallible.”

<!-- paragraph:7037 para_id:00001B7D style:Normal -->
Aldira rolled her eyes. “I am not having that argument again.”

<!-- paragraph:7038 para_id:00001B7E style:Normal -->
Maya cut in. “Enough. The entire point of this arrangement is to get the information we need. Clearly the Registry is fallible, and the only person who can tell us what actually happened is sitting right here.”

<!-- paragraph:7039 para_id:00001B7F style:Normal -->
She looked at Davian as she said it and, unbidden, the memory of that night in the courtyard surfaced—his hand brushing hers, the heat between them. He flushed and looked away first.

<!-- paragraph:7040 para_id:00001B80 style:Normal -->
Davian cleared his throat. “We agreed that you’d teach me how to use magic in exchange for information.”

<!-- paragraph:7041 para_id:00001B81 style:Normal -->
Maya’s eyes snapped back to him. “You’re bringing that up now? After I just saved you from Niall?”

<!-- paragraph:7042 para_id:00001B82 style:Normal -->
“I just don’t want you to get what you need out of me and then forget what we agreed on.”

<!-- paragraph:7043 para_id:00001B83 style:Normal -->
“Oh, like I—” She stopped herself, jaw tightening. “Like we are the ones who ‘get what we need’ and then run away?”

<!-- paragraph:7044 para_id:00001B84 style:Normal -->
Davian’s flush deepened. “I just want to actually help going forward. Not just hand over information and sit uselessly somewhere.”

<!-- paragraph:7045 para_id:00001B85 style:Normal -->
“This isn’t about your power-related insecurities,” Maya said sharply. “This is about protecting my family. Right now.”

<!-- paragraph:7046 para_id:00001B86 style:Normal -->
They glared at one another across the room, tension climbing.

<!-- paragraph:7047 para_id:00001B87 style:Normal -->
Aldira looked between them, expression growing more puzzled by the second. “Why don’t we start with something small,” she suggested carefully. “A token of good faith. The most important information you have, ‘Davian,’ concerns the Harbingers’ goals and your family’s relationship with Archeon. We can work up to that. For now, how about you tell us how you got past the Registry?”

<!-- paragraph:7048 para_id:00001B88 style:Normal -->
Maya rolled her eyes. “Aldira, you’re only focusing on that because it bothers you.”

<!-- paragraph:7049 para_id:00001B89 style:Normal -->
Aldira gave a small shrug that did nothing to deny it.

<!-- paragraph:7050 para_id:00001B8A style:Normal -->
Davian exhaled. “Fine. I told you about the Ink the Harbingers give out?”

<!-- paragraph:7051 para_id:00001B8B style:Normal -->
“I already knew about that from informants’ reports,” Aldira said smoothly.

<!-- paragraph:7052 para_id:00001B8C style:Normal -->
Maya shot Aldira a smug look, knowing Aldira had dismissed those reports as rumor.

<!-- paragraph:7053 para_id:00001B8D style:Normal -->
Davian continued. “They gave me something like that - Ink that let me… steal someone’s aura.”

<!-- paragraph:7054 para_id:00001B8E style:Normal -->
He pushed up his sleeve and held out his arm. The dark patterns wound along his skin in layered designs—some faded to gray, others still sharply defined. He pointed to a particularly intricate lattice near his wrist.

<!-- paragraph:7055 para_id:00001B8F style:Normal -->
Aldira stepped closer, studying it with intense focus. “And all of these patterns are Harbinger Ink?”

<!-- paragraph:7056 para_id:00001B90 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. “No. Some of them are just… normal tattoos.”

<!-- paragraph:7057 para_id:00001B91 style:Normal -->
Harvick leaned over to inspect them and gave a low chuckle. “You paid actual money for those?”

<!-- paragraph:7058 para_id:00001B92 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Davian said defensively, pulling his arm back slightly. “Where I’m from everyone gets these. They matter.”

<!-- paragraph:7059 para_id:00001B93 style:Normal -->
Aldira tapped one of the simpler markings. “Isn’t this just the zan for ‘blade’ repeated over and over? What special meaning does that carry?”

<!-- paragraph:7060 para_id:00001B94 style:Normal -->
Davian folded his arms across his chest. “I’m here to answer questions about cataclysmic plots. Not to have you make fun of my tattoos.”

<!-- paragraph:7061 para_id:00001B95 style:Normal -->
Aldira exhaled through her nose. “Fine. What do you mean by ‘steal’? How is something like that possible?”

<!-- paragraph:7062 para_id:00001B96 style:Normal -->
Davian looked down at the faded Ink that had once held Arrek’s aura in place. His voice dropped. “I had to kill him to get it. I didn’t want to. But it was the only way.”

<!-- paragraph:7063 para_id:00001B97 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s mouth tightened. “Wonderful. So you’re not only an attempted murderer.”

<!-- paragraph:7064 para_id:00001B98 style:Normal -->
“You think I wanted to do it?” Davian shot back. “It’s not like I can undo it now by feeling bad. This—” he gestured vaguely around the room, “—this is me trying to make things right.”

<!-- paragraph:7065 para_id:00001B99 style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression hardened. “Poor you. Having to do all these terrible things you didn’t want to do. If you really want to make things right,” she continued, “why don’t you tell us why you did this? All of it.”

<!-- paragraph:7066 para_id:00001B9A style:Normal -->
“That’s about the Harbingers—”

<!-- paragraph:7067 para_id:00001B9B style:Normal -->
“No.” She cut him off sharply. “That’s not what I mean. Why did you—” Her mind flashed to the courtyard, to the warmth of his mouth against hers, to him running from her only to return with a blade. “Why did you, Davian, agree to this? Why did you follow through even after meeting us?”

<!-- paragraph:7068 para_id:00001B9C style:Normal -->
He looked down, shoulders tensing. “I didn’t have a choice.”

<!-- paragraph:7069 para_id:00001B9D style:Normal -->
Maya rose to her feet so abruptly her chair scraped across the stone. “You’re going to lie to the person who can see the threads of fate? I saw it. I know you had a choice. You could have trusted me. You could have told me before any of this. Why does no one trust me?”

<!-- paragraph:7070 para_id:00001B9E style:Normal -->
Davian stood as well, jaw set. “Why should I have trusted you? You say you’re different from the other Clades, but all you’re doing is expecting everyone else to do exactly what you want because you have the strongest magic. How is that a ‘better world’?”

<!-- paragraph:7071 para_id:00001B9F style:Normal -->
The room seemed to constrict around them.

<!-- paragraph:7072 para_id:00001BA0 style:Normal -->
Aldira looked between them, one brow lifting slightly. “I can tell things are very… heated right now,” she said carefully. “Why don’t we take a break? I will devise an equitable structure for the exchange of information, and we can reconvene in a training courtyard tomorrow morning.”

<!-- paragraph:7073 para_id:00001BA1 style:Normal -->
“Fine,” Maya said tightly.

<!-- paragraph:7074 para_id:00001BA2 style:Normal -->
She turned on her heel and left before anyone could respond.

<!-- paragraph:7075 para_id:00001BA3 style:Normal -->
Her steps echoed sharply down the corridor. She had barely made it halfway to the next turn when she heard lighter footsteps hurrying after her.

<!-- paragraph:7076 para_id:00001BA4 style:Normal -->
Erynn caught up, a little breathless, and fell into step beside her. They walked in silence for several moments.

<!-- paragraph:7077 para_id:00001BA5 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Erynn said at last, “that was really something. You’re really… mad at him?”

<!-- paragraph:7078 para_id:00001BA6 style:Normal -->
Maya kept her eyes forward. “That sounds like a question. Of course I’m furious. He stole from us and tried to kill Niall.”

<!-- paragraph:7079 para_id:00001BA7 style:Normal -->
“Mmhmm,” Erynn said mildly. “Yeah. He looks really… mad at you too.”

<!-- paragraph:7080 para_id:00001BA8 style:Normal -->
Maya stopped abruptly and turned to face her. “Stop saying it like that.”

<!-- paragraph:7081 para_id:00001BA9 style:Normal -->
“Like what?” Erynn asked, all innocence.

<!-- paragraph:7082 para_id:00001BAA style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head and resumed walking, faster this time, leaving Erynn to hurry after her once again.

<!-- paragraph:7083 para_id:00001BAB style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7084 para_id:00001BAC style:Normal -->
The next morning arrived quickly. Aldira had somehow convinced—coerced—a pair of Harvick’s forerunner friends into hauling a heavy conference table to Maya’s training courtyard. The older men now lounged against the outer wall, feigning indifference. Clearly, Aldira wanted this discussion contained.

<!-- paragraph:7085 para_id:00001BAD style:Normal -->
Davian was already present when Maya entered. He looked rumpled and faintly sleep-deprived, dark hair disordered as if he had run his hands through it too many times. For a brief, uncharitable moment, Maya considered that although she had broken him out of the prison, she had never formally rescinded the order to hold him there. The thought gave her a small, sharp satisfaction. It served him right for behaving like a criminal.

<!-- paragraph:7086 para_id:00001BAE style:Normal -->
She took her seat at the table. Erynn standing just behind her shoulder. Aldira sat opposite, Davian to one side, while Harvick paced restlessly along the wall, unable to commit to stillness.

<!-- paragraph:7087 para_id:00001BAF style:Normal -->
Aldira folded her hands atop the table. “I think it would be helpful to begin with an exchange that benefits all of us. Davian, I understand that your mother claims Archeon lineage. Yet she never manifested a Shroud. You, however, can. You’ve also demonstrated some basic magical manipulation. If we examine this, we may better understand Archeon's power structure—and possibly its connection to the Drakhals and the Harbingers.”

<!-- paragraph:7088 para_id:00001BB0 style:Normal -->
Davian shifted in his seat. “I don’t really know what you want me to say. I don’t understand magic well enough. The Shroud feels like some kind of defensive reflex I had to discover. Maybe the Ink helped. And when I tried to use energy, it just felt… messy. Exhausting. I didn’t know what to do with it.”

<!-- paragraph:7089 para_id:00001BB1 style:Normal -->
Aldira inclined her head. “Then perhaps something more practical would clarify matters. Go to the center of the courtyard and raise your Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:7090 para_id:00001BB2 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated for a moment before obeying. He stepped away from the table and drew up his Shroud. Under controlled conditions, without panic or chaos, it settled around him with reasonable steadiness.

<!-- paragraph:7091 para_id:00001BB3 style:Normal -->
Aldira turned to Maya. “And now, perhaps you could provide some… energy input.”

<!-- paragraph:7092 para_id:00001BB4 style:Normal -->
Maya rose smoothly and walked around the table to face Davian. His eyes widened as she approached. She allowed herself a faint smile.

<!-- paragraph:7093 para_id:00001BB5 style:Normal -->
She carved with minimal effort, shaping channels in the air as naturally as breathing. Heat drained from the surrounding space and condensed into a tight, inverted sphere hovering before her palm. The exercise required so little strain it barely registered.

<!-- paragraph:7094 para_id:00001BB6 style:Normal -->
“Wait—” Davian began.

<!-- paragraph:7095 para_id:00001BB7 style:Normal -->
“Focus on your Shroud!” Aldira snapped.

<!-- paragraph:7096 para_id:00001BB8 style:Normal -->
Maya carved a direct channel from the sphere toward Davian.

<!-- paragraph:7097 para_id:00001BB9 style:Normal -->
The coiled energy released in a sharp, explosive surge, snapping forward and slamming into Davian’s Shroud. The impact hurled him backward across the courtyard and into the stone wall with a resounding crack. He bounced off the wall and onto the ground in an almost comical fashion, then lay still for a breath, staring up at the sky as if reconsidering every decision that had brought him here. Then he rolled to his side and pushed himself upright.

<!-- paragraph:7098 para_id:00001BBA style:Normal -->
“That hurt,” he said, brushing dust from his sleeves. “How am I supposed to figure anything out if I keep getting thrown into walls?”

<!-- paragraph:7099 para_id:00001BBB style:Normal -->
Maya rolled her eyes. “That was barely anything. Any less and I might as well have tossed a rock at you.”

<!-- paragraph:7100 para_id:00001BBC style:Normal -->
Aldira, pen poised over her notebook, looked up at him with sharp interest. “Yes, yes—but what did you feel?”

<!-- paragraph:7101 para_id:00001BBD style:Normal -->
“I don’t know. Pain?”

<!-- paragraph:7102 para_id:00001BBE style:Normal -->
Aldira’s expression did not change. “Then we’ll simply have to repeat the process. Maya?”

<!-- paragraph:7103 para_id:00001BBF style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes widened. “Wait—just a second—”

<!-- paragraph:7104 para_id:00001BC0 style:Normal -->
“Shroud,” Aldira barked. “Again. Pay attention this time.”

<!-- paragraph:7105 para_id:00001BC1 style:Normal -->
Maya allowed Davian the smallest courtesy of watching his Shroud settle into place before carving another channel. The second surge struck cleanly, snapping him off his feet and driving him back into the wall. Stone shuddered under the impact.

<!-- paragraph:7106 para_id:00001BC2 style:Normal -->
He bounced forward, lost his balance, and landed flat on his back again, staring up at the pale morning sky. He groaned.

<!-- paragraph:7107 para_id:00001BC3 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed the courtyard and crouched over him, hands resting loosely on her knees. “You asked for magical training. This is what it looks like.”

<!-- paragraph:7108 para_id:00001BC4 style:Normal -->
“I feel like,” Davian said weakly, “getting launched into a wall repeatedly is not an optimal learning strategy.”

<!-- paragraph:7109 para_id:00001BC5 style:Normal -->
Harvick wandered over and peered down at him. “You’re trying to compress years of guarded Clade instruction into a few days. It’s going to involve unconventional methods. We have to play to your strengths.”

<!-- paragraph:7110 para_id:00001BC6 style:Normal -->
“And those are?”

<!-- paragraph:7111 para_id:00001BC7 style:Normal -->
“You’re extremely hardheaded.”

<!-- paragraph:7112 para_id:00001BC8 style:Normal -->
Davian glared up at him. “Hilarious. This was all a lot easier when I had the Ink.”

<!-- paragraph:7113 para_id:00001BC9 style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression cooled immediately. “Oh, the evil magic that let you ignore consequences because it would patch you back together? The one that now gives you murderous hallucinations?” She stood and looked down at him. “Excellent long-term strategy. Maybe you need to stop looking for the easy way out.”

<!-- paragraph:7114 para_id:00001BCA style:Normal -->
Aldira did not look up from her notes. “Anything this time, or shall we continue?”

<!-- paragraph:7115 para_id:00001BCB style:Normal -->
Davian pushed himself into a sitting position and squeezed his eyes shut. “Just… give me a second.” He breathed in and out a few times, deliberately.

<!-- paragraph:7116 para_id:00001BCC style:Normal -->
“There was something,” he said slowly. “I could feel Maya’s mind on my Shroud. Pushing energy into it. And my Shroud wanted to respond. It wanted to change the energy into something else, to put it somewhere. But I didn’t have anywhere to send it. I couldn’t figure it out fast enough.”

<!-- paragraph:7117 para_id:00001BCD style:Normal -->
He opened his eyes.

<!-- paragraph:7118 para_id:00001BCE style:Normal -->
Maya nodded once. “You saw yesterday, with Niall. The Shroud tries to convert incoming energy into something aligned with your hereditary magic. That’s why speed of thought matters. You need to redirect it quickly enough that it dissipates before it”—she gestured toward the cracked stone behind him—“acts directly.”

<!-- paragraph:7119 para_id:00001BCF style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “I tried something like that with the Archeon magi. I pulled the energy from their attack and sent it back. Or I think I did. But it was exhausting. How am I supposed to fight like that for more than a few seconds?”

<!-- paragraph:7120 para_id:00001BD0 style:Normal -->
“You were almost certainly doing it inefficiently,” Aldira said. “Channeling it incorrectly.”

<!-- paragraph:7121 para_id:00001BD1 style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head slightly. “Channeling isn’t quite the right word. You’re not guiding something that already has a path. You’re cutting one. You carve space so the energy can move where you want it to. And reality resists that.”

<!-- paragraph:7122 para_id:00001BD2 style:Normal -->
Davian grimaced. “Carving up reality seems… like a bad idea.”

<!-- paragraph:7123 para_id:00001BD3 style:Normal -->
Maya gave a small shrug. “That’s just how things are. The point is repetition. A magus drills what feels impossible until it becomes reflex. Then you layer those reflexes together into something more complex.”

<!-- paragraph:7124 para_id:00001BD4 style:Normal -->
Harvick folded his arms. “So it’s the same as normal fighting.”

<!-- paragraph:7125 para_id:00001BD5 style:Normal -->
Maya straightened. “That’s enough rest. Get up. We’ll try again.”

<!-- paragraph:7126 para_id:00001BD6 style:Normal -->
Davian pushed himself to his feet, eyeing her warily as she returned to her position in the courtyard.

<!-- paragraph:7127 para_id:00001BD7 style:Normal -->
“We call it sublimation,” she continued, “when the Shroud converts incoming energy into something aligned with your internal circuits.”

<!-- paragraph:7128 para_id:00001BD8 style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “My mother called our magic Subversion. She used it to drain zan that powered defenses when we broke into places. She always just said it drained energy.” He paused, thinking. “When I was in the Treasury fighting Niall, I could feel the energy in all the zan around the vault sphere. I pulled on it with my mind, converted it somehow, and released it. That’s what let me throw the vault at him. But there was backlash. It shattered both my arms. The Ink healed me. Without it, I’d probably have died. It’s like that every time I push too far.”

<!-- paragraph:7129 para_id:00001BD9 style:Normal -->
Maya nodded. “That’s exactly why the Clades force their children to manifest a Shroud first. Unfocused energy is volatile. It will tear through you before you can control it.” She lifted her hand again. “So keep your Shroud up. Focus. Do something intentional with the energy this time.”

<!-- paragraph:7130 para_id:00001BDA style:Normal -->
Davian raised his Shroud faster now, concentration sharpening. Maya carved and released.

<!-- paragraph:7131 para_id:00001BDB style:Normal -->
The impact slammed into him and drove him back into the wall, but this time he collapsed to one knee rather than sprawling flat.

<!-- paragraph:7132 para_id:00001BDC style:Normal -->
Harvick tilted his head. “Wow. An almost imperceptible improvement. Did you actually do something, or did Maya decide to be charitable?”

<!-- paragraph:7133 para_id:00001BDD style:Normal -->
Davian sucked in air, chest heaving. Maya held up a hand to silence Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:7134 para_id:00001BDE style:Normal -->
After a moment, Davian blinked hard and shook his head. “I tried to send it somewhere else. Away from me. But carving a channel fast enough—it's too hard.”

<!-- paragraph:7135 para_id:00001BDF style:Normal -->
Maya studied him, something clicking into place. “Maybe we’re approaching this incorrectly.” She frowned slightly. “I’ve never seen Archeon rely on long-range projection. They use gauntlets. Anchors. Tools. And you have your knife.”

<!-- paragraph:7136 para_id:00001BE0 style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. “The Ink told me—or showed me—when I attacked the Treasury sphere, to stab the knife into it. That helped. It felt… easier.”

<!-- paragraph:7137 para_id:00001BE1 style:Normal -->
“Then let’s test that principle,” Maya said. “Stand with your back against the wall.”

<!-- paragraph:7138 para_id:00001BE2 style:Normal -->
He obeyed, though tension crept into his posture.

<!-- paragraph:7139 para_id:00001BE3 style:Normal -->
“Now put your hands flat against the stone. Feel it.”

<!-- paragraph:7140 para_id:00001BE4 style:Normal -->
Davian pressed his palms against the wall and glanced back at her. “All right. Now wha—”

<!-- paragraph:7141 para_id:00001BE5 style:Normal -->
Maya released another controlled blast.

<!-- paragraph:7142 para_id:00001BE6 style:Normal -->
The force struck him and drove him forward, but instead of flying free across the courtyard, he rebounded hard and dropped to the ground in front of the wall.

<!-- paragraph:7143 para_id:00001BE7 style:Normal -->
He scrambled up immediately, scowling. “What was the point of that?”

<!-- paragraph:7144 para_id:00001BE8 style:Normal -->
“You seem slightly more energized than usual,” Maya said mildly, gesturing past him.

<!-- paragraph:7145 para_id:00001BE9 style:Normal -->
Davian turned.

<!-- paragraph:7146 para_id:00001BEA style:Normal -->
Two shallow craters marked the wall where his hands had been braced, stone fractured inward as if something had been forced into it rather than bounced off.

<!-- paragraph:7147 para_id:00001BEB style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7148 para_id:00001BEC style:Normal -->
They were seated back around the table. Davian sat slumped in his chair, dust clinging to his hair and collar, drinking straight from a water pitcher without bothering with a cup. No one stopped him.

<!-- paragraph:7149 para_id:00001BED style:Normal -->
Aldira tapped her pen thoughtfully against her notebook. “So that’s Archeon's technique. They possess a fairly useful alignment—some form of kinesis—but lack refined long-distance carving. That would severely limit their external projections. Which raises the obvious question: where do they source the power for their attacks?”

<!-- paragraph:7150 para_id:00001BEE style:Normal -->
“It isn’t the gauntlets,” Maya said immediately. “Serathis doesn’t wear them. They’re a crutch, not the foundation. Whatever they’re doing, it predates the equipment. It has to be something embedded in physical technique.” She slowly turned her head towards Harvick.

<!-- paragraph:7151 para_id:00001BEF style:Normal -->
It took Harvick a few seconds to realize everyone was looking at him. Harvick sighed dramatically and dropped into a chair. “I knew you’d eventually find a way to pry this out of me.”

<!-- paragraph:7152 para_id:00001BF0 style:Normal -->
Maya folded her arms.

<!-- paragraph:7153 para_id:00001BF1 style:Normal -->
“Fine,” he continued. “It sounds like—maybe, possibly—Archeon is using some of the same foundational techniques I learned in Naeroth.”

<!-- paragraph:7154 para_id:00001BF2 style:Normal -->
Davian looked up, interest sharpening through the fatigue.

<!-- paragraph:7155 para_id:00001BF3 style:Normal -->
“I started suspecting it earlier,” Harvick went on. “Some of the things Davian showed me—things his mother taught him—felt related. Not the techniques themselves, but… precursors. Preparatory movements. Pre-techniques.”

<!-- paragraph:7156 para_id:00001BF4 style:Normal -->
He leaned forward, elbows on the table.

<!-- paragraph:7157 para_id:00001BF5 style:Normal -->
“If his mother learned those at Archeon, then it suggests they teach every hopeful the same physical framework. Build the body first. Drill the mind through motion. That way, if a Shroud manifests later—or hereditary magic awakens—they’re not starting from nothing. They’re layering magic on top of deeply ingrained structure.”

<!-- paragraph:7158 para_id:00001BF6 style:Normal -->
Maya leaned back slightly, considering the implications. “Isn’t this almost absurdly lucky? The form of magic Davian needs to use is rooted in something he already knows. And he didn’t grow up in Archeon.”

<!-- paragraph:7159 para_id:00001BF7 style:Normal -->
Aldira’s expression sharpened rather than softened. “It isn’t lucky. It’s deliberate.”

<!-- paragraph:7160 para_id:00001BF8 style:Normal -->
Davian looked between them.

<!-- paragraph:7161 para_id:00001BF9 style:Normal -->
“Your mother escaped Archeon,” Aldira continued, “after learning foundational techniques that, as it happens, would perfectly support someone of Archeon blood in manifesting and controlling hereditary magic later in life—provided they received the right linchpin instruction.” She shook her head slowly. “That is far too convenient to be an accident.”

<!-- paragraph:7162 para_id:00001BFA style:Normal -->
Davian’s jaw tightened. “What are you saying? That my mother is still working for Archeon? She hates them.”

<!-- paragraph:7163 para_id:00001BFB style:Normal -->
“I’m saying,” Aldira corrected evenly, “that she didn’t escape Archeon - they let her go.”

<!-- paragraph:7164 para_id:00001BFC style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7165 para_id:00001BFD style:Normal -->
Maya stood over her desk, fingers smudged with ink, staring down at a half-finished zan lattice.

<!-- paragraph:7166 para_id:00001BFE style:Normal -->
“If I can isolate the Registry’s detection to passive only,” she muttered, sketching another loop, “and focus it around the entrances… maybe we could power it with zan sinks instead of a centralized feed.”

<!-- paragraph:7167 para_id:00001BFF style:Normal -->
Behind her, Erynn cleared her throat. “So he really didn’t take that well, huh.”

<!-- paragraph:7168 para_id:00001C00 style:Normal -->
Maya didn’t look up. “It’s Aldira’s fault. She should have known that was a sore spot for him, and to be honest I’m sure that Archeon has something terrible planned for her. But we can’t just go storming into another High Clade’s citadel with no plan, not with Niall in the weakened state that he’s in.”

<!-- paragraph:7169 para_id:00001C01 style:Normal -->
She added another stroke to the diagram, then crossed it out and redrew it.

<!-- paragraph:7170 para_id:00001C02 style:Normal -->
Erynn stepped closer. “How many times have you knocked Davian out now?”

<!-- paragraph:7171 para_id:00001C03 style:Normal -->
Maya dropped the charcoal onto the desk with more force than necessary. “You make it sound terrible. He has a Shroud now and some very half-formed ideas about how to use hereditary techniques. I either knock him out or he barrels through a dozen guards and escapes. This is the best containment option we have. And it won’t even work once he’s strong enough to bend his cell bars.” She threw her hands up. “Besides, we have healers.”

<!-- paragraph:7172 para_id:00001C04 style:Normal -->
“I don’t know,” Erynn said mildly. “I’ve heard that even if you heal head injuries, they’re still not good for you.”

<!-- paragraph:7173 para_id:00001C05 style:Normal -->
Maya pressed her palms against the desk and leaned forward. “What else am I supposed to do? He’s certainly not being reasonable.”

<!-- paragraph:7174 para_id:00001C06 style:Normal -->
“Or,” Erynn said, drawing the word out, “maybe you just like them dumb and handsome.”

<!-- paragraph:7175 para_id:00001C07 style:Normal -->
Maya straightened sharply. “Erynn, this man is an underclass thief-assassin related to a High Clade full of thugs. He has some unnameable dark magic threaded through his skin and mind that periodically tells him to kill my father.”

<!-- paragraph:7176 para_id:00001C08 style:Normal -->
Erynn nodded thoughtfully. “You know, when you put it like that… I can see why you like him.”

<!-- paragraph:7177 para_id:00001C09 style:Normal -->
“This isn’t a joke.” Maya turned fully toward her. “Just because he’s—” she caught herself, “—very handsome and loves his family does not erase everything else. We have a temporary agreement because he’s too stubborn to accept that he should simply tell us everything and be grateful we haven’t executed him.” She paused. “I don’t like him, we need him.”

<!-- paragraph:7178 para_id:00001C0A style:Normal -->
Erynn folded her arms, clearly unconvinced.

<!-- paragraph:7179 para_id:00001C0B style:Normal -->
“When this is over,” Maya continued, pacing now, “he can go back to hiding in the city and committing petty heists to fund low-quality tattoos.”

<!-- paragraph:7180 para_id:00001C0C style:Normal -->
Maya turned back to her desk, staring at the half-finished lattice.

<!-- paragraph:7181 para_id:00001C0D style:Normal -->
“Maybe I should go back to Zirene,” she muttered. “Have her modify the metals so they activate if he leaves the citadel grounds.”

<!-- paragraph:7182 para_id:00001C0E style:Normal -->
Erynn waited for a few moments, then she said quietly, “You know… it’s okay to want something for yourself. Not everything has to be ‘my Clade’ this and ‘my magic’ that. You’re really just going to toss him out when this is all over?”

<!-- paragraph:7183 para_id:00001C0F style:Normal -->
Maya tossed her papers to the side and whirled on Erynn.

<!-- paragraph:7184 para_id:00001C10 style:Normal -->
“Toss him out?” she said incredulously. “He’s the one who ran away from me. Everything you saw before was just him trying to get close enough that I’d be blinded to his true nature.”

<!-- paragraph:7185 para_id:00001C11 style:Normal -->
Erynn tilted her head. “And what is his true nature?”

<!-- paragraph:7186 para_id:00001C12 style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms. “A thief. A murderer. Someone who infiltrated our citadel under false pretenses and nearly killed my father.”

<!-- paragraph:7187 para_id:00001C13 style:Normal -->
“And what is yours?” Erynn asked calmly.

<!-- paragraph:7188 para_id:00001C14 style:Normal -->
Maya stared at her.

<!-- paragraph:7189 para_id:00001C15 style:Normal -->
“Don’t even pretend we’re the same,” Maya said finally. “I know who I am now. And I know that he is someone who is… not to be trusted. I am the heir to the most powerful High Clade in the known world, and he is some kind of cast-off scion from the enemies who envy our position.”

<!-- paragraph:7190 para_id:00001C16 style:Normal -->
Erynn raised an eyebrow. “And what about when he saved your brother?”

<!-- paragraph:7191 para_id:00001C17 style:Normal -->
Maya threw her hands up. “Everyone keeps bringing that up! Over and over. Yes, I’m grateful he did one good thing once, but that doesn’t erase everything else he’s done.”

<!-- paragraph:7192 para_id:00001C18 style:Normal -->
Erynn opened her mouth to respond, but Maya cut her off.

<!-- paragraph:7193 para_id:00001C19 style:Normal -->
“Let it go, Erynn. If Niall can’t be the leader we need, then I have to be. And I can’t afford silly distractions.”

<!-- paragraph:7194 para_id:00001C1A style:Normal -->
Erynn stared at her for a moment, then flopped sideways across a nearby chair so she was facing away from Maya.

<!-- paragraph:7195 para_id:00001C1B style:Normal -->
For a while the room was quiet except for the rustle of papers on the desk.

<!-- paragraph:7196 para_id:00001C1C style:Normal -->
Then Maya heard Erynn muttering under her breath.

<!-- paragraph:7197 para_id:00001C1D style:Normal -->
“It just seemed like you were less grouchy when things were going well with him.”

<!-- paragraph:7198 para_id:00001C1E style:Normal -->
Maya pretended not to hear.

<!-- paragraph:7202 para_id:00001C22 style:Title -->
# 🤱 Chapter 35

<!-- paragraph:7203 para_id:00001C23 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 35

<!-- paragraph:7205 para_id:00001C25 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7206 para_id:00001C26 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s fist drove forward and sank into the man’s short ribs. She felt the impact travel cleanly through her knuckles, into bone, into the soft resistance beneath. Something gave with a dull, wet crack, and his breath burst out in a strangled grunt as the ribs shifted under the force.

<!-- paragraph:7207 para_id:00001C27 style:Normal -->
Before he could fall, she pivoted, sweeping his legs out from under him with the back of her calf. A strike came from behind. She spun and dipped under it without looking, stepping inside the arc of the blow, and drove her elbow upward into the attacker’s diaphragm. The motion started at her heels, rolled through her hips and shoulders, and ended in the point of bone. She felt the air leave the woman’s lungs in a spasming gasp as she folded and collapsed to the ground.

<!-- paragraph:7208 para_id:00001C28 style:Normal -->
Kelyra straightened, breathing hard but steady. The remaining three assailants broke off and circled her warily, their attempt to overwhelm her stymied in a heartbeat. In the dim light of the arena, many bodies lay twisted where they had fallen, some groaning, some not moving at all.

<!-- paragraph:7209 para_id:00001C29 style:Normal -->
The chamber was cut deep into stone, the floor a wide pit filled with coarse sand. Iron rings lined the walls, and torches burned in brackets instead of zanlight, their uneven glow throwing long shadows across the bodies.

<!-- paragraph:7210 para_id:00001C2A style:Normal -->
To her left, an iron grate rattled and slid upward with a smooth, well-oiled scrape. Two more figures stepped through the opening and dropped into the arena, boots sinking slightly as they landed. They took their places without speaking.

<!-- paragraph:7211 para_id:00001C2B style:Normal -->
Kelyra reached for the strip of filthy cloth wrapped around her right hand and tightened it, pulling the fabric hard until the bones of her fist felt braced again. The knuckles were split open, skin slick with blood and sand; joints throbbed from the pressure of repeated impact. She flexed her fingers once, testing the pain, then let her hand fall back into guard.

<!-- paragraph:7212 para_id:00001C2C style:Normal -->
A dry voice cut across the arena. “Now, I have to say, this may be one of the more successful experiments so far.”

<!-- paragraph:7213 para_id:00001C2D style:Normal -->
Kelyra turned sharply. Above the pit, standing at the edge of the stone lip, Serathis stepped into the torchlight. Her hands were folded behind her back as she looked down into the arena, expression thoughtful rather than impressed, as if she were studying a piece of machinery that had begun to function slightly closer to its intended design.

<!-- paragraph:7214 para_id:00001C2E style:Normal -->
Footsteps pounded through the sand behind her. Kelyra heard them before the first grain shifted under their boots, her racing heart dragging the world into slow motion. Every movement sharpened, every sound stretched thin.

<!-- paragraph:7215 para_id:00001C2F style:Normal -->
An experiment? Two of them were coming fast, trying to take advantage of her focus on Serathis. One from the left, one from directly behind. They thought she was distracted. How is this an experiment?

<!-- paragraph:7216 para_id:00001C30 style:Normal -->
She waited. Waited longer than instinct wanted her to, forcing herself to hold still until the last possible instant, until both of them committed their weight forward. Then she moved. She slipped between the first pair of blows, turning sideways so one strike passed in front of her face and the other cut harmlessly through the space where she had been. Her shoulder slammed into one attacker’s chest as she pivoted, using his own momentum to drive him off balance and straight into the path of the second.

<!-- paragraph:7217 para_id:00001C31 style:Normal -->
They collided hard enough to stagger both. The other three combatants leaped into the fray. No time to think now. Kelyra drove forward, staying inside their reach, never letting the circle close. A knee into a thigh. A palm into a throat. Her heel crushed down on a foot, then snapped up into a knee. She hammered her palm into an ear, sending a man reeling. Someone grabbed for her shoulder and she ducked, driving her elbow back into a diaphragm hard enough to make the body behind her fold.

<!-- paragraph:7218 para_id:00001C32 style:Normal -->
She kept moving, never stopping long enough to be surrounded, never striking the same target twice when a different one was closer. Nose. Jaw. Knee. Throat. Each hit bought a heartbeat. Each heartbeat kept her alive.

<!-- paragraph:7219 para_id:00001C33 style:Normal -->
When the last one fell, she stayed in guard for another second, waiting for the next rush that never came. Silence crept back into the arena, broken only by the crackle of torches and the ragged sound of her breathing. Five more bodies lay in the sand.

<!-- paragraph:7220 para_id:00001C34 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s vision swam. She forced herself to stay upright for a moment longer, then the iron grate beside the wall failed to move, and the tension finally drained out of her legs. She dropped into a seated crouch where she stood, elbows resting on her knees, hands hanging loose as they trembled with exhaustion and adrenaline.

<!-- paragraph:7221 para_id:00001C35 style:Normal -->
She tilted her head back toward the edge of the pit.

<!-- paragraph:7222 para_id:00001C36 style:Normal -->
“Why?” she demanded, voice rough. “Why waste all of these on a pointless fight with me?”

<!-- paragraph:7223 para_id:00001C37 style:Normal -->
Serathis’ face twitched towards a smile. “The fact that the fight was so pointless,” she said at last, “means there was nothing to waste.”

<!-- paragraph:7224 para_id:00001C38 style:Normal -->
Kelyra let out a slow breath, forcing her lungs to settle. She gestured weakly at the bodies scattered across the sand.

<!-- paragraph:7225 para_id:00001C39 style:Normal -->
“You don’t even try to train them to fight together. They get in each other’s way. It’s almost worse for them to come at me as a group.”

<!-- paragraph:7226 para_id:00001C3A style:Normal -->
Serathis laughed softly and lowered herself to sit on the edge of the arena, legs dangling over the drop with an ease that looked almost girlish despite the sharp lines of her ancient face and the bone-white braid hanging over her shoulder.

<!-- paragraph:7227 para_id:00001C3B style:Normal -->
“That isn’t true, daughter,” she said. “You’re a genius for martial combat. Always were. But you never manifested a Shroud while you were here, so I let you go.”

<!-- paragraph:7228 para_id:00001C3C style:Normal -->
She tilted her head, studying Kelyra like a craftsman inspecting a blade. “And it worked. You became exactly what I was hoping for.”

<!-- paragraph:7229 para_id:00001C3D style:Normal -->
Kelyra pushed herself to her feet, legs shaking but holding. “You’re lying,” she snapped. “I escaped. I became my own person with my own skills. Nothing I have came from you.”

<!-- paragraph:7230 para_id:00001C3E style:Normal -->
Serathis rolled her eyes. “Still a rebellious child, I see. Even after having children of your own.”

<!-- paragraph:7231 para_id:00001C3F style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s jaw tightened.

<!-- paragraph:7232 para_id:00001C40 style:Normal -->
“But he found you,” Serathis went on calmly, “just as he found me. And now you’ll finish the process I began all those years ago, when I let you run after you’d received the instruction you needed.”

<!-- paragraph:7233 para_id:00001C41 style:Normal -->
Kelyra said nothing. She just stood there, breathing hard, fists clenched, forcing the words out of her head. She’s just trying to get in my mind. She only ever speaks in half-truths and manipulation.

<!-- paragraph:7234 para_id:00001C42 style:Normal -->
Serathis watched her in silence, head tilted slightly. “Nothing?” she said at last. “No response?”

<!-- paragraph:7235 para_id:00001C43 style:Normal -->
Kelyra kept her eyes on the sand in front of her, forcing her breathing to stay steady.

<!-- paragraph:7236 para_id:00001C44 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Serathis went on lightly, “it seems that—as you so astutely pointed out—none of this has been enough of a challenge for you. So I suppose we’ll have to make things a bit more difficult, won’t we?”

<!-- paragraph:7237 para_id:00001C45 style:Normal -->
A figure stepped into the torchlight beside her. The woman standing there couldn’t have been more than a few years into adulthood, her hair pulled back tight, expression rigid with the kind of focus Kelyra remembered all too well from her own time here.

<!-- paragraph:7238 para_id:00001C46 style:Normal -->
Serathis didn’t even look at her. She simply flicked her fingers toward the arena.

<!-- paragraph:7239 para_id:00001C47 style:Normal -->
The woman jumped. She landed in the sand with controlled precision, knees bending to absorb the impact, and as she straightened Kelyra saw the faint, shifting sheen around her body—the unmistakable distortion of a Shroud, barely visible in the uneven torchlight but impossible to miss once you knew what to look for.

<!-- paragraph:7240 para_id:00001C48 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s stomach tightened. The woman lifted her head, eyes locking onto hers. Recognition hit a heartbeat later.

<!-- paragraph:7241 para_id:00001C49 style:Normal -->
“…Lethira?” Kelyra said, the name coming out before she could stop it.

<!-- paragraph:7242 para_id:00001C4A style:Normal -->
Lethira didn’t answer. She only shifted her weight and raised her hands, settling into a tight, disciplined fighting stance. Her face was rigid, jaw clenched, eyes fixed on Kelyra with an expression that held both anger and something colder beneath it.

<!-- paragraph:7243 para_id:00001C4B style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s mind raced. Children born in Archeon were never raised by their mothers. Never even allowed to know them. Taken at birth, handed over to the training halls, returned to the compound at five years old to be shaped with the rest. Not raised. Trained.

<!-- paragraph:7244 para_id:00001C4C style:Normal -->
That was why she had run the moment she realized she was pregnant with Selina.

<!-- paragraph:7245 para_id:00001C4D style:Normal -->
Lethira’s face was older now, harder, but Kelyra could still see it—the same narrow lines, the same solemn eyes from the girl who had stood in front of her years ago, small fists clenched, trying to hold a stance for longer than her muscles could bear. She remembered correcting that stance, pushing her shoulders down, turning her hips, telling her to stop thinking and let the motion come from the ground.

<!-- paragraph:7246 para_id:00001C4E style:Normal -->
The child had been soft then. What stood in front of her now looked like the same shape beaten into a different purpose, the way a workman’s knife gets ground thinner and thinner until it holds an edge whether the steel is good or not.

<!-- paragraph:7247 para_id:00001C4F style:Normal -->
Lethira moved. She came forward in a blur, sand spraying under her feet, the shimmer of her Shroud warping the torchlight around her as she closed the distance with impossible speed.

<!-- paragraph:7248 para_id:00001C50 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s heart slammed once, hard, and the world slowed again. She pivoted at the last instant, narrowly avoiding the lethal strike. Lethira’s hand cut through the space where Kelyra’s throat had been, her whole body behind the blow, the pressure of her passing buffeting Kelyra’s skin.

<!-- paragraph:7249 para_id:00001C51 style:Normal -->
Kelyra twisted, sliding away, already searching for space, for balance, to keep out of that deadly reach. Lethira turned with her, faster than any normal fighter could have managed, the Shroud carrying her movement through the turn as if the ground itself pushed her forward.

<!-- paragraph:7250 para_id:00001C52 style:Normal -->
Above them, Serathis spoke, almost lazily.

<!-- paragraph:7251 para_id:00001C53 style:Normal -->
“Oh, and if you can’t beat her. I’ll kill that husband of yours. Whatever his name is.” The next rush came so fast Kelyra barely had time to register the words.

<!-- paragraph:7252 para_id:00001C54 style:Normal -->
Defeat a Shrouded magus with my bare hands? For a heartbeat the thought almost froze her where she stood, the sheer unfairness of it crashing down on her harder than any blow. There was no time to argue with it.

<!-- paragraph:7253 para_id:00001C55 style:Normal -->
Lethira lunged again, speed snapping the air like a whip, and Kelyra threw herself sideways, rolling across the sand as the strike tore past where her chest had been. The girl’s technique was still rigid, still bound to the forms she’d been taught, and that was the only reason Kelyra stayed alive.

<!-- paragraph:7254 para_id:00001C56 style:Normal -->
She scrambled to her feet, mind racing. There has to be a way to win. Serathis wouldn’t do all this for no reason.

<!-- paragraph:7255 para_id:00001C57 style:Normal -->
Another charge. Another last-second pivot, sand spraying under her heels as she twisted clear.

<!-- paragraph:7256 para_id:00001C58 style:Normal -->
Or does she just want to punish me? To humiliate me? For a moment the weight of it almost drove her to her knees. Lethira streaked past again, turning too wide, her Shroud carrying her farther than she intended.

<!-- paragraph:7257 para_id:00001C59 style:Normal -->
Above them, Serathis’s voice cut down into the pit. “All right, that’s enough, Lethira. I know your teachers have been over this.” A faint note of irritation crept in. “Actually fight her. Don’t rely on your opening strike to win.”

<!-- paragraph:7258 para_id:00001C5A style:Normal -->
Lethira stopped, pivoted, and this time approached more carefully.

<!-- paragraph:7259 para_id:00001C5B style:Normal -->
Kelyra saw the change immediately. The girl’s shoulders lowered, her stance tightening as she forced herself back into discipline. Her jaw clenched, eyes locked forward, the same desperate focus Kelyra remembered from years ago. Still trying to please them.

<!-- paragraph:7260 para_id:00001C5C style:Normal -->
Lethira stepped in with the classic Archeon forms—clean, efficient, exactly as they had all been taught. Predictable in rhythm, predictable in angle. Kelyra knew every motion before it came, but knowing didn’t help the way it should have. Every instinct told her to meet the blows, to redirect, to break the line of force the way she always had. She couldn’t; a Shroud lay behind every strike. If she blocked wrong, her arm would shatter.

<!-- paragraph:7261 para_id:00001C5D style:Normal -->
She slipped the first punch, ducked the second, twisted away from a knee that would have crushed her ribs, forcing herself to fight against her own training, against every reflex drilled into her since childhood.

<!-- paragraph:7262 para_id:00001C5E style:Normal -->
Am I supposed to use the Archeon magic here? I barely know how. Even draining a little zan hurts.

<!-- paragraph:7263 para_id:00001C5F style:Normal -->
Serathis’s voice came again, sharp with amusement. “I can see you thinking much harder than you need to, granddaughter. It isn’t a secret. All you need is a Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:7264 para_id:00001C60 style:Normal -->
The words hit like a slap. Kelyra’s mind recoiled as if she’d touched burning iron.

<!-- paragraph:7265 para_id:00001C61 style:Normal -->
No.

<!-- paragraph:7266 para_id:00001C62 style:Normal -->
If she had a Shroud, they would keep her.
 If she had a Shroud, they would take the child.
 If she had a Shroud, she would never leave this place again.

<!-- paragraph:7267 para_id:00001C63 style:Normal -->
They already have you, part of her tried to argue.

<!-- paragraph:7268 para_id:00001C64 style:Normal -->
The fear surged up anyway, raw and instinctive, deeper than thought.

<!-- paragraph:7269 para_id:00001C65 style:Normal -->
If I have a Shroud they’ll know. They’ll take everything. I have to hide. I have to hide it.

<!-- paragraph:7270 para_id:00001C66 style:Normal -->
She reached inward anyway, groping blindly for the place she had always refused to touch, the place Serathis insisted was there.

<!-- paragraph:7271 para_id:00001C67 style:Normal -->
For an instant she felt something—pressure, heat, the edge of something coiled inside her mind—and nausea crashed over her so violently she almost doubled over.

<!-- paragraph:7272 para_id:00001C68 style:Normal -->
Lethira was already moving. Kelyra stumbled back, vision blurring, barely managing to slip the first strike. The second clipped her shoulder, the force behind it slamming through her body and knocking her off balance. She tried to roll with it, but the third blow caught her side and sent her tumbling across the sand. She hit hard, breath driven out of her lungs, the world spinning as she struggled to push herself back up before the next attack landed.

<!-- paragraph:7273 para_id:00001C69 style:Normal -->
Before Kelyra could get her knees under her, Lethira was on her. The impact drove her face-first into the sand. A hand clamped on the back of her head with crushing strength, Shroud-backed force pinning her down so completely she couldn’t even twist her shoulders free. Grit filled her mouth, her nose, her eyes, the weight on her skull grinding her cheek into the dirt until she thought her jaw would crack.

<!-- paragraph:7274 para_id:00001C6A style:Normal -->
She tried to push up and nothing happened. Her arms shook uselessly against strength she couldn’t match. Hot breath hissed in her ear.

<!-- paragraph:7275 para_id:00001C6B style:Normal -->
“I was a child,” Lethira snarled, voice shaking with something far past anger. “And you left me. You ran away and left me all alone.”

<!-- paragraph:7276 para_id:00001C6C style:Normal -->
Kelyra tried to turn her head, tried to get her mouth clear enough to breathe, but the sand packed tighter under the pressure, choking off the air. Her chest spasmed, lungs burning as she sucked in nothing but dust.

<!-- paragraph:7277 para_id:00001C6D style:Normal -->
Above them, Serathis spoke, the same dry disappointment in her voice as before.

<!-- paragraph:7278 para_id:00001C6E style:Normal -->
“All right. That’s enough.”

<!-- paragraph:7279 para_id:00001C6F style:Normal -->
The pressure didn’t lift. Lethira leaned harder, grinding Kelyra’s face deeper into the sand. “You abandoned me,” she hissed. “You left me here and I stayed. All these years. Do you know what that’s like? I did everything they asked. Everything.” Her voice cracked, then sharpened again. “And she still wants you more than me.”

<!-- paragraph:7280 para_id:00001C70 style:Normal -->
Black crept in at the edges of Kelyra’s vision. Her chest convulsed, body trying to breathe even though there was no air to take. Her thoughts scattered, slipping out of her grasp as the world narrowed to the crushing weight on her skull and the suffocating heat in her lungs.

<!-- paragraph:7281 para_id:00001C71 style:Normal -->
She was going to pass out. Maybe die. Then the pressure vanished.

<!-- paragraph:7282 para_id:00001C72 style:Normal -->
For a split second she felt weightless, tossed into the air by some titanic force to bounce across the sandy floor, spinning onto her side, coughing sand and blood as she dragged in a ragged breath. Through watering eyes she saw a blur of motion—too fast to track—followed by the sickening crack of stone.

<!-- paragraph:7283 para_id:00001C73 style:Normal -->
Lethira’s body hit the arena wall face-first with a force that cratered the rock, dust exploding outward from the impact.

<!-- paragraph:7284 para_id:00001C74 style:Normal -->
Serathis stood there, one hand still extended where she had seized the girl. She let the limp body drop without looking at it and turned back toward the center of the pit.

<!-- paragraph:7285 para_id:00001C75 style:Normal -->
“So many disappointments in the same day,” she said calmly.

<!-- paragraph:7286 para_id:00001C76 style:Normal -->
She gestured lazily toward the crumpled form on the ground. “One granddaughter with no talent at all, but the will to do whatever is necessary.”

<!-- paragraph:7287 para_id:00001C77 style:Normal -->
Her finger shifted, pointing at Kelyra. “And the other bursting with talent, but no motivation.”

<!-- paragraph:7288 para_id:00001C78 style:Normal -->
The words reached Kelyra through a haze. The arena swayed above her, the torches blurring into streaks of orange as the last of her adrenaline burned away. Her limbs felt like they belonged to someone else, heavy and slow, and she let herself sink back into the sand, staring up at the ceiling she could barely keep in focus.

<!-- paragraph:7289 para_id:00001C79 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s face appeared above her, pale in the torchlight, the sharp lines of it swimming in and out of clarity as Kelyra fought to stay conscious. She wasn’t even sure the woman was really there.

<!-- paragraph:7290 para_id:00001C7A style:Normal -->
“And you were always my favorite, too,” Serathis said softly.

<!-- paragraph:7291 para_id:00001C7B style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7293 para_id:00001C7D style:Normal -->
Full consciousness trickled back in pieces. First the ache, deep in her bones, the dull throb of muscles pushed past their limits. Then the smell—dust, oil, damp stone. Then the sensation of something warm flowing through her veins that wasn’t her own strength.

<!-- paragraph:7294 para_id:00001C7E style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s eyes opened. She was seated, chained against a stone wall, wrists bound in iron cuffs that bit into her skin. A dim, uneven light flickered across the chamber, torches again instead of zanlight, their glow too weak to show more than rough stone and shadow. Somewhere close by, a familiar hummed softly, its glow fading as a healer pressed both hands against Kelyra’s ribs, finishing the transfer.

<!-- paragraph:7295 para_id:00001C7F style:Normal -->
The warmth ebbed. The healer stepped back, breathing hard, and wiped sweat from his forehead. Serathis stood a few paces away, watching. She made a small motion with her fingers. The healer bowed his head immediately and hurried out through a narrow passage, the familiar drifting after him like a dying ember.

<!-- paragraph:7296 para_id:00001C80 style:Normal -->
Serathis waited until the sound of his footsteps faded before speaking.

<!-- paragraph:7297 para_id:00001C81 style:Normal -->
“You should feel honored,” she said. “We can only afford to keep one healer around.”

<!-- paragraph:7298 para_id:00001C82 style:Normal -->
Kelyra swallowed, her throat raw, her body heavy. She did not feel honored. Serathis stepped closer and looked her over like she was inspecting a repaired tool.

<!-- paragraph:7299 para_id:00001C83 style:Normal -->
“Where is Jalen?” Kelyra choked out. “You said you would kill him.”

<!-- paragraph:7300 para_id:00001C84 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s expression softened with infuriating ease. “Of course he’s still alive. I would never do something like that - I was only trying to motivate you to reach your potential.” She tilted her head, almost tenderly. “You make me do these things because I love you.”

<!-- paragraph:7301 para_id:00001C85 style:Normal -->
Kelyra stared at her.

<!-- paragraph:7302 para_id:00001C86 style:Normal -->
Serathis stepped closer. “I killed that girl because I love you too. Do you understand? Lethira had no real value beyond obedience. You do. Everything I do here is for your sake.”

<!-- paragraph:7303 para_id:00001C87 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s stomach turned.

<!-- paragraph:7304 para_id:00001C88 style:Normal -->
The chains unlocked with a click but remained looped through the wall-rings. She flexed her wrists and frowned at the iron. “Then why am I chained up?”

<!-- paragraph:7305 para_id:00001C89 style:Normal -->
“For your safety, of course.” The answer came so smoothly it was almost worse than if Serathis had laughed. “Get up, granddaughter. Your body is whole again. Prove you deserve the effort.”

<!-- paragraph:7306 para_id:00001C8A style:Normal -->
Kelyra pushed herself upright, swaying as blood rushed back through exhausted muscles. She got one foot under her, then the other, and forced herself to stand against the stone. “Stop calling me that,” she said hoarsely. “We both know I’m not really your granddaughter.”

<!-- paragraph:7307 para_id:00001C8B style:Normal -->
Serathis’ eyes narrowed, “If I say you are my granddaughter, then you are.”

<!-- paragraph:7308 para_id:00001C8C style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s jaw tightened. “How old are you? How many generations are there between us?”

<!-- paragraph:7309 para_id:00001C8D style:Normal -->
Serathis smiled faintly.

<!-- paragraph:7310 para_id:00001C8E style:Normal -->
“That,” she said, “is another good question, daughter. How long can a High Clade magus live?”

<!-- paragraph:7311 para_id:00001C8F style:Normal -->
Kelyra said nothing.

<!-- paragraph:7312 para_id:00001C90 style:Normal -->
Serathis waved one hand as if brushing the matter aside. “We don’t need to concern ourselves with numbers. Not generations, not children. You may not be exactly my granddaughter in the narrow way you mean it, but you are my descendant, and that is enough. The generations between us are irrelevant.”

<!-- paragraph:7313 para_id:00001C91 style:Normal -->
Kelyra frowned. “How can something like that be irrelevant?”

<!-- paragraph:7314 para_id:00001C92 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s smile faded. She looked at Kelyra for a long moment, her eyes sharp now, no amusement left in them.

<!-- paragraph:7315 para_id:00001C93 style:Normal -->
“Because we want the same thing, granddaughter,” she said quietly. “And we will both keep at it as long as it takes until we get it.”

<!-- paragraph:7316 para_id:00001C94 style:Normal -->
She turned away, pacing slowly across the chamber as she spoke.

<!-- paragraph:7317 para_id:00001C95 style:Normal -->
“When he came to me with the gift, I was nothing. A runaway. Just like you. And just like you, I didn’t even know I had been released on purpose.” Her mouth twisted slightly. “I took what I was given, and then I came back. And I built this Clade into its position from nothing.”

<!-- paragraph:7318 para_id:00001C96 style:Normal -->
She stopped and looked back at Kelyra. “And he came to you too.”

<!-- paragraph:7319 para_id:00001C97 style:Normal -->
The air seemed to tighten. Serathis’s expression hardened, irritation flashing through her voice.

<!-- paragraph:7320 para_id:00001C98 style:Normal -->
“And all that stands in the way now is your weakness. Your lack of will. After everything you’ve been given—by me, by him. It’s disgusting.”

<!-- paragraph:7321 para_id:00001C99 style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s brow furrowed. “What are you talking about?” she said. “Who is ‘he’? Getting in the way of what?”

<!-- paragraph:7322 para_id:00001C9A style:Normal -->
Serathis clicked her tongue in annoyance. “You must have forgotten, but here, you have to earn answers.” She stepped closer, stopping just within arm’s reach.

<!-- paragraph:7323 para_id:00001C9B style:Normal -->
“That means you need to work for them. Think for yourself for once.” Her eyes narrowed. “Ask the right question. What makes a High Clade a High Clade? And how could a Low Clade ever rise high enough to become one?”

<!-- paragraph:7324 para_id:00001C9C style:Normal -->
Before Kelyra could answer, hurried footsteps echoed down the passage. A young messenger skidded to a stop at the entrance, breathing hard. “I’m sorry,” he said, voice tight with fear, “but they just… refused to be stopped.”

<!-- paragraph:7325 para_id:00001C9D style:Normal -->
Serathis turned sharply. Two figures were already striding down the corridor toward them, ignoring the guards who trailed helplessly behind. Kelyra recognized them at once, even though she had only ever seen them from a distance, and never this close.

<!-- paragraph:7326 para_id:00001C9E style:Normal -->
Evana Drakhal moved with the effortless poise of someone who had never once in her life been uncertain of her place in the world, tall and perfectly composed, her sleeveless waistcoat worked with fine gold filigree over fitted, intricately embroidered trousers. Beside her walked her son, Caius, dressed in the same immaculate style, every line of his clothing precise and deliberate.

<!-- paragraph:7327 para_id:00001C9F style:Normal -->
Even here, underground, in torchlight and stone dust, they looked as if they had stepped out of a formal audience hall.

<!-- paragraph:7328 para_id:00001CA0 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s eyes narrowed. “Now isn’t a good time.”

<!-- paragraph:7329 para_id:00001CA1 style:Normal -->
Evana did not slow. “Well, we came to bring you important news,” she said pleasantly, as if they were meeting at a garden reception instead of a dungeon corridor. “But you weren’t… available. After a few minutes we decided we should come find you. In case you needed—”

<!-- paragraph:7330 para_id:00001CA2 style:Normal -->
Her gaze flicked across the chamber in a single sweep, taking in the chains on the wall, the healer’s fading traces, and Kelyra standing there, still unsteady.

<!-- paragraph:7331 para_id:00001CA3 style:Normal -->
“—assistance.”

<!-- paragraph:7332 para_id:00001CA4 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s expression didn’t change, but the air around her seemed to tighten. “You should know,” she said coldly, “that I can take care of myself.”

<!-- paragraph:7333 para_id:00001CA5 style:Normal -->
Evana’s smile thinned, just slightly.

<!-- paragraph:7334 para_id:00001CA6 style:Normal -->
“Well. Since your agents failed so spectacularly to bring in the thief who stole the Rezin Heart, it has made us wonder about the overall strength and merits of Archeon as a whole.”

<!-- paragraph:7335 para_id:00001CA7 style:Normal -->
Behind her, Caius smirked openly.

<!-- paragraph:7336 para_id:00001CA8 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s jaw flexed. “The Rezins helped him,” she said sharply. “Bodies were burned at the scene. It’s the only explanation.”

<!-- paragraph:7337 para_id:00001CA9 style:Normal -->
Evana rolled her eyes with theatrical patience. “So you say. Of course it’s eminently plausible that the Rezins would come ‘rescue’ the thief that robbed them. I simply wanted to verify your information myself. To make sure everything is… proceeding in order.”

<!-- paragraph:7338 para_id:00001CAA style:Normal -->
For an instant, Kelyra caught something under the polished tone. A flicker of tension. Concern, quickly hidden.

<!-- paragraph:7339 para_id:00001CAB style:Normal -->
Serathis saw it too. “Oh,” she said softly. “So that’s what this is about. You’re worried that if the assassin is more capable than you were led to believe, then perhaps Niall isn’t as weak as you hoped. Not weak enough for what you really want. A completely one-sided fight where your victory is assured.”

<!-- paragraph:7340 para_id:00001CAC style:Normal -->
Evana laughed, light and dismissive. “That’s ridiculous. Niall is the most powerful magus alive. Which is exactly why the man you failed to capture is a thief, not an assassin.”

<!-- paragraph:7341 para_id:00001CAD style:Normal -->
Serathis’s teeth ground together. “What was so important,” she said, each word tight, “that you needed to come down here and interrupt me?”

<!-- paragraph:7342 para_id:00001CAE style:Normal -->
Caius answered before Evana could. “Our agents here informed us they identified yet another one of your workers passing information to the Rezins.”

<!-- paragraph:7343 para_id:00001CAF style:Normal -->
Evana shot him a quick, irritated look, then stepped smoothly back into control of the conversation. “Of course, we have a few agents assisting you during this transitional period,” she said. “You can’t be accustomed to having anyone else take interest in what happens inside your walls.”

<!-- paragraph:7344 para_id:00001CB0 style:Normal -->
Serathis didn’t blink. “Then give the information to my spymaster,” she said. “She will pursue the appropriate course of action.”

<!-- paragraph:7345 para_id:00001CB1 style:Normal -->
Caius spoke again, almost cheerfully. “We already had him killed. You know how these things work. You don’t investigate spies. You eliminate them immediately. Drive up the cost until the other side stops sending them.”

<!-- paragraph:7346 para_id:00001CB2 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s hands clenched at her sides, knuckles whitening. “You come into my citadel,” she said slowly, “and make decisions about my affairs for me.”

<!-- paragraph:7347 para_id:00001CB3 style:Normal -->
Evana lifted both hands slightly, smoothing the moment away. “We were only trying to help a… less-fortunate ally,” she said. “It’s a well-established doctrine. Life is never cheap. Even the Rezins can’t afford to keep paying the price forever.”

<!-- paragraph:7348 para_id:00001CB4 style:Normal -->
Serathis lifted one hand and pointed back down the corridor. “Get out,” she said flatly. “Go back to your political maneuvering. Or whatever it is you do at your expensive parties.”

<!-- paragraph:7349 para_id:00001CB5 style:Normal -->
Caius stopped. For a moment he just stared at her, clearly not used to being spoken to that way. The smirk vanished, replaced by a tight, offended glare. A faint glow flickered around his fingers, the air near his hands distorting as his magic stirred in response to his temper.

<!-- paragraph:7350 para_id:00001CB6 style:Normal -->
Serathis didn’t move. She only smiled, lips pulled thin over clenched teeth. “Try it,” she said softly. “See for yourself why our plan for Niall involves him coming here.”

<!-- paragraph:7351 para_id:00001CB7 style:Normal -->
Caius’s eyes narrowed. “You’re not that dangerous once someone knows your tricks.”

<!-- paragraph:7352 para_id:00001CB8 style:Normal -->
Serathis’s expression sharpened into a matching smirk. “There’s always another trick.”

<!-- paragraph:7353 para_id:00001CB9 style:Normal -->
For a heartbeat the corridor felt too small, the torchlight wavering as if the air itself was waiting for something to break. Evana’s gaze flicked past them, scanning the hallway. At the far end, several Archeon magi stood in silence, their gauntlets catching the light as they watched without moving. She exhaled sharply.

<!-- paragraph:7354 para_id:00001CBA style:Normal -->
“Please, Caius,” she said, her tone suddenly edged with command. “Restrain yourself.”

<!-- paragraph:7355 para_id:00001CBB style:Normal -->
Her eyes shifted briefly down the corridor. “Not in front of her children.”

<!-- paragraph:7356 para_id:00001CBC style:Normal -->
Caius followed the glance, then seemed to understand exactly how surrounded he was. The tension in his shoulders held for another second before he let the energy die, the distortion around his hands fading as he forced himself to relax. He looked away, jaw tight, and stepped back. Evana’s smile returned at once, smooth and effortless.

<!-- paragraph:7357 para_id:00001CBD style:Normal -->
“Well,” she said lightly, “what a lively conversation between friends. I look forward to our next meeting.” Her eyes swept the chamber one more time, taking in everything before she turned on her heel. Over her shoulder she quipped “And remember to keep an eye out for any more spies.”

<!-- paragraph:7358 para_id:00001CBE style:Normal -->
The Archeon magi at the end of the corridor parted just enough to let them pass. Caius strode through without looking at any of them, his posture stiff with restrained anger, while Evana followed at an unhurried pace, as if she had never doubted for a moment that she could leave exactly when she chose.

<!-- paragraph:7359 para_id:00001CBF style:Normal -->
As the corridor emptied and the last of the footsteps faded, the chamber seemed suddenly smaller. Serathis turned as if the conversation were over and started down the hall without another glance back.

<!-- paragraph:7360 para_id:00001CC0 style:Normal -->
Kelyra pushed herself off the wall, her legs still unsteady but holding. “Why?” she called after her. “Serathis—what could you possibly have to gain from allying with someone who clearly thinks they’re far above you?”

<!-- paragraph:7361 para_id:00001CC1 style:Normal -->
Serathis stopped. For a moment she didn’t turn around. Then she looked back over her shoulder, her eyes hard now, the last trace of amusement gone.

<!-- paragraph:7362 para_id:00001CC2 style:Normal -->
“He told me,” she said, “always have a backup plan.”

<!-- paragraph:7363 para_id:00001CC3 style:Normal -->
Kelyra frowned. Serathis faced her fully. “If you insist on remaining as unmotivated as you are now,” she went on, voice cool, “then we must make use of the gifts you are ableto provide. Apparently, you have the ability to bear powerful children.”

<!-- paragraph:7364 para_id:00001CC4 style:Normal -->
Her gaze dropped briefly, taking Kelyra in with clinical detachment.

<!-- paragraph:7365 para_id:00001CC5 style:Normal -->
“Even with that non-Clade gutter trash you managed to produce the man who robbed Niall Rezin. Imagine what I could make with you under a proper breeding contract. With a Presented Drakhal magus.”

<!-- paragraph:7366 para_id:00001CC6 style:Normal -->
For a second the words didn’t register. Then Kelyra let out a short, incredulous laugh.

<!-- paragraph:7367 para_id:00001CC7 style:Normal -->
“That’s what they offered you?” she said. “Caius would never go through with something like that.”

<!-- paragraph:7368 para_id:00001CC8 style:Normal -->
Serathis stepped even closer and spoke, close enough that Kelyra could see the pale braids at her temples, the fine cracks in her skin, the cold gleam in her eyes.

<!-- paragraph:7369 para_id:00001CC9 style:Normal -->
“And that leads to another good question for you, daughter. Is Caius the only Presented Drakhal magus?”

<!-- paragraph:7370 para_id:00001CCA style:Normal -->
Kelyra’s breath caught, the implication just beginning to form—and something shifted under her skin.

<!-- paragraph:7371 para_id:00001CCB style:Normal -->
A faint crawling sensation ran along the old Ink on her arm. She had thought it was gone, burned out with the rest, nothing but scars now. Her head snapped up. Serathis was standing too close. For a moment Kelyra had the sickening certainty the Ink was reacting to her. To something in her.

<!-- paragraph:7372 para_id:00001CCC style:Normal -->
Serathis only watched her for another heartbeat, then turned away. She strode down the corridor without another word, her footsteps echoing against the stone, growing fainter with every step. The crawling sensation faded with the distance.

<!-- paragraph:7373 para_id:00001CCD style:Normal -->
Just before the sound of her footsteps disappeared, something brushed the edge of Kelyra’s thoughts. A whisper, like a faint touch of satin.

<!-- paragraph:7374 para_id:00001CCE style:Normal -->
…kill her.

<!-- paragraph:7375 para_id:00001CCF style:Title -->
# 🔪 Chapter 36

<!-- paragraph:7376 para_id:00001CD0 style:Heading2 -->
## Chapter 36

<!-- paragraph:7378 para_id:00001CD2 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7379 para_id:00001CD3 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s sword cut straight through Davian’s flickering Shroud and smacked into his ribs with a flat crack. Pain exploded through his side. Davian reeled back with a gasp, one hand flying down to his ribs. “Ow—you son of a skag. That really hurts!”

<!-- paragraph:7380 para_id:00001CD4 style:Normal -->
“Quit whining,” Harvick said. He rolled his shoulders and shifted lightly from foot to foot, the glow slowly fading from the zan on his obsidian sword. “I’m not even hitting you with the sharp side. You should be able to block that with your Shroud if it actually worked.”

<!-- paragraph:7381 para_id:00001CD5 style:Normal -->
Davian looked down at himself. His chest was bare, sweat-slick, and striped with long purple bruises in varying states of freshness. “It’s hard to focus on a Shroud,” he muttered, still rubbing his ribs, “when I’m constantly worried about getting smacked in the chest.”

<!-- paragraph:7382 para_id:00001CD6 style:Normal -->
Harvick rolled his eyes. “You know the point is that you’re able to do this while worried about getting smashed into a million pieces, or burned to a crisp, or whatever other nasty thing the other magus is trying to do to you.”

Davian bent, grabbed the curved practice sword off the stone, and straightened with a wince. “Can I at least use my knife?” he asked. “You know you’re way better at this than me.”

<!-- paragraph:7383 para_id:00001CD7 style:Normal -->
“Oh, no you don’t,” Harvick said immediately. “I didn’t spend all that time teaching you how to use a sword just so you could go back to using a knife the second you figured out how to use magic. Besides, Zirene said that thing is basically garbage and it offends her that it exists at all.”

<!-- paragraph:7384 para_id:00001CD8 style:Normal -->
Davian shifted his grip on the sword, chest heaving. “Okay,” he panted, “so is she going to make me something better?”

<!-- paragraph:7385 para_id:00001CD9 style:Normal -->
Harvick blurred forward. Davian barely got his practice sword up in time. The impact slammed down through his arms hard enough to rattle his teeth. “What was that for?”

<!-- paragraph:7386 para_id:00001CDA style:Normal -->
Harvick drove his shoulder into Davian’s chest, knocking him stumbling backward across the practice yard. Another flurry of swings came immediately after—high, low, then across. Davian barely managed to duck the first, block the second, and twist away from the third.

<!-- paragraph:7387 para_id:00001CDB style:Normal -->
“Well,” Harvick said, sounding barely winded, “I could tell you were just trying to buy time to get your breath back—” He blurred again.

<!-- paragraph:7388 para_id:00001CDC style:Normal -->
Davian’s sword was knocked aside and Harvick’s foot lashed out toward his legs. Davian awkwardly hopped backward before it could take him off his feet.

<!-- paragraph:7389 para_id:00001CDD style:Normal -->
“—and being able to fight while you’re tired is another important skill you apparently don’t have.”

<!-- paragraph:7390 para_id:00001CDE style:Normal -->
Davian tried to respond, but he was panting too hard. His arms felt heavy. His bruised ribs screamed every time he drew breath. Harvick hammered down on his guard again and again, forcing him backward.

<!-- paragraph:7391 para_id:00001CDF style:Normal -->
Then Davian saw an opening in Harvick’s guard and swung for it. Harvick blurred out of the way so quickly Davian barely tracked the movement before the zan-lit sword was whipping toward his ankle.
