He got his Shroud up just in time. For a split second the translucent layer around him resisted, the blade grinding against it with enough force to make the whole thing ripple.

<!-- paragraph:7392 para_id:00001CE0 style:Normal -->
Then it flickered apart. The sword clipped his ankle and the world spun sideways. A second later Davian hit the ground hard enough to drive the air out of his lungs. He lay there gasping, staring up at the pale morning sky while Harvick loomed over him with entirely too much satisfaction.

<!-- paragraph:7393 para_id:00001CE1 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Harvick said. “I didn’t say you could take a nap.”

<!-- paragraph:7394 para_id:00001CE2 style:Normal -->
Davian dragged in a painful breath and closed his eyes. “Oldest... joke... ever.”

<!-- paragraph:7395 para_id:00001CE3 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s voice sounded from past Davian’s eyelids. “Sometimes things are still around because they’re great.”

<!-- paragraph:7396 para_id:00001CE4 style:Normal -->
Davian took a few more breaths, hoping Harvick would have the decency not to hit him while his eyes were closed. After a moment he sat up. Harvick was crouched in front of him at eye level, his forearms resting across his knees.

<!-- paragraph:7397 para_id:00001CE5 style:Normal -->
“So when you said the difference between a good swordsman and a bad one was his mind,” Davian said, “what you really meant was that the difference is being able to do special tricks.”

<!-- paragraph:7398 para_id:00001CE6 style:Normal -->
Harvick opened his mouth, but before he could answer the sounds of footsteps echoed from behind him. He whipped around, looking instantly like a child caught stealing a pie.

<!-- paragraph:7399 para_id:00001CE7 style:Normal -->
“I said no spectators—”

<!-- paragraph:7400 para_id:00001CE8 style:Normal -->
Maya and Erynn were crossing the edge of the practice yard. Maya was dressed once again in the sleeveless attire of a High Clade magus, her pale arms bare and her dark hair pulled back by a silver clasp at the back of her head. Erynn followed a few steps behind her with her hands folded behind her back, looking entirely too entertained.

<!-- paragraph:7401 para_id:00001CE9 style:Normal -->
Davian swallowed and awkwardly shifted, trying to sit in a way that both concealed the bruises striping his chest and somehow looked casual.

<!-- paragraph:7402 para_id:00001CEA style:Normal -->
“Actually,” Maya said as she approached, “the difference shouldn’t be between a good swordsman and a bad one. It should be between a swordsman who is a magus and one who is not.”

<!-- paragraph:7403 para_id:00001CEB style:Normal -->
Davian gave up trying to hide the bruises and settled instead for trying to look confident. He dipped his head slightly. “Lady Maya.”

<!-- paragraph:7404 para_id:00001CEC style:Normal -->
When he looked back up, her palm was stretched out toward him. A sphere of fire hovered before it, rapidly brightening. Davian instinctively threw his arms up over his face, bracing for another impact. It never came. When he lowered his arms, the flame was gone.

<!-- paragraph:7405 para_id:00001CED style:Normal -->
Maya looked down at him. “A magus doesn’t block with their hands,” she said. “Or their sword. Or a knife. They block with their Shroud. That leaves the shape of your body open to facilitate setting your mind to the correct countering schema.”

<!-- paragraph:7406 para_id:00001CEE style:Normal -->
Davian lowered his arms slowly. “It’s a reflex,” he muttered. “I don’t know what I can do about it right now.”

<!-- paragraph:7407 para_id:00001CEF style:Normal -->
“Oh,” Maya said. “So you can’t unlearn anything? Even if it’s what you have to do to help your family—to help us?” She shook her head. “You have to learn to tolerate the sensation of being open before you can be an effective magus.”

<!-- paragraph:7408 para_id:00001CF0 style:Normal -->
Davian pushed himself the rest of the way to his feet with a grimace. “This was all a lot easier when I still had some Ink left.”

<!-- paragraph:7409 para_id:00001CF1 style:Normal -->
Maya’s expression hardened slightly. “If anyone offers you an easy solution to something like this, they’re lying. Was the Ink really worth it?”

<!-- paragraph:7410 para_id:00001CF2 style:Normal -->
Davian let out a short breath through his nose. “It’s easy for you to say that. Not everything is so easy for the rest of us.”

<!-- paragraph:7411 para_id:00001CF3 style:Normal -->
For just a second, something flashed across Maya’s face. “This is all I’ve been learning to do my whole life,” she said. “You think that was easy? That I wouldn’t sometimes rather have been able to hide in the shadows and pretend I didn’t have any responsibility for what happens in the world?”

<!-- paragraph:7412 para_id:00001CF4 style:Normal -->
Davian straightened. “Well, why don’t you?” he shot back. “What makes it so necessary for you to be the one who decides what happens?”

<!-- paragraph:7413 para_id:00001CF5 style:Normal -->
“Oh,” Maya said coolly, “so you’d rather Drakhal and Archeon be the ones making those decisions? That’s turned out really well for you so far.”

<!-- paragraph:7414 para_id:00001CF6 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at her. He was angry at not being able to think of a response. Angry at the way she could always make him feel cornered. Angry at himself for being distracted by the way the sunlight caught in her hair.

<!-- paragraph:7415 para_id:00001CF7 style:Normal -->
Harvick coughed loudly. Davian immediately looked away with heat climbing into his face. “I have to practice more,” he muttered.

<!-- paragraph:7416 para_id:00001CF8 style:Normal -->
“Actually,” Maya said from behind him, “that’s part of what I’m here for.”

<!-- paragraph:7417 para_id:00001CF9 style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes widened. He looked helplessly at Harvick. Harvick shrugged.

<!-- paragraph:7418 para_id:00001CFA style:Normal -->
“Clearly,” Maya continued, “you need Shroud instruction more than you need more sword training. And Harvick—don’t think you can weasel your way out of this. I saw what you were doing.” Harvick immediately looked alarmed.

<!-- paragraph:7419 para_id:00001CFB style:Normal -->
Maya continued, “If there’s some other hidden technique you can teach us to keep us alive—all of us—you’d better not hold out.”

<!-- paragraph:7420 para_id:00001CFC style:Normal -->
Harvick looked down at the ground. “I said no spectators...” he mumbled petulantly.

<!-- paragraph:7421 para_id:00001CFD style:Normal -->
Maya raised an eyebrow. “And you thought that applied to me?”

<!-- paragraph:7422 para_id:00001CFE style:Normal -->
“No...”

<!-- paragraph:7423 para_id:00001CFF style:Normal -->
Maya crossed her arms. “Then go ahead. Teach us.”

<!-- paragraph:7424 para_id:00001D00 style:Normal -->
Davian watched the color drain from Harvick’s face. “I...” Harvick stammered. “I was just going to teach Davian. Like, in secret.”

<!-- paragraph:7425 para_id:00001D01 style:Normal -->
Davian cleared his throat. “I mean,” he said carefully, “if she wants to be here maybe it’s all right?”

<!-- paragraph:7426 para_id:00001D02 style:Normal -->
Maya shot Davian a sidelong glance that made it very clear she did not feel she needed his help. “Harvick,” she said, “what’s wrong? Don’t tell me you can’t learn these techniques unless you already have years of martial arts experience.”

<!-- paragraph:7427 para_id:00001D03 style:Normal -->
Harvick perked up instantly. “Yes,” he said. “That’s it. It’s just, uh... really super hard to learn and I’m so sorry but it would—it would take too long.”

<!-- paragraph:7428 para_id:00001D04 style:Normal -->
He gave Maya an oversized apologetic grin. Her eyes narrowed.

<!-- paragraph:7429 para_id:00001D05 style:Normal -->
“Harvick,” Davian said, “I hate to bring this up right now, but there’s a reason everyone always asks you to play cards with them.”

<!-- paragraph:7430 para_id:00001D06 style:Normal -->
Harvick’s grin faltered. “...because I’m fun and popular?”

<!-- paragraph:7431 para_id:00001D07 style:Normal -->
“Because,” Maya said, “you are the worst liar I—not just me—anyone, anywhere has ever met.”

<!-- paragraph:7432 para_id:00001D08 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7433 para_id:00001D09 style:Normal -->
Harvick made Maya send Erynn away, which had taken longer than it should have. Maya had argued at length about Erynn’s trustworthiness. Eventually Harvick had stood there looking so genuinely distressed that Maya relented.

<!-- paragraph:7434 para_id:00001D0A style:Normal -->
Now the three of them stood in the far corner of the courtyard, tucked behind a low stone wall and a patch of dead winter vines. It was already secluded, but Harvick still looked around nervously every few seconds like he expected someone to leap out of the bushes and arrest him. He looked cornered in more ways than one.

<!-- paragraph:7435 para_id:00001D0B style:Normal -->
“Okay,” Davian said, “what is going on? You’re acting really odd, and you’re normally pretty odd, so the fact that I’m saying anything should indicate just how odd you’re being.”

<!-- paragraph:7436 para_id:00001D0C style:Normal -->
Harvick swallowed. “Okay,” he said quietly, lowering his voice enough that they had to lean in to hear him. “So I wasn’t lying. Davian is the only one who can learn this. But not because it’s hard.”

<!-- paragraph:7437 para_id:00001D0D style:Normal -->
The silence stretched. Maya folded her arms. “Well, that’s a relief. He’s already struggling to learn things that should be pretty basic.”

<!-- paragraph:7438 para_id:00001D0E style:Normal -->
Davian shot her an irritated look. Harvick still said nothing.

<!-- paragraph:7439 para_id:00001D0F style:Normal -->
“Harvick,” Maya said, more sharply this time, “just tell us already. What’s the reason?”

<!-- paragraph:7440 para_id:00001D10 style:Normal -->
Harvick grimaced. “It’s that... um...” He rubbed the back of his neck. “I don’t know how to say this. That, uh... the Ink stuff.”

<!-- paragraph:7441 para_id:00001D11 style:Normal -->
Davian’s eyes widened. “You’re saying you know about the Ink?” he demanded. “What? How?”

<!-- paragraph:7442 para_id:00001D12 style:Normal -->
Maya cut in almost at the same time. “How could you know about that and still not realize what was going on with ‘Arrek’?”

<!-- paragraph:7443 para_id:00001D13 style:Normal -->
Harvick raised both hands. He looked a little less tense now that the secret was actually out.

<!-- paragraph:7444 para_id:00001D14 style:Normal -->
“Hey, I’m not an expert in this stuff,” he said. “Everybody has tattoos. That’s why it’s a good disguise. It’s easy to hide.”

<!-- paragraph:7445 para_id:00001D15 style:Normal -->
“When you say everybody,” Davian said slowly, “I’ve never seen one on you. Faded or otherwise. Are you saying you have one?”

<!-- paragraph:7446 para_id:00001D16 style:Normal -->
Maya tilted her head. “Show us.”

<!-- paragraph:7447 para_id:00001D17 style:Normal -->
Harvick flushed bright red. After a moment he mumbled, “It’s... hidden.”

<!-- paragraph:7448 para_id:00001D18 style:Normal -->
Davian gave him a very serious look. “I don’t know,” he said. “I think we might have to see it to be sure.”

<!-- paragraph:7449 para_id:00001D19 style:Normal -->
“Just believe me, okay?” Harvick blurted out. “Why would I make something like that up?”

<!-- paragraph:7450 para_id:00001D1A style:Normal -->
He saw the smirk spreading across Davian’s face. “Oh, very funny.”

<!-- paragraph:7451 para_id:00001D1B style:Normal -->
Maya cut back in before Davian could say anything else. “All right,” she said, “so let’s say we believe you. Isn’t this some kind of thing criminals have been getting for the last ten years or so? You’ve worked for us much longer than that.”

<!-- paragraph:7452 para_id:00001D1C style:Normal -->
Harvick shifted awkwardly. “I thought you worked it out already,” he said. “All this stuff started with Elric. Or at least he knew about it and was studying it. He said he wanted to do some experiments and needed a reliable subject. Someone he trusted.”

<!-- paragraph:7453 para_id:00001D1D style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “And it didn’t seem suspicious at all when he wanted to hide the tattoo?” he asked. “Or that it was some crazy black ink magic no one had ever heard of?”

<!-- paragraph:7454 para_id:00001D1E style:Normal -->
Harvick snorted. “Hey, that was just what being around Elric was like. Half the stuff anybody knows about... anything... is because he studied it. He did weird things all the time. After a while you learn to stop asking questions.”

<!-- paragraph:7455 para_id:00001D1F style:Normal -->
Davian looked over at Maya. “Well,” she said, “Elric was a genius who lived for hundreds of years and devoted himself to science and philosophy. I suppose it would be more surprising if he wasn’t eccentric in some ways.”

<!-- paragraph:7456 para_id:00001D20 style:Normal -->
Davian folded his arms. “I have a lot of other questions,” he said, “but I suppose the most relevant one is this: okay, so you got some kind of Ink Pact. How has it not run out by now? You clearly use it all the time.”

<!-- paragraph:7457 para_id:00001D21 style:Normal -->
Harvick looked down at his hands. “Well, that’s the thing,” he said quietly. “When I first got it, it felt like there was this ocean of Ink that could let me do anything. But the second I touched it, it dried up.”

<!-- paragraph:7458 para_id:00001D22 style:Normal -->
He rubbed one thumb across the back of his other hand. “The tattoo was really small. It faded almost right away.”

<!-- paragraph:7459 para_id:00001D23 style:Normal -->
He glanced up at them. “But Elric didn’t care. He told me right away to start doing sword forms while he watched and took notes. After a while he started telling me to listen for a right way or a wrong way to move.”

<!-- paragraph:7460 para_id:00001D24 style:Normal -->
Harvick hesitated. “And it sort of felt like there was a right way to move,” he said. “And when I moved that way, it was... faster.”

<!-- paragraph:7461 para_id:00001D25 style:Normal -->
He looked up at them. “I don’t know if that makes any sense to either of you.”

<!-- paragraph:7462 para_id:00001D26 style:Normal -->
Maya frowned thoughtfully. “So is it like Davian?” she asked. “The Ink tells you what to do and you just do it?”

<!-- paragraph:7463 para_id:00001D27 style:Normal -->
Harvick shook his head. “No. It doesn’t tell me to do anything. I can just sort of feel that there are sometimes faster ways to get somewhere. It’s more like it’s letting me know those ways are there. It’s not a voice. It’s just a feeling.”

<!-- paragraph:7464 para_id:00001D28 style:Normal -->
Davian groaned. “Great,” he said. “So I have to learn how to use a Shroud while someone is hitting me with a sword, figure out how to use Archeon sigil magic without ripping my own arms off, and the last piece is I have to somehow listen to these dark whispers from the Ink without listening to them too much?”

<!-- paragraph:7465 para_id:00001D29 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Maya said, “don’t complain. At least you have all the pieces you need to become a High Clade magus. Do you know how many people would dream of an opportunity like that?”

<!-- paragraph:7466 para_id:00001D2A style:Normal -->
Davian gave her a flat look. “What High Clade? Archeon? Am I supposed to go over there and say, ‘Hey, teach me all your magic secrets so I can come kill you later’?”

<!-- paragraph:7467 para_id:00001D2B style:Normal -->
Maya folded her arms. “Don’t be dramatic. Every valuable ability is made up of lots of small skills. Swordsmanship involves stance, grip, forms, reading opponents, and I’m sure all sorts of other small things.” She gestured toward Harvick. “It’s hard. You’re not going to be an expert in all of it immediately, and you’re going to have to drill each of the pieces over and over until you’re good at them.”

<!-- paragraph:7468 para_id:00001D2C style:Normal -->
She looked back at Davian. “Just thank the Creator that you’re not learning all of this from nothing. You’ve been using your sigil magic since you were young, your mother taught you enough bodily awareness through martial arts that you can manifest a Shroud even if you’re terrible at it, and you’ve been exposed to the Ink for quite a while now.”

<!-- paragraph:7469 para_id:00001D2D style:Normal -->
Davian looked down at the practice sword in his hand. “It just feels like too many things.”

<!-- paragraph:7470 para_id:00001D2E style:Normal -->
“You’re the one who forced us to agree to teach you,” Maya said. “It’s too late to back out now.”

<!-- paragraph:7471 para_id:00001D2F style:Normal -->
“Fine,” Davian muttered. “Clearly I have no idea where to even start. What do you suggest?”

<!-- paragraph:7472 para_id:00001D30 style:Normal -->
A small smirk tugged at the corner of Maya’s mouth. Davian did not remember her doing that before. Was he rubbing off on her?

<!-- paragraph:7473 para_id:00001D31 style:Normal -->
“Well,” she said, “then I suppose you should train with someone who’s at the same level you are.”

<!-- paragraph:7474 para_id:00001D32 style:Normal -->
---

<!-- paragraph:7475 para_id:00001D33 style:Normal -->
They crossed the citadel grounds in a long, meandering line, Maya in front with Erynn beside her while Harvick trailed behind Davian. The Rezin citadel was massive.

<!-- paragraph:7476 para_id:00001D34 style:Normal -->
Davian had known that already, of course, but actually walking through it was different. Every time he thought they had reached the edge of it, another courtyard opened up, or another long stone path lined with old statues and dead winter hedges. Towers rose overhead in every direction, connected by bridges and covered walkways that crisscrossed high above them.

<!-- paragraph:7477 para_id:00001D35 style:Normal -->
For a while nobody said anything. Eventually Davian glanced around at yet another broad courtyard paved in dark stone.

<!-- paragraph:7478 para_id:00001D36 style:Normal -->
“So is this place just long walks and courtyards forever,” he asked Maya, “or is there actually anything else in here?”

<!-- paragraph:7479 para_id:00001D37 style:Normal -->
Maya glanced back at him. “Well, originally when the citadels were built, the idea was that they could house the entire city-wedge population during a war. A lot of these courtyards that seem pointless would have been staging areas. Places for supply storage, livestock, tents, temporary shelters.”

<!-- paragraph:7480 para_id:00001D38 style:Normal -->
She gestured vaguely around them. “They were designed to be flexible. Of course, now that High Clade magi have become this powerful, all you’d really be doing is corralling people into one place where they could take massive casualties.”

<!-- paragraph:7481 para_id:00001D39 style:Normal -->
Davian immediately thought of Caius. The aftermath of his magic in that narrow side street. Bodies scattered across the stone. Walls blasted apart. Blood smeared through shattered glass and rubble. He shuddered. The idea of that happening on a larger scale was almost impossible to imagine.

<!-- paragraph:7482 para_id:00001D3A style:Normal -->
He shook his head. “Wait,” he said, “I get that the citadel is huge, but there’s no way you could fit everyone in the city in here.”

<!-- paragraph:7483 para_id:00001D3B style:Normal -->
Maya laughed softly. “Of course not. The cities used to be much smaller. Mostly just support settlements for the citadels themselves. Trade and farming changed that. Now they’ve grown into what they are.”

<!-- paragraph:7484 para_id:00001D3C style:Normal -->
“And what are they?” Davian asked. “Terrible? Overcrowded and undersupported?”

<!-- paragraph:7485 para_id:00001D3D style:Normal -->
The smile disappeared from Maya’s face. Davian immediately regretted saying it. For a while there was only the sound of their footsteps echoing across the stone.

<!-- paragraph:7486 para_id:00001D3E style:Normal -->
Then Maya said quietly, “Look, I know that you—a lot of people—have a hard life in the city. I’m sorry about that. But without Niall things would be a lot worse. You’d have all the problems you do now, except you’d also have rampaging High Clade magi settling border disputes inside civilian districts.”

<!-- paragraph:7487 para_id:00001D3F style:Normal -->
Davian looked down at the ground as he walked. “Yeah,” he said. “I guess things could always be worse. It just sucks when you’re stuck on the end where you have to constantly be reminded of that.”

<!-- paragraph:7488 para_id:00001D40 style:Normal -->
The courtyard they eventually reached was both isolated and nowhere near the outer walls. It sat tucked between two squat stone buildings, hidden from the main paths by a line of dead hedges and an old statue with its head broken off. One of Harvick’s forerunner friends sat lazily near the entrance with his back against the wall. He raised one arm when he saw them coming.

<!-- paragraph:7489 para_id:00001D41 style:Normal -->
Harvick waved back. “Dren,” he called. “So you’re not dead yet, huh?”

<!-- paragraph:7490 para_id:00001D42 style:Normal -->
Dren stood up with a groan. “Man, I wish,” he said. “The only thing that might kill us out here is boredom.”

<!-- paragraph:7491 para_id:00001D43 style:Normal -->
Davian peered past him. Selwin sat cross-legged in the center of the courtyard with his eyes closed, facing away from them. He held perfectly still. Another forerunner lay sprawled against the far wall at a respectful distance, looking half asleep until Davian noticed the man’s eyes track them.

<!-- paragraph:7492 para_id:00001D44 style:Normal -->
Davian pulled back and lowered his voice. “We already did this,” he said to Maya. “This is what kicked the whole thing off.”

<!-- paragraph:7493 para_id:00001D45 style:Normal -->
“Well,” Maya said, “to be honest, Selwin hasn’t really been getting full training.”

<!-- paragraph:7494 para_id:00001D46 style:Normal -->
She folded her arms. “I’d blame Niall, but I’m a full magus now, so I suppose I have to take some of the blame too. There’s just been a lot going on recently, and Selwin is still too far away from being useful in combat to be a priority.”

<!-- paragraph:7495 para_id:00001D47 style:Normal -->
She looked Davian up and down. “You, on the other hand...”

<!-- paragraph:7496 para_id:00001D48 style:Normal -->
Davian immediately straightened. He could feel himself flushing.

<!-- paragraph:7497 para_id:00001D49 style:Normal -->
“...are also very far away from being useful in combat.”

<!-- paragraph:7498 para_id:00001D4A style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Davian protested, “that’s not fair. I have... lots of skills.”

<!-- paragraph:7499 para_id:00001D4B style:Normal -->
“I actually have something for this,” Harvick said. He stepped forward holding a piece of chalk. Davian had absolutely no idea where it had come from. Harvick grabbed him lightly by the arm and pulled him toward a relatively blank section of wall just outside the courtyard entrance.

<!-- paragraph:7500 para_id:00001D4C style:Normal -->
Maya followed with an expression somewhere between curiosity and amusement.

<!-- paragraph:7501 para_id:00001D4D style:Normal -->
“Okay,” Harvick said. “Let’s lay out what you have.”

<!-- paragraph:7502 para_id:00001D4E style:Normal -->
He pressed the chalk to the wall and wrote.

<!-- paragraph:7503 para_id:00001D4F style:Normal -->
Magic sijil stuff

<!-- paragraph:7504 para_id:00001D50 style:Normal -->
Then, next to it, he wrote: “weak.” Davian rolled his eyes. “Okay. Fine.”

<!-- paragraph:7505 para_id:00001D51 style:Normal -->
Harvick moved down to the next line.

<!-- paragraph:7506 para_id:00001D52 style:Normal -->
Sword skills — weak

<!-- paragraph:7507 para_id:00001D53 style:Normal -->
“Hey,” Davian said, “those are at least average.”

<!-- paragraph:7508 para_id:00001D54 style:Normal -->
Harvick shook his head. “You can’t argue with the chalk.” He continued to write.

<!-- paragraph:7509 para_id:00001D55 style:Normal -->
Knife skills — okay. (No good knife though.)

<!-- paragraph:7510 para_id:00001D56 style:Normal -->
Shroud — very weak

<!-- paragraph:7511 para_id:00001D57 style:Normal -->
Harvick underlined “very” three separate times.

<!-- paragraph:7512 para_id:00001D58 style:Normal -->
Maya snorted. Davian tried not to look at her. “Okay,” he said, “what about my hand-to-hand skills? Those are pretty good.”

<!-- paragraph:7513 para_id:00001D59 style:Normal -->
Harvick wrote the next line.

<!-- paragraph:7514 para_id:00001D5A style:Normal -->
Hand-to-hand fighting — strong. Works with Ink??

<!-- paragraph:7515 para_id:00001D5B style:Normal -->
Davian smiled. Then Maya stepped forward, took the chalk from Harvick, and wrote beneath it: “not useful without a Shroud”

<!-- paragraph:7516 para_id:00001D5C style:Normal -->
She turned back toward Davian and pointed at the wall. “Do you understand?” she asked. “We don’t have time to make you good at everything on here. We might have time to make you better at one thing.”

<!-- paragraph:7517 para_id:00001D5D style:Normal -->
She tapped the last line with the chalk. “The only option is for you to get your Shroud strong enough that you can lean on your martial arts and maybe work a little bit of magic in on top of it.”

<!-- paragraph:7518 para_id:00001D5E style:Normal -->
Davian stared at the list. “That’s not a very encouraging wall.”

<!-- paragraph:7519 para_id:00001D5F style:Normal -->
“Yes,” Maya said. “That’s why you need to take my advice seriously.”

<!-- paragraph:7520 para_id:00001D60 style:Normal -->
Davian glanced over at Harvick. “Any encouraging words?”

<!-- paragraph:7521 para_id:00001D61 style:Normal -->
Harvick had been staring off into the distance. “Huh?” he said, looking back quickly. “Oh. No, you’re definitely going to die.”

<!-- paragraph:7522 para_id:00001D62 style:Normal -->
Davian looked back at the wall. Too many things. But he already knew that. If he wanted any hope of saving his parents, he needed every piece of training he could get. He needed more power, more control, more information.

<!-- paragraph:7523 para_id:00001D63 style:Normal -->
And information was the other problem. The Rezins had Aldira. They had money. They had networks of spies and contractors and people they could pressure into getting results. On his own, he was like a single etched zan—limited by isolation, able to do one thing well but unable to do everything that needed doing.

<!-- paragraph:7524 para_id:00001D64 style:Normal -->
Here, he was part of a lattice. Each piece had its own role. One moved information. One stored it. One protected the others. One struck. Together they could become something larger. More adaptive. More powerful. The thoughts flashed through his mind in an instant.

<!-- paragraph:7525 para_id:00001D65 style:Normal -->
Davian looked up into Maya’s eyes, forcing his breath not to pick up. “Fine,” he said. “I’ll do whatever you say.”

<!-- paragraph:7526 para_id:00001D66 style:Normal -->
They entered the courtyard together. Selwin was still sitting cross-legged in the center with his eyes closed, but as they approached Maya called out, “Selwin. I brought you a training partner.”

<!-- paragraph:7527 para_id:00001D67 style:Normal -->
Selwin jumped to his feet and spun around. “Arrek!” he said brightly.

<!-- paragraph:7528 para_id:00001D68 style:Normal -->
Before anyone could respond he hurried over, grinning. “Oh, are we going to do swords?” he asked. “I think I could be really good with a sword.”

<!-- paragraph:7529 para_id:00001D69 style:Normal -->
He started mimicking a series of dramatic swings with his hands. Davian crouched down in front of him.

<!-- paragraph:7530 para_id:00001D6A style:Normal -->
“Actually,” he said, “my name is Davian. And I’m here to learn about Shrouds with you.”

<!-- paragraph:7531 para_id:00001D6B style:Normal -->
Selwin frowned. “I don’t understand,” he said. “Why is your name different now? And I thought only somebody whose parents were magi could learn a Shroud.”

<!-- paragraph:7532 para_id:00001D6C style:Normal -->
Davian hesitated. “Well,” he said slowly, “there was a misunderstanding, I guess. I thought your family was my enemy, but it turns out I was wrong and you’re all actually my friends.”

<!-- paragraph:7533 para_id:00001D6D style:Normal -->
The words felt strange coming out of his mouth. “I’m sorry for lying to you. It was a big mistake. I’m here to try to help all of you make things right.” Davian risked a quick sidelong glance at Maya.

<!-- paragraph:7534 para_id:00001D6E style:Normal -->
Selwin smiled. Then he reached out and rested one small hand on Davian’s head. “You know,” he said, “that’s all right. You’re not as sad today as you were before.”

<!-- paragraph:7535 para_id:00001D6F style:Normal -->
Davian blinked. “What do you mean?”

<!-- paragraph:7536 para_id:00001D70 style:Normal -->
Selwin lifted his hand and shrugged. “I don’t know. You care about what you said.”

<!-- paragraph:7537 para_id:00001D71 style:Normal -->
Davian stared at him for a moment. “I guess everyone in your family is just really smart, huh?”

<!-- paragraph:7538 para_id:00001D72 style:Normal -->
Maya clapped her hands together once. “Okay,” she said. “Let’s start with the basics.”

<!-- paragraph:7539 para_id:00001D73 style:Normal -->
Selwin immediately sat up straighter. Davian lowered himself down beside him and tried to mimic the posture.

<!-- paragraph:7540 para_id:00001D74 style:Normal -->
“We’ve been over this before,” Maya said, pacing slowly in front of them, “but it’s worth saying again. The Shroud is a defensive reflex. It’s somehow hooked into the basic systems your body uses to tell where it is. Like how you know where your arm is without looking at it. At the same time, you still have to be able to maintain it through focus. Knowing where your legs are doesn’t mean you can immediately balance on a narrow ledge.”

<!-- paragraph:7541 para_id:00001D75 style:Normal -->
Selwin raised his hand. “But I can’t figure out the reflex,” he said. “It’s just not working.”

<!-- paragraph:7542 para_id:00001D76 style:Normal -->
He waved his raised arm back and forth. “But I know where my arm is.”

<!-- paragraph:7543 para_id:00001D77 style:Normal -->
Maya sighed. “You know I can’t just describe it to you. At some point a magus just feels the reflex, and from there they can work on training it.”

<!-- paragraph:7544 para_id:00001D78 style:Normal -->
Selwin lowered his arm, looking disappointed. “In the meantime,” Maya continued, “whether or not you can bring up a Shroud—”

<!-- paragraph:7545 para_id:00001D79 style:Normal -->
She looked pointedly at Davian. “—we can work on bodily awareness. And more importantly, maintaining that awareness under pressure.”

<!-- paragraph:7546 para_id:00001D7A style:Normal -->
She folded her hands behind her back. “The last piece is that everyone’s Shroud has the same capacity. More or less.”

<!-- paragraph:7547 para_id:00001D7B style:Normal -->
Davian frowned. “I thought some Shrouds were stronger than others,” he said. “And that modern magi had much stronger Shrouds than the old ones.”

<!-- paragraph:7548 para_id:00001D7C style:Normal -->
Maya shook her head. “No. Shrouds have always been roughly the same. The difference is throughput. Magi today have become much more efficient at sublimating the energy that builds up in their Shrouds before they’re overwhelmed.”

<!-- paragraph:7549 para_id:00001D7D style:Normal -->
Selwin nodded immediately. “That’s why Father is so effective,” he said in a rehearsed tone. “And why Maya decided to train for strong single attacks. Enough energy delivered fast enough can overwhelm a Shroud before the magus can sublimate it away.”

<!-- paragraph:7550 para_id:00001D7E style:Normal -->
Maya gave him a small nod. “Right. Thank you, Selwin.”

<!-- paragraph:7551 para_id:00001D7F style:Normal -->
She looked back to Davian. “So the key here is keeping your Shroud up no matter what. If it goes down for even a second, you’re probably going to die if you’re fighting someone powerful.”

<!-- paragraph:7552 para_id:00001D80 style:Normal -->
She began pacing again. “After that, the next step is drilling a schema that lets you sublimate efficiently based on your sigil magic.”

<!-- paragraph:7553 para_id:00001D81 style:Normal -->
Davian groaned. “I really hope this doesn’t involve me getting blasted into walls over and over again.”

<!-- paragraph:7554 para_id:00001D82 style:Normal -->
Maya’s wide grin was not reassuring.

<!-- paragraph:7564 para_id:00001D8C style:Title -->
# Chapter summaries

<!-- paragraph:7566 para_id:00001D8E style:Title -->
# Ch28 summary

<!-- paragraph:7567 para_id:00001D8F style:Normal -->
Chapter 28

Davian follows the “golden thread” out of his dissociative void straight to Maya, and they finally connect emotionally and kiss. But the Harbinger leader’s voice resurfaces through the Ink Boon, triggering a panic spiral. Davian dissociates, believes Maya is in danger from him, and runs.

<!-- paragraph:7568 para_id:00001D90 style:Normal -->
Maya, shaken and exhausted, tries to understand what’s wrong and goes to Aldira for answers about “Arrek.” She checks the Registry and then has a catastrophic Fateweaving episode that hurls her through traumatic visions—including Vorren’s past, Elric’s corruption, and an impossible vision of Niall’s death.

<!-- paragraph:7569 para_id:00001D91 style:Normal -->
Erynn grounds her and she realizes Davian, Niall, and Harvick are together in the Treasury.

<!-- paragraph:7570 para_id:00001D92 style:Normal -->
Davian returns home seeking clarity. His parents reveal the truth: the family’s origins in Archeon, the Harbingers’ threats, and their expectations for him. Kelyra gives him a repaired mask—marked with the niza za of negation.

<!-- paragraph:7571 para_id:00001D93 style:Normal -->
Following a dark fate-thread, Davian goes to the Treasury. Harvick tries to stop him, but Davian, overtaken by the Ink, injures him fatally. Niall arrives and revives Harvick with a lightning jolt.

<!-- paragraph:7572 para_id:00001D94 style:Normal -->
Davian, panicked and consumed by the Ink’s directive, enters the weightless chamber and Subverts the Tzorrax Sphere—hurling it at Niall, shattering his arms in the process. The Sphere breaks, revealing the Heart, which Davian takes.

<!-- paragraph:7573 para_id:00001D95 style:Normal -->
Maya arrives too late. She confronts Davian, realizes he has lied about everything, and knocks the blade from his hand—breaking his arm. Davian puts on his mask and escapes. Maya gives chase but overchannels, triggering an explosion that slams her into the outer wall. She blacks out after ordering the guards to check on Niall.

<!-- paragraph:7576 para_id:00001D98 style:Title -->
# Ch29 summary

<!-- paragraph:7577 para_id:00001D99 style:Normal -->
Chapter 29 Summary

<!-- paragraph:7578 para_id:00001D9A style:Normal -->
Kelyra and Jalen wait anxiously after Davian’s chaotic return home, arguing about what to do and wrestling with the weight of their past decisions. When a massive crash comes from outside, they fear an Archeon attack—but instead find Davian broken and ink-wreathed in the courtyard. As they watch in horror, his body begins healing itself unnaturally. He briefly wakes, raving in cryptic, prophetic language before giving Kelyra the Heart and collapsing again.

<!-- paragraph:7579 para_id:00001D9B style:Normal -->
Realizing their position is now dangerously exposed, Kelyra decides they must take the Heart to the Harbingers immediately and demand the power they were promised. She, Jalen, and the Concord bruiser Karra travel through the city to a hidden drop, are collected by a Harbinger agent, and are led deep into their underground meeting chamber.

<!-- paragraph:7580 para_id:00001D9C style:Normal -->
There, the Harbinger leader—ink-covered, erratic, ecstatic—reveals that Karra has already taken an Ink Boon. Kelyra is forced into a ritual combat “penalty” for failing to complete the full contract. She kills Karra—only for Karra to rise whole again, empowered far beyond the Ink Kelyra carries. Kelyra’s own Ink runs out mid-fight, and Karra brutalizes her.

<!-- paragraph:7581 para_id:00001D9D style:Normal -->
The Harbinger revels in the spectacle, speaking in apocalyptic riddles about dark threads and inevitability. As Kelyra staggers upright, she realizes too late that the Harbingers have betrayed her completely. A figure steps into the light—Serathis, her tormentor from the Archeon breeding pits. Overwhelmed with terror, Kelyra tries to flee, but a Shroud-wielding enforcer captures and throws her back.

<!-- paragraph:7582 para_id:00001D9E style:Normal -->
The Harbinger proclaims that the “dark flame” must be kindled, and Serathis leans down with a mock-gentle greeting:
 “I missed you, daughter.”

<!-- paragraph:7583 para_id:00001D9F style:Normal -->
Kelyra screams—ending the chapter on the moment of her re-enslavement.
